topics.title: Types of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Ships (KTU 2024 Definition):** Classification of marine vessels based on their **operational purpose, means of propulsion, hull form, type of cargo carried, and the principle of buoyancy support** that keeps them afloat. Each ship type is uniquely engineered to optimize stability, hydrodynamics, structural strength, and cargo-handling efficiency for a specific maritime mission.

The taxonomy of ships is the **foundation of naval architecture**, where every vessel is designed from the keel upward to fulfill a precise operational role — whether transporting millions of barrels of crude oil across oceans, ferrying thousands of TEU containers, defending territorial waters, or supporting offshore oil rigs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "Ships are like specialized trucks, but for the ocean."**
>
> Just as on land we have *tankers* (fuel trucks), *box trailers* (container trucks), *flatbeds* (bulk carriers), and *refrigerated vans* (reefer ships) — each optimized for a *single type of cargo* — in the maritime world, the same specialization exists. A **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** is essentially a giant floating fuel tank; a **Container Ship** is a floating parking garage for boxes; a **Naval Frigate** is a floating military base; and a **Hydrofoil** is a floating race car that lifts itself out of the water to reduce drag.
>
> **The key engineering rule:** *No single ship design can efficiently do everything.* Hence the diversity of ship types.

## 1.3 Standard Maritime Metrics Used in Classification

> [!NOTE]
> Every ship type is defined by these universal naval-architecture metrics:
> - **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** Total weight (in tonnes) a ship can carry including cargo, fuel, crew, and stores. Unit: **tonnes (t)** or **Long Tons (LT)**.
> - **Gross Tonnage (GT):** A volumetric measure of total enclosed space; unitless index.
> - **Length Overall (LOA):** Tip-to-tip length in **metres (m)**.
> - **Displacement (Δ):** Weight of water displaced by the hull, equal to the ship's mass (Archimedes' Principle). Unit: **tonnes**.
> - **Service Speed (Vs):** Design cruising speed in **knots (kn)** where **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s**.
> - **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit):** Standard container capacity measure for containerships.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Ships

Ships are classified along **five orthogonal engineering axes**. Mastering this hierarchy is essential for KTU Module 1.

### Axis A — Classification by **Operational Purpose**

| Category | Sub-Type | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Merchant / Commercial** | Cargo, Tanker, Container, Bulk, Ro-Ro, LNG | Commercial freight & trade |
| **Naval / Warships** | Aircraft Carrier, Destroyer, Frigate, Corvette, Submarine | Defense & combat |
| **Fishing Vessels** | Trawler, Purse-seiner, Long-liner | Catching marine life |
| **Pleasure Craft** | Yacht, Cruise Liner, Ferry | Recreation & tourism |
| **Special Purpose** | Dredger, Cable Layer, Tug, Ice-breaker, Research Vessel, OSV | Specific industrial/scientific task |

### Axis B — Classification by **Means of Propulsion**

- **Self-Propelled (Motor Ships):** Diesel, Steam Turbine, Gas Turbine, Nuclear-powered.
- **Sailing Vessels:** Wind-driven (rare commercially, used in training).
- **Non-Propelled:** Barges, Dhows — must be towed or pushed.
- **Hybrid:** Modern dual-fuel LNG-electric, solar-assisted.

### Axis C — Classification by **Hull Form & Buoyancy Principle**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most physics-rich classification** for a Naval Architecture course.

- **Displacement Hull:** Floats by Archimedean buoyancy. Operates fully submerged at the waterline. Froude Number $F_n < 0.4$.
- **Planing Hull:** At speed, hydrodynamic lift raises most of the hull above water. $F_n > 0.4$. Used in patrol boats.
- **Hydrofoil:** Underwater foils generate lift at speed, lifting the hull clear of the water. **Drag reduced by up to 70 %**.
- **Air-Cushion Vehicle (ACV) / Hovercraft:** Air cushion supports the craft above water/land.
- **SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull):** Twin submerged torpedo hulls give exceptional stability.
- **Submarine:** Operates both on the surface and submerged by ballasting.

### Axis D — Classification by **Cargo Carried**

- **Liquid Bulk:** Crude carriers, Product tankers, Chemical tankers, LNG/LPG carriers.
- **Dry Bulk:** Bulk carriers (Capesize, Panamax, Handymax), Ore carriers.
- **Containerized:** Cellular container ships (Feeder, Panamax, Post-Panamax, ULCS).
- **General Cargo:** Multi-deck, break-bulk, geared ships.
- **Unitised / Wheeled:** Ro-Ro, Car carriers (PCTC), Con-Ro hybrids.
- **Refrigerated (Reefer):** Specialized for perishables.

### Axis E — Classification by **Size Class**

| Size Class | DWT Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Dhow / Barge** | < 500 t | Harbour, river |
| **Handysize** | 15,000 – 35,000 t | Tramping trade |
| **Handymax / Supramax** | 35,000 – 60,000 t | Minor bulks |
| **Panamax** | 60,000 – 80,000 t | Old Panama Canal limit |
| **Capesize** | 100,000 – 200,000 t | Iron ore, coal |
| **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** | 200,000 – 320,000 t | Crude oil |
| **ULCC (Ultra Large Crude Carrier)** | > 320,000 t | Crude oil (rare) |
| **ULCS (Ultra Large Container Ship)** | > 20,000 TEU | Asia–Europe trade |

## 2.2 The Underlying Physics of Ship Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many ship types?** Because **resistance, stability, and structural loading** differ vastly with mission profile:
>
> - A *Bulk Carrier* has a wide, shallow hull → low resistance when half-loaded.
> - A *Container Ship* has a long, narrow hull with high freeboard → low wave-making resistance at high $F_n$.
> - A *Tanker* has a flat, broad deck with internal subdivision → maximizes volumetric capacity.
> - A *Hydrofoil* trades buoyancy for hydrodynamic lift → minimum drag at high speed.
> - A *Submarine* trades surface area for a pressure hull → withstands hydrostatic pressure at depth.

The governing relationships (covered in detail in later modules) are:

$$\text{Buoyancy Force: } F_B = \rho_w \, g \, \nabla$$

$$\text{Resistance Total: } R_T = R_F + R_W + R_A + R_B$$

where $R_F$ = frictional, $R_W$ = wave-making, $R_A$ = air, $R_B$ = appendage.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **all formulas, ratios, and constants** relevant to *Types of Ships* as per KTU 2024 OENST611 Module 1.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Dimensionless | Hull-speed similarity parameter |
| 2 | Displacement | $\Delta = \rho_w \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | By Archimedes |
| 3 | Deadweight Ratio | $DWR = \dfrac{DWT}{\Delta}$ | Dimensionless | Typical 0.55 – 0.75 for tankers |
| 4 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Dimensionless | Tankers: 0.80 – 0.85 |
| 5 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates fullness of ends |
| 6 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_WP = \dfrac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Dimensionless | Affects initial stability |
| 7 | Speed Conversion | $1 \, \text{knot} = 0.5144 \, \text{m/s}$ | — | Universal |
| 8 | Service Speed vs Trial Speed | $V_s \approx 0.90 \cdot V_{\text{trial}}$ | knots | Allowance for fouling |
| 9 | TEU Capacity | $1 \, \text{TEU} = 1 \times 20\text{ft container}$ | TEU | 1 FEU = 2 TEU |
| 10 | Hydrofoil Lift Condition | $L_{\text{foil}} \ge W_{\text{ship}}$ at $F_n > 0.6$ | N | Lift-off threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` for absolute values** — For example, use $\vert x \vert$ in math mode to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The classification system is **not academic — it determines:**
> - **Port fees & tariffs** (based on GT).
> - **Insurance premiums** (warships vs tankers have different risk profiles).
> - **Canal transit rights** (Panamax / Suezmax / Malaccamax locks).
> - **Regulatory compliance** (SOLAS, MARPOL, IMDG codes differ by ship type).
> - **Crew certification requirements** (STCW modules differ for tankers/passenger ships).
> - **Hull insurance clauses** (single-deck vs double-bottom tankers).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Block Coefficient $C_B$ Differentiates Ship Types

The **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** is the single most diagnostic geometric ratio for ship-type identification. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the Underlying Volume

The displaced volume of a ship $\nabla$ is the volume of water pushed aside by the underwater hull. For a rectangular prismatic reference body of length $L$, beam $B$, and draught $T$:

$$V_{\text{ref}} = L \cdot B \cdot T$$

This is the *maximum* volume the ship could possibly displace if it were a perfect rectangular box.

### Step 2 — Define the Actual Underwater Volume

The actual underwater volume of a real ship is $\nabla$, which is **always less** than $V_{\text{ref}}$ because the hull tapers at the ends (fore and aft), and the keel rises.

$$\nabla < L \cdot B \cdot T$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio (Block Coefficient)

The Block Coefficient is defined as the ratio of the actual displaced volume to the reference box volume:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

This is a **dimensionless number between 0 and 1**:
- $C_B \to 1$ → ship is a *full-form* vessel (e.g., a tugboat, a tanker).
- $C_B \to 0$ → ship is a *fine-form* vessel (e.g., a racing yacht, a warship).

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Displacement and Density

Since by Archimedes' principle, the displacement mass $\Delta$ equals the displaced water weight:

$$\Delta = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

Therefore the displaced volume is:

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_w \cdot g}$$

Substituting into the Block Coefficient formula:

$$C_B = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### Step 5 — Typical Numerical Values per Ship Type

Using saltwater density $\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$:

$$\rho_w \cdot g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25 \, \text{N/m}^3 \approx 10.055 \, \text{kN/m}^3$$

For a typical VLCC tanker with $L = 330 \, \text{m}$, $B = 58 \, \text{m}$, $T = 22 \, \text{m}$, $\Delta = 320{,}000$ tonnes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{320{,}000 \times 10^3 \, \text{kg}}{1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3} \\
&= 312{,}195 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ref}} &= 330 \times 58 \times 22 \\
&= 421{,}080 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{312{,}195}{421{,}080} \\
&\approx 0.741
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *VLCC* with $C_B \approx 0.74$ is **fat and slow** — built to maximize cargo volume. A *frigate* with $C_B \approx 0.45$ is **slim and fast** — built to slice through waves with low resistance. The Block Coefficient *alone* can tell you the ship type before you even see the deck layout.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Ship Types vs Design Parameters

The following matrix is the **definitive reference** for KTU Module 1 numerical problems.

| Ship Type | $C_B$ Range | Length/Breadth (L/B) | Breadth/Draught (B/T) | Froude No. $F_n$ | Typical DWT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tug / Harbour Craft** | 0.55 – 0.65 | 3.5 – 4.0 | 2.0 – 2.5 | 0.30 – 0.40 | 100 – 500 t |
| **Trawler (Fishing)** | 0.50 – 0.60 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 2.0 – 2.3 | 0.30 – 0.40 | 200 – 2,000 t |
| **General Cargo** | 0.60 – 0.70 | 6.0 – 7.0 | 2.4 – 2.8 | 0.22 – 0.30 | 5,000 – 25,000 t |
| **Bulk Carrier** | 0.72 – 0.82 | 6.0 – 7.5 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 0.18 – 0.24 | 30,000 – 200,000 t |
| **Crude Oil Tanker (VLCC)** | 0.78 – 0.85 | 5.5 – 6.0 | 2.4 – 2.7 | 0.13 – 0.18 | 200,000 – 320,000 t |
| **Product Tanker** | 0.70 – 0.78 | 6.0 – 7.0 | 2.4 – 2.7 | 0.20 – 0.25 | 10,000 – 80,000 t |
| **Container Ship (Cellular)** | 0.55 – 0.65 | 7.0 – 9.0 | 2.6 – 3.2 | 0.22 – 0.30 | 20,000 – 200,000 t |
| **LNG Carrier (Moss / Membrane)** | 0.65 – 0.72 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.0 – 2.3 | 0.18 – 0.22 | 50,000 – 100,000 t |
| **Naval Frigate / Destroyer** | 0.40 – 0.50 | 8.5 – 10.0 | 2.8 – 3.4 | 0.30 – 0.45 | 3,000 – 9,000 t |
| **Aircraft Carrier (Nuclear)** | 0.55 – 0.65 | 7.0 – 8.0 | 2.8 – 3.0 | 0.25 – 0.32 | 60,000 – 100,000 t |
| **Submarine (Surfaced)** | 0.40 – 0.50 | 7.5 – 9.0 | — | 0.20 – 0.35 | 5,000 – 25,000 t |
| **Hydrofoil (Passenger)** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 4.0 – 6.0 | — | 0.60 – 1.20 | 50 – 500 t |
| **SWATH** | 0.40 – 0.50 | 4.0 – 5.0 | — | 0.30 – 0.45 | 500 – 5,000 t |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem — Model Solution**

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has $L = 225 \, \text{m}$, $B = 32.2 \, \text{m}$, $T = 13.5 \, \text{m}$, and its Block Coefficient is $C_B = 0.78$. Calculate the displaced volume in $\text{m}^3$ and the displacement in tonnes at sea ($\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the reference box volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ref}} &= L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 225 \times 32.2 \times 13.5 \\
&= 97{,}807.5 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the displaced volume using $C_B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 0.78 \times 97{,}807.5 \\
&= 76{,}289.85 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert displaced volume to displacement (mass):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_w \cdot \nabla \\
&= 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 76{,}289.85 \, \text{m}^3 \\
&= 78{,}197{,}096 \, \text{kg} \\
&\approx 78{,}197 \, \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification against typical bulk-carrier range:**
A *Capesize bulk carrier* of this length typically has DWT ≈ 75,000 – 80,000 t. Our calculated $\Delta$ of ~78,000 t (which is the *lightweight + DWT* combined mass) is consistent with the design range. ✔

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ship-Type Classification

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of ship-type classification as per KTU Module 1. This serves as a hierarchical flow of how naval architects select hull form, propulsion, and structural arrangement based on operational purpose.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START - Mission Profile] --> B{Primary Function?}
    B -->|Cargo Trade| C1[Merchant Ship]
    B -->|Defense| C2[Naval Warship]
    B -->|Marine Harvest| C3[Fishing Vessel]
    B -->|Recreation| C4[Passenger Pleasure Craft]
    B -->|Specialized Industry| C5[Special Purpose Ship]

    C1 --> D{Cargo Form?}
    D -->|Liquid Bulk| E1[Crude Tanker VLCC ULCC]
    D -->|Dry Bulk| E2[Bulk Carrier Capesize Panamax]
    D -->|Containerised| E3[Container Ship ULCS PostPanamax]
    D -->|Wheeled Units| E4[RoRo PCTC]
    D -->|Refrigerated| E5[Reefer Ship]
    D -->|Gas Liquid| E6[LNG LPG Carrier]

    C2 --> F{Role?}
    F -->|Strike Power| G1[Aircraft Carrier]
    F -->|Escort Patrol| G2[Frigate Corvette]
    F -->|Offence| G3[Destroyer Cruiser]
    F -->|Subsea Ops| G4[Submarine]

    C5 --> H{Specialization?}

    H -->|Polar| I2[Ice Breaker]
    H -->|Subsea| I3[Cable Layer Pipe Layer]
    H -->|Offshore| I4[OSV PSV AHTS]

    C1 --> J[Buoyancy Principle Axis]
    C2 --> J
    C3 --> J
    C4 --> J
    C5 --> J

    J --> K{Hull Form?}
    K -->|Full Buoyancy| L1[Displacement Hull]
    K -->|Dynamic Lift| L2[Planing Hull]
    K -->|Foil Lift| L3[Hydrofoil]
    K -->|Air Lift| L4[Air Cushion Vehicle]
    K -->|Multi Hull| L5[SWATH Catamaran Trimaran]

    L1 --> M[FINAL SELECTED SHIP DESIGN]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    L5 --> M

    E1 --> M
    E2 --> M
    E3 --> M
    E4 --> M
    E5 --> M
    E6 --> M
    G1 --> M
    G2 --> M

    G4 --> M

```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Ship-Type Selection Decision Tree

This second diagram captures the **engineering decision process** the naval architect follows to finalize a ship type.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Customer Mission Brief] --> P2[Required Deadweight DWT]
    P2 --> P3[Required Service Speed Vs knots]
    P3 --> P4{Cargo Volumetric Density}

    P4 -->|High gt per m3| Q1[Tanker Hull High CB 0.80]
    P4 -->|Low gt per m3| Q2[Bulk Carrier Hull Wide Deck]
    P4 -->|Mixed| Q3[General Cargo Multi Deck]

    P3 --> R1{Speed Regime}
    R1 -->|Fn less than 0.25| S1[Displacement Dominant]
    R1 -->|Fn 0.25 to 0.45| S2[Semi Displacement]
    R1 -->|Fn greater than 0.45| S3[Planing or Hydrofoil]

    S1 --> T1[Conventional Hull Form Selected]
    S2 --> T2[Fine Form or SWATH Selected]
    S3 --> T3[Planing Hull or Hydrofoil Selected]

    Q1 --> U[Combine Hull Form Cargo Speed]
    Q2 --> U
    Q3 --> U
    T1 --> U
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U

    U --> V[Final Ship Type e.g. VLCC Capesize Frigate]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks (with internal choice), and questions map to the Course Outcomes **CO1 & CO2** of OENST611.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Classify ships based on the principle of buoyancy support. Give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

Ships are classified by buoyancy principle into the following categories:

1. **Displacement Hulls:** Float entirely by Archimedean buoyancy. Operate with full hull submerged at waterline. *Examples:* Oil tanker, bulk carrier, container ship.
2. **Planing Hulls:** At speed, hydrodynamic lift raises the hull partially out of the water. *Examples:* Patrol boats, racing powerboats.
3. **Hydrofoils:** Underwater foils generate lift, lifting the hull clear of the water. *Examples:* Passenger hydrofoils, military hydrofoils.
4. **Air-Cushion Vehicles (Hovercraft):** Air cushion supports the craft. *Examples:* Military hovercraft, civil SR.N4 hovercraft.
5. **Submarines:** Surface displacement and submerged pressure-hull operation. *Examples:* Diesel-electric and nuclear attack submarines.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a VLCC and a Capesize bulk carrier in terms of DWT, hull form, and cargo.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | VLCC | Capesize Bulk Carrier |
|---|---|---|
| **DWT Range** | 200,000 – 320,000 t | 100,000 – 200,000 t |
| **Cargo** | Crude oil | Iron ore, coal, grain |
| **Hull Form** | Full, flat-bottomed, high $C_B$ (0.78–0.85) | Wide, shallow, high $C_B$ (0.72–0.82) |
| **Hull Type** | Double hull (MARPOL mandated) | Single hull with hopper tanks |
| **Service Speed** | 15 – 16 kn | 13 – 15 kn |
| **Canal Restriction** | Too large for Suez/Panama | Too large for Suez/Panama |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> *Students commonly lose 2-3 marks in Part B by (a) failing to **draw a labelled sketch** of the ship type — examiners expect at least one schematic, (b) omitting **numerical values** of typical DWT and $C_B$ ranges, and (c) confusing **classification axes** — e.g., mixing up 'purpose' with 'hull form' in the same answer. Always state the *axis of classification* before listing types.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify ships based on the **type of cargo carried**, with a detailed description of any **four major ship types**, including their typical DWT, $C_B$ values, and a labelled sketch of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has principal dimensions $L = 190 \, \text{m}$, $B = 30 \, \text{m}$, $T = 12.5 \, \text{m}$. Its block coefficient is $C_B = 0.80$. Calculate the **displaced volume** in $\text{m}^3$ and the **displacement** in tonnes assuming sea water density $\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A (a)**

**Major cargo-based ship types:**

**1. Crude Oil Tanker (VLCC):**
- Carries crude petroleum in segregated cargo tanks.
- DWT: 200,000 – 320,000 t.
- $C_B$: 0.78 – 0.85 (very full form).
- Double-hull construction mandated by MARPOL Annex I.
- Long, flat, broad hull with machinery at the stern.

**2. Container Ship (ULCS):**
- Carries standardized TEU/FEU containers in cellular guides.
- DWT: 20,000 – 200,000 t; capacity up to 24,000 TEU.
- $C_B$: 0.55 – 0.65 (fine form for high $F_n$).
- High freeboard, narrow beam relative to length, bridge-aft design.

**3. LNG Carrier:**
- Transports liquefied natural gas at −163 °C.
- DWT: 50,000 – 100,000 t.
- $C_B$: 0.65 – 0.72.
- Two main containment systems: **Moss spherical tanks** and **membrane tanks** (GTT NO96 / Mark III).

**4. Ro-Ro (Roll-on/Roll-off):**
- Wheeled cargo drives on/off via stern or bow ramps.
- DWT: 5,000 – 30,000 t.
- $C_B$: 0.55 – 0.70.
- Multiple internal decks; large door openings; no onboard cranes.

**Labelled Sketch (Description for Examiner):**

> A simple side-view and cross-section should be drawn showing the cargo tank arrangement for each ship type, with major dimensions L, B, T, and freeboard marked. The sketch must show the bridge position, machinery space, and cargo holds clearly differentiated.

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A (b)**

**Step 1 — Reference box volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ref}} &= L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 190 \times 30 \times 12.5 \\
&= 71{,}250 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual displaced volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot V_{\text{ref}} \\
&= 0.80 \times 71{,}250 \\
&= 57{,}000 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_w \cdot \nabla \\
&= 1025 \times 57{,}000 \\
&= 58{,}425{,}000 \, \text{kg} \\
&= 58{,}425 \, \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against typical Handymax bulk-carrier range (typical DWT 50,000 – 60,000 t):** The calculated displacement of ~58,425 t (which includes lightweight + DWT) is in good agreement with a Handymax class vessel. ✔

**Final Answer:** $\nabla = 57{,}000 \, \text{m}^3$ and $\Delta = 58{,}425 \, \text{tonnes}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **five major axes of ship classification** with at least two examples under each axis. Draw a hierarchical chart showing the relationship between purpose, hull form, and cargo. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VLCC has principal dimensions $L = 330 \, \text{m}$, $B = 58 \, \text{m}$, $T = 22 \, \text{m}$, with a Block Coefficient $C_B = 0.82$. Calculate the **displacement in tonnes** and comment on whether the ship would be **Suezmax compliant** (Suezmax limit: $L \leq 240 \, \text{m}$, $B \leq 32.2 \, \text{m}$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B (a)**

**Five Major Axes of Ship Classification:**

**Axis 1 — Operational Purpose:** Merchant, Naval, Fishing, Pleasure, Special-purpose.
*Examples:* VLCC (merchant), Frigate (naval), Trawler (fishing), Yacht (pleasure), Dredger (special).

**Axis 2 — Means of Propulsion:** Self-propelled (motor ships), Sailing, Non-propelled (barges), Hybrid.
*Examples:* Diesel-electric submarine, sailing training ship, harbour barge.

**Axis 3 — Hull Form / Buoyancy Principle:** Displacement, Planing, Hydrofoil, Air-cushion, Submarine.
*Examples:* Container ship (displacement), patrol boat (planing), Boeing 929 Jetfoil (hydrofoil), SR.N4 (air-cushion), Scorpene-class (submarine).

**Axis 4 — Cargo Carried:** Liquid bulk, dry bulk, containerized, general, unitized, refrigerated.
*Examples:* VLCC, Capesize bulk carrier, ULCS, break-bulk cargo ship, PCTC, reefer ship.

**Axis 5 — Size Class:** Dhow, Handysize, Handymax, Panamax, Capesize, VLCC, ULCC, ULCS.
*Examples:* Harbour dhow, MR product tanker, Capesize ore carrier, HMM Algeciras-class ULCS.

**Hierarchical Chart:** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B (b)**

**Step 1 — Reference box volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ref}} &= L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 330 \times 58 \times 22 \\
&= 421{,}080 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual displaced volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot V_{\text{ref}} \\
&= 0.82 \times 421{,}080 \\
&= 345{,}285.6 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_w \cdot \nabla \\
&= 1025 \times 345{,}285.6 \\
&= 353{,}917{,}740 \, \text{kg} \\
&\approx 353{,}918 \, \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Suezmax Compliance Check:**

- $L = 330 \, \text{m} > 240 \, \text{m}$ ✗ (Fails length criterion)
- $B = 58 \, \text{m} > 32.2 \, \text{m}$ ✗ (Fails beam criterion)

**Conclusion:** The vessel is a **VLCC** and is **NOT Suezmax compliant**. It cannot transit the Suez Canal fully loaded. Such a vessel must use the **Cape of Good Hope route** to avoid the canal, increasing voyage time and fuel cost. This is a classic example of how ship-type classification by *size class* directly determines global trade routing.

**Final Answer:** $\Delta \approx 353{,}918 \, \text{tonnes}$; ship is **not** Suezmax compliant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Types of Ships**

- ⭐ **Five Classification Axes:** Purpose, Propulsion, Hull Form/Buoyancy, Cargo, Size.
- ⭐ **Block Coefficient $C_B$ is the master geometric ratio:** Tankers $\approx 0.80$, Warships $\approx 0.45$, Containerships $\approx 0.60$.
- ⭐ **Froude Number $F_n$** distinguishes displacement ($F_n < 0.4$) from planing/hydrofoil ($F_n > 0.4$) regimes.
- ⭐ **VLCC DWT range = 200,000 – 320,000 t; ULCC > 320,000 t.**
- ⭐ **Capesize** ships cannot transit Suez or Panama — they go via Cape of Good Hope / Cape Horn.
- ⭐ **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.**
- ⭐ **Double-hull tankers** are mandated by MARPOL Annex I after the *Erika* (1999) and *Prestige* (2002) disasters.
- ⭐ **Container ship size** is measured in **TEU**, not DWT.
- ⭐ **Hydrofoils reduce drag by up to 70%** by lifting the hull clear of water at speed.
- ⭐ **Submarines** are the only ship type that operates on two distinct buoyancy principles (surface displacement + submerged ballast).
- ⭐ **LNG carriers** carry cargo at −163 °C — use either Moss spherical or GTT membrane tanks.
- ⭐ Always **state the axis of classification** before answering any "classify ships" question — examiners explicitly test this.
- ⭐ Always **draw at least one labelled sketch** in 14-mark questions.
- ⭐ Memorize the **displacement formula** $\Delta = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ — it appears in every numerical question.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Ocean and Naval Architecture engineering**, the **Classification of Floating Bodies** refers to the systematic categorization of marine vessels and floating structures into distinct groups based on shared physical, operational, and geometrical characteristics. The classification parameters include buoyancy behavior, hull form, mode of support, service purpose, structural arrangement, and regulatory compliance under classification societies (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), DNV, Bureau Veritas**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Classification is a foundational module because the equations governing a fishing trawler differ fundamentally from those governing a planing speedboat. Engineers must first classify the body before applying any governing formula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **basket of fruits at a grocery store**. The shopkeeper does NOT just dump apples, mangoes, and grapes into one big pile. Instead, the shopkeeper sorts them by:
- Type (fruit family)
- Size (small, medium, large)
- Use (eating fresh vs. juicing)
- Weight (light vs. heavy)

Marine engineers do the **EXACT same thing** with floating bodies. A **cargo ship** is sorted into one category, a **submarine** into another, and a **hydrofoil ferry** into a third because each behaves differently in water. **Classification = Sorting by behavior & purpose.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential in the classification analysis:
- **Density of fresh water (ρ_fw): 1000 kg/m³**
- **Density of sea water (ρ_sw): 1025 kg/m³** (standard)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g): 9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific gravity of sea water: 1.025**
- **1 Nautical Mile: 1852 m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Specific Gravity (SG):** The ratio of the density of a substance to the density of fresh water at 4 °C. It is a dimensionless quantity that determines whether a body floats or sinks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Parameters (Structured Logic)

A floating body is classified using a hierarchical decision tree:

- **Step 1: Buoyancy Behavior**
  - **Floating:** Weight of body < Maximum buoyant force
  - **Submerged (sunk):** Weight of body > Maximum buoyant force
  - **Suspended (neutrally buoyant):** Weight of body = Buoyant force at any depth

- **Step 2: Mode of Support (Speed-based Classification)**
  - **Displacement Type:** Hull fully supports vessel at low Froude Numbers (Fn < 0.4)
  - **Semi-Displacement Type:** Transitional regime (0.4 < Fn < 0.6)
  - **Planing Type:** Dynamic lift supports vessel (Fn > 0.6)
  - **Hydrofoil Type:** Foils lift the hull clear of water
  - **Air-Cushion Vehicle (ACV):** Air pressure supports the craft

- **Step 3: Hull Configuration**
  - **Monohull:** Single hull (most common)
  - **Multihull:** Catamaran, Trimaran

- **Step 4: Service/Purpose**
  - **Merchant:** Cargo, Tankers, Bulk Carriers, Container Ships
  - **Naval:** Warships, Aircraft Carriers, Submarines
  - **Fishing:** Trawlers, Tuna Seiners
  - **Recreational:** Yachts, Sailboats
  - **Special Purpose:** Towing, Dredging, Research

- **Step 5: Classification by Society Rules**
  - Structural integrity standards, welding codes, material specifications

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes Principle) | Newtons (N) |
| $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of floating body | Newtons (N) |
| $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement (mass of water displaced) | kg or tonnes |
| $C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block Coefficient (fullness) | Dimensionless |
| $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Prismatic Coefficient | Dimensionless |
| $C_W = \frac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane Area Coefficient | Dimensionless |
| $SG = \frac{\rho_{body}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific Gravity | Dimensionless |
| $Fn = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | Froude Number (speed classification) | Dimensionless |
| $DWT = \Delta - LWT$ | Deadweight Tonnage | Tonnes |
| $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Transverse Metacentric Height (stability) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **Displacement** $(\Delta)$ (a mass/weight quantity) with **Displaced Volume** $(\nabla)$ (a volume quantity). Many students lose marks for this swap.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Classification is the **first step in ship design** because:
- Determines the **applicable naval architecture formulas** (e.g., Savitsky's planing equations vs. Taylor's resistance series).
- Dictates **regulatory standards** (SOLAS, MARPOL, classification society rules).
- Influences **material selection** (mild steel for cargo, aluminum for hydrofoils).
- Governs **economic feasibility studies** and **insurance premium calculations**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Archimedes' Principle and the Floating Condition

The fundamental derivation that justifies the classification of a body as "floating", "submerged", or "suspended" is based on Archimedes' principle.

**Statement:** Any body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.

**Derivation:**

Consider a floating body of mass $m$ and volume $V$ in a fluid of density $\rho_f$. The body is in static equilibrium.

The weight of the body acting downward is:
$$W = m \cdot g = \rho_b \cdot V \cdot g$$

where $\rho_b$ is the density of the body material, $V$ is the total volume, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

The buoyant force (upward) equals the weight of the displaced fluid:
$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot \nabla \cdot g$$

where $\nabla$ is the submerged (displaced) volume of the body.

For static equilibrium, the sum of vertical forces must equal zero:
$$\sum F_z = 0$$

$$F_B - W = 0$$

$$\rho_f \cdot \nabla \cdot g = \rho_b \cdot V \cdot g$$

Cancelling $g$ on both sides:
$$\rho_f \cdot \nabla = \rho_b \cdot V$$

Rearranging to find the submerged volume fraction:
$$\frac{\nabla}{V} = \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$$

This yields the **Floating Condition (Generalized):**

$$\frac{\nabla}{V} = SG_{body}$$

**Three Classification Outcomes:**

- **If $\rho_b < \rho_f$ (i.e., SG < 1):** The body floats with $\nabla < V$. Part of the body remains above the waterline.

- **If $\rho_b = \rho_f$ (i.e., SG = 1):** The body is neutrally buoyant. It can be suspended at any depth (used in submarines for trim control).

- **If $\rho_b > \rho_f$ (i.e., SG > 1):** The body cannot achieve equilibrium at the surface. It sinks until it reaches the seabed (a fully submerged body in equilibrium).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A solid steel block of dimensions 2 m × 1 m × 0.5 m and density $7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is placed in sea water ($\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Classify the body and calculate the submerged depth.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the weight of the body:
$$W = \rho_b \cdot V \cdot g$$

$$W = 7800 \cdot (2 \times 1 \times 0.5) \cdot 9.81$$

$$W = 7800 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 9.81 = 76{,}518 \text{ N}$$

Step 2 — Compute the buoyant force required for equilibrium:
$$F_B = W = 76{,}518 \text{ N}$$

Step 3 — Compute the required displaced volume:
$$\nabla = \frac{F_B}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{76{,}518}{1025 \cdot 9.81}$$

$$\nabla = \frac{76{,}518}{10{,}055.25} = 7.6095 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 4 — Compare to total volume:
$$V_{total} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$$

Since $\nabla > V_{total}$, the body **CANNOT FLOAT**. It is classified as a **Sinking Body (Submerged)**.

Step 5 — Upon reaching the seabed, the equilibrium condition becomes:
$$F_B = W$$
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = \rho_b \cdot V \cdot g$$
$$\text{Normal Reaction from seabed} = W - F_B$$
$$N = 76{,}518 - (1025 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 9.81) = 76{,}518 - 10{,}055.25 = 66{,}462.75 \text{ N}$$

**Classification Result:** The steel block is a **Submerged (Sunk) Body** with a seabed normal reaction of **66,462.75 N**.

### 3.3 Froude Number-Based Classification (Speed Regime)

The Froude Number is the **governing non-dimensional parameter** for classifying a vessel by its hydrodynamic support mechanism.

$$Fn = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

| **Froude Number Range** | **Speed Regime** | **Support Mechanism** | **Example Vessel** |
|------------------------|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| $Fn < 0.4$ | Low Speed | Pure Buoyancy (Hull-borne) | Cargo ships, Tankers |
| $0.4 < Fn < 0.6$ | Transitional | Mixed buoyancy and dynamic lift | Patrol boats |
| $Fn > 0.6$ | High Speed | Dynamic lift (planing) | Speedboats, Racing yachts |
| $Fn > 1.0$ | Very High Speed | Hull clears water via foils | Hydrofoils, ACVs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **"Below 0.4 Floats, Above 0.6 Planes."** Anything in between is "suspicious" and is classified as semi-displacement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Body] --> B[Buoyancy Check]
    B --> C{rho_body vs rho_fluid}
    C -->|rho_b less than rho_f| D[FLOATING]
    C -->|rho_b equals rho_f| E[SUSPENDED NEUTRALLY BUOYANT]
    C -->|rho_b greater than rho_f| F[SUNKEN SUBMERGED]
    D --> G[Service Type]
    G --> G1[Merchant]
    G --> G2[Naval]
    G --> G3[Fishing]
    G --> G4[Recreational]
    D --> H[Speed Regime]
    H --> H1[Displacement Fn less than 0.4]
    H --> H2[Semi Displacement 0.4 to 0.6]
    H --> H3[Planing Fn greater than 0.6]
    D --> I[Hull Form]
    I --> I1[Monohull]
    I --> I2[Multihull Catamaran]
    I --> I3[Multihull Trimaran]
    D --> J[Classification Society]
    J --> J1[LR]
    J --> J2[ABS]
    J --> J3[DNV]
    J --> J4[BV]
```

### 4.2 Buoyancy Equilibrium Force Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Weight W down] -->|acts on| FB[Floating Body]
    FB -->|displaces| DV[Displaced Volume nabla]
    DV -->|generates| FB1[Buoyant Force F_B up]
    FB1 -->|equals| EQ[Static Equilibrium Condition]
    W --> EQ
    EQ --> CLS{Classification}
    CLS -->|F_B greater than W| FL1[Floats Higher]
    CLS -->|F_B equals W| FL2[Neutral Buoyancy]
    CLS -->|F_B less than W| FL3[Sinks]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Vessel Input] --> Step1[Step 1: Measure Density]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Compute Specific Gravity]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Apply Archimedes Check]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Determine Service Type]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Measure Operating Speed]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Compute Froude Number]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Assign Speed Regime Tag]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Verify with Classification Society Rules]
    Step8 --> End[Output: Classified Vessel Profile]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "specific gravity" and explain its role in classifying a floating body as floating, suspended, or sunken.*

**Model Answer:**
Specific gravity (SG) is the dimensionless ratio of the density of a body to the density of fresh water at 4 °C. It is the **primary classifier** of floating bodies:
- SG < 1 → Body floats (partially submerged)
- SG = 1 → Body is neutrally buoyant (suspended)
- SG > 1 → Body sinks (fully submerged)
The SG determines the submerged volume fraction according to the relation $\nabla / V = SG$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the Froude Number classification of marine vessels.*

**Model Answer:**
The Froude Number is defined as $Fn = V / \sqrt{gL}$, where $V$ is the vessel speed, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $L$ is the waterline length. It is used to classify vessels by their hydrodynamic support mechanism:
- **Fn < 0.4:** Displacement-type (buoyancy-supported) vessels such as tankers.
- **0.4 < Fn < 0.6:** Semi-displacement vessels (e.g., patrol craft).
- **Fn > 0.6:** Planing-type vessels (e.g., speedboats) supported by dynamic lift.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*(a)* A wooden block of dimensions 1.5 m × 1.0 m × 0.8 m and density 650 kg/m³ floats in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Determine:
(i) Whether the block floats or sinks. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The submerged depth of the block. **(4 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the speed-based classification of marine vessels using the Froude Number, with suitable examples and a labeled sketch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Total volume of the block:
$$V = 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 = 1.2 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Weight of the block:
$$W = 650 \times 1.2 \times 9.81 = 7651.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Specific gravity check:
$$SG = \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{650}{1025} = 0.634$$

Since SG < 1, the body **FLOATS**.

**Step 4:** Submerged volume:
$$\nabla = SG \times V = 0.634 \times 1.2 = 0.7608 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Submerged depth (assuming horizontal block with 1.5 m × 1.0 m base):
$$d = \frac{\nabla}{A_{base}} = \frac{0.7608}{1.5 \times 1.0} = 0.5072 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The block floats with a submerged depth of **0.5072 m**.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

The Froude Number $Fn = V / \sqrt{gL}$ is a dimensionless number comparing inertial and gravitational forces. It governs the speed regime classification:
- **Fn < 0.4 (Displacement):** The hull is fully water-borne. Resistance increases steeply with speed. Examples: oil tankers, bulk carriers.
- **0.4 < Fn < 0.6 (Semi-displacement):** The bow wave begins to lift the hull partially. Resistance is at its maximum. Examples: fast patrol boats, trawlers.
- **Fn > 0.6 (Planing):** Dynamic lift raises most of the hull above the waterline. Resistance drops. Examples: speedboats, racing yachts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often forget to **convert units** (e.g., knots to m/s) before computing the Froude Number. (Lose 2 marks)
> - Students confuse the **displaced volume (∇)** with **displacement (Δ)**. (Lose 2 marks)
> - Forgetting to state the **assumed orientation** of the floating block (which face is the base) leads to mark deductions. (Lose 1 mark)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*(a)* Define "Classification Society" and list the four major classification societies. Explain the role of classification in ensuring structural integrity of a vessel. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A spherical buoy of diameter 1.2 m and mass 350 kg is moored in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Determine:
(i) Whether it floats or sinks. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The fraction of the sphere submerged. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of marine vessels. The four major societies are:
1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** – United Kingdom
2. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** – USA
3. **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)** – Norway
4. **Bureau Veritas (BV)** – France

Their role includes verifying material quality, structural scantlings, welding procedures, and periodic survey compliance to ensure the vessel remains fit for its intended service throughout its operational life.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Volume of the sphere:
$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.6)^3 = 0.9048 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Weight of the buoy:
$$W = 350 \times 9.81 = 3433.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Buoyant force if fully submerged:
$$F_{B,max} = 1025 \times 0.9048 \times 9.81 = 9101.5 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_{B,max} > W$ (9101.5 N > 3433.5 N), the buoy **FLOATS**.

**Step 4:** Equilibrium displaced volume:
$$\nabla = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{3433.5}{1025 \times 9.81} = 0.3413 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Submerged fraction:
$$\frac{\nabla}{V} = \frac{0.3413}{0.9048} = 0.3772 \approx 37.72\%$$

**Result:** About **37.72%** of the sphere's volume is submerged.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in Part (b):**
> - Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ instead of $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ gives slightly different answers. (Lose 1 mark)
> - Forgetting the $\frac{4}{3}$ factor in the sphere volume formula. (Lose 2 marks)
> - Confusing **mass** (350 kg) with **weight** (3433.5 N) in Archimedes' calculation. (Lose 2 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ is the **foundation** of all classification problems.
- **Three Floating States:** Floating (SG < 1), Suspended (SG = 1), Sunken (SG > 1).
- **Froude Number is King:** $Fn = V / \sqrt{gL}$ governs speed-regime classification.
- **Speed Regime Rule:** $Fn < 0.4$ = Displacement; $0.4 \text{ to } 0.6$ = Semi-displacement; $Fn > 0.6$ = Planing.
- **Hull Forms:** Monohull, Catamaran (2 hulls), Trimaran (3 hulls).
- **Four Major Classification Societies:** LR, ABS, DNV, BV.
- **Coefficient Symbols:** $C_B$ (Block), $C_P$ (Prismatic), $C_W$ (Waterplane) — all dimensionless.
- **Stability is Next:** The Metacentric Height $GM = KB + BM - KG$ determines transverse stability post-classification.
- **Always state assumptions** (water density, body orientation, g value) to earn full marks in KTU exams.
- **Tonnage is NOT mass of steel:** Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) is payload; Lightweight is the empty ship.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cargo Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cargo Ship (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A *cargo ship* is a powered, sea-going vessel primarily designed and constructed for the transportation of commercial goods, commodities, raw materials, or containerized cargo between designated ports of loading and discharge. As per KTU Module 1 nomenclature, a cargo ship is classified as a *floating body* whose operational efficiency is governed by **buoyancy**, **stability**, **resistance**, and **deadweight capacity** — making it a fundamental study object in Naval Architecture and Ocean Engineering.

## 1.2 Historical Context & Engineering Relevance

The study of cargo ships forms the bedrock of commercial maritime engineering. From the wooden clippers of the 19th century to today's **Ultra Large Container Vessels (ULCVs)** exceeding **24,000 TEU**, the cargo ship has evolved as a complex optimization problem balancing:
- **Hydrodynamic efficiency** (hull form)
- **Structural strength** (steel scantlings)
- **Cargo productivity** (volume/weight utilization)
- **Regulatory compliance** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line Convention)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Warehouse"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Floating Warehouse Analogy**
> Imagine a cargo ship as a **multi-storey floating warehouse** that must simultaneously satisfy three masters:
> 1. **The Cargo Master** – demands maximum space and easy loading/discharge.
> 2. **The Sea Master** – demands stability, safety, and seaworthiness.
> 3. **The Profit Master** – demands low fuel consumption and high utilization.
>
> Just as a building needs a strong foundation, a cargo ship needs a properly designed **hull**, **propulsion system**, and **stability system** — all of which are governed by Archimedes' Principle: the buoyancy force equals the weight of displaced water.

## 1.4 Standard Maritime Metrics (Bold Highlight)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Length Overall | **LOA** | 100 m – 400 m |
| Length Between Perpendiculars | **LBP** | 95 m – 380 m |
| Beam (Breadth) | **B** | 15 m – 60 m |
| Depth (Moulded) | **D** | 8 m – 30 m |
| Design Draft | **T** | 5 m – 16 m |
| Service Speed | **Vs** | 12 – 25 knots |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The *Displacement* ($\Delta$) of a fully loaded cargo ship is often in the range of **50,000 tonnes to 400,000 tonnes** — equivalent to the weight of **35,000 to 280,000 average cars**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cargo Ships

Cargo ships are classified based on the **type of cargo carried**, **hull form**, and **operational profile**.

### A. By Cargo Type

1. **General Cargo Ships** – Carry packaged goods (boxes, bags, drums, pallets) in holds accessed by ship's gear (cranes/derricks).
2. **Bulk Carriers** – Transport dry bulk (coal, iron ore, grain, bauxite) in large unobstructed holds.
3. **Tankers** – Carry liquid cargo (crude oil, chemicals, LNG) in segregated tanks.
4. **Container Ships** – Carry standardized **Twenty-foot Equivalent Units (TEU)** in cellular guides.
5. **Roll-on/Roll-off (Ro-Ro)** – Vehicles drive on and off via ramps.
6. **Reefer Ships** – Refrigerated cargo (fruits, meat, pharmaceuticals).
7. **Multi-Purpose Vessels (MPV)** – Combined capability for project/heavy lift cargo.

### B. By Hull Form

- **Single Hull** (older designs, phased out for tankers post-MARPOL).
- **Double Hull** (mandatory for new tankers under **MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19**).
- **Catamaran / SWATH** (specialized high-speed cargo).

## 2.2 Principal Particulars of a Cargo Ship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Particulars** are the fundamental geometric and operational parameters that completely define a ship for design and regulatory purposes. They are the first entries on a **General Arrangement (GA) drawing**.

### 2.2.1 Linear Dimensions

| Dimension | Description | Measurement Location |
|---|---|---|
| **LOA** | Length Overall | Forwardmost to aftermost point (including bulbous bow) |
| **LBP** ($L_{pp}$) | Length Between Perpendiculars | Forward perpendicular (FP) to aft perpendicular (AP) |
| **B** | Moulded Breadth | Maximum breadth at the **design waterline (DWL)** |
| **D** | Moulded Depth | Keel to the **main deck** at the side, measured at midship |
| **T** | Design Draft | Keel to the **design waterline** at midship |
| **T_A, T_F** | Drafts at AP and FP | For trim calculation |

### 2.2.2 Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

The **Block Coefficient** is the single most important hull form coefficient in cargo ship design. It represents the *fineness* of the hull.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$$

where:
- $\nabla$ = Volumetric displacement (m³)
- $L_{pp} \times B \times T$ = Volume of the rectangular block enveloping the underwater hull

**Typical values:**

| Ship Type | $C_B$ Range |
|---|---|
| Ultra Large Tanker (VLCC) | 0.82 – 0.85 |
| Bulk Carrier | 0.78 – 0.83 |
| Container Ship (slow) | 0.65 – 0.72 |
| Container Ship (fast) | 0.55 – 0.62 |
| General Cargo | 0.62 – 0.72 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** A higher $C_B$ means a *fuller* hull → more cargo capacity but higher resistance at speed. A lower $C_B$ means a *finer* hull → less capacity but better speed and fuel economy. Cargo ship design is fundamentally a **compromise** between capacity and resistance.

### 2.2.3 Other Form Coefficients

- **Prismatic Coefficient:** $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \times L_{pp}}$ (relates displacement to midship area)
- **Midship Coefficient:** $C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \times T}$ (fullness of the cross-section)
- **Waterplane Coefficient:** $C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{pp} \times B}$ (fullness of the waterplane)

These are linked by: $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

## 2.3 Tonnage — The Three Critical Definitions

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Tonnage** (volume measure) with **Weight** (mass measure). Tonnage has **three** distinct meanings, each used for different purposes.

### A. Gross Tonnage (GT)

A dimensionless, regulatory measure of the *total internal volume* of a ship, used for:
- Port dues
- Safety regulations (SOLAS)
- Classification society records

$$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$

where $V$ = total volume of all enclosed spaces (m³) and $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$ (per IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention).

### B. Net Tonnage (NT)

A measure of the *revenue-earning* volume only (cargo + passenger spaces, excluding machinery, crew, ballast).

$$NT = K_2 \cdot V_{c} \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$$

where $V_c$ = cargo volume, $d$ = moulded draft, $D$ = moulded depth, $K_2$ = constant.

### C. Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** is the actual *weight* (in metric tonnes) that a ship can carry — cargo + fuel + freshwater + crew + stores + provisions. It is **NOT a regulatory measure** but a *commercial* parameter.

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

where:
- $\Delta_{loaded}$ = displacement when fully loaded
- $\Delta_{lightship}$ = displacement of the empty ship (hull + machinery only)

### D. Displacement ($\Delta$)

The actual **weight of water displaced** by the ship. For a ship floating in equilibrium:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

In marine practice, using **salt water density** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, displacement in **metric tonnes** equals the volume in m³ × 1.025 (since 1 tonne = 1000 kg, $g$ ≈ 9.81 m/s² gives 1.025 t/m³ for seawater).

$$\Delta \text{ (tonnes)} = 1.025 \times \nabla \text{ (m}^3\text{)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} B T}$ | dimensionless | Hull form analysis |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M L_{pp}}$ | dimensionless | Hull form analysis |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B T}$ | dimensionless | Section design |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{pp} B$ | dimensionless | Stability & added mass |
| Displacement (mass) | $\Delta = \rho \nabla$ | tonnes | Buoyancy equilibrium |
| Displacement (volume) | $\nabla = C_B L_{pp} B T$ | m³ | Hull volume |
| DWT | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$ | tonnes | Cargo capacity |
| Trim | $Trim = T_A - T_F$ | m | Longitudinal inclination |
| Freeboard | $F = D - T$ | m | Reserve buoyancy |
| Deadweight Ratio | $DWR = \dfrac{DWT}{\Delta_{loaded}}$ | dimensionless | Design efficiency |
| Lightweight Ratio | $LWR = \dfrac{\Delta_{lightship}}{\Delta_{loaded}}$ | dimensionless | Steel weight efficiency |

> **Note:** The "$\vert$" for absolute value is replaced with `\vert` in LaTeX; in this table the dimensional column uses $L_{pp} B T$ (no absolute value needed).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull form coefficients** are used in **CFD resistance prediction** before model testing.
- **DWT** determines port slot allocation, charter rates, and ECA compliance.
- **Freeboard** directly impacts **SOLAS Load Line Convention (1966)** compliance — see Section 2.6.

## 2.6 Load Line Convention (Plimsoll Mark)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Plimsoll Line** is a marking on the ship's side that indicates the **maximum legal draft** under various water and weather conditions. Established by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966**, it prevents overloading and ensures reserve buoyancy.

### Standard Load Line Markings (Read from top to bottom on a fresh-water summer scale):

| Mark | Abbreviation | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| TF | Tropical Fresh Water | Tropical zone, fresh water |
| F | Fresh Water | Temperate zone, fresh water |
| T | Tropical | Tropical zone, salt water |
| S | Summer | Temperate zone, salt water (S = baseline) |
| W | Winter | Winter conditions, salt water |
| WNA | Winter North Atlantic | Severe winter N. Atlantic |

**Freeboard Formula (Simplified, Summer Salt Water):**

$$F_{min} = K \cdot \frac{DWT^{1/3}}{100}$$

For a typical bulk carrier, **F** ranges from **1.5 m to 4 m** depending on size.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship between $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$

Starting from the geometric definition of the **Block Coefficient**:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

The volumetric displacement can also be expressed using the **Midship Section Area** $A_M$ integrated over the length. By the geometric mean (Riemann sum for constant prismatic shape):

$$\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot A_M \cdot C_P$$

where the **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P$ accounts for the *tapering* of the hull from the midship to the ends.

Substituting into the Block Coefficient formula:

$$C_B = \frac{L_{pp} \cdot A_M \cdot C_P}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Cancelling $L_{pp}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$C_B = \frac{A_M \cdot C_P}{B \cdot T}$$

Now, the **Midship Coefficient** is defined as:

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

Therefore:

$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

**Significance (KTU):** This identity is the foundation of *Hull Form Optimization*. Designers first choose $C_P$ (based on speed-length ratio) and $C_M$ (based on structural needs), and $C_B$ follows automatically. For example, a high-speed container ship might have $C_P = 0.7$, $C_M = 0.93$, giving $C_B = 0.651$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DWT to Displacement Ratio (DWR)

By definition:

$$\Delta_{loaded} = \Delta_{lightship} + DWT$$

The **Deadweight Ratio (DWR)** is:

$$DWR = \frac{DWT}{\Delta_{loaded}}$$

Substituting the identity above:

$$DWR = \frac{\Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}}{\Delta_{loaded}}$$

$$DWR = 1 - \frac{\Delta_{lightship}}{\Delta_{loaded}}$$

$$DWR = 1 - LWR$$

where $LWR$ is the **Lightweight Ratio**.

**Engineering implication:** A higher DWR means a *more efficient* cargo ship (more revenue per tonne of steel). Modern container ships achieve DWR ≈ 0.65–0.75.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Buoyancy-Displacement Equilibrium (Archimedes for Cargo Ships)

For a floating cargo ship in still water, the fundamental equilibrium condition is:

$$W_{total} = F_B$$

where:
- $W_{total}$ = Total weight of the ship (lightweight + deadweight)
- $F_B$ = Buoyancy force = weight of displaced water

Expressing in tonnes (using salt water density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³):

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot \nabla \text{ (tonnes)}$$

Combining with the Block Coefficient definition $\nabla = C_B L_{pp} B T$:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \text{ (tonnes)}$$

**Worked Example (KTU 2024 Sample Calculation):**

> A bulk carrier has the following principal particulars:
> - $L_{pp} = 200$ m
> - $B = 32$ m
> - $T = 12$ m
> - $C_B = 0.80$
>
> **Find:** (a) The volumetric displacement, (b) The mass displacement in salt water, (c) The deadweight if the lightweight is 8,000 tonnes.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric Displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \times 76{,}800$$

$$\nabla = 61{,}440 \text{ m}^3$$

**(b) Mass Displacement in Salt Water:**

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 61{,}440$$

$$\Delta = 62{,}976 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(c) Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

$$DWT = 62{,}976 - 8{,}000$$

$$DWT = 54{,}976 \text{ tonnes}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Trim Calculation (Longitudinal Inclination)

When cargo is unevenly distributed, the ship *trims* (rotates about its center of flotation).

$$\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F$$

The **Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm (MCTC)** is a key stability parameter:

$$MCTC = \frac{\Delta \cdot \overline{GM}_L}{100 \cdot L_{pp}} \text{ (tonne-m/cm)}$$

where $\overline{GM}_L$ is the longitudinal metacentric height. The **trim moment** required to achieve a trim $\Delta T$ is:

$$\text{Trim Moment} = MCTC \times 100 \times \Delta T \text{ (tonne-m)}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cargo Type vs. Hull Parameter

| Ship Type | Typical $C_B$ | Typical $L_{pp}/B$ | Typical $B/T$ | DWR | Primary Cargo |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| VLCC Tanker | 0.83 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.4 – 2.8 | 0.80 – 0.85 | Crude oil |
| Capesize Bulk | 0.82 | 5.0 – 6.0 | 2.6 – 3.0 | 0.78 – 0.85 | Iron ore, coal |
| Panamax Container | 0.65 | 6.5 – 7.5 | 2.4 – 2.8 | 0.55 – 0.65 | TEU containers |
| Reefer | 0.60 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.2 – 2.5 | 0.45 – 0.55 | Frozen goods |
| General Cargo | 0.70 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.3 – 2.7 | 0.60 – 0.70 | Mixed palletized |
