# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) of a cargo ship. How is it different from Gross Tonnage?

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** It is the total weight (in metric tonnes) that a ship can carry when fully loaded, including cargo, fuel oil, lubricating oil, freshwater, crew, provisions, and stores.

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

**Difference from Gross Tonnage (GT):**
- **DWT** is a *weight* measure (in tonnes) reflecting actual carrying capacity.
- **GT** is a *volumetric* measure (dimensionless) reflecting the total enclosed internal volume of the ship, used for regulatory purposes (port dues, SOLAS, classification).

(1 mark for DWT definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for difference with GT)

---

### Question 2: What is Block Coefficient? Mention its typical range for a bulk carrier and a fast container ship.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** It is the ratio of the volumetric displacement of a ship to the product of its length between perpendiculars, breadth, and draft.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$$

It is a measure of the *fineness* of the hull. A higher $C_B$ indicates a fuller hull (more cargo capacity, higher resistance), while a lower $C_B$ indicates a finer hull (better speed, lower resistance).

**Typical values:**
- **Bulk Carrier:** $C_B = 0.78$ to $0.83$
- **Fast Container Ship:** $C_B = 0.55$ to $0.62$

(1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for bulk carrier range, 1 mark for container ship range)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** A Panamax container ship has the following principal particulars:
- $L_{pp} = 245$ m, $B = 32.2$ m, $T = 12.0$ m, $D = 19.0$ m
- $C_B = 0.66$
- Lightship weight = 18,000 tonnes
- Salt water density = 1.025 t/m³

**Calculate:**
(i) Volumetric displacement ($\nabla$)
(ii) Loaded displacement ($\Delta$)
(iii) Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)
(iv) Deadweight Ratio (DWR)
(v) Freeboard (F)

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) Volumetric Displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_B \times L_{pp} \times B \times T$$

$$\nabla = 0.66 \times 245 \times 32.2 \times 12.0$$

$$\nabla = 0.66 \times 94{,}668$$

$$\nabla = 62{,}480.88 \text{ m}^3 \approx 62{,}481 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(ii) Loaded Displacement in Salt Water:**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \times \nabla = 1.025 \times 62{,}480.88$$

$$\Delta = 64{,}042.9 \text{ tonnes} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

$$DWT = 64{,}042.9 - 18{,}000$$

$$DWT = 46{,}042.9 \text{ tonnes} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Deadweight Ratio:**

$$DWR = \frac{DWT}{\Delta_{loaded}} = \frac{46{,}042.9}{64{,}042.9}$$

$$DWR = 0.719 \approx 71.9\% \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(v) Freeboard:**

$$F = D - T = 19.0 - 12.0$$

$$F = 7.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** With neat sketches, classify cargo ships based on the type of cargo they carry. Explain the role of Block Coefficient in determining the operational profile of a cargo ship.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Classification of Cargo Ships (3 marks):**

**1. General Cargo Ships:** Carry break-bulk cargo (bagged, palletized, drummed) in single- or multi-deck holds. Equipped with onboard cranes/derricks. Typical DWT: 5,000 – 25,000 t.

**2. Bulk Carriers:** Transport dry bulk commodities (coal, iron ore, grain) in large unobstructed holds. Classed as Capesize, Panamax, Handymax. Typical DWT: 30,000 – 400,000 t.

**3. Container Ships:** Carry standardized TEU/FEU containers in cellular guides. ULCVs now exceed 24,000 TEU. Typical $C_B$ = 0.55 – 0.72.

**4. Tankers:** Carry liquid cargo in segregated tanks.
- *Crude carriers* (VLCC, ULCC) – up to 550,000 DWT
- *Product tankers* – refined products
- *Chemical tankers* – IMO Type 1/2/3 coatings
- *LNG/LPG carriers* – cryogenic or pressurized

**5. Ro-Ro Ships:** Vehicles (cars, trucks, Mafi trailers) drive on/off via stern or side ramps.

**6. Reefer Ships:** Insulated holds with refrigeration for perishable cargo.

**Role of Block Coefficient (4 marks):**

The Block Coefficient $C_B$ is the most fundamental *hull form parameter* in cargo ship design and directly determines the operational profile:

- **High $C_B$ (0.80 – 0.85):** Indicates a *full* hull. Used for slow, heavy-lift vessels (tankers, bulk carriers). Maximizes cargo capacity at the expense of speed. Lower propulsion power required.

- **Low $C_B$ (0.55 – 0.65):** Indicates a *fine* hull. Used for fast container ships, naval auxiliaries. Minimizes wave-making resistance for higher service speeds. Requires more powerful engines.

- **Medium $C_B$ (0.65 – 0.75):** General cargo ships, medium-speed container ships. Balanced compromise.

**Decision Logic:** For a given required DWT and service speed, the designer selects $C_B$ using empirical formulas such as the **Ayre's formula** or **Schneekluth's curves**, then derives the principal dimensions.

$$L_{pp} = \frac{DWT}{C_B \times B \times T \times \rho}$$

(A 1-mark sketch of a typical cargo ship GA showing the principal particulars is expected.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of Plimsoll Line and its significance in cargo ship design. List the standard load line marks used in the International Convention on Load Lines (1966) with their meaning.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The Plimsoll Line (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Plimsoll Mark** (named after British politician Samuel Plimsoll) is a standardized marking on the hull of a cargo ship, placed amidships on both sides. It indicates the **maximum safe draft** of the vessel under various water density and climatic conditions.

The mark consists of:
- A **horizontal line** through a circle, indicating the **Summer Load Line (S)** — the baseline for temperate, salt-water conditions.
- A **deck line** above it showing the upper limit of the freeboard deck.
- **Additional lines** above and below indicating zones for different conditions.

**Significance:**
- **Prevents overloading** → ensures adequate **reserve buoyancy**.
- Provides **standardization** → international maritime safety.
- Accounts for **water density variations** (fresh vs. salt water) and **seasonal severity** (winter storms, tropical heat).
- Legally enforced under the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966**, administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

**Standard Load Line Marks (4 marks):**

| Mark | Meaning | Position |
|---|---|---|
| **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water | Highest line |
| **F** | Fresh Water | Above S |
| **T** | Tropical (Salt Water) | Above S |
| **S** | Summer (Salt Water) | Reference (Baseline) |
| **W** | Winter (Salt Water) | Below S |
| **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic | Lowest line (severe conditions) |

Additionally, **timber load lines** (LTF, LT, LS, LW) apply when carrying timber deck cargo.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse the **positions** of TF, F, T, S, W, WNA. Remember: **TF > F > T > S > W > WNA** from top to bottom.
> - Loading above the appropriate line is a **criminal offense** under maritime law.
> - Forgetting the **deck line** reference (1 mark loss).

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A general cargo ship has principal particulars: $L_{pp} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 13$ m, $C_B = 0.70$, Midship Coefficient $C_M = 0.94$. Calculate:
(i) Volumetric displacement
(ii) Prismatic Coefficient
(iii) Draft at design displacement, given freeboard F = 3 m
(iv) If the ship enters fresh water ($\rho = 1.000$ t/m³), what is the new draft (assuming weight remains constant)?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Volumetric Displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_B \times L_{pp} \times B \times T$$

We need draft first. Since $F = D - T$:
$$T = D - F = 13 - 3 = 10 \text{ m}$$

$$\nabla = 0.70 \times 150 \times 22 \times 10 = 23{,}100 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient:**

Using the identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.70}{0.94} = 0.7447 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) Draft at Design Displacement:**

$$T = 10 \text{ m (as computed above)} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) New Draft in Fresh Water:**

In salt water: $\Delta = 1.025 \times 23{,}100 = 23{,}677.5$ tonnes

In fresh water, for the same weight:
$$T_{fw} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{fw} \times C_B \times L_{pp} \times B}$$

But since weight is constant:
$$\rho_{sw} \times C_B \times L_{pp} \times B \times T_{sw} = \rho_{fw} \times C_B \times L_{pp} \times B \times T_{fw}$$

$$T_{fw} = T_{sw} \times \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} = 10 \times \frac{1.025}{1.000}$$

$$T_{fw} = 10.25 \text{ m} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Note:** Draft increases by 2.5% when entering fresh water — this is why the **F** mark on the Plimsoll line is *above* the S mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cargo Ship Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GT/NT (volume) with DWT (weight)** – This is the #1 mistake. GT and NT are dimensionless, DWT is in tonnes.
> 2. **Using 1000 kg/m³ (fresh water) instead of 1025 kg/m³ (salt water)** for displacement calculations. Always check the question.
> 3. **Forgetting the constant 1.025 in $\Delta = 1.025 \times \nabla$** – You lose 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Mixing up $L_{pp}$ and LOA** – Always use $L_{pp}$ in $C_B$ and resistance formulas. LOA includes the bulbous bow and stern overhang.
> 5. **Forgetting units** – $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$ are dimensionless; $\nabla$ in m³; $\Delta$ in tonnes.
> 6. **Plimsoll line order** – TF, F, T, S, W, WNA from top to bottom. Drawing them in wrong order = 0 marks.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch** – A clear General Arrangement (GA) sketch of a cargo ship with principal particulars labelled is worth **at least 1.5 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Core Definitions to Memorize
- **$C_B$ (Block Coefficient):** $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} B T}$ — Hull fullness indicator
- **$C_P$ (Prismatic Coefficient):** $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M L_{pp}}$ — Longitudinal tapering
- **$C_M$ (Midship Coefficient):** $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B T}$ — Cross-section fullness
- **$C_{WP}$ (Waterplane Coefficient):** $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{pp} B}$ — Waterplane fullness
- **Identity:** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ (must remember!)
- **DWT:** $\Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$ (carrying capacity in tonnes)
- **GT:** Volume-based regulatory measure (dimensionless)
- **DWR:** $\dfrac{DWT}{\Delta_{loaded}}$ = $1 - LWR$ (design efficiency metric)
- **Freeboard $F$:** $D - T$ (reserve buoyancy indicator)
- **Displacement:** $\Delta = 1.025 \times \nabla$ (in tonnes, salt water)

### ✅ Key Numerical Values (Memorize for MCQs)
- Sea water density = **1.025 t/m³** (or 1025 kg/m³)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Typical bulk carrier $C_B \approx 0.80$
- Typical container ship $C_B \approx 0.60$ – $0.65$
- Typical tanker $C_B \approx 0.83$
- Panamax beam ≈ 32.2 m
- Capesize beam > 32.2 m

### ✅ Standard Load Line Order (Top to Bottom)
**TF → F → T → S → W → WNA**

### ✅ Cargo Ship Classification (Mnemonic: "GB-CTRS-L")
**G**eneral, **B**ulk, **C**ontainer, **T**anker, **R**o-Ro, **S**pecialized → **L**iquid

### ✅ Important Conventions
- **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea)
- **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution)
- **ICLL 1966** (Load Line Convention)
- **IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention** (for GT, NT calculation)

### ✅ Typical $C_B$ Quick-Reference Table

| $C_B$ Range | Ship Type |
|---|---|
| 0.55 – 0.65 | Fast container, naval auxiliary |
| 0.65 – 0.72 | Container ship (medium speed), general cargo |
| 0.72 – 0.80 | Slow container, small bulk |
| 0.80 – 0.85 | Tanker, bulk carrier, ore carrier |

### ✅ Formula to Always Carry in Your Head
$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}}$$

### ✅ KTU 2024 Likely Question Pattern
- **3-mark Part A:** Direct definition (DWT, GT, $C_B$, Plimsoll line) — ~60% probability
- **7-mark sub-part:** Numerical on $C_B$ / $\nabla$ / $\Delta$ / DWT — ~70% probability
- **7-mark sub-part:** Sketch + classification OR Plimsoll line — ~50% probability

> **End of Module 1 – Cargo Ships Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Cargo Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Cargo Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — General Cargo Ships

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> **General Cargo Ship**: A multi-deck, single-island, mechanically propelled seagoing vessel designed primarily for the transportation of **break-bulk, packaged, palletized, unitized, and small-containerized general cargo** between established liner-trade ports. It is a multi-purpose tramp/liner vessel equipped with cargo gear (derricks/cranes), hatch covers, and internal compartmentation to carry heterogeneous cargoes in the same voyage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** General Cargo Ships form the baseline reference vessel type in Module 1 because they encompass almost every traditional naval-architecture design feature (single island, tweendecks, hatch-and-gear arrangement, side/bottom/strength longl structures) that students will compare against bulk carriers, tankers, and container ships later in the course.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **floating multi-storey warehouse on wheels** — that is exactly what a general cargo ship is.

- Each floor is a **deck** (tweendeck or main deck).
- The roof of each floor is a **hatch cover** that can be lifted.
- A **crane/derrick** stands on the roof to lift goods in and out.
- The whole building floats and **drives itself** across oceans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key distinguishing trait:** Unlike a bulk carrier (loose grain/coal/ore) or a tanker (liquid), a general cargo ship carries *countable, packaged* items — boxes, crates, drums, bags, machinery, vehicles. Because of this, the ship must have a **box-shaped hull** with maximum internal volume, multiple decks, and strong deck reinforcement to take **point loads** (e.g., a single 30-ton machine).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used for General Cargo Ships

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** | **5,000 – 25,000 tonnes** |
| **Service Speed** | **14 – 18 knots** |
| **Number of Holds** | **3 – 5** |
| **Tweendecks** | **1 or 2** |
| **Cargo Gear (SWL)** | **5 – 30 tonnes per derrick** |
| **Block Coefficient (C_B)** | **0.60 – 0.72** |
| **Length-to-Breadth Ratio (L/B)** | **6.0 – 7.5** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A general cargo ship is engineered around **five interlocking design pillars**. Each pillar is the answer to one specific engineering question:

#### Pillar 1 — Maximize Internal Volume
- **Why?** Cargo is *not* liquid (no tank filling); it is *not* loose (no hold filling like ore). It is **packaged**, so wasted volume = lost revenue.
- **How?** Designers use a **box-shaped hull** with high freeboard, full-length superstructure is avoided (replaced by a **single aft/forward island**), and the hull form has a **prismatic coefficient (C_P) ≈ 0.65–0.72**.

#### Pillar 2 — Provide Point-Load Strength
- **Why?** A 30-tonne turbine or a stack of steel coils concentrates weight on a few square metres.
- **How?** The double bottom and tank top are reinforced; **pillarless holds** are avoided in machinery-heavy holds; **deck sockets** are welded at standard positions for **shoring** (vertical wooden/steel props) and **stools**.

#### Pillar 3 — Allow Rapid Loading / Discharging
- **Why?** Port time costs money (≈ $20,000–$80,000/day for a 15,000 DWT ship).
- **How?** **Hatch covers** (steel pontoon, rolling, or folding) open quickly; ships carry their own **derricks or cranes** (king posts, samson posts, or deck cranes) so they don't depend on shore infrastructure.

#### Pillar 4 — Compartmentalize for Safety
- **Why?** General cargo includes *hazardous, dry, perishable, and wet* items in one voyage.
- **How?** The hull is divided by **transverse bulkheads** (every ~ 1/6 of L) and an **inner bottom**. **Tweendecks** are removable horizontal platforms.

#### Pillar 5 — Maintain Safe Stability with Variable Loading
- **Why?** Each voyage has different cargo density, so the ship's **KG** changes voyage-to-voyage.
- **How?** **Ballast tanks** (double bottom, side, forepeak, afterpeak) are provided to maintain **GM > 0.15 m** at all times.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `\vert x\vert`) inside any markdown cell to prevent table-breaking. Below, equations are written as LaTeX in dedicated math blocks.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deadweight (DWT)** | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{light}$ | **tonnes** (1 tonne = 1000 kg) |
| 2 | **Displacement** | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | $\rho$ = 1.025 t/m³ (sea) |
| 3 | **Block Coefficient** | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless, **0.60–0.72** |
| 4 | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | $A_M$ = midship area |
| 5 | **Midship Coefficient** | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | **≈ 0.90 – 0.98** for boxy cargo hull |
| 6 | **Stowage Factor (SF)** | $SF = \dfrac{\text{Cargo Volume (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Cargo Mass (tonnes)}}$ | m³/t — **light cargo = high SF** |
| 7 | **Cargo Deadweight** | $DWT_{cargo} = \dfrac{\text{Hold Volume}}{SF}$ | tonnes |
| 8 | **Load Density** | $\rho_{load} = \dfrac{1}{SF}$ | t/m³ |
| 9 | **GM (Free Surface)** | $GM_{eff} = GM - \dfrac{\sum (i \cdot \rho \cdot \nu)}{V}$ | $i$ = FS moment of inertia |
| 10 | **Hatchway Area Ratio** | $HAR = \dfrac{A_{hatch}}{A_{hold}} \times 100\%$ | typically **40% – 60%** |
| 11 | **Number of Holds (Rule of Thumb)** | $n_{holds} \approx \dfrac{L}{6 \cdot B}$ | provides transverse bulkhead spacing |
| 12 | **Derrick SWL Check** | $SWL \geq 1.25 \times M_{max}$ | with **1.25 safety factor** |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architect:** Uses these formulas in **preliminary design** to size the ship for an owner's trade route.
- **Marine Surveyor:** Uses **Stowage Factor** to verify that the requested cargo physically fits inside the hold without overstressing the deck.
- **Port Engineer:** Uses **Hatchway Area Ratio** to decide whether shore cranes can reach every corner of the hold.
- **Stability Officer:** Uses **GM correction for free surface** because liquid in slack tanks during a general-cargo voyage can dangerously reduce stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cargo Deadweight from Hold Volume

**Problem:** A general cargo ship has **3 holds**, each of length **18 m**, breadth **16 m**, and height **8 m** (excluding tweendeck). The ship will carry bagged sugar with a **Stowage Factor SF = 1.4 m³/t**. Find the **maximum cargo deadweight**.

**Step 1 — Compute hold volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{hold} &= L_{hold} \times B_{hold} \times H_{hold} \\
&= 18 \times 16 \times 8 \\
&= 2304 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the geometric internal volume of one hold.

**Step 2 — Total hold volume for 3 holds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= 3 \times V_{hold} \\
&= 3 \times 2304 \\
&= 6912 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply stowage factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT_{cargo} &= \frac{V_{total}}{SF} \\
&= \frac{6912}{1.4} \\
&= 4937.14 \; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **4937 tonnes** of bagged sugar can be loaded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Stowage Factor:** A low SF (e.g., 0.6 for steel) means **heavy, dense cargo** that *fills the ship by weight before volume* — this is called a **deadweight-limited** voyage. A high SF (e.g., 3.5 for light machinery) means **light, bulky cargo** that fills the ship by volume — a **volume-limited** voyage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block Coefficient from Principal Dimensions

**Problem:** A general cargo ship has L = 120 m, B = 18 m, T = 7.5 m. Its lightweight displacement is 2,800 t and loaded displacement is 14,500 t. The volumetric displacement is 11,415 m³. Find the block coefficient $C_B$.

**Step 1 — Write definition**

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cdot B \cdot T &= 120 \times 18 \times 7.5 \\
&= 16200 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert volumes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{11415}{16200} \\
&= 0.7046
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $C_B \approx \mathbf{0.705}$

This is **typical for a general cargo ship** — a moderately full hull.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hatchway Area Ratio

**Problem:** A single cargo hold is 18 m × 16 m in plan area. The rectangular hatch opening is 12 m × 9 m. Compute the **Hatchway Area Ratio (HAR)**.

**Step 1 — Compute hold plan area**

$$
A_{hold} = 18 \times 16 = 288 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute hatch area**

$$
A_{hatch} = 12 \times 9 = 108 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HAR &= \frac{108}{288} \times 100\% \\
&= 37.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 37.5% opening means **tweendeck beams and 'tween deck pillars** are needed to support deck plating on either side of the hatch. General cargo ships typically aim for **40%–60%** — designers often increase this by widening the hatch or reducing pillar count to ease loading.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Cargo Type on Ship Loading

| Cargo Type | Stowage Factor (m³/t) | Density (t/m³) | Loading Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel coils, plates | **0.30 – 0.60** | 3.33 – 1.67 | **Deadweight-limited** |
| Bagged sugar, cement | **1.20 – 1.50** | 0.83 – 0.67 | Balanced |
| Timber (deck load) | **2.00 – 3.00** | 0.50 – 0.33 | **Volume & stability-limited** |
| Empty containers | **3.50 – 5.00** | 0.29 – 0.20 | **Volume-limited** |
| Light machinery (crated) | **3.00 – 6.00** | 0.33 – 0.17 | **Volume-limited** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — General Cargo Ship Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP[GENERAL CARGO SHIP - SIDE VIEW]
        direction TB
        BW[Bow with bulbous bow] --> FP[Forepeak tank - ballast]
        FP --> H1[Hold 1 - tweendeck optional]
        H1 --> H2[Hold 2 - main cargo hold]
        H2 --> H3[Hold 3 - main cargo hold]
        H3 --> H4[Hold 4 - tweendeck optional]
        H4 --> AP[Afterpeak tank - ballast]
        AP --> ST[Rudder and propeller]
        MA[Engine room - below H3] -.->|drives| ST
        SU[Superstructure - aft island] --> BR[Bridge and accommodation]
        CR[Deck cranes / king posts] --> H1
        CR --> H2
        CR --> H3
        CR --> H4
        HC[Hatch covers - steel pontoon] --> H1
        HC --> H2
        HC --> H3
        HC --> H4
        DB[Double bottom tanks - ballast and fuel] --> MA
    end
    classDef tank fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef hold fill:#ffe7c2,stroke:#663300
    classDef gear fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#006600
    class BW,FP,AP,DB tank
    class H1,H2,H3,H4,MA hold
    class CR,HC,BR,ST gear
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cargo Loading Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Owner requests cargo manifest] --> B{Compute total volume and total mass}
    B --> C{Is V cargo greater than V hold}
    C -->|YES| D[VOLUME LIMITED voyage - reduce cargo mass]
    C -->|NO| E{Is M cargo greater than DWT}
    E -->|YES| F[DEADWEIGHT LIMITED voyage - top up with ballast]
    E -->|NO| G[BALANCED voyage - maximize both]
    D --> H[Verify GM stability with light cargo]
    F --> I[Adjust ballast to maintain GM greater than 0.15 m]
    G --> J[Proceed with loading plan]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hull Form Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN[General Cargo Ship]
        G1[Block coefficient 0.60 to 0.72]
        G2[Full midship section]
        G3[Moderate prismatic 0.65 to 0.72]
        G4[Boxy cross-section with tumblehome or vertical sides]
    end
    subgraph BULK[Bulk Carrier]
        B1[Block coefficient 0.78 to 0.85]
        B2[No tweendecks]
        B3[Large hatch ratio greater than 60 percent]
    end
    subgraph TANK[Tanker]
        T1[Block coefficient 0.80 to 0.85]
        T2[Double hull only]
        T3[Smooth prismatic lines]
    end
    GEN -->|Contrast| BULK
    GEN -->|Contrast| TANK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the block coefficient of a general cargo ship lower than that of a tanker?"* The answer is hidden in the cargo: tankers are volume-limited and benefit from a fuller hull; general cargo ships must have some slenderness for **speed** and **maneuverability in ports**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define a General Cargo Ship. Mention any four of its distinguishing design features.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A General Cargo Ship is a single-island, multi-deck vessel designed for transporting **break-bulk, packaged, palletized, and unitized cargo** between liner ports.

**Four distinguishing features (½ mark each):**

1. **Single-island superstructure** (aft or forward) — keeps amidships free for hatches and gear.
2. **Tweendecks** — horizontal platforms for cargo segregation.
3. **Onboard cargo gear** — derricks, king posts, or deck cranes.
4. **Pillar-supported hatch openings** — allow large hatchway area ratio for easy loading.

---

**Q2. What is Stowage Factor? Why is it important for a general cargo ship?**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Stowage Factor (SF) is the volume in cubic metres occupied by **one tonne** of cargo.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
SF = \frac{\text{Cargo Volume (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Cargo Mass (tonnes)}}
$$

**Importance (1 Mark):** It determines whether a voyage is **deadweight-limited** (heavy cargo like steel) or **volume-limited** (light cargo like machinery), and guides the loading plan to ensure the ship is neither overstressed nor under-loaded.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** Define the term **Block Coefficient (C_B)**. For a general cargo ship of L = 110 m, B = 17 m, T = 7 m, displacement volume ∇ = 8,955 m³, calculate the block coefficient. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** List and briefly explain any **five structural components** that distinguish a general cargo ship from a tanker. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):**

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

It is the ratio of the underwater displaced volume to the volume of a rectangular block having the ship's principal dimensions. It indicates hull fullness.

**Calculation (5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute denominator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cdot B \cdot T &= 110 \times 17 \times 7 \\
&= 13090 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{8955}{13090} \\
&= 0.684
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The value **0.684** falls within the typical general cargo range (0.60 – 0.72), confirming a moderately full hull.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| # | Component | Function (1.4 marks each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tweendecks** | Provide horizontal separation of cargo types and increase number of cargo compartments. |
| 2 | **Hatch covers (pontoon/folding)** | Provide weather-tight sealing of holds while permitting fast opening for cranes. |
| 3 | **Derricks / Deck cranes** | Enable self-loading/discharging without shore-side gear. |
| 4 | **King posts / Samson posts** | Vertical supports for derrick guys; allow heavy lifts up to 30 t SWL. |
| 5 | **Transverse bulkheads** | Compartmentalize holds, localize flooding, and stiffen the hull girder. |

*(Additional valid components: side tanks, double bottom, forepeak/afterpeak, deck sockets for shoring, bulbous bow.)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** A general cargo ship has **3 identical holds**. Each hold is 20 m long, 15 m broad, and 9 m deep. The ship is to load bagged rice with **SF = 1.6 m³/t**. Find the **maximum cargo deadweight** and the **hatch area ratio** if each hatch measures 13 m × 8 m. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the role of **ballast tanks** and **GM correction for free surface effect** in a general cargo ship. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Volume per hold**

$$
V_{hold} = 20 \times 15 \times 9 = 2700 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Total cargo volume**

$$
V_{total} = 3 \times 2700 = 8100 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Cargo deadweight**

$$
DWT_{cargo} = \frac{8100}{1.6} = 5062.5 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Hold plan area**

$$
A_{hold} = 20 \times 15 = 300 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Hatch area**

$$
A_{hatch} = 13 \times 8 = 104 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Hatchway Area Ratio**

$$
HAR = \frac{104}{300} \times 100\% = 34.67\%
$$

**Result:** Cargo = **5062.5 t**; HAR = **34.67%** (low — more pillars needed; opening should be enlarged in design).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Ballast Tanks (3.5 Marks):**
- Located in **double bottom, forepeak, afterpeak, and side tanks (wing tanks)**.
- Used during **ballast voyage** to maintain safe draft, propeller immersion, and **GM > 0.15 m**.
- Capacity typically **25% – 35% of DWT**.

**Free Surface Effect on GM (3.5 Marks):**

$$
GM_{eff} = GM - \frac{i \cdot \rho \cdot \nu}{V}
$$

- Slack tanks in a general cargo ship (especially when partly loaded with liquid cargo such as latex or chemicals) create a **free surface** that **reduces effective GM**.
- If $GM_{eff}$ becomes negative, the ship **capsizes**.
- **Mitigation:** Keep ballast tanks either **full** or **empty** — never **partially full** — to eliminate free surface.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in General Cargo Ship Questions:**
> 1. **Units confusion:** Stowage factor is in **m³/t**, NOT t/m³. Mixing these inverts your answer. Examiner deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Hatch ratio percentage vs. decimal:** Always multiply by 100 to express in %. Writing 0.375 instead of 37.5% costs **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.025 t/m³ saltwater density:** If the question asks for *mass* displacement from volume, students forget $\rho_{sw}$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Free surface correction sign:** $GM_{eff} = GM - (\text{FS moment}/\nabla)$. Many students add instead of subtract.
> 5. **Hatchway Area Ratio formula:** A common mistake is dividing hold length by hatch length without including breadth.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **General Cargo Ship = floating warehouse** carrying *packaged* heterogeneous goods.
- ✅ **Single aft/forward island** keeps amidships clear for cargo gear and hatches.
- ✅ **Tweendecks** subdivide the hold vertically and increase cargo mix options.
- ✅ **Onboard cargo gear (derricks, king posts, cranes)** ensures port independence.
- ✅ **Typical DWT:** 5,000–25,000 t; **Speed:** 14–18 knots; **C_B:** 0.60–0.72.
- ✅ **Stowage Factor (SF)** is the master key to load planning — it tells you whether the ship is **deadweight-limited** or **volume-limited**.
- ✅ **Hatchway Area Ratio** of 40%–60% is the design sweet spot; lower ratios force more pillars and slower loading.
- ✅ **Ballast management** is critical because partially filled liquid tanks cause **free surface loss of GM**.
- ✅ **Formula must-knows:**
  - $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{light}$
  - $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$
  - $SF = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{M_{cargo}}$
  - $DWT_{cargo} = \dfrac{V_{hold}}{SF}$
  - $HAR = \dfrac{A_{hatch}}{A_{hold}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **KTU favourite exam contrast:** General Cargo Ship vs. Bulk Carrier vs. Tanker — know the **C_B**, **hatch ratio**, and **gear difference** for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Carriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Carriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bulk Carrier** is a merchant vessel specifically designed and constructed to transport **unpackaged bulk cargo** in large quantities. The cargo is loaded directly into the ship's holds without the use of intermediate containers or packaging. The cargo may be dry bulk (such as grain, coal, iron ore, bauxite, cement, or fertilizer) or wet bulk (in specialized bulk tankers, though the term "bulk carrier" traditionally refers to dry bulk carriers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> A bulk carrier is a single-deck, multi-hatch vessel with cargo holds accessed by large hatches on the deck, designed to carry homogeneous dry bulk cargo, characterized by high block coefficients (typically **0.75 to 0.85**), large deadweight capacities ranging from **15,000 DWT** to over **400,000 DWT**, and reinforced hull structures to withstand the stresses of uneven loading and slamming forces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Warehouse"

Imagine a **giant floating warehouse on a river**. Picture a colossal open-top room with several large compartments (holds) into which you pour loose material — like pouring sand, rice, or coal into a giant pit. The ship has a flat bottom (like a barge) to maximize the volume of cargo it can carry, and large hatches (like trapdoors) on the top to allow pouring in the cargo. The walls of these compartments are extremely strong because the weight of the cargo pushes outward with tremendous force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Box:**
> Think of bulk carriers as the **"18-wheelers of the seas"** — they are the workhorses of global maritime trade, similar to how trucks transport unpackaged grains, gravel, and sand on land. The "holds" are like the trailer's container, but the material is loose rather than bagged.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025 \ t/m^3}$
> - **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000 \ t/m^3}$
> - **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
> - **Typical Block Coefficient of Bulk Carriers:** $C_B = \mathbf{0.78 \ to \ 0.85}$
> - **Typical Service Speed:** $V_s = \mathbf{12 \ to \ 15 \ knots}$

### 1.4 Historical Context and Industry Relevance

The modern bulk carrier emerged in the mid-20th century with the industrialization of post-war Japan and the mass shipment of iron ore and coal. The single most defining design change was the **advent of self-discharging gear** and **hatch-cover sealing systems** that allowed rapid port turnarounds. Today, bulk carriers account for approximately **21% of the world merchant fleet by tonnage**, making them the second-largest ship type after oil tankers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Bulk Carriers (Operational Logic Steps)

Bulk carriers are classified based on several criteria. The most important classification scheme used by KTU examiners is based on **size (DWT)**:

**Step 1 — By Deadweight Capacity (Most Common):**

- **Handysize:** **15,000 – 35,000 DWT.** Used for minor bulk cargoes, fitted with cargo cranes, can enter small ports.
- **Handymax / Supramax:** **35,000 – 60,000 DWT.** Slightly larger, often gearless (without onboard cranes), used for grains and minor ores.
- **Panamax:** **60,000 – 80,000 DWT.** Sized to fit through the original **Panama Canal** locks (before the 2016 expansion).
- **Capesize:** **100,000 – 200,000 DWT.** Too large for the Suez and Panama canals; must transit the Cape of Good Hope or Cape Horn. Primarily used for **iron ore** and **coal**.
- **VLOC (Very Large Ore Carrier):** **200,000 – 400,000+ DWT.** Specialized for long-haul iron ore transport, typically between Brazil/ Australia and China.

**Step 2 — By Hull Configuration:**

- **Single-deck bulk carriers:** Most common; one continuous weather deck.
- **Double-deck bulk carriers:** Used primarily for **cedar carriers (timber carriers)** to maximize cubic capacity for low-density cargo.
- **Combination carriers (OBO — Ore/Bulk/Oil):** Can alternate between dry bulk and liquid cargo. Rare today due to operational complexity.

**Step 3 — By Structural Generation:**

- **First generation:** Pre-1980, prone to catastrophic structural failure.
- **Second generation:** 1980–1990, improved hatch covers.
- **Third generation:** Post-1990, fully SOLAS Chapter XII compliant.

### 2.2 General Design Features

The "Why" and "How" behind each design feature:

- **Single-deck configuration:** Maximizes hold volume and simplifies hatch opening. **Why:** Bulk cargo does not need to be stowed in tiers.
- **Large hatches (typically 50–60% of ship breadth):** **Why:** Allows rapid loading/discharge using shore-based gantry cranes or ship's own gear.
- **Self-trimming holds:** Sloped hoppers at the bottom of the hold that allow cargo to spread evenly under gravity. **Why:** Prevents excessive shear forces and bending moments from cargo pile-up.
- **Double-bottom tanks:** **Why:** Provide additional structural strength (hogging/sagging resistance) and double as fuel oil or ballast tanks.
- **Wing ballast tanks (side tanks):** **Why:** Critical for stability when the ship is in ballast (empty) condition; they lower the center of gravity.
- **Reinforced transverse bulkheads:** **Why:** Prevents progressive flooding if the hull is breached, as per SOLAS Chapter XII.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the most frequently tested formulas for bulk carrier-related numerical problems in KTU examinations. Memorize the variables and their units.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement Volume | $\nabla = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_B$ | Volume of water displaced by the hull | $\text{m}^3$ |
| Displacement Mass | $\Delta = \nabla \times \rho$ | Mass of water displaced (Archimedes' Principle) | $\text{tonnes}$ |
| Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT = \Delta - LWT$ | Total cargo, fuel, freshwater, stores, crew | $\text{tonnes}$ |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \times B \times T}$ | Hull fullness ratio | dimensionless |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \times L_{pp}}$ | Longitudinal fullness | dimensionless |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_W = \dfrac{A_W}{L_{pp} \times B}$ | Waterplane area ratio | dimensionless |
| Coefficient Relation | $C_B \approx C_P \times C_W$ | Approximate form coefficient relation | dimensionless |
| Length–Beam Ratio | $L/B$ | Affects speed and stability | dimensionless |
| Beam–Draft Ratio | $B/T$ | Affects stability and resistance | dimensionless |
| Tons per Centimeter (TPC) | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W}{100}$ | Mass required to change draft by 1 cm | $\text{t/cm}$ |
| Loading Rate Constraint | $Load_{rate} \le \dfrac{SF_{allow}}{\gamma_{cargo}}$ | For safe loading to avoid structural failure | $\text{m/hr}$ |

**Where:**
- $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars
- $B$ = Molded breadth
- $T$ = Mean molded draft
- $C_B$ = Block coefficient
- $A_M$ = Midship section area
- $A_W$ = Waterplane area
- $LWT$ = Lightweight (empty ship mass)
- $\rho$ = Water density
- $SF_{allow}$ = Allowable shearing force
- $\gamma_{cargo}$ = Specific weight of cargo

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are used by **Naval Architects** during the **preliminary design stage** to:
1. Estimate the cargo-carrying capacity of a new vessel.
2. Perform **loading condition analysis** to ensure the ship does not exceed safe stress limits.
3. Conduct **stability assessments** for SOLAS compliance.
4. Optimize hull form for fuel efficiency (lower $C_B$ = better speed, higher $C_B$ = more capacity).

In shipyards, naval architects use these formulas daily to design bulk carriers that comply with **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** standards and **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Style):**
> 
> **Problem Statement:**
> A bulk carrier is floating at a draft of **T = 12.5 m** in seawater of density **ρ = 1.025 t/m³**. The principal particulars of the vessel are:
> - Length between perpendiculars: $L_{pp} = 185 \ \text{m}$
> - Molded breadth: $B = 30 \ \text{m}$
> - Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.80$
> - Lightweight (mass of empty ship): $LWT = 9{,}200 \ \text{tonnes}$
> - Fuel oil + Freshwater + Stores + Crew constant: $1{,}800 \ \text{tonnes}$
> 
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The displacement volume $\nabla$ in $\text{m}^3$ and the displacement mass $\Delta$ in tonnes.
> (b) The deadweight tonnage (DWT) at this loading condition.
> (c) The cargo deadweight (cargo capacity) available at this draft.
> (d) Verify whether the ship's block coefficient is consistent if $C_P = 0.72$ and $C_W = 0.92$, using the relation $C_B \approx C_P \times C_W$.

### 3.1 Sub-Part (a) — Derivation of Displacement Volume and Mass

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters from the problem statement.**

We are given:
- $L_{pp} = 185 \ \text{m}$ (length)
- $B = 30 \ \text{m}$ (breadth)
- $T = 12.5 \ \text{m}$ (draft)
- $C_B = 0.80$ (block coefficient)
- $\rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3$ (seawater density)
- $LWT = 9{,}200 \ \text{tonnes}$ (lightweight)

**Step 2: Apply the displacement volume formula.**

The displacement volume is the volume of the underwater hull, which equals the rectangular volume of the principal dimensions multiplied by the block coefficient. This is the core naval architecture relationship:

$$
\nabla = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_B
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values into the formula.**

We perform the multiplication step-by-step. First, multiply $L_{pp}$ and $B$:

$$
L_{pp} \times B = 185 \times 30 = 5{,}550 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Multiply the result by T (the draft).**

This gives the rectangular bounding volume of the underwater hull:

$$
L_{pp} \times B \times T = 5{,}550 \times 12.5 = 69{,}375 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by the block coefficient $C_B$.**

The block coefficient accounts for the fact that the hull is not a perfect rectangular box. It is a fullness ratio less than 1, so multiplying the rectangular volume by $C_B$ gives the actual displaced volume:

$$
\nabla = 69{,}375 \times 0.80
$$

**Step 6: Compute the final displacement volume.**

$$
\nabla = 55{,}500 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 7: Convert displacement volume to displacement mass using Archimedes' Principle.**

Archimedes' Principle states that the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid. Therefore, the displacement mass is the displaced volume multiplied by the water density:

$$
\Delta = \nabla \times \rho
$$

**Step 8: Substitute the values and compute.**

$$
\Delta = 55{,}500 \times 1.025
$$

Let us perform the multiplication carefully:
- $55{,}500 \times 1 = 55{,}500$
- $55{,}500 \times 0.025 = 1{,}387.5$
- Total: $55{,}500 + 1{,}387.5 = 56{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}$

$$
\boxed{\Delta = 56{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}}
$$

### 3.2 Sub-Part (b) — Derivation of Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)

**Step 1: Recall the definition of Deadweight Tonnage.**

The DWT is the total mass of cargo, fuel, freshwater, stores, crew, and any other variable loads that the ship can carry at a given draft. It is defined as:

$$
DWT = \Delta - LWT
$$

**Step 2: Understand the physical meaning.**

The displacement mass $\Delta$ includes the **lightweight** (mass of the empty ship, including hull, machinery, and outfit) plus all **deadweight** (variable loads). Therefore, DWT = total displacement − lightweight.

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

We have $\Delta = 56{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}$ and $LWT = 9{,}200 \ \text{tonnes}$:

$$
DWT = 56{,}887.5 - 9{,}200
$$

**Step 4: Perform the subtraction.**

$$
\boxed{DWT = 47{,}687.5 \ \text{tonnes}}
$$

### 3.3 Sub-Part (c) — Derivation of Cargo Deadweight

**Step 1: Recall the components of DWT.**

The total DWT includes the constant (fuel, water, stores, crew) and the cargo. Therefore:

$$
DWT = DWT_{cargo} + DWT_{constant}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for the cargo deadweight.**

$$
DWT_{cargo} = DWT - DWT_{constant}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

We are given that the constant (fuel oil + freshwater + stores + crew) = $1{,}800 \ \text{tonnes}$:

$$
DWT_{cargo} = 47{,}687.5 - 1{,}800
$$

**Step 4: Compute the final result.**

$$
\boxed{DWT_{cargo} = 45{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}}
$$

### 3.4 Sub-Part (d) — Verification of Block Coefficient Consistency

**Step 1: Recall the form coefficient relation.**

For a typical bulk carrier with a parallel middle body, the approximate form coefficient relationship is:

$$
C_B \approx C_P \times C_W
$$

**Step 2: Compute the product of $C_P$ and $C_W$.**

$$
C_P \times C_W = 0.72 \times 0.92
$$

Performing the multiplication:
- $0.72 \times 0.9 = 0.648$
- $0.72 \times 0.02 = 0.0144$
- Total: $0.648 + 0.0144 = 0.6624$

$$
C_P \times C_W = 0.6624
$$

**Step 3: Compare with the given $C_B$.**

The given $C_B = 0.80$, but the product $C_P \times C_W = 0.6624$.

**Step 4: Interpret the discrepancy.**

The values are **inconsistent**. This indicates that either:
- The hull does not have a parallel middle body (it has a fine or hollow entry/run), or
- One of the coefficients has been specified incorrectly in the problem data, or
- The approximate relation $C_B \approx C_P \times C_W$ does not hold for this specific hull form (the exact relation is $C_B = \dfrac{\int C_P(x) \, dx}{L_{pp}}$ where $C_P$ varies longitudinally).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix Summary:**

| **Sub-Part** | **Parameter Calculated** | **Final Value** |
|---|---|---|
| (a) | Displacement Volume $\nabla$ | $55{,}500 \ \text{m}^3$ |
| (a) | Displacement Mass $\Delta$ | $56{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}$ |
| (b) | Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) | $47{,}687.5 \ \text{tonnes}$ |
| (c) | Cargo Deadweight | $45{,}887.5 \ \text{tonnes}$ |
| (d) | Consistency Check | Inconsistent ($0.80 \neq 0.6624$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — General Arrangement of a Typical Bulk Carrier

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the principal structural components and arrangement of a typical Panamax bulk carrier. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TB
    SHIP[Bulk Carrier - General Arrangement]
    
    SHIP --> BOW[Bow Region]
    SHIP --> FCS[Forecastle Deck]
    SHIP --> H1[Hold No 1]
    SHIP --> H2[Hold No 2]
    SHIP --> H3[Hold No 3]
    SHIP --> H4[Hold No 4]
    SHIP --> H5[Hold No 5]
    SHIP --> ER[Engine Room]
    SHIP --> ACC[Aft Accommodation]
    SHIP --> STERN[Stern Region]
    
    BOW --> BST[Bow Ballast Tank]
    FCS --> FCH[Fwd Cargo Hatch Cover]
    
    H1 --> H1C[Hold 1 Cargo Space]
    H1 --> H1T[Tank Top]
    H1 --> H1D[Double Bottom Tank]
    H1 --> H1S[Side Ballast Wing Tank]
    
    H2 --> H2C[Hold 2 Cargo Space]
    H2 --> H2T[Tank Top]
    H2 --> H2D[Double Bottom Tank]
    
    H3 --> H3C[Hold 3 Cargo Space]
    H3 --> H3T[Tank Top]
    H3 --> H3D[Double Bottom Tank]
    
    H4 --> H4C[Hold 4 Cargo Space]
    H4 --> H4T[Tank Top]
    H4 --> H4D[Double Bottom Tank]
    
    H5 --> H5C[Hold 5 Cargo Space]
    H5 --> H5T[Tank Top]
    H5 --> H5D[Double Bottom Tank]
    
    ER --> ERM[Main Engine]
    ER --> ERG[Generator Room]
    ER --> ERS[Steering Gear]
    
    ACC --> ACC1[Crew Cabins]
    ACC --> ACC2[Navigation Bridge]
    ACC --> ACC3[Galley and Mess]
    
    STERN --> STF[Stern Frame]
    STERN --> RUD[Rudder]
    STERN --> SBT[Stern Ballast Tank]
    
    style SHIP fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H1 fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H4 fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H5 fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ER fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ACC fill:#5a4a7c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram shows the **functional architecture of bulk carrier loading, stability, and discharge systems**, mapping how cargo flows from port to ship and how supporting systems interact.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PORT[Port Facility] -->|Shore Cranes| HATCH[Hatch Covers Open]
    HATCH --> HOLDS[Cargo Holds 1 to 5]
    HOLDS -->|Stability Check| LOAD[Load Computer System]
    LOAD --> BALLAST[Ballast Water System]
    LOAD --> FUEL[Fuel and FW System]
    
    BALLAST -->|Pump Control| WINGBT[Side Ballast Tanks]
    BALLAST -->|Pump Control| DBBT[Double Bottom Tanks]
    
    FUEL --> ENGINE[Main Engine]
    ENGINE --> PROP[Propeller and Shaft]
    
    HOLDS --> TRIM[Trim and Stability Monitor]
    TRIM -->|Feedback| LOAD
    TRIM --> NAV[Bridge Navigation System]
    
    HOLDS -->|Discharge| CRANES[Ship Cranes or Shore Cranes]
    CRANES --> DEST[Destination Port]
    
    NAV -->|Route Data| PROP
    
    style PORT fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style HOLDS fill:#4a7c3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LOAD fill:#8b6a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ENGINE fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style NAV fill:#5a4a7c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insights from the Diagrams:**
> 
> 1. **Forecastle to Accommodation Span:** Modern bulk carriers place the engine room and accommodation at the **stern** (aft) to keep the cargo area uninterrupted. Older designs had midship engine rooms.
> 
> 2. **Double Bottom Continuity:** The double-bottom tanks run continuously under the cargo holds, providing structural strength against **hogging** (bow and stern sag) and **sagging** (mid-ship sag) bending moments.
> 
> 3. **Side Ballast Tanks (Wing Tanks):** Critical for ballasting during the empty voyage (return trip). Without these, the ship would be too light and propeller immersion would be insufficient.
> 
> 4. **Hatch-to-Hold Ratio:** The hatch opening is typically **50–60% of the ship's breadth** to allow rapid grab-based discharge using shore cranes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instructions:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be precise and within **30–50 words** unless a sketch is requested. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the explanation, and 1 mark for the example or application.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
Define the term **Bulk Carrier** and give two examples of cargo typically transported.

**Model Answer:**
A bulk carrier is a single-deck, multi-hatch merchant ship designed to transport unpackaged dry bulk cargo in its dedicated holds. Examples include **iron ore**, **coal**, **grain**, **bauxite**, **cement**, and **fertilizer**. The ship is optimized for high cargo density and rapid loading/discharge using large hatches. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
What is meant by **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** of a bulk carrier? Distinguish it from **Displacement**.

**Model Answer:**
DWT is the total variable load a ship can carry, including cargo, fuel, freshwater, stores, and crew. **Displacement** is the total mass of the ship including lightweight and all deadweight, equal to the volume of water displaced times water density. DWT = Displacement − Lightweight. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **one full question** out of the two choices provided. Sub-parts test **Apply / Analyze / Evaluate** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
Classify bulk carriers based on **deadweight tonnage (DWT)**. For each class, specify the typical size range and a representative trade route or cargo type. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| **Class** | **DWT Range** | **Typical Cargo / Trade** |
|---|---|---|
| Handysize | 15,000 – 35,000 t | Minor bulks, grains, fertilizers, small ports |
| Handymax / Supramax | 35,000 – 60,000 t | Grains, coal, minor ores, gearless |
| Panamax | 60,000 – 80,000 t | Grains, coal, iron ore, fits old Panama Canal |
| Capesize | 100,000 – 200,000 t | Iron ore (Brazil–China), coal (Australia–Japan) |
| VLOC | 200,000 – 400,000+ t | Long-haul iron ore (Vale, Rio Tinto dedicated trades) |

The classification aids in matching vessel size to port infrastructure, cargo volume, and route economics. Capesize vessels, for example, must transit the Cape of Good Hope or Cape Horn, increasing voyage time but reducing per-tonne transport cost. (7 marks — 3 for classification table, 2 for trade route explanation, 2 for economic/route significance)

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the **general arrangement** of a typical bulk carrier. Highlight the function of at least **five major structural components**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A typical bulk carrier consists of the following major components arranged longitudinally from bow to stern:

1. **Forecastle Deck:** A short raised deck at the bow that provides a safe working area and prevents green seas from entering the hatches.
2. **Cargo Holds (typically 5–7):** The main cargo-carrying spaces, accessed through large hatches on the upper deck. Equipped with hopper tanks and tank tops for self-trimming.
3. **Hatch Covers:** Watertight steel covers that seal the cargo holds. They can be sliding, pontoon, or roll-stowing types.
4. **Engine Room:** Located aft in modern designs, houses the main propulsion engine, generators, and auxiliary machinery.
5. **Aft Accommodation:** Crew quarters, bridge, galley, and mess rooms, located at the stern for better separation from cargo.
6. **Double Bottom Tanks:** Run continuously under the holds, providing structural strength (resistance to hogging/sagging) and serving as fuel or ballast tanks.
7. **Side Ballast Wing Tanks:** Critical for trimming and stability in ballast condition (empty voyage).

(7 marks — 2 marks for sketch, 5 marks for functional description of five components, distributed 1 mark each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — General Arrangement Sketch:**
> 
> Students frequently lose marks by:
> - Drawing a **side elevation of a generic ship** (like a tanker or container ship) instead of a bulk carrier (which has large hatches and is single-decked).
> - **Missing the hatch covers** or showing them as too small relative to the ship breadth.
> - **Forgetting the double bottom** in the cross-section.
> - Placing the **engine room midship** (an outdated practice) instead of at the stern.
> - Not labeling at least 5 components clearly.
> 
> **Examiner Tip:** Always draw a cross-section view showing the **hopper tanks**, **double bottom**, and **side ballast tanks**. This single addition can secure 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
Explain the **SOLAS Chapter XII regulations** applicable to bulk carriers. What structural and operational requirements are imposed on bulk carriers of length 150 m and above? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

SOLAS Chapter XII (Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers) was introduced in 1997 following a series of catastrophic bulk carrier losses in the 1980s and 1990s. The key requirements include:

1. **Structural Strength Requirements:**
   - All bulk carriers of 150 m and above must comply with **IACS Unified Requirements S21** and **S22**, which specify allowable bending moments and shearing forces under varied loading conditions.
   - The hull must be able to withstand **hogging** (when partially loaded) and **sagging** (when fully loaded) stresses with a 1.5x safety margin.

2. **Watertight Bulkheads and Double Skin:**
   - The number of transverse watertight bulkheads and their spacing are regulated.
   - Side shell plating in cargo hold regions must have sufficient thickness to resist **slamming** (impact of waves on the bow flare) — known as the "sideshell collapse" failure mode.

3. **Hold Loading and Unloading Manuals:**
   - Mandatory loading sequences must be documented in a **Cargo Hold Loading and Unloading Manual**.
   - Rates of loading/unloading must not exceed structural limits to avoid **over-stressing the hatch covers**.

4. **Hatch Cover Strength:**
   - Hatch covers must withstand **green sea loading** (waves washing over the deck) and the weight of cargo grab impacts.

5. **Stability Information:**
   - The ship must carry a **Loading Manual** and **Stability Booklet** for the Master to ensure safe loading.

(7 marks — 1.5 marks per regulation topic, with 0.5 mark reserved for examples/applications)

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
A bulk carrier is floating in seawater ($\rho = 1.025 \ t/m^3$) at a draft of **T = 14.2 m**. The principal particulars are:
- $L_{pp} = 220 \ m$
- $B = 32 \ m$
- $C_B = 0.82$
- Lightweight $LWT = 11{,}500 \ t$

**Calculate:**
(i) Displacement mass $\Delta$ in tonnes.
(ii) Deadweight tonnage (DWT).
(iii) If the cargo is iron ore with a stowage factor of **0.45 m³/t**, and the total cargo hold volume is **52,000 m³**, determine the maximum cargo deadweight that can be carried by volume. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Displacement Mass:**

The displacement volume is:
$$
\nabla = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_B
$$
$$
\nabla = 220 \times 32 \times 14.2 \times 0.82
$$

Step-by-step:
- $220 \times 32 = 7{,}040$
- $7{,}040 \times 14.2 = 99{,}968$
- $99{,}968 \times 0.82 = 81{,}973.76 \ m^3$

The displacement mass:
$$
\Delta = \nabla \times \rho = 81{,}973.76 \times 1.025
$$
$$
\Delta = 84{,}023.104 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**(ii) Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$
DWT = \Delta - LWT = 84{,}023.104 - 11{,}500 = 72{,}523.104 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Cargo Deadweight by Volume:**

The stowage factor (SF) is the volume occupied by 1 tonne of cargo. Therefore, 1 tonne of iron ore occupies $0.45 \ m^3$. The maximum cargo mass that can fit in the holds is:
$$
DWT_{cargo, vol} = \dfrac{\text{Hold Volume}}{SF} = \dfrac{52{,}000}{0.45}
$$
$$
DWT_{cargo, vol} = 115{,}555.56 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

However, the ship can only carry up to the available deadweight (72,523.104 t). Therefore, the **governing (limiting) factor is the deadweight**, not the volume. The ship will be **fully loaded by weight** before the volume limit is reached.

(7 marks — 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with the critical comparison between weight and volume limits)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical / Cargo Calculation:**
> 
> - Students often **forget to multiply by density** when calculating displacement mass (confusing $\nabla$ with $\Delta$).
> - In volume-limited problems, students frequently assume the **volume governs** without checking against the weight limit. **Always identify the governing constraint** (weight vs. volume) — this is the key KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation criterion.
> - Watch for the units of **stowage factor**: $m^3/t$ (cubic meters per tonne).
> - Always express the final answer in **tonnes**, not in $m^3$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Bulk Carriers:**

- [x] **Definition:** Single-deck, multi-hatch vessel for unpackaged dry bulk cargo.
- [x] **Classification by DWT:** Handysize (15–35k), Handymax (35–60k), Panamax (60–80k), Capesize (100–200k), VLOC (200k+).
- [x] **Key Cargoes:** Iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite, cement, fertilizer.
- [x] **General Arrangement:** Bow → Forecastle → Cargo Holds (with hatches) → Engine Room (aft) → Accommodation (aft) → Stern.
- [x] **Hatch-to-Breadth Ratio:** 50–60% of ship breadth.
- [x] **Block Coefficient Range:** $C_B = 0.75 \ to \ 0.85$ (high fullness).
- [x] **Core Formula:** $\Delta = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_B \times \rho$.
- [x] **DWT Formula:** $DWT = \Delta - LWT$.
- [x] **SOLAS Chapter XII:** Mandatory for bulk carriers ≥ 150 m, covering structural strength, hatch covers, and loading manuals.
- [x] **Form Coefficient Relation:** $C_B \approx C_P \times C_W$ (approximate, valid for parallel middle body hulls).
- [x] **Double Bottom:** Provides both structural strength (hogging/sagging) and tankage (fuel/ballast).
- [x] **Side Ballast Tanks:** Essential for stability in ballast condition.
- [x] **Self-Trimming Holds:** Hopper-shaped tank tops allow cargo to flow and level by gravity.
- [x] **Cargo Density Check:** Always check whether weight or volume is the limiting factor (DWT vs. hold volume).
- [x] **Density of Seawater:** Always use $\rho = 1.025 \ t/m^3$ unless freshwater is specified.
- [x] **Stowage Factor:** Volume per unit mass of cargo ($m^3/t$) — inverse of cargo density.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic: Bulk Carriers. Proceed to Module 1 — Sub-Topic: General Cargo Ships and Container Ships for the next chapter in the syllabus.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Container Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Container Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: CONTAINER SHIPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> A **Container Ship** is a specialized cargo vessel engineered with cellular deck structures (cell guides) to load, stow, and transport standardized intermodal containers (TEU/FEU) safely across oceans. It is a high-block-coefficient, high-density floating body where the primary cargo is modular, stackable, and unitized.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant floating multi-storey parking garage**, but instead of cars, every "parking slot" is a steel box of fixed width (8 ft) and height (8.5 ft or 9.5 ft). Now, picture stacking these boxes 20–24 levels high, both on deck and inside the hull. The ship is essentially a **floating warehouse with vertical "bookshelves"** running from bow to stern. Each shelf is precisely spaced so that cranes can drop containers in from above without the ship shifting them around like a traditional cargo hold.

- **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = the standard "ruler" used to count containers. One 20-ft container = **1 TEU**; one 40-ft container = **2 TEU**.
- **FEU (Forty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = 1 FEU = 2 TEU.
- A modern Ultra Large Container Vessel (ULCV) can carry **20,000+ TEU** — equivalent to stacking containers taller than a 10-storey building.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Container Ships Dominate Global Trade:**
> Approximately **80% of global trade by volume** moves in containers. They revolutionized shipping by standardizing cargo handling — the same box that rolls off a truck in Shanghai can ride a train to Chicago, then a ship to Rotterdam, with no repacking.

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard container width:** **8 ft (2.438 m)**
- **Standard container height:** **8 ft 6 in (2.591 m)** — called "standard" or "high cube" is **9 ft 6 in (2.896 m)**
- **TEU length:** **20 ft (6.058 m)**
- **FEU length:** **40 ft (12.192 m)**
- **Maximum TEU capacity (current record):** **~24,000 TEU** (class record-holders as of 2024)
- **Service speed of modern container ships:** **20–25 knots** (UCLVs run slower, ~16–18 knots, to save fuel)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A container ship must balance **three competing engineering demands** that traditional cargo ships do not face so acutely:

1. **Maximum Cargo Density (Stacking Efficiency):**
   - The hull is a long, narrow box with a high block coefficient ($C_B$).
   - Cell guides (vertical steel columns) form a grid that holds containers upright.
   - Stack weights on deck can reach **150–200 tonnes per stack**, creating enormous localized loads.

2. **Stability & Trim Optimization:**
   - Heavy deck cargo raises the **vertical center of gravity (KG)**, reducing initial metacentric height (**GM**).
   - The ship must maintain **GM ≥ 0.15 m** even at full load (SOLAS requirement).
   - Trim must be controlled within ± 0.5% of LBP to avoid excessive hogging/sagging.

3. **Longitudinal Hull Girder Strength:**
   - The hull behaves as a **box girder** spanning from bow to stern.
   - Wave-induced and cargo-induced bending moments can be very large.
   - Design must ensure still water bending moment (**SWBM**) + wave bending moment (**WBM**) does not exceed allowable stress.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam-essential. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols & Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³), $L$ = LBP (m), $B$ = beam (m), $T$ = draft (m) | Container ships: $C_B \approx 0.60$–$0.68$ (lower than tankers) |
| 2 | **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P$ | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | $A_M$ = midship area (m²) | Indicates fullness of underwater form |
| 3 | **Waterplane Coefficient** $C_WP$ | $C_WP = \dfrac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | $A_W$ = waterplane area (m²) | High $C_WP$ → large deck area for containers |
| 4 | **TEU to Volume Conversion** | $V_{\text{TEU}} \approx 33 \text{ m}^3$ (20-ft standard) | — | Used for stowage planning |
| 5 | **Initial GM (transverse stability)** | $GM = KM - KG$ | $KM = KB + BM$, $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | $I_T$ = transverse 2nd moment of waterplane area |
| 6 | **Transverse BM (waterplane method)** | $BM_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot \nabla}$ | For rectangular waterplane approximation | Exact $I_T$ from offset table preferred |
| 7 | **Longitudinal BM (hull girder)** | $BM_L = \dfrac{B \cdot L^3}{12 \cdot \nabla}$ | For rectangular approximation | Container ships have **very large $BM_L$** |
| 8 | **Trim equation** | $\text{Trim} = \dfrac{MCT \cdot \Delta T}{LBP \cdot 100}$ per cm | $MCT$ = Moment to Change Trim (t·m/cm) | $\Delta T = T_{aft} - T_{fore}$ |
| 9 | **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM)** | $SWBM = w \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{8}$ (uniform load approx.) | $w$ = weight per unit length (t/m) | Hogging (+) or Sagging (-) |
| 10 | **Wave Bending Moment (WBM) — IACS Rule** | $WBM = f \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | $f$ = wave coefficient (from IACS S11) | For design verification |
| 11 | **Shear Force at midship (uniform load)** | $SF = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2}$ | — | Maximum occurs at ends |
| 12 | **Stack weight (max)** | $W_{\text{stack}} = n \cdot W_{\text{TEU}}$ | $n$ = tiers, $W_{\text{TEU}} \approx 24 \text{ t}$ (loaded) | Local strength check |
| 13 | **Lashing force (rule of thumb)** | $F_{\text{lash}} = m \cdot a$ | $m$ = stack mass, $a$ = design acceleration (g's) | IMO CSS Code Annex 13 |
| 14 | **Deadweight vs Displacement** | $\Delta = DWT + LWT$ | $LWT$ = lightweight (t) | $\Delta$ in tonnes (salt water: 1 t = 1 m³) |
| 15 | **TPC (Tonnes per cm immersion)** | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W}{100}$ (salt water) | $A_W$ in m² | Draft change per cm load change |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, the absolute value / division bars in formulas use standard math rendering. When writing inline in your exam, use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your own notes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architects** use the block coefficient and prismatic coefficient to size the hull form for a given TEU capacity.
- **Marine Surveyors (IACS members: LR, DNV, BV, ABS)** apply the WBM and SWBM formulas during class approval to certify hull girder strength.
- **Port Operators** use TEU throughput statistics to size berths, quay cranes (STS — Ship-to-Shore), and yard equipment.
- **Ship Operators (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM)** use trim optimization formulas to minimize fuel consumption by **5–10% per voyage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Block Coefficient $C_B$ and Its Physical Meaning

The **block coefficient** is the single most important hull form parameter for a container ship. It tells us "how boxy" the underwater hull is.

**Starting Concept:**
The displaced volume of a ship $\nabla$ equals the weight of water it pushes aside (Archimedes' principle).

$$
\Delta_{mt} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

For salt water, $\rho_{sw} \approx 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ (often approximated as 1.025).

**Definition Step:**
A rectangular box of length $L$, beam $B$, and draft $T$ would have volume:
$$
\nabla_{\text{box}} = L \cdot B \cdot T
$$

The block coefficient is defined as the ratio of the actual displaced volume to this bounding box:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{\nabla_{\text{box}}} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Algebraic Manipulation (dimensionless check):**
- Numerator: m³
- Denominator: m × m × m = m³
- Therefore $C_B$ is **dimensionless**, as expected.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $C_B = 1.0$ → perfectly rectangular box (a barge)
- $C_B = 0.60$ → typical container ship (efficient for moderate speed, large deck area)
- $C_B = 0.85$ → typical tanker (very full hull, slow speed)

### 3.2 Derivation: Longitudinal Bending Moment (Hull Girder Analogy)

The hull of a container ship behaves like a **simply supported beam** floating between two "supports" — the bow and stern wave systems. The cargo and buoyancy act as distributed loads.

**Step 1: Establish the Load Distribution**
Assume a uniformly distributed cargo load $w$ (tonnes per metre) along the length. Buoyancy also acts approximately uniformly (for a block coefficient case).

**Step 2: Shear Force Equation**
For a simply supported beam of length $L$ with UDL $w$:

$$
SF(x) = \frac{wL}{2} - w \cdot x
$$

At the ends ($x = 0$ and $x = L$), the shear force is maximum:
$$
SF_{\max} = \pm \frac{wL}{2}
$$

At the midship ($x = L/2$), the shear force is zero.

**Step 3: Bending Moment Equation**
Integrating the shear force:

$$
BM(x) = \int_0^x SF(x') \, dx' = \frac{wL \cdot x}{2} - \frac{w \cdot x^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Moment (at Midship)**
Setting $x = L/2$:

$$
BM_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{w}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2
$$

$$
BM_{\max} = \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8}
$$

$$
BM_{\max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The classic formula $BM = wL^2 / 8$ is for a uniformly loaded simply supported beam. For the actual hull girder, classification societies (IACS S11) use more complex load envelopes derived from wave spectra. For KTU exams, this $wL^2/8$ form is sufficient at the introductory level.**

**Step 5: Physical Meaning — Hogging vs Sagging**

- **Hogging (+):** Bow and stern are supported by wave crests, midship is in a wave trough. The hull bends "convex up" like a hog's back.
- **Sagging (-):** Bow and stern are in wave troughs, midship is on a wave crest. The hull bends "convex down."

For a container ship:
- **At full load** → buoyancy concentrated at ends (squat) → tends to **sag**.
- **At ballast (partial load)** → heavy ballast at ends, light in middle → tends to **hog**.

### 3.3 Derivation: TEU Capacity Estimation

**Given:**
- Length between perpendiculars $L = 300$ m
- Beam $B = 40$ m
- Number of container rows across beam: 14 (typical for 40-m beam)
- Number of bays along length: 35 (each bay = 2 TEU = 6.058 m)
- Number of tiers (stacks) below deck: 8
- Number of tiers on deck: 6

**Step 1: Rows × Bays × Tiers (Below Deck)**
$$
N_{\text{below}} = 14 \text{ rows} \times 35 \text{ bays} \times 8 \text{ tiers} = 3920 \text{ TEU}
$$

Wait — for 14 rows of 40-ft containers stacked width-wise, this needs adjustment. A 40-m beam fits **17 rows of 20-ft containers (TEU)** or **8 rows of 40-ft containers (FEU)** plus 1 TEU. But for simplicity, let us count **TEU-equivalent slots**.

Let us use the bay-row-tier method directly:

**Step 1 (revised):**
Each bay = 2 TEU slots lengthwise. Each row = 1 TEU wide. Each tier = 1 TEU high.

**Step 2: Total slots (TEU) below deck:**
$$
N_{\text{below}} = (\text{rows}) \times (\text{bays}) \times (\text{tiers}) = 17 \times 35 \times 8 = 4760 \text{ TEU}
$$

**Step 3: Total slots on deck:**
$$
N_{\text{deck}} = 17 \times 35 \times 6 = 3570 \text{ TEU}
$$

**Step 4: Total nominal capacity:**
$$
N_{\text{total}} = 4760 + 3570 = 8330 \text{ TEU}
$$

**Step 5: Account for non-uniform slot distribution:**
Real ships have hatch covers, engine room, bow thruster tunnel, and accommodation block that reduce slots. Apply a **stowage factor** of ~0.85:

$$
N_{\text{real}} = 8330 \times 0.85 \approx 7080 \text{ TEU}
$$

This matches a typical "Panamax" size container ship (~5,000–8,000 TEU).

### 3.4 Stability Check — Free Surface Effect

Container ships often carry ballast water in partially filled tanks. This creates a **free surface effect** that reduces GM.

**Formula:**
$$
GG'_{\text{FSE}} = \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot i}{\Delta}
$$

Where:
- $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free surface (m⁴)
- $\Delta$ = ship displacement (t)
- $\rho_{liquid}$ = density of liquid (t/m³)

**Corrected GM:**
$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - GG'_{\text{FSE}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **IACS / SOLAS Requirement:** $GM_{\text{eff}} \geq 0.15$ m at all operational conditions. Loss of GM due to free surface is the **#1 cause of container ship capsize incidents** (e.g., MSC Napoli, 2007).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safety protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and all labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside quotes.

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Container Ship Systems

```mermaid

    nodeF["DOUBLE BOTTOM TANKS"]

    nodeK["BAY ROW TIER SLOT GRID"]
```
