
### 4.2 Hull Girder Loading Modes — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    mode1["LOADING CONDITION FULL LOAD"]
    mode2["LOADING CONDITION BALLAST"]
    mode3["LOADING CONDITION PARTIAL"]

    eff1["BUOYANCY ENDS HIGH MIDDLE LOW"]
    eff2["BUOYANCY UNIFORM BUT ENDS BALLLASTED"]
    eff3["MIXED BUOYANCY DISTRIBUTION"]

    res1["RESULT SAGGING MOMENT NEGATIVE"]
    res2["RESULT HOGGING MOMENT POSITIVE"]
    res3["RESULT COMPLEX BIAXIAL STRESS"]

    cnt1["MAX STRESS AT DECK PLATING MIDSHIP"]
    cnt2["MAX STRESS AT BOTTOM PLATING MIDSHIP"]
    cnt3["STRESS VARIES ALONG HULL GIRDER"]

    mode1 --> eff1 --> res1 --> cnt1
    mode2 --> eff2 --> res2 --> cnt2
    mode3 --> eff3 --> res3 --> cnt3
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Container Loading Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["STEP 1 BAY PLAN PREPARATION BY CHIEF OFFICER"]
    step2["STEP 2 STABILITY AND TRIM CHECK"]
    step3["STEP 3 LASHING GEAR INSPECTION"]
    step4["STEP 4 CRANE OPERATOR ENGAGES TWISTLOCKS"]
    step5["STEP 5 CONTAINER LIFTED FROM YARD"]
    step6["STEP 6 CONTAINER LOWERED INTO CELL GUIDE"]
    step7["STEP 7 TWISTLOCKS ENGAGED ON CORNERS"]
    step8["STEP 8 STOWAGE CONFIRMED IN BAPLIE FILE"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Physical Drawings (in exam answer sheets):** Draw a side elevation showing the bow, stern, hatch covers, cell guides, double bottom, and accommodation block. Label: LBP, B, T, and indicate hogging/sagging with curved arrows. This single drawing can earn **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
**Define TEU and FEU. Why is the TEU used as the standard measurement for container ship capacity?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit):** A unit of measurement based on a standard 20-ft long × 8-ft wide × 8.5-ft (or 9.5-ft high-cube) intermodal container. **(1 mark)**
> - **FEU (Forty-foot Equivalent Unit):** A 40-ft container, equivalent to **2 TEU**. **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Why TEU is standard:**
>   - Container ships carry mixed lengths (20-ft, 40-ft, 45-ft, 53-ft).
>   - TEU provides a **common denominator** to express total slot capacity regardless of mix.
>   - It allows **simple comparison** between ships, ports, and trade routes.
>   - It is used in **port throughput statistics, charter party agreements, and stowage planning**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List the four main structural features that distinguish a container ship hull from a conventional general cargo ship hull.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Cell guides** — vertical steel columns forming a grid for container stowage. **(0.75 mark)**
> 2. **Large hatch covers** spanning almost the full beam (no longitudinal subdivision into holds). **(0.75 mark)**
> 3. **High freeboard and high block coefficient** hull form to maximize deck cargo. **(0.75 mark)**
> 4. **Double bottom tanks** used for ballast and fuel to maintain trim. **(0.75 mark)**

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> For all 14-mark questions on Container Ships, students **commonly lose marks** at the following points:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in final answers (e.g., writing $BM = 5000$ without specifying t·m). → Lose **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing hogging and sagging** — always state which condition and why (buoyancy distribution).
> 3. **Skipping the "assumption" step** in derivations — examiners want to see you state "assume uniform load" or "assume rectangular waterplane" before applying formulas.
> 4. **Not mentioning the SOLAS / IACS rule** when discussing stability (always cite the 0.15 m GM minimum).
> 5. **Failing to draw a side profile diagram** — the KTU marking scheme typically awards **2–3 marks** for a properly labelled sketch.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Load Stability & Trim**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, S1 Model Paper]**

A container ship has the following particulars at full load:
- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 280$ m
- Beam: $B = 32.2$ m
- Design draft: $T = 12.5$ m
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.65$
- Vertical center of gravity: $KG = 14.2$ m
- Lightweight displacement: $\Delta_{LWT} = 28{,}000$ t
- Cargo to be loaded: 6,000 TEU at an average weight of 14 t per TEU
- Bunker and stores: 4,500 t
- Fresh water: 200 t

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the displacement, draft, and block coefficient check at full load.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** The transverse metacentric radius is calculated as $BM_T = 4.8$ m. Calculate the initial GM. Does the ship satisfy the SOLAS stability criterion ($GM \geq 0.15$ m)? If free surface effect reduces GM by 0.35 m, what is the effective GM?

---

> **Complete Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Displacement, Draft, and Block Coefficient Check**

**Step 1: Calculate total deadweight (DWT)**

$$
DWT = W_{cargo} + W_{bunker} + W_{FW}
$$

$$
W_{cargo} = 6000 \text{ TEU} \times 14 \text{ t/TEU} = 84{,}000 \text{ t}
$$

$$
DWT = 84{,}000 + 4{,}500 + 200 = 88{,}700 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 2: Total displacement at full load**

$$
\Delta = \Delta_{LWT} + DWT = 28{,}000 + 88{,}700 = 116{,}700 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3: Convert displacement to displaced volume (salt water, $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$)**

$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{116{,}700}{1.025} = 113{,}854 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Calculate actual block coefficient**

$$
C_{B,\text{actual}} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{113{,}854}{280 \times 32.2 \times 12.5}
$$

$$
C_{B,\text{actual}} = \frac{113{,}854}{112{,}700} = 1.0103
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anomaly Alert:** The actual $C_B$ comes out > 1.0, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates a discrepancy in the problem data. In a KTU exam, the student should **flag this clearly** in the answer:
>
> *"Since $C_B$ cannot exceed 1.0 (it represents a ratio of displaced volume to a bounding box), the given lightweight and cargo figures are inconsistent with the hull dimensions. For exam purposes, we proceed using the design $C_B = 0.65$ and back-calculate the draft."*

**Step 4 (corrected): Back-calculate draft using design $C_B$**

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = \frac{\nabla}{C_B \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{113{,}854}{0.65 \times 280 \times 32.2}
$$

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = \frac{113{,}854}{5{,}860.4} = 19.43 \text{ m}
$$

> This draft (19.43 m) is deeper than the design draft (12.5 m) — again confirming the data inconsistency. **Always show your work and state assumptions.** **(Marks awarded: 7)**

---

**Part (b) — GM Calculation and SOLAS Check**

**Step 1: Calculate KM (metacenter above keel)**

$$
KB = \frac{T}{2} \text{ (approximate)} = \frac{19.43}{2} = 9.715 \text{ m}
$$

$$
KM = KB + BM_T = 9.715 + 4.8 = 14.515 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate initial GM**

$$
GM = KM - KG = 14.515 - 14.2 = 0.315 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: SOLAS compliance check**

$$
GM = 0.315 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad GM_{min} = 0.15 \text{ m}
$$

Since $0.315 > 0.15$, the ship **satisfies** the basic SOLAS criterion. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Apply free surface effect**

$$
GM_{eff} = GM - GG'_{FSE} = 0.315 - 0.35 = -0.035 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Finding:** $GM_{eff}$ is **negative**, meaning the ship is in an **unstable condition** and would capsize. The chief officer must take corrective action:
> - Transfer ballast to reduce free surface.
> - Re-stow heavy cargo lower in the holds.
> - Consider deballasting partially filled tanks.

For exam purposes, the conclusion should state: *"The negative $GM_{eff}$ indicates an unstable condition; corrective action per the ship's Stability Information Booklet is required."* **(Marks awarded: 7)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Longitudinal Strength**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S2 Model Paper, Alternative Choice]**

A container ship of length $L = 250$ m carries a uniformly distributed cargo of total weight 50,000 t. Assume the ship behaves as a simply supported beam in still water.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the maximum still water bending moment (SWBM) and maximum shear force.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If a wave with wavelength $\lambda = L$ imposes a triangular distribution of buoyancy that varies linearly from $+20\%$ at midship to $-20\%$ at the ends, calculate the additional wave bending moment. Is the combined moment within the IACS rule of $0.05 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ for $C_B = 0.65$ and $B = 32$ m?

---

> **Complete Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation of SWBM and Shear Force**

**Step 1: Set up the load**
Uniform load per unit length:
$$
w = \frac{W_{total}}{L} = \frac{50{,}000}{250} = 200 \text{ t/m}
$$

**Step 2: Shear force (reactions at supports)**
For a simply supported beam with UDL, the support reactions are:
$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{200 \times 250}{2} = 25{,}000 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3: Shear force at any section x from the bow**
$$
SF(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 25{,}000 - 200x
$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear force (at supports)**
$$
SF_{max} = 25{,}000 \text{ t (at } x = 0 \text{ and } x = L\text{)}
$$

**Step 5: Bending moment at section x**
$$
BM(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w \cdot x^2}{2} = 25{,}000 \cdot x - 100x^2
$$

**Step 6: Maximum bending moment (at midship, $x = L/2 = 125$ m)**
$$
BM_{max} = 25{,}000 \times 125 - 100 \times 125^2
$$

$$
BM_{max} = 3{,}125{,}000 - 1{,}562{,}500 = 1{,}562{,}500 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Verification using $wL^2/8$:**
$$
BM_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{200 \times 250^2}{8} = \frac{12{,}500{,}000}{8} = 1{,}562{,}500 \text{ t·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(Marks awarded: 7)**

---

**Part (b) — Wave Bending Moment and IACS Check**

**Step 1: Buoyancy distribution (triangular, peak at midship)**
Assume a wave such that buoyancy is concentrated at midship and reduced at ends. Let buoyancy $b(x)$ vary as:
$$
b(x) = b_0 + b_1 \left(1 - \frac{4(x - L/2)^2}{L^2}\right)
$$

For a triangular variation from +20% at midship to −20% at ends:
- Net buoyancy = $w$ (must balance total weight)
- Excess buoyancy at midship: $+0.20w$ over half-length region
- Deficit at ends: $-0.20w$ over quarter-length regions

**Step 2: Simplified approach — Hogging/Sagging Equivalent**

The triangular buoyancy creates an additional bending moment. Treating it as a "load adjustment" $\Delta b(x)$ that is positive at midship and negative at ends:

**Net moment from wave distribution** (using $M = \int b(x) \cdot x \, dx$ relative to midship):

The peak-to-peak amplitude of the wave load (20% variation) over half the length gives an additional moment:

$$
WBM_{approx} = 0.10 \cdot w \cdot \frac{L^2}{6}
$$

(The factor 0.10 comes from averaging the 20% variation, and 1/6 comes from integrating the triangular distribution.)

Substituting $w = 200$ t/m and $L = 250$ m:

$$
WBM_{approx} = 0.10 \times 200 \times \frac{250^2}{6} = \frac{1{,}250{,}000}{6} = 208{,}333 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Step 3: Total bending moment**

$$
BM_{total} = SWBM + WBM = 1{,}562{,}500 + 208{,}333 = 1{,}770{,}833 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Step 4: IACS allowable check**

$$
BM_{allow} = 0.05 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B = 0.05 \times 250^2 \times 32 \times 0.65
$$

$$
BM_{allow} = 0.05 \times 62{,}500 \times 32 \times 0.65 = 0.05 \times 62{,}500 \times 20.8
$$

$$
BM_{allow} = 65{,}000 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Step 5: Compare**

$$
BM_{total} = 1{,}770{,}833 \text{ t·m} \gg BM_{allow} = 65{,}000 \text{ t·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The combined bending moment vastly exceeds the IACS rule value.** This means:
> - Either the IACS formula here is being misapplied (the 0.05 factor is an empirical approximation and not directly comparable to a deterministic load case), OR
> - The hull cross-section (section modulus) must be sufficient to resist this moment.
>
> In KTU exam answers, **always state the IACS comparison qualitatively** and note that the actual hull is designed with a section modulus to safely resist the combined moment per IACS S11 requirements. **(Marks awarded: 7)**

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ✅ **TEU = 20-ft container**; **FEU = 2 TEU**. Standard dimensions: **8 ft × 8.5 ft × 20/40 ft**.
- ✅ **Block coefficient** $C_B$ for container ships: **0.60 – 0.68** (lower than tankers for speed).
- ✅ **Cell guides** = vertical steel columns forming a grid; allow rapid loading without internal shifting.
- ✅ **Hatch covers** span almost full beam — a defining structural feature.
- ✅ **Hogging** = wave-supported ends, trough at midship (typical in ballast).
- ✅ **Sagging** = wave-supported midship, trough at ends (typical at full load).
- ✅ **Bay-Row-Tier** notation is the universal system to locate any container slot.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$
- ✅ $GM = KM - KG$, where $KM = KB + BM_T$
- ✅ $BM_T = L \cdot B^3 / (12 \cdot \nabla)$ (rectangular waterplane approximation)
- ✅ $BM_L = B \cdot L^3 / (12 \cdot \nabla)$ (very large for container ships)
- ✅ $SWBM = wL^2 / 8$ (uniform load, simply supported beam)
- ✅ $SF_{max} = wL / 2$ (at bow and stern)
- ✅ $TPC = A_W / 100$ (salt water, tonnes per cm)
- ✅ $\Delta = DWT + LWT$

### Critical Regulations (Examiner Loves These)
- ✅ **SOLAS GM minimum** = **0.15 m** at all operational conditions.
- ✅ **IACS S11** — Longitudinal Strength Rules for container ships (added post-1970s losses).
- ✅ **IMO CSS Code Annex 13** — Container Securing Systems (lashing force calculations).
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex VI** — Air pollution, drives slow steaming and LNG-fueled designs.

### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These in Exam)
- ❌ Forgetting units in final answers.
- ❌ Confusing hogging with sagging — always state the **buoyancy distribution** first.
- ❌ Not stating assumptions (uniform load, rectangular waterplane, etc.).
- ❌ Forgetting to draw a labelled side profile / cross-section diagram.
- ❌ Mixing up **DWT, LWT, and Δ** (displacement).
- ❌ Not citing SOLAS / IACS / IMO rules when discussing stability or strength.

### Numerical Quick-Pick
- A 20-ft container weighs ~24 t (loaded), ~2 t (empty).
- A 40-ft container weighs ~30 t (loaded), ~3.7 t (empty).
- 1 TEU ≈ 33 m³ (internal volume, standard dry container).
- 14,000 TEU ship ≈ 1 million tonnes of cargo capacity in a single voyage.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Container Ships**
> **Course: OENST611 — Fundamentals of Floating Bodies**

topics.title: Ro-Ro Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ro-Ro Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ro-Ro Ship (Roll-on/Roll-off Vessel):** A specialised merchant or passenger vessel in which **wheeled cargo** — such as automobiles, trucks, trailers, semi-trailers, railway wagons, or heavy machinery — is driven (rolled) on and off the ship across built-in **shore-based, ship-based, or floating ramps**, without the use of shipboard cranes or lifting gear. The cargo remains on its own wheels or on purpose-built cassettes/trailers throughout the voyage.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course *OENST611 – Fundamentals of Floating Bodies*, the study of Ro-Ro ships is important because they are **floating bodies with extreme stability and trim challenges** — the cargo itself is loaded dynamically, asymmetrically, and often across multiple internal decks (car decks) connected by ramps, requiring continuous monitoring of the ship's **centre of gravity (G), centre of buoyancy (B), metacentric height (GM), trim, and draught**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Ro-Ro vessels are treated as a floating-body problem with moving internal masses. As cargo rolls across the ship, the **longitudinal centre of gravity (LCG)** and **vertical centre of gravity (VCG/KG)** shift continuously, making them an excellent case-study for **stability, trim, and draught calculations** in Module 1.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey car park floating on water**. Instead of using a lift to move cars between floors, there are **gently sloped ramps** connecting each level — just like in a shopping mall parking lot. When the ship docks, the **driver simply steers the car on** at water level and drives it up to whichever deck it belongs on. At the destination port, the same process is reversed — the cars **roll off**.

Real-world analogy:
- A **Ro-Ro ferry** at Kochi or Beypore port is the closest local KTU-context example. Trucks and buses roll on from a shore ramp at the bow, are parked and lashed, and the same vehicles roll off at the next port — no cranes, no lifting.

### 1.3 Physical & Operational Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Typical service speed:** **18–25 knots** (RoPax) and **16–22 knots** (pure cargo Ro-Ro).
> - **Typical displacement:** **10,000 to 70,000+ tonnes** (modern Pure Car and Truck Carriers – PCTCs).
> - **Number of car decks:** **8 to 12 decks** in modern PCTCs (e.g., Höegh Target class, BYD Explorer No.1).
> - **Ramp gradient:** generally limited to **1:8 to 1:12** for safe vehicle transit.
> - **IMO Damage Stability Standard:** **SOLAS 2009 Damage Stability Regulations** apply to Ro-Ro passenger ferries.

### 1.4 Why Ro-Ro Ships Exist (Engineering Rationale)

- **Loading rate:** A Ro-Ro terminal can load/unload **1,000+ vehicles per hour** versus **40–80 vehicles/hour** for a lift-on/lift-off (Lo-Lo) crane terminal.
- **No special lifting gear required** → reduced port infrastructure cost.
- **Vehicles drive themselves on/off** → no need to remove packaging or chassis.
- **Damage to cargo is minimised** → no swinging loads above the ship.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Ro-Ro Ship

**Step 1 — Approach and Ramp Engagement**
The vessel approaches the berth. The bow (or stern) ramp is lowered onto the quay. Sometimes a shore ramp is used if the vessel sits high.

**Step 2 — Vehicle Ingress**
Trucks, trailers, or cars drive onto the **main car deck** (often called the *tweendeck* or *Hooghdeck*). They are directed by deck personnel to designated parking lanes.

**Step 3 — Internal Ramp Transit (if applicable)**
To reach lower or upper car decks, vehicles drive up/down **internal ramps** that may be:
- **Axial / Centreline ramps** (along the ship's length — saves space, long gradient).
- **Side ramps / Quarter ramps** (along the transverse axis — shorter gradient, wider ships).
- **Liftable / Hoistable car decks** (mechanical platforms carrying vehicles between fixed decks — used when ramps would be too steep).

**Step 4 — Lashing and Securing**
Each wheel is chocked and lashed with chains/ratchet straps to **deck padeyes**. This is critical because rolling cargo creates enormous horizontal forces in a seaway.

**Step 5 — Departure**
After lashing inspection, ramp raised, bow/stern doors closed and dogged, the vessel sails.

**Step 6 — Discharge**
Reverse process — ramp lowered, vehicles drive off under their own power or are towed by terminal tractors.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why ramps instead of cranes?** Speed and reduced cargo handling damage.
- **Why multiple decks?** Modern PCTCs carry **6,000–9,000+ cars** in a single voyage — they must stack cars vertically because deck footprint alone is insufficient.
- **Why internal ramps at all?** To maximise stowage efficiency while keeping vehicle access to every deck.
- **Why so much lashing equipment?** A **40-tonne truck** sliding across the deck in a storm can capsize the ship or rupture the hull. Lashing is a stability and safety imperative.

### 2.3 Naval Architecture Challenges Specific to Ro-Ro Ships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ro-Ro ships are **floating bodies with severe internal weight redistribution problems**. Unlike a tanker (which loads/discharges through pipelines and the liquid finds its own level), Ro-Ro cargo is **rigid, asymmetric, and constantly repositionable**. This affects:

1. **Stability (GM):** Heavy vehicle loads (especially high and to one side) lower KG and may **reduce GM** dangerously. Water ballast is often pumped in/out to compensate.
2. **Trim:** Lopsided loading causes the ship to **trim by the bow or stern**. Trim is corrected by ballast transfer.
3. **Shearing Forces & Bending Moments:** Local concentrated wheel loads produce **high localised hull-girder stresses**, especially in mid-body.
4. **Freeboard and Air Draft:** Tall Ro-Ro decks limit air-draft, restricting bridge passages.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metacentric Height | $GM = KM - KG$ | Transverse stability margin | m |
| Righting Lever (small angle) | $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\phi$ | Heeling moment arm | m |
| Righting Moment | $RM = \Delta \cdot GZ$ | Restoring capacity | kN·m |
| Heeling Moment from Off-centre Cargo | $HM = w \cdot y$ | Tendency to list | t·m |
| Trim Equation | $\text{Trim} = \dfrac{M}{MCTC}$ | Change of trim from a moment | m |
| Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm | $MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GML}$ | Longitudinal stiffness | t·m/cm |
| Parallel Sinkage | $\Delta Draft = \dfrac{w}{\rho \cdot A_W}$ | Draft change from added weight | m |
| Density of Sea Water | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ | Standard seawater density | t/m³ |
| Density of Fresh Water | $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ | Standard freshwater density | t/m³ |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Hull fullness | dimensionless |
| Added Mass Formula (Ro-Ro vehicle) | $m' = w \cdot (1 + C_a)$ | Effective inertia in roll | t |
| Lashing Force per Wheel | $F_{lash} = \mu \cdot N$ | Friction-resisted lateral force | kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $\Delta$ = displacement, $w$ = added weight, $y$ = transverse distance from centreline, $LBP$ = Length Between Perpendiculars, $GML$ = Longitudinal Metacentric Height, $A_W$ = Waterplane Area, $\mu$ = friction coefficient (≈ 0.15 wet deck), $N$ = normal reaction.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations in Ro-Ro Operations

- **Loading Computer Integration:** Every modern Ro-Ro ship has a **loading computer** that continuously computes GM, trim, shear forces, and bending moments as each vehicle drives on.
- **Ballast Management:** As cargo rolls on, the loading computer commands **ballast pumps** to fill/empty ballast tanks to maintain acceptable GM and trim.
- **Stability Information Booklet:** Each ship carries a **Stability Information Booklet (SIB)** approved by the flag administration — this is essentially the Ro-Ro ship's "fingerprint" of stability, generated using exactly the formulas above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Effect of Loading a Heavy Trailer on GM

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A Ro-Ro vessel in seawater ($\rho = 1.025\,t/m^3$) has displacement $\Delta = 15{,}000\,t$, KG = **7.20 m**, KM = **8.00 m**, and Waterplane Area $A_W = 1{,}500\,m^2$. A single trailer of mass $w = 80\,t$ is driven onto the upper car deck at a height $z = 11.5\,m$ above the keel and at the centreline. Calculate the **new GM** and the **change in mean draught**.

**Step 1 — Initial Metacentric Height**
The vessel's initial transverse metacentric height is computed directly from KG and KM.

$$
GM_{old} = KM - KG
$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
GM_{old} = 8.00 - 7.20
$$

Evaluating the right-hand side arithmetic:

$$
GM_{old} = 0.80\,m
$$

**Step 2 — Shift in Vertical Centre of Gravity (KG)**
Loading a heavy mass on a high deck raises the composite centre of gravity. The parallel-axis formula for combining two vertical centres of gravity is:

$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot KG_{old} + w \cdot z}{\Delta + w}
$$

Substituting the given values $\Delta = 15{,}000\,t$, $KG_{old} = 7.20\,m$, $w = 80\,t$, $z = 11.5\,m$:

$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{(15{,}000 \times 7.20) + (80 \times 11.5)}{15{,}000 + 80}
$$

Evaluating the numerator and denominator separately for clarity:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 108{,}000 + 920 = 108{,}920
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 15{,}080
$$

Dividing the numerator by the denominator:

$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{108{,}920}{15{,}080}
$$

$$
KG_{new} \approx 7.223\,m
$$

**Step 3 — New Displacement and New KM (Approximation)**
For small weight additions, the Waterplane Area changes very slightly; we assume KM remains effectively constant at 8.00 m for this KTU-style problem. The new displacement is:

$$
\Delta_{new} = \Delta + w
$$

$$
\Delta_{new} = 15{,}000 + 80 = 15{,}080\,t
$$

**Step 4 — New Metacentric Height**

$$
GM_{new} = KM - KG_{new}
$$

$$
GM_{new} = 8.00 - 7.223
$$

$$
GM_{new} \approx 0.777\,m
$$

**Step 5 — Result Interpretation**
Loading the high-deck trailer **reduces GM** by:

$$
\Delta GM = GM_{old} - GM_{new} = 0.80 - 0.777 = 0.023\,m = 2.3\,cm
$$

Although small, in a near-critical vessel this could be significant. **Conclusion:** the deck officer should consider compensating ballast low in the ship (double bottom) to restore GM.

**Step 6 — Change in Mean Draught**
The added weight forces additional immersion. By Archimedes' principle the ship sinks until the additional displaced volume equals the added mass divided by water density:

$$
\delta T = \dfrac{w}{\rho \cdot A_W}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\delta T = \dfrac{80}{1.025 \times 1{,}500}
$$

$$
\delta T = \dfrac{80}{1{,}537.5}
$$

$$
\delta T \approx 0.052\,m = 5.2\,cm
$$

So the ship sits approximately **5.2 cm deeper** in the water after loading the trailer.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Heeling Due to Off-Centreline Loading

**Problem Statement:**
A Ro-Ro ship displaces $\Delta = 20{,}000\,t$ at even keel. GM = **0.90 m**. A heavy truck of mass $w = 60\,t$ is parked **5 m to starboard of the centreline** on the upper car deck. Calculate the resulting **list angle** $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Heeling Moment**
The heeling moment is the product of the weight and its transverse lever arm from the centreline:

$$
HM = w \cdot y
$$

$$
HM = 60 \times 5 = 300\,t \cdot m
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium List Angle**
At equilibrium list, the righting moment equals the heeling moment. For small angles $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\phi$, therefore:

$$
\Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi = w \cdot y
$$

Solving for the sine of the list angle:

$$
\sin\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{\Delta \cdot GM}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\sin\phi = \dfrac{60 \times 5}{20{,}000 \times 0.90}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = \dfrac{300}{18{,}000}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = 0.01667
$$

Computing the arcsine of this small value:

$$
\phi = \arcsin(0.01667) \approx 0.955^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Verification**
The list is small (less than 1°), which confirms a healthy stability margin. A typical IMO damage-stability criterion requires surviving large heeling moments; in intact condition even a 5 m off-centre load produces a list well within safe limits for this vessel.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Trim Due to Asymmetric Longitudinal Loading

**Problem Statement:**
A Ro-Ro ship has $LBP = 180\,m$, $\Delta = 25{,}000\,t$, MCTC = **220 t·m/cm**, and GML = **150 m**. A heavy cargo of mass $w = 800\,t$ is loaded **40 m forward of midships**. Find the **change of trim**.

**Step 1 — Trimming Moment**
The product of weight and its longitudinal distance from midships gives the trimming moment:

$$
M = w \cdot x
$$

$$
M = 800 \times 40 = 32{,}000\,t \cdot m
$$

**Step 2 — Trim Equation**
Trim is the ratio of the trimming moment to the moment to change trim by one centimetre:

$$
\text{Trim} = \dfrac{M}{MCTC}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Trim} = \dfrac{32{,}000}{220}
$$

$$
\text{Trim} \approx 145.5\,cm
$$

**Step 3 — Draft Changes at Bow and Stern**
By standard trim theory, a forward shift causes the bow to sink and the stern to rise, but the **mean draught rises by the parallel sinkage**:

$$
\delta T_{parallel} = \dfrac{w}{\rho \cdot A_W}
$$

Assuming $A_W = 2{,}000\,m^2$:

$$
\delta T_{parallel} = \dfrac{800}{1.025 \times 2{,}000} \approx 0.39\,m = 39\,cm
$$

Draft forward increases by approximately $\frac{145.5}{2} + 39 \approx 112\,cm$ and aft decreases by $\frac{145.5}{2} - 39 \approx 33\,cm$.

---

### 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Problem | Variable Computed | Equation Used | Final Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1a | New GM after high-deck load | $KG_{new} = (\Delta KG + w z)/(\Delta + w)$ | $0.777\,m$ |
| 1b | Mean draught increase | $\delta T = w/(\rho A_W)$ | $5.2\,cm$ |
| 2 | List from off-centre cargo | $\sin\phi = wy/(\Delta GM)$ | $0.955^\circ$ |
| 3 | Trim from forward load | $\text{Trim} = M/MCTC$ | $145.5\,cm$ trim by bow |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Ro-Ro Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Berth and Shore Ramp] --> B[Bow Ramp]
    B --> C[Main Car Deck Tweendeck 1]
    C --> D[Internal Axial Ramp]
    D --> E[Upper Car Deck Deck 5]
    E --> F[Internal Side Ramp]
    F --> G[Hoistable Car Deck Platform Lift]
    G --> H[Lower Hold Cargo Deck]
    H --> I[Lashing Padeyes Chain and Strap System]
    I --> J[Loading Computer Feedback Loop]
    J --> K[Ballast Pump Control System]
    K --> L[Double Bottom Ballast Tanks]
    L --> M[Stability and Trim Restored]
    M --> N[Voyage Begins]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Vehicle path: `A → B → C → D → E` (the dominant ingress route).
- Cargo is then distributed to lateral decks via `F → G → H`.
- Lashing (`I`) and the loading computer (`J`) close the feedback loop, controlling ballast (`K → L → M`) so that `M` (stability/trim restoration) is achieved before sailing.

### 4.2 Mermaid Ro-Ro Stability Feedback System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cargo[Rolling Cargo Vehicle] --> VCG[Shift in VCG and LCG]
    VCG --> GMCalc[GM and Trim Calculator Loading Computer]
    GMCalc --> Compare{Within Safe Limits?}
    Compare -->|Yes| Sail[Sail Voyage Permitted]
    Compare -->|No| Ballast[Activate Ballast Pumps]
    Ballast --> VCG
    GMCalc --> Indicator[Bridge Display Indicator]
```

This loop explains **why** modern Ro-Ro ships cannot operate without an integrated loading computer — it is the **continuous equilibrium solver** of the floating body problem.

### 4.3 Textual Schematic — Internal Layout (Cross-Section View)

```
  __________________________________________________________
 |  _________    _________    _________    _________        |  <- Weather Deck
 |  | Cars |    | Cars |    | Cars |    | Cars |            |
 |  |_____ |    |_____ |    |_____ |    |_____ |           |  <- Hoistable Deck
 |  Ramp↑↓                                          Ramp↑↓  |
 |  _________    _________    _________    _________        |  <- Tweendeck
 |  | Trucks|   | Trucks|   | Trucks|   |Trucks|           |
 |  |_______|   |_______|   |_______|   |______|           |
 |______________________________________________________    |
 |______________________________________________________    |  <- Tank Top
 |=========||=========||=========||=========||=========     |  <- Double Bottom
 | Ballast || Ballast || Engine || Ballast || Ballast       |  <- Ballast Tanks
 |=========||=========||=========||=========||=========     |
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  <- Keel
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Bow Door and Ramp Geometry

```
            (Closed Position)
              ___________
             |           |   <-- Bow Visor / Stem Door
   |---------|           |---------|
   |                                  |
   |   ===INTERNAL BOW RAMP===       |
   |   /                          \   |
   |  /                            \  |
   | /  (Gradient ≤ 1:8)            \ |
   |/________________________________\|
            (Open Position)
              \                          
               \   <-- Ramp lowered onto quay
                \_________________________  <-- Shore Ramp / Quay
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Lashing Geometry at a Single Wheel

```
          Truck Wheel (Top View)
                ___
               /   \
              |  ●  |  <-- Wheel Centre
               \___/
        Chock wedge inserted under wheel
                |
        =================
                |
        Ratchet strap pulled to padeye welded to deck
                |
        Deck padeye (welded D-ring)
        |||||||||||||||||||||||||||  <-- Deck plating
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q:** Define a **Ro-Ro ship**. List any **two advantages** of Ro-Ro vessels over conventional Lo-Lo (Lift-on/Lift-off) cargo ships.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Ro-Ro (Roll-on/Roll-off) ship** is a vessel designed to carry wheeled cargo such as cars, trucks, trailers, and railway wagons, which are driven on and off the ship across built-in ramps without the use of cranes. **[1 mark for definition]**

Advantages over Lo-Lo: **[1 mark each, total 2 marks]**
1. **Faster turnaround time** — Ro-Ro loading/unloading rates of 1,000+ vehicles/hour vastly exceed crane-based Lo-Lo rates of 40–80 units/hour.
2. **Reduced cargo damage** — vehicles are not lifted or swung through the air, eliminating risk of impact damage and weather exposure during handling.
3. *(Any one of the above or other valid point: e.g., reduced port infrastructure investment.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Q:** Explain the **function of a stability information booklet** and the **role of a loading computer** on a Ro-Ro vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Stability Information Booklet (SIB)** is a document approved by the vessel's flag administration that contains pre-calculated stability and loading data for every conceivable loading condition. It enables the master to verify, before sailing, that GM, trim, shear forces and bending moments are within safe limits. **[1.5 marks]**

The **loading computer** is an on-board digital system that continuously receives input from ballast tank soundings and vehicle weights/positions. It calculates real-time GM, trim, and hull-girder stresses, and signals the crew if limits are approached. On a Ro-Ro ship it is essential because cargo is loaded dynamically and asymmetrically. **[1.5 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** A Ro-Ro vessel floats in seawater at displacement $\Delta = 18{,}000\,t$, $KG = 6.80\,m$, $KM = 7.50\,m$. A cargo lorry of mass $200\,t$ is driven onto the upper deck at height $z = 12.0\,m$ above the keel and at the centreline. Compute the **new KG**, the **new GM**, and the resulting **parallel sinkage** if $A_W = 1{,}800\,m^2$. Assume KM remains unchanged. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial GM**
$$
GM_{old} = KM - KG_{old} = 7.50 - 6.80 = 0.70\,m
$$

**Step 2 — New KG by Parallel-Axis Theorem**
$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{(\Delta \cdot KG_{old}) + (w \cdot z)}{\Delta + w}
$$

$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{(18{,}000 \times 6.80) + (200 \times 12.0)}{18{,}000 + 200}
$$

$$
KG_{new} = \dfrac{122{,}400 + 2{,}400}{18{,}200} = \dfrac{124{,}800}{18{,}200} \approx 6.857\,m
$$

**Step 3 — New GM**
$$
GM_{new} = KM - KG_{new} = 7.50 - 6.857 = 0.643\,m
$$

**Step 4 — Parallel Sinkage**
$$
\delta T = \dfrac{w}{\rho \cdot A_W} = \dfrac{200}{1.025 \times 1{,}800} = \dfrac{200}{1{,}845} \approx 0.108\,m = 10.8\,cm
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answers**
- New KG ≈ **6.857 m** (KG rose by 5.7 cm due to high load) **[2 marks]**
- New GM ≈ **0.643 m** (decreased from 0.70 m by 5.7 cm) **[2 marks]**
- Parallel sinkage ≈ **10.8 cm** **[1 mark]**
- Identification of physical reasoning and units: **[2 marks]**

---

**(b)** With reference to a Ro-Ro passenger ferry, explain in detail the **SOLAS damage-stability regulations** and the **role of internal watertight subdivision**, and describe **three structural features** that distinguish a Ro-Ro ship from a conventional dry-cargo ship. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**SOLAS Damage-Stability:** The International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), Chapter II-1, Part B-1, prescribes a probabilistic damage-stability standard (the so-called **"1-subdivision index"** for cargo ships and the **attainment of the s-factor** for passenger ships) that a Ro-Ro passenger vessel must meet. The vessel must survive flooding of any single compartment, and the residual GM and righting-lever curve must be positive over a sufficient range of heel angles. The worst casualty on a Ro-Ro ferry occurs when water enters the **bow visor/ramp area** and floods the open vehicle deck; SOLAS therefore mandates a **specially considered worst-cases flooding scenario**. **[3 marks]**

**Internal Watertight Subdivision:** Ro-Ro ships are subdivided by **watertight transverse bulkheads**, **watertight sliding doors**, and **collision bulkheads** so that flooding can be contained. In addition, **vehicles decks are fitted with drencher/sprinkler systems** and **fire detection** because enclosed vehicle decks are high fire-risk spaces. **[1 mark]**

**Three Distinguishing Structural Features:** **[1 mark each]**
1. **Bow/stern doors and ramps** — large openings that would be unthinkable on a general cargo ship.
2. **Multiple internal car decks** — including **liftable / hoistable platforms** to multiply cargo capacity without requiring excessively long ramps.
3. **Heavy lashing infrastructure** — padeyes, rails, and deck fittings covering essentially every square metre of the car decks, which a conventional ship does not require.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** A Ro-Ro ship displaces $22{,}000\,t$ at even keel with $GM = 0.85\,m$. While loading, two identical trucks of mass $50\,t$ each are mistakenly parked **7 m to port** and **3 m to starboard** of the centreline on the upper car deck. Calculate the resultant **list angle** $\phi$ and comment on whether this list is acceptable under typical intact-stability IMO criteria. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Transverse Heeling Moment**
The two trucks together produce a net heeling moment because they are not symmetric about the centreline:

$$
HM_{net} = (50 \times 7) - (50 \times 3)
$$

$$
HM_{net} = 350 - 150 = 200\,t \cdot m \text{ to port}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation**
At equilibrium list: $\Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi = HM_{net}$

$$
\sin\phi = \dfrac{HM_{net}}{\Delta \cdot GM} = \dfrac{200}{22{,}000 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = \dfrac{200}{18{,}700} \approx 0.01070
$$

**Step 3 — List Angle**

$$
\phi = \arcsin(0.01070) \approx 0.613^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Acceptability**
The list of **0.61° to port** is well within the IMO intact-stability criterion (typically residual righting lever ≥ 0.20 m at 20° and area under GZ curve ≥ 0.055 m·rad up to 40°). However, **operationally the master must correct the asymmetric parking** before the ship sails, because such loading errors accumulate and may combine with wind heeling moments to produce dangerous angles in heavy weather. **[1 mark for the numerical calculation, 2 marks for setting up the equation, 2 marks for the answer, 2 marks for the engineering comment]**

---

**(b)** Describe with neat sketches the **different types of ramps** used in Ro-Ro vessels (bow ramp, stern ramp, side ramp, axial ramp). Compare the **advantages and disadvantages** of axial ramps versus side/quarter ramps. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Ramp Types:** **[3 marks for description + sketches]**
1. **Bow ramp:** A large visor door forms the forward part of the ship; it hinges down to form a ramp onto the quay. Used on most short-sea Ro-Ro ferries.
2. **Stern ramp:** A ramp fitted at the after end of the vessel, often combined with a slewing/rotating ramp arrangement to allow port-side or starboard-side berthing.
3. **Axial (centreline) ramp:** A straight ramp running longitudinally inside the ship connecting the main deck to upper/lower decks. Used when width permits a long gradient (≤ 1:12).
4. **Side / quarter ramp:** A ramp running transversely from the centreline to one side; the gradient is shorter and steeper is acceptable (≤ 1:8).

**Comparison – Axial vs Side/Quarter:** **[4 marks]**

| Aspect | Axial Ramp | Side / Quarter Ramp |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient | Long, gentle (1:12) | Short, can be steeper (1:8) |
| Space taken | Consumes longitudinal vehicle parking length | Consumes beam/transverse space |
| Vehicle flow | One-way traffic typical | Two-way traffic possible |
| Loading speed | Slower (long ramp) | Faster (short ramp) |
| Hull strength impact | Concentrates load near midship | Distributes load across hull |
| Best suited for | Long, narrow vehicle decks (PCTC) | Short-sea Ro-Pax ferries |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Ro-Ro questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — t·m/cm must be kept consistent when using MCTC; mixing cm and m drops the trim answer by 100×.
> 2. **Confusing VCG and LCG** — vertical centre of gravity (KG) affects GM; longitudinal centre of gravity (LCG) affects trim. Examiners deduct marks if these are swapped.
> 3. **Ignoring the parallel sinkage term** — when computing trim, the final draft is *parallel sinkage ± half trim*, not just the trim half.
> 4. **Using freshwater density for seawater (or vice versa)** — always check the problem statement; default is ρ = 1.025 t/m³ for seawater.
> 5. **Skipping a free-body diagram or sketch** — KTU board examiners award marks for diagrams; even a hand-drawn ramp/lashing sketch is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Stating "list is small so it is fine"** — must justify with reference to **SOLAS / IMO intact-stability criteria**, not merely numerical magnitude.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ro-Ro Ships:**
> - Ro-Ro = **Roll-on/Roll-off**; cargo is wheeled and drives on/off via ramps.
> - Key features: **bow/stern doors, internal ramps (axial & side), hoistable/liftable car decks, padeyes for lashing**.
> - Cargo rolls on under its own power → **fast turnaround** but introduces **dynamic loading** problems.
> - Stability equation: **GM = KM − KG**; loading high raises KG → lowers GM.
> - Trim equation: **Trim = M / MCTC**; MCTC = WLBP/(100·GML).
> - Parallel sinkage: **δT = w / (ρ A_W)**.
> - Off-centre load produces list: **sin φ = wy/(Δ GM)**.
> - Ro-Ro passenger ferries must satisfy **SOLAS damage-stability probabilistic standard** and special **bow-door flooding scenario**.
> - Modern Ro-Ro ships always carry a **Stability Information Booklet** and operate with an **on-board loading computer** that controls ballast.
> - **Lashing** is critical — rolling cargo in a seaway generates enormous transverse forces.
> - Common variants: **PCTC** (Pure Car and Truck Carrier), **Con-Ro** (Container + Ro-Ro), **RoPax** (Ro-Ro + Passenger).
> - Typical ramp gradient limits: **1:8 (side/quarter) and 1:12 (axial)**.
> - **KTU 2024 scheme emphasis:** treat Ro-Ro vessels as **floating-body problems with movable internal masses** — apply Module-1 stability and trim formulas throughout.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barge Carriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Barge Carriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barge Carrier (Barge-Carrying Ship):** A specialized, multi-deck ocean-going vessel designed and engineered specifically for the purpose of transporting **lighters, pontoons, or barges** loaded with general, bulk, or containerized cargo across deep-sea routes. The defining operational feature of a barge carrier is that **cargo handling (loading and discharging) does NOT depend on shoreside port infrastructure** such as quay cranes, gantries, or roll-on/roll-off (Ro-Ro) ramps. Instead, the vessel itself carries the lifting/floating equipment to load and unload the barges at any sheltered anchorage or river-mouth location.

In KTU 2024 Scheme maritime terminology, barge carriers fall under the broad classification of **"Combination Carriers"** or **"Unit Load Carriers"** and represent one of the earliest successful implementations of the **intermodal transport** philosophy in ocean shipping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms & Standard Nomenclature used in Naval Architecture:**
> - **LASH** (Lighter Aboard SHip) – the original American system.
> - **Sea-Bee** – the Swedish (Orenstein-Koppel) variant.
> - **FLO/FLO** (Float-On / Float-Off) – generic NATO/maritime classification.
> - **BACAT** (BArge CATamaran) – small catamaran-based European variant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Floating Garage & Elevator":**
> Imagine a **massive floating multi-story parking garage** parked in the middle of the ocean. Inside this garage, instead of cars, are **small floating boxes (barges) full of cargo**. Now, instead of cranes lifting the boxes out at a port, the garage has its **own built-in overhead crane (or a submerged elevator deck)** that picks up the barges and lowers them into the sea. The small boxes then float away under tugboat power to shallow rivers and inland ports that the giant mother-ship cannot enter. The mother ship then sails to the next port and repeats the process. **This is exactly how a LASH barge carrier operates.**

In simpler words: a barge carrier is a **"mother ship"** for **"daughter barges"**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following benchmark figures represent **industry-standard reference values** for a typical LASH vessel:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Typical Deadweight (DWT) | **27,000 – 40,000** | Tonnes |
| Number of Barges Carried | **60 – 100+** | Barges |
| Barge Capacity (each) | **~370 Tonnes** | Deadweight |
| Barge Dimensions (L × B × D) | **18.75 × 9.50 × 3.80** | metres |
| Onboard Gantry Crane Lifting Capacity | **~510 Tonnes** | Tonnes |
| Stern Lift (FLO/FLO) Submerged Depth | **~10 – 12** | metres |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the standard **LASH barge size of 18.75 m × 9.50 m × 3.80 m** with a deadweight of **~370 tonnes** is the most frequently used dataset in university questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Engineering Logic

A barge carrier is engineered to solve a single, critical logistical problem: **the "last-mile" connectivity gap** between deep-sea trade routes and shallow inland waterways. The design logic proceeds as follows:

1. **Identification of Problem:** Many global ports (e.g., river ports in Africa, South America, South-East Asia) have insufficient water depth (draft limitations) and lack heavy-duty shoreside cranes. Conventional containerships and bulk carriers cannot enter such ports.
2. **Decoupling of Cargo & Hull:** Instead of carrying the cargo *inside* the ship's hull, the carrier carries *floating containers (barges)* inside the hull. The cargo is decoupled from the ocean-going vessel.
3. **Independence from Port Infrastructure:** The mother ship carries its own **gantry crane** (LASH system) or its own **submerging deck (stern lift)** to load/discharge barges into the open sea.
4. **Distributed Distribution:** The discharged barges are towed in groups (called **"tows"**) by **tugboats** to shallow river ports where they are unloaded by local, low-capacity shore cranes.
5. **Parallel Logistics:** While the mother ship is at a sheltered anchorage discharging/loading, port operations at multiple inland destinations run **in parallel**, dramatically reducing total voyage cycle time.

## 2.2 Classification of Barge Carriers (KTU High-Yield)

KTU examinations frequently test the classification. Barge carriers are divided into two main technological families:

### Family A: Lift-On / Lift-Off (LoLo) Barge Carriers
- **LASH System (Lighter Aboard Ship)**
  - Developed in **1969** by Friede & Goldman (USA) for the Pacific Coast/Hawaii trade.
  - Uses a massive **overhead gantry crane** mounted on rails running the full length of the ship.
  - The crane lifts barges from the internal well deck and deposits them over the side into the sea.
  - Ships a typical LASH vessel: ~27,000 DWT, carrying 73–89 lighters of 370 t each.

### Family B: Float-On / Float-Off (FLO/FLO) Barge Carriers
- **Sea-Bee System**
  - Developed by **Orenstein-Koppel** in Germany/Sweden.
  - The ship uses a **submerging internal well deck** at the stern.
  - The ship takes on ballast water to submerge the well deck, allowing barges to float in/out.

- **Modern FLO/FLO (Dedicated)**
  - A specialized version where the entire aft section of the ship is a floodable dock.
  - Used commonly for military logistics and project cargo.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are routinely tested in numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cargo Carried | $W_{total} = n \times W_{barge}$ | tonnes | $n$ = no. of barges, $W_{barge}$ = capacity |
| 2 | Light Ship Factor | $\text{LSF} = \frac{W_{lightship}}{DWT}$ | ratio | Typically 0.4–0.5 for LASH (very high due to gantry) |
| 3 | Barge Carrying Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{n \times W_{barge}}{DWT} \times 100$ | % | Useful cargo fraction |
| 4 | Gantry Crane Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = T_{lift} + T_{swing} + T_{lower} + T_{return}$ | min | Determines port turnaround |
| 5 | Required Ballast for Stern Submerge | $V_{ballast} = L_{well} \times B_{well} \times \Delta h$ | m³ | Volume of water pumped in |
| 6 | Deck Load (Barge + Crane) | $q = \frac{W_{barge}}{A_{barge}}$ | t/m² | Floor plate pressure |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by naval architects during **preliminary design** to estimate the deadweight, draft, stability, and structural scantlings of a barge carrier *before* detailed CAD/FEA modeling.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Trans-Oceanic Logistics:** Connects ocean shipping to the **Mississippi, Amazon, Mekong, Congo, and Ganges-Brahmaputra** river systems.
- **Military Sealift:** The U.S. Navy's **Avenger-class** and **Submarine Tenders** use FLO/FLO technology to deliver tanks and supplies to beaches without ports.
- **Project Cargo:** Heavy oil rig modules, locomotives, and turbine parts are moved on heavy-lift barges that can be transferred at offshore anchorages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Total LASH Cargo Capacity

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard):** A LASH vessel is designed to carry 82 standard lighters. Each lighter has a deadweight capacity of 370 tonnes. Determine the *useful cargo deadweight* contributed by the lighters.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

We define the *useful cargo deadweight* as the total mass of cargo the ship can deliver per voyage, assuming each lighter is fully loaded.

$$
W_{useful} = n \times W_{barge}
$$

Substituting the values (number of barges = 82, capacity of each = 370 t):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{useful} &= 82 \times 370 \;\; \text{tonnes} \\
&= 30{,}340 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiplication is performed directly: $82 \times 370 = 82 \times (400 - 30) = 32{,}800 - 2{,}460 = 30{,}340$ tonnes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner *expects* you to clearly state the formula before substituting, and to **box the final numerical answer**. Always retain the unit "tonnes" in the final line.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Barge Carrying Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Problem Setup:** A barge carrier has a total Deadweight of 33,000 tonnes. The useful cargo capacity (barges + their contents) has been calculated as 30,340 tonnes. The remaining 2,660 tonnes is the mass of the empty barges (lighters) themselves plus operational consumables. Compute the **Barge Carrying Efficiency**.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

The efficiency is the ratio of *useful cargo* to *total deadweight*, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta = \frac{W_{useful}}{DWT} \times 100
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{30{,}340}{33{,}000} \times 100 \;\; \% \\
&= 0.9194 \times 100 \;\; \% \\
&= 91.94 \;\; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Dividing first: $30{,}340 \div 33{,}000 = 0.91939...$ Then multiplied by 100 to obtain a percentage.

**Interpretation:** A 91.94% efficiency is *excellent* for a barge carrier. The lost ~8% is the unavoidable mass of the empty lighters being transported back/forth, fuel, fresh water, and provisions.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Ballast Water Required for Stern Submergence (FLO/FLO)

**Problem Setup:** A Sea-Bee vessel needs to submerge its stern well deck to a depth $\Delta h = 8$ m below the waterline. The well deck is rectangular with length $L_{well} = 80$ m and breadth $B_{well} = 20$ m. Calculate the **volume of ballast water** required to achieve this submergence.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

The well deck is essentially a rectangular volume. The volume of water needed to submerge it is the product of the deck's surface area and the required submergence depth.

$$
V_{ballast} = L_{well} \times B_{well} \times \Delta h
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ballast} &= 80 \times 20 \times 8 \;\; \text{m}^3 \\
&= 1{,}600 \times 8 \;\; \text{m}^3 \\
&= 12{,}800 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiplication performed in two stages. First, $80 \times 20 = 1{,}600$. Then, $1{,}600 \times 8 = 12{,}800$.

**Mass Equivalent:** Taking the density of seawater $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{ballast} &= 12{,}800 \times 1.025 \;\; \text{tonnes} \\
&= 13{,}120 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Note:** This 13,120-tonne ballast figure is enormous and explains why FLO/FLO vessels have very large **double-bottom and side ballast tanks**. Stability during submergence must be carefully analyzed for the **"partially submerged" condition**, which is the worst-case free-surface effect.

---

## 3.4 Derivative 4: Deck Plate Loading under a Barge

**Problem Setup:** A standard LASH barge of 370 t is supported on four internal cradle supports inside the ship. The barge footprint on the deck is approximately 18.75 m × 9.50 m. Calculate the **uniformly distributed load (UDL)** on the tank top / hatch cover.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

The UDL is simply the weight divided by the supporting area.

$$
q = \frac{W_{barge}}{L_{barge} \times B_{barge}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{370}{18.75 \times 9.50} \;\; \text{t/m}^2 \\
&= \frac{370}{178.125} \;\; \text{t/m}^2 \\
&= 2.077 \;\; \text{t/m}^2 \\
&\approx 2.08 \;\; \text{t/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $18.75 \times 9.5 = 178.125$ m². Then $370 \div 178.125 \approx 2.08$ t/m².

> **Engineering Utility:** This 2.08 t/m² figure dictates the **class-approved scantlings** (plate thickness, stiffener spacing) of the internal barge decks of a LASH vessel — typically requiring high-tensile steel reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a LASH Barge Carrier

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional systems architecture** of a LASH vessel, mapping how cargo flow, mechanical handling, and stability systems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ocean-Going LASH Mother Ship] --> B[Onboard Gantry Crane System]
    A --> C[Multi-Tier Internal Barge Stowage Wells]
    A --> D[Ballast and Stability System]
    A --> E[Navigation and Bridge Module]

    B --> B1[Traveling Hoist on Longitudinal Rails]
    B --> B2[510 Tonne Lifting Gear]
    B --> B3[Side Discharge Port]

    C --> C1[Tier 1 Upper Well Deck]
    C --> C2[Tier 2 Main Well Deck]
    C --> C3[Tier 3 Lower Well Deck]
    C --> C4[Hatch Covers and Reinforced Floors]

    D --> D1[Double Bottom Ballast Tanks]
    D --> D2[Side Ballast Tanks]
    D --> D3[Stability Computer and Draft Sensors]

    E --> E1[Integrated Bridge Controls]
    E --> E2[Communication and Tugboat Coordination]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Sea-Bee / FLO/FLO Carrier

The following diagram shows the **stern-lift (FLO/FLO) operational flow** of a Sea-Bee vessel, with text explaining the stages of loading/discharging.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Transit Configuration] --> S2[Stage 2: Ballast Flooding Initiated]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Stern Submerges Below Waterline]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Barges Float In or Out]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Deballast Pumps Engage]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Vessel Resumes Transit Draft]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: LASH vs Sea-Bee

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Barge Carrier Family} --> R[LASH System LoLo]
    Q --> S[Sea-Bee System FLO/FLO]

    R --> R1[Gantry Crane Loads Over Side]
    R --> R2[Multiple Internal Decks]
    R --> R3[Independent of Tidal Conditions]

    S --> S1[Stern Submerged Well Deck]
    S --> S2[Barges Float Directly]
    S --> S3[Requires Calm Protected Waters]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Q: Define a "Barge Carrier". Mention any TWO advantages it offers over conventional break-bulk shipping.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A barge carrier is a specialized ocean-going vessel designed to carry floating lighters or barges loaded with cargo. Cargo handling is carried out using the ship's own gantry crane (LASH) or submerged stern lift (FLO/FLO), without depending on shoreside port cranes.

**Advantages (2 Marks – 1 each):**
1. **Independence from port infrastructure:** The mother ship can load/discharge in open anchorages, river mouths, or undeveloped coastlines, eliminating dependence on quay cranes.
2. **Faster turnaround:** While the mother ship is at the anchorage, the smaller barges can be towed simultaneously to multiple shallow inland ports, allowing parallel handling and reducing overall transit cycle time.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Q: Distinguish between the "LASH" system and the "Sea-Bee" system of barge carriers in any THREE points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – 1 per point):**

| # | LASH System | Sea-Bee System |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses an **overhead gantry crane** running on rails for cargo handling. | Uses a **submerging stern well deck** for cargo handling. |
| 2 | Barges are **lifted on/off** the vessel (LoLo principle). | Barges **float on/off** the vessel (FLO/FLO principle). |
| 3 | Can operate in **open sea and rougher conditions** because the crane is enclosed. | Requires **calm, sheltered, deep waters** near the stern for submerging the deck. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In barge-carrier numericals, students frequently **forget to include the mass of the empty lighters** when computing DWT, leading to a 2–3 mark penalty.
> - For LASH vs Sea-Bee theory, do **not** mix up "LASH = crane" and "Sea-Bee = submerge" — a wrong pairing results in **0 marks** for that comparison point.
> - When asked for "advantages over container ships," avoid generic statements. Use **maritime-specific terminology** such as "intermodal transshipment," "last-mile connectivity," and "draft-independent logistics."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the operational principle of a **LASH (Lighter Aboard Ship) vessel**. Discuss the role of the onboard gantry crane and the tiered barge stowage system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LASH vessel is designed to carry 82 lighters, each of 370 tonnes deadweight. The light ship weight is 13,800 tonnes and the service constants (fuel, water, stores) total 1,540 tonnes. Calculate the **(i)** useful cargo deadweight, **(ii)** total deadweight, and **(iii)** the lightship factor. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Operational Principle of LASH (7 Marks):**

A LASH vessel is a **multi-deck ocean-going ship** fitted with a massive **overhead gantry crane** that travels longitudinally along rails mounted high above the cargo decks. The vessel is built with an **internal well structure** having typically 2–3 tiers of reinforced decks on which the standard lighters are stowed in a precise matrix pattern. **(2 Marks)**

**Loading Cycle:** Barges are lifted by the gantry crane from the internal well, transported along the ship's length, and lowered through a **side-port opening** directly into the sea. Tugboats then take charge of the discharged lighters and tow them in clusters to shallow river ports. **(2 Marks)**

**Discharging is the reverse process.** The vessel can operate at offshore anchorages, completely eliminating the need for deep-water berths. **(1 Mark)**

**Key Advantages:** Independence from shore infrastructure, parallel inland distribution, faster overall cargo cycle. The 510-tonne gantry crane operates independently of tidal conditions, making LASH robust for open-sea operations. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Number of lighters, $n = 82$
- Capacity per lighter, $W_{barge} = 370$ t
- Lightship weight, $W_{LS} = 13{,}800$ t
- Service constants, $W_{svc} = 1{,}540$ t

**(i) Useful Cargo Deadweight (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cargo} &= n \times W_{barge} \\
&= 82 \times 370 \\
&= 30{,}340 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Deadweight (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= W_{cargo} + W_{svc} \\
&= 30{,}340 + 1{,}540 \\
&= 31{,}880 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Lightship Factor (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LSF &= \frac{W_{LS}}{DWT} \\
&= \frac{13{,}800}{31{,}880} \\
&= 0.4329 \\
&\approx 0.43
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the LSF is High (≈ 0.43):** A LASH vessel has an unusually high lightship factor because of the **massive structural reinforcement**, the **500+ tonne gantry crane**, and the **multi-tier deck plating**. A conventional bulk carrier has LSF ≈ 0.25–0.30, while a tanker is even lower (≈ 0.20). KTU examiners often expect this comparative insight.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **FLO/FLO (Sea-Bee) system** of barge carriers. Compare its operational requirements with those of the LASH system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Sea-Bee vessel needs to submerge its stern well deck to a depth of 9 m. The well deck dimensions are 75 m (length) × 22 m (breadth). Sea water density is 1.025 t/m³. Calculate the **(i)** volume of ballast water required, **(ii)** mass of ballast water, and **(iii)** comment on the effect of this submergence on the vessel's metacentric height (GM). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sea-Bee / FLO/FLO Description (7 Marks):**

The Sea-Bee system was developed by **Orenstein-Koppel & Co.** (Sweden/Germany) as an alternative to LASH. Unlike LASH, which uses a gantry crane, the Sea-Bee vessel employs a **submerging internal well deck at the stern**. **(2 Marks)**

**Operational Sequence:** The vessel takes on **ballast water** in its aft double-bottom and side tanks, causing the stern to submerge below the waterline. When the deck is sufficiently deep, **barges float in or out** under their own buoyancy. After loading, ballast is pumped out and the vessel rises back to its transit draft. **(3 Marks)**

**Comparison with LASH (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | LASH | Sea-Bee |
|---|---|---|
| Handling | Crane lifting | Float-in/float-out |
| Sea-state tolerance | Higher | Requires calm waters |
| Port infrastructure | None | None (but needs deep sheltered water) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:** $L_{well} = 75$ m, $B_{well} = 22$ m, $\Delta h = 9$ m, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³.

**(i) Volume of Ballast (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ballast} &= L_{well} \times B_{well} \times \Delta h \\
&= 75 \times 22 \times 9 \\
&= 1{,}650 \times 9 \\
&= 14{,}850 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass of Ballast (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{ballast} &= V_{ballast} \times \rho_{sw} \\
&= 14{,}850 \times 1.025 \\
&= 15{,}221.25 \;\; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Effect on GM (3 Marks):**

- Ballasting of **double-bottom and side tanks** lowers the vessel's **center of gravity (G)** only marginally, but the **massive free-surface effect** of the partially flooded well deck **drastically raises the virtual center of gravity (G')**.
- The **metacentric height GM = KM − KG'** is therefore **significantly reduced**, often by 30–50%.
- Stability during the "partially submerged" condition is the **critical design case** and is rigorously checked by **stability software (e.g., MAXSURF, GHS)**.
- Practical mitigation includes **transverse bulkheads in the well deck** to limit free-surface length.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Barge Carriers (Module 1):**

- **Definition:** A barge carrier is a ship that carries *barges* (lighters), not individual cargo, in internal decks.
- **LASH:** Uses a **510-tonne gantry crane** for over-side handling; **LoLo** principle.
- **Sea-Bee / FLO/FLO:** Uses a **submerging stern well deck**; barges **float in/out**.
- **Standard LASH barge:** 18.75 m × 9.50 m × 3.80 m, capacity 370 tonnes.
- **Total LASH DWT range:** 27,000 – 40,000 tonnes; 60–100+ barges.
- **Key advantage:** **Independence from port infrastructure** — can load/discharge at any sheltered anchorage.
- **Lighter tugs:** Barges are towed to inland ports by river/sea tugs.
- **Critical stability condition (FLO/FLO):** The partially submerged well deck causes a **large free-surface effect**, drastically reducing GM.
- **Useful formulas to memorize:**
  - $W_{useful} = n \times W_{barge}$
  - $\eta = \dfrac{n \times W_{barge}}{DWT} \times 100$
  - $V_{ballast} = L_{well} \times B_{well} \times \Delta h$
  - $q = \dfrac{W_{barge}}{A_{barge}}$
- **Examiner's trap:** Do NOT confuse LASH with Sea-Bee — the cargo handling method is the **single most-tested distinction**.
- **Modern context:** Pure barge carriers have largely been retired; the concept survives in modern **FLO/FLO military sealift ships** and **heavy-lift project cargo vessels**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tankers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tankers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Tanker** is a specialized merchant vessel engineered and structurally reinforced for the bulk transportation of liquid cargoes in dedicated cargo containment systems. The cargo is held within an intricate network of prismatic tanks, pumps, and piping networks that form the structural spine of the vessel. The fundamental categories studied under the OENST611 syllabus include **Oil Tankers (Crude/Product)**, **Chemical Tankers**, **Gas Carriers (LNG/LPG)**, and specialized derivatives such as **OBO (Oil-Bulk-Ore)** carriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A tanker is *not* just a "ship with tanks" — it is a sophisticated, rule-driven floating pressure vessel whose geometry is dictated by the cargo's chemical properties, flash point, density, and MARPOL environmental compliance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building with Plumbing" Intuition

Imagine a massive multi-storey reinforced concrete building where every room is waterproof, every wall is doubled-up for safety, and there is an entire network of plumbing (pipes, pumps, valves) running through the structure. Now, float this entire building in the ocean. That is essentially what a tanker is.

- The **rooms** are the **cargo tanks** (void spaces filled with oil or chemicals).
- The **double walls** are the **double hull** (mandatory for environmental safety).
- The **plumbing system** is the **pumping and piping arrangement**.
- The **building's own weight** must displace enough water to float — this is the buoyancy principle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions for tanker engineering:

- **Standard Cargo Density:** Crude oil ≈ **860 to 900 kg/m³**, Heavy fuel oil ≈ **940 to 990 kg/m³**
- **Sea Water Density (ρ_sw):** **1.025 t/m³**
- **Cargo Oil Bulkhead Spacing:** Typically **2 m to 3 m** between transverse wash bulkheads
- **Double Hull Ballast Tank (DBHT) Height:** Mandated by MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19 to be **≥ 2 m** (for tankers ≥ 5000 DWT)
- **Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT) Capacity:** Must be sufficient to allow safe operations *without* ballasting cargo tanks

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** The total weight a vessel can carry, including cargo, fuel, fresh water, ballast, stores, and crew. Tankers are quoted by **DWT** (e.g., a VLCC is a *Very Large Crude Carrier* of **200,000–320,000 DWT**).

---

### 1.4 Classification of Tankers (By Cargo Type)

| Class | Cargo Carried | Tank Coating | Key Design Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crude Oil Tanker | Raw petroleum | None (epoxy possible) | Cargo volume; longitudinal bulkhead to limit sloshing |
| Product Tanker | Refined petroleum (diesel, jet fuel) | Often coated | Multi-grade segregation |
| Chemical Tanker | Acids, alcohols, veg oils | Stainless steel / phenolic / zinc | High corrosion resistance |
| LNG Carrier | Liquefied Natural Gas (−163 °C) | Membrane or Moss spheres | Cryogenic insulation |
| OBO Carrier | Oil + dry bulk (ore/grain) | Coated/Clean | Two-mode operation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Philosophy — The Double Hull

A modern KTU-mandated study of tankers must start with the **double hull concept**. The double hull is a structural double-bottom and double-side arrangement that provides:
1. A redundant barrier between cargo and sea (environmental protection).
2. Reserve buoyancy in case of bottom/side damage.
3. Spaces for ballast water (clean, segregated from cargo).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 19** mandates double hulls for all new tankers of **5000 DWT and above** delivered after 6 July 1996. KTU questions frequently test this threshold value.

### 2.2 General Arrangement (GA) of a Typical Crude Oil Tanker

A modern crude oil tanker's midship section contains the following zones, listed from the shell plating inward:

- **Outer Shell (Side & Bottom plating)**
- **Double Side Tanks (DST)** — ballast spaces, **2 m wide**
- **Inner Hull (Hopper & Side Bulkhead)**
- **Cargo Tanks (sloshing-managed, with longitudinal bulkhead)**
- **Centreline Bulkhead** — for tankers above a certain beam

A longitudinal cross-section reveals the symmetric arrangement: shell → DST → inner hull → cargo tank → centerline bulkhead → cargo tank → inner hull → DST → shell.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Cargo Discharge

The discharge sequence of a tanker is a critical KTU exam topic:

1. **Stripping** — main cargo pump removes bulk oil down to the **stripping level** (about 10–15 cm above tank bottom).
2. **Edulcor (Stripping Eductor)** — a vacuum/eductor system recovers oil below the main pump suction.
3. **Final Cleaning** — fixed or portable cleaning machines (Butterworth type) wash residual oil from tank walls.
4. **Slop Management** — washings are routed to the **slop tank** for separation and disposal ashore.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The **International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers and Terminals (ISGOTT)** defines the *stripping quantity* as the volume of cargo remaining in a tank after the main cargo pump has run dry.

### 2.4 Free Surface Effect (Critical for Tanker Stability)

Because cargo tanks are large, partly filled rectangular spaces, they pose a severe **free surface effect (FSE)** hazard. The effective metacentric height (GM) is reduced:

$$GM_{eff} = GM - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\rho_i \cdot i_i}{\Delta}$$

where:
- $\rho_i$ = density of liquid in tank *i*
- $i_i$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free surface of tank *i*
- $\Delta$ = total ship displacement

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do *not* confuse $i_i$ (free surface moment, in m⁴) with the **tank's sectional moment of inertia**. Use only the area of the *free surface* (the tank top area at the liquid level), not the tank's vertical cross-section.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta$ | Displacement | $V \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | tonnes (t) |
| $TPC$ | Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion | $\dfrac{A_W}{100}$ | t/cm |
| $MCT$ | Moment to Change Trim 1 cm | $\dfrac{I_L \cdot \rho_{sw}}{100 \cdot L}$ | t·m/cm |
| $BM_T$ | Transverse metacentre radius | $\dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | m |
| $GM$ | Metacentric height | $KB + BM - KG$ | m |
| $GM_{eff}$ | Effective GM with FSE | $GM - \dfrac{\rho \cdot i_s}{\Delta}$ | m |
| $i_s$ | Free surface moment | $\dfrac{L \cdot b^3}{12}$ (rect. tank) | m⁴ |
| $M_{max}$ | Sloshing Bending Moment (analytical) | Per DNV / ABS rules | kN·m |
| $Q$ | Cargo flow rate | $\dfrac{V_{cargo}}{t_{discharge}}$ | m³/h |
| $P_{pump}$ | Stripping pump head | $\rho g H_{strip}$ | Pa |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The absolute-value-style bars inside table cells (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are intentionally avoided. All delimiters use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve the markdown table integrity required by KTU e-content rules.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tanker design directly feeds into:
- **Hydrodynamic loading** (sloshing-induced bending moments) which feed classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS).
- **Accident consequence modelling** (e.g., the *Erika* and *Prestige* spills drove the EU's **COZOLIP** double-hull directive).
- **Cargo–ballast trade-off optimisation** in voyage planning software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Free Surface Effect on Effective GM

**Statement to Prove:** A partially filled rectangular cargo tank reduces the ship's effective metacentric height by the term $\dfrac{\rho \cdot i_s}{\Delta}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Free Surface Moment.**

For a rectangular free surface of length $L$ and breadth $b$, the transverse second moment of area is:

$$i_s = \frac{L \cdot b^3}{12}$$

**Reasoning:** A liquid free surface tilts under ship roll. The shift in the centre of gravity of the liquid creates a destabilising righting-moment that effectively *reduces* the metacentric height.

**Step 2 — Virtual Rise of G.**

When the ship rolls by a small angle $\phi$, the liquid wedge shifts. The virtual rise in G is:

$$GG' = \frac{\rho \cdot i_s}{\Delta}$$

**Step 3 — Effective GM.**

Since $GG'$ acts to reduce the metacentric height, the effective metacentric height becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{eff} &= GM - GG' \\
GM_{eff} &= GM - \frac{\rho \cdot i_s}{\Delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multi-Tank Extension.**

For $n$ partially filled tanks, the losses add up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{eff} &= GM - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\rho_j \cdot i_{s,j}}{\Delta} \\
&= GM - \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \rho_j \cdot \frac{L_j b_j^3}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In Part B 14-mark questions, students are often asked to compute the *cumulative* free surface correction across all cargo tanks. Always include **units** at each step.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: TPC and Displacement Relationship

**Statement:** TPC is the change in displacement per centimetre of parallel sinkage.

**Step 1 — Displacement Change per Centimetre.**

If the waterplane area is $A_W$ (in m²), then for a 1 cm (0.01 m) sinkage, the added displaced volume is $A_W \cdot 0.01$ m³.

**Step 2 — Convert to Tonnes Using Sea Water Density.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass added} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot A_W \cdot 0.01 \text{ tonnes} \\
&= 1.025 \cdot \frac{A_W}{100} \text{ tonnes} \\
TPC &= \frac{A_W}{100} \quad \text{(using } \rho_{sw} \approx 1 \text{ t/m³ in fresh, 1.025 in salt)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sloshing Bending Moment (Simplified Analytical)

For a rectangular cargo tank of length $L_t$, the fundamental sloshing period is:

$$T_{slosh} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L_t}{g}}$$

**Step 1 — Tuning Check.**

Tuning with the ship roll period $T_{roll}$ must be avoided:

$$\frac{T_{slosh}}{T_{roll}} \notin [0.8, 1.2]$$

**Step 2 — Hydrodynamic Bending Moment.**

Using DNV simplified method, the equivalent static bending moment induced by sloshing impact is:

$$
M_{slosh} = K \cdot \rho_{cargo} \cdot g \cdot L_t^2 \cdot h_{ullage}
$$

where $K$ is a coefficient depending on fill ratio, and $h_{ullage}$ is the empty space above the liquid.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Part B 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A product tanker has three centre tanks each of length $L = 18$ m, breadth $b = 12$ m, and is half-filled with diesel (ρ = 850 kg/m³). The ship displacement Δ = 25 000 t. Given the intact GM = 0.80 m, compute the *effective* GM.

**Solution.**

Step 1 — Free surface moment of one tank:

$$
i_s = \frac{L \cdot b^3}{12} = \frac{18 \cdot 12^3}{12} = 18 \cdot 144 = 2592 \text{ m}^4
$$

Step 2 — Total free surface moment for three tanks:

$$
\sum i_s = 3 \times 2592 = 7776 \text{ m}^4
$$

Step 3 — Virtual rise in G (convert units carefully — Δ in tonnes = 25 000 t = 25 000 000 kg):

$$
GG' = \frac{\rho \cdot \sum i_s}{\Delta} = \frac{850 \times 7776}{25\,000\,000}
$$

$$
GG' = \frac{6\,609\,600}{25\,000\,000} = 0.2644 \text{ m}
$$

Step 4 — Effective GM:

$$
GM_{eff} = 0.80 - 0.264 = 0.536 \text{ m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The free surface effect has eroded ~33% of the original metacentric height. The vessel is still positively stable, but margin is critically low — a KTU examiner will award full marks only if the student comments on the operational implication.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert Δ from tonnes to kilograms (or vice versa) is the #1 mark-losing mistake. Use *consistent SI units* throughout. Δ in kg, ρ in kg/m³, i in m⁴ → GG′ in metres.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Tanker Type

| Selection Criterion | Crude Oil Tanker | Chemical Tanker | LNG Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
