**Step 1 — Compute the basic GM (without free surface correction).**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 2.80 + 1.60 - 5.20 = -0.80 \ \text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative GM!** The ship is **unstable** in this condition. We must apply the free surface correction and possibly redistribute or ballast the cargo.

**Step 2 — Compute the free surface correction $GG'$.**

$$
GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{fo} \cdot i_{fs}}{\Delta}
$$

Note: $i_{fs}$ for a rectangular free surface = $\dfrac{\ell \cdot b^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^3}{12} = 1000 \ \text{m}^4$ (this is the *idealized* value; the actual $i_{fs}$ includes baffling effects — here we use the given 1,440 m⁴ to account for un-baffled tank).

$$
GG' = \dfrac{0.95 \times 1440}{7500} = \dfrac{1368}{7500} = 0.1824 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective GM.**

$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - GG' = -0.80 - 0.1824 = -0.9824 \ \text{m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ship is **doubly unstable** — the negative $GM$ is *worsened* by the slack tank.

**Step 4 — Required corrective action.**

To restore stability, we need $GM_{\text{eff}} \geq 0.15$ m. Therefore we must **lower KG** by shifting cargo to lower decks or **fill the fuel tank completely** (eliminating free surface, so $GG' \to 0$).

If we fill the tank, the fuel oil mass becomes:

$$
m_{fo} = \rho_{fo} \cdot \ell \cdot b \cdot h = 0.95 \times 12 \times 10 \times 1.2 = 136.8 \ \text{t}
$$

This added mass at, say, $z = 1.5$ m above keel lowers the combined $KG$ by:

$$
\Delta KG \approx \dfrac{m_{fo} \cdot (KG_{\text{old}} - 1.5)}{\Delta + m_{fo}} = \dfrac{136.8 \times (5.20 - 1.5)}{7636.8} = 0.0662 \ \text{m}
$$

New $KG \approx 5.134$ m, and $GG' = 0$ (no free surface), giving:

$$
GM_{\text{eff, new}} = 2.80 + 1.60 - 5.134 = -0.734 \ \text{m}
$$

Still negative → **more aggressive ballasting required.** This illustrates why supply ships carefully plan load sequence and ballasting at every port.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Supply Ship Types

| Parameter | **PSV** | **AHTS** | **FSV / Crew Boat** | **Naval AOR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical LOA | 70–95 m | 60–90 m | 25–50 m | 150–230 m |
| DWT | 3,000–6,000 t | 1,500–3,500 t | 50–300 t | 15,000–45,000 t |
| Deck Area | 600–1,200 m² | 300–600 m² | 50–150 m² | 1,500–3,000 m² |
| Bollard Pull | 30–60 t | 60–120 t | 5–15 t | 30–60 t |
| Service Speed | 12–14 kn | 12–14 kn | 25–35 kn | 16–20 kn |
| Block Coeff. $C_B$ | 0.68–0.75 | 0.65–0.72 | 0.45–0.55 | 0.60–0.70 |
| DP Class | DP-1 / DP-2 | DP-2 | DP-0 / DP-1 | — (UNREP) |
| Main Engines | 2 × 2,500 kW | 2 × 3,000+ kW | 2 × 2,000 kW (waterjet) | 2 × 10,000 kW |
| Key Cargo | Drill mud, cement, base oil, food, water | Same + towing winch | Personnel, mail, light stores | Fuel, ammo, stores |
| Hull Form | Round-bilge, U-shaped | Round-bilge, fuller midship | Deep-V planing | Conventional cruiser stern |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern PSV

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional subsystems** of a Platform Supply Vessel — the student should be able to sketch this on a KTU answer script and label each block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Environment: Wind, Waves, Current] --> B[Hull Form: Round Bilge, High Freeboard]
    A --> C[Aerodynamic Loads on Superstructure]
    B --> D[Hull Resistance and Propulsion Block]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Main Engines 2 x 2500 kW]
    E --> F[Controllable Pitch Propellers CPP]
    F --> G[Flanking Rudders and Bow Thruster]
    G --> H[DP Controller Module]
    H --> I[DP Reference Sensors: DGPS, Gyro, MRU]
    I --> H
    H --> G
    B --> J[Cargo Handling Block]
    J --> K[Deck Crane Pedestal 1]
    J --> L[Deck Crane Pedestal 2]
    J --> M[Pipe Reel and Mud Tank System]
    J --> N[ROV Hangar and Moonpool LARS]
    B --> O[Tankage and Stability Block]
    O --> P[Fresh Water Tanks]
    O --> Q[Marine Fuel Oil Tanks]
    O --> R[Base Oil and Brine Tanks]
    O --> S[Ballast Tanks]
    O --> T[Stability Computer with Free Surface Correction]
    T --> O
    B --> U[Habitability Block: Crew Quarters, Hospital, Galley]
    B --> V[Safety and Survival Block: FFA, Lifeboats, EPIRBs]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Cargo Transfer at an Offshore Platform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Vessel approaches within 2 NM] --> S2[Step 2: DP system activated DP-2]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Position reference verified DGPS, HPR, Fanbeam]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Crane operator transfers first load deck to platform]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Continuous monitoring of stability GM and draft]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Ballast water exchange as cargo unloads]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Departure and transit to next call]
```

## 4.3 Hull Form Cross-Section (Schematic — Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AboveWaterline
        X1[Superstructure: Bridge, Cabins, Funnel]
        X2[Main Deck: Cargo Area with Crane Pedestals]
        X3[Freeboard Deck Edge]
    end
    subgraph AtWaterline
        Y1[Design Waterline LWL]
        Y2[Waterplane Area AWP for Stability]
    end
    subgraph BelowWaterline
        Z1[Bilge: Round Shape, Block Coeff 0.72]
        Z2[Double Bottom: Ballast and Fuel Tanks]
        Z3[Propeller and Rudder Aperture]
    end
    X3 --- Y1
    Y1 --- Z1
    Z1 --- Z2
    Z2 --- Z3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When asked to *sketch and label* a supply ship in the exam, always include: (1) bulbous bow, (2) high freeboard bow with flare, (3) clear aft deck, (4) two pedestal cranes, (5) DP equipment dome on bow, (6) azimuth thruster tunnel forward.

## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (Verbal — for Answer Book)

Since physical hull lines cannot be drawn in Mermaid, here is the **textual schematic** of a typical PSV side profile (left-to-right, bow to stern):

> **[BOW]** Bulbous bow below waterline → Flared forecastle (high freeboard, houses anchor windlass and chain locker) → Foremast with navigation lights and DP sensor mast → Superstructure block (3–4 decks: bridge top, crew cabins, engine room ventilation) → Two pedestal deck cranes amidships (port & starboard) → Clear working deck (no masts, no obstructions — VDL marked in 1 t/m² zones) → Pipe racks and reel drums at centerline → Funnel → Helideck at stern (certified for Sikorsky S-92 class) → **[STERN]** Transom with azimuth thruster and towing winch drum on AHTS variant.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define a Platform Supply Vessel (PSV) and list its three main cargo categories.**

**Model Answer:**
A Platform Supply Vessel (PSV) is a specialized offshore vessel designed to transport and transfer dry and liquid cargoes from shore bases to offshore oil & gas platforms. Its three main cargo categories are:
1. **Liquid cargoes:** Drilling mud, cement slurries, base oil, brine, fresh water, marine fuel oil.
2. **Dry cargoes:** Drill pipes, casings, cement bags, spare parts, food and provisions.
3. **Containerized/Unit cargoes:** ISO containers, pallets, hazardous stores in IMO-approved packaging.

> **Cognitive Level:** Remember (L1). **CO Mapping:** CO1 – Fundamentals of Floating Bodies.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is "Variable Deck Load" (VDL) in a supply ship? Why is it critical for offshore operations?**

**Model Answer:**
Variable Deck Load (VDL) is the maximum distributed cargo load that can be safely carried on the exposed working deck of a supply ship, expressed in tonnes per square meter (typically 5–7.5 t/m² as approved by the classification society).

It is **critical** because:
- Deck cargo is **concentrated and dynamic** (e.g., drill pipes can shift during transit in waves).
- Exceeding VDL risks **structural failure** of deck plating, girders, and stiffeners.
- VDL directly affects the ship's **stability** — high VDL raises the vertical center of gravity (KG) and can reduce the metacentric height (GM) below safe limits.
- Class rules (DNV NAUT-OSV) require marking VDL zones clearly on deck.

> **Cognitive Level:** Understand (L2). **CO Mapping:** CO1, CO2.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the two sub-questions (a) and (b) **OR** the alternative Question B in entirety.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A (a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the general arrangement and main features of a modern Platform Supply Vessel (PSV). Highlight the function of the working deck, the pedestal cranes, and the dynamic positioning system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. General Layout (3 marks):**
A modern PSV is a monohull vessel of length 70–95 m, with the following longitudinal zones:
- **Bow zone:** Bulbous bow, high flared forecastle, chain locker, forward azimuth thruster tunnel.
- **Forward accommodation block:** Bridge (top), officer cabins, galley, mess room, hospital.
- **Mid-ship working deck:** Clear, unobstructed deck 40–60 m long, fitted with two pedestal cranes (typically 30-tonne SWL each) port and starboard, pipe racks, reel drums, and a moonpool (in some vessels).
- **Engine room and funnel:** Two main engines, CPP shaft lines, generators.
- **Stern zone:** Helideck, towing winch (if AHTS), emergency accommodation.

**2. Working Deck (2 marks):**
The clear working deck is the operational heart of the PSV. It must:
- Provide maximum unobstructed area (no king posts or masts on the cargo deck).
- Withstand a uniform **VDL of 5–7.5 t/m²**.
- Be fitted with flush **sockets for cargo lashings** (typically D-rings every 2 m).
- Have **non-skid coating** and **drainage scuppers** for safe operation at sea.

**3. Pedestal Cranes (1 mark):**
Two deck cranes (e.g., NOV knuckle-boom or Liebherr) are mounted on raised pedestals at the forward edge of the working deck. They are **offshore-rated**, with certified Sea State 3–4 operating limits, and have **heave compensation** to safely transfer loads to a moving platform.

**4. DP System (1 mark):**
A computer-controlled system using **azimuth thrusters** (fore and aft) and **reference sensors** (DGPS, Hydroacoustic Position Reference – HPR, Fanbeam, taut-wire) holds the vessel within ±2 m of a target point despite wind/wave/current, enabling safe cargo transfer without mooring.

---

**Q.A (b)** A supply ship has the following particulars at loaded condition:
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{pp} = 75$ m
- Beam $B = 16$ m$
- Draft $T = 5.0$ m
- Block coefficient $C_B = 0.70$
- $\text{Waterplane area} \ A_{WP} = 1,080 \ \text{m}^2$
- $KB = 2.50$ m, $KG = 4.80$ m, $BM = 1.40$ m
- Density of seawater = $1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3$

Calculate:
(i) The displacement in tonnes.
(ii) The metacentric height $GM$.
(iii) The new draft after loading an additional 1,500 t of deck cargo (assume parallel-sided hull). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Displacement:**

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.70 \times 75 \times 16 \times 5.0
$$

Step-by-step:
- $0.70 \times 75 = 52.5$
- $52.5 \times 16 = 840$
- $840 \times 5.0 = 4,200 \ \text{m}^3$

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 4200 = 4305 \ \text{t} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Metacentric height:**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 2.50 + 1.40 - 4.80 = -0.90 \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students forget the correct sign convention: $GM = KB + BM - KG$. A *negative* answer means the ship is **unstable** at this loading — the examiner awards **0 marks** if you simply write the final number without comment. Always interpret the result.

**(iii) New draft after adding 1,500 t:**

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{\text{Cargo mass}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}} = \dfrac{1500}{1.025 \times 1080} = \dfrac{1500}{1107} = 1.355 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = 5.0 + 1.355 = 6.355 \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall #2:** Do not write $T_{\text{new}} = 6.35$ m and stop. Always state the **change in draft** ($\Delta T$) AND the new draft separately. Many students lose 1 mark for missing the wording.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the classification of offshore supply vessels. Compare in detail the features of a PSV and an AHTS, giving typical parameters for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of OSVs (3 marks):**
Offshore support vessels are classified by function:
- **Platform Supply Vessel (PSV)** — Cargo transfer.
- **Anchor Handling Tug Supply (AHTS)** — Towing, anchor handling, positioning.
- **Fast Supply Vessel / Crew Boat (FSV)** — Personnel transfer, urgent spares.
- **ROV/DSV Support Vessel** — Subsea intervention.
- **Cable Lay & Pipe Lay Vessels** — Subsea installation.
- **Well Stimulation / Acidizing Vessel** — Chemical treatment of wells.
- **Standby Safety Vessel (SSV)** — Emergency response, oil spill containment.
- **SOV (Service Operation Vessel)** — Wind farm support (newest class).

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | **PSV** | **AHTS** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary role | Cargo transport | Towing & anchor handling |
| Deck space | **Very large** (clear, open) | **Moderate** (winch takes ~30% of deck) |
| Bollard pull | 30–60 t | 60–120 t |
| Crane | 2 × pedestal cranes | 1 × crane + 1 × towing winch |
| Engine power | 2 × 2,000–2,500 kW | 2 × 3,000–4,000 kW |
| Stern | Open transom, shark jaw/towing pins | Shark jaw, stern roller, winch drum |
| Speed | 12–14 kn | 12–14 kn |
| Typical DWT | 3,000–6,000 t | 1,500–3,500 t |
| DP class | DP-1 or DP-2 | DP-2 (mandatory for rig moves) |

**3. Distinguishing features (1 mark):**
The AHTS is uniquely characterized by its **towing winch** (200–400 t line pull) and **shark jaws / stern roller** for handling rig chains. The PSV is characterized by a **maximally clear aft deck** and **large VDL** capacity.

---

**Q.B (b)** A supply ship has a displacement of 5,000 t in seawater. The waterplane area is 1,200 m². A fuel oil tank of length 10 m and width 8 m is **partially filled**, giving a free surface moment of inertia of 850 m⁴. The vertical center of gravity is 5.0 m, KB = 2.6 m, and BM = 1.2 m. Density of fuel oil = 0.95 t/m³.

Determine:
(i) The metacentric height $GM$ without free surface correction.
(ii) The free surface correction $GG'$.
(iii) The effective $GM_{\text{eff}}$ and comment on the ship's stability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) $GM$ without correction:**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 2.6 + 1.2 - 5.0 = -1.2 \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Free surface correction:**

$$
GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{fo} \cdot i_{fs}}{\Delta} = \dfrac{0.95 \times 850}{5000} = \dfrac{807.5}{5000} = 0.1615 \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Effective GM and comment:**

$$
GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - GG' = -1.2 - 0.1615 = -1.3615 \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The ship is **grossly unstable** in this condition. Both the basic $GM$ and the free surface effect are negative, so the ship will capsize at even a small angle of heel. **Required actions:**
1. Fill the fuel tank completely to eliminate the free surface.
2. Lower $KG$ by shifting heavy cargo to lower decks or adding bottom ballast.
3. Re-compute $GM$ until $GM_{\text{eff}} \geq 0.15$ m before sailing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign errors** are the #1 cause of lost marks. Always write $GM = KB + BM - KG$ and **substitute with units**.
> - **Free surface correction SUBTRACTS** from GM (always makes ship less stable). Students who write $GM + GG'$ will lose full marks.
> - **Final "comment"** is worth 1 mark in most KTU answer keys — never skip it.
> - **Density of seawater is 1.025 t/m³** in saline and 1.000 t/m³ in freshwater. The exam often gives "seawater" — don't default to 1.000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 2-Minute Revision Before Exam — Memorize These Anchors**

✅ **Supply Ship = floating grocery store + tow truck** for offshore platforms. Carries dry cargo, liquid mud, fresh water, fuel.

✅ **Four iconic types:** PSV (cargo), AHTS (tow), FSV (fast crew), Naval AOR (fleet replenishment).

✅ **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ is **0.65–0.75** for OSVs (high for PSV, lower for FSV).

✅ **Variable Deck Load (VDL)** is critical — typically 5–7.5 t/m² — and directly affects stability via $KG$.

✅ **Displacement formula** $\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$ — must know the **arrangement of variables**.

✅ **Metacentric height** $GM = KB + BM - KG$. **Always positive** for stable ship (typically $\geq 0.15$ m).

✅ **Free surface effect** $GG' = \dfrac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot i_{fs}}{\Delta}$. It **reduces** GM → fill all tanks or use baffled tanks.

✅ **Draft change** for added mass: $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}}$ (parallel-sided assumption).

✅ **Bollard Pull** is the **static tow force** — used for AHTS classification, not PSV.

✅ **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** classes: **DP-1, DP-2, DP-3** per IMO MSC/Circ.645. AHTS usually DP-2.

✅ **Helideck** at the stern is mandatory on all modern OSVs above 500 GRT per CAP 437 / ICAO standards.

✅ **Working deck must be clear** — no king posts, no masts on cargo area (to enable crane sweeps and helicopter safety).

✅ **Hull form:** round bilge, high freeboard bow (flare), transom stern, bulbous bow for reduced resistance.

✅ **Key constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², 1 nm = 1852 m, TPC $\approx A_{WP}/100 \times 1.025$ t/cm.

✅ **Classification society rules** referenced: DNV NAUT-OSV, ABS OSV Guide, Lloyd's SSCV, IRS OSV.

✅ **Froude Number** $F_n$ governs wave pattern; OSVs operate in **displacement regime** ($F_n < 0.4$).

✅ **Difference between $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_WP$:** $C_B$ uses full volume; $C_P$ uses mid-ship area; $C_WP$ uses waterplane area. They are **NOT** interchangeable.

✅ **In KTU 2024 ESE**, every stability problem must end with a **comment line** ("the ship is stable / unstable / marginally stable") to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cable Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cable Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cable Ship** (also called a **Cable Layer** or **Cable Repair Vessel**) is a specially designed marine vessel equipped with highly sophisticated machinery, large storage tanks, and precision deck equipment for the **laying, burying, repairing, recovering, and maintenance of submarine cables** on the ocean floor. These cables include submarine telecommunication cables (fiber optic and coaxial), power transmission cables, and scientific/observatory cables.

According to the **International Cable Protection Committee (ICPC)** and **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)** standards, a cable ship is classified as a vessel whose primary function is the handling of submarine cables, and its design must comply with **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) and **IMO** (International Maritime Organization) regulations while providing the stability, maneuverability, and station-keeping ability required for precise cable operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 (Cable Ships)**
> Students must be able to identify the major components of a cable ship, explain the operational sequence of cable laying and cable recovery, and describe the role of linear cable engines, dynamometers, cable tanks, bow sheaves, and stern sheaves in modern cable operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to lay a very long, very heavy garden hose at the bottom of a swimming pool — but the pool is **3,000 meters deep** and the hose is **5,000 kilometers long**. You cannot simply drop it; you must control the speed at which it leaves your hand, maintain a constant tension so it does not snap, navigate around underwater mountains, and remember exactly where you put it. A **cable ship is the industrial, ocean-going equivalent of that person holding the hose**, except it carries thousands of tons of cable in massive onboard tanks, has computer-controlled engines that pay out cable at the precise tension and speed, and uses GPS and sonar to place the cable exactly where it is supposed to go.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cable Ships Matter in Modern Engineering**
> Over **95% of international data traffic** (internet, financial transactions, phone calls) is carried by submarine fiber-optic cables, not satellites. Without cable ships, the modern internet and global power grids would simply cease to function. They are the unsung backbone of globalization.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are essential for understanding cable ship operations:

- **Service Speed**: Typically **12 – 16 knots** (≈ 6 – 8 m/s) for transit
- **Cable Laying Speed**: Typically **1 – 6 knots** (≈ 0.5 – 3 m/s), depending on depth and cable type
- **Maximum Operating Depth**: Modern vessels can lay cable at depths up to **8,000 – 10,000 meters**
- **Cable Tank Capacity**: Ranges from **500 tonnes** (small repair ships) to **9,000+ tonnes** (large cable layers like *René Descartes* of Orange Marine)
- **Linear Cable Engine Tension Capacity**: Typically **2 – 40 tonnes** of controllable braking/paying tension
- **Cable Diameter Range**: From **~17 mm** (lightweight telecom) to **~150 mm** (heavy-armored power cables)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of a Cable Ship

A cable ship is functionally a **mobile cable-handling factory** combining four engineering systems:

1. **Storage System** — Cable tanks (cylindrical or conical) holding thousands of kilometers of cable.
2. **Drive System** — Linear cable engines, drums, and capstans that pay out or recover cable at controlled tensions.
3. **Routing System** — Bow sheaves, stern sheaves, cable guides, and quadrant blocks that direct the cable over the hull.
4. **Monitoring & Control System** — Dynamometers, tensiometers, payout counters, and computer-based cable engine controllers (often using SCADA or PLC systems).

### 2.2 Key Operational Phases

A cable ship's lifecycle operation can be divided into **four distinct phases**:

- **Phase 1 — Loading**: Cable is loaded into tanks at the cable factory dockside using specialized cranes.
- **Phase 2 — Laying (Payout)**: Ship sails along the pre-surveyed route; cable is paid out over bow or stern sheave at a speed that matches ship speed, maintaining a controlled catenary shape in the water column.
- **Phase 3 — Jointing / Splicing**: At mid-ocean joints, the ship stops, recovers both cable ends, and a jointing operation (splicing) is performed on deck in a clean, climate-controlled hut.
- **Phase 4 — Repair / Recovery**: A faulty cable is grappled from the seabed, cut, and the working end is brought on deck for repair.

### 2.3 Critical Engineering Parameters

During laying, the cable descends through the water column in a **catenary shape**, governed by the equilibrium of three forces:

- **Weight of suspended cable** (gravity)
- **Hydrodynamic drag** (water resistance)
- **Ship's forward motion** (horizontal component)

The shape of the catenary, the tension at the touchdown point, and the tension at the ship are critical because:

- If **tension is too high** → cable may snap, especially at weak points (joints, repeaters).
- If **tension is too low** → cable may form kinks, loops, or backlashes on the seabed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Symbol** | **Description** | **Typical Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catenary Tension at Ship | $T_s = W \cdot d \cdot \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{d}\right)$ | Where $W$ = weight per unit length in water, $d$ = horizontal distance, $h$ = depth | Newtons (N) or tonnes (t) |
| Sag Ratio (KS) | $KS = \dfrac{h}{T_s / W}$ | Ratio of depth to top tension; controls catenary shape | Dimensionless |
| Minimum Top Tension (Catenary) | $T_{s,min} = W \cdot h \cdot 1.5$ to $2.0$ (empirical) | Empirical lower bound to avoid kinking | N or t |
| Linear Cable Engine Capacity | $T_{LCE}$ | Must exceed $T_s$ for safe control | tonnes (t) |
| Weight in Water | $W = w_{air} - \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A$ | $w_{air}$ = dry weight per unit length, $A$ = cross-sectional area | N/m |
| Payout Speed | $V_p \approx V_{ship}$ | Cable leaves ship at the same speed as ship advances | m/s or knots |
| Touchdown Point Tension | $T_{TD} = W \cdot h$ | Tension at the seabed contact point | N or t |
| Suspended Cable Length (Catenary) | $L_s = d \cdot \sinh\left(\dfrac{x}{d}\right)$ | Length of cable hanging in water column | meters (m) |
| Specific Gravity of Cable in Water | $SG_w = \dfrac{w_{air}}{A \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g}$ | Used to determine sinking behavior | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> The **KS ratio** (often called the *sag-to-tension ratio*) is the single most important design parameter in cable laying. A typical target is **KS = 0.7 to 1.0** for telecom cables in deep water, ensuring the catenary is neither too flat (risk of snap-back) nor too steep (excessive ship tension).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cable ship design principles extend to:

- **Offshore renewable energy** — submarine power cable installation for offshore wind farms
- **Oil & Gas industry** — umbilical and riser installation
- **Oceanographic science** — deployment of seafloor observatories (e.g., NEPTUNE, DONET)
- **Submarine pipeline** — although pipelines use specialized lay barges, the catenary mechanics are identical

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Catenary Equation for a Suspended Cable

The catenary is the equilibrium shape of a flexible, inextensible cable suspended between two points under its own weight. For a submarine cable being paid out from a cable ship, we consider a **cable hanging in seawater**, where the effective weight per unit length is reduced by buoyancy.

**Step 1 — Define the coordinate system**

Let the origin be at the lowest point of the catenary (the touchdown point on the seabed). The x-axis is horizontal, and the y-axis is vertical (positive upward). The cable extends from the touchdown point up to the ship's sheave at height $h$ above the seabed.

**Step 2 — Free-body diagram of a small cable element**

Consider a small element of cable of arc length $ds$ at position $(x, y)$. The weight of this element in seawater is:

$$dW = w \cdot ds$$

where $w$ is the **effective weight per unit length in seawater** (N/m). The tension $T$ at any point acts tangentially to the cable. At the lowest point, the tension is purely horizontal and equal to $T_0$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of horizontal force component**

Since there are no horizontal external forces (ignoring water drag for the static case), the horizontal component of tension is constant throughout the cable:

$$T \cos\theta = T_0$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of vertical force component**

The vertical component of tension must support the weight of cable from the lowest point to position $x$:

$$T \sin\theta = w \cdot s$$

where $s$ is the arc length from the lowest point.

**Step 5 — Eliminate the parameter $\theta$**

Dividing the vertical equation by the horizontal equation:

$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{ds}{dx} = \dfrac{w \cdot s}{T_0}$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate and integrate**

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{w}{T_0} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

This is a standard differential equation whose solution is the **catenary equation**:

$$y = a \cdot \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right) - a$$

where $a = T_0 / w$ is the **catenary constant** (with units of length).

**Step 7 — Top tension at the ship (the sheave point)**

The tension at any point on the catenary is:

$$T(x) = w \cdot y + T_0 = w \cdot a \cdot \cosh\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)$$

At the ship's sheave, $y = h$ and $x$ corresponds to the horizontal projection:

$$T_{ship} = w \cdot a \cdot \cosh\left(\dfrac{x_{ship}}{a}\right)$$

Expanding using $a \cdot \cosh(x/a) = a + h$:

$$T_{ship} = w \cdot (a + h) = T_0 + w \cdot h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Result**
> The top tension at the ship is simply the sum of the **horizontal tension at touchdown** ($T_0$) and the **weight of the entire suspended cable** ($w \cdot h$). This is the tension the linear cable engine must control.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A submarine fiber-optic cable has a weight in seawater of $w = 15$ N/m. It is being laid in a water depth of $h = 3{,}000$ m. The ship speed is 4 knots and the design horizontal tension at touchdown is $T_0 = 9{,}000$ N. Calculate:
- (a) The catenary constant
- (b) The top tension at the ship
- (c) The KS ratio

**Solution (Step-by-step)**:

**(a) Catenary constant:**

$$a = \dfrac{T_0}{w} = \dfrac{9{,}000}{15} = 600 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Top tension at the ship:**

$$T_{ship} = T_0 + w \cdot h = 9{,}000 + (15)(3{,}000) = 9{,}000 + 45{,}000 = 54{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne-force ≈ 9,810 N):

$$T_{ship} = \dfrac{54{,}000}{9{,}810} \approx 5.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(c) KS ratio:**

$$KS = \dfrac{h}{T_{ship} / w} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{54{,}000 / 15} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{3{,}600} \approx 0.833$$

Since $KS = 0.833$ lies in the **recommended range of 0.7 – 1.0**, the laying configuration is **safe and acceptable**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cable Laying vs. Recovery

| **Parameter** | **Cable Laying (Payout)** | **Cable Recovery (Pickup)** |
|---|---|---|
| Cable Engine Mode | Brake mode (controlled drag) | Drive mode (powered pull) |
| Tension Direction | Tension at ship **resists** cable weight | Tension at ship **overcomes** cable weight + seabed friction |
| Typical Speed | 1 – 6 knots | 0.5 – 2 knots (slower, careful) |
| Risk | Snap-back if cable parts | Cable fatigue, drum crushing |
| Critical Parameter | Maintain $T_{ship} > T_{min}$ | Avoid tension overshoot (shock loads) |
| Equipment Status | Linear engine acts as brake | Linear engine acts as motor |
| Dynamic Effects | Bow wave, ship heave | Seabed suction, cable entanglement |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Major Functional Systems of a Cable Ship

The following block diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a modern cable ship, showing how the major subsystems interact during a typical cable-laying operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bridge and Navigation Control] --> B[Dynamic Positioning System DP]
    B --> C[Ship Hull and Propulsion]
    A --> D[Cable Operations Control Room]
    D --> E[Linear Cable Engine LCE]
    D --> F[Dynamometer Tension Sensor]
    D --> G[Cable Payout Counter]
    D --> H[Touchdown Monitor]
    E --> I[Bow Sheave or Stern Sheave]
    I --> J[Submarine Cable Catenary in Water]
    J --> K[Seabed Touchdown Point]
    E --> L[Cable Tank Drum 1]
    E --> M[Cable Tank Drum 2]
    E --> N[Cable Tank Drum 3]
    F --> D
    G --> D
    H --> D
    L --> E
    M --> E
    N --> E
    C --> B
    K --> O[Submarine Cable Route]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Cable Laying Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Pre-Lay Survey of Route] --> P2[Load Cable into Tanks at Cable Factory]
    P2 --> P3[Transit to Start of Cable Route]
    P3 --> P4[Deploy Buoy at Starting End]
    P4 --> P5[Begin Cable Payout at Controlled Tension]
    P5 --> P6[Maintain Catenary using LCE Brake]
    P6 --> P7{Is Mid-Ocean Joint Required?}
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Stop Ship, Splice Cable Onboard]
    P7 -->|No| P9[Continue Laying]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Monitor Touchdown Tension and Position]
    P10 --> P11[Reach Beach Manhole End]
    P11 --> P12[Cut Cable and Float to Shore]
    P12 --> P13[Complete Laying - Post-Lay Inspection]
```

### 4.3 Equipment Layout — Cross-Section of a Cable Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Bridge Top] --> Q2[Upper Deck with Cable Guides]
    Q2 --> Q3[Main Deck with Cable Engine Room]
    Q3 --> Q4[Cable Tank 1 - Forward]
    Q3 --> Q5[Cable Tank 2 - Midship]
    Q3 --> Q6[Cable Tank 3 - Aft]
    Q3 --> Q7[Linear Cable Engine and Drum]
    Q7 --> Q8[Stern Sheave Assembly]
    Q8 --> Q9[Over the Stern Cable Exit]
    Q9 --> Q10[Water Column and Catenary]
    Q10 --> Q11[Seabed Touchdown]
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction Matrix

| **Component** | **Function** | **Interacts With** | **Failure Consequence** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bow Sheave | Redirects cable over the bow for forward laying | Cable engine, seabed cable | Cable chafe, parting |
| Stern Sheave | Redirects cable over the stern for stern laying | Cable engine, seabed cable | Cable chafe, parting |
| Linear Cable Engine (LCE) | Provides controlled tension during payout/recovery | Cable tank, dynamometer, control room | Loss of tension control |
| Dynamometer | Real-time tension measurement | LCE, control room | Over-tension or slack events |
| Cable Tank | Stores thousands of km of cable | LCE, loading crane | Tangle, crush damage |
| Dynamic Positioning (DP) | Holds ship on station during jointing | Bridge, thrusters | Drifting off route |
| Cable Payout Counter | Tracks length of cable paid out | Control room, GPS | Cable shortage, overrun |
| Touchdown Monitor | Monitors cable touchdown point on seabed | ROV, sonar, control room | Misplaced cable, snags |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a cable ship and state any two of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer**:
A **cable ship** is a specially designed marine vessel equipped with machinery, storage tanks, and sheave systems for laying, recovering, repairing, and maintaining submarine cables on the ocean floor.

**Two primary functions**:
1. **Cable Laying (Payout)**: Paying out submarine telecommunication or power cable along a pre-surveyed route, maintaining a controlled catenary shape through the water column.
2. **Cable Recovery / Repair**: Grappling a damaged cable from the seabed, bringing it on deck, and performing splicing or replacement operations.

*(Valid alternates: jointing, cable burial, route survey support)* — **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is a linear cable engine? State its role during cable laying.**

**Model Answer**:
A **Linear Cable Engine (LCE)** is the principal deck machinery on a cable ship, consisting of a pair of motor-driven wheels (or caterpillars) that grip the cable and either **pay it out under controlled braking tension** or **pull it in under controlled drive tension**.

**Role during cable laying**: It acts as a **variable brake** that holds back the cable against the weight of the suspended catenary, ensuring the cable leaves the ship at a controlled tension that prevents both snapping (over-tension) and slack loops on the seabed (under-tension). — **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **major functional systems of a cable ship** and describe how the **linear cable engine, dynamometer, and cable tank** interact during a typical cable-laying operation.

**Model Answer**:

A modern cable ship is composed of **four major functional systems**, as illustrated in the block diagram below.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Cable Storage System: Cable Tanks] --> R2[Drive System: Linear Cable Engine LCE]
    R2 --> R3[Routing System: Bow or Stern Sheave]
    R3 --> R4[Monitoring System: Dynamometer and Payout Counter]
    R1 --> R4
    R2 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Control Room]
    R5 --> R2
```

**Interaction during laying**:

1. Cable is stored under controlled cone shape in the **cable tanks** (typically 2 – 3 tanks) and flanged out through tank openings.
2. The **Linear Cable Engine (LCE)** grips the cable between two motor-driven caterpillars and pays it out at a rate matching the ship's forward speed. In laying mode, the LCE acts as a **brake**, dissipating energy through water-cooled resistors or regenerative feedback.
3. The cable passes over a **sheave** (bow or stern) where a **dynamometer** continuously measures the actual tension. The signal is fed back to the LCE controller in real time.
4. The **control room** operator monitors the payout counter, GPS position, and seabed chart to keep the ship on the planned track.
5. As the ship advances, the cable forms a **catenary** in the water column and reaches the seabed at the **touchdown point**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> Students often forget to mention the **feedback loop** between the dynamometer and the LCE controller. Drawing an **arrow back from the dynamometer to the LCE** is essential to score full marks. The LCE is not just a passive brake — it is a **closed-loop controlled actuator**. *(Loss: up to 2 marks if omitted.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cable with weight in seawater $w = 20$ N/m is being laid in a depth of $h = 2{,}000$ m. The horizontal tension at the touchdown point is $T_0 = 8{,}000$ N. Calculate: (i) the catenary constant, (ii) the top tension at the ship, and (iii) the KS ratio. Comment on whether the laying configuration is safe.

**Model Answer**:

**(i) Catenary constant:**

$$a = \dfrac{T_0}{w} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{20} = 400 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Top tension at the ship:**

$$T_{ship} = T_0 + w \cdot h = 8{,}000 + (20)(2{,}000) = 8{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Converting:

$$T_{ship} = \dfrac{48{,}000}{9{,}810} \approx 4.89 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) KS ratio:**

$$KS = \dfrac{h}{T_{ship} / w} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{48{,}000 / 20} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{2{,}400} = 0.833$$

**Comment**: The KS ratio of **0.833** lies within the recommended design range of **0.7 – 1.0** for submarine cables. Therefore, the laying configuration is **safe** — the catenary is sufficiently slack to absorb ship motion and sufficiently taut to prevent loop formation on the seabed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **process of submarine cable recovery and repair** using a cable ship. List the major steps involved and explain the role of the **grapnel and the linear cable engine** in this operation.

**Model Answer**:

The process of cable recovery and repair is a multi-step operation:

1. **Locating the Fault**: A faulty cable is located using **OTDR** (Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer) signals from a shore station, which sends light pulses down the fiber and analyzes the backscatter to pinpoint the break.

2. **Deploying the Grapnel**: A specialized **grapnel** (a hooked grapnel weight or wheel grapnel) is lowered from the cable ship and dragged along the seabed. When it catches the cable, the tension rises sharply, indicating a successful grab.

3. **Bringing the Cable Onboard**: Once grappled, the cable is lifted to the surface using a **cutting grapnel** or **lifting rope** and routed over the bow or stern sheave into the LCE.

4. **Cutting and Buoying**: The recovered cable end is cut, sealed, and buoyed off so it does not sink back. The ship then **grapnels for the second end** at a small distance from the first cut.

5. **Jointing / Splicing**: Both ends are brought on deck. A new **cable insertion** (a length of fresh cable from the tank) is spliced between the two ends in a **clean, air-conditioned jointing hut** on deck.

6. **Re-laying and Lowering**: The repaired joint is re-laid over the sheave, the cable is gently lowered back to the seabed, and the second end is also spliced and lowered.

**Role of the Grapnel**: It is the **mechanical hook** that physically engages the cable on the seabed and allows the ship to lift it. Without the grapnel, no recovery is possible.

**Role of the Linear Cable Engine**: In recovery mode, the LCE acts as a **powered winch**, pulling the cable in from the seabed against gravity, hydrodynamic drag, and seabed suction. It must control tension to avoid **shock loads** (which occur when a stuck cable suddenly releases).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **laying** and **recovery** operations of a cable ship. Highlight at least **four differences** with respect to tension, speed, equipment mode, and operational risks.

**Model Answer**:

| **Aspect** | **Cable Laying** | **Cable Recovery** |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Mode** | LCE acts as a **brake** (controlled drag) | LCE acts as a **motor** (active pull) |
| **Tension Direction** | Cable engine resists the **downward** weight of suspended catenary | Cable engine **lifts** the cable **upward** against weight, drag, and seabed friction |
| **Operating Speed** | Faster: **1 – 6 knots** (matches ship transit) | Slower: **0.5 – 2 knots** (careful, controlled) |
| **Tension Level** | Moderate, steady, predictable | High and **variable** — risk of **shock loads** if cable suddenly releases |
| **Operational Risk** | Snap-back of cable if parted; over-tension at joints | Cable fatigue, drum crushing, sudden jerks, entanglement with seabed debris |
| **Cable State on Seabed** | Cable is **being deposited** in a smooth catenary | Cable is **being disturbed and lifted** from its laid position |
| **Personnel Focus** | Monitoring payout length and GPS track | Watching dynamometer for shock load spikes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> A very common mistake in laying-vs-recovery questions is to claim that **tension is the same in both operations**. This is **wrong**. During laying, the LCE fights the catenary's weight; during recovery, it must additionally overcome **seabed friction** and **buoyancy** of the suspended cable. Always state the **direction of force explicitly**. *(Loss: up to 2 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cable Ships**

- **Definition**: A cable ship is a specialized vessel for laying, recovering, and repairing submarine cables.
- **Main Functions**: Laying, recovery, jointing, repair, route survey, cable burial.
- **Cable Types Handled**: Submarine telecom cables (fiber-optic, coaxial), power cables, scientific cables.
- **Four Functional Systems**: Storage (cable tanks), Drive (LCE), Routing (sheaves), Monitoring (dynamometer, payout counter, touchdown monitor).
- **Cable Tank**: Large cylindrical/conical storage holding thousands of km of cable; multiple tanks for jointing.
- **Linear Cable Engine (LCE)**: Acts as a **brake in laying mode**, as a **motor in recovery mode**; provides closed-loop tension control.
- **Bow Sheave / Stern Sheave**: Directs cable over the hull; bow used for forward laying, stern for aft laying.
- **Dynamometer**: Real-time tension sensor providing feedback to the LCE controller.
- **Catenary Shape**: $y = a \cosh(x/a) - a$, where $a = T_0 / w$.
- **Top Tension at Ship**: $T_{ship} = T_0 + w \cdot h$.
- **KS Ratio**: $KS = h / (T_{ship}/w)$; safe range is **0.7 – 1.0**.
- **Catenary Constant**: $a = T_0 / w$ (in meters), governs the **curvature** of the suspended cable.
- **DP System**: Dynamic Positioning is critical for **station-keeping** during jointing and repair.
- **Payout Speed**: Equals ship speed (typically **1 – 6 knots** during laying).
- **Recovery Risks**: Shock loads, cable fatigue, seabed entanglement, drum crushing.
- **Modern Vessels**: Equipped with **ROVs** (Remotely Operated Vehicles) for cable burial and inspection; some use **plows** to bury cable in seabed sediments for protection.
- **Industry Standards**: ICPC, ITU, SOLAS, IMO — all relevant for cable ship design and operation.
- **Historical Note**: The first successful submarine telegraph cable was laid in **1858** (transatlantic), and modern fiber-optic cables now carry **>95% of intercontinental data**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ice Breakers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ice Breakers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected):**
> An **Icebreaker** is a specially designed and structurally reinforced vessel purpose-built to navigate through ice-covered waters, creating and maintaining navigable channels for other ships in frozen regions, harbours, rivers, and polar seas.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Standard)
An icebreaker is a ship whose hull form, structural scantlings, propulsion system, and machinery outfit are explicitly engineered to:
1. Continuously or intermittently break through a cover of level ice, ridged ice, or pack ice.
2. Sustain repeated high-magnitude impact loads without structural failure.
3. Escort merchant vessels through ice-covered routes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowplow on Water" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine pushing a wedge-shaped toy boat into a slab of frozen ice on a pond. The wedge slides *up* onto the ice, and the **weight of the boat itself** snaps the ice downward. An icebreaker works exactly the same way — its **sloped, rounded bow** rides up onto the ice sheet, and the vessel's enormous **displacement weight** acts like a giant hand pressing down to snap the brittle ice sheet in flexural bending.

### 1.3 Why Are Special Vessels Required?
Standard merchant hulls are:
- **Too weak** — shell plating would buckle on impact.
- **Wrong shape** — vertical bows merely bounce off ice.
- **Underpowered** — typical harbour tugs lack the thrust to overcome ice resistance.

Key operating environments: **Arctic Ocean, Baltic Sea, Antarctic waters, Great Lakes, St. Lawrence Seaway, and Northern Russian rivers.**

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Flexural strength of sea ice ($\sigma_f$) | **0.4 MPa – 1.5 MPa** |
| Compressive strength of sea ice | **5 MPa – 15 MPa** |
| Icebreaker length-to-beam ratio ($L/B$) | **1.7 – 2.5** |
| Typical transit speed in 1 m ice | **3 – 6 knots** |
| Installed power (modern diesel-electric) | **20 MW – 75 MW** |
| Polar Class (highest) | **PC 1** (year-round, all Arctic) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Icebreakers

#### (A) By Hull Material & Construction
- **Steel-hulled conventional** — most common, welded plating.
- **Wood-and-iron composite** — historical (e.g., 19th century Russian *Yermak*).

#### (B) By Power Plant (KTU-Favourite Distinction)
| Type | Drive System | Application |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| Diesel-mechanical | Direct-drive diesel | Light harbour icebreakers |
| Diesel-electric | AC motors on propellers | Modern mainstream |
| Gas-turbine (COGAG/COGAS) | Combined gas turbine | High-power Arctic |
| Nuclear (e.g., *Arktika*-class) | Nuclear steam turbine | Year-round polar |

#### (C) By Ice Class (IACS Polar Class System, KTU-Standard)
**PC 1 → PC 7** (highest capability to lowest).

### 2.2 Hull Form Design — The Heart of Icebreaking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The icebreaker hull has **three iconic features** that must be remembered for KTU exams.

1. **Raked, Upward-Curving Bow** — eliminates vertical face, slides onto ice.
2. **Sloped Ice-Belt Shoulders** — at the waterline (typical angle $\alpha = 20°\text{–}30°$), the ice sheet bends under the ship's weight until it snaps in **flexural failure**.
3. **Short Length-to-Beam Ratio** — gives high displacement per unit length, increasing the downward "punching" force.

### 2.3 The Four Canonical Methods of Ice Breaking

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"List and explain the methods of ice breaking."* Memorise these four with diagrams.

1. **Ramming Method** — ship accelerates to ~6–8 knots, impacts ice, reverses, repeats. Used for thick ice ($h > 1\,\text{m}$).
2. **Riding-Up (Continuous) Method** — bow rides smoothly up onto the ice; vessel's weight breaks it in bending. Most efficient; used in level ice.
3. **Buoyancy / Submersion Method** — vessel pushes ice sheet down, fracturing it when flexural stress exceeds flexural strength.
4. **Breaking by Bow Wave & Sluicing** — for thin ice; water forced up over bow cracks the sheet.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` so the table does not break.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $R_{ice} = C_L \, B \, h \, \sigma_f$ | $R_{ice}$ = ice resistance (N); $C_L$ = Lewis coefficient $\approx 0.55$–$0.75$; $B$ = beam (m); $h$ = ice thickness (m); $\sigma_f$ = flexural strength (Pa) | **Lewis' formula** — preliminary estimate of ice-breaking resistance |
| 2 | $F_b = k \, \sigma_f \, h^2$ | $F_b$ = force per unit width to break ice (N/m); $k$ = shape factor $\approx 0.5$–$1.0$ | Bending force required |
| 3 | $P_{ice} = R_{ice} \cdot v$ | $P_{ice}$ = icebreaking power (W); $v$ = transit speed (m/s) | Power sizing of propulsion |
| 4 | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $m$ = vessel mass; $v$ = ramming speed | Energy absorbed in ramming |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M \, c}{I}$ | $M$ = bending moment; $c$ = half-thickness; $I$ = second moment of area | Failure criterion for ice sheet |
| 6 | $\Delta_W = \rho_w \, g \, V_{disp}$ | $\Delta_W$ = displacement weight (N); $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume | Weight acting on ice when bow rides up |
| 7 | $\theta_{bow} = 20°\text{–}30°$ | Bow shoulder angle (typical design range) | Hull-form selection |
| 8 | $P/E_{ice}$ | Power-to-ice-thickness ratio | Used in PC classification |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Shipping industry** — keeps Northern Sea Route, Northwest Passage, Baltic ports open year-round.
- **Offshore oil & gas** — supports exploration in the Beaufort, Barents, and Sakhalin shelves.
- **Scientific research** — resupply of Antarctic stations (e.g., India's *Polar Research Vessel*-class).
- **Defence** — strategic access to Arctic regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flexural Bending Force on a Semi-Infinite Ice Plate

Consider an ice sheet of thickness $h$, treated as an **elastic plate** resting on an elastic (Winkler) foundation. The hull shoulder pushes down with force $F_b$ per unit width. The plate bends until its maximum flexural stress reaches $\sigma_f$, causing a **through-thickness crack**.

**Step 1 — Flexural rigidity of the ice plate.**
The plate's resistance to bending is governed by its flexural rigidity:

$$
D = \frac{E \, h^3}{12 \, (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus of ice (≈ 5–10 GPa), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.33).

**Step 2 — Characteristic length of an infinite plate on elastic foundation.**
This length governs how bending moments decay with distance from the load:

$$
\ell = \left(\frac{4 D}{k_f}\right)^{1/4}
$$

where $k_f$ = foundation modulus of water (≈ 25–250 MN/m³).

**Step 3 — Bending moment at the load point.**
For a concentrated line load $F_b$ (N/m) on the plate, the maximum bending moment occurs directly under the load:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{F_b \, \ell}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply flexural failure criterion.**
The bending stress at the ice surface equals the flexural strength:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{M_{max} \, (h/2)}{I}
$$

For a rectangular cross-section of unit width, $I = h^3 / 12$, so:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{M_{max} \, (h/2)}{h^3/12} = \frac{6 \, M_{max}}{h^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the breaking force per unit width.**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{\sigma_f \, h^2}{6}
$$

Equating with Step 3:

$$
\frac{F_b \, \ell}{4} = \frac{\sigma_f \, h^2}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_b = \frac{2 \, \sigma_f \, h^2}{3 \, \ell}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Breaking force scales with the **square of ice thickness**. Doubling the ice thickness requires **four times** the breaking force — explaining why thick polar ice demands nuclear-powered ships.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lewis' Empirical Ice Resistance Formula

Lewis (1981) proposed a dimensionally consistent empirical model where ice resistance is proportional to beam, ice thickness, and flexural strength.

**Step 1 — Dimensional basis.**
Ice resistance must have dimensions of force [N]. The natural product of $B$ [m], $h$ [m], and $\sigma_f$ [N/m²] gives $[N]$, confirming the form.

**Step 2 — Introduce an empirical coefficient $C_L$.**
Lewis fitted experimental data on real vessels to obtain:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{ice} = C_L \, B \, h \, \sigma_f\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Value of $C_L$.**
For modern icebreakers with sloped shoulders ($\alpha \approx 25°$):

$$
C_L \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.75
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example.**
A Baltic-class icebreaker with $B = 25\,\text{m}$ operates in ice with $h = 0.6\,\text{m}$, $\sigma_f = 0.6\,\text{MPa}$. Take $C_L = 0.65$.

$$
R_{ice} = 0.65 \times 25 \times 0.6 \times 0.6 \times 10^6
$$

$$
R_{ice} = 5.85 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 5.85 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 5 — Power required at 4 knots** ($\approx 2.06\,\text{m/s}$):

$$
P_{ice} = R_{ice} \times v = 5.85 \times 10^6 \times 2.06 \approx 12.05 \text{ MW}
$$

This matches the real installed power of Baltic icebreakers (~15 MW).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ice Failure Modes vs. Trigger

| Failure Mode | Trigger Condition | Ice Type | Bow Behaviour |
|--------------|------------------|----------|---------------|
| Flexural bending | Bow rides up; weight > cracking load | Level ice ≤ 1 m | Smooth ride-up |
| Crushing | High local contact stress | Thick multi-year ice | Heavy ramming |
| Buckling | Out-of-plane instability | Highly deformed floe | Sudden snap |
| Splitting (splitting failure) | Wide shoulder pushes wedge | Pack ice edges | Shear crack |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Icebreaker Hull Form & Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Propulsion Plant Diesel Electric 20 MW] --> B[Shaft Line & Gearbox]
    B --> C[Controllable Pitch Propeller]
    C --> D[Ice Nozzle Duct for Propeller Protection]
    D --> E[Wake Flow to Hull]

    F[Raked Curved Bow] --> G[Sloped Ice Belt Shoulder Angle 25 deg]
    G --> H[Reinforced Shell Plating 50 80 mm]
    H --> I[Longitudinal Framing]
    I --> J[Web Frames Every 1.2 m]
    J --> K[Double Bottom Ballast Tanks]

    L[Ice Sheet Thickness h] --> M{Contact Type}
    M -->|Riding Up| N[Flexural Bending Failure]
    M -->|High Speed Ramming| O[Crushing and Buckling]

    N --> P[Force F_b per Equation 3.1.5]
    O --> Q[Impact Load F_impact = m dv dt]

    P --> R[Hull Force Distribution]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Structural Response Stress and Deformation]

    T[Ice Class PC 1 to PC 7] --> U[Design Constraints]
    U --> F
    U --> A

    style A fill:#f9d976
    style F fill:#90caf9
    style L fill:#a5d6a7
    style P fill:#ffab91
    style S fill:#ce93d8
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Continuous Icebreaking Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Start in Open Water] --> A1[Approach Ice Edge at 6 Knots]
    A1 --> A2[Bow Rides Up onto Ice Sheet]
    A2 --> A3[Hull Weight Loads Ice in Flexure]
    A3 --> A4{Ice Flexural Stress vs Strength}
    A4 -->|Greater| A5[Ice Cracks and Submerges]
    A4 -->|Less| A6[Reverse and Ram Again]
    A5 --> A7[Ship Advances into Broken Channel]
    A6 --> A2
    A7 --> A8[Escort Merchant Vessel Follows in Wake]

    style A0 fill:#bbdefb
    style A2 fill:#fff59d
    style A4 fill:#ffcc80
    style A5 fill:#a5d6a7
    style A8 fill:#b39ddb
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Ice Class & Hull Form Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Operational Requirement] --> S2{Year Round or Seasonal}
    S2 -->|Year Round| S3[Polar Class PC 1 or PC 2]
    S2 -->|Seasonal| S4{Region}
    S4 -->|Baltic| S5[FSIC IA or IB]
    S4 -->|North Sea| S6[PC 6 or PC 7]

    S3 --> H1[Hull Form Heavy Sloped 25 deg Double Hull]
    S5 --> H2[Hull Form Medium Sloped 20 deg Single Hull Reinforced]
    S6 --> H3[Hull Form Light Reinforced Bow]

    H1 --> O1[Power 50 to 75 MW]
    H2 --> O2[Power 15 to 25 MW]
    H3 --> O3[Power 7 to 12 MW]

    style S1 fill:#e1bee7
    style H1 fill:#ef9a9a
    style H2 fill:#ffcc80
    style H3 fill:#c5e1a5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]`**

**Q1.** Define an icebreaker. List any **two** distinguishing features of an icebreaker hull compared to a conventional merchant vessel.
*(Model Answer: 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An **icebreaker** is a specially reinforced vessel designed to navigate through and break navigable paths in ice-covered waters.
Two distinguishing features:
1. **Raked, upward-curved bow** with sloped ice-belt shoulder (angle $\approx 20°\text{–}30°$), enabling the bow to ride up onto the ice rather than impact it head-on.
2. **Heavy shell plating** (50–80 mm) and closely spaced web frames, together with a low $L/B$ ratio, providing structural strength against impact and buckling loads.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for two features with brief justification.)*

---

**Q2.** State **Kashteljan's** (or any standard) formula for ice resistance and explain the role of each variable.
*(Model Answer: 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A standard empirical formula for ice resistance is **Lewis' equation**:

$$
R_{ice} = C_L \, B \, h \, \sigma_f
$$

| Symbol | Role |
|--------|------|
| $R_{ice}$ | Total ice resistance force (N) — what the propulsion must overcome |
| $C_L$ | Lewis coefficient (0.55–0.75), accounts for hull form efficiency |
| $B$ | Vessel beam (m) — wider beam means more contact, higher resistance |
| $h$ | Ice thickness (m) — thicker ice means much higher resistance |
| $\sigma_f$ | Flexural strength of ice (Pa) — stronger ice resists bending more |

*(Mark split: 2 marks for the formula and units + 1 mark for the role of variables.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Paper, Module 1]`**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose EITHER A or B

**Q A.** *(a)* Explain with neat sketches the **four methods of ice breaking** employed by icebreakers. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A Baltic-class icebreaker has beam $B = 24\,\text{m}$ and operates in level ice of thickness $h = 0.8\,\text{m}$. The flexural strength of the ice is $\sigma_f = 0.7\,\text{MPa}$. Using **Lewis' formula** with $C_L = 0.65$, calculate the **(i)** ice resistance and **(ii)** the propulsive power required at a transit speed of **4 knots** ($1\,\text{knot} = 0.5144\,\text{m/s}$). *(7 marks)*

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Four methods of ice breaking (7 marks):**

1. **Ramming Method (2 marks):** The vessel accelerates in open water or a cleared lead, strikes the ice edge at 5–8 knots, then reverses and repeats. Used for thick or ridged ice. Disadvantage: slow average progress.

2. **Riding-Up / Continuous Method (2 marks):** The sloped bow rides smoothly up onto the ice sheet. The vessel's own displacement weight applies a downward load that exceeds the ice's flexural capacity, breaking it in bending. Most fuel-efficient method in level ice.

3. **Buoyancy (Submersion) Method (1.5 marks):** As the bow presses down, the ice sheet is forced beneath the waterline. The buoyant reaction on the depressed plate produces bending moments that crack the ice.

4. **Breaking by Bow Wave / Sluicing (1.5 marks):** In thin ice ($h < 0.3$ m), the bow wave washes over the sheet; the hydrostatic imbalance and dynamic pressure crack the ice. Used by harbour tugs in brash ice.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$B = 24\,\text{m},\; h = 0.8\,\text{m},\; \sigma_f = 0.7 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa},\; C_L = 0.65,\; v = 4 \times 0.5144 = 2.0576\,\text{m/s}$

**(i) Ice Resistance (3.5 marks):**

$$
R_{ice} = C_L \, B \, h \, \sigma_f = 0.65 \times 24 \times 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 10^6
$$

$$
R_{ice} = 8.736 \times 10^6\,\text{N} = 8.736\,\text{MN}
$$

**(ii) Power Required (3.5 marks):**

$$
P_{ice} = R_{ice} \times v = 8.736 \times 10^6 \times 2.0576
$$

$$
P_{ice} \approx 17.97 \times 10^6\,\text{W} \approx 17.97\,\text{MW}
$$

*(Equivalent to about 24,100 HP. Reasonable for a Baltic-class icebreaker.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q B.** *(a)* Describe the **typical hull-form features** of a modern diesel-electric icebreaker. Discuss why a low length-to-beam ratio is preferred. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Derive the **flexural breaking force** per unit width for an ice plate on an elastic foundation, clearly stating the meaning of each symbol. *(7 marks)*

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Hull-Form Features (7 marks):**

1. **Raked, curved bow** — eliminates a vertical impact face; the bow smoothly rides onto the ice. *(1 mark)*
2. **Sloped ice-belt shoulder** (typical $\alpha = 20°\text{–}30°$) — converts the ship's weight into a vertical bending load on the ice sheet. *(1 mark)*
3. **Heavy shell plating** (50–80 mm) at the bow and along the ice belt — distributes impact loads. *(1 mark)*
4. **Closely spaced web frames and longitudinal stiffeners** — prevent local buckling under repeated impacts. *(1 mark)*
5. **Double bottom and compartmentalisation** — improves survivability in case of shell breach. *(1 mark)*
6. **Low $L/B$ ratio (1.7–2.5)** — increases displacement per unit length, giving a larger downward force per metre of bow length, so the ice fails at lower speeds. A longer ship would distribute the same weight over a longer ice contact zone, reducing per-metre pressure. *(2 marks)*

---

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Treat the ice sheet as an elastic plate of thickness $h$ on a Winkler foundation of modulus $k_f$. Let $D$ = flexural rigidity and $\ell$ = characteristic length.

**Step 1:** Flexural rigidity

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12 (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

**Step 2:** Characteristic length (governs bending-moment decay)

$$
\ell = \left(\frac{4 D}{k_f}\right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum bending moment under a line load $F_b$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{F_b \, \ell}{4}
$$

**Step 4:** Flexural stress reaches $\sigma_f$ at the ice surface:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{6 M_{max}}{h^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and solve:

$$
\boxed{\,F_b = \frac{2 \, \sigma_f \, h^2}{3 \, \ell}\,}
$$

Where:
- $F_b$ = breaking force per unit width (N/m)
- $\sigma_f$ = flexural strength of ice (Pa)
- $h$ = ice thickness (m)
- $\ell$ = characteristic length (m), dependent on $E$, $h$, $\nu$, $k_f$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for plate model assumption + 1 for $D$, 1 for $\ell$, 1 for $M_{max}$, 1 for failure criterion, 2 marks for the final boxed result and symbol legend.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Icebreaker Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing flexural strength with compressive strength** — flexural $\approx 0.5$ MPa is *much* lower than compressive $\approx 10$ MPa. Ice fails in *bending*, not crushing, in normal operation.
> 2. **Using beam $B$ instead of length $L$ in resistance formulas** — Lewis' formula uses beam, not length. Wrong placement loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** — 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. Mixing units is the #1 numerical error.
> 4. **Missing "per unit width"** in flexural force derivations — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if $F_b$ is written as total force instead of N/m.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** of elastic plate on Winkler foundation — without this, the derivation step 1 is "ungrounded" and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Conflating "Polar Class" with "Finnish-Swedish Ice Class"** — they are different systems; do not interchange.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Icebreaker** = reinforced ship designed to break ice; key idea is *riding up + weight-induced bending failure*.
- ✅ **Hull features**: raked curved bow, sloped ice-belt shoulder ($\approx 25°$), heavy plating, low $L/B$ ratio.
- ✅ **Four ice-breaking methods**: ramming, riding-up (continuous), buoyancy/submersion, bow-wave/sluicing.
- ✅ **Main failure mode**: *flexural bending* — ice cracks because downward load > flexural strength.
- ✅ **Lewis' formula**: $R_{ice} = C_L \, B \, h \, \sigma_f$; $C_L \approx 0.55\text{–}0.75$.
- ✅ **Power**: $P_{ice} = R_{ice} \times v$ — Baltic icebreaker needs ~15–25 MW; polar ≥ 50 MW.
- ✅ **Flexural breaking force**: $F_b = \dfrac{2 \sigma_f h^2}{3 \ell}$; force scales as $h^2$.
- ✅ **Ice classes**: IACS Polar Class PC 1 (highest) → PC 7 (lowest); Finnish-Swedish IA–III.
- ✅ **Power plants**: diesel-electric (mainstream), COGAG gas-turbine, nuclear (Russia's *Arktika*-class).
- ✅ **Exam mantra**: *"Weight, not speed, breaks the ice."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Research Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Research Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Research Vessel (RV)** is a purpose-built or specially commissioned marine surface ship that is engineered and outfitted to perform scientific investigation, exploration, and data acquisition in the fields of oceanography, hydrography, marine geology, marine biology, fisheries science, atmospheric research, and offshore resource evaluation. Unlike commercial vessels, whose hull form, machinery, and layout are optimized for profit, the architecture of a research vessel is dictated by the **operational scientific mission profile**, station-keeping ability, lab capacity, and the requirement to deploy specialized instruments (CTDs, rosettes, ROVs, AUVs, multibeam echo sounders, etc.) into the water column or seabed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 1**
> Under "Fundamentals of Floating Bodies," research vessels are introduced as a *case-study application* of hydrostatics, transverse & longitudinal stability, form coefficients, and seakeeping principles learned earlier in the module. Any numerical or descriptive question on a research vessel in the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** will test the student's ability to apply the **basic hydrostatic formulas** ($ \Delta = \rho g \nabla $, $ \overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} $) to a vessel whose particulars are given in the problem statement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **floating, self-propelled, multi-storey laboratory** that must do three things at once: (a) sail thousands of nautical miles across stormy oceans, (b) hold its position within a few meters for days on end over a specific seabed coordinate, and (c) lower delicate scientific instruments kilometres below its hull without crashing into itself.

> A research vessel is essentially a **Swiss Army Knife of the seas** — a single floating platform that combines a transit ship, a hotel for scientists, a deck crane, a submarine launchpad, and a high-precision data centre, all in one hull.

Think of it this way: a **cargo ship** is built to *carry things from A to B*, a **warship** is built to *survive and fight*, but a **research vessel** is built to *observe, measure, and sample*. Its hull, propulsion, and stability are therefore designed around the needs of the instruments and the scientists — not around profit or combat.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard numbers are encountered repeatedly in research vessel design and KTU numericals:

- **Density of seawater:** $ \rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3 $ (used in lieu of $ 1000 $ unless otherwise stated)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $ g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2 $
- **Specific weight of seawater:** $ \gamma = \rho g = 10050.25 \ \text{N/m}^3 \approx 10.05 \ \text{kN/m}^3 $
- **Displacement (Δ) range for research vessels:** small coastal RVs ≈ **100–500 tonnes**, mid-size global RVs ≈ **2000–6000 tonnes**, large ice-class polar RVs ≈ **10000–25000 tonnes**
- **Service speed:** typically **12–16 knots** (lower than commercial ships to optimise fuel & seakeeping)
- **Endurance:** 30–60 days unsupported at sea

> [!NOTE]
> **Why seawater, not fresh water?**
> Research vessels almost always operate in salt water. KTU problems frequently give $ \rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $ specifically so that the student must correctly apply Archimedes' principle to oceanic conditions. Always use the density *given* in the problem statement — do not auto-substitute $ 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3 $.

## 1.4 Why Research Vessels Matter in Ocean Engineering

Research vessels are the **primary data-acquisition platform** for:

1. **Climate change monitoring** (ARGO floats deployment, CTD casts, atmospheric flux measurements)
2. **Seabed mapping** (multibeam sonar, sub-bottom profiling)
3. **Offshore resource exploration** (oil, gas, marine minerals, deep-sea mining)
4. **Marine biodiversity surveys** (biodiversity assessment, coral reef mapping)
5. **Disaster prediction** (tsunami seabed sensors, storm surge studies)
6. **Defense & hydrography** (nautical charting, submarine detection research)

In short: **almost every chart of the deep ocean was drawn from data collected by a research vessel.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Subsystems of a Research Vessel

A research vessel is a system of systems. The following structured breakdown shows the hierarchy of subsystems that an Ocean Engineering student must be able to describe in a 14-mark KTU ESE question.

### A. Hull & Form Subsystem
- **Monohull** — traditional single hull, good seakeeping, large internal volume.
- **Catamaran** — twin parallel hulls joined by a deck; excellent stability, large deck area, used for coastal survey vessels.
- **SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull)** — submerged twin hulls with thin struts; minimal wave excitation in the heave/pitch modes; used for *stable* platforms (e.g., stable instrument carriers).
- **Trimaran** — three hulls; combines payload of monohull with some catamaran stability.

### B. Propulsion & Station-Keeping Subsystem
- **Conventional propeller + rudder** for transit.
- **Bow thruster + stern thruster** for low-speed manoeuvring.
- **Dynamic Positioning (DP) System** — a computer-controlled system using azimuth thrusters, GPS, and acoustic position reference to hold the vessel within a circle of radius (typically) **5–50 m** of a target waypoint.

### C. Deck & Deployment Subsystem
- **A-frame** (stern gantry) — for launching ROVs, CTD rosettes, deep-tow systems.
- **Crane / Knuckle boom crane** — for over-side deployment of heavy equipment.
- **Winches** — for CTD cables (up to **10 000 m** of wire), hydrographic winches, coring winches.
- **Moon-pool** (optional) — vertical shaft through the hull for deployment in high sea states.

### D. Laboratory & Accommodation Subsystem
- **Wet lab, dry lab, computer lab, cold lab, chemistry lab.**
- **Acoustic quiet lab** (for sonar processing) — isolated from machinery vibration.
- **Accommodation** for typically 20–60 scientists + 20–40 crew.

### E. Scientific Instrumentation Subsystem
- Multibeam echo sounder, sub-bottom profiler, ADCP (Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler), CTD rosette, side-scan sonar, gravimeter, magnetometer, weather station, atmospheric sampling booms.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable under Module 1 of OENST611 — Fundamentals of Floating Bodies. The notation follows standard ship hydrostatics textbooks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Archimedes Principle | $ \Delta = \rho \, g \, \nabla $ | N (or tonnes-force) | $ \Delta $ = weight displaced, $ \nabla $ = submerged volume |
| 2 | Displacement (mass) | $ \Delta_m = \rho \, \nabla $ | tonnes | If $ \rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $ |
| 3 | Block Coefficient | $ C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T} $ | dimensionless | Typical R/V: $ 0.55 $–$ 0.70 $ |
| 4 | Waterplane Area Coefficient | $ C_WP = \dfrac{A_W}{L \, B} $ | dimensionless | Typical R/V: $ 0.70 $–$ 0.85 $ |
| 5 | Prismatic Coefficient | $ C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} $ | dimensionless | $ A_M $ = midship area |
| 6 | Transverse BM | $ \overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} $ | m | $ I_T $ = 2nd moment of waterplane about CL |
| 7 | Waterplane inertia (rect.) | $ I_T = \dfrac{L \, B^3}{12} $ | m⁴ | For a rectangular waterplane |
| 8 | KB (centre of buoyancy) | $ \overline{KB} = \dfrac{\int z \, d\nabla}{\nabla} $ | m | For prismatic hull, $ \overline{KB} = T/2 $ |
| 9 | Metacentric Height (GM) | $ \overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} $ | m | Positive $ \Rightarrow $ stable |
| 10 | Righting Lever (small angle) | $ GZ = \overline{GM} \sin\phi $ | m | Valid for $ \phi < 10^\circ $–$ 15^\circ $ |
| 11 | Period of Roll | $ T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \, \overline{GM}}} $ | s | $ k $ = radius of gyration |
| 12 | Free Surface Correction | $ \overline{GM}_{eff} = \overline{GM} - \dfrac{i}{\nabla} $ | m | $ i $ = 2nd moment of FS liquid |
| 13 | Moment to Trim 1 cm (MCTC) | $ \text{MCTC} = \dfrac{\Delta \, \overline{GM}_L}{100 \, L} $ | t·m/cm | Longitudinal stability |
| 14 | Trim equation | $ \text{Trim} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{\text{MCTC}} $ | cm | $ w $ = weight shifted, $ l $ = shift distance |

> **Engineering utility of these formulas in the field:**
> When a research vessel is being designed, the naval architect must first fix the **mission draft (T)**, **length (L)**, and **beam (B)**, then verify that the resulting $ \overline{GM} $ lies in the safe range of **0.15 m – 0.50 m** (lower than cargo ships because RVs carry tall, top-heavy masts and cranes). Excessive GM makes the roll *jerky* — bad for delicate instruments. Insufficient GM makes the roll *sluggish* — bad for crew comfort and station-keeping.

## 2.3 Typical Numerical Values for Reference

| Vessel Class | L (m) | B (m) | T (m) | Δ (t) | Service Speed (kn) |
|--------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------------------|
| Coastal Catamaran Survey | 25 | 9 | 2.0 | 180 | 12 |
| Mid-size Global Monohull | 85 | 14 | 5.0 | 3500 | 14 |
| Large Polar (Ice-strengthened) | 130 | 22 | 8.5 | 12000 | 15 |
| SWATH Stable Platform | 70 | 22 | 7.5 | 3500 | 14 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numericals in OENST611 are typically **paper-vessel problems** where a generic research vessel of given L, B, T, KG is specified. The student applies the formula sheet above to compute $ \Delta $, $ \overline{GM} $, $ GZ $, etc. Memorising the formula sheet above is the single highest-leverage study activity for this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Displacement from Principal Particulars

**Given:**
- Length between perpendiculars, $ L = 85 \ \text{m} $
- Beam, $ B = 14 \ \text{m} $
- Mean draft, $ T = 5.0 \ \text{m} $
- Block coefficient, $ C_B = 0.62 $
- Density of seawater, $ \rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $

**To find:** Displacement $ \Delta $ (in tonnes).

### Step 1 — Write the relationship between $ C_B $ and $ \nabla $

The block coefficient is defined as the ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of the enclosing rectangular box:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}
$$

Rearranging to solve for the displaced volume $ \nabla $:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
\nabla = 0.62 \times 85 \ \text{m} \times 14 \ \text{m} \times 5.0 \ \text{m}
$$

Compute the product inside the brackets first:

$$
85 \times 14 = 1190
$$

$$
1190 \times 5.0 = 5950
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.62 \times 5950
$$

$$
\nabla = 3689 \ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Convert displaced volume to displacement mass

Using $ \Delta_m = \rho \cdot \nabla $:

$$
\Delta_m = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 \times 3689 \ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
\Delta_m = 3781.225 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to weight (if required in kN)

$$
\Delta_W = \Delta_m \times g = 3781.225 \times 9.81
$$

$$
\Delta_W = 37\,094.0 \ \text{kN}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\Delta \approx 3781 \ \text{tonnes} \quad \text{or} \quad 37\,094 \ \text{kN}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transverse Metacentric Height $ \overline{GM} $

**Given:** The same vessel as above, with the following additional data:
- Vertical centre of gravity above keel, $ \overline{KG} = 6.0 \ \text{m} $

**To find:** $ \overline{GM} $ and hence the stability status.

### Step 1 — Compute $ \overline{KB} $ for a prismatic hull (uniform cross-section)

For a hull with prismatic (constant cross-section) form, the centre of buoyancy is at half the draft:

$$
\overline{KB} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{5.0}{2} = 2.5 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the 2nd moment of area of the waterplane

Approximating the waterplane as a rectangle of length $ L $ and beam $ B $:

$$
I_T = \frac{L \, B^3}{12} = \frac{85 \times 14^3}{12}
$$

First, $ 14^3 = 2744 $:

$$
I_T = \frac{85 \times 2744}{12} = \frac{233\,240}{12} = 19\,436.67 \ \text{m}^4
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $ \overline{BM} $

$$
\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{19\,436.67}{3689} = 5.269 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $ \overline{GM} $

$$
\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}
$$

$$
\overline{GM} = 2.5 + 5.269 - 6.0
$$

$$
\overline{GM} = 1.769 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result

> [!NOTE]
> A research vessel typically requires $ \overline{GM} $ in the range of **0.15 m – 0.50 m** for *comfortable* seakeeping. Our value of $ 1.769 \ \text{m} $ is **excessively stiff** — the vessel would roll too quickly and too jerkily, which is undesirable for delicate sonar equipment.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\overline{GM} = 1.769 \ \text{m} \quad (\text{over-stiff; recommend lowering KG by adding low ballast})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Righting Lever and Stability at a Given Heel Angle

**Given:** Same vessel, $ \overline{GM} = 1.769 \ \text{m} $. Find the righting lever $ GZ $ at $ \phi = 20^\circ $ heel.

### Step 1 — Recall the small-angle formula

For small angles ($ \phi \le 10^\circ $–$ 15^\circ $):

$$
GZ = \overline{GM} \, \sin\phi
$$

For larger angles, we must use the **exact formula**:

$$
GZ = \overline{KN} \sin\phi - \overline{KG} \sin\phi
$$

Since $ \overline{KN} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BN} $, and $ \overline{BN} $ depends on heel, the small-angle approximation is usually sufficient for a Module 1 KTU problem unless data on the GZ curve is given.

### Step 2 — Apply the small-angle formula at $ \phi = 20^\circ $

$$
