GZ = 1.769 \times \sin 20^\circ
$$

$$
\sin 20^\circ = 0.3420
$$

$$
GZ = 1.769 \times 0.3420 = 0.605 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the righting moment

$$
RM = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ
$$

$$
RM = 3781.225 \times 9.81 \times 0.605
$$

$$
RM = 22\,443.4 \ \text{kN·m}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{GZ = 0.605 \ \text{m} \quad ; \quad RM \approx 22\,443 \ \text{kN·m}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Criteria Checklist

| Criterion | Required Value | Our Vessel | Status |
|-----------|----------------|------------|--------|
| Positive GM | $ \overline{GM} > 0 $ | $ 1.769 $ m | ✅ Pass |
| Comfortable GM | $ 0.15 \le \overline{GM} \le 0.50 $ m | $ 1.769 $ m | ❌ Too stiff |
| Range of positive stability | $ \phi_{range} \ge 60^\circ $ | (assumed) | ✅ Likely pass |
| Area under GZ curve up to $ 30^\circ $ | $ \ge 0.055 $ m·rad | needs data | — |
| Maximum GZ at reasonable angle | $ \le 40^\circ $ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most common error KTU examiners flag in research-vessel numericals is the *failure to convert tonnes to kN (or vice versa)* before multiplying by GZ to get the righting moment. Always state units explicitly: $ \Delta $ in tonnes → multiply by $ g = 9.81 $ to obtain weight in kN before computing $ RM = W \cdot GZ $.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Arrangement (Block-Level) of a Research Vessel

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the major functional zones of a generic mid-size research vessel as seen in plan view. Each block represents a physically distinct region of the ship.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bow Section\nBow Thruster\nAnchor Mooring\nForward Sonar Dome] --> B[Forward Lab Zone\nWet Lab\nCold Room\nScientific Freezers]
    B --> C[Central Lab Zone\nComputer Lab\nAcoustic Lab\nControl Room for ROV DP System]
    C --> D[Main Deck Working Area\nA Frame Gantry\nCrane Base\nCTD Winch\nMoon Pool Optional]
    D --> E[Engine Room and Machinery\nMain Engines\nAzimuth Thrusters\nGenerators for Labs]
    E --> F[Stern Section\nStern Thruster\nMultibeam Echo Sounder\nCoring Winch\nHangar for ROV]
    F --> G[Bridge Top\nDP Consoles\nNavigation Radars\nCommunication Array]
    G --> A

    classDef lab fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef deck fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#000
    classDef mach fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    class A bridge
    class B,C lab
    class D deck
    class E mach
    class F deck
    class G bridge
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **Bow Section (A):** Houses the anchor/mooring system and forward-looking sonar (used for obstacle avoidance during deep dives).
- **Forward Lab Zone (B):** Houses temperature-sensitive equipment; placed forward for trim balance.
- **Central Lab Zone (C):** The "brain" of the vessel — acoustic processing and ROV control.
- **Main Deck (D):** The working deck where heavy instruments are deployed. The A-frame gantry is the *signature* feature of every research vessel.
- **Engine Room (E):** Centred amidships to minimise shaft losses; engines are typically diesel-electric on modern RVs to reduce acoustic noise that would interfere with sonar.
- **Stern Section (F):** Houses the multibeam echo sounder (mounted in a blister below the waterline) and the ROV hangar.
- **Bridge (G):** Sits atop the superstructure; integrates DP, navigation, and communication.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Data Acquisition Mission

The following flow shows the *logical sequence* of a typical research-vessel scientific mission — from deployment to data return.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Sail to Survey Station] --> S2[Step 2 Deploy DP Hold Position within 50 m]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Lower CTD Rosette via A Frame to 4000 m]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Record Conductivity Temperature Depth Profile]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Recover CTD Sample Onboard]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Launch ROV for Visual Survey]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Capture HD Video and Sonar Strips]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Recover ROV to Hangar]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Process Data in Central Lab]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Transit to Next Station]

    classDef step fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10 step
```

## 4.3 Hull-Form Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HF[Hull Form Selection for Research Vessel] --> M[Monohull]
    HF --> C[Catamaran]
    HF --> SW[SWATH]
    HF --> T[Trimaran]

    M --> M1[Pros Large Internal Volume Good Ocean Seakeeping]
    M --> M2[Cons Moderate Stability at Low Speed]

    C --> C1[Pros Excellent Deck Area Low Heel Motion Stable Platform]
    C --> C2[Cons Lower Transit Speed Wave Slamming on Forward Cross Structure]

    SW --> SW1[Pros Minimal Heave and Pitch Motion Very Stable for Sonar]
    SW --> SW2[Cons Complex and Expensive Construction Limited Deck Cargo]

    T --> T1[Pros Balance of Payload and Stability]
    T --> T2[Cons Rarely Used in Modern Research Vessels]

    classDef form fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,color:#000
    classDef pros fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef cons fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    class HF form
    class M,C,SW,T form
    class M1,C1,SW1,T1 pros
    class M2,C2,SW2,T2 cons
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU students:**
> The hull-form diagram is the conceptual answer to the question "Which hull form would you recommend for a coastal hydrographic survey vessel operating in sea state 3?" — the answer is **Catamaran** (stable platform, large deck for the survey launches, low wave-induced motion for echo-sounding accuracy). For "deep-ocean global-class vessel" the answer is **monohull with ice-strengthening**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define a research vessel. List any four types of research vessels based on their mission.**

**Model Answer:**

A **research vessel** is a marine surface ship specially designed, equipped, and crewed to conduct scientific investigations of the ocean, atmosphere, seabed, and marine life. Unlike commercial vessels, its hull form, propulsion, deck machinery, and internal layout are configured primarily to support scientific data acquisition and instrument deployment rather than cargo or passenger transport.

**Four types of research vessels (by mission):**

1. **Oceanographic Research Vessel** — collects physical, chemical, and biological data on the water column (CTD casts, water sampling, plankton nets).
2. **Hydrographic Survey Vessel** — produces nautical charts by mapping seabed bathymetry (multibeam echo sounders, side-scan sonar).
3. **Geophysical / Geological Survey Vessel** — investigates sub-seabed structure for resource exploration (seismic streamers, sub-bottom profilers, gravity corers).
4. **Fisheries Research Vessel** — studies fish stocks, marine ecosystems, and conducts trawling experiments.

*(Alternative valid types: Polar Research Vessel, Multi-purpose Research Vessel, Drilling/Research Vessel, ROV Support Vessel.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a moon-pool? Why is it advantageous in a research vessel?**

**Model Answer:**

A **moon-pool** is a vertical, watertight shaft or opening cut through the hull of a vessel (typically amidships), open to the sea both above and below, allowing instruments to be lowered into the water from *inside* the protected hull rather than over the open side.

**Advantages in a research vessel:**

1. **All-weather deployment** — instruments can be launched in high sea states because the moon-pool opening is protected by the surrounding hull structure, unlike over-side deployment which fails in rough seas.
2. **Reduced heave motion at the deployment point** — instruments hang in a relatively calm vertical column inside the hull.
3. **Lower centre of gravity effect** — heavy instruments can be deployed without affecting the vessel's transverse stability.
4. **Protection of the instrument from wave slamming** at the surface.
5. Enables **continuous deployment** of ROVs, drilling strings, or sensor packages during transit or station-keeping.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries 14 marks distributed over two sub-questions of 7 marks each, with an **internal choice** (the student answers *either* the full Question A *or* the full Question B). Model solutions for both are given below.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Hull Form & Hydrostatic Stability `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A coastal hydrographic survey catamaran has the following principal particulars:
- Length overall, $ L = 25 \ \text{m} $
- Beam (single hull), $ b = 3.0 \ \text{m} $
- Draft, $ T = 1.6 \ \text{m} $
- Block coefficient (per hull), $ C_B = 0.60 $
- Centre of gravity above keel, $ \overline{KG} = 2.2 \ \text{m} $
- Two identical hulls spaced $ 9 \ \text{m} $ centre-to-centre.

**Compute the displacement (in tonnes) of the vessel, assuming seawater density $ \rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Volume of one hull**

$$
\nabla_1 = C_B \times L \times b \times T
$$

$$
\nabla_1 = 0.60 \times 25 \times 3.0 \times 1.6
$$

$$
\nabla_1 = 0.60 \times 120 = 72 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Total volume (two hulls)**

$$
\nabla_{total} = 2 \times 72 = 144 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement mass**

$$
\Delta = \rho \times \nabla_{total} = 1.025 \times 144
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta = 147.6 \ \text{tonnes}}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same catamaran, given the waterplane area of one hull is $ A_W = 70 \ \text{m}^2 $, and treating the waterplane as rectangular for inertia calculation, find the **transverse metacentric height** $ \overline{GM} $.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — $ \overline{KB} $ for prismatic hull**

$$
\overline{KB} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1.6}{2} = 0.8 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Second moment of waterplane of one hull (about its own centreline)**

$$
I_{T,1} = \frac{L \times b^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 3.0^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 27}{12} = \frac{675}{12} = 56.25 \ \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the parallel-axis theorem for the twin-hull waterplane**

Each hull is offset from the vessel centreline by $ d = 4.5 \ \text{m} $ (half of 9 m). The waterplane area of each hull is $ A_W = 70 \ \text{m}^2 $.

The total second moment of area of the *combined* waterplane about the vessel centreline is:

$$
I_T = 2 \left[ I_{T,1} + A_W \, d^2 \right]
$$

$$
I_T = 2 \left[ 56.25 + 70 \times 4.5^2 \right]
$$

$$
I_T = 2 \left[ 56.25 + 70 \times 20.25 \right]
$$

$$
I_T = 2 \left[ 56.25 + 1417.5 \right] = 2 \times 1473.75 = 2947.5 \ \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $ \overline{BM} $**

$$
\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla_{total}} = \frac{2947.5}{144}
$$

$$
\overline{BM} = 20.47 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $ \overline{GM} $**

$$
\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}
$$

$$
\overline{GM} = 0.8 + 20.47 - 2.2
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{GM} = 19.07 \ \text{m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** This is a *very* high GM, characteristic of catamarans. The wide spacing of the hulls gives an enormous transverse moment of inertia, which is precisely why catamarans are preferred for hydrographic survey — they remain nearly level even in beam winds, which is critical for echo-sounding accuracy.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Dynamic Positioning & Seakeeping `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **Dynamic Positioning (DP) System** in a research vessel. List its main components.

#### Model Solution:

A **Dynamic Positioning (DP) System** is a computer-controlled system that automatically maintains a vessel's position and heading (within a defined envelope, typically a circle of radius 5–50 m) using only the vessel's own thrusters — *without* the use of anchors or mooring lines.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Position Reference** — The vessel's actual position is measured continuously by one or more sensors:
   - **DGPS (Differential Global Positioning System)** for surface position.
   - **Acoustic positioning** (USBL, LBL) for relative position to a seabed transponder.
   - **Taut wire** for shallow-water station-keeping.
2. **Heading Reference** — Gyrocompass measures the vessel's heading.
3. **Environmental Sensors** — Anemometers (wind), motion reference units (wave-induced motion), and current sensors feed data on disturbances.
4. **DP Computer (Controller)** — Compares the *measured* position/heading against the *desired* setpoint. It runs a **Kalman filter** to combine the noisy sensor data into an optimal estimate of vessel position.
5. **Thruster Allocation** — The controller commands azimuth thrusters, bow thrusters, and main propellers to generate the **X-force (surge), Y-force (sway), and Yaw moment** needed to counteract environmental disturbances (wind, current, waves) and return the vessel to the setpoint.
6. **Feedback Loop** — Continuous repetition of the above steps at high frequency (1–10 Hz) keeps the vessel on station.

**Main Components:**
- Position reference sensors (DGPS, USBL, LBL, Taut wire)
- Heading & motion sensors (Gyro, MRU)
- Wind sensors
- DP controller (computer with redundancy)
- Thrusters (azimuth, tunnel)
- Power generation system (diesel-electric)
- Operator console on the bridge

**(b) [7 Marks]** A research vessel has $ \overline{GM} = 1.0 \ \text{m} $ and a radius of gyration $ k = 4.5 \ \text{m} $. Calculate the **natural period of roll**.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the formula for the natural period of roll**

$$
T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \, \overline{GM}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**

$$
T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(4.5)^2}{9.81 \times 1.0}}
$$

$$
T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{20.25}{9.81}}
$$

$$
T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{2.0642}
$$

$$
T_\phi = 2\pi \times 1.4368
$$

$$
T_\phi = 9.027 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> A period of roll of about **9 seconds** indicates a *sluggish, comfortable* roll — typical of a well-designed research vessel. If the period is *less than 6 s*, the vessel is *too stiff* (uncomfortable for crew, interferes with delicate scientific instruments). If *greater than 12 s*, the vessel is *too tender* and may capsize in beam seas.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{T_\phi \approx 9.03 \ \text{seconds (well within the comfort range)}}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common mark-losing mistakes in research-vessel numericals (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in twin-hull waterplane inertia.** When computing $ I_T $ for a catamaran, the parallel-axis theorem must be applied for *each* hull and the results summed. Students often compute $ I_T $ for *one* hull and forget the second hull — losing **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Mixing tonnes and kN without conversion.** When computing righting moment $ RM = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ $, the displacement must be in consistent units. If $ \Delta $ is given in tonnes, multiply by $ g = 9.81 $ to obtain weight in kN *before* multiplying by GZ (in metres). A common error is $ RM = 3781 \times 0.605 $, which yields wrong units.
>
> 3. **Using $ \rho = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3 $ when the problem specifies seawater.** Always re-read the problem. Seawater problems give $ \rho = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3 $ (or $ 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $) explicitly.
>
> 4. **Applying the small-angle formula $ GZ = \overline{GM} \sin\phi $ at $ \phi = 30^\circ $ or higher.** For angles beyond $ 15^\circ $, the exact GZ curve must be used (or the BN data must be read from a Bonjean diagram). Examiners frequently test this boundary to distinguish rote learners from real understanding.
>
> 5. **Confusing the "service speed" of a research vessel with a cargo ship.** Cargo ships do 20–25 knots; research vessels do 12–16 knots. Writing a wrong value in a descriptive question signals lack of subject familiarity — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
>
> 6. **In a 14-mark question, failing to draw a labelled sketch.** A 14-mark question on research vessels *always* benefits from a clear free-body / GZ-curve / hull-form sketch. Even a rough diagram shows the examiner you understand the geometry.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Research Vessels (Module 1, OENST611)**
>
> - [x] A **research vessel** is a *mission-driven floating laboratory*, optimised for data acquisition and instrument deployment rather than transport of cargo.
> - [x] The four classic mission-types: **Oceanographic, Hydrographic, Geophysical, Fisheries** (+ Polar, Multi-purpose).
> - [x] **Hull form options:** Monohull (deep ocean), Catamaran (coastal, stable), SWATH (ultra-stable), Trimaran (rare).
> - [x] **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** holds a vessel on station using azimuth thrusters + GPS/acoustic reference, *without* anchors.
> - [x] **Moon-pool** is an internal vertical shaft through the hull for all-weather instrument launch.
> - [x] **A-frame gantry** at the stern is the *signature* deployment feature.
> - [x] Seawater density: $ \rho = 1.025 \ \text{t/m}^3 $.
> - [x] Archimedes: $ \Delta = \rho g \nabla $.
> - [x] Block coefficient: $ C_B = \nabla / (L B T) $ — typical R/V value 0.55–0.70.
> - [x] Transverse stability: $ \overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} $.
> - [x] Twin-hull (catamaran) $ I_T $ requires the **parallel-axis theorem** with **factor of 2** for both hulls.
> - [x] Desired $ \overline{GM} $ for a research vessel: **0.15–0.50 m** (lower than cargo ships for comfort).
> - [x] Period of roll: $ T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot \overline{GM})} $ — comfort range **6–12 seconds**.
> - [x] Righting moment: $ RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ $ (ensure unit consistency).
> - [x] Service speed of RVs is **12–16 knots** — *slower* than commercial vessels.
> - [x] Endurance: typically **30–60 days**.
> - [x] Acoustic quietness (diesel-electric propulsion, isolated machinery) is essential to protect sonar data quality.
> - [x] For a coastal hydrographic survey vessel — **recommend catamaran** (stable platform, large deck).
> - [x] For deep-ocean global research — **recommend monohull** (large internal volume, good seakeeping in heavy seas).
> - [x] Always include a **labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual clarity.
> - [x] Always **state units explicitly** in every numerical step — examiners penalise silent conversions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Warships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ Module 1 — Warships: Naval Architecture of Combat Vessels

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Warship (Naval Vessel)** — A military ship built and primarily intended for combat purposes, designed and constructed according to naval architecture principles that prioritize **survivability, firepower delivery, sea-keeping, and operational endurance** over commercial metrics like cargo capacity or fuel economy.

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST611)** naval engineering terminology, a warship is fundamentally characterized by:

- **Displacement (Δ)** — The actual mass of water displaced by the hull, typically expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **long tons**. Warships are predominantly displacement-type or semi-displacement hull forms.
- **Combatant vs. Non-Combatant Classification** — Frigates, destroyers, cruisers, battleships, aircraft carriers, corvettes, submarines (combatant); auxiliaries, replenishment ships, hospital ships (non-combatant).
- **Stealth and Signature Reduction** — Radar Cross Section (RCS), Infrared Signature (IR), Underwater Radiated Noise (URN), and Magnetic Signature minimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Warships in floating bodies module emphasizes **hydrostatic stability**, **form coefficients**, **hull form**, **damage stability**, and **intact stability criteria** — all critical for combat survivability. The topic directly links Module 1 hydrostatics with naval design philosophy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Armored Floating Fortress" Analogy

Imagine a **medieval armored knight standing in a small fishing boat during a storm**. The boat must:
1. **Stay upright** when the knight shifts his weight suddenly (transverse stability)
2. **Not capsize** when hit by a wave from the side (metacentric height)
3. **Float** even when armor pieces (heavy equipment) are damaged or lost (damage stability)
4. **Cut through waves** without burying itself forward (bow flare, sheer line)
5. **Return to upright** after being tilted by an enemy hit (righting lever)

A warship is **precisely this** — a floating combat platform engineered for maximum resilience. Unlike merchant ships optimized for revenue per ton-mile, warships are optimized for **survivability per impact**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A merchant ship is like a *delivery truck* — efficient cargo mover. A warship is like a *safari jeep fitted with weapons* — every gram of weight serves combat or survival. This drives radically different design choices in **block coefficient (C_B)**, **length-to-beam ratio (L/B)**, and **metacentric height (GM)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Naval Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|--------|--------|------------------------|
| Sea-water density | ρ | **1.025 t/m³** (1025 kg/m³) |
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Block coefficient (warship) | C_B | 0.50 – 0.65 |
| Prismatic coefficient | C_P | 0.60 – 0.72 |
| Length-to-beam ratio | L/B | 6 : 1 – 9 : 1 |
| Beam-to-draft ratio | B/T | 2.5 : 1 – 3.5 : 1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Warships (Naval Taxonomy)

Warships are systematically classified based on **role, tonnage, and mission profile**:

### A. By Hull Form & Submergence

1. **Surface Ships** — Operate at the air-water interface
   - *Capital ships*: Battleships, Aircraft Carriers
   - *Escort vessels*: Destroyers, Frigates, Corvettes
   - *Patrol craft*: Fast Attack Craft (FAC), Patrol Boats
2. **Submersibles** — Operate below the surface
   - *Submarines*: Diesel-Electric (SSK), Nuclear (SSN, SSBN)
   - *Diving vessels*: Mini-subs, Swimmer Delivery Vehicles

### B. By Mission Role

| Class | Primary Role | Tonnage Range |
|-------|--------------|---------------|
| **Aircraft Carrier (CV)** | Air superiority, power projection | 30,000 – 100,000 t |
| **Destroyer (DDG)** | Anti-air, anti-sub, escort | 5,000 – 10,000 t |
| **Frigate (FFG)** | Anti-submarine warfare (ASW) | 3,000 – 6,000 t |
| **Corvette (FS)** | Coastal patrol, light combat | 500 – 2,500 t |
| **Submarine (SSK/SSN)** | Stealth, underwater strike | 1,000 – 12,000 t |

## 2.2 Design Philosophy — The Combat Triangle

Warship design revolves around the **"Iron Triangle of Naval Design"**:

1. **Firepower (Weapons & Sensors)** → Weight aloft, top-heavy
2. **Mobility (Speed & Endurance)** → Length, powerplant weight
3. **Protection (Armor & Survivability)** → Heavy plating, compartmentalization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three compete for the same **displacement budget (Δ)**. The naval architect's task is to **optimize the trade-off** — a destroyer carries less armor but more missiles; a battleship carries heavy armor at the cost of speed.

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Stability of Warships

The **metacentric height (GM)** is the single most critical stability parameter:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

Where:
- **KB** = Height of centre of buoyancy above keel
- **BM** = B/12 for rectangular sections (approx.) = **I_T / ∇** (true)
- **KG** = Height of centre of gravity above keel
- **I_T** = Transverse second moment of waterplane area
- **∇** = Volume of displacement

### Stability Criteria (KTU Naval Standards)

| Criterion | Required Value | Purpose |
|-----------|----------------|---------|
| Initial GM (intact) | **≥ 0.15 m** (frigate); **≥ 0.30 m** (carrier) | Static stability against small heel angles |
| Range of positive stability | **≥ 40°** either side | Capsize resistance |
| Area under GZ curve (0–30°) | **≥ 0.055 m·rad** | Energy to resist knockdown |
| Area (30°–40°) | **≥ 0.030 m·rad** | Second-stage stability |
| Maximum GZ at angle ≥ 25° | — | Prevents excessive list |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Warship Hydrostatics

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All warship stability problems reduce to these six fundamental equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement = density × gravity × submerged volume | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $W = \Delta \cdot g$ | Total weight of vessel (Archimedes' principle) | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $KB = \frac{I_T}{V \cdot BM}$ relationship | Centre of buoyancy height via integration | metres (m) |
| 4 | $BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius (transverse) | metres (m) |
| 5 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | metres (m) |
| 6 | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\phi)$ (small φ) | Righting lever at small heel angle | metres (m) |
| 7 | $\tan(\phi) = \frac{\overline{GG'}}{GM}$ | Angle of heel due to shift of weight | degrees / radians |
| 8 | $BM_L = \frac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | Longitudinal metacentric radius (very large) | metres (m) |
| 9 | $C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Warship-Specific Stability Challenges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Surface Effect (FSE):** Warships carry large partially-filled tanks (fuel, water, ballast). Liquid shifting in a partially-filled tank **effectively raises the virtual G** by a quantity:

$$GG'_{\text{FSE}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{liquid}} \cdot i}{\Delta}$$

Where $i$ = transverse second moment of free-surface area. **KTU problems frequently ask students to compute FSE reduction in GM.**

### Damage Stability (Survivability)

Warships are subdivided into **watertight compartments** by transverse bulkheads. After flooding of one or more compartments, the vessel must still float with positive residual stability. The **permeability (μ)** of each compartment represents the fraction of its volume occupied by water:

$$\Delta_{\text{flooded}} = \mu \cdot \nabla_{\text{compartment}}$$

| Compartment Type | Permeability (μ) |
|------------------|------------------|
| Machinery spaces | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Storage / cargo | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| Accommodation | 0.95 |
| Void spaces | 0.00 – 0.10 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Damage Control (DC):** Warship crews train to counter flooding using dewatering pumps, collision mats, and compartment isolation.
- **Submarine Design:** Double hulls and ballast tanks allow controlled submergence; critical GM when surfaced.
- **Aircraft Carrier Catapults:** Require longitudinal stability (GM_L) high enough to handle launching 30-ton aircraft from the bow.
- **Stealth Hulls:** Tumblehome hull forms (e.g., Zumwalt-class destroyers) reduce RCS but complicate stability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Warship Metacentric Height (GM)

### Problem Context
A frigate of **displacement W = 3,500 tonnes**, length L = **120 m**, beam B = **14 m**, draft T = **4.2 m**, and centre of gravity KG = **5.8 m above keel**. Find the metacentric height GM.

### Step 1 — Compute the Submerged Volume (∇)

Using Archimedes' principle with sea-water density **ρ = 1.025 t/m³**:

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{W_{\text{displacement}}}{\rho}$$

Since **W (weight) = Δ (displacement mass) = 3,500 t**:

$$\nabla = \frac{3500 \text{ t}}{1.025 \text{ t/m}^3}$$

$$\nabla = 3414.63 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Waterplane Second Moment (I_T)

For a rectangular waterplane of length L and beam B:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

$$I_T = \frac{120 \cdot (14)^3}{12}$$

$$I_T = \frac{120 \cdot 2744}{12}$$

$$I_T = \frac{329280}{12} = 27440 \text{ m}^4$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Metacentric Radius (BM)

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$$

$$BM = \frac{27440}{3414.63} = 8.035 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute KB (Centre of Buoyancy Above Keel)

For a hull of prismatic form, KB is approximately **T/2** (for a uniform rectangular block). For a more realistic warship hull with C_P ≈ 0.65, KB ≈ **0.53 × T**:

$$KB \approx 0.53 \times 4.2 = 2.226 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Metacentric Formula

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

$$GM = 2.226 + 8.035 - 5.8$$

$$GM = 4.461 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6 — Validate Against KTU Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GM = **4.461 m** is unrealistically high for a frigate (typical 0.5 – 1.5 m). The discrepancy arises because the rectangular waterplane overestimates I_T. In practice, naval architects use the **actual waterplane area coefficient (C_WP)**:

$$I_T^{\text{actual}} = C_{WP} \cdot \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

With **C_WP ≈ 0.78**:

$$I_T^{\text{actual}} = 0.78 \times 27440 = 21403.2 \text{ m}^4$$

$$BM_{\text{actual}} = \frac{21403.2}{3414.63} = 6.267 \text{ m}$$

$$GM_{\text{actual}} = 2.226 + 6.267 - 5.8 = 2.693 \text{ m}$$

**GM ≈ 2.7 m** — realistic for a modern ASW frigate. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivations Matrix — All Core Warship Formulas

| # | Formula | Derivation / Logic | Where Used |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------|
| D1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Archimedes' principle; weight of displaced fluid equals buoyant force | All hydrostatics |
| D2 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Definition of metacentric radius from metacentre geometry | Stability |
| D3 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Geometric construction of metacentre relative to G and B | Stability |
| D4 | $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ | Small-angle approximation of righting lever | Intact stability |
| D5 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot a}{\Delta \cdot GM}$ | Heel angle due to transverse weight shift | Loading conditions |
| D6 | $\Delta G_{\text{FSE}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{\Delta}$ | Free surface effect on virtual G rise | Tank management |
| D7 | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W}{100}$ (in cm) | Tonnes per centimetre immersion | Loading |
| D8 | $MCT = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot GM_L}{100 \cdot L}$ (tonne·cm) | Moment to change trim 1 cm | Trim calculations |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Damage Stability of a Destroyer

### Problem
A destroyer of displacement **Δ = 6,000 t**, GM = **1.2 m**, beam B = **16 m**, length L = **140 m**, is holed amidships. One machinery compartment (length 12 m, full beam, full depth) is flooded with **permeability μ = 0.85**. Find:
1. New displacement (added weight)
2. New waterline and final equilibrium

### Step 1 — Flooded Compartment Volume

$$\nabla_{\text{compartment}} = L_c \cdot B \cdot T = 12 \times 16 \times 4.5 = 864 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Flooded Water Mass (Added Weight)

$$W_{\text{flood}} = \mu \cdot \nabla_{\text{compartment}} \cdot \rho_{\text{sw}}$$

$$W_{\text{flood}} = 0.85 \times 864 \times 1.025$$

$$W_{\text{flood}} = 752.76 \text{ t}$$

### Step 3 — New Total Displacement

$$\Delta_{\text{new}} = 6000 + 752.76 = 6752.76 \text{ t}$$

### Step 4 — Loss of GM Due to Flooding

The flooded compartment reduces waterplane inertia and raises virtual G. Naval rule of thumb: **GM loss ≈ 0.3 to 0.5 × GM_original** for one-compartment flooding.

$$GM_{\text{new}} \approx 1.2 \times 0.65 = 0.78 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> GM = **0.78 m** is positive → vessel survives but with **reduced margin of safety**. Damage control teams must counter-flood or dewater immediately.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Warship Design Constraints

| Design Parameter | Symbol | Typical Warship Range | Limitation |
|------------------|--------|------------------------|------------|
| Block coefficient | C_B | 0.50 – 0.65 | Lower C_B → finer hull → speed ↑ but volume ↓ |
| Length/Beam | L/B | 6 – 9 | High L/B → speed & SLF stability, but poor maneuverability |
| Beam/Draft | B/T | 2.5 – 3.5 | High B/T → initial GM ↑, but heavier roll |
| Prismatic | C_P | 0.60 – 0.72 | Hull form efficiency |
| Midship coefficient | C_M | 0.85 – 0.95 | Hull fullness amidships |
| Waterplane coefficient | C_WP | 0.75 – 0.85 | Affects BM and GM |

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Question 1: Define "Metacentric Height" of a warship. Why is it critical for naval vessels? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Metacentric Height (GM)** is the vertical distance between the **centre of gravity (G)** of the vessel and the **metacentre (M)**. It is a measure of the **initial transverse static stability** of a floating body.

> Mathematically: $\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}$

> **Importance for warships:**
> 1. Determines ability to **return upright** after being heeled by enemy fire, waves, or weight shifts.
> 2. A warship requires **positive GM at all loading conditions** — otherwise it will capsize.
> 3. **Damage stability** post-combat hit depends critically on residual GM after flooding.

> **Typical warship GM range:** 0.5 m (submarine surfaced) to 1.5 m (frigate), up to 3.0 m for some corvettes.

---

### Question 2: What is "Block Coefficient" and how does it differ between a warship and a merchant ship? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Block Coefficient (C_B)** is the ratio of the **submerged volume of the hull** to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length, beam, and draft:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

> **Warship C_B range:** **0.50 – 0.65** (finer, faster hull)
> **Merchant ship C_B range:** **0.65 – 0.85** (fuller, cargo-efficient hull)
> 
> Warships have **lower C_B** because:
> - Priority is **speed and maneuverability** over cargo volume
> - Lower C_B → reduced hydrodynamic resistance → higher speed
> - Combat vessels carry **dense, high-value equipment** (weapons, sensors) in less volume

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📘 Question A — Warship Stability and Damage Survivability **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question May 2024 Style]**

A warship of **displacement Δ = 4,500 tonnes**, length L = **130 m**, beam B = **15 m**, draft T = **5.0 m**, and centre of gravity KG = **6.0 m** has waterplane coefficient C_WP = **0.80**.

#### Part (a) — Compute the Metacentric Height (GM) **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute displaced volume ∇**

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{4500}{1.025} = 4390.24 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute transverse second moment of waterplane (rectangular approximation)**

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{130 \times 15^3}{12} = \frac{130 \times 3375}{12} = 36562.5 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3: Apply waterplane coefficient correction**

$$I_T^{\text{actual}} = C_{WP} \times I_T = 0.80 \times 36562.5 = 29250 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4: Compute BM**

$$BM = \frac{I_T^{\text{actual}}}{\nabla} = \frac{29250}{4390.24} = 6.662 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute KB** (assuming C_P = 0.65)

$$KB \approx 0.53 \times T = 0.53 \times 5.0 = 2.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Compute GM**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

$$GM = 2.65 + 6.662 - 6.0$$

$$\boxed{GM = 3.312 \text{ m}}$$

> **Validation:** This is at the upper end of warship GM range, indicating excellent initial stability suitable for a frigate. ✓

#### Part (b) — Effect of Damage on Stability **[7 Marks]**

**Scenario:** One midship compartment (length 10 m, full beam B = 15 m, full depth = 5 m) is holed and flooded with permeability **μ = 0.90**. Determine:
(i) Added weight from flooding
(ii) New GM after damage (assume KB rises by 0.3 m and BM reduces to 5.0 m due to loss of effective waterplane)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flooded compartment volume:**

$$\nabla_{\text{comp}} = L_c \times B \times T = 10 \times 15 \times 5.0 = 750 \text{ m}^3$$

**Added weight of floodwater:**

$$W_{\text{flood}} = \mu \times \nabla_{\text{comp}} \times \rho_{sw}$$

$$W_{\text{flood}} = 0.90 \times 750 \times 1.025 = 691.88 \text{ t}$$

**New total displacement:**

$$\Delta_{\text{new}} = 4500 + 691.88 = 5191.88 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) New GM after damage:**

Given:
- New KB = 2.65 + 0.30 = **2.95 m** (buoyancy centre rises as vessel sits lower)
- New BM = **5.0 m** (effective waterplane inertia reduced)
- KG also rises as floodwater mass is added at compartment level: new KG ≈ **6.15 m**

$$GM_{\text{damaged}} = KB_{\text{new}} + BM_{\text{new}} - KG_{\text{new}}$$

$$GM_{\text{damaged}} = 2.95 + 5.0 - 6.15$$

$$\boxed{GM_{\text{damaged}} = 1.80 \text{ m}}$$

**Conclusion:**
- Original GM = 3.31 m → Damaged GM = 1.80 m
- **Loss of GM = 1.51 m (≈ 45.6 % reduction)**
- Damaged vessel **still floats with positive stability** — survives the hit, but with **significantly reduced safety margin**.
- Damage control teams must dewater rapidly to restore stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the waterplane coefficient correction** — students often use full rectangular I_T, overestimating GM by 20-30%.
> 2. **Not updating KG after flooding** — the floodwater mass acts at the compartment centroid and raises the VIRTUAL G.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — ρ must be in **t/m³** if Δ is in tonnes and ∇ in m³.
> 4. **Ignoring free surface effect** in partially filled tanks during damage scenarios.

---

### 📗 Question B — Warship Hull Form and Form Coefficients **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module Choice]**

#### Part (a) — Explain Warship Hull Form Design **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> A warship hull form is designed to optimize **three conflicting requirements**: speed, stability, and survivability. Key form parameters:

**1. Block Coefficient (C_B):**
- $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$
- Warships: **0.50 – 0.65** (low — emphasizes speed)
- A lower C_B implies a **finer, more streamlined hull** with reduced wave-making resistance.

**2. Prismatic Coefficient (C_P):**
- $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$
- Warships: **0.60 – 0.72** — indicates moderate fullness at midships, fine ends.

**3. Midship Coefficient (C_M):**
- $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$
- Warships: **0.85 – 0.95** — fuller midships for buoyancy and internal volume.

**4. Waterplane Coefficient (C_WP):**
- $C_WP = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$
- Warships: **0.75 – 0.85** — affects **GM** through transverse moment of inertia.

**Hull Form Features:**

| Feature | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **Bow flare** | Deflects water, prevents deck wetness |
| **Sheer line** | Raised forward & aft for reserve buoyancy |
| **Tumblehome** | Inward-sloping above WL — radar signature reduction (Zumwalt-class) |
| **Hard chine** | Sharp transition for planing at speed (FAC, patrol boats) |
| **Round bilge** | Smooth flow, low resistance (destroyers, frigates) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Form Coefficients **[7 Marks]**

**Problem:** A corvette has L = **90 m**, B = **11 m**, T = **3.5 m**, midship area A_M = **30.8 m²**, and displaced volume ∇ = **2,150 m³**. Find C_B, C_M, and C_P.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Coefficient**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{2150}{90 \times 11 \times 3.5}$$

$$C_B = \frac{2150}{3465} = 0.6205$$

$$\boxed{C_B \approx 0.62}$$

**Step 2: Midship Coefficient**

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{30.8}{11 \times 3.5} = \frac{30.8}{38.5}$$

$$\boxed{C_M = 0.80}$$

**Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient**

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{2150}{30.8 \times 90} = \frac{2150}{2772}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 0.7756}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** C_B = 0.62 (typical warship), C_M = 0.80 (moderately full midships), C_P = 0.78 (fine ends relative to midships) → this is a **fast, streamlined corvette hull** suitable for coastal patrol. The high C_P combined with low C_B suggests **fine, hydrodynamic entrance and run**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Critical Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Areas in Warship Stability Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply C_WP correction** to I_T — loses 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up BM (metacentric radius) with GM (metacentric height)** — fundamental conceptual error.
> 3. **Not stating units explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct for ambiguous units in numerical answers.
> 4. **Confusing displacement W (weight in N) with Δ (mass in t)** — use ρ·g·∇ for weight.
> 5. **In damage stability problems, forgetting to update KG** after adding floodwater mass.
> 6. **Drawing free body diagrams without showing the shifted B' and new vertical through B'** — essential for full marks on heel problems.
> 7. **Rounding intermediate steps** — carry 4-5 significant figures until final answer.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Warships (Module 1)**

### 🚢 Warship Classification
- ✦ Combatants: **Carrier, Cruiser, Destroyer, Frigate, Corvette, Submarine**
- ✦ Non-combatants: **Tankers, Replenishment, Hospital ships, Tenders**
- ✦ Mission roles: **Strike, ASW, AAW, MCM, Amphibious**

### 📐 Key Hydrostatic Parameters
- ✦ **Δ = ρ·∇** (displacement from Archimedes)
- ✦ **GM = KB + BM − KG** (stability master equation)
- ✦ **BM = I_T / ∇** (metacentric radius)
- ✦ **I_T = C_WP × L·B³/12** (waterplane second moment)
- ✦ **C_B = ∇ / (L·B·T)** (block coefficient, 0.50–0.65 for warships)
- ✦ **C_P = ∇ / (A_M·L)** (prismatic coefficient)

### ⚖️ Stability Criteria (Warship-Specific)
- ✦ Initial GM ≥ **0.15 – 0.30 m** (mission-dependent)
- ✦ Range of positive stability ≥ **40°**
- ✦ GZ curve area (0–30°) ≥ **0.055 m·rad**
- ✦ GZ curve area (30°–40°) ≥ **0.030 m·rad**
- ✦ Maximum GZ at angle ≥ **25°**

### 💥 Damage Stability Essentials
- ✦ Watertight compartmentalization via **transverse bulkheads**
- ✦ Flooded weight: **W_flood = μ × ∇_comp × ρ_sw**
- ✦ Permeability (μ): machinery **0.85–0.95**, accommodation **0.95**, voids **0–0.10**
- ✦ Free surface effect: **ΔG_FSE = ρ·liquid·i / Δ**
- ✦ Loss of GM post-damage must leave **positive residual GM**

### ⚔️ Warship Design Priorities
- ✦ **Survivability > Speed > Firepower > Comfort** (hierarchy of design drivers)
- ✦ Stealth: RCS, IR, URN, magnetic signature reduction
- ✦ Hull forms: **round-bilge, hard-chine, tumblehome, catamaran**
- ✦ Length/Beam ratio: **6:1 – 9:1** for surface combatants
- ✦ Beam/Draft ratio: **2.5:1 – 3.5:1** for initial GM optimization

### 🧠 Exam Quick-Fire Facts
- ✦ Sea-water density = **1.025 t/m³**
- ✦ TPC = A_WP / 100 (tonnes per cm immersion)
- ✦ Submarine surfaced GM = **0.2 – 0.5 m** (very low!)
- ✦ Aircraft carrier GM ≈ **1.5 – 3.0 m** (high to handle topweight)
- ✦ Corvette GM ≈ **0.8 – 2.5 m**

---
