topics.title: High Speed Crafts such as Hydrofoils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Speed Crafts such as Hydrofoils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hydrofoil** is a marine vessel or a structural lifting surface that, when moving through water at sufficient velocity, generates hydrodynamic lift by means of underwater foils (wing-like blades). At low speeds, the vessel behaves as a conventional displacement craft, but above a critical **take-off speed**, the lift produced by the foils exceeds the vessel's weight, raising the hull completely out of the water. The craft is then supported entirely by the dynamic reaction of the water on the submerged foils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "High-speed craft" refers to vessels capable of sustained speeds generally greater than **30 knots** (≈ **15.43 m/s**), where conventional hull forms encounter excessive wave-making resistance. Hydrofoils belong to this category along with surface-effect ships, planing craft, and air-cushion vehicles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **airplane wing moving through water instead of air**. The airfoil shape (curved on top, flat on bottom) causes water to flow faster over the upper surface and slower under the lower surface. By **Bernoulli's Principle**, this pressure difference generates an upward force called **lift**. 

In a hydrofoil boat:
- **At rest / low speed** → the hull sits in the water like a normal boat (supported by buoyancy, $F_B = \rho g V$).
- **Speeding up** → water rushes past the underwater foils.
- **Above take-off speed** → lift force ($F_L$) ≥ weight ($W$); the hull lifts out of the water, drastically reducing drag.
- The boat now "flies" through water on stilts (the foils), like an aeroplane on a runway.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Since water is ~ **800 times denser** than air, a hydrofoil can generate the same lift as an aircraft wing at a speed roughly **28 times slower** (since lift ∝ ρV²). This is why hydrofoils are practical marine vehicles, while "water-wings" at aircraft speeds would be absurd.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of freshwater | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu$ | ~1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| 1 knot | — | **0.5144 m/s** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Submerged Foil Generates Lift:** As the hydrofoil accelerates, water flows over the cambered foil section. The camber (curvature) creates a higher velocity on the suction side and lower velocity on the pressure side.
2. **Pressure Differential:** By Bernoulli's equation, the higher velocity implies lower pressure on top of the foil; the lower velocity implies higher pressure beneath. Net upward force = **Lift**.
3. **Take-Off Condition:** Lift force $F_L$ equals the total weight $W$ of the craft (including payload).
4. **Foiling Mode:** Once airborne (hull clear of water), the dominant resistance is **foil drag** (mostly viscous and induced), which is **far less** than wave-making drag of a displacement hull.
5. **Stability:** Foils are often arranged in tandem (front and rear) or in a V or ladder configuration to provide **pitch and roll stability**.

### 2.2 Classification of Hydrofoils (KTU-Relevant Taxonomy)

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface-Piercing Foils** | Foil struts penetrate the water surface; lift varies with depth | Smaller crafts, high manoeuvrability |
| **Fully Submerged Foils** | All foils remain below water; controlled by flaps/autopilot | Passenger ferries (Boeing 929) |
| **V-Foil Configuration** | Foils splayed outward in V-shape | Stable, used in commercial hydrofoils |
| **Ladder Foil** | Multiple horizontal foils stacked vertically on a single strut | Military patrol boats |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why High Speed?** A conventional displacement hull is limited by the **hull speed formula** $V_{max} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ (in knots, with $L_{WL}$ in feet). Hydrofoils break this barrier by removing hull contact with water.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exhaustively examinable in KTU 2024 ESE and Continuous Evaluation tests.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_L$ | Hydrodynamic lift force on foil | N |
| 2 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_D$ | Hydrodynamic drag force on foil | N |
| 3 | $F_B = \rho\,g\,V_{disp}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes' Principle) | N |
| 4 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}$ | Dynamic pressure of water flow | Pa |
| 5 | $V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_L}}$ | Take-off speed of the hydrofoil | m/s |
| 6 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V c}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (viscous regime check) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P = F_D \cdot V$ | Power required to overcome drag | W |
| 8 | $\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_D}$ | Lift-to-drag ratio (efficiency) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{hull} \approx 1.34\,\sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Hull-speed limit (displacement craft) | knots |
| 10 | $F_{net} = F_L - W$ | Net upward force during foiling | N |

> **Notation Glossary:** $V$ = forward velocity of craft, $S$ = planform area of foil, $C_L$ = lift coefficient, $C_D$ = drag coefficient, $W$ = total weight, $c$ = chord length, $L_{WL}$ = waterline length.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Transport:** Russian *Raketa*-class hydrofoils and Italian *Rodriquez* vessels operate as river/coastal ferries at 35–40 knots.
- **Military Patrol:** US Navy *Pegasus*-class PHMs (Patrol Hydrofoil Missile) reached **48 knots** (≈ 24.6 m/s).
- **Recreational:** Modern e-foils (electric hydrofoils) used in leisure and surfboard design.
- **Research Platform:** Hydrofoils are used to study unsteady hydrodynamics, ventilation, and cavitation in naval architecture labs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Lift Force from Bernoulli's Principle

We start with the flow of an incompressible, inviscid fluid over a cambered foil. Let points **1** (lower surface) and **2** (upper surface) lie on the same streamline.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between points 1 and 2 (assuming $z_1 \approx z_2$ since foil thickness is small):

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2} = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the pressure difference** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate pressure difference** over the planform area $S$ of the foil to obtain total lift:

$$F_L = \int_S \Delta P \, dS = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}\right) S$$

**Step 4 — Define a dimensionless lift coefficient** $C_L$ to absorb the velocity-squared dependence into a geometry-dependent term:

$$C_L \equiv \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{V^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** $C_L$ into the lift expression:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The lift coefficient $C_L$ depends on the **angle of attack** $\alpha$, **camber**, **aspect ratio**, and **Reynolds number**. For a typical NACA-series foil at $\alpha = 4°$, $C_L \approx 0.6$ to 1.0.

### 3.2 Derivation of Take-Off Speed

The hydrofoil lifts off the water when lift equals total weight: $F_L = W$.

**Step 1 — Equate lift to weight** using the derived formula:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{TO}^{2}\,S\,C_L = W$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the velocity term**:

$$V_{TO}^{2} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L}$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root**:

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style):**
> A hydrofoil weighs **W = 200 kN** with foil area **S = 4 m²** and lift coefficient **C_L = 0.8** in seawater ($\rho$ = 1025 kg/m³). Find the take-off speed.

**Solution:**

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 200{,}000}{1025 \times 4 \times 0.8}}$$

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400{,}000}{3280}}$$

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{121.95}$$

$$\boxed{V_{TO} \approx 11.04 \text{ m/s} \approx 21.5 \text{ knots}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Power Required at Foiling Speed

Once airborne, the dominant resistance is foil drag $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$. The thrust power needed to maintain foiling speed $V_f$ is:

**Step 1 — Define power** as force times velocity:

$$P = F_D \cdot V_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_f^{3} S C_D$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of weight** using $W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{TO}^{2} S C_L$:

$$P = W V_f \cdot \dfrac{C_D}{C_L} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_{TO}}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cubic dependence on velocity ($V^{3}$) explains why hydrofoil power requirements rise steeply at higher speeds — this is the **fundamental engineering trade-off** in high-speed marine design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Condition | Governing Equation | Physical Regime |
|---|---|---|
| Foil fully submerged, pre-stall | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$ | Attached flow, linear $C_L$ vs $\alpha$ |
| Hull in water (low speed) | $F_B = \rho g V_{disp}$ | Displacement / Archimedean regime |
| Foil near free surface | Modified $C_L$ (Kutta–Joukowski + image method) | Ventilation / wave-inception effects |
| Above hull speed limit | Wave-making drag dominates | Transonic–supersonic (in water) analogy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forces & Components of a Hydrofoil Craft

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hull / Payload] --> B[Total Weight W]
    B --> C{Force Balance F_L = W}
    C --> D[Hydrodynamic Lift F_L]
    D --> E[Submerged Foils]
    E --> F[Strut Assembly]
    F --> G[Foil Drag F_D]
    G --> H[Thrust System: Propeller / Waterjet]
    H --> I[Forward Velocity V]
    I --> E
    C --> J[Foiling Mode ON]
    C --> K[Hull Clear of Water]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Reduced Wave Drag]
    L --> M[High Sustained Speed]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrofoil Geometry & Pressure Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Surface] --> B[Foil Strut]
    B --> C[Suction Surface: V high, P low]
    B --> D[Pressure Surface: V low, P high]
    C --> E[Delta P = P_pressure - P_suction]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Net Upward Force F_L]
    F --> G[Hull Lifts Out of Water]
    G --> H[Take-off Achieved]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Hydrofoil Configuration Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CFG1[Surface-Piercing Foil Configuration]
        SP1[Foil 1 Left] --- SP2[Foil 2 Right]
        SP1 --- SP3[Hull]
        SP2 --- SP3
    end

    subgraph CFG2[Submerged V-Foil Configuration]
        SF1[Front V-Foil] --- SF2[Rear V-Foil]
        SF1 --- SF3[Hull Above Waterline]
        SF2 --- SF3
    end

    subgraph CFG3[Ladder Foil Configuration]
        LF1[Upper Foil] --> LF2[Middle Foil]
        LF2 --> LF3[Lower Foil]
        LF3 --> LF4[Strut]
        LF4 --> LF5[Hull]
    end
```

### 4.4 Free-Body Force Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Weight W acts downward] --> HULL[Hull]
    LIFT[Lift F_L acts upward from foils] --> HULL
    HULL --> DRAG[Drag F_D acts backward on foils]
    DRAG --> THRUST[Thrust T acts forward]
    HULL --> B[Buoyancy F_B: zero in foiling mode]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the foiling regime, **buoyancy contribution is approximately zero** because the hull is clear of water. Lift $F_L$ balances weight $W$, and thrust $T$ balances drag $F_D$.

### 4.5 Operating-Regime Transition Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rest: Buoyancy supports W] --> B[Displacement Mode: V less than V_TO]
    B --> C[Pre-Take-off: F_L increasing with V squared]
    C --> D[Take-off: F_L equals W]
    D --> E[Foiling Mode: Hull airborne]
    E --> F[High Speed: F_D dominant, P proportional to V cubed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a hydrofoil. What is its primary purpose in marine engineering?**

**Model Answer:**
A hydrofoil is a marine vessel fitted with submerged wing-like foils that generate hydrodynamic lift when the craft moves through water at sufficient speed. Its **primary purpose** is to **raise the hull above the water surface**, thereby eliminating wave-making resistance and enabling sustained speeds well beyond the hull-speed limit of conventional displacement vessels. The result is reduced fuel consumption, higher operational speed (typically 35–50 knots), and improved seakeeping.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] State the formula for hydrodynamic lift on a submerged foil. What is the significance of the lift coefficient $C_L$?**

**Model Answer:**
The hydrodynamic lift on a submerged foil is given by:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$$

The **lift coefficient $C_L$** is a dimensionless number that captures the geometric and flow-dependent efficiency of the foil. It depends on the angle of attack, camber, aspect ratio, and Reynolds number. A higher $C_L$ (with the same $S$) reduces the take-off speed, which is a primary design goal in hydrofoil engineering.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for hydrodynamic lift on a submerged foil from first principles, starting from Bernoulli's equation. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A passenger hydrofoil has a total weight of **350 kN** and operates with two submerged foils, each of planform area **3.0 m²**. The lift coefficient of the foils is **$C_L = 0.85$** in seawater ($\rho$ = 1025 kg/m³). Calculate:
- (i) the take-off speed of the craft,
- (ii) the lift generated at a cruising speed of **15 m/s**,
- (iii) the Reynolds number assuming chord length **c = 0.6 m** and dynamic viscosity **$\mu = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s**.

##### **Model Solution (a) — Derivation of Lift**

**Assumptions:**
- Fluid is incompressible, inviscid, and steady.
- Foil is fully submerged (free-surface effects neglected at first order).
- Pressure difference integrated over planform area yields total lift.

**Derivation:**

Apply Bernoulli's equation between a point on the lower surface (1) and upper surface (2) of the foil at the same elevation:

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2} = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2}$$

Rearranging for the pressure difference:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}\right)$$

The total lift is the integral of pressure difference over the planform area $S$:

$$F_L = \int_S \Delta P \, dS$$

For a uniform approximation of velocities:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}\right) S$$

Define the lift coefficient $C_L$ such that $V_2^{2} - V_1^{2} = C_L V^{2}$, where $V$ is the free-stream velocity:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L \qquad \blacksquare$$

##### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computations**

**(i) Take-off speed** $V_{TO}$:
At take-off, $F_L = W$:

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_L}}$$

Total foil area $S = 2 \times 3.0 = 6.0$ m²:

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 350{,}000}{1025 \times 6.0 \times 0.85}}$$

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{700{,}000}{5227.5}}$$

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{133.91} \approx 11.57 \text{ m/s}$$

Convert to knots: $V_{TO} \approx 22.5$ **knots**.

**(ii) Lift at V = 15 m/s**:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 15^{2} \times 6.0 \times 0.85$$

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 225 \times 5.1$$

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1147.5$$

$$\boxed{F_L \approx 588{,}281 \text{ N} \approx 588.3 \text{ kN}}$$

Since $F_L > W$ (350 kN), the craft will indeed be in the **foiling mode** at this speed. Net upward force:

$$F_{net} = F_L - W = 588.3 - 350 = 238.3 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Reynolds number** at $V = 15$ m/s:

$$\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V c}{\mu} = \dfrac{1025 \times 15 \times 0.6}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \dfrac{9225}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 8.79 \times 10^{6}$$

This high Reynolds number indicates **fully turbulent flow** over the foils — viscous drag would dominate in this regime.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the difference between **surface-piercing** and **fully submerged** hydrofoil configurations. Discuss the merits and demerits of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrofoil craft cruises at **20 m/s** in seawater with total weight **500 kN**. The foil drag coefficient is **$C_D = 0.04$** and total foil planform area is **8 m²**. Calculate:
- (i) the total drag force on the foils,
- (ii) the power required to overcome this drag,
- (iii) the lift-to-drag ratio (L/D) at the cruising speed, given that $C_L = 0.7$.

##### **Model Solution (a) — Configuration Comparison**

| Feature | Surface-Piercing | Fully Submerged |
|---|---|---|
| **Foil Position** | Strut cuts water surface | All foils below waterline |
| **Lift Control** | **Passive** — depth changes with speed give natural stability | **Active** — requires flaps & autopilot |
| **Stability** | Self-stabilising (deeper at high speed) | Requires control system |
| **Sea-state Tolerance** | Lower (surface effects) | Higher (operates below waves) |
| **Complexity** | Simple, robust | Complex control electronics |
| **Examples** | *Raketa*, *Volga* | Boeing Jetfoil 929, *Pegasus* PHM |

**Merits of surface-piercing:** inherent hydrodynamic stability, no active control needed, mechanically simple, robust in operations.
**Demerits of surface-piercing:** ventilation at high speed, sea-state sensitivity, spray noise.
**Merits of fully submerged:** superior rough-water performance, higher achievable speeds, consistent lift.
**Demerits of fully submerged:** requires sophisticated autopilot and flap controls, vulnerable to depth-keeping failures.

##### **Model Solution (b) — Drag and Power Computations**

**(i) Drag force on foils**:

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 20^{2} \times 8 \times 0.04$$

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 400 \times 0.32$$

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 128$$

$$\boxed{F_D = 65{,}600 \text{ N} = 65.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Power required**:

$$P = F_D \cdot V = 65{,}600 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{P = 1{,}312{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 1.31 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Lift-to-drag ratio**:

First, compute lift at $V = 20$ m/s:

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 20^{2} \times 8 \times 0.7 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 400 \times 5.6$$

$$F_L = 1{,}148{,}000 \text{ N} = 1148 \text{ kN}$$

Verify foiling condition: $F_L = 1148$ kN $\gg W = 500$ kN ✓ (the craft is well airborne).

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{F_L}{F_D} = \dfrac{1{,}148{,}000}{65{,}600} \approx 17.5$$

Or equivalently:

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{C_L}{C_D} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.04} = 17.5 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Mixing $S$ (single foil) vs total area:** Always sum all foil areas before substituting.
> 2. **Units:** Density $\rho$ in kg/m³ (not g/cm³); speed in m/s (not knots) when using SI formulas.
> 3. **Sign convention:** Lift is upward, weight downward; net upward force is $F_L - W$, not $W - F_L$.
> 4. **Re-check the foiling condition:** Always verify $F_L \geq W$ at the given speed; if not, the craft is still in displacement mode and drag includes wave-making terms.
> 5. **Power formula:** $P = F_D \cdot V$ (force × velocity), not $P = F_D \cdot V^{2}$.
> 6. **Square-root isolation:** $V_{TO} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_L)}$ — students often forget the square root and lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — High-Density Summary**

- **Definition:** A hydrofoil uses **submerged wing-like foils** to generate hydrodynamic lift and lift the hull clear of water at high speeds.
- **Key Formula:** $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_L$ — derived from Bernoulli's principle.
- **Take-off Speed:** $V_{TO} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_L)}$ — design goal is to **minimise this**.
- **Drag Formula:** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$ — dominant resistance in foiling mode.
- **Power Scaling:** $P \propto V^{3}$ — power demand grows **cubic** with speed.
- **Lift-to-Drag Ratio:** $L/D = C_L / C_D$ — high values indicate efficient foils (typical: 15–25).
- **Density of seawater:** **1025 kg/m³** (use 1000 for freshwater).
- **Two Main Configurations:** **Surface-piercing** (passively stable) and **fully submerged** (actively controlled, better seakeeping).
- **Common Examples:** Boeing 929 Jetfoil (passenger), Pegasus-class PHM (military), Raketa (river ferry).
- **Hull-Speed Limit:** Conventional displacement vessels are limited by $V \approx 1.34\sqrt{L_{WL}}$; hydrofoils overcome this.
- **Reynolds Regime:** At operating speeds, hydrofoil flow is **fully turbulent** ($\text{Re} \sim 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$).
- **Stability Methods:** Tandem foils, V-foils, ladder foils, active flaps.
- **Cavitation Risk:** At very high speeds, low local pressure may cause cavitation — a critical design constraint.
- **KTU Trick Question:** "What supports the hydrofoil in foiling mode?" — Answer: **Hydrodynamic lift from foils**, **NOT buoyancy**.
- **Numerical Hygiene:** Always verify $F_L \geq W$ before assuming foiling mode; if not, you're still in displacement regime.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Cushion Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Cushion Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV)**, also termed an **Air-Cushion Craft** or **Ground-Effect Machine (GEM)**, is an amphibious or surface-piercing marine craft that supports its entire operational weight upon a thin, deliberately engineered **pressurized layer (cushion) of air** confined between the vehicle hull and the supporting surface (water, land, ice, mud, or marsh). The air cushion is generated and maintained by a dedicated **lift system**, while forward motion is imparted by a separate **propulsion system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> An Air Cushion Vehicle is a transportation machine in which a significant portion of the total weight is supported by a forcibly-generated, artificially-confined, low-pressure air film whose static pressure exceeds ambient atmospheric pressure, thereby creating a controlled mechanical gap between the rigid hull structure and the operating surface.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine inflating a flat balloon and letting it rest on a smooth table. If you could keep pumping a tiny bit of air in to compensate for leakage, the balloon would float on a thin film of air, and you could slide it around with almost zero friction. An **Air Cushion Vehicle works on exactly this principle** — the "balloon" is a huge engineered platform (the hull), and the "tiny pump" is actually a multi-megawatt industrial fan continuously feeding high-pressure air into the cushion. The moment the fan stops, the vehicle settles onto the surface like a beached whale.

This is **fundamentally different** from a conventional ship, which is supported by hydrostatic buoyancy (Archimedes' principle on displaced water). An ACV trades the buoyancy of millions of tonnes of water for the **dynamic pressure of a few tonnes of compressed air** — making it a hybrid of aircraft, ship, and ground vehicle.

## 1.3 Classification of Air Cushion Vehicles

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies ACVs into the following broad categories, each distinguished by how the air cushion is laterally sealed:

| Class | Seal Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Skirted Hovercraft (ACV – Sidewall type)** | Flexible rubberised skirt draping below the hull | Amphibious military & civilian transport |
| **Surface Effect Ship (SES)** | Rigid sidewalls + flexible seals fore and aft | High-speed ferry service |
| **Captured Air Bubble (CAB)** | Pressurized cavity beneath hull held by hydrostatic pressure | Submerged high-speed hulls (partial ACV) |
| **Air-Bearing Platform** | Recessed air pad under load | Heavy-load transport (industrial) |

## 1.4 Primary Subsystems of an ACV

Every Air Cushion Vehicle, irrespective of size, contains the following five functional subsystems:

1. **Hull (Plenum Chamber)** — Rigid box-like structure forming the upper boundary of the air cushion.
2. **Cushion Seal (Skirt or Sidewall)** — Lateral containment preventing air from escaping sideways.
3. **Lift System** — Centrifugal fans, ducting, and valves that pressurize the cushion.
4. **Propulsion System** — Air propellers, waterjets, or marine propellers providing thrust.
5. **Control System** — Rudders, ports, and vectored thrust for steering and stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Module 1 Outcome:** Students must be able to *distinguish ACVs from displacement hulls and planing hulls*, and *identify the function of each major ACV subsystem*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Physics of the Air Cushion

The air cushion is essentially a **controlled pneumatic pressure vessel** open only at its lower periphery. A centrifugal fan forces air into the plenum at a rate $Q$ (m³/s). The air then escapes through the narrow annular gap beneath the skirt. A quasi-steady equilibrium is reached when the **mass flow in equals the mass flow out**.

### Why is the cushion stable?

In the static (parked) condition, the cushion pressure $p_c$ must support the vehicle's total weight $W$:

$$
p_c \; A_c \;=\; W
$$

where $A_c$ is the effective cushion area. Because $p_c$ is only marginally above atmospheric (typically **300 Pa to 3000 Pa**, i.e., **0.3% to 3%** above ambient), the cushion is extremely "soft" — which is why ACVs exhibit characteristic **heave, pitch, and roll oscillations** when stationary over water.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Cushion Formation

1. **Fan engagement** — Lift fan draws ambient air through intakes and forces it into the plenum.
2. **Pressure rise** — Restricted escape beneath the skirt causes plenum pressure to rise above atmospheric.
3. **Lift generation** — The overpressure $p_c$ acting over $A_c$ produces an upward force $F_L = p_c A_c$.
4. **Equilibrium** — When $F_L = W$, the hull lifts clear of the surface by a gap $h$ (the **cushion height**, typically 1–3 m for large craft).
5. **Leakage compensation** — Continuous fan delivery compensates for skirt leakage $Q_{out}$.
6. **Steady-state hover** — The craft hovers; propulsion fans then provide forward thrust.

## 2.3 Forces Acting on an ACV in Steady Forward Motion

For KTU problem-solving, the **net thrust equation** in the longitudinal direction is:

$$
T \;=\; D_{total} \;=\; D_{wave} \;+\; D_{aero} \;+\; D_{skirt} \;+\; D_{friction}
$$

| Symbol | Force / Component | Physical Origin | Dominant Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Total thrust from propellers | Propulsion system | Always present |
| $D_{wave}$ | Wave-making resistance | Cushion pressure acting on water surface | Low–medium speed |
| $D_{aero}$ | Aerodynamic drag | Hull above water moving through air | All speeds |
| $D_{skirt}$ | Skirt drag | Air impingement on flexible skirt | All speeds |
| $D_{friction}$ | Skin-friction drag | Turbulent boundary layer on hull | All speeds |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every high-yield equation** required for Module 1 examination questions on Air Cushion Vehicles. Memorise the symbols, units, and applicability.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_c \;=\; W \,/\, A_c$ | Cushion pressure for static hover | Pa; $W$ in N, $A_c$ in m² |
| 2 | $Q_{out} \;=\; C_d \, A_{gap} \,\sqrt{2\,\Delta p \,/\, \rho_{air}}$ | Annular gap leakage (orifice equation) | m³/s; $C_d \approx 0.6$ |
| 3 | $P_{lift} \;=\; Q \cdot \Delta p$ | Power consumed by lift fan | W |
| 4 | $\Delta p \;=\; p_c - p_{atm}$ | Cushion overpressure above ambient | Pa; small positive value |
| 5 | $F_L \;=\; p_c \cdot A_c$ | Total lift force from cushion | N |
| 6 | $h_{cushion} \;\propto\; \sqrt[3]{Q^{2} \,/\, g}$ | Cushion height correlation | m |
| 7 | $D_{total} \;=\; D_{wave} + D_{aero} + D_{skirt} + D_{friction}$ | Total resistance | N |
| 8 | $L\,/\,D \;=\; W \,/\, D_{total}$ | Lift-to-drag efficiency ratio | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{total} \;=\; P_{lift} + P_{prop}$ | Installed power sum | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} \;=\; (T \cdot V) \,/\, P_{total}$ | Propulsive efficiency | Dimensionless $\le 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse** $p_c$ (absolute cushion pressure) with $\Delta p$ (gauge overpressure). KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for failing to specify which quantity is being used.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of ACVs

Air Cushion Vehicles are deployed worldwide for the following engineering-critical applications:

- **Amphibious military assault** (e.g., LCAC – Landing Craft Air Cushion, US Navy).
- **Civilian ferry service** where port infrastructure is absent (e.g., Norwegian fjords, Thames Estuary).
- **Arctic/Antarctic logistics** over ice and tundra.
- **Oil & gas exploration** in swamp and shallow-water regions.
- **Search-and-rescue (SAR)** over mixed terrain (beach + surf + ice).
- **Heavy-load industrial transport** on air-bearing platforms (shipyard moving of mega-blocks).

The defining engineering advantage is the **removal of hull-water contact**, which eliminates wave-making resistance and allows the same vehicle to operate on land and sea.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written in the **exhaustive KTU valuation style** — every algebraic transition is explicitly stated. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Cushion Pressure for Static Hover

### Given
- Total weight of ACV (loaded): $W = 500{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Effective cushion area: $A_c = 200 \text{ m}^2$
- Atmospheric pressure: $p_{atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### To Find
Cushion absolute pressure $p_c$ and overpressure $\Delta p$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Write the vertical force-balance equation.**

For the ACV in static hover, the upward cushion force must exactly balance the downward gravitational weight. Apply Newton's first law in the vertical direction:

$$
\sum F_{vertical} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F_L - W \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express the lift force in terms of cushion pressure.**

By definition of pressure, the total upward force exerted by the cushion on the hull is the product of cushion pressure and effective cushion area:

$$
F_L \;=\; p_c \cdot A_c
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation.**

$$
p_c \cdot A_c - W \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p_c \cdot A_c \;=\; W
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $p_c$.**

$$
p_c \;=\; \frac{W}{A_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
p_c \;=\; \frac{500{,}000 \text{ N}}{200 \text{ m}^2} \;=\; 2{,}500 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the overpressure above ambient.**

$$
\Delta p \;=\; p_c - p_{atm} \;=\; 2{,}500 - 101{,}325 \;\approx\; -98{,}825 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result (KEY INSIGHT).**

The result above reveals a **physical contradiction**: the cushion pressure must exceed ambient to lift the craft, yet our calculation gives a *negative* overpressure. The resolution is that $p_c$ in the formula is the **absolute pressure inside the plenum**, and for small craft, the cushion overpressure is on the order of a few hundred Pa — the **lift area $A_c$ must be substantially larger** than the footprint area for real craft. Corrected for typical ACVs (e.g., $A_c = 1000 \text{ m}^2$ for the same 500 kN weight):

$$
p_c \;=\; \frac{500{,}000}{1000} \;=\; 500 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta p \;=\; 500 - 101{,}325 \;=\; -100{,}825 \text{ Pa} \quad (\text{still incorrect sign})
$$

**Final correction — definition of $\Delta p$:**

Conventionally, $\Delta p$ is the **excess of cushion pressure over ambient**, but in practice the cushion operates at a *small fraction* of $p_{atm}$. We redefine:

$$
p_c \;\equiv\; p_{atm} + \Delta p
$$

So for a small overpressure of $\Delta p = 500 \text{ Pa}$:

$$
p_c \;=\; 101{,}325 + 500 \;=\; 101{,}825 \text{ Pa}
$$

And the required cushion area for this 500 kN craft:

$$
A_c \;=\; \frac{W}{\Delta p} \;=\; \frac{500{,}000}{500} \;=\; 1000 \text{ m}^2
$$

This 1000 m² area is consistent with a **hovercraft of ~40 m length × 25 m beam** — physically reasonable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Air Flow Rate Required to Maintain the Cushion

### Given
- Cushion overpressure: $\Delta p = 500 \text{ Pa}$
- Skirt gap area (annular leakage path): $A_{gap} = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$
- Discharge coefficient: $C_d = 0.6$
- Air density: $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Apply the standard orifice-flow equation for compressible low-pressure drop.**

For $\Delta p \ll p_{atm}$ (which is satisfied since $500 \ll 101{,}325$), the air behaves as incompressible:

$$
Q_{out} \;=\; C_d \cdot A_{gap} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known quantities.**

$$
Q_{out} \;=\; 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 500}{1.225}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the term inside the square root.**

$$
\frac{2 \cdot 500}{1.225} \;=\; \frac{1000}{1.225} \;=\; 816.33
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{816.33} \;\approx\; 28.57 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $C_d$ and $A_{gap}$.**

$$
Q_{out} \;=\; 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 28.57 \;=\; 0.3 \cdot 28.57 \;\approx\; 8.57 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{air} \cdot Q_{out} \;=\; 1.225 \cdot 8.57 \;\approx\; 10.5 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 7 — State the interpretation.**

About **8.57 m³/s of air** (≈ **10.5 kg/s**) must be continuously supplied by the lift fan to keep the cushion pressurised. This is the **single most important design parameter** for sizing the lift fan.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Power Required by the Lift Fan

### Given
- $Q = 8.57 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (from Derivation II)
- $\Delta p = 500 \text{ Pa}$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Apply the thermodynamic power equation for an ideal fan.**

The minimum shaft power required to drive a volume $Q$ against a pressure rise $\Delta p$ is:

$$
P_{lift} \;=\; Q \cdot \Delta p
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
P_{lift} \;=\; 8.57 \cdot 500 \;=\; 4{,}285 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.29 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for fan mechanical and hydraulic efficiency.**

Real centrifugal fans have efficiencies $\eta_{fan} \approx 0.70$. The **brake power (input power)** to the fan shaft is:

$$
P_{brake} \;=\; \frac{P_{lift}}{\eta_{fan}} \;=\; \frac{4{,}285}{0.70} \;\approx\; 6{,}121 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 6.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalise the result.**

For a heavier craft with weight $W = 5 \times 10^6$ N and proportional cushion area $A_c = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$, the cushion overpressure remains $\Delta p = 500$ Pa, but the leakage scales with the perimeter gap. For large craft, leakage may be **10× higher**, giving $Q \approx 85 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and $P_{lift} \approx 42.5 \text{ kW}$ (ideal), or ~60 kW (brake).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality check:** A 100-tonne hovercraft typically requires **1500–2500 kW of installed lift power** alone, before any propulsion power is added. This explains why ACVs are **fuel-thirsty** and not economically competitive with conventional ships below ~30 knots.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Total Resistance and Lift-to-Drag Ratio

### Given (typical SES at 40 knots)
- $W = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ N}$ (≈ 100 tonne)
- $D_{wave} = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $D_{aero} = 15{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $D_{skirt} = 8{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $D_{friction} = 5{,}000 \text{ N}$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Sum the resistance components.**

$$
D_{total} \;=\; D_{wave} + D_{aero} + D_{skirt} + D_{friction}
$$

$$
D_{total} \;=\; 20{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 5{,}000 \;=\; 48{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lift-to-drag ratio.**

$$
\frac{L}{D} \;=\; \frac{W}{D_{total}} \;=\; \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{48{,}000} \;\approx\; 20.83
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

A conventional displacement hull has $L/D \approx 4$–$6$; a planing hull has $L/D \approx 6$–$10$. The **ACV's $L/D \approx 20$ reflects its dramatic reduction in wave-making resistance**, justifying its use at high speeds despite the high installed power.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ACV vs Displacement Hull vs Planing Hull

| Parameter | Displacement Hull | Planing Hull | Air Cushion Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Support mechanism | Hydrostatic buoyancy | Hydrodynamic lift | Pneumatic pressure |
| $L/D$ at cruise | 4 – 6 | 6 – 10 | **15 – 25** |
| Wave-making drag | Dominant | Reduced | **Near zero (above cushion)** |
| Installed power per tonne | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Maximum practical speed (sea) | ~30 knots | ~50 knots | **~60–80 knots** |
| Amphibious capability | None | None | **Full** |
| Fuel consumption | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Sensitivity to sea state | Moderate | High | Low (cushion absorbs) |
| Typical KTU example | Tanker, bulk carrier | Patrol boat, speedboat | LCAC, SR.N4, SES-200 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ACV

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interconnections** between every major subsystem of a typical skirted hovercraft. It is rendered as a block architecture rather than a physical sketch to remain unambiguous and easily reproducible.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Lift Fan - Centrifugal Type]
    B --> C[Plenum Chamber - Hull Interior]
    C --> D[Pressurized Air Cushion - 500 to 3000 Pa]
    D --> E[Flexible Skirt - Rubberized Nylon]
    E --> F[Annular Leakage Gap - Perimeter]
    F --> G[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    D --> H[Lift Force Acting on Hull]
    H --> I[Vehicle Weight W - mg]
    I --> J[Vertical Equilibrium Check]
    J --> K{Is Lift equal to Weight?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Steady Hover Achieved]
    K -->|No| B
    M[Diesel or Gas Turbine Prime Mover] --> N[Gearbox and Transmission]
    N --> B
    N --> O[Propulsion Fan or Propeller]
    O --> P[Thrust Force T]
    P --> Q[Forward Motion at Speed V]
    Q --> R[Aerodynamic Drag D_aero]
    Q --> S[Wave Drag D_wave]
    Q --> T[Skirt Drag D_skirt]
    R --> U[Total Resistance D_total]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Thrust Balance T equals D_total]
    V --> W[Cruise Condition]
    X[Rudder and Control Surfaces] --> O
    Y[Helmsman or Autopilot] --> X
```

## 4.2 Subsystem Interaction Matrix

| Subsystem | Provides | Receives From | Failure Mode in KTU context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lift Fan | Pressurised air to plenum | Prime mover (diesel/gas turbine) | Cushion collapse → hull drops to surface |
| Skirt | Lateral air containment | Hull attachment points | Air leaks → $\Delta p$ drops → loss of lift |
| Plenum | Pressure distribution | Lift fan | Structural breach → catastrophic depressurisation |
| Propulsion Fan | Forward thrust $T$ | Prime mover via gearbox | Vehicle drifts; cannot maintain course |
| Control Surfaces | Yaw and pitch control | Helmsman / autopilot | Loss of directional stability |
| Prime Mover | Shaft power (kW) | Fuel bunker | Total power loss → vehicle beaches |

## 4.3 Cushion Pressure Profile (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plenum Center: Maximum pc] --> P2[Mid-radius: pc drops 5 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Skirt Inner Edge: pc minimum]
    P3 --> P4[Ambient Atmosphere: patm]
    P4 --> P5[Skirt Outer Edge: patm]
    P5 --> P6[Below Skirt: Low pressure wake]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cushion pressure is highest at the geometric centre of the plenum and falls radially outward as air leaks through the skirt gap. The pressure gradient drives the **radial outflow** that maintains the cushion in dynamic equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an Air Cushion Vehicle. Mention any two advantages of ACVs over conventional displacement hulls.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV)** is a craft that supports its weight on a thin, pressurised layer of air confined between the hull and the operating surface (water, land, or ice), generated by a lift fan and contained by a flexible skirt or rigid sidewalls.

**Two advantages** (any two of the following — 1 mark each):
1. **Reduced wave-making resistance** at high speed (since the hull does not contact the water), giving higher attainable speeds (up to 60–80 knots).
2. **Amphibious operation** — the same vehicle can transition from water to land without port infrastructure.
3. **Lower sensitivity to sea state** because the cushion absorbs surface irregularities.
4. **Access to shallow, ice-covered, or marshy regions** where conventional ships cannot operate.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List the four main components of resistance of an ACV in forward motion and state which one is *most reduced* compared to a displacement hull.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four components of resistance are:

1. Wave-making resistance ($D_{wave}$)
2. Aerodynamic resistance ($D_{aero}$)
3. Skirt resistance ($D_{skirt}$)
4. Skin-friction resistance ($D_{friction}$)

The component **most reduced** compared to a displacement hull is the **wave-making resistance ($D_{wave}$)**, because the cushion decouples the hull from direct water contact, eliminating most of the wave system generated by a displacement hull.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student answers **either Question A or Question B** from each module. Both options are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional subsystems of a skirted Air Cushion Vehicle** and describe how the air cushion is generated and maintained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hovercraft weighs **800 kN** in fully-loaded condition and is designed to hover with a cushion overpressure of **600 Pa**. Compute:
  - (i) the required effective cushion area $A_c$,
  - (ii) the volumetric leakage flow rate if the skirt gap area is **0.4 m²** and discharge coefficient is **0.65**,
  - (iii) the ideal lift-fan power required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The functional subsystems of a skirted ACV are (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4):

1. **Hull / Plenum Chamber** (1 mark): Rigid structure forming the upper boundary of the cushion; houses passengers, cargo, and machinery.

2. **Lift Fan System** (1 mark): Centrifugal or axial fan that draws atmospheric air and forces it into the plenum at rate $Q$, raising the cushion pressure to $\Delta p$ above ambient.

3. **Flexible Skirt** (2 marks): Rubberised fabric-nylon envelope that drapes below the hull; contains the cushion laterally, defines the leakage gap, and absorbs surface irregularities. Without the skirt, the cushion would dissipate within milliseconds.

4. **Propulsion System** (1 mark): Separate propeller or waterjet providing forward thrust $T$.

5. **Control System** (1 mark): Rudders, thrust-vectoring ducts, and skirt air valves for steering and attitude control.

6. **Prime Mover** (1 mark): Diesel engine or gas turbine supplying mechanical power to both lift and propulsion fans via gearbox.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $W = 800{,}000$ N; $\Delta p = 600$ Pa; $A_{gap} = 0.4$ m²; $C_d = 0.65$; $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**(i) Cushion Area (2 marks):**

$$
A_c \;=\; \frac{W}{\Delta p} \;=\; \frac{800{,}000}{600} \;\approx\; 1333.33 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(ii) Leakage Flow Rate (3 marks):**

$$
Q \;=\; C_d \cdot A_{gap} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

$$
\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho_{air}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 600}{1.225} \;=\; 979.59
$$

$$
\sqrt{979.59} \;\approx\; 31.30 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.65 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 31.30 \;\approx\; 8.14 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Ideal Lift-Fan Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{lift} \;=\; Q \cdot \Delta p \;=\; 8.14 \cdot 600 \;\approx\; 4{,}884 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.88 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:** Students frequently confuse **cushion pressure $p_c$** (absolute) with **cushion overpressure $\Delta p$** (gauge). Always state explicitly which one is being used in the formula. Also, do not forget to write **units** (Pa, m², m³/s, W) at every step — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **support mechanism, lift-to-drag ratio, maximum speed, and amphibious capability** of an ACV with those of a conventional displacement hull and a planing hull. Use a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **ideal power required by the lift fan** of a hovercraft and explain why, in practice, the installed lift power is **2 to 3 times** the ideal value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Parameter | Displacement Hull | Planing Hull | Air Cushion Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Support mechanism | Hydrostatic buoyancy (Archimedes) | Hydrodynamic lift (dynamic pressure) | Pneumatic pressure (air cushion) |
| Typical $L/D$ | 4 – 6 | 6 – 10 | 15 – 25 |
| Maximum practical speed | ~30 knots | ~50 knots | 60 – 80 knots |
| Amphibious capability | None | None | Full |
| Resistance dominant | Wave-making | Friction + spray | Aerodynamic + skirt |
| Operates over land | No | No | Yes |
| Hull contact with water | Continuous | Partial (rear) | None (cushioned) |

(1 mark per correctly-filled row × 5 rows = 5 marks; 2 marks for overall comparative conclusion)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The ideal (thermodynamic minimum) power required by the lift fan equals the rate of work done by the fan in raising the cushion air against the overpressure:

$$
P_{lift,\,ideal} \;=\; \dot{W}_{cushion} \;=\; (\text{volume flow rate}) \times (\text{pressure rise})
$$

$$
P_{lift,\,ideal} \;=\; Q \cdot \Delta p
$$

Substituting the orifice-flow expression for $Q$:

$$
Q \;=\; C_d \, A_{gap} \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

$$
P_{lift,\,ideal} \;=\; C_d \, A_{gap} \, \Delta p \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

Simplifying by grouping $\Delta p$ terms:

$$
P_{lift,\,ideal} \;=\; C_d \, A_{gap} \, \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho_{air}}} \cdot (\Delta p)^{3/2}
$$

**Why installed power is 2–3× the ideal value (2 marks):**

1. **Fan hydraulic efficiency** ($\eta_{fan} \approx 0.60$–$0.75$) — real fans lose energy to turbulence, recirculation, and blade-tip clearance.
2. **Mechanical transmission losses** in the gearbox and shaft bearings (typically 3–5%).
3. **Ducting and plenum losses** — friction and bends in the air supply ducts.
4. **Margin for cushion perturbations** — sea state and accelerations require transient overpressure that the fan must be sized to deliver.
5. **Engine accessibility and packaging constraints** — prime movers are often oversized for operational safety.

Therefore:

$$
P_{installed} \;=\; \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta_{fan} \cdot \eta_{mech}} \;\approx\; (2 \text{ to } 3) \times P_{ideal}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:** In part (b), students often **forget to substitute the leakage equation** into the power formula, leaving the derivation incomplete and losing 3 marks. Also, the **exponent $3/2$** on $\Delta p$ in the final expression must be explicitly derived — examiners check this carefully.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this bulleted summary as a final 5-minute revision pass before the KTU examination.

- **ACV Definition:** Vehicle supported on a pressurised air film confined between hull and surface.
- **Five Subsystems:** Hull, Lift fan, Skirt/sidewall, Propulsion, Control — know each function.
- **Static Hover Equation:** $p_c \cdot A_c = W$ — *always check* whether $p_c$ is absolute or gauge.
- **Cushion Overpressure $\Delta p$:** Typically **300 Pa to 3000 Pa** — only ~1% of atmospheric.
- **Leakage Equation:** $Q = C_d \, A_{gap} \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta p / \rho_{air}}$ — orifice flow at low $\Delta p$.
- **Lift Fan Power:** $P_{lift} = Q \cdot \Delta p = C_d \, A_{gap} \, \sqrt{2/\rho_{air}} \cdot (\Delta p)^{3/2}$.
- **Installed Power Multiplier:** Real installation needs **2× to 3×** the ideal value due to fan and mechanical losses.
- **Resistance Components (4):** $D_{wave} + D_{aero} + D_{skirt} + D_{friction}$.
- **Dominant Reduced Component:** $D_{wave}$ is dramatically reduced because hull does not contact water.
- **ACV $L/D$ ratio:** Typically **15–25**, far exceeding displacement hulls (4–6).
- **Amphibious Capability:** ACVs can transit water → land → ice without port infrastructure.
- **Two ACV Variants to Distinguish:** **Hovercraft (skirted)** vs **SES (rigid sidewalls)** — know the difference.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** (i) Confusing $p_c$ and $\Delta p$; (ii) omitting units; (iii) missing the $3/2$ exponent in the power derivation; (iv) forgetting that the cushion is **pneumatic, not hydrodynamic** lift.
- **Typical KTU Exam Weightage for Module 1, Topic ACVs:** 1 question in Part A (3 marks) + choice question in Part B (14 marks) = up to **17 marks** per paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Pleasure Crafts like Yachts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Small Pleasure Crafts like Yachts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Small Pleasure Craft** — Any floating, water-borne vessel of limited size (typically less than **24 m** in length, per the **EU Recreational Craft Directive (RCD) 2013/53/EU** and **ISO 12217**) intended for sports, leisure, or recreational purposes, regardless of the means of propulsion (sail, oar, inboard/outboard engine, or hybrid). A **Yacht** is the higher-end, typically larger (>$8\,\text{m}$ LOA) subclass of pleasure craft, originally defined as a fast, light sailing vessel but now broadly covering luxury motor and sailing recreational boats.

**Key Classification Boundaries (per RCD / ISO 12217-1:2015):**

| Craft Category | Length Range (L_H) | Typical Use Case |
|----------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Small Craft | $<6\,\text{m}$ | Dinghies, rowing boats, personal watercraft (PWC) |
| Medium Craft | $6\,\text{m} \le L_H < 12\,\text{m}$ | Day-sailing yachts, runabouts, small cabin cruisers |
| Large Craft | $12\,\text{m} \le L_H \le 24\,\text{m}$ | Ocean-going yachts, luxury motor yachts |
| Mega Yacht | $>24\,\text{m}$ | Commercial charter vessels |

where $L_H$ is the **Hull Length** measured to the nearest whole number as per ISO 8666.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a **floating, self-contained home-office** that must simultaneously (a) **float stably** like a cork on water, (b) **cut through waves** like a sports car slices through air, and (c) **resist capsizing** like a tight-rope walker with arms outstretched. A pleasure yacht is *not* a ship — it is a compromise between **buoyancy**, **speed**, **comfort**, and **aesthetic elegance**, all engineered into a fiberglass or aluminum shell weighing only a few tonnes.

## 1.3 Why Yachts Matter in Naval Architecture Education

Pleasure crafts are the **ideal pedagogical vehicle** for learning floating-body fundamentals because:
- They are small enough that **Froude scaling** assumptions are realistic.
- Their **slender hull forms** make hydrodynamic boundary layers tractable.
- **Stability is critical** yet easy to calculate (small free surface effects).
- They bridge the gap between **pure hydrostatics** (covered in Module 1) and **hydrodynamics** (Modules 2–4).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Weightage Tip:** Approximately **15–20%** of the ESE Module-1 marks are routinely allocated to pleasure-craft classification, general arrangement, and basic geometric/hydrostatic definitions. Master these terminologies first.

---

## 1.4 Primary Components of a Yacht (General Arrangement)

A pleasure yacht, despite its compact size, contains a **multi-deck compartmentalized architecture**:

1. **Hull** — the watertight shell providing buoyancy.
2. **Deck** — upper watertight surface; supports crew and rigging.
3. **Superstructure** — cabin/coachroof above the main deck.
4. **Keel** — fin/ballast structure below the hull (sailing yachts).
5. **Rudder** — directional control surface aft of the propeller.
6. **Mast & Rigging** — sail-supporting structure (sailing yachts).
7. **Propulsion System** — inboard diesel, outboard engine, or sail.
8. **Internal Bulkheads** — watertight subdivisions for safety.
9. **Tankage** — fuel, fresh water, black/grey water, and ballast tanks.
10. **Lifting Keel / Bilge Keel** — used in some cruising yachts for shallow-water access.

> [!NOTE]
> The **General Arrangement (GA) drawing** is the master plan of every yacht and is the first drawing produced during the **conceptual design phase** — long before the lines plan, structural drawings, or system schematics.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Characterization of a Yacht Hull

A yacht's hull is defined using **principal dimensions** (the "Big Six"):

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range (12 m Sailing Yacht) |
|-----------|--------|------------|------------------------------------|
| Length Over All | $L_{OA}$ | Tip of bowsprit to aft transom | $12.00\,\text{m}$ |
| Length on Waterline | $L_{WL}$ | Wetted-length envelope at design draft | $10.80\,\text{m}$ |
| Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{PP}$ | Forward perpendicular to aft perpendicular | $9.60\,\text{m}$ |
| Beam (Max. Breadth) | $B$ | Widest point of the hull | $3.80\,\text{m}$ |
| Depth (Moulded) | $D$ | Keel-to-deck at midships | $1.85\,\text{m}$ |
| Design Draft | $T$ | Keel bottom to design waterline | $0.95\,\text{m}$ (canoe body) + $1.35\,\text{m}$ (keel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** $L_{WL}$ — *not* $L_{OA}$ — is used in **Froude number** and **hull-speed** calculations, because only the wetted length interacts hydrodynamically with the water.

## 2.2 Form Coefficients (Slenderness Indicators)

The **form coefficients** quantify how "full" or "fine" the hull is. They are dimensionless ratios, always $\le 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ — the *most important* single number in naval architecture. A yacht with $C_B < 0.40$ is a **slender, fast displacement hull**; a yacht with $C_B > 0.55$ is a **full-bodied cruiser** sacrificing speed for volume.

The family of form coefficients:

**Prismatic Coefficient:**
$$
C_P = \frac{V_{\text{displ}}}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}
$$
where $A_M$ is the **midship-section area**.

**Block Coefficient:**
$$
C_B = \frac{V_{\text{displ}}}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Midship Coefficient:**
$$
C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Waterplane Area Coefficient:**
$$
C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}
$$

These are linked by the **fundamental identity**:
$$
C_B = C_P \times C_M
$$

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Force Balance (Archimedes Principle)

For a yacht floating in still water at design draft $T$, three conditions must hold simultaneously:

1. **Equilibrium of Vertical Forces:**
$$
W = \Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$
where $\nabla$ is the **displaced volume** (submerged volume of the hull, in $\text{m}^3$).

2. **Equilibrium of Horizontal Forces:** Automatically satisfied for a symmetric hull in still water.

3. **Equilibrium of Moments:** Center of Gravity (G) and Center of Buoyancy (B) must lie on the same vertical axis.

Using **seawater** with $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$:
$$
W = 1025 \times 9.81 \times \nabla \;\;\; [\text{N}]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering shortcut:** $1\,\text{m}^3$ of displaced seawater weighs **$1.025$ tonnes** (metric). This is why yacht displacement is quoted in **tonnes** (not Newtons).

## 2.4 Transverse Stability — The Metacentric Height

> [!WARNING]
> **Most critical concept in Module 1.** Students who fail to correctly compute **GMT** lose marks in *every* stability question on the ESE.

When a yacht heels by a small angle $\phi$, the center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$, and a **new vertical through $B_1$** intersects the original vertical centerline at the **metacenter M**.

**Key Definitions:**

- **KB** = vertical distance from keel ($K$) to center of buoyancy ($B$).
- **BM** = distance from $B$ to the metacenter $M$.
- **KG** = vertical distance from $K$ to center of gravity $G$.
- **GM** = metacentric height = $KB + BM - KG$.
- **GMT** = transverse metacentric height (the one we use for roll stability).

**The Three Stability Regimes:**

| Condition | Physical Meaning | Yacht Posture |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| $GM > 0$ | Stable (positive righting moment) | Upright after disturbance |
| $GM = 0$ | Neutral equilibrium | Will stay heeled at any angle |
| $GM < 0$ | Unstable (capsize) | Rolls over without righting |

**BM for any ship-shaped hull (analytical formula):**
$$
BM_T = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla}
$$

where $I_T$ is the **transverse second moment of waterplane area** about the centerline. For a rectangular waterplane (approximation for box-hulls):
$$
I_T = \frac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12}
$$

For a yacht with **parabolic waterplane ends** (more realistic), the integration yields:
$$
I_T = \frac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12} \times C_{WP}^{\,2} \;\;\text{(approx.)}
$$

**KB (Keel-to-Buoyancy) for a typical wall-sided yacht:**
$$
KB = T - \frac{T}{C_M} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{3} \right) \approx T \left(1 - \frac{1}{3 C_M}\right) \;\;\text{for prismatic hulls}
$$

or more accurately via direct integration of the underwater hull form.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 equations — they cover ~95% of Module-1 ESE questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Displacement = weight of displaced fluid | $\text{N}$ or $\text{kgf}$ |
| 2 | $\nabla = C_B L_{WL} B T$ | Submerged volume from block coefficient | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 3 | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | Coefficient identity | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} B}$ | Waterplane coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $KB = T - \dfrac{\int z \, dA}{A_{WP}}$ | Keel-to-Buoyancy (general) | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Transverse BM | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $I_T \approx \dfrac{L_{WL} B^3}{12}$ | 2nd moment of rectangular WP | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 8 | $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | Metacentric height | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $M_{R}(\phi) = \Delta \cdot GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$ | Righting moment at heel $\phi$ | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 10 | $GZ(\phi) = GM_T \sin\phi - \text{(free-surface corr.)}$ | Righting lever | $\text{m}$ |
| 11 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{WL}}}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 12 | $V_{\text{hull-speed}} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Hull-speed limit (sailing yachts, knots) | knots |

> **Engineering Field Utility:** These equations are the **daily bread** of yacht designers at firms like *Beneteau, Jeanneau, Sunseeker, Princess Yachts, Royal Huisman*, and at classification societies such as *Lloyd's Register, RINA, Bureau Veritas*. Every cruising yacht's **stability booklet** (mandatory under RCD) is a direct application of these formulas.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Displacement from Principal Dimensions

**Given:** A sailing yacht with $L_{WL} = 10.80\,\text{m}$, $B = 3.80\,\text{m}$, $T = 0.95\,\text{m}$, and block coefficient $C_B = 0.42$.

**Find:** Displacement in tonnes (seawater).

**Step 1 — Write the volume formula.**
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**
$$
\nabla = 0.42 \times 10.80 \times 3.80 \times 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — Compute sequentially (showing all arithmetic).**
$$
10.80 \times 3.80 = 41.040
$$

$$
41.040 \times 0.95 = 38.988
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.42 \times 38.988 = 16.375\,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass using seawater density.**
$$
\Delta_{\text{mass}} = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1025 \times 16.375
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{mass}} = 16{,}784.4\,\text{kg} \approx 16.78\,\text{tonnes}
$$

**Result:** The yacht displaces approximately **$16.4\,\text{m}^3$** of seawater, equivalent to a mass of **$16.78$ tonnes** — i.e., the yacht weighs the same as ~28 mid-size saloon cars.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transverse Metacentric Height (GMT)

**Given:** The same yacht with $\nabla = 16.375\,\text{m}^3$, $C_M = 0.78$, $KG = 1.05\,\text{m}$ above the keel.

**Find:** Transverse metacentric height $GM_T$.

**Step 1 — Calculate KB (Keel-to-Buoyancy).**

For a prismatic (constant-section) hull, the center of buoyancy lies at $\dfrac{T}{2}$ above the keel at the midship, but for a wall-sided form the general integral formula is:
$$
KB = T - \frac{1}{A_M} \int z \, dA_{\text{underwater}}
$$

For a hull with prismatic coefficient $C_P = 0.54$ and $C_M = 0.78$, an empirical closed-form for a typical sailing-yacht form is:
$$
KB \approx T \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} C_P \right) \cdot \frac{1}{C_M} \;\;\text{(wall-sided prismatic approximation)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$
KB \approx 0.95 \times \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} \times 0.54 \right) \times \frac{1}{0.78}
$$

Inside the parenthesis:
$$
\frac{1}{3} + \frac{2 \times 0.54}{3} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1.08}{3} = \frac{1 + 1.08}{3} = \frac{2.08}{3} \approx 0.6933
$$

Continuing:
$$
KB \approx 0.95 \times 0.6933 \times \frac{1}{0.78}
$$

$$
0.95 \times 0.6933 = 0.6587
$$

$$
KB \approx \frac{0.6587}{0.78} = 0.8445\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate BM using the rectangular waterplane approximation.**
$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

$$
I_T = \frac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{10.80 \times (3.80)^3}{12}
$$

$$
(3.80)^3 = 3.80 \times 3.80 \times 3.80 = 14.44 \times 3.80 = 54.872
$$

$$
I_T = \frac{10.80 \times 54.872}{12} = \frac{592.62}{12} = 49.385\,\text{m}^4
$$

$$
BM_T = \frac{49.385}{16.375} = 3.016\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate GMT.**
$$
GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG
$$

$$
GM_T = 0.8445 + 3.016 - 1.05
$$

$$
GM_T = 3.8605 - 1.05 = 2.8105\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\boxed{GM_T \approx 2.81\,\text{m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A GMT of ~$2.8\,\text{m}$ indicates a **stiff, stable yacht** that will resist heeling strongly but may feel "stiff" in gusts (a long, uncomfortable roll period). Modern racing yachts target $GM_T = 1.2$–$1.6\,\text{m}$; cruising yachts prefer $1.5$–$2.0\,\text{m}$. A value of $2.8\,\text{m}$ is **stiff** — possibly overly so for crew comfort.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hull-Speed Limit (Classic Yacht Rule)

**Step 1 — Write the Froude number condition at hull-speed.**

Hull-speed is the speed at which a **displacement hull** generates a wave whose crest-to-crest length equals the waterline length. The wave speed is given by the **gravity-wave dispersion relation** for deep water:
$$
V_{\text{wave}} = \sqrt{\frac{g \lambda}{2 \pi}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the condition $\lambda = L_{WL}$.**
$$
V_{\text{hull}} = \sqrt{\frac{g L_{WL}}{2 \pi}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the constant using $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.**
$$
\frac{g}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81}{6.2832} = 1.5615
$$

$$
V_{\text{hull}} = \sqrt{1.5615 \cdot L_{WL}}\;\;\;[\text{m/s}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to knots (multiply by $1.944$).**
$$
V_{\text{hull, knots}} = 1.944 \times \sqrt{1.5615 \cdot L_{WL}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{hull, knots}} = 1.944 \times 1.2496 \times \sqrt{L_{WL}} = 2.429 \sqrt{L_{WL}}
$$

Empirically, designers round this to:
$$
\boxed{V_{\text{hull}} \approx 1.34\,\sqrt{L_{WL}\,[\text{ft}]}\,[\text{knots}]}
$$

(in foot units) or $\approx 2.43 \sqrt{L_{WL}\,[\text{m}]}$ in SI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** A $10.80\,\text{m}$ (≈35.4 ft) yacht has $V_{\text{hull}} \approx 1.34\sqrt{35.4} \approx 7.97$ knots. **Planing or semi-displacement hulls can exceed this**, but pure displacement yachts are limited — this is the famous **"hull-speed barrier"** of sailing yachts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stability vs. Hull Form

| Hull Type | $C_B$ | $C_M$ | $L/B$ | Typical $GM_T$ | Speed Regime |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|----------------|--------------|
| Racing sailing yacht | $0.32$–$0.40$ | $0.55$–$0.70$ | $3.5$–$4.5$ | $1.2$–$1.6\,\text{m}$ | Displacement, near hull-speed |
| Cruising sailing yacht | $0.40$–$0.50$ | $0.75$–$0.85$ | $2.8$–$3.4$ | $1.5$–$2.2\,\text{m}$ | Displacement |
| Motor yacht (semi-disp.) | $0.50$–$0.60$ | $0.85$–$0.95$ | $2.5$–$3.2$ | $0.8$–$1.5\,\text{m}$ | Semi-displacement |
| Motor yacht (planing) | $0.35$–$0.45$ | $0.70$–$0.85$ | $2.8$–$3.8$ | $0.5$–$1.0\,\text{m}$ | Planing $>15$ knots |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Block Coefficient" of a yacht hull. State its typical range for a modern sailing yacht and explain why designers prefer lower values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **block coefficient** $C_B$ is the ratio of the **submerged (displaced) volume** $\nabla$ of the hull to the volume of its enclosing **rectangular box** of length $L_{WL}$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$:
>
> $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$
>
> For a modern sailing yacht, $C_B$ typically lies in the range **$0.32$ to $0.45$** (compared to $0.55$–$0.65$ for cargo ships). Designers prefer lower $C_B$ because (i) a **finer hull form reduces wave-making resistance**, allowing the yacht to approach its theoretical **hull-speed limit**, and (ii) lower $C_B$ implies a **slender waterline** with reduced wetted surface, lowering frictional resistance. The trade-off is **reduced internal volume** for accommodations — hence racing yachts are spartan while cruising yachts have higher $C_B$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain Archimedes' Principle as applied to a floating yacht. What happens to the draft when (a) a heavy sailor's weight is added at the bow, and (b) freshwater ballast is pumped into a previously empty tank?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Archimedes' Principle (for floating bodies):** A yacht floating in equilibrium displaces a volume of water whose weight exactly equals the yacht's total weight:
>
> $$\Delta = W = \rho \, g \, \nabla$$
>
> (a) When a heavy sailor steps to the **bow**, the total weight $W$ **increases** by the sailor's weight. To restore equilibrium, $\nabla$ must increase. Because the bow has a **fine waterplane area**, the bow **sinks more** (locally increasing draft forward) while the stern **rises slightly** — the yacht assumes a **trim by the bow** posture.
>
> (b) When freshwater ballast is pumped into an empty tank, total weight increases, increasing $\nabla$. The yacht **sinks deeper uniformly** if the tank is symmetric about midships, or **trims** toward the tank's location if it is off-center. There is no change in $C_B$ or hull form — only the operating point on the same curves changes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define and explain the terms **Center of Buoyancy (B), Metacenter (M), and Metacentric Height (GM)** with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the three regimes of equilibrium stability of a floating yacht. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sailing yacht has the following particulars at design draft: $L_{WL} = 12.0\,\text{m}$, $B = 3.60\,\text{m}$, $T = 1.50\,\text{m}$, $C_B = 0.45$, $C_M = 0.80$, $KG = 1.20\,\text{m}$. Calculate the **displacement in tonnes (seawater)**, the **transverse BM**, and the **transverse metacentric height GMT**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Center of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the **total buoyancy force** (equal to the weight of displaced water) is considered to act. It is the **centroid of the underwater volume** of the hull.

**Definition of Metacenter (M):** When a yacht heels by a small angle $\phi$, the center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$. The **vertical line through $B_1$** intersects the original vertical centerline at a point called the **Metacenter M**. For small heel angles ($< 10°$), $M$ is approximately fixed in position.

**Definition of Metacentric Height (GM):** The **vertical distance between the center of gravity G and the metacenter M**. It is a single-number indicator of the yacht's initial transverse stability.

**The Three Regimes of Equilibrium Stability:**

| Regime | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Stable | $GM_T > 0$ | A heel produces a **positive righting moment** $M_R = \Delta \cdot GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$; the yacht returns upright. |
| Neutral | $GM_T = 0$ | No righting moment; the yacht **stays at the heeled angle** indefinitely. |
| Unstable | $GM_T < 0$ | The righting moment becomes **capsizing**; the yacht rolls over without recovery. |

**Sketch (described in words for the answer booklet):**
> Draw a yacht section with the **keel K** at the bottom, the **waterline WL** at the top of the submerged body, point **B** on the centerline at the centroid of the underwater volume, point **G** above B (since $G$ is normally above $B$ in a loaded yacht), point **M** on the centerline *above* $G$ (for positive GMT). On heeling, $B$ moves to $B_1$; the vertical through $B_1$ meets the centerline at $M$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Displacement in tonnes.**

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.45 \times 12.0 \times 3.60 \times 1.50
$$

$$
12.0 \times 3.60 = 43.20
$$

$$
43.20 \times 1.50 = 64.80
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.45 \times 64.80 = 29.16\,\text{m}^3
$$

Convert to mass using seawater ($\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
\Delta_{\text{mass}} = 1025 \times 29.16 = 29{,}889\,\text{kg} \approx 29.89\,\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Transverse BM (using rectangular waterplane approximation).**

$$
I_T = \frac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{12.0 \times (3.60)^3}{12}
$$

$$
(3.60)^3 = 3.60 \times 3.60 \times 3.60 = 12.96 \times 3.60 = 46.656
$$

$$
I_T = \frac{12.0 \times 46.656}{12} = 46.656\,\text{m}^4
$$

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{46.656}{29.16} = 1.600\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — KB and KG, then GMT.**

$$
KB \approx T \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} C_P \right) \cdot \frac{1}{C_M}
$$

We need $C_P$. From $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$:

$$
C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.45}{0.80} = 0.5625
$$

$$
KB \approx 1.50 \times \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2 \times 0.5625}{3} \right) \times \frac{1}{0.80}
$$

Inside the parenthesis:
$$
\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1.125}{3} = \frac{2.125}{3} = 0.7083
$$

$$
KB \approx 1.50 \times 0.7083 \times \frac{1}{0.80}
$$

$$
1.50 \times 0.7083 = 1.0625
$$

$$
KB \approx \frac{1.0625}{0.80} = 1.328\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute GMT.**

$$
GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG
$$

$$
GM_T = 1.328 + 1.600 - 1.20
$$

$$
\boxed{GM_T = 1.728\,\text{m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

> A GMT of **$1.73\,\text{m}$** indicates a **stable cruising yacht** — neither too stiff nor too tender. This is consistent with a well-designed family cruiser.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of small pleasure crafts based on (i) length, (ii) hull form, and (iii) propulsion type. Briefly describe the general arrangement of a typical sailing yacht, listing at least **six** major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motor yacht is fitted with a planing hull. At displacement-mode operation, the yacht floats with $L_{WL} = 18.0\,\text{m}$, $B = 5.0\,\text{m}$, $T = 1.20\,\text{m}$, $C_B = 0.52$, $C_M = 0.88$, $KG = 1.50\,\text{m}$. Calculate:
  - (i) The **displacement volume** and **displacement mass** in tonnes.
  - (ii) The **transverse BM**.
  - (iii) The **GMT**.
  - (iv) The **hull-speed limit in knots**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Small Pleasure Crafts:**

**(i) By Length (ISO 8666 / RCD 2013/53/EU):**
- **Class A** — Ocean (length $> 12\,\text{m}$, suitable for open-ocean conditions, Beaufort Force 8+).
- **Class B** — Offshore (length $8$–$12\,\text{m}$, Force 7-8 conditions).
- **Class C** — Inshore (length $6$–$8\,\text{m}$, Force 6 conditions).
- **Class D** — Sheltered waters (length $< 6\,\text{m}$, Force 4 conditions).

**(ii) By Hull Form:**
- **Displacement hull** ($C_B = 0.40$–$0.55$): Always floats in equilibrium; speed limited by Froude/hull-speed.
- **Semi-displacement hull** ($C_B = 0.45$–$0.55$ with planing features): Transitional regime.
- **Planing hull** ($C_B < 0.45$, deadrise $15°$–$25°$): Rises out of water at speed; dynamic lift supports weight.
- **Multi-hull** (catamaran, trimaran): Two/three slender hulls; lower resistance.

**(iii) By Propulsion Type:**
- **Sailing yacht** — wind-driven; fin keel + ballast.
- **Motor yacht** — inboard diesel / outboard engine.
- **Hybrid** — diesel-electric with battery banks.
- **Human-powered** — rowing, canoeing, kayaks.
- **Personal Water Craft (PWC)** — jet-propelled small craft.

**General Arrangement of a Typical Sailing Yacht (six major components):**

1. **Hull** — GRP (fiberglass) or aluminum monohull shell providing buoyancy and accommodations.
2. **Keel** — lead/cast-iron fin below the hull containing 30–50% of total displacement as ballast for stability.
3. **Rudder** — aft steering surface, controlled by tiller or wheel via cables/quadrant.
4. **Mast & Standing Rigging** — aluminum spar supported by shrouds and stays carrying the mainsail and headsails.
5. **Deck Hardware** — winches, cleats, tracks, hatches for sail handling.
6. **Internal Spaces** — V-berth forward, saloon amidships with galley and nav station, head (toilet), and quarter-berth aft, plus engine compartment under the cockpit sole.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume.**

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.52 \times 18.0 \times 5.0 \times 1.20
$$

$$
18.0 \times 5.0 = 90.0
$$

$$
90.0 \times 1.20 = 108.0
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.52 \times 108.0 = 56.16\,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Displacement Mass (seawater, $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$).**

$$
\Delta_{\text{mass}} = 1025 \times 56.16 = 57{,}564\,\text{kg} \approx 57.56\,\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Transverse BM.**

$$
I_T = \frac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{18.0 \times (5.0)^3}{12} = \frac{18.0 \times 125}{12} = \frac{2250}{12} = 187.5\,\text{m}^4
$$

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{187.5}{56.16} = 3.338\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate KG, KB, then GMT.**

$C_P = C_B / C_M = 0.52 / 0.88 = 0.5909$

$$
KB \approx T \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} C_P \right) \cdot \frac{1}{C_M}
$$

$$
KB \approx 1.20 \times \left( \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2 \times 0.5909}{3} \right) \times \frac{1}{0.88}
$$

Inside the parenthesis:
$$
\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1.1818}{3} = \frac{2.1818}{3} = 0.7273
$$

$$
KB \approx 1.20 \times 0.7273 \times \frac{1}{0.88}
$$

$$
1.20 \times 0.7273 = 0.8727
$$

$$
KB \approx \frac{0.8727}{0.88} = 0.9917\,\text{m}
$$

$$
GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG = 0.9917 + 3.338 - 1.50 = 2.830\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM_T \approx 2.83\,\text{m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Hull-speed limit.**

Using $V_{\text{hull}} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}\,[\text{ft}]}\,$ knots:

Convert $L_{WL}$ to feet: $L_{WL} = 18.0\,\text{m} \times 3.281 = 59.06\,\text{ft}$

$$
V_{\text{hull}} = 1.34 \times \sqrt{59.06} = 1.34 \times 7.685 = 10.30\,\text{knots}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{hull}} \approx 10.3\,\text{knots}}
$$

> **Note:** This is the **displacement-mode** hull-speed limit. As a **planing hull**, the yacht can exceed this dramatically once it climbs onto the plane at higher power settings (typically $>15$–$20$ knots).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Errors in Pleasure-Craft Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using $L_{OA}$ instead of $L_{WL}$ in Froude/hull-speed calculations** — Examiner deducts 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $L_{WL}$ from meters to feet** before applying $V_{\text{hull}} = 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ — common mistake; loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing KB with KG** — These are *different distances from the same reference* (keel). KG depends on weight distribution; KB depends on hull form.
> 4. **Using freshwater density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** when the question says "seawater." Examiners explicitly specify; do not default.
> 5. **Sign error in $GM = KB + BM - KG$** — leading to a negative GMT where positive is expected, or vice-versa. Always sanity-check: a well-designed yacht has $GM > 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Time Management:** In a 14-mark Part-B question, budget ~7 minutes for the conceptual part and ~12 minutes for the numerical part. Show *every* algebraic step — KTU examiners reward stepwise work even if the final answer has a minor arithmetic slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Yachts: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** Pleasure craft = recreational vessel $\le 24\,\text{m}$ (RCD); Yacht = typically $>8\,\text{m}$ luxury version.
> - **Principal Dimensions:** $L_{OA}$, $L_{WL}$, $L_{PP}$, $B$, $D$, $T$ — know each precisely.
> - **Form Coefficients:** $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_{WP}$ — linked by $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$. All dimensionless, $\le 1$.
> - **Displacement:** $\Delta = \rho g \nabla = \rho g C_B L_{WL} B T$. Seawater: $1\,\text{m}^3 \approx 1.025$ tonnes.
> - **Archimedes:** Floating yacht displaces its own weight in water.
> - **Stability Trio:** KB (hull form), BM (waterplane shape, $BM_T = I_T/\nabla$), KG (weight distribution).
> - **GMT Formula:** $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$. Positive = stable; negative = capsize.
> - **Stability Regimes:** $GM > 0$ stable, $GM = 0$ neutral, $GM < 0$ unstable.
> - **Righting Moment:** $M_R = \Delta \cdot GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$.
> - **Hull-Speed:** $V_{\text{hull}} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}\,[\text{ft}]}$ in knots — **always use $L_{WL}$, in feet**.
> - **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L_{WL}}$ — dimensionless; $< 0.4$ displacement, $> 0.5$ planing.
