| 6 | $GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 7 | $GM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - \left( \overline{KG} - \overline{KB} \right)$ | Alternate form using keel reference | m |
| 8 | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{W \cdot GG'}{F_B \cdot GM}$ | Heel angle under shifting load | rad |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$ | 2nd moment for rectangular waterplane (rolling about length) | m⁴ |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | 2nd moment for circular waterplane | m⁴ |

> **Critical Note on Sign Convention (KTU Exam Pitfall):**
> - $GM > 0$ → **Stable Equilibrium** ($M$ lies above $G$)
> - $GM = 0$ → **Neutral Equilibrium** ($M$ coincides with $G$)
> - $GM < 0$ → **Unstable Equilibrium** ($M$ lies below $G$)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Ship designers ensure $GM > 0$ for stable vessels; passenger ships typically require $GM \geq 0.15$ m.
- **Submarine Design:** Ballast tanks are flooded/emptied to control buoyancy.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Floating oil rigs rely on metacentric height for stability in waves.
- **Aeronautics:** Balloons use Archimedes' Principle with hot air or helium.
- **Hydrometer:** A floating instrument whose immersion depth directly reads specific gravity of a liquid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Law of Floatation (From Archimedes' Principle)

**Given:**
- A body of weight $W$ is floating in a fluid of density $\rho_f$.
- The submerged volume of the body is $V_d$.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the body:**

- Downward force: $W$ (weight of body acting through $G$)
- Upward force: $F_B$ (buoyant force acting through $B$)

**Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle:**

The buoyant force equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the immersed portion:

$$F_B = (\text{mass of displaced fluid}) \times g = (\rho_f \cdot V_d) \cdot g$$

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$$

**Step 3 — For equilibrium of a floating body, the net force must be zero:**

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

$$W - F_B = 0$$

$$W = F_B$$

**Step 4 — Equate and simplify:**

$$\rho_b \cdot g \cdot V_b = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$$

$$\boxed{\rho_b \cdot V_b = \rho_f \cdot V_d}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

The mass of the entire body ($\rho_b V_b$) equals the mass of fluid displaced by the submerged portion ($\rho_f V_d$). This proves the **Law of Floatation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Metacentric Height ($GM$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most repeated derivation** in KTU ESE papers for this module. Practice it thoroughly.

**Setup:**
Consider a floating body (e.g., a rectangular pontoon) of length $L$, breadth $B$, and depth $D$. It floats with draft $h$. A small weight is shifted horizontally by a distance $x$, causing the body to tilt by a small angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Identify volumes and areas:**

- Displaced volume: $V_d = L \cdot B \cdot h$
- Waterplane area: $A = L \cdot B$
- Second moment of area of waterplane about the longitudinal axis (rolling axis): $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$

**Step 2 — Shift in centre of buoyancy:**

When the body tilts by $\theta$, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$. The horizontal shift $BB_1$ is:

$$\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{I \cdot \sin \theta}{V_d}$$

**Step 3 — Locate the Metacentre:**

The metacentre $M$ lies on the line through $B_1$ (vertical to the new waterline) intersected with the original vertical line through $B$. The distance $BM$ is:

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{\overline{BB_1}}{\sin \theta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I \cdot \sin \theta}{V_d \cdot \sin \theta}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V_d}}$$

**Step 5 — Express metacentric height:**

$$GM = BM - BG$$

$$GM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG$$

**Step 6 — Moment balance for shifting load:**

When a load $w$ shifts by $x$, the restoring couple equals the overturning couple:

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin \theta = w \cdot x$$

For small angles, $\sin \theta \approx \tan \theta$:

$$\boxed{\tan \theta = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{W \cdot GM}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Showcase

**KTU-Style Numerical (Examiner's Reference):**

> A rectangular pontoon $5 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$ floats in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The centre of gravity is at $0.5 \text{ m}$ above the keel. If the draft is $0.8 \text{ m}$, determine:
> (a) Metacentric height
> (b) Stability status

**Solution:**

**Step A — Displaced Volume:**

$$V_d = 5 \times 3 \times 0.8 = 12 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step B — Second Moment of Waterplane Area (rolling about length $L$):**

$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{5 \times 3^3}{12} = \dfrac{5 \times 27}{12} = 11.25 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step C — Centre of Buoyancy (centroid of submerged rectangular volume from keel):**

$$\overline{KB} = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step D — Distance BG:**

$$BG = \overline{KG} - \overline{KB} = 0.5 - 0.4 = 0.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step E — Calculate BM:**

$$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} = \dfrac{11.25}{12} = 0.9375 \text{ m}$$

**Step F — Metacentric Height:**

$$GM = BM - BG = 0.9375 - 0.1 = 0.8375 \text{ m}$$

**Step G — Stability Decision:**

Since $GM = 0.8375 \text{ m} > 0$, the body is in **STABLE EQUILIBRIUM**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Architecture of Floatation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **logical architecture** of floatation theory, showing how Archimedes' Principle branches into the Law of Floatation, which in turn governs stability through the Metacentre concept.

    vertNode["Vertical Equilibrium\nSum of Forces equals zero"]
    centeNode["Key Geometric Points\nG Centre of Gravity\nB Centre of Buoyancy\nM Metacentre"]
    stabNode["Stability Analysis\nGM equals BM minus BG"]
    bmNode["BM equals I over V sub d"]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**State Archimedes' Principle. How does it lead to the Law of Floatation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle:** When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation leading to Law of Floatation:**

Consider a body of weight $W$ floating in equilibrium. The upward buoyant force must equal the downward weight. **(1 mark)**

By Archimedes' Principle: $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$

For equilibrium: $W = F_B$

$$\therefore W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$$

This is the **Law of Floatation**: *a floating body displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own weight.* **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Define: (a) Centre of Buoyancy (b) Metacentre (c) Metacentric Height**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

**(a) Centre of Buoyancy ($B$):** It is the centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the immersed portion of a floating body. The buoyant force is considered to act vertically upward through this point.

**(b) Metacentre ($M$):** It is the point at which the line of action of the buoyant force intersects the original vertical line through the centre of buoyancy, when the body is given a small angular displacement.

**(c) Metacentric Height ($GM$):** It is the distance between the centre of gravity ($G$) of the floating body and the metacentre ($M$). It is a measure of the **initial static stability** of the floating body.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Series-I Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and prove the Law of Floatation. Distinguish clearly between a floating body and a submerged body. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wooden block of dimensions $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m}$ and density $650 \text{ kg/m}^3$ floats in water. Determine:
  (i) The depth of immersion
  (ii) The minimum specific gravity of a liquid in which this block would just float fully submerged **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Statement:** A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight is exactly equal to the weight of the body itself. **(2 marks)**

**Proof:** Let $W$ = weight of the body, $V_d$ = displaced volume, $\rho_f$ = density of fluid.

By Archimedes' Principle: $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d$

For a floating body, equilibrium demands $W = F_B$

$$\therefore W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_d \quad \text{... Law of Floatation} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Distinction Table: (2 marks)**

| Aspect | Floating Body | Submerged Body |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Immersion | Partially immersed | Fully immersed |
| Position of $B$ | Depends on draft | At geometric centroid of body (if uniform) |
| Free surface exposure | Part of body above fluid surface | None — entirely below surface |
| Example | Ship, log of wood | Submarine, fully submerged bell |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $L = 2 \text{ m}$, $B = 1 \text{ m}$, $D = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $\rho_b = 650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Total volume and weight of block:**

$$V_b = 2 \times 1 \times 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$W = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V_b = 650 \times 9.81 \times 1.6 = 10202.4 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Depth of immersion $h$:**

The immersed volume is $V_d = L \times B \times h = 2 \times 1 \times h = 2h$

By Law of Floatation:

$$W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_d$$

$$10202.4 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2h$$

$$h = \dfrac{10202.4}{19620} = 0.52 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum specific gravity for full submersion:**

For the block to *just* float fully submerged, the fluid density must equal the block's density:

$$\rho_{f,min} = \rho_b = 650 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\text{Specific Gravity} = \dfrac{\rho_{f,min}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{650}{1000} = \mathbf{0.65} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Answer:** Depth of immersion = **0.52 m**, Minimum SG = **0.65**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series-II Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define metacentric height. Derive an expression for metacentric height in terms of the second moment of area of the waterplane and the displaced volume. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular pontoon $6 \text{ m}$ long, $4 \text{ m}$ wide and $1.5 \text{ m}$ deep floats in seawater (density $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) with a draft of $1 \text{ m}$. Find the metacentric height if the centre of gravity is $0.6 \text{ m}$ above the keel. Comment on the stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Metacentric height $GM$ is the distance between the centre of gravity ($G$) and the metacentre ($M$) of a floating body. It is the primary indicator of initial transverse stability.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Consider a floating body** with weight $W$, displaced volume $V_d$, and waterplane area $A$ tilted by a small angle $\theta$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — On tilting**, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$. The wedge-shaped volume $v$ on one side is submerged, while an equal wedge is emerged on the other side. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Volume of wedge:** $v = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot A \cdot \overline{BB_1} \cdot \cos \theta$. For small angles: $v \approx \dfrac{1}{2} A \cdot \overline{BB_1}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Locate Metacentre:** The metacentre $M$ is found by extending $B_1$ vertically up to meet the original vertical through $B$. Then:

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{\overline{BB_1}}{\sin \theta}$$

But $\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{v \cdot 2}{V_d} \cdot ...$ through the second moment of area, we obtain:

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V_d} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $L = 6 \text{ m}$, $B = 4 \text{ m}$, $D = 1.5 \text{ m}$, draft $h = 1 \text{ m}$, $\rho_f = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\overline{KG} = 0.6 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Displaced Volume:**

$$V_d = L \times B \times h = 6 \times 4 \times 1 = 24 \text{ m}^3 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Waterplane Area:**

Rolling about the longitudinal axis (rolling breadth = $B$):

$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = \dfrac{6 \times 64}{12} = 32 \text{ m}^4 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Distance KB:**

$$\overline{KB} = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Distance BG:**

$$BG = \overline{KG} - \overline{KB} = 0.6 - 0.5 = 0.1 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate BM:**

$$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} = \dfrac{32}{24} = 1.333 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Metacentric Height:**

$$GM = BM - BG = 1.333 - 0.1 = 1.233 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(0.5 marks)}$$

**Stability Comment (0.5 marks):**

Since $GM = 1.233 \text{ m} > 0$, the pontoon is in **STABLE EQUILIBRIUM**. The metacentric height is comfortably positive, indicating a stable floating condition suitable for marine operations.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Floatation problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $BG$ and $GB$:** They are *not the same*. $BG = \overline{KG} - \overline{KB}$ — the sign matters! Always compute from keel reference.
> 2. **Wrong axis for moment of inertia:** If the body rolls about its **length**, use $I = \dfrac{LB^3}{12}$. If it pitches about its **breadth**, use $I = \dfrac{BL^3}{12}$. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Forgetting units in $I$:** Second moment of area is always in **m⁴**, not m³ or m².
> 4. **Sign of $GM$:** KTU examiners specifically test whether you state the stability condition. Always write "Since $GM > 0$, the body is stable" — never leave it implicit.
> 5. **Using fresh water density for seawater problems:** A 2.5% error that examiners *will* deduct marks for. Read the problem!
> 6. **No unit conversion:** Density in g/cm³ needs conversion to kg/m³ (multiply by 1000) before applying standard formulae.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision summary for last-minute KTU prep:**

- ✅ **Law of Floatation** = Archimedes' Principle applied to a body in **equilibrium** — weight of displaced fluid = weight of body.
- ✅ The fundamental equation: $\rho_b V_b = \rho_f V_d$
- ✅ **Centre of Buoyancy ($B$)** is the centroid of the **submerged volume**, not the whole body.
- ✅ **Metacentre ($M$)** is defined only for **small angular tilts** — large tilts need a different analysis (resistance curves).
- ✅ The position of $M$ is **independent** of the body's tilt for small angles (only depends on geometry and waterplane shape).
- ✅ **$BM = I/V_d$** — memorize this; KTU derives it in nearly every question paper.
- ✅ **Stability Rule:** $M$ above $G$ → stable; $M$ at $G$ → neutral; $M$ below $G$ → unstable.
- ✅ For **rectangular** waterplane: $I = LB^3/12$ (rolling) or $BL^3/12$ (pitching).
- ✅ For **circular** waterplane: $I = \pi D^4 / 64$.
- ✅ Draft formula: $h = V_d / A_{wp}$ — a quick way to find submerged depth.
- ✅ Heel angle due to shifting load: $\tan \theta = \dfrac{wx}{W \cdot GM}$
- ✅ Numerical answers should always carry the **unit** — examiners award partial marks for correct units alone.
- ✅ **Always interpret the result** in words (stable/unstable) — KTU marks this as a separate checkpoint.
- ✅ Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
- ✅ Distinguish **submerged** vs **floating** bodies — they have different $B$ positions and stability behaviours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability of Floating Bodies

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Floating Body** is defined as the ability of a floating body (partially submerged in a fluid) to return to its original equilibrium position when slightly tilted (heeled) by an external disturbing couple, due to the action of gravity and buoyancy forces.

When a floating body is displaced from its equilibrium position by a small angle $\theta$ (called the **angle of heel** or **angle of list**), the point of application of the buoyant force (centre of buoyancy, $B$) shifts to a new position $B_1$. The vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy intersects the original axis of symmetry (or the central vertical axis through $G$) at a point called the **Metacentre ($M$)**.

The distance $GM$ (where $G$ is the centre of gravity of the body) is called the **Metacentric Height** — the single most critical parameter governing floating body stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Conditions for a Floating Body:**
> - The **weight ($W$)** of the body and the **buoyant force ($F_B$)** must be equal and act vertically along the same line.
> - Mathematically: $W = F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ball and the Hill"

Imagine three scenarios with a ball placed on different surfaces:

1. **A Ball Inside a Bowl (Stable Equilibrium)** — If pushed slightly, the ball rolls back to the bottom. A ship with a positive metacentric height behaves identically — the **restoring couple** always rights the vessel.

2. **A Ball Balanced on Top of a Hill (Unstable Equilibrium)** — Even a tiny disturbance causes the ball to roll down irreversibly. A ship with a negative $GM$ will capsize — this is exactly why the **Titanic's stability** after flooding was such a critical failure.

3. **A Ball on a Perfectly Flat Surface (Neutral Equilibrium)** — The ball neither returns nor moves away; it rests anywhere. A ship with $GM = 0$ will stay at whatever heel angle it is pushed to.

> [!NOTE]
> The metacentre ($M$) is **not a fixed point** in the body — it moves as the angle of heel changes. For small angles ($\theta \leq 10°$), $M$ is assumed to remain effectively stationary on the centre line — this is called the **"Small Angle Stability Assumption"** taught throughout the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard earth gravity used in all KTU numerical problems unless stated otherwise).
- **Density of fresh water**: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of sea water**: $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (standard KTU textbook value)
- **Density of mercury**: $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stability of a Submerged Body (Recap Foundation)

A body fully submerged in a fluid is **stable** when the centre of gravity ($G$) lies **below** the centre of buoyancy ($B$). This is because any tilt causes $B$ to shift in a way that the buoyant force creates a restoring moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For fully submerged bodies, stability depends purely on the relative vertical positions of $G$ and $B$. For **floating (partially submerged)** bodies, the analysis becomes more nuanced because the **shape of the submerged volume** now determines stability.

### 2.2 Stability of a Floating Body — Detailed Theory

When a floating body is tilted through a small angle $\theta$, three points become critical:

1. **$G$ (Centre of Gravity)** — Remains fixed relative to the body since mass distribution is unchanged.
2. **$B$ (New Centre of Buoyancy)** — Shifts to $B_1$ because the geometry of the submerged wedge changes.
3. **$M$ (Metacentre)** — The intersection of the vertical line through $B_1$ with the original vertical axis through $G$ and $B$.

The shift of $B$ occurs because as the body tilts, one side submerges deeper (gaining a wedge of volume $\delta V$) while the opposite side rises (losing an identical wedge $\delta V$).

#### The Three Conditions of Equilibrium for Floating Bodies

| Condition | Metacentric Height ($GM$) | Physical Meaning | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stable Equilibrium** | $GM > 0$ (i.e., $M$ lies **above** $G$) | Restoring couple returns the body upright | Safe ship design — vessel self-rights |
| **Unstable Equilibrium** | $GM < 0$ (i.e., $M$ lies **below** $G$) | Overturning couple capsizes the body | Catastrophic failure — vessel rolls over |
| **Neutral Equilibrium** | $GM = 0$ (i.e., $M$ coincides with $G$) | Neither restoring nor overturning couple | Vessel stays at the heel angle (no oscillation back) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Righting Couple** (Restoring Couple) is given by:
> $$\text{Righting Couple} = W \times GM \times \sin\theta$$
> where $W$ is the weight of the body and $\theta$ is the angle of heel. **For small angles**, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians), which simplifies to:
> $$\text{Righting Couple} \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains **every** formula you must memorise for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) and internal assessments on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Variables | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$ | $\rho_f$ = fluid density; $V_{sub}$ = submerged volume | N (Newton) |
| 2 | Weight Equilibrium | $W = F_B$ | Weight = Buoyant force at floating equilibrium | N |
| 3 | Second Moment of Waterline Area | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | For a **rectangular** pontoon of length $L$ and breadth $B$ | m$^4$ |
| 4 | Second Moment of Waterline Area | $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | For a **circular** pontoon of diameter $D$ | m$^4$ |
| 5 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$ | $I$ = Second moment of waterline plane area; $V_{sub}$ = submerged volume | m |
| 6 | Position of Metacentre | $KM = KB + BM$ | Distance from keel ($K$) to metacentre ($M$) | m |
| 7 | Metacentric Height | $GM = KM - KG$ | Distance from $G$ to $M$ — **THE KEY STABILITY PARAMETER** | m |
| 8 | Alternative GM Formula | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Equivalent form used directly in numerical problems | m |
| 9 | Righting Couple | $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Couple that restores the tilted body | N·m |
| 10 | Overturning Couple | $C_O = W \cdot h \cdot \sin\theta$ | Disturbing couple due to shifted load $W$ at eccentricity $h$ | N·m |
| 11 | Period of Oscillation (Rolling) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | $k$ = radius of gyration of the body about its longitudinal axis | s |
| 12 | Time of Oscillation | $t = \dfrac{T}{2}$ | Time for half-oscillation (one complete roll back) | s |
| 13 | Moment of Inertia of Ship | $I_{ship} = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot k^2$ | Mass moment of inertia of the floating body | kg·m$^2$ |
| 14 | Total Mass of Floating Body | $m = \dfrac{W}{g}$ | Required to compute period of roll | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU problems, when the question says "a rectangular pontoon floats," assume $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ (where $B$ is the dimension perpendicular to the axis of tilting). For a **circular disc** or **cylindrical buoy**, use $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of metacentric height is **not just academic** — it directly governs:

1. **Naval Architecture & Ship Design** — Every commercial vessel (cargo ships, tankers, cruise liners) has its $GM$ carefully calculated during design. A very high $GM$ makes the ship "stiff" (rolls too fast, uncomfortable for passengers). A very low $GM$ makes the ship "tender" (slow rolling, dangerously close to capsizing). The IMO recommends $GM \geq 0.15$ m for ocean-going vessels.

2. **Submarine Stability** — Submarines use ballast tanks to deliberately raise or lower $G$ to control $GM$ when submerging vs. surfacing.

3. **Offshore Platforms & Buoys** — Floating oil rigs, navigation buoys, and floating docks all rely on the metacentre principle.

4. **Aerospace (Floating in Two Fluids)** — Balloons and airships use a related analysis with two fluids (helium + air).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius $BM = I / V_{sub}$

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2 and is a guaranteed KTU Part B question.

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular floating body of length $L$, breadth $B$, with its axis of rotation along the longitudinal (length) axis. When the body tilts by a small angle $\theta$:

- The original centre of buoyancy was at $B$ on the centre line.
- After tilting, the new centre of buoyancy is $B_1$, shifted horizontally by a distance $\overline{BB_1}$.
- The vertical through $B_1$ meets the original vertical axis at $M$ (the metacentre).

**Step 1: Express the shift $\overline{BB_1}$ as the moment of the wedge volumes about the centre line.**

The body is tilted so that one side submerges an additional wedge of volume $\delta V$, while the opposite side rises by an equal wedge. The buoyant force $F_B = W$ now effectively acts through $B_1$, the centroid of the new submerged volume.

By the principle of moments, the shift of the buoyancy centre equals the moment of the wedge volumes divided by the total submerged volume:

$$\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{\text{Moment of wedge about centre plane}}{V_{sub}}$$

**Step 2: Express the wedge moment using the second moment of area $I$.**

Let the centre line of the waterplane be the y-axis. A small strip of the waterplane at distance $x$ from the centre has width $dx$ and length $L$ (along the ship's length). When tilted by $\theta$, this strip moves vertically by $x \cdot \theta$, sweeping out a triangular wedge of volume:

$$dV_{wedge} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot (x \theta) \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{...}}$$

A more rigorous derivation treats each infinitesimal area element $dA = L \, dx$ at distance $x$ from the axis. The wedge of fluid on one side has centroid at $x = \dfrac{2x}{3}$ from the centre line, and area $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (x\theta) \cdot L$ (a triangle).

The moment of this wedge about the centre plane is:

$$dM_{wedge} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot x \theta\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{2x}{3}\right) = \dfrac{L \theta x^2}{3} \, dx$$

Wait — we need to be careful with factors. The correct derivation proceeds by considering the **change in moment of buoyancy** as the body tilts. The buoyant force on each side is $F_B = \rho_f g \cdot V$, and its moment about the original centre line is what changes.

**Correct Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let the waterplane area have a second moment of area $I$ about the longitudinal axis through its centroid. Define $I$ as:

$$I = \int x^2 \, dA$$

where $x$ is measured across the waterplane (perpendicular to the tilt axis) and $dA$ is an infinitesimal waterplane area element.

When the body tilts by $\theta$, each element $dA$ at distance $x$ from the centre line moves through a vertical distance $x\theta$. The wedge on the descending side has volume equal to the volume traced by the swept area. For small $\theta$, the wedge volume on one side is approximately:

$$\delta V = \int_0^{B/2} x \theta \, dA = \theta \int_0^{B/2} x \, dA$$

But more precisely, the **centroid of the descending wedge** lies at a horizontal distance of $\dfrac{2}{3} x$ from the centre line for a triangular wedge. The shift in centre of buoyancy is:

$$\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot \int x^2 \, dA}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot I}{V_{sub}}$$

**Step 3: Geometrically relate $\overline{BB_1}$ to $BM$.**

From the geometry of the metacentre (refer to the figure in Section 4):

In triangle $B B_1 M$, with $B B_1$ horizontal and $B_1 M$ vertical:

$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\overline{BB_1}}{BM}$$

For small $\theta$: $\tan\theta \approx \theta$, so:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\overline{BB_1}}{BM} \implies BM = \dfrac{\overline{BB_1}}{\theta}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\overline{BB_1} = \theta \cdot I / V_{sub}$ into the above:**

$$BM = \dfrac{\theta \cdot I / V_{sub}}{\theta}$$

$$\boxed{BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}}$$

**Step 5: Algebraic finalisation.**

Combining with the geometric relation $KM = KB + BM$ and the definition $GM = KM - KG$:

$$\boxed{GM = KB + \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - KG}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B numerical problems. **Memorise it verbatim.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Period of Oscillation (Roll Period)

The period of oscillation $T$ is critical in naval architecture because resonance with wave frequencies can cause dangerous rolling.

**Step 1: Set up the energy equation.**

When a floating body rolls through a small angle $\theta$ from equilibrium, the restoring couple is:

$$\text{Restoring Couple} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta \quad (\text{for small } \theta)$$

This restoring couple is analogous to a spring restoring force in simple harmonic motion.

**Step 2: Apply the analogy with a simple pendulum / torsional pendulum.**

The equation of angular motion (rotational SHM) is:

$$I_{ship} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

where $I_{ship}$ is the mass moment of inertia of the ship about its rolling axis.

Since $W = m g$, we get:

$$I_{ship} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -m \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3: Define radius of gyration $k$.**

The mass moment of inertia is expressed as $I_{ship} = m k^2$, where $k$ is the **radius of gyration** of the ship about the rolling axis. Substituting:

$$m k^2 \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -m \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$

$$\ddot{\theta} = -\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^2} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4: Compare with standard SHM form $\ddot{\theta} = -\omega^2 \theta$.**

This gives:

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^2} \implies \omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^2}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the period $T = 2\pi / \omega$.**

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, when $k$ is not given, you may use the relation $k^2 = I_{ship} / m = g \cdot I_{ship} / W$, or alternatively use $T = 2\pi \sqrt{I_{ship} / (W \cdot GM)}$ if the moment of inertia is directly given.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Righting Couple Formula

When a body of weight $W$ is tilted by angle $\theta$ and the metacentre $M$ lies above $G$ by distance $GM$:

**Step 1:** The weight $W$ acts vertically downward through $G$.
**Step 2:** The buoyant force $F_B = W$ acts vertically upward through $B_1$ (which, by definition, lies on the vertical line through $M$).
**Step 3:** These two equal, opposite, and parallel forces form a **couple** whose moment arm is the perpendicular distance between the two vertical lines.

The perpendicular distance between the line of action of $W$ (through $G$) and the line of action of $F_B$ (through $M$) is, by geometry:

$$\text{Moment arm} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4:** Hence, the righting couple magnitude is:

$$\boxed{C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta}$$

For small angles: $C_R \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Type of Stability from Given Data

Use this decision matrix to solve KTU numerical problems efficiently:

**Given:** $KG$, $KB$, $V_{sub}$, waterline area dimensions, $W$, $\rho_f$.

| Step | Action | Equation | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Find $BM$ | $BM = I / V_{sub}$ | Value in metres |
| 2 | Find $KM$ | $KM = KB + BM$ | Value in metres |
| 3 | Find $GM$ | $GM = KM - KG$ | **Sign determines stability** |
| 4 | Interpret Sign | $GM > 0$: Stable; $GM < 0$: Unstable; $GM = 0$: Neutral | Conclusion |
| 5 | If asked for period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$ | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** If $KG > KM$, the body is unstable. If $KB + BM > KG$, the body is stable. **Do not confuse $KB$ (distance from keel to buoyancy centre) with $KG$ (distance from keel to gravity centre)** — they are entirely different quantities and confusing them is the #1 cause of lost marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stability Analysis Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **decision logic** a student must follow when solving KTU numerical problems on this topic. Note that every node ID is alphanumeric and labels are kept free of pipe characters and reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Floating Body Data Given] --> nodeB{W equals Buoyant Force?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC[Body not in floating equilibrium - stop]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Compute Submerged Volume Vsub]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Second Moment I of Waterplane Area]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute BM equals I divided by Vsub]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute KB from geometry of submerged shape]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute KM equals KB plus BM]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compare KM with KG given]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{KM greater than KG?}
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK[GM positive - STABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeL{KM less than KG?}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[GM negative - UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
    nodeL -- No --> nodeN[GM equals zero - NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM]
    nodeK --> nodeO[Optional: Compute Righting Couple]
    nodeK --> nodeP[Optional: Compute Period of Oscillation]
    nodeM --> nodeQ[Body will capsize on slightest disturbance]
    nodeN --> nodeR[Body remains at any heel angle]
    style nodeK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style nodeM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style nodeN fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Position Relationships of Key Points

This diagram illustrates the spatial relationship between $K$ (keel), $B$ (centre of buoyancy), $G$ (centre of gravity), and $M$ (metacentre) along the vertical axis of the floating body.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[K - Keel Reference Point - Lowest point of body] --> node2[B - Centre of Buoyancy - Centroid of submerged volume]
    node2 --> node3[G - Centre of Gravity - Centroid of body mass]
    node3 --> node4[M - Metacentre - Intersection of vertical through new B with original axis]
    node4 --> node5[Note: For small heel angle, M is stationary on centre line]
    node1 --> node6[KB is distance from keel to B]
    node2 --> node7[KG is distance from keel to G - Note KG can be above or below KB]
    node1 --> node8[KM is distance from keel to M]
    node8 --> node9[GM equals KM minus KG - This is THE key stability metric]
    style node9 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Couple Mechanics on a Heeled Body

This diagram shows how the weight $W$ and buoyant force $F_B$ form a restoring couple when the metacentre is above the centre of gravity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Body tilted at angle theta from vertical] --> nodeY[Weight W acts downward through G]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Buoyant Force FB acts upward through new centre B1]
    nodeY --> nodeAA[Line of action of W is vertical through G]
    nodeZ --> nodeAB[Line of action of FB is vertical through M]
    nodeAA --> nodeAC[Two parallel, equal, opposite forces form a COUPLE]
    nodeAB --> nodeAC
    nodeAC --> nodeAD[Moment arm of couple equals GM times sin theta]
    nodeAD --> nodeAE[Restoring Couple equals W times GM times sin theta]
    nodeAE --> nodeAF{Is GM positive?}
    nodeAF -- Yes --> nodeAG[Couple restores body upright - STABLE]
    nodeAF -- No --> nodeAH[Couple overturns body - UNSTABLE]
    style nodeAG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style nodeAH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the terms: (a) Metacentre, (b) Metacentric height, and (c) Centre of buoyancy.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Metacentre ($M$):** When a floating body is slightly tilted (heeled) from its equilibrium position, the vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy intersects the original vertical axis (line through the centre of gravity $G$ and the original centre of buoyancy $B$) at a point called the **metacentre**. It is denoted by $M$.

(b) **Metacentric Height ($GM$):** The distance between the centre of gravity ($G$) of a floating body and its metacentre ($M$) is known as the **metacentric height**. It is the key parameter that determines whether the floating body is in stable, unstable, or neutral equilibrium.

(c) **Centre of Buoyancy ($B$):** The point through which the entire buoyant force (upthrust) of the fluid is assumed to act is called the **centre of buoyancy**. It is essentially the centroid of the submerged volume of the floating body. For small angles of heel, the metacentre is defined relative to the original position $B$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students confuse $B$ (centre of buoyancy, in the fluid region) with $G$ (centre of gravity, in the body mass). $B$ shifts when the body tilts; $G$ remains fixed relative to the body.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the three conditions of equilibrium for a floating body.**

**Model Answer:**

A floating body can be in one of three states of equilibrium depending on the relative position of its metacentre ($M$) and centre of gravity ($G$):

**1. Stable Equilibrium ($GM > 0$):** The metacentre $M$ lies **above** the centre of gravity $G$. When the body is tilted, the buoyant force and weight form a **restoring couple** which brings the body back to its original upright position. *Example: A ship floating in still water.*

**2. Unstable Equilibrium ($GM < 0$):** The metacentre $M$ lies **below** the centre of gravity $G$. When the body is tilted, the buoyant force and weight form an **overturning couple** which causes the body to capsize. *Example: A tall narrow cylinder floating with most of its mass concentrated at the top.*

**3. Neutral Equilibrium ($GM = 0$):** The metacentre $M$ coincides with the centre of gravity $G$ (i.e., $GM = 0$). There is **no restoring couple and no overturning couple** — the body remains at any angle at which it is placed. *Example: A perfectly homogeneous spherical buoy floating in a fluid.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always mention both the geometric condition (position of $M$ relative to $G$) AND the resulting couple type (restoring/overturning/none). Answering only one part loses 1 mark.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**A rectangular pontoon of length 8 m, width 4 m, and height 2 m weighs 200 kN. Find:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The depth of immersion when it floats in sea water ($\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Also calculate the position of the centre of buoyancy $B$ from the keel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If a load of 50 kN is placed at a height of 1.5 m above the centre of the pontoon (on its vertical axis), determine the new metacentric height and comment on the stability.

**Given:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

---

##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Find the volume submerged using floating equilibrium.**

$$W = F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{1025 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{200000}{10055.25}$$

$$V_{sub} = 19.8907 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Find depth of immersion $h$.**

For a rectangular pontoon with base area $L \times B = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ m}^2$:

$$V_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot h = 32 \cdot h$$

$$h = \dfrac{19.8907}{32} = 0.6216 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.622 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Find position of $B$ from keel.**

For a rectangular block with uniform cross-section, the centre of buoyancy is at the centroid of the submerged (rectangular) volume, which is at half the depth of immersion from the keel:

$$KB = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{0.6216}{2} = 0.3108 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{KB \approx 0.311 \text{ m}}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Find new total weight and new submerged depth.**

$$W_{new} = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ kN}$$

$$V_{sub,new} = \dfrac{250000}{1025 \times 9.81} = 24.8633 \text{ m}^3$$

$$h_{new} = \dfrac{24.8633}{32} = 0.7770 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Find new $KB$.**

$$KB_{new} = \dfrac{0.7770}{2} = 0.3885 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Find $BM$ using the waterplane second moment of area.**

For a rectangular waterplane (rolling about the longitudinal axis):

$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \cdot (4)^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 64}{12} = \dfrac{512}{12} = 42.6667 \text{ m}^4$$

$$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub,new}} = \dfrac{42.6667}{24.8633} = 1.7162 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Find new $KG$ using the principle of moments.**

The pontoon's own centre of gravity is at height $h/2 = 1.0$ m above keel (centroid of the 2 m tall block).

Wait — we need to be careful. The pontoon's original $G$ is at the geometric centre of the pontoon (since it is a uniform block): $G_{old}$ is at $1.0$ m above keel (since height is 2 m).

When the load of 50 kN is placed on top at $1.5$ m above the centre of pontoon, the new $G$ position from keel is:

The pontoon's centre is at $1.0$ m from keel. The load is at $1.5$ m **above the centre of the pontoon**, so the load's height from keel = $1.0 + 1.5 = 2.5$ m.

Using moments to find new $KG$:

$$KG_{new} = \dfrac{W_{pontoon} \cdot G_{pontoon} + W_{load} \cdot G_{load}}{W_{new}}$$

$$KG_{new} = \dfrac{200 \times 1.0 + 50 \times 2.5}{250} = \dfrac{200 + 125}{250} = \dfrac{325}{250} = 1.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate $GM$.**

$$KM = KB_{new} + BM = 0.3885 + 1.7162 = 2.1047 \text{ m}$$

$$GM = KM - KG_{new} = 2.1047 - 1.3 = 0.8047 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM \approx 0.805 \text{ m}}$$

**Comment on Stability:** Since $GM > 0$ (positive), the pontoon is in **stable equilibrium**. The metacentre lies above the centre of gravity, providing a restoring couple when the pontoon is tilted.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Trap):** Students often forget to recompute the submerged depth after adding the load, and incorrectly use the original $h$. **Always recompute $V_{sub}$ and $h$ when the weight changes.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**A cylindrical buoy of diameter 1.5 m and height 1.8 m weighs 15 kN. It floats in sea water ($\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$).**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the depth of immersion, position of centre of buoyancy from the base, and the metacentric height.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the buoy is tilted by $5°$ from vertical, calculate the magnitude of the righting couple. Given: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute submerged volume using floating equilibrium.**

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{15000}{1025 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{15000}{10055.25} = 1.4917 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute depth of immersion $h$.**

Cross-sectional area of cylinder = $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 2.25}{4} = 1.7671 \text{ m}^2$

$$h = \dfrac{V_{sub}}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{1.4917}{1.7671} = 0.8441 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.844 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Find $KB$.**

For a uniform cylinder, the centroid of submerged (cylindrical) volume is at half the immersion depth from the base:

$$KB = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{0.8441}{2} = 0.4221 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{KB \approx 0.422 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Find $BM$ using the waterplane second moment of area.**

For a circular waterplane:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.5)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 5.0625}{64} = \dfrac{15.904}{64} = 0.2485 \text{ m}^4$$

$$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{0.2485}{1.4917} = 0.1666 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{BM \approx 0.167 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Find $KG$ and $GM$.**

Assuming the buoy is made of uniform material, its centre of gravity lies at the geometric centre, i.e., at half the height = $1.8 / 2 = 0.9$ m from the base (keel).

$$KG = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

$$KM = KB + BM = 0.4221 + 0.1666 = 0.5887 \text{ m}$$

$$GM = KM - KG = 0.5887 - 0.9 = -0.3113 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM \approx -0.311 \text{ m (UNSTABLE)}}$$

**Comment:** Since $GM$ is **negative**, the buoy is in **unstable equilibrium** and will capsize if tilted even slightly. This is because the cylinder is tall and narrow with most of its mass high above the waterline.

---

##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Conceptual Note:** When $GM$ is **negative**, there is no "righting couple" in the traditional sense — instead, there is an **overturning couple**. The problem statement asks for the magnitude of the couple, which we compute using:

$$\vert C \vert = W \cdot \vert GM \vert \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 1: Identify values.**

$W = 15000$ N, $\vert GM \vert = 0.3113$ m, $\theta = 5°$, $\sin 5° = 0.08716$

**Step 2: Compute the couple magnitude.**

$$\vert C \vert = 15000 \times 0.3113 \times 0.08716$$

$$\vert C \vert = 15000 \times 0.02713 = 406.95 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\vert C \vert \approx 407 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Interpretation:** Because $GM < 0$, this is an **overturning couple** of magnitude 407 N·m. The buoy will tip over and will not return to vertical on its own.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compute a "righting couple" blindly using $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ without checking the sign of $GM$. If $GM$ is negative, the formula still gives a number — but it is an **overturning couple**, not a righting couple. Always **check the sign of $GM$ first** before labelling the couple type.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **The Four Key Points** (memorise their definitions): $K$ (Keel), $B$ (Centre of Buoyancy), $G$ (Centre of Gravity), $M$ (Metacentre). All measured vertically from the keel $K$.

- **The Master Equation**: $GM = KB + \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - KG$ — this single equation solves 90% of KTU numerical problems on this topic.

- **Stability Sign Rule**: $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable, $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ Unstable, $GM = 0 \Rightarrow$ Neutral.

- **Waterplane Second Moments of Area**:
   - Rectangle: $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ (where $B$ is the dimension perpendicular to the tilt axis).
   - Circle: $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$.

- **Righting Couple**: $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (only valid for small angles when $\sin\theta \approx \theta$).

- **Period of Oscillation**: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$. Note that $T$ **decreases** as $GM$ **increases** — a stiffer ship rolls faster (and is more uncomfortable in waves).

- **When Weight is Added**: Always recompute $V_{sub}$ (and hence $h$, $KB$) since the floating equilibrium condition changes.

- **When Load Position is Given**: Use the **principle of moments** to find the new $KG$: $KG_{new} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot y_i}{\sum W_i}$.

- **Sign Discipline**: $GM$ must be computed with sign. Never quote $\vert GM \vert$ without first stating whether the body is stable or unstable.

- **Small Angle Assumption**: All KTU problems implicitly use the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (radians), valid for $\theta \leq 10°$.

- **Units Check**: $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $V$ in m³, $W$ in N (not kN — convert if needed: $1 \text{ kN} = 1000 \text{ N}$), $I$ in m⁴, $BM$ in m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Six Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Ship and Offshore Hydrodynamics** (KTU 2024 Scheme terminology), a **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as an independent mode of motion or displacement by which the position and orientation of a free-floating rigid body can be uniquely described in three-dimensional space. A completely unrestrained rigid body possesses exactly **six (6) independent degrees of freedom**, comprising three translational motions and three rotational motions.

For a vessel operating on a free water surface, these six motions are conventionally expressed in a ship-fixed coordinate system (origin at the center of gravity **G**, with the **x-axis** pointing forward toward the bow, the **y-axis** pointing to the starboard side, and the **z-axis** pointing vertically downward toward the keel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The six degrees of freedom describe the complete set of independent rigid-body motions of a floating body, comprising three translations (Surge, Sway, Heave) and three rotations (Roll, Pitch, Yaw)."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Crate"

Imagine a **wooden crate** dropped gently into a calm swimming pool. The crate is not tied to anything and is free to move anywhere on the surface.

- Can you **push it forward**? Yes → this is one motion.
- Can you **push it sideways**? Yes → second motion.
- Can you **push it down** so that it sinks deeper? Yes → third motion.
- Can you **tilt it** so that the right corner goes under water? Yes → fourth motion.
- Can you **tip it forward** so the bow points down? Yes → fifth motion.
- Can you **spin it** in place like a clock hand, without moving its center? Yes → sixth motion.

These six independent ways of moving are exactly the **Six Degrees of Freedom** of a rigid floating body. No matter how complex the actual motion of a ship at sea appears, when decomposed into its components, it is a superposition (combination) of these six elementary motions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exactly six?** Because in classical (Newtonian) 3D rigid-body mechanics, a body has **3 coordinates to fix its position** $(x, y, z)$ and **3 angles to fix its orientation** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$. The minimum number of independent scalar coordinates required to fully specify the body's configuration is therefore **6** — and this count is the **number of degrees of freedom**.

## 1.3 The Six Motions — Classification

| Category | Motion Name | Standard Symbol | Direction of Displacement | Axis of Rotation |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Translation** | **Surge** | $\xi$ or $x$ (capital $X$) | Longitudinal (forward/aft) | None (pure translation along **x**) |
| **Translation** | **Sway** | $\eta$ or $y$ (capital $Y$) | Transverse (port/starboard) | None (pure translation along **y**) |
| **Translation** | **Heave** | $\zeta$ or $z$ (capital $Z$) | Vertical (up/down) | None (pure translation along **z**) |
| **Rotation** | **Roll** | $\phi$ | Rotational tilting sideways | About the longitudinal (**x**) axis |
| **Rotation** | **Pitch** | $\theta$ | Rotational tilting fore-aft | About the transverse (**y**) axis |
| **Rotation** | **Yaw** | $\psi$ | Rotational turning left/right | About the vertical (**z**) axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU/Seakeeping Symbol Convention** (ITTC recommended):
> Translations are denoted by lower-case Greek letters or small displacements: **surge $x$**, **sway $y$**, **heave $z$**.
> Rotations are denoted by small angles: **roll $\phi$**, **pitch $\theta$**, **yaw $\psi$**.
> Some textbooks (e.g., Newman) use capital letters $X, Y, Z$ for translation and the Greek $\phi, \theta, \psi$ for rotation. Both conventions are accepted in KTU examinations.

## 1.4 Physical Meaning of Each Motion

1. **Surge (x)** — The ship slides forward (positive) or backward (negative) along its longitudinal axis. Caused mainly by the propeller thrust overcoming drag.
2. **Sway (y)** — The ship slides sideways (positive = starboard). Primarily caused by transverse forces such as wind, current, or wave drift.
3. **Heave (z)** — The ship moves bodily up and down in the vertical direction. Primarily caused by passing wave crests and troughs.
4. **Roll ($\phi$)** — The ship rotates about its longitudinal axis, tilting port or starboard down. This is the **most critical** motion for passenger comfort and stability.
5. **Pitch ($\theta$)** — The ship rotates about its transverse axis, the bow goes up while the stern goes down (and vice versa). Caused by waves of length comparable to ship length.
6. **Yaw ($\psi$)** — The ship rotates about its vertical axis, swinging the bow left or right. Important for course-keeping and maneuvering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of General Rigid-Body Motion

The theorem of **Euler** (a foundational result in classical mechanics) states that any general displacement of a free rigid body in three-dimensional space can be uniquely decomposed into:

1. A **pure translation** of an arbitrary reference point (conventionally the center of gravity $G$), and
2. A **pure rotation** about an axis passing through that same point.

For a floating vessel, the reference point is the center of gravity $G$, and the six DOFs mathematically parameterize the full 6-dimensional configuration manifold of the ship.

The instantaneous position and orientation of the ship at time $t$ can be written as the column vector:

$$
\boldsymbol{\eta}(t) = \begin{Bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ z(t) \\ \phi(t) \\ \theta(t) \\ \psi(t) \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\eta}_1(t) \\ \boldsymbol{\eta}_2(t) \end{Bmatrix}
$$

where the **upper block** $\boldsymbol{\eta}_1 = (x, y, z)^T$ contains the three translational coordinates and the **lower block** $\boldsymbol{\eta}_2 = (\phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ contains the three rotational coordinates (Euler angles).

## 2.2 Why Six and Not More, Not Less

The number of degrees of freedom of an unconstrained rigid body in 3D space equals:

$$
\text{DOF} = 3 \text{ (position)} + 3 \text{ (orientation)} = 6
$$

- A single point particle in 3D has **3 DOFs** (its $x, y, z$ coordinates).
- A rigid body is a collection of many particles that maintain fixed relative distances.
- Each additional particle on the body removes 3 DOFs (because its position is fixed relative to the first particle).
- So for $N$ particles: $\text{DOF} = 3N - 3(N-1) = 6$.

This number 6 is **invariant**: a ship at sea, a satellite in orbit, a wrench floating in zero gravity — all have exactly 6 DOFs when free.

## 2.3 Translations vs. Rotations — Key Distinctions

| Property | Translations (Surge, Sway, Heave) | Rotations (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) |
|----------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Variable type | Linear displacement (length units, **m**) | Angular displacement (angle units, **rad**) |
| Derivatives used in dynamics | Velocity $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}$ (m/s) | Angular velocity $p, q, r$ (rad/s) |
| Axes convention | Along x, y, z respectively | About x, y, z respectively |
| Frames of reference | Body-fixed and Earth-fixed coincide for translation (no rotation needed for small translations in analysis) | Must use rotation matrix $R$ to transform |
| Effect on volume/displacement | Does not change the displaced volume (to first order for small motions) | Changes the displaced volume (rotational motions are critical for buoyancy-restoring effects) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Note:** Although all six DOFs coexist, in **ship hydrodynamics** the rotational DOFs (roll, pitch, yaw) are the most important for seakeeping analysis because they directly couple to the **hydrostatic restoring forces** (stability) and **wave-exciting moments**. The translations, while present, are often treated with simpler models.

## 2.4 Coupling Between the Six DOFs

In an ideal symmetric ship (port-starboard symmetry about the $x$-$z$ plane, and fore-aft symmetry about the $y$-$z$ plane), the equations of motion **decouple** into three independent pairs:

1. **Longitudinal (surge-heave-pitch) coupled set** — motion in the plane of symmetry ($x$-$z$ plane).
2. **Lateral (sway-roll-yaw) coupled set** — motion in the transverse plane.
3. (No third independent set — the two above cover all six DOFs.)

For a ship with full port-starboard symmetry, the roll motion **does not couple** with surge, sway, heave, or yaw at the linear level — only pitch couples with surge and heave. This **decoupling property** is exploited in KTU numerical problems to solve roll dynamics separately from surge-pitch-heave dynamics.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Position vector of ship | $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ | 6-DOF state vector | mixed (m, rad) |
| 2 | Number of DOFs | $\text{DOF} = 3N - 3(N-1) = 6$ | For any free rigid body in 3D | dimensionless |
| 3 | Translational DOFs | $x$ (surge), $y$ (sway), $z$ (heave) | Linear motion along body axes | **m** |
| 4 | Rotational DOFs | $\phi$ (roll), $\theta$ (pitch), $\psi$ (yaw) | Angular motion about body axes | **rad** |
| 5 | Euler angle rotation matrix (small angle) | $R \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\psi & \theta \\ \psi & 1 & -\phi \\ -\theta & \phi & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | For small rotations (linear theory) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Angular velocity in body frame | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [p, q, r]^T$ where $p=\dot{\phi}-\dot{\psi}\sin\theta$, $q=\dot{\theta}\cos\phi+\dot{\psi}\cos\theta\sin\phi$, $r=\dot{\psi}\cos\theta\cos\phi-\dot{\theta}\sin\phi$ | Used in 6-DOF dynamics | **rad/s** |
| 7 | Generalized velocity vector | $\boldsymbol{\nu} = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ | Body-fixed linear and angular velocities | **m/s**, **rad/s** |
| 8 | Kinetics (Newton-Euler 6-DOF) | $M\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + C(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{restore} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{damping} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{wave}$ | 6 coupled ODEs governing ship motion | mixed |
| 9 | Hydrostatic restoring for roll | $M_x = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi$ | Roll restoring moment per unit angle | N·m/rad |
| 10 | Hydrostatic restoring for pitch | $M_y = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L \cdot \theta$ | Pitch restoring moment per unit angle | N·m/rad |
| 11 | Equation count | 6 scalar ODEs (3 translation + 3 rotation) | Number of equations in 6-DOF model | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** All **displacements** are in **meters (m)** or **radians (rad)**; all **velocities** in **m/s** or **rad/s**; all **accelerations** in **m/s²** or **rad/s²**; all **forces** in **Newtons (N)**; all **moments** in **N·m**; **mass** in **kg**; **moment of inertia** in **kg·m²**; **density** $\rho$ in **kg/m³**; **gravity** $g$ in **m/s²**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the six degrees of freedom is the foundation of:

- **Ship Motion Prediction (Seakeeping Analysis):** Designers simulate ship response to waves using 6-DOF codes to ensure comfort, safety, and operability.
- **Stability Calculations:** Roll and pitch stability are calculated from the rotational DOFs; GM (metacentric height) governs the **roll restoring moment**.
- **Maneuvering Analysis:** Yaw, sway, and roll are coupled during turning; this is the **3-DOF maneuvering problem** (a sub-set of 6-DOF).
- **Offshore Engineering:** Semi-submersibles, FPSOs, and SPAR platforms are analyzed in 6-DOF for wave response and mooring system design.
- **Submarine Hydrodynamics:** Submerged bodies have all 6 DOFs (no hydrostatic restoring in surge, sway, yaw — only in roll, pitch, heave).
- **Naval Architecture Software:** Codes like WAMIT, AQWA, ORCAFLEX, and MOSES all use 6-DOF formulations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a Free Rigid Body

### Setup

Consider a free rigid body composed of $N$ point particles in 3-dimensional space. Each particle $i$ has position vector $\mathbf{r}_i$ with three Cartesian coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$.

### Step 1: Count the unconstrained DOFs

If the $N$ particles are completely independent (no constraints), the total number of degrees of freedom is:

$$
\text{Total scalar coordinates} = 3N
$$

### Step 2: Apply the rigidity constraint

A **rigid body** is defined by the requirement that the distance between any two particles $i$ and $j$ remains constant:

$$
\vert \mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_j \vert = \text{constant} = d_{ij}
$$

This single scalar equation removes **1 DOF** (per pair of particles). The number of independent pairs in a set of $N$ particles is $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$.

### Step 3: Subtract the constraints

$$
\text{DOF}_{rigid} = 3N - \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \cdot 1 + (\text{overcounting correction})
$$

A cleaner way is to **construct the body step by step**:

- Place the **first particle** $\Rightarrow$ needs $3$ coordinates $\Rightarrow$ DOF so far = $3$.
- Place the **second particle** at a fixed distance from the first $\Rightarrow$ $3 - 1 = 2$ new coordinates $\Rightarrow$ DOF so far = $3 + 2 = 5$.
- Place the **third particle** at fixed distances from both particles 1 and 2 $\Rightarrow$ $3 - 2 = 1$ new coordinate $\Rightarrow$ DOF so far = $5 + 1 = 6$.
- Each additional particle $i \geq 4$ is fixed by distances from particles 1, 2, 3 $\Rightarrow$ adds $3 - 3 = 0$ new DOFs.

### Step 4: Final result

$$
\boxed{\text{DOF} = 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 0 + \ldots = 6}
$$

This result is **independent of $N$** — any rigid body, regardless of how many particles it contains, has exactly **6 degrees of freedom** in 3D space.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Euler Angles and Rotation Matrix

### Step 1: Define the elementary rotations

A general rotation in 3D can be built from three elementary rotations about the body-fixed axes:

- **Roll** (rotation by $\phi$ about $x$-axis):

$$
R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Pitch** (rotation by $\theta$ about $y$-axis):

$$
R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Yaw** (rotation by $\psi$ about $z$-axis):

$$
R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Combine them in the standard nautical order

The standard convention (used in ship hydrodynamics) is to apply rotations in the order **yaw → pitch → roll** (i.e., $z \rightarrow y \rightarrow x$):

$$
R = R_x(\phi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the product

Multiplying step by step:

$$
R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & -\cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\theta \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then multiplying by $R_x(\phi)$ from the left:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & -\cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\theta \\ \cos\phi\sin\psi + \sin\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi & \cos\phi\cos\psi - \sin\phi\sin\theta\sin\psi & -\sin\phi\cos\theta \\ \sin\phi\sin\psi - \cos\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\phi\cos\psi + \cos\phi\sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\phi\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Small-angle linearization (crucial for KTU problems)

For small oscillations (typical of ship motion in mild-to-moderate waves), we assume $\phi, \theta, \psi \ll 1$ rad, so that $\sin(\cdot) \approx (\cdot)$ and $\cos(\cdot) \approx 1$. The rotation matrix reduces to:

$$
R \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\psi & \theta \\ \psi & 1 & -\phi \\ -\theta & \phi & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **linearized 6-DOF rotation matrix** used in KTU standard problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Transformation (Earth-frame $\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}}$ vs. Body-frame $\boldsymbol{\nu}$)

### Step 1: The problem

The 6-DOF configuration vector $\boldsymbol{\eta} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ is written in the **Earth-fixed frame**, but the linear and angular velocities $(u, v, w, p, q, r)^T$ are most conveniently expressed in the **body-fixed frame**. We need a transformation $J(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ such that:

$$
\boldsymbol{\nu} = J(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \cdot \dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}}
$$

### Step 2: Linear velocity part

The linear velocity in the body frame $(u, v, w)^T$ relates to Earth-frame position derivatives $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z})^T$ via the rotation matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \end{bmatrix} = R^T(\phi, \theta, \psi) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{z} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Angular velocity part

The body-fixed angular velocity $(p, q, r)^T$ relates to the Euler-angle rates $(\dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi})^T$ through the **Euler-rate transformation matrix** $T(\boldsymbol{\eta})$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix} = T(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The exact form of $T$ is:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \cos\phi & \sin\phi\cos\theta \\ 0 & -\sin\phi & \cos\phi\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Combined transformation

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & 0_{3\times 3} \\ 0_{3\times 3} & T(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

So the full 6-DOF kinematic relationship is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\nu} = J(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}}
$$

with the inverse:

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = J^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \boldsymbol{\nu}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of 6-DOF Equations

| DOF | Coordinate | Force/Moment Equation (linear, simplified) | Dominant Restoring Force/Moment |
|-----|-----------|--------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **1. Surge ($x$)** | $x$ | $m\ddot{x} = X_{wave} + X_{prop} - X_{drag}$ | None (no hydrostatic restoring) |
| **2. Sway ($y$)** | $y$ | $m\ddot{y} = Y_{wave} + Y_{wind} - Y_{drag}$ | None (no hydrostatic restoring) |
| **3. Heave ($z$)** | $z$ | $m\ddot{z} = Z_{wave} - \rho g A_{WP} z$ | $Z_{restore} = -\rho g A_{WP} z$ |
| **4. Roll ($\phi$)** | $\phi$ | $I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} = K_{wave} - \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \phi - B_{44}\dot{\phi}$ | $K_{restore} = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \phi$ |
| **5. Pitch ($\theta$)** | $\theta$ | $I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} = M_{wave} - \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L \theta - B_{55}\dot{\theta}$ | $M_{restore} = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L \theta$ |
| **6. Yaw ($\psi$)** | $\psi$ | $I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} = N_{wave} + N_{rudder} - B_{66}\dot{\psi}$ | None (no hydrostatic restoring) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology:**
> - $m$ = ship mass (kg)
> - $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz}$ = moments of inertia about the body axes (kg·m²)
> - $A_{WP}$ = waterplane area (m²)
> - $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)
> - $\overline{GM}_T$ = transverse metacentric height (m)
> - $\overline{GM}_L$ = longitudinal metacentric height (m)
> - $B_{ii}$ = damping coefficients
> - $X, Y, Z$ = external forces; $K, M, N$ = external moments

## 3.5 Key Numerical Insight: Order-of-Magnitude of 6-DOF Terms

For a typical large cargo ship of length $L \approx 200$ m, mass $m \approx 5 \times 10^7$ kg, $I_{xx} \approx 2 \times 10^{10}$ kg·m², $\nabla \approx 5 \times 10^4$ m³, $A_{WP} \approx 1500$ m², $\overline{GM}_T \approx 1.5$ m:

- **Heave natural period:** $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{\rho g A_{WP}}} \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^7}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1500}} \approx 11.4$ s
- **Roll natural period:** $T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T}} \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5 \times 10^4 \cdot 1.5}} \approx 23$ s
- **Roll is much slower** than heave, demonstrating why roll stability is a critical design concern in KTU problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A complete 6-DOF ship motion analysis pipeline takes environmental inputs (waves, wind, thrust, rudder) and computes external forces/moments. These are then combined with the hydrodynamic coefficient matrices (added mass $M$, damping $B$, restoring $C$) in a single coupled 6×6 system of ordinary differential equations. The solution yields the six time-histories of motion $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$, which are then post-processed for design checks.
