# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Q: Define the term "Six Degrees of Freedom" as applied to a floating body. Name the six motions with their conventional symbols.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** of a free-floating rigid body refer to the six independent modes of motion required to completely describe the position and orientation of the body in three-dimensional space. These comprise **three translational** and **three rotational** motions.

In ship hydrodynamics, using a body-fixed coordinate system with origin at the center of gravity $G$, the six DOFs are:

1. **Surge** ($x$) — translation along the longitudinal (bow-stern) axis.
2. **Sway** ($y$) — translation along the transverse (port-starboard) axis.
3. **Heave** ($z$) — translation along the vertical (keel-upward) axis.
4. **Roll** ($\phi$) — rotation about the longitudinal $x$-axis.
5. **Pitch** ($\theta$) — rotation about the transverse $y$-axis.
6. **Yaw** ($\psi$) — rotation about the vertical $z$-axis.

A free floating body has exactly **6** degrees of freedom because in 3D space, a rigid body requires 3 position coordinates and 3 orientation angles to fully specify its configuration. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for naming the six with symbols)**

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Style]**
**Q: Distinguish between translational and rotational degrees of freedom of a floating vessel. Which of the six DOFs possess a hydrostatic restoring force/moment, and which do not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Translational DOFs (Surge, Sway, Heave) | Rotational DOFs (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) |
|--------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Nature | Linear displacement (m) | Angular displacement (rad) |
| Type of motion | Body moves as a whole in a straight line | Body rotates about an axis |

**Hydrostatic Restoring Presence:**
- **Heave** — possesses hydrostatic restoring (from waterplane area $A_{WP}$): $F_z = -\rho g A_{WP} \cdot z$.
- **Roll** — possesses hydrostatic restoring (from transverse metacentric height $\overline{GM}_T$): $M_x = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi$.
- **Pitch** — possesses hydrostatic restoring (from longitudinal metacentric height $\overline{GM}_L$): $M_y = -\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L \cdot \theta$.
- **Surge, Sway, Yaw** — **do NOT possess** any hydrostatic restoring force or moment (the vessel can drift indefinitely under a steady force).

**(3 marks: 1 for distinction, 2 for restoring analysis)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Paper Pattern]**
**Q: With neat sketches, describe the Six Degrees of Freedom of a floating body. Derive the 6-DOF equation of motion in vector-matrix form and explain the role of the added-mass, damping, and hydrostatic restoring matrices.**

#### (a) Describe the Six Degrees of Freedom with sketches (7 marks)

**Solution:**

A floating vessel, treated as a free rigid body, exhibits **six independent modes of motion** in three-dimensional space. In a body-fixed coordinate system with origin at the center of gravity $G$ and axes as defined below, the six DOFs are:

**Three Translational DOFs (along the axes):**

1. **Surge ($x$):** Linear motion along the longitudinal axis (bow-stern direction). A pure surge moves the ship forward or backward without any rotation.

2. **Sway ($y$):** Linear motion along the transverse axis (port-starboard direction). A pure sway slides the ship sideways.

3. **Heave ($z$):** Linear motion along the vertical axis (up-down). A pure heave raises or lowers the ship bodily.

**Three Rotational DOFs (about the axes):**

4. **Roll ($\phi$):** Rotation about the longitudinal $x$-axis. The ship tilts port or starboard. This motion is critical for stability and comfort.

5. **Pitch ($\theta$):** Rotation about the transverse $y$-axis. The bow rises while the stern falls (or vice versa). Important in head or following seas.

6. **Yaw ($\psi$):** Rotation about the vertical $z$-axis. The ship turns left or right about a vertical axis through $G$. Governs course-keeping.

> *[A neat sketch showing the ship with the 3 axes and the 6 motion arrows would carry 2 marks; a clean table listing them would carry 2 marks; correct symbol convention carries 1 mark; identification of translations vs rotations carries 2 marks — total 7 marks]*

#### (b) Derive the 6-DOF equation of motion in vector-matrix form and explain the matrices (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: General vector-matrix form**

The complete 6-DOF equations of motion of a floating vessel, expressed in the body-fixed frame, are written in the standard compact form:

$$
M \dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + C(\boldsymbol{\nu}) \boldsymbol{\nu} + D(\boldsymbol{\nu}) \boldsymbol{\nu} + g(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{wave} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{control}
$$

where:

- $\boldsymbol{\nu} = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ is the body-fixed velocity vector (6×1).
- $M$ is the **6×6 system mass + added-mass matrix**.
- $C(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ is the **Coriolis-centripetal matrix** (arises from rotation in non-inertial body frame).
- $D(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ is the **damping matrix** (viscous, wave-drift, lift, etc.).
- $g(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ is the **hydrostatic restoring vector** (function of position/orientation).
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{wave}$ is the **wave excitation force/moment vector**.
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{control}$ is the **control force/moment vector** (thrust, rudder, etc.).

**Step 2: Expansion into the individual DOFs**

Writing out all 6 scalar equations:

$$
m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) = X_{wave} + X_{prop} - X_{drag}
$$

$$
m(\dot{v} + ur) = Y_{wave} + Y_{wind} - Y_{drag}
$$

$$
m(\dot{w} - uq) = Z_{wave} - \rho g A_{WP} z - Z_{damp}
$$

$$
I_{xx}\dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr = K_{wave} - \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \phi - B_{44}\dot{\phi}
$$

$$
I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pr = M_{wave} - \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L \theta - B_{55}\dot{\theta}
$$

$$
I_{zz}\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq = N_{wave} + N_{rudder} - B_{66}\dot{r}
$$

**Step 3: Role of each matrix**

- **$M$ — System Inertia Matrix (6×6):** Combines the rigid-body mass/inertia with the **added-mass** matrix $M_A$ (hydrodynamic inertia of the surrounding water). $M = M_{RB} + M_A$. The added-mass represents the inertial resistance of the fluid to acceleration of the body. Units are kg or kg·m².

- **$C(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ — Coriolis Matrix (6×6):** Contains the Coriolis and centripetal terms that arise because the body-fixed frame is non-inertial (it rotates with the ship). Skew-symmetric, important at moderate speeds.

- **$D(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ — Damping Matrix (6×6):** Represents energy dissipation. Includes linear viscous damping, nonlinear drag ($\propto \vert u \vert u$), and wave-drift damping. The diagonal terms $B_{ii}$ are the principal damping coefficients.

- **$g(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ — Hydrostatic Restoring Vector (6×1):** Represents buoyancy-driven restoring forces. Nonzero for heave, roll, and pitch only.

> *[Marks allocation: 2 marks for correct vector-matrix form, 2 marks for expansion into 6 scalar equations with hydrostatic restoring terms, 1 mark each for role of $M$, $C$, $D$ matrices — total 7 marks]*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Paper Pattern]**
**Q: (a) With the help of a suitable sketch, explain the body-fixed coordinate system used in ship hydrodynamics and identify the six degrees of freedom with their positive sign conventions. (b) A floating vessel of mass $m = 5 \times 10^6$ kg, waterplane area $A_{WP} = 1000$ m², displaced volume $\nabla = 5000$ m³, transverse metacentric height $\overline{GM}_T = 1.2$ m, and roll moment of inertia $I_{xx} = 1.5 \times 10^9$ kg·m², executes small oscillations in still water. Compute (i) the heave natural period, and (ii) the roll natural period. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

#### (a) Body-fixed coordinate system and sign convention (7 marks)

**Solution:**

In ship hydrodynamics, a **right-handed body-fixed (ship-fixed) coordinate system** is conventionally used:

- **Origin $G$** at the center of gravity of the vessel.
- **$x$-axis** — pointing from stern to bow (forward direction). Positive $x$ = forward.
- **$y$-axis** — pointing to the starboard side. Positive $y$ = starboard.
- **$z$-axis** — pointing vertically downward (from G toward the keel). Positive $z$ = downward.

The **six DOFs** with positive sign conventions (ITTC standard):

| DOF | Symbol | Positive Direction |
|-----|--------|--------------------|
| Surge | $x$ | Forward (toward bow) |
| Sway | $y$ | Starboard |
| Heave | $z$ | Downward |
| Roll | $\phi$ | Starboard down (rotation about $x$) |
| Pitch | $\theta$ | Bow up (rotation about $y$) |
| Yaw | $\psi$ | Bow to starboard (rotation about $z$) |

> *[A clearly labeled 3D sketch of a ship with the axes and 6 motion arrows (3 linear + 3 rotational) would earn 3 marks; correct naming + symbol convention = 2 marks; positive sign convention table = 2 marks — total 7 marks]*

#### (b) Computation of natural periods (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 5 \times 10^6$ kg
- $A_{WP} = 1000$ m²
- $\nabla = 5000$ m³
- $\overline{GM}_T = 1.2$ m
- $I_{xx} = 1.5 \times 10^9$ kg·m²
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Heave natural period (3.5 marks):**

The linearized heave equation in still water is:

$$
m\ddot{z} + \rho g A_{WP} z = 0
$$

The heave natural frequency is:

$$
\omega_z = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g A_{WP}}{m}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\omega_z = \sqrt{\frac{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000}{5 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}055{,}250}{5 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{2.01105} \approx 1.4181 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The heave natural period is:

$$
T_z = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_z} = \frac{2\pi}{1.4181} \approx 4.43 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Roll natural period (3.5 marks):**

The linearized roll equation in still water is:

$$
I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} + \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi = 0
$$

The roll natural frequency is:

$$
\omega_\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T}{I_{xx}}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\omega_\phi = \sqrt{\frac{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5000 \cdot 1.2}{1.5 \times 10^9}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.0332 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^9}} = \sqrt{0.040221} \approx 0.2006 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The roll natural period is:

$$
T_\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_\phi} = \frac{2\pi}{0.2006} \approx 31.32 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{T_z \approx 4.43 \text{ s} \quad \text{and} \quad T_\phi \approx 31.3 \text{ s}}
$$

The roll period is about **7 times** longer than the heave period, which is why roll is the dominant low-frequency motion concern in ship design.

> *[Marks: 1.5 for heave formula, 1 for heave numerical evaluation, 1 for final period; same for roll — total 7 marks]*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in 6-DOF Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 marks):** Confusing the direction of $z$-axis (some textbooks use $z$ pointing UP, KTU/ITTC uses $z$ pointing DOWN). This changes signs in heave restoring force equation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to distinguish hydrostatic restoring (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students incorrectly write a "restoring force" for surge, sway, or yaw. Mark strictly: only heave, roll, and pitch have hydrostatic restoring.
>
> 3. **Wrong moment-of-inertia used in roll calculation (–1 mark):** Use $I_{xx}$ for roll (about longitudinal axis), NOT $I_{zz}$ (which is for yaw). Similarly, pitch uses $I_{yy}$.
>
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers (–0.5 mark):** Always include units (s, m, rad, m/s², etc.) in final answers.
>
> 5. **Confusing degrees with radians (–0.5 mark):** All formulas (e.g., $\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$) require angles in **radians**. If a problem gives roll in degrees, convert first.
>
> 6. **Omitting the "3 translations + 3 rotations = 6" derivation (–1 mark):** Examiners expect at least a brief justification (e.g., rigidity constraint argument or 3 position + 3 orientation argument) when asked to "show that there are six DOFs."
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention Euler angles / rotation matrix (–1 mark):** For 6-DOF problems, examiners often expect a mention that the orientation is described using **Euler angles** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ and a **rotation matrix** $R(\phi, \theta, \psi)$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **6-DOF** = **3 translations (Surge $x$, Sway $y$, Heave $z$) + 3 rotations (Roll $\phi$, Pitch $\theta$, Yaw $\psi$)**.
- [ ] **Origin of the coordinate system** is at the **center of gravity $G$**; $x$ forward, $y$ starboard, $z$ downward (KTU/ITTC convention).
- [ ] **Why exactly 6?** 3 position coordinates + 3 orientation coordinates (Euler angles).
- [ ] **Hydrostatic restoring exists ONLY for heave, roll, and pitch** — not for surge, sway, or yaw.
- [ ] **Heave restoring coefficient** = $\rho g A_{WP}$ (waterplane area).
- [ ] **Roll restoring coefficient** = $\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T$ (transverse GM).
- [ ] **Pitch restoring coefficient** = $\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L$ (longitudinal GM).
- [ ] **Heave natural period** $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{\rho g A_{WP}}}$.
- [ ] **Roll natural period** $T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx}}{\rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T}}$.
- [ ] **6-DOF equation in compact form:** $M\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + C(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + D(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + g(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}$.
- [ ] **Coupling in symmetric ships:** {Surge, Heave, Pitch} form one coupled subset; {Sway, Roll, Yaw} form another.
- [ ] **Units check:** displacements in **m** or **rad**; velocities in **m/s** or **rad/s**; forces in **N**; moments in **N·m**; inertia in **kg·m²**; $\rho$ in **kg/m³**; $g$ in **m/s²**.
- [ ] **Newton-Euler 6-DOF** combines three translational equations (Newton's second law) and three rotational equations (Euler's second law).
- [ ] **Small-angle linearization** is a standard KTU assumption: $\sin(\cdot) \approx (\cdot)$, $\cos(\cdot) \approx 1$.
- [ ] **Roll is the slowest and most critical** motion for ship stability and passenger comfort.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces Acting on a Ship in Static Condition and in Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Forces Acting on a Ship in Static Condition and in Waves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Forces Acting on a Ship in Static Condition ### Formal Definition A floating ship at rest in still water is acted upon by **two primary force systems**: the gravitational force (Weight, $W$) acting vertically downward through the **Center of Gravity (G)**, and the hydrostatic force (Buoyancy, $B$ or $\Delta$) acting vertically upward through the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**. For a ship in **static equilibrium**, these two forces must be equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and collinear (acting along the same vertical line), satisfying the conditions: $$W = B = \rho g \nabla$$ where $\rho$ is the density of water ($1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for seawater), $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$), and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The study of ship statics is fundamental to naval architecture because it defines the conditions for a ship to float upright, the metacentric height, and the righting lever at various angles of heel — all crucial for the IMO intact stability code compliance. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of a ship as a giant cork floating in a bathtub. The cork's weight pulls it down, but the water pushes it back up with an equal force. The "magic" is that the water has to move out of the way to make room for the cork, and the weight of that displaced water is exactly what pushes the cork back up — this is **Archimedes' Principle**. When the ship tilts (heels), the shape of the displaced water changes, and the upward push point shifts, creating a "self-righting" couple much like a pendulum that always swings back to vertical. --- ## 1.2 Forces Acting on a Ship in Waves ### Formal Definition When a ship operates in a seaway (waves), it is subjected to **time-varying hydrodynamic forces** superimposed on the static weight and buoyancy. These wave-induced forces are classified into: - **Froude-Krylov Force ($F_{FK}$):** The pressure field from the undisturbed incident wave acting on the instantaneous wetted surface of the ship. - **Diffraction Force ($F_D$):** Force due to the modification of the wave field caused by the presence of the hull. - **Radiation Force ($F_R$):** Force generated by the ship's own oscillatory motions (added mass and damping). - **Wave Drift Force ($F_{WD}$):** A slowly-varying second-order mean force that adds to the calm-water resistance. The total hydrodynamic force in waves is: $$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{FK} + \vec{F}_{D} + \vec{F}_{R} + \vec{F}_{WD}$$ ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Imagine standing in a swimming pool with waves passing by. The waves push you around (Froude-Krylov), your body blocks and deflects the waves, creating new disturbances around you (Diffraction), and as you sway to keep balance, you generate your own ripples that interact with the incoming waves (Radiation). The slow drift you feel sideways is the Wave Drift Force. A ship experiences all these simultaneously — and this is why navigating in storms is so challenging! ### Key Physical Constants - **Sea water density:** $\rho = \mathbf{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ - **Fresh water density:** $\rho = \mathbf{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ - **Standard gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$ - **Specific weight of seawater:** $\gamma = \mathbf{10.05 \text{ kN/m}^3}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static Condition: Transverse Stability Theory ### Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — The Two Forces:** Weight $W$ (down at $G$) and Buoyancy $B$ (up at $B$) act on the ship. - **Step 2 — Equilibrium Conditions:** $W = B$ and the lines of action must coincide. The line of action is the **vertical through G** and the **vertical through B** must be the same. - **Step 3 — Small Heel Angle $\phi$:** As the ship tilts, $G$ does not move (weight is unchanged), but $B$ shifts to a new position $B_\phi$ because the underwater hull shape changes. The new line of buoyancy passes through a new point. - **Step 4 — Metacenter M:** The vertical through the new $B_\phi$ intersects the original vertical centerline at point $M$. This intersection point is called the **Metacenter**. - **Step 5 — Metacentric Radius ($BM$):** $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$, where $I_T$ is the second moment of area of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline. - **Step 6 — Metacentric Height ($GM$):** $GM = KM - KG$, the fundamental measure of initial transverse stability. - **Step 7 — Righting Lever ($GZ$):** At a small angle $\phi$, $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$. For initial stability, $GZ \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (in radians). - **Step 8 — Stability Criterion:** Ship is stable if $GM > 0$ (positive righting moment) and unstable if $GM < 0$ (negative or capsizing moment). ### The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why two forces?** Newton's third law and gravity demand it. - **Why does $B$ shift?** The underwater volume redistribution due to heeling shifts the centroid of the displaced water. - **Why is $BM = I_T / \nabla$?** Geometrically derived from the change in the center of buoyancy with heel angle using the shift-of-buoyancy theorem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** For very large heel angles (> 10°–15°), $M$ is no longer fixed. The locus of $M$ as the ship heels is called the **Metacentric Evolute**. The true righting lever at large angles is computed using **Wall-sided correction** or **integration methods** (Simpson's rule on the ship's half-breadth or Bonjean curves). --- ## 2.2 Forces in Waves: Decomposition and Physical Meaning ### Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — Froude-Krylov Force ($F_{FK}$):** The pressure of the incident (undisturbed) wave field integrated over the **instantaneous wetted surface** of the ship. This is the largest contribution in long waves. - **Step 2 — Diffraction Force ($F_D$):** The scattering of the wave field by the hull. Dominant when the ship is large compared to the wavelength ($\lambda / L < 1$). - **Step 3 — Radiation Force ($F_R$):** Force due to the ship oscillating in otherwise still water. Has two components: - **Added Mass (Inertia) Force:** Proportional to acceleration, with coefficient $\mu(\omega)$. - **Damping Force:** Proportional to velocity, with coefficient $\lambda(\omega)$. - **Step 4 — Wave Drift Force ($F_{WD}$):** A second-order (in wave amplitude) mean force in the direction of wave propagation. Increases the effective resistance of the ship in waves (called **Added Resistance in Waves, $R_{AW}$**). - **Step 5 — Equation of Motion in Waves:** In frequency domain, for each mode of motion $j$ (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw): $$\left( m + \mu_{jj}(\omega) \right) \ddot{x}_j + \lambda_{jj}(\omega) \dot{x}_j + C_{jj} x_j = F_{j,exc}(\omega)$$ where $F_{j,exc} = F_{FK,j} + F_{D,j}$ is the **exciting force**. ### Real-World Utility - Ship designers use these formulations to predict **seakeeping performance**, fuel consumption in waves, and crew/passenger comfort. - Critical for FPSOs, naval vessels, and offshore platforms operating in harsh seas. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $W = \rho g \nabla$ | Archimedes' Principle (Displacement) | N or kN | | 2 | $B = W$ | Equilibrium of floating body | N | | 3 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius (transverse) | m | | 4 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Distance from keel to metacenter | m | | 5 | $KG = \dfrac{\sum (w_i \cdot z_i)}{W}$ | Height of center of gravity above keel | m | | 6 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m | | 7 | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$ | Righting lever at small angle $\phi$ | m | | 8 | $GZ = V \cdot \sin\phi \cdot BM$ | Alternative form (initial stability) | m | | 9 | $F_{FK}$ | Froude-Krylov force = $\int_S p_{inc} \, dS$ | N | | 10 | $F_D$ | Diffraction force (from scattered potential) | N | | 11 | $F_R$ | Radiation force (added mass + damping) | N | | 12 | $R_{AW}$ | Added resistance in waves (2nd-order) | N | | 13 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{gM}}$ | Natural roll period | s | | 14 | $\mu(\omega)$ | Added mass coefficient (frequency dependent) | kg | | 15 | $\lambda(\omega)$ | Damping coefficient (frequency dependent) | Ns/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Consistency Check (KTU Examiners Look for This!):** Always express weight in kN, length in m, and use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified. Mixed SI units lead to mark deductions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Metacentric Radius $BM = I_T / \nabla$ ### Setup Consider a ship of volume $\nabla$ heeling by a small angle $\phi$. The wedge $W_1$ on one side is emerged; the wedge $W_2$ on the other side is immersed. The buoyant volume is conserved because the ship is still floating at the same waterline (ignoring free surface effects). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the wedges.** Let the waterplane area be $A_W$, and the second moment of the waterplane about the centerline (longitudinal axis) be $I_T$. The volume of each wedge is: $$v = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot A_{W} \cdot \text{(shift)}$$ **Step 2 — Shift in center of buoyancy.** By the principle of moments, the shift of the center of buoyancy is: $$\overline{BB_\phi} = \dfrac{v \cdot (\text{moment arm})}{\nabla}$$ **Step 3 — Moment arm calculation.** For a small angle $\phi$, the centroid of the emerged wedge $W_1$ and the immersed wedge $W_2$ are at horizontal distances of approximately $y_1$ and $y_2$ from the centerline. By the second moment of area theorem: $$\dfrac{\overline{BB_\phi}}{\sin\phi} = BM$$ **Step 4 — Combine using the shift-of-buoyancy theorem.** The shift of $B$ perpendicular to the original vertical is: $$BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$$ This is the famous **Wall-sided formula** for small angles, valid when the sides of the ship near the waterline are vertical. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Righting Lever $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ (Initial Stability) ### Setup For a small heel angle $\phi$, the metacenter $M$ is approximately fixed. The weight $W$ acts down at $G$, and buoyancy $B$ acts up at $B_\phi$, with $B_\phi$ lying on the new vertical through $M$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the geometry.** Triangle $GMB$ is formed where $GM$ is on the centerline, $B$ has shifted by $BM \sin\phi$ horizontally and $BM(1 - \cos\phi)$ vertically. **Step 2 — Perpendicular distance from G to the new buoyancy line.** The horizontal distance from $G$ to the line of action of $B$ (the new vertical through $M$) is: $$GZ = GM \sin\phi$$ **Step 3 — Righting moment.** The righting moment is: $$\text{RM} = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \phi \text{ (radians)}$$ **Step 4 — Stability criteria.** - If $GM > 0$: positive righting moment → **Stable** - If $GM = 0$: neutral equilibrium - If $GM < 0$: negative righting moment → **Unstable** (ship will capsize) --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equation of Motion in Waves (Heave, for example) ### Setup A ship in regular head waves of amplitude $\zeta_a$ and frequency $\omega_e$ experiences a heave exciting force. The equation of motion is: ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Total heave force in waves.** $$F_{heave}(t) = F_{exc}(t) - F_{radiation}(t)$$ **Step 2 — Exciting force (Froude-Krylov + Diffraction).** $$F_{exc}(t) = F_a \cos(\omega_e t + \epsilon_F)$$ where $F_a$ is the exciting force amplitude and $\epsilon_F$ is the phase. **Step 3 — Radiation force (Newton's second law for added mass + damping).** $$F_{rad}(t) = \mu_{33}(\omega_e) \ddot{z} + \lambda_{33}(\omega_e) \dot{z}$$ **Step 4 — Combine into a single ODE.** $$\left(m + \mu_{33}\right) \ddot{z} + \lambda_{33} \dot{z} + C_{33} z = F_a \cos(\omega_e t + \epsilon_F)$$ **Step 5 — Steady-state solution.** Assuming $z(t) = z_a \cos(\omega_e t - \epsilon_z)$: $$z_a = \dfrac{F_a}{\sqrt{\left[C_{33} - (m + \mu_{33})\omega_e^2\right]^2 + \left[\lambda_{33} \omega_e\right]^2}}$$ This is the **frequency response function** for heave motion. Peak response occurs near the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{C_{33} / (m + \mu_{33})}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Ship Behavior Comparison | Condition | Forces | Equilibrium | Stability Test | |-----------|--------|-------------|----------------| | **Upright in calm water** | $W \downarrow$, $B \uparrow$ | $W = B$, lines collinear | $G$ and $B$ on centerline | | **Small heel in calm water** | $W$, $B$ shifted | New $B_\phi$ on vertical through $M$ | $GM > 0$ | | **Large heel in calm water** | $W$, $B$, $M$ moves | $M$ traces metacentric evolute | $GZ$ curve, IMO criteria | | **Small ship in long waves** | $F_{FK}$ dominant | Coupled heave/pitch/roll | RAO analysis | | **Large ship in short waves** | $F_D$ dominant | Slender-body theory applies | 3D diffraction panel method | | **Head seas, low speed** | $R_{AW}$ + slamming | Pure added resistance | 2nd-order strip theory | | **Beam seas** | Strong roll excitation | Parametric roll possible | $T_{roll}$ vs $T_{wave}$ | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always draw a free-body diagram showing $G$, $B$, $M$, and the righting lever $GZ$ in stability problems. Marks are awarded for clear diagrams even if calculations are partially incomplete. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Metacenter Geometry (Transverse Stability) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Original[Original Upright Position] K1[K - Keel] B1[B - Center of Buoyancy] G1[G - Center of Gravity] M1[M - Metacenter] end subgraph Heeled[Heeled Position phi] K2[K - Keel] B2[New B at angle phi] G2[G unchanged] M2[M on new vertical] end K1 --> B1 B1 --> G1 G1 --> M1 M1 --> B1 K2 --> B2 B2 --> G2 G2 --> M2 M2 --> B2 B1 -.heel angle phi.-> B2 M1 -.metacentric evolute.-> M2 style K1 fill:#e1f5ff style B1 fill:#fff4e1 style G1 fill:#ffe1e1 style M1 fill:#e1ffe1 style K2 fill:#e1f5ff style B2 fill:#fff4e1 style G2 fill:#ffe1e1 style M2 fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 4.2 Force Decomposition in Waves (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Input[Wave Environment] WAVES[Incident Wave Field
amplitude zeta_a, frequency omega_e] end subgraph Forces[Hydrodynamic Forces on Ship] FFK[Froude Krylov Force F_FK
Incident pressure on hull] FD[Diffraction Force F_D
Wave scattering by hull] FR[Radiation Force F_R
Ship-induced oscillations] FWD[Wave Drift Force F_WD
Second-order mean force] end subgraph Ship[Motions and Stability] HEAVE[Heave Motion] PITCH[Pitch Motion] ROLL[Roll Motion] SURGE[Surge Motion] STAB[Dynamic Stability Check] end subgraph Output[Engineering Outputs] RAO[Response Amplitude Operators] RAW[Added Resistance R_AW] COMFORT[Crew Comfort Index] end WAVES --> FFK WAVES --> FD FFK --> HEAVE FFK --> PITCH FFK --> ROLL FD --> HEAVE FD --> PITCH FD --> ROLL FR --> HEAVE FR --> PITCH FR --> ROLL FR --> SURGE FWD --> SURGE FWD --> STAB HEAVE --> RAO PITCH --> RAO ROLL --> RAO SURGE --> RAW STAB --> COMFORT RAO --> COMFORT style WAVES fill:#b3e5fc style FFK fill:#fff9c4 style FD fill:#fff9c4 style FR fill:#fff9c4 style FWD fill:#fff9c4 style HEAVE fill:#c8e6c9 style PITCH fill:#c8e6c9 style ROLL fill:#c8e6c9 style SURGE fill:#c8e6c9 style STAB fill:#c8e6c9 style RAO fill:#f8bbd0 style RAW fill:#f8bbd0 style COMFORT fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ## 4.3 Stability States Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A[Ship at Rest] --> B{Is GM positive?} B -->|Yes| C[STABLE
Positive Righting Moment] B -->|No| D[UNSTABLE
Capsize Risk] B -->|Zero| E[NEUTRAL
No Restoring Force] C --> F[Small Heel: GZ approx GM sin phi] C --> G[Large Heel: Use GZ Curve] G --> H{Angle of Vanishing Stability
GZ equals 0?} H -->|Yes| I[Deck Edge Immersion Check] H -->|No| J[Stable up to max GZ] D --> K[Add Ballast Below G] D --> L[Redesign Hull Form] style A fill:#e3f2fd style C fill:#c8e6c9 style D fill:#ffcdd2 style E fill:#fff9c4 style F fill:#b2dfdb style G fill:#b2dfdb style H fill:#ffe0b2 style I fill:#ffccbc style J fill:#a5d6a7 style K fill:#ce93d8 style L fill:#ce93d8 ``` ## 4.4 Roll Motion in Beam Seas (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Excitation[Wave Excitation] WAVEFORCE[Wave Exciting Moment
M_wave from F_FK and F_D] end subgraph Restoring[Restoring System] GMSTIFF[GM Sin phi
Hydrostatic Stiffness] end subgraph Damping[Damping System] LIN_DAMP[Linear Damping
B_1 phi dot] QUAD_DAMP[Quadratic Damping
B_2 phi dot mod phi dot] LIFT_DAMP[Lift Damping
B_3 phi dot mod phi dot] end subgraph Inertia[Inertia] ROLL_IN[Roll Inertia
I_xx phi double dot] ADD_IN[Added Moment of Inertia
A_44 phi double dot] end subgraph Output[Output] ROLL_ANGLE[Roll Angle phi] RAO_ROLL[Roll RAO] end WAVEFORCE --> ROLL_ANGLE GMSTIFF --> ROLL_ANGLE LIN_DAMP --> ROLL_ANGLE QUAD_DAMP --> ROLL_ANGLE LIFT_DAMP --> ROLL_ANGLE ROLL_IN --> ROLL_ANGLE ADD_IN --> ROLL_ANGLE ROLL_ANGLE --> RAO_ROLL style WAVEFORCE fill:#ffcdd2 style GMSTIFF fill:#c8e6c9 style LIN_DAMP fill:#fff9c4 style QUAD_DAMP fill:#fff9c4 style LIFT_DAMP fill:#fff9c4 style ROLL_IN fill:#b3e5fc style ADD_IN fill:#b3e5fc style ROLL_ANGLE fill:#f8bbd0 style RAO_ROLL fill:#ce93d8 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Metacenter and Metacentric Height. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The **metacenter (M)** of a floating body is the point of intersection of the vertical line through the center of buoyancy in the upright position and the vertical line through the new center of buoyancy when the body is tilted by a small angle. The **metacentric height (GM)** is the distance between the center of gravity (G) and the metacenter (M). Mathematically: $$GM = KM - KG$$ A positive $GM$ indicates initial transverse stability of the ship. ### Question 2: What is the Froude-Krylov Force? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Froude-Krylov force** is the force exerted on a ship by the pressure field of the **undisturbed incident waves**, integrated over the **instantaneous wetted surface** of the hull. It represents the largest contribution to the wave-induced exciting force in long waves. Mathematically: $$F_{FK} = \iint_S p_{incident}(x, y, z, t) \, dS$$ It is named after William Froude and Aleksandr Krylov, who pioneered wave-force calculations in ship hydrodynamics. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A rectangular barge of length $L = 80 \text{ m}$, breadth $B = 12 \text{ m}$, and draft $T = 3 \text{ m}$ floats in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The center of gravity is at $KG = 2.5 \text{ m}$ above the keel. #### (a) Calculate the metacentric height $GM$ of the barge. (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute the displaced volume.** $$\nabla = L \times B \times T = 80 \times 12 \times 3 = 2880 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute $KB$.** For a rectangular barge, the center of buoyancy is at half the draft: $$KB = \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the second moment of waterplane area $I_T$.** For a rectangular waterplane: $$I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{80 \times 12^3}{12} = 80 \times 144 = 11520 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4 — Compute $BM$.** $$BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{11520}{2880} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $KM$.** $$KM = KB + BM = 1.5 + 4.0 = 5.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Compute $GM$.** $$GM = KM - KG = 5.5 - 2.5 = 3.0 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > The metacentric height $GM = 3.0 \text{ m}$ is positive, confirming that the barge is stable at small heel angles. #### (b) If the barge heels by an angle of $\phi = 8°$, determine the righting lever $GZ$ and the righting moment. (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Convert angle to radians.** $$\phi = 8° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 0.1396 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the righting lever.** $$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi = 3.0 \times \sin(8°) = 3.0 \times 0.1392 = 0.4175 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the weight (displacement).** $$W = \rho g \nabla = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2880 = 28,963,920 \text{ N} \approx 28.96 \text{ MN}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the righting moment.** $$\text{RM} = W \cdot GZ = 28,963,920 \times 0.4175 = 12,092,463 \text{ N·m} \approx 12.09 \text{ MN·m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Summary:** $GZ \approx 0.42 \text{ m}$, Righting Moment $\approx 12.09 \text{ MN·m}$. The ship will experience a strong restoring force tending to return it to the upright position. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A ship of mass $m = 25,000$ tonnes operates in head waves of amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and wave period $T_w = 8 \text{ s}$. The added mass coefficient in heave is $\mu_{33} = 0.3m$, the damping coefficient is $\lambda_{33} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Ns/m}$, and the hydrostatic restoring coefficient is $C_{33} = 4.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$. The Froude-Krylov + Diffraction heave exciting force amplitude is $F_a = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$. #### (a) Calculate the natural heave period and the steady-state heave amplitude. (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute the wave frequency.** $$\omega_e = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_w} = \dfrac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the total heave mass (ship + added mass).** $$m_{total} = m + \mu_{33} = 25,000,000 + 0.3 \times 25,000,000 = 32,500,000 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the natural heave frequency.** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{33}}{m_{total}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.0 \times 10^6}{32.5 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{0.1231} = 0.3509 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the natural period.** $$T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.3509} = 17.91 \text{ s}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the frequency ratio.** $$r = \dfrac{\omega_e}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{0.7854}{0.3509} = 2.238$$ **Step 6 — Compute the steady-state heave amplitude.** $$z_a = \dfrac{F_a}{\sqrt{\left[C_{33} - m_{total}\omega_e^2\right]^2 + \left[\lambda_{33}\omega_e\right]^2}}$$ **Step 6a — Bracket 1 (stiffness − inertia term).** $$C_{33} - m_{total}\omega_e^2 = 4.0 \times 10^6 - 32.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.7854)^2$$ $$= 4.0 \times 10^6 - 32.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.6169$$ $$= 4.0 \times 10^6 - 20.05 \times 10^6 = -16.05 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$ **Step 6b — Bracket 2 (damping term).** $$\lambda_{33}\omega_e = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7854 = 1.178 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$ **Step 6c — Magnitude of denominator.** $$\sqrt{(-16.05)^2 + (1.178)^2} \times 10^6 = \sqrt{257.6 + 1.388} \times 10^6$$ $$= \sqrt{258.99} \times 10^6 = 16.094 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$ **Step 6d — Final heave amplitude.** $$z_a = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{16.094 \times 10^6} = 0.1554 \text{ m} \approx 0.155 \text{ m}$$ #### (b) Comment on the seakeeping behavior and discuss the role of radiation forces. (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Comment on heave amplitude.** The heave amplitude $z_a = 0.155 \text{ m}$ is much smaller than the wave amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.5 \text{ m}$. The **heave Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** is: $$\text{RAO}_{heave} = \dfrac{z_a}{\zeta_a} = \dfrac{0.155}{1.5} = 0.103$$ This is well below unity, indicating the ship is operating in the **low-frequency (mass-controlled) regime** since the wave frequency is much higher than the natural heave frequency. **Step 2 — Role of Froude-Krylov force.** The Froude-Krylov force dominates at this frequency because the wavelength is much greater than the ship length. The pressure field of the incident wave acts on a near-vertical underwater hull surface, producing a net oscillating heave force. **Step 3 — Role of Diffraction force.** The diffraction force is smaller in this case because the ship is small relative to the wavelength ($\lambda \gg L$). Diffraction effects are more important for large ships in steep, short waves. **Step 4 — Role of Radiation forces.** The radiation force consists of: - **Added mass force:** $\mu_{33} \ddot{z}$ — represents the inertia of the water that must be accelerated along with the ship. - **Damping force:** $\lambda_{33} \dot{z}$ — represents energy radiated away from the ship as it heaves. Without this damping, the heave amplitude would grow unboundedly at resonance. **Step 5 — Overall assessment.** The ship exhibits **good seakeeping** in this wave condition due to the large separation between wave and natural frequencies. The radiation damping $\lambda_{33} \omega_e = 1.178 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$ is small compared to the inertial term, indicating the ship is far from resonance. Designers can further reduce heave motions by: - Increasing the waterplane area (larger $C_{33}$) - Adding bilge keels (increase $\lambda_{33}$) - Using anti-roll tanks or fin stabilizers > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to add the **added mass** $\mu_{33}$ to the ship mass $m$ when computing the natural frequency. This leads to a wrong $\omega_n$ and wrong RAO. > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $GM$ (metacentric height) with $BM$ (metacentric radius). Remember: $BM$ is computed from the waterplane geometry, $KG$ comes from weight distribution, and $GM = KM - KG$. > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Using the wrong sign in the righting lever. For a heeled stable ship, $GZ$ is **positive** when measured from $G$ to the buoyancy line, and the righting moment is **restoring** (i.e., opposing the heel). > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In wave problems, students often compute only the Froude-Krylov force and forget the **diffraction** and **radiation** components. The total exciting force is the sum of FK + Diffraction. > 5. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Always convert angles from degrees to radians before using $\sin\phi$ or $\cos\phi$ in formulas, and ensure mass is in kg (not tonnes) when using SI units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Static Condition – Key Points - A floating ship in static equilibrium requires **Weight = Buoyancy** ($W = B = \rho g \nabla$) and collinear lines of action. - The **Center of Buoyancy ($B$)** shifts as the ship heels; the new vertical through $B$ intersects the original centerline at the **Metacenter ($M$)**. - **Metacentric Radius:** $BM = I_T / \nabla$ (valid for small angles and vertical sides near the waterline). - **Metacentric Height:** $GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG$. - **Stability Criterion:** Ship is **stable** if $GM > 0$; **unstable** (capsizes) if $GM < 0$. - **Righting Lever at small angle:** $GZ = GM \sin\phi$. - **Righting Moment:** $\text{RM} = W \cdot GZ \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \phi$. - For large angles, $M$ is **not fixed** — the locus of $M$ is the **metacentric evolute**, and $GZ$ must be computed from the $GZ$ curve (using Bonjean curves and Simpson's rule). - IMO intact stability criteria require checks for area under the $GZ$ curve up to 40°, 30°, 40°, etc. ### Waves Condition – Key Points - A ship in waves experiences **Froude-Krylov**, **Diffraction**, **Radiation**, and **Wave Drift** forces. - **Froude-Krylov force** = pressure of incident wave on the wetted hull surface. - **Diffraction force** = force due to wave scattering by the hull (dominant for large ships in short waves). - **Radiation force** = force from ship's own oscillations (added mass + damping components). - **Wave drift force** = second-order mean force that increases resistance in waves ($R_{AW}$). - The **equation of motion** in heave: $(m + \mu_{33})\ddot{z} + \lambda_{33}\dot{z} + C_{33}z = F_{exc}(t)$. - **RAO (Response Amplitude Operator)** = motion amplitude / wave amplitude. RAO peaks near resonance. - The **natural heave period** $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{(m + \mu_{33})/C_{33}}$ should differ from the wave period to avoid excessive motions. - **Parametric roll** can occur in head/following seas when the wave period is approximately **twice** the natural roll period. - Designers use **strip theory** (Salvesen, Tuck, Newman) and **3D panel methods** (WAMIT, HydroStar) to predict wave-induced loads. ### Quick-Reference Stability Numbers - **Merchant ships:** Typical $GM = 0.15$ to $1.5$ m. - **Naval combatants:** Typical $GM = 0.5$ to $2.0$ m. - **Passenger ships:** Typical $GM = 0.5$ to $1.5$ m. - **Sailing yachts:** Typical $GM = 0.5$ to $1.2$ m. ### Common Physical Values - Sea water density: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (used in 90% of KTU problems) - Fresh water density: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (for river/inland problems) - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard, used unless problem specifies otherwise)

topics.title: Heel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **HEEL (Transverse Inclination / Rolling Motion)**
> 
> In naval architecture and the study of floating bodies, **Heel** is defined as the *transverse inclination of a ship or floating vessel* caused by an off-center transverse force, moment, or shift of weight, resulting in a rotational motion about the **longitudinal axis (fore-and-aft axis)** of the vessel. The angular measure of this inclination is called the **Angle of Heel**, denoted by the Greek letter **φ (phi)** or **θ (theta)**.

In simpler engineering terms, heel is the **sideways tilt** of a floating body. While the *upright* (un-inclined) condition represents a ship floating with its centerline vertical, a heeled condition shows the ship tilted to one side — either to **port (left)** or **starboard (right)**.

| Term | Axis of Rotation | Direction | Standard Symbol |
|------|-----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Heel** | Longitudinal (X-axis) | Athwartships (sideways) | **φ** or **θ** |
| **Trim** | Transverse (Y-axis) | Fore-aft | **τ** |
| **List** | Longitudinal | Permanent transverse tilt | **φ** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Heel** = General, temporary transverse inclination due to any cause (wind, turning, cargo shift).
> - **List** = A *permanent* heel caused by an off-center weight (e.g., asymmetric loading, flooded compartment). List is typically *undesirable* and often asymmetrical to the designer's intent.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing in a small rowboat and you lean to one side to pick something up out of the water. The boat tilts sideways — your center of gravity shifts, and the boat rolls. That sideways tilt is exactly what naval architects call **heel**.

Now imagine the boat is much larger — a cargo ship. When cargo is loaded on only one side, or a strong wind blows across the deck, or the ship executes a sharp turn, the vessel **heels**. The angle it makes with the vertical is the angle of heel, and the **ability of the ship to resist** this tilt and return upright is governed by a quantity called the **metacentric height (GM)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a cork floating in water. Push the cork sideways — it tilts. Let go — it springs back. The *stiffness* of this restoring action is analogous to the ship's **GM (metacentric height)**. A tall GM = stiff ship (resists heeling strongly). A small GM = tender ship (heels easily, but slowly).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally accepted in ship stability analysis under the KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Angle of heel (φ):** measured in **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**
- **Displacement (W or Δ):** measured in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)** — represents the *weight of water displaced* by the floating body
- **Metacentric height (GM):** measured in **metres (m)**
- **Transverse shift of G (GG'):** measured in **metres (m)**
- **Righting Lever (GZ):** measured in **metres (m)** — the horizontal distance between the vertical line through G and the vertical line through B (center of buoyancy) when heeled
- **Acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²**

## 1.4 Primary Causes of Heel (Syllabus-Listed Triggers)

Under the KTU 2024 OENST611 syllabus (Module 2 — Heel), the following are the recognized *causes* that must be studied:

1. **Transverse shift of weight** (e.g., cargo moved across the deck, passengers moving to one side)
2. **Off-center vertical weights** (e.g., a crane lifting a load off-center)
3. **Wind pressure on the sail area** above the waterline
4. **Centrifugal force during turning** (heel due to course alteration)
5. **Asymmetric flooding** of a compartment
6. **Free surface effect** of loose liquids in a partially filled tank

Each of these will be examined in the deep-dive section that follows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Heel — Step-by-Step Theory

When a floating body heels, three distinct physical phenomena occur simultaneously. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate each one:

### Step 1 — Shift of the Center of Gravity (G)
- When a weight **w** is moved transversely by a distance **y** across the deck, the ship's center of gravity **G** shifts *parallel* to the direction of movement.
- Magnitude of shift: **GG' = (w × y) / W**, where **W** is the total displacement of the ship.
- The new center of gravity is denoted **G'**.

### Step 2 — Shift of the Center of Buoyancy (B)
- As the ship heels, the underwater hull shape (and thus the displaced water volume) becomes *asymmetric*.
- The center of buoyancy **B** therefore shifts *toward* the submerged side (the side that is now deeper in the water).
- The new buoyancy center is denoted **B'**.

### Step 3 — Metacenter Migration and Restoring Moment
- The **transverse metacenter M** is a (nearly) fixed point for small angles, located at height **KM = KB + BM** above the keel.
- **BM = I_T / V**, where **I_T** is the second moment of area of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline, and **V** is the displaced volume.
- The moment arm **GZ** (righting lever) is what produces the **righting moment** = **W × GZ**.
- For small angles, **GZ ≈ GM × sin(φ) ≈ GM × φ** (in radians).

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Heel Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE MASTER HEEL EQUATION (Most frequently tested KTU formula):**
> 
> $$\tan(\phi) = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$$
> 
> where:
> - **φ** = Angle of heel (degrees or radians)
> - **w** = Weight moved (tonnes)
> - **y** = Transverse distance moved (metres)
> - **W** = Total ship displacement (tonnes)
> - **GM** = Metacentric height (metres)

**Why this works (The Physics):**
The shift of G creates an *overturning moment* = **w × y**.
This is balanced by the *restoring moment* = **W × GM × tan(φ)**.
At equilibrium, the two are equal, giving the master equation.

**How it's used:**
- **Given a desired angle of heel φ**, solve for any of the other variables (e.g., the maximum permissible cargo shift).
- **Given a weight shift**, predict the resulting heel angle.
- For **small angles (< 10°)** where **tan(φ) ≈ sin(φ) ≈ φ** (in radians), the formula simplifies to:

$$\phi_{rad} = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot GM}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every KTU-relevant formula for the Heel topic. **Memorize this entire sheet** — it carries 70–80% of the marks in any Part B question on heel.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Master Heel Equation** | $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ | w [t], y [m], W [t], GM [m] | Finding heel angle or allowable weight shift |
| 2 | **Small-angle approximation** | $\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ | φ in radians | When φ < 10° |
| 3 | **Transverse shift of G** | $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{W}$ | GG', y [m]; w, W [t] | Locating new G after weight shift |
| 4 | **BM (Stiffness Formula)** | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | I_T [m⁴], ∇ [m³] | Computing metacenter location |
| 5 | **KM** | $\overline{KM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM}$ | All in metres | Vertical position of metacenter from keel |
| 6 | **GM** | \overline{GM} = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG} | All in metres | Metacentric height (stability criterion) |
| 7 | **Righting Lever (Small Angle)** | \overline{GZ} \approx \overline{GM} \cdot \sin(\phi) | GZ, GM [m] | For φ ≤ ~10° |
| 8 | **Righting Moment** | RM = W \cdot \overline{GZ} | W [t], GZ [m], RM [t·m] | Stability check at given heel |
| 9 | **Overturning Moment (Weight Shift)** | OM = w \cdot y | w [t], y [m] | The disturbing moment |
| 10 | **Heel due to Wind (Pressure)** | \tan(\phi) = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot h}{W \cdot \overline{GM}} | P [kPa], A [m²], h [m] | When wind pressure is the cause |
| 11 | **Heel due to Turning** | \tan(\phi) = \dfrac{v^{2} \cdot \overline{OG}}{g \cdot R \cdot \overline{GM}} | v [m/s], R [m], OG [m] | Steady turning heel |
| 12 | **Free Surface Effect (FSE)** | \Delta \overline{GM}_{FSE} = \dfrac{i \cdot \rho}{W} | i [m⁴], ρ [t/m³] | Loss of GM due to slack tanks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit consistency is critical.** If **W** is in tonnes and **w** in tonnes, **y** in metres, and **GM** in metres, the resulting **tan(φ)** is dimensionless — exactly as required. Do **not** mix kg and tonnes!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The heel analysis is not just an academic exercise. It is the cornerstone of:

- **Passenger Ship Safety:** A cruise ship with 4,000 passengers shifting to one side (e.g., during a photo opportunity) must not exceed a safe heel angle (~5°) to avoid panic and to keep walkways usable.
- **Cargo Operations:** Crane operators on container ships rely on heel equations to predict the maximum outreach of a crane lift before the ship lists dangerously.
- **Warship Survivability:** Damage-control officers must quickly compute the heel angle after a compartment is flooded to decide counter-flooding strategies.
- **Submarines:** Heel (or "banking") during sharp turns is actively controlled to ensure periscope stability.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a KTU problem mentions *GM* explicitly, the master heel equation **must** be used. If *GM* is not given, look for clues to compute it via **KM = KB + BM**, where **BM = I_T / V**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Heel Equation: tan(φ) = w·y / (W·GM)

This is the most-tested derivation in Module 2. The KTU board expects a *clean, labelled* derivation with a free-body sketch in the answer sheet.

### Setup (Refer to FIG-1 in Section 4)

Consider a floating body in equilibrium with:
- Original center of gravity at **G**
- Original center of buoyancy at **B**
- Total displacement **W** (vertical line through B supports the buoyancy force)
- A weight **w** originally located at the centerline is moved transversely by a horizontal distance **y** to a new position.

### Step 1 — The Weight Shift and the Overturning Moment

The weight **w**, by being moved a distance **y**, creates an **overturning moment (OM)** about the original vertical axis through B:

$$OM = w \cdot y$$

**Reasoning:** A moment is force × perpendicular distance. The "force" is the weight **w** (acting downward due to gravity), and the "lever arm" is the transverse shift **y**.

### Step 2 — The New Equilibrium Position

To maintain equilibrium, the ship must heel through an angle **φ** such that the new center of buoyancy **B'** (and the new center of gravity **G'**) generate a moment that exactly balances the overturning moment.

The shift of G from the original position is:

$$\overline{GG'} = \frac{w \cdot y}{W}$$

**Reasoning:** By the principle of moments (similar to Varignon's theorem for parallel force systems), the total moment of all weights about any vertical axis must be conserved. Originally the total moment was zero (about the centerline); after shifting **w** by **y**, G must shift by an amount such that:

$$W \cdot \overline{GG'} = w \cdot y$$

### Step 3 — Geometry of the Heeled Condition

In the heeled condition, draw the line **GM** vertical (perpendicular to the new waterline, *not* to the original). For small angles, this vertical line is approximately aligned with the original centerline.

The point **G'** is now offset horizontally from **M** by the perpendicular distance **GM · sin(φ)**. The righting moment generated is:

$$RM = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

**Reasoning:** The weight **W** acts downward through **G'**, while the buoyancy **W** acts upward through **B'** (which lies on the vertical through **M** for small angles). The horizontal separation between these two vertical lines is **GM · sin(φ)**, giving a couple of moment **W × GM × sin(φ)**.

### Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, **Overturning Moment = Restoring Moment**:

$$w \cdot y = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

For **small angles of heel** (φ < 10°), we use the small-angle approximation **sin(φ) ≈ tan(φ)**:

$$w \cdot y = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \tan(\phi)$$

Rearranging for the heel angle:

$$\boxed{\tan(\phi) = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

**Reasoning for the approximation:** Both **sin(φ)** and **tan(φ)** are equal to **φ** (in radians) to first order, so they are interchangeable for small angles. The error introduced at φ = 10° is only about 0.5%, which is well within engineering tolerance.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **PROBLEM:** A ship of displacement **W = 10,000 tonnes** has a metacentric height **GM = 0.80 m**. A weight of **w = 50 tonnes** is moved transversely by **y = 8 m**. Calculate:
> (a) The shift of the center of gravity GG'
> (b) The angle of heel φ (in degrees)

### Part (a) — Shift of G

$$\overline{GG'} = \frac{w \cdot y}{W} = \frac{50 \times 8}{10{,}000}$$

$$\overline{GG'} = \frac{400}{10{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation:** The center of gravity moved only **4 cm** sideways — a small absolute shift, but the resulting *moment arm* against GM produces a meaningful heel.

### Part (b) — Angle of Heel

Apply the master heel equation:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}} = \frac{50 \times 8}{10{,}000 \times 0.80}$$

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{400}{8{,}000} = 0.0500$$

Now solve for **φ**:

$$\phi = \arctan(0.0500) = 2.862^{\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Small-Angle Check):**
> Using the small-angle approximation directly:
> 
> $$\phi_{rad} \approx 0.0500 \text{ rad} = 0.0500 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 2.864^{\circ}$$
> 
> Difference from arctan result: **0.002°** — negligible. This confirms the small-angle approximation is valid for φ < 10°.

## 3.3 Derivation of Heel due to Wind Pressure

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **DERIVATION:** A ship of displacement **W** is subject to a wind force **F = P × A** acting horizontally at a height **h** above the waterline, where **P** is the wind pressure (kN/m²) and **A** is the projected sail area (m²). Derive the heel angle.

The wind exerts a horizontal force **F = P · A** at a height **h** above the waterline. This force is equivalent to:
- A horizontal force **F** acting at the waterline (level of B)
- An **overturning moment** = **F · h** = **P · A · h**

The overturning moment is balanced by the righting moment **W · GM · tan(φ)**:

$$P \cdot A \cdot h = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \tan(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{\tan(\phi) = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot h}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

**Reasoning:** The wind's moment about the center of buoyancy is **F × h**, and the ship's restoring moment is unchanged from the weight-shift case.

## 3.4 Derivation of Heel due to Turning (Steady-State Heel)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **DERIVATION:** A ship turns in a circle of radius **R** at speed **v**. The center of gravity **G** lies at a height **OG** above the center of buoyancy **B**. Derive the steady-state heel angle.

In steady turning, the ship experiences a **centrifugal force** **F_c = (W/g) · (v²/R)** acting horizontally outward through **G**.

The centrifugal force acts at height **OG** above **B**, creating an overturning moment:

$$OM = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{R} \cdot \overline{OG}$$

This is balanced by the righting moment **W · GM · tan(φ)**:

$$\frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{R} \cdot \overline{OG} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \tan(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{\tan(\phi) = \frac{v^{2} \cdot \overline{OG}}{g \cdot R \cdot \overline{GM}}}$$

**Reasoning:** The centrifugal force is **mass × centripetal acceleration** = **(W/g) × (v²/R)**, applied at G, which is **OG** above B. Note the **inverted heel direction** — the ship heels *inward* (to the inside of the turn), which is the opposite of intuition for a casual observer.

## 3.5 Free Surface Effect (FSE) — Loss of GM

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **DERIVATION:** A tank of surface moment of inertia **i** (m⁴) is partially filled with liquid of density **ρ** (t/m³). Derive the reduction in GM.

When a tank is partially full, the liquid surface is free to slope. As the ship heels, the liquid shifts, effectively raising G by a small amount. The virtual rise in G is:

$$\Delta \overline{GM}_{FSE} = \frac{i \cdot \rho}{W}$$

The corrected (effective) metacentric height becomes:

$$\overline{GM}_{eff} = \overline{GM} - \frac{i \cdot \rho}{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to *subtract* the FSE. Always compute **GM_effective** before applying the master heel equation if any partially-filled tank exists.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 valuation scheme, a *neat, labelled diagram* carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself in any Part B question on Heel. The Mermaid block diagrams below represent the *force-and-moment architecture* of a heeled ship — these correspond to the standard free-body sketches students must draw in their answer booklets.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Force-Moment Architecture of a Heeled Ship

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Upright Position] --> B[Weight w Shifted Transversely by y]
    B --> C[Overturning Moment OM = w times y]
    C --> D[Ship Heels Through Angle phi]
    D --> E[Center of Buoyancy Shifts B to B prime]
    D --> F[Center of Gravity Shifts G to G prime]
    D --> G[Metacenter M Remains Approximately Fixed]
    F --> H[New Lever Arm G prime M sin phi]
    H --> I[Restoring Moment RM = W times G prime M sin phi]
    I --> J{OM equals RM}
    J -->|Yes| K[Equilibrium at heel angle phi]
    J -->|No| L[Ship Continues to Heel]
    L --> M[Angle of Loll or Capsize if GM is negative]
    K --> N[Tan phi equals w times y divided by W times GM]
<!-- /mermaid -->

## 4.2 Schematic: Transverse Section Showing Heel Geometry

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    K1[Keel Point K] --> M1[Metacenter M at height KM]
    B1[Center of Buoyancy B] --> M1
    G1[Center of Gravity G] --> M2[Shifted G prime]
    W1[Weight W acts down through G prime] --> O1[Overturning Couple]
    BF1[Buoyancy W acts up through B prime] --> O1
    O1 --> R1[Restoring Moment RM]
    G1 -.-> GM1[GM = KM minus KG]
    M2 -.-> H1[Heel Angle phi between original vertical and new vertical through B prime]
<!-- /mermaid -->

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Causes → Effects Matrix of Heel

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    C1[Cause 1 Weight Shift w] --> E1[Effect Heel Angle phi1]
    C2[Cause 2 Wind Pressure P] --> E2[Effect Heel Angle phi2]
    C3[Cause 3 Centrifugal Force in Turn] --> E3[Effect Heel Angle phi3]
    C4[Cause 4 Asymmetric Flooding] --> E4[Effect Permanent List]
    C5[Cause 5 Free Surface Effect] --> E5[Effect Reduced GM then Increased Heel]
    E1 --> S1[Total Heel = Algebraic Sum of all phi contributions]
    E2 --> S1
    E3 --> S1
    E4 --> S1
    E5 --> S1
    S1 --> F1[Final Equilibrium Check phi within safe limits]
<!-- /mermaid -->

## 4.4 Schematic: Stability States as a Function of Heel Angle

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A0[phi = 0 Upright] --> A1[phi less than 10 Small angle]
    A1 --> A2[phi between 10 and 30 Moderate heel]
    A2 --> A3[phi between 30 and 60 Large heel]
    A3 --> A4[phi greater than 60 Approaching deck edge immersion]
    A4 --> A5[phi approx 90 Extreme heel or capsize]
    A0 -.GZ equals 0.-> A1
    A1 -.GZ rises linearly with phi.-> A2
    A2 -.GZ reaches maximum then may decrease.-> A3
    A3 -.GZ decreases as phi approaches angle of vanishing stability.-> A4

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> 
> **Define the term "Angle of Heel" in a floating body. Differentiate between "Heel" and "List" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Angle of Heel (1 mark):** The angle of heel is the angle made by a floating body's vertical axis (or centerline) with the true vertical when the body is inclined transversely about its longitudinal axis.

**Heel vs. List (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Heel | List |
|--------|------|------|
| **Definition** | General transverse inclination due to any cause (wind, turn, weight shift) | *Permanent* transverse tilt due to off-center weight or flooding |
| **Duration** | Temporary (returns to upright when force removed) | Persistent (does not self-correct) |
| **Example** | A ship heeling 5° in a 20-knot turn | A ship listed 2° to port after one side bunker tank is fully consumed |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> 
> **State the formula for the angle of heel due to a transverse shift of weight. Mention the condition under which the small-angle approximation is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$$

where **w** = weight shifted, **y** = transverse distance, **W** = ship displacement, **GM** = metacentric height.

**Condition for small-angle approximation (1 mark):** The small-angle approximation (**sin φ ≈ tan φ ≈ φ** in radians) is valid when the angle of heel **φ ≤ 10°**, giving an error of less than 1%.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Weight-Shift Heel Problem (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> 
> A box-shaped vessel of length **L = 80 m**, breadth **B = 12 m**, and draught **d = 4 m** floats in seawater (density **ρ = 1.025 t/m³**). The center of gravity **G** is located at a height of **3.0 m** above the keel, and the center of buoyancy **B** is at **2.0 m** above the keel. A cargo of mass **100 tonnes** originally stowed on the centerline is moved transversely by **5 m** to one side.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the metacentric height **GM** of the vessel in its original condition. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Determine the resulting **angle of heel** in degrees. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): GM Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Volume of Displacement (∇):**

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot d = 80 \times 12 \times 4 = 3{,}840 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Displacement (W):**

$$W = \nabla \cdot \rho = 3{,}840 \times 1.025 = 3{,}936 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Second Moment of Waterplane Area (I_T):**

For a rectangular waterplane, the transverse second moment of area about the longitudinal centerline is:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times 12^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times 1{,}728}{12}$$

$$I_T = 11{,}520 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4 — BM (Height of Metacenter above B):**

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{11{,}520}{3{,}840} = 3.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — KM (Height of Metacenter above Keel):**

$$\overline{KM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — GM (Metacentric Height):**

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG} = 5.0 - 3.0 = \boxed{2.0 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Angle of Heel (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the Master Heel Equation:**

Given: **w = 100 t**, **y = 5 m**, **W = 3,936 t**, **GM = 2.0 m**

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}} = \frac{100 \times 5}{3{,}936 \times 2.0}$$

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{500}{7{,}872} = 0.06352$$

**Step 2 — Solve for φ:**

$$\phi = \arctan(0.06352) = 3.635^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Small-Angle Assumption:**

Since **φ = 3.635° < 10°**, the small-angle approximation is valid. The student may alternatively compute:

$$\phi_{rad} = 0.06352 \text{ rad} = 0.06352 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 3.640^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer:** **φ ≈ 3.64°**

---

### QUESTION B — Wind-Pressure Heel Problem (14 Marks)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**
> 
> A ship of displacement **5,000 tonnes** has a metacentric height **GM = 0.6 m**. The projected sail area above the waterline is **A = 200 m²**, and its centroid lies at a height **h = 6 m** above the waterline. A wind of pressure **P = 0.5 kN/m²** acts perpendicular to the sail area.
> 
> **(a)** Compute the **heeling moment** due to the wind and the resulting **angle of heel**. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** If the ship now executes a steady turn at speed **v = 10 m/s** in a circle of radius **R = 250 m**, with the center of gravity located at a height **OG = 1.0 m** above the center of buoyancy, calculate the **additional heel angle due to turning** and the **total heel angle**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Heel Due to Wind (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the Wind Force:**

$$F = P \cdot A = 0.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \times 200 \text{ m}^2 = 100 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Heeling (Overturning) Moment:**

$$OM = F \cdot h = 100 \times 6 = 600 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Heel Equation:**

For the heeling moment to be in consistent units, note that **W = 5,000 tonnes ≈ 5,000 × 9.81 = 49,050 kN** (weight, not mass). However, in the standard naval-architecture form, we use the master equation:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot h}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$$

(Here, **P·A·h** is the heeling moment in kN·m, **W** is the *weight* in kN, and **GM** in metres gives a dimensionless tan(φ).)

$$\tan(\phi_{wind}) = \frac{0.5 \times 200 \times 6}{49{,}050 \times 0.6} = \frac{600}{29{,}430} = 0.02039$$

$$\phi_{wind} = \arctan(0.02039) = 1.168^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer (a):** **Heeling moment = 600 kN·m**, **φ_wind ≈ 1.17°**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Heel Due to Turning (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Centrifugal Force:**

$$F_c = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{R} = \frac{49{,}050}{9.81} \times \frac{10^{2}}{250} = 5{,}000 \times 0.4 = 2{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Overturning Moment Due to Turning:**

$$OM_{turn} = F_c \cdot \overline{OG} = 2{,}000 \times 1.0 = 2{,}000 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Heel Angle from Turning:**

$$\tan(\phi_{turn}) = \frac{v^{2} \cdot \overline{OG}}{g \cdot R \cdot \overline{GM}} = \frac{10^{2} \times 1.0}{9.81 \times 250 \times 0.6} = \frac{100}{1{,}471.5} = 0.06796$$

$$\phi_{turn} = \arctan(0.06796) = 3.888^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Total Heel Angle (Algebraic Sum, assuming both heeling in the same direction):**

$$\phi_{total} = \phi_{wind} + \phi_{turn} = 1.168 + 3.888 = 5.06^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer (b):** **φ_turn ≈ 3.89°**, **φ_total ≈ 5.06°**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Sign Convention:** If the wind is from the *same* side as the centrifugal effect, the two heels add. If from the *opposite* side, they subtract. The KTU examiner expects students to *state the assumption* explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON HEEL QUESTIONS — 2024 SCHEME**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Master Equation:** Using **w** in kg while **W** is in tonnes gives a 1000× error. **Always convert to the same mass unit.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the FSE in the GM:** If a tank is mentioned as "partially full," compute **GM_eff = GM − (i·ρ/W)** *first*. This omission can cause a 30–50% error in φ.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Heel with Trim:** Heel is about the *longitudinal* axis (sideways tilt). Trim is about the *transverse* axis (fore-aft). Students writing "longitudinal trim" or "transverse heel" lose 1 mark instantly.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Radians to Degrees:** The master equation gives **tan(φ)**, not φ in degrees. **Always apply arctan()** to get degrees.
> 
> 5. **Missing the Diagram:** A free-body sketch (ship upright vs. heeled, with G, B, M, and forces labelled) is worth **2–3 marks** by itself in Part B. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Print this before entering the exam hall!**

- ☐ **Heel = transverse inclination** about the **longitudinal (fore-aft) axis**, angle **φ** in degrees/radians.
- ☐ **Master Equation:** $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ — memorize the form, units, and applicability range.
- ☐ **Three shifts to remember when a weight moves transversely:** G shifts by **w·y/W**; B shifts due to asymmetric immersion; M remains approximately fixed.
- ☐ **BM = I_T / ∇** — for a **rectangular waterplane**, $I_T = L \cdot B^{3} / 12$.
- ☐ **KM = KB + BM** and **GM = KM − KG** — these are the three vertical distances from the keel.
- ☐ **Small-angle rule:** φ < 10° ⇒ use **tan φ ≈ sin φ ≈ φ (rad)**.
- ☐ **Free Surface Effect reduces GM** by **i·ρ/W** — subtract this *before* applying the heel equation if any tank is slack.
- ☐ **Wind heel:** $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot h}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ — pressure, area, height above waterline.
- ☐ **Turning heel:** $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{v^{2} \cdot \overline{OG}}{g \cdot R \cdot \overline{GM}}$ — ship heels *inward* to the turn.
- ☐ **Heel ≠ List:** Heel is temporary; list is permanent. The equations apply to *heel* analysis.
- ☐ **Always include a labelled free-body diagram** showing G, B, M, GZ, and the heel angle φ.
- ☐ **Final answer must be in degrees** (not radians) unless explicitly asked otherwise.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** Conventionally, heel to starboard is **positive φ**, to port is **negative φ**.

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: HEEL**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — TRIM: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TRIM (T):** *Trim of a floating vessel is defined as the algebraic difference between the drafts at the perpendiculars, measured at the aft (T_A) and forward (T_F) stations along the ship's length.*
> $$\boxed{T = T_A - T_F}$$
> It is expressed in **meters (m)** or **centimeters (cm)**. Trim quantifies the *longitudinal inclination* of a vessel about a transverse axis passing through the **Longitudinal Centre of Floatation (LCF)**.

### 1.1.1 Sign Convention (KTU Board Examination Standard)

| Convention | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|------------|------------|------------------|
| **Trim by the Stern** | $T > 0$ | $T_A > T_F$ (Stern deeper, Bow lifted) |
| **Even Keel** | $T = 0$ | $T_A = T_F$ (Vessel level) |
| **Trim by the Head/Bow** | $T < 0$ | $T_F > T_A$ (Bow deeper, Stern lifted) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trim Angle (θ):** The small angle (in radians) made by the ship's waterline with the designed waterline. For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$.
> $$\theta = \frac{T}{L_{BP}}$$
> where $L_{BP}$ is the **Length Between Perpendiculars** = **100 m** (typical reference unit).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The See-Saw on Water"

Imagine a **wooden plank floating on a calm pond**. If you stand at one end, that end sinks and the other rises. The plank tilts (trims) about a *pivot point* — for a uniform plank, this pivot is at the geometric centre.

A ship behaves identically, except the pivot point is the **Longitudinal Centre of Floatation (LCF)** — the centroid of the waterplane area, *not* the geometric centre (because ships have non-uniform cross-sections).

> 🎯 **Real-World Insight:** When a container ship loads heavy containers at the bow, it trims by the head. The captain counter-trims by pumping ballast water aft — a routine naval architecture operation governed entirely by the equations in these notes.

## 1.3 Why Trim Matters — Engineering Significance

- **Propulsion Efficiency:** Excessive trim increases resistance and bunker (fuel) consumption.
- **Safety:** Bow trim reduces visibility and may cause "squat" in shallow water.
- **Comfort:** Severe trim strains structural members and affects crew habitability.
- **Cargo Operations:** Trim must be controlled for proper crane reach and port clearance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY DESIGN METRIC:** **MCT (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm)** — measured in **tonne-metre per centimetre (t·m/cm)**. This is the *single most important numerical value* used in trim calculations for KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Setup — What Happens When Trim Changes?

When a ship experiences a longitudinal weight shift (e.g., cargo loaded forward), the equilibrium waterplane tilts through a small angle $\theta$ about the **LCF**. The vessel's Wetted Volume changes — and crucially, the **centre of buoyancy (B) shifts longitudinally** by an amount $\overline{GG'}$ where $G'$ is the new virtual centre of rotation.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Trim Phenomenon

1. **Initial State:** Ship floats at design waterline, even keel, displacing volume $\nabla$.
2. **Disturbance:** A longitudinal moment $\mathcal{M}$ is applied (cargo shift, ballast transfer).
3. **Restoring Action:** Buoyancy shifts longitudinally to create a counter-moment.
4. **Final State:** Ship trims by angle $\theta$; new waterline is inclined.
5. **Mathematical Statement:** Equilibrium requires $\mathcal{M} = \Delta \cdot \overline{GG_L} \cdot g$, where $\overline{GG_L}$ is the longitudinal shift of B.

---

## 2.2 The Longitudinal Metacentre ($M_L$) and $BM_L$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Longitudinal Metacentric Radius ($BM_L$):** This is the *longitudinal* analogue of $BM_T$.
> $$\boxed{BM_L = \frac{I_L}{\nabla}}$$
> where $I_L$ is the **Second Moment of Waterplane Area about the transverse axis through LCF**, and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume.

For a **rectangular waterplane approximation** (used extensively in KTU problems):
$$I_L = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12}$$
giving:
$$BM_L = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot \nabla}$$

### Longitudinal Metacentric Height
$$\boxed{GM_L = KB + BM_L - KG}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $GM_L$ is typically **10× to 20× larger** than $GM_T$ — that's why ships are vastly more stable longitudinally than transversely.

---

## 2.3 KTU FORMULA SHEET — TRIM (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Trim | $T = T_A - T_F$ | m or cm | Algebraic difference of drafts |
| 2 | Trim Angle | $\theta = T / L_{BP}$ | radians | Small-angle assumption |
| 3 | $BM_L$ (rectangular) | $I_L / \nabla = L_{BP}B^3 / (12\nabla)$ | m | Longitudinal metacentric radius |
| 4 | $GM_L$ | $KB + BM_L - KG$ | m | Longitudinal metacentric height |
| 5 | **MCT (Moment to Change Trim 1 cm)** | $\displaystyle MCT = \frac{\Delta \cdot GM_L \cdot 100}{L_{BP}}$ | **t·m/cm** | $\Delta$ in tonnes, lengths in m |
| 6 | Trim in cm | $\text{Trim (cm)} = \dfrac{\text{Trimming Moment (t·m)}}{\text{MCT (t·m/cm)}}$ | cm | Inverse of MCT |
| 7 | Draft Change Forward | $d_{TF} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{L_{BP}} \times \left(\dfrac{L_{BP}}{2} - x_{LCF}\right)$ | m | Where $x_{LCF}$ = LCF from midship (+ve aft) |
| 8 | Draft Change Aft | $d_{TA} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{L_{BP}} \times \left(\dfrac{L_{BP}}{2} + x_{LCF}\right)$ | m | Opposite sign of forward |
| 9 | New Draft Forward | $T_{F,\text{new}} = T_F + d_{TF}$ | m | Apply with correct sign |
| 10 | New Draft Aft | $T_{A,\text{new}} = T_A + d_{TA}$ | m | Apply with correct sign |
| 11 | Trim Moment (Weight Shift) | $\mathcal{M} = w \cdot x$ | t·m | $w$ = shifted mass, $x$ = LCG shift |
| 12 | $I_L$ (Ship-form approximation) | $\dfrac{L_{BP}^3 \cdot B}{12}$ for slender hull | m⁴ | Used when actual $I_L$ unavailable |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL PIPE-SYMBOL WARNING:** In the table above, note that all vertical bars (e.g., in absolute-value contexts) are written as `100` factors — never as `\|...\|`. This avoids LaTeX-table syntax breakage.
