Specific strength is defined as the ultimate tensile stress divided by the material's weight density (ρ·g). To eliminate gravity dependence (so the metric is consistent across Earth, Moon, and Mars), we use mass density ρ.

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Numerical Evaluation:**

Let us compare three common shipbuilding materials using the typical published values.

**For Mild Steel:**
- σ_u = 460 MPa = 460 × 10⁶ Pa
- ρ = 7850 kg/m³

$$\text{SS}_{steel} = \frac{460 \times 10^6}{7850} = 58{,}599 \ \text{N·m/kg}$$

**For Aluminum Alloy 5083-H116:**
- σ_u = 317 MPa = 317 × 10⁶ Pa
- ρ = 2660 kg/m³

$$\text{SS}_{Al} = \frac{317 \times 10^6}{2660} = 119{,}173 \ \text{N·m/kg}$$

**For FRP (E-Glass / Polyester, typical):**
- σ_u = 250 MPa = 250 × 10⁶ Pa
- ρ = 1600 kg/m³

$$\text{SS}_{FRP} = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{1600} = 156{,}250 \ \text{N·m/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result:**

Although steel has the highest absolute tensile strength, **FRP has the highest specific strength**, followed by aluminum and then steel. This is why FRP and aluminum are chosen for **high-speed, lightweight vessels**, while steel dominates the **large commercial fleet** due to its lower cost and superior weldability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Factor of Safety and Allowable Working Stress

**Step 1 — Definition of Factor of Safety (FoS):**

The factor of safety is a dimensionless number that represents the structural reserve capacity of a material beyond the expected working load.

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrangement for Working Stress:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\text{FoS}}$$

**Step 3 — Standard KTU Numerical Problem:**

A ship hull plate is made of Grade A mild steel with yield strength **σ_y = 235 MPa**. The expected maximum stress under combined wave and cargo loading is **σ_max = 110 MPa**. The classification society requires a minimum FoS of **2.0** for hull plating. Verify whether the design is safe.

**Step 4 — Compute Actual FoS:**

$$\text{FoS}_{actual} = \frac{235 \ \text{MPa}}{110 \ \text{MPa}} = 2.136$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Required FoS:**

Since **FoS_actual = 2.136 > FoS_required = 2.0**, the design is **safe and acceptable** under the classification rules.

**Step 6 — Compute Allowable Working Stress:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{235}{2.0} = 117.5 \ \text{MPa}$$

Since the actual working stress (110 MPa) is less than the allowable stress (117.5 MPa), the plate design is approved.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Buckling Stress for a Ship Hull Plate Panel

Hull plates are thin rectangular panels supported by stiffeners. Under in-plane compression, they may fail by **elastic buckling** before yielding.

**Step 1 — Plate Geometry:**

Consider a rectangular plate of length **a** (long span), breadth **b** (short span), and thickness **t**, simply supported on all four edges and loaded by a uniform in-plane compressive stress **σ_x**.

**Step 2 — Classical Plate Buckling Equation (Bryan, 1891):**

The critical elastic buckling stress for an ideal simply-supported plate is:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2$$

where:
- **k** = buckling coefficient (4.0 for long plate under uniform compression with simply supported edges)
- **E** = Young's modulus
- **ν** = Poisson's ratio
- **t** = plate thickness
- **b** = plate breadth (perpendicular to load)

**Step 3 — Substituting Values for a Typical Steel Plate:**

Let E = 210 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.3, t = 0.012 m (12 mm), b = 0.6 m, k = 4.0.

**Step 4 — Compute Poisson Term:**

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.3)^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

**Step 5 — Compute (t/b)²:**

$$\left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.012}{0.6} \right)^2 = (0.02)^2 = 0.0004$$

**Step 6 — Substitute All Values:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot (210 \times 10^9)}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot 0.0004$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate Numerator:**

$$4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 210 \times 10^9 = 4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 210 \times 10^9 = 8.291 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate Denominator:**

$$12 \cdot 0.91 = 10.92$$

**Step 9 — Final Result:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{8.291 \times 10^{12}}{10.92} \cdot 0.0004 = 7.593 \times 10^{11} \cdot 0.0004 = 3.037 \times 10^8 \ \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 303.7 \ \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 10 — Comparison with Yield Strength:**

Since σ_cr = 303.7 MPa > σ_y = 235 MPa, **the plate will yield before it buckles** — so the **buckling check is automatically satisfied** for this mild steel plate. This is a common result in ship hull design: the actual plate is thicker than the theoretical minimum needed to prevent buckling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Property Comparison

| Property | Mild Steel (Grade A) | High Tensile Steel (AH36) | Aluminum 5083 | FRP (E-Glass) |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 7850 | 2660 | 1600 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 235 | 355 | 215 | 180 (laminate) |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 400–520 | 490–620 | 317 | 250 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 210 | 210 | 70 | 15–25 |
| Elongation (%) | 22 | 21 | 12 | 2–3 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor (needs coating) | Poor (needs coating) | Excellent (oxide layer) | Excellent |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (preheat required) | Good (TIG/MIG) | Not weldable (adhesive bonded) |
| Relative Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium-High |
| Typical Ship Use | Hull plating (most ships) | Strength deck, hatch coamings | Superstructure, fast ferries | Fishing boats, minehunters |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Poor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Shipbuilding Materials (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS] --> B1[Ferrous Metals]
    A0 --> B2[Non Ferrous Metals]
    A0 --> B3[Non Metallic Composites]
    A0 --> B4[Advanced Hybrid Materials]

    B1 --> C1[Mild Steel Grade A B D E]
    B1 --> C2[High Tensile Steel AH DH EH 32 36 40]
    B1 --> C3[Stainless Steel 304 316 L]
    B1 --> C4[Corrosion Resistant Steel for LNG]

    B2 --> D1[Aluminum Alloys 5083 5086 5456 6082]
    B2 --> D2[Copper Nickel Alloys for Piping]
    B2 --> D3[Titanium for Submarines]
    B2 --> D4[Magnesium Alloys Rare Naval Use]

    B3 --> E1[GRP Glass Reinforced Plastic]
    B3 --> E2[FRP Fiber Reinforced Plastic]
    B3 --> E3[Sandwich Panels PVC Foam Core]
    B3 --> E4[Marine Grade Wood Teak Mahogany]
    B3 --> E5[Rubber and Elastomers for Bearings]

    B4 --> F1[CFRP Carbon Fiber Composites]
    B4 --> F2[Hybrid FRP with Nano Clay]
    B4 --> F3[Coated and Clad Steels]

    C1 --> G1[Hull Plating Bulkheads Small Vessels]
    C2 --> G2[Strength Deck Hatch Covers Side Shell]
    D1 --> H1[Superstructure Fast Ferries Patrol Boats]
    E1 --> H2[Fishing Boats Pleasure Craft Mine Hunters]
    F1 --> H3[Masts Hydrofoils High Speed Craft]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Material Selection Decision Flow for Ship Hull

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[START Design Problem] --> S1{Vessel Type}
    S1 --> S2[Large Commercial Ship]
    S1 --> S3[High Speed Naval Craft]
    S1 --> S4[Small Fishing Boat]

    S2 --> P1[Material Mild or HTS Steel]
    P1 --> Q1{Welding Required}
    Q1 --> R1[Yes Use Steel with CE 0.45 max]

    S3 --> P2[Material Aluminum or FRP]
    P2 --> Q2{Speed above 30 knots}
    Q2 --> R2[Yes Use Al 5083 or CFRP]
    Q2 --> R3[No Use FRP for cost saving]

    S4 --> P3[Material FRP or Wood]
    P3 --> Q3{Budget Limited}
    Q3 --> R4[Yes Use Wood or Single Skin FRP]
    Q3 --> R5[No Use Sandwich Panel FRP]

    R1 --> END1[Selected Mild Steel Grade A AH36]
    R2 --> END2[Selected Aluminum 5083 H116]
    R3 --> END3[Selected FRP E Glass Polyester]
    R4 --> END4[Selected Traditional Wood]
    R5 --> END5[Selected Sandwich Composite]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Properties Required from a Shipbuilding Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Required Properties of Hull Material] --> P1[Mechanical]
    P0 --> P2[Physical]
    P0 --> P3[Chemical]
    P0 --> P4[Manufacturing]

    P1 --> M1[Yield Strength]
    P1 --> M2[Tensile Strength]
    P1 --> M3[Impact Toughness]
    P1 --> M4[Fatigue Resistance]
    P1 --> M5[Ductility]

    P2 --> PH1[Density]
    P2 --> PH2[Modulus of Elasticity]
    P2 --> PH3[Thermal Expansion]

    P3 --> CH1[Corrosion Resistance]
    P3 --> CH2[Fire Resistance]

    P4 --> MA1[Weldability]
    P4 --> MA2[Formability]
    P4 --> MA3[Machinability]
    P4 --> MA4[Availability and Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The first diagram classifies the four broad families. The second shows how a naval architect makes a decision based on vessel type, speed, and budget. The third enumerates the **must-have properties** that any hull material must satisfy to pass classification society approval.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Yield Strength and Ultimate Tensile Strength of a material. How are they relevant to shipbuilding?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**Yield Strength (σ_y):** It is the stress at which a material begins to undergo permanent (plastic) deformation. For shipbuilding steel (Grade A), σ_y ≈ 235 MPa.

**Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u):** It is the maximum stress the material can withstand under tension before necking and fracture. For mild steel, σ_u ranges from **400–520 MPa**.

**Relevance to Shipbuilding:**
- The hull plating must not yield under maximum still-water and wave bending moments; hence σ_y governs the working stress limit.
- σ_u provides a safety margin against accidental overloading (collision, grounding).
- Classification societies prescribe minimum values of σ_y and σ_u to ensure hull integrity.

---

### **Q2. List any three advantages of using aluminum alloys in shipbuilding.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Low Density (≈ 2700 kg/m³):** Reduces hull weight, increases payload and speed. A 50% weight reduction is achievable compared to mild steel.
2. **Excellent Corrosion Resistance:** The natural Al₂O₃ oxide layer protects the hull from marine corrosion, reducing maintenance costs.
3. **High Specific Strength:** The strength-to-weight ratio is roughly twice that of mild steel, making it ideal for high-speed ferries and patrol boats.
4. *(Bonus)* Non-magnetic — useful for naval mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **"Explain in detail the different types of steel used in shipbuilding, with reference to their mechanical properties, grades, and applications."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification and Grading of Ship Steel**

**Model Answer:**

Steels used in shipbuilding are classified based on **yield strength** and **impact toughness**. The two broad categories are:

**(i) Mild Steel (MS) — Grades A, B, D, E**
- **Yield Strength:** 235 MPa (minimum)
- **Tensile Strength:** 400–520 MPa
- **Elongation:** ≥ 22% in 50 mm gauge length
- The letters A, B, D, E denote successively **lower impact test temperatures**: ambient, 0°C, −20°C, −40°C.
- **Applications:** Hull plating, bulkheads, decks, and small to medium commercial vessels.

**(ii) High Tensile Steel (HTS) — Grades AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36, AH40, DH40, EH40**
- The first letter (A, D, E) again denotes the test temperature.
- The number denotes minimum yield strength in the ksi-equivalent system (e.g., 32 → 315 MPa, 36 → 355 MPa, 40 → 390 MPa).
- **Applications:** Strength deck plating, side shell, hatch coamings, and bottom plating of large container ships and tankers.

**(iii) Corrosion-Resistant Steel (for LNG carriers)**
- Contains 9% Nickel (9%Ni steel) for cryogenic service down to −196°C.
- Used in **membrane-type LNG carrier cargo tanks**.

**Grading System (Per LR / ABS / IRS Rules):**
- **Class 1 — Grade A:** Ordinary strength, ambient test.
- **Class 2 — Grade AH/DH/EH 32:** Higher strength for general purpose.
- **Class 3 — Grade AH/DH/EH 36:** Higher strength for critical zones.
- **Class 4 — Grade AH/DH/EH 40:** For ice-strengthened hulls.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Mechanical Properties and Their Significance**

**Model Answer:**

The mechanical properties critical for ship steel are:

| Property | Typical Value (Grade A) | Significance |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Yield Strength | 235 MPa | Maximum allowable working stress limit |
| Tensile Strength | 400–520 MPa | Reserve capacity beyond yield |
| Elongation | ≥ 22% | Ductility for forming and welding |
| Charpy Impact Energy | 27 J at 0°C | Resistance to brittle fracture |
| Fatigue Strength | ~120 MPa at 10⁷ cycles | Long-life wave-induced loading |
| Carbon Equivalent (CE) | ≤ 0.45% | Weldability without preheating |

**Key Significance:**
1. The combination of **adequate strength and high ductility** makes steel easy to roll, cut, and weld into complex hull shapes.
2. The **Charpy impact test** ensures the material remains ductile at low service temperatures — crucial for ships operating in cold seas.
3. The **fatigue strength** governs the long-term integrity of the hull under millions of wave cycles over a 25-year service life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse the grading of HTS steel. Remember — **the number is yield strength (in ksi/10), not the test temperature.** The letter (A/D/E) is the test temperature. Mixing these up will cost 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **"Compare Mild Steel, Aluminum Alloys, and FRP composites as shipbuilding materials. Discuss the criteria for material selection in modern ship design."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis of Three Materials**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Mild Steel | Aluminum 5083-H116 | FRP (E-Glass/Polyester) |
|----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2660 | 1600 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 235 | 215 | 180 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 400–520 | 317 | 250 |
| Modulus E (GPa) | 210 | 70 | 20 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Not possible (adhesive bonded) |
| Cost (per kg) | Low | High (3–4× steel) | Medium |
| Repairability | Easy (weld) | Moderate (weld) | Difficult |
| Typical Use | Tankers, bulk carriers, containers | Fast ferries, patrol boats | Fishing boats, mine hunters |

**Observations:**
- **Mild Steel** is the **workhorse of shipbuilding** — strong, ductile, weldable, and economical.
- **Aluminum 5083** offers **superior strength-to-weight ratio**, ideal where weight reduction is critical (e.g., superstructure of cruise ships, hulls of fast ferries).
- **FRP** provides **best corrosion resistance and formability** into complex shapes, but is limited by lower stiffness and higher cost per kg.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Criteria for Material Selection**

**Model Answer:**

The following **seven criteria** govern material selection in modern ship design:

1. **Mechanical Performance:** Yield strength, tensile strength, fatigue life, and toughness must satisfy classification rules.
2. **Density / Weight:** Lower density → higher payload, better speed, lower fuel consumption. Critical for naval craft.
3. **Corrosion Resistance:** Marine environment demands either a corrosion-resistant alloy (Al, FRP) or a coating system (steel).
4. **Weldability / Manufacturability:** Steel dominates due to ease of welding. Aluminum requires special TIG/MIG procedures. FRP uses adhesive bonding.
5. **Cost:** Material cost + fabrication cost + lifecycle cost must fit the project budget.
6. **Mission Profile:** Operational speed, sea conditions, service temperature, and cargo type dictate the material.
7. **Regulatory Compliance:** The selected material must be approved by classification societies (ABS, LR, IRS, DNV).

**Selection Logic Example:**
- A **VLCC oil tanker** → **Mild Steel HTS (AH36)** — heavy, cheap, weldable.
- A **patrol boat (40 knots)** → **Aluminum 5083** — light, strong, fast.
- A **fishing trawler (50 ft)** → **FRP** — cheap, corrosion-free, easy to mold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write only the **advantages** of composites without mentioning the **drawbacks** (low stiffness, poor fire resistance, difficult repair). A **balanced comparison** is mandatory to score above 10/14. Always include both pros and cons.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Shipbuilding materials** are broadly classified into **ferrous (steel)**, **non-ferrous (Al, Ti)**, **composites (FRP, GRP)**, and **advanced (CFRP)**.
- **Mild Steel (Grade A, B, D, E)** has σ_y = **235 MPa** and is used for most commercial ship hulls.
- **High Tensile Steel (AH/DH/EH 32/36/40)** has σ_y between **315 and 390 MPa**; the **number is yield strength**, the **letter is test temperature**.
- **Aluminum 5083-H116** is the most common marine aluminum alloy due to its excellent weldability and corrosion resistance.
- **FRP (Fiber Reinforced Plastic)** offers the highest **specific strength** (~156 kN·m/kg) and is widely used for small craft and mine countermeasure vessels.
- **Weldability** is governed by **Carbon Equivalent (CE) ≤ 0.45%**; higher CE requires preheating.
- **Charpy Impact Test** is mandatory at **0°C (A)**, **−20°C (D)**, and **−40°C (E)** to ensure ductility at low temperatures.
- **Specific Strength** = σ_u / ρ; **Specific Stiffness** = E / ρ — these are the **two most important metrics** for lightweight design.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** for hull plating is typically **2.0** as per classification rules.
- **Plate buckling stress** σ_cr = k·π²·E / [12·(1−ν²)]·(t/b)² must be greater than the working compressive stress.
- **~90% of commercial ships** are built with **steel**, ~7% with **aluminum**, and ~3% with **FRP/wood/combined**.
- **Material selection** depends on **mechanical performance, density, corrosion, weldability, cost, mission, and regulatory compliance**.
- **LNG carriers** use **9% Nickel steel** for cryogenic cargo containment at −163°C.
- **Submarines** use **HY-80 / HY-100 steel** for deep-diving pressure hulls (yield strength up to 690 MPa).
- **Modern naval combatants** (DDG, LCS) increasingly use **aluminum superstructures and composite masts** to reduce top weight and radar signature.
- **Always remember the unit conversions:** 1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa = 1 N/mm².

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compositions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compositions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Naval Architecture and Floating Bodies (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3), the term **"Compositions"** refers to the systematic **method of determining the centroid (center of gravity, center of buoyancy, or center of area) of a complex composite body, cross-section, or volume by resolving it into a finite number of simple, regular, and easily computable geometric primitives** (such as rectangles, triangles, semicircles, trapeziums, circular segments, and elliptical quadrants).

The mathematical foundation rests upon the **Principle of Moments** (also known as the **Varignon's Theorem** for centroids), which states that *the algebraic sum of the moments of the component parts of a composite figure about any arbitrary reference axis is equal to the moment of the entire composite figure taken as a whole about the same reference axis.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Compositions form the **backbone calculation** for determining the **Longitudinal Center of Gravity (LCG)** and **Vertical Center of Gravity (VCG/VCB)** of a ship, which are indispensable inputs for the **Statical Stability**, **Trim**, and **Free Surface Effect** calculations required in higher naval architecture modules and the **MEO Class IV competency examinations** (Marine Engineering Officer competency, IMO aligned).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Heavy See-Saw (Teeter-Totter):**
> Suppose you have a wooden plank resting on a triangular pivot. If you place a heavy boy (say **40 kg**) at the **left end** and a lighter girl (say **20 kg**) at the **right end**, the see-saw will obviously tilt towards the boy. To balance it perfectly, you cannot simply place the girl midway; you must shift the **pivot point** towards the heavier side. 
> 
> Now imagine the boy and girl are **welded together** into a single, irregular shape. If someone asks you "Where is the balancing point of this combined, irregular mass?", the **"compositions" method** answers this by treating the boy and girl as two separate, simple masses whose individual balancing points are known, and then mathematically combines them. This balancing point is the **Centroid (Center of Gravity)**.
> 
> In ship design, instead of a boy and a girl, we deal with the **hull plating, engine, cargo tanks, ballast, fuel, and superstructure** — each treated as a simple geometric primitive to find the overall ship's CG.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard for naval architecture weight calculations).
- **Density of sea water (ρ_sw):** **1025 kg/m³** (used when compositions are computed for buoyancy-based sections).
- **Density of fresh water (ρ_fw):** **1000 kg/m³**.
- **Standard reference axes** in ship compositions:
  - **X-axis:** Longitudinal (forward-aft), through midships.
  - **Y-axis:** Transverse (port-starboard), through centerline.
  - **Z-axis:** Vertical (up-down), through the baseline.
- **Units to remember:** Area in **m²**, Volume in **m³**, Weight in **tonnes (t)** or **kg**, Moments in **tonne·m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logical Principle (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

The **"Compositions" method** is built upon four elementary logical steps. Understanding the *why* behind each step is critical for scoring in KTU derivations.

1. **Step 1 — Decomposition (Discretisation):**
   The complex composite body or cross-section is mentally subdivided into a **finite number (n)** of elementary geometric shapes — rectangles, triangles, semicircles, trapeziums, quadrants of circles, etc. Each elementary shape must have a **known, standard formula** for its area (or volume) and its centroidal position.

2. **Step 2 — Compute Individual Areas and Centroid Locations:**
   For each elementary shape *i*, we calculate:
   - Its area $A_i$ (or weight $W_i$, or volume $V_i$).
   - The coordinates of its individual centroid $(\bar{x}_i, \bar{y}_i)$ measured from a common reference axis.

3. **Step 3 — Apply the Principle of Moments (Varignon's Theorem):**
   The *first moment of area* of the entire composite figure about a reference axis equals the **algebraic sum** of the first moments of all component areas about the *same* axis. This is the **physical equilibrium condition** — if the composite body balances at its centroid, the sum of all clockwise moments must equal the sum of all counter-clockwise moments.

4. **Step 4 — Solve for the Composite Centroid:**
   Divide the total moment by the total area (or total weight) to obtain the centroid coordinates of the composite figure.

### 2.2 Why This Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> In real **shipyards and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS)**, ships are not designed with single continuous curves when calculating stability — they are modeled as a **discrete set of stations, sections, and offsets**. The compositions method allows naval architects to:
> - Locate the **LCG of a fully loaded vessel** for trim optimization.
> - Determine the **VCG of a damaged compartment** for floodable length calculations.
> - Compute the **center of buoyancy (B)** at any waterline by summing moments of submerged wedges.
> - Verify compliance with **SOLAS Chapter II-1** intact stability criteria.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the **canonical formulas** that KTU examiners expect verbatim. Memorize these — they appear in **nearly every Module 3 question paper**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Composite Centroid (x-axis) | $\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \cdot \bar{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}$ | m | $\bar{x}_i$ measured from common Y-axis |
| 2 | Composite Centroid (y-axis) | $\bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \cdot \bar{y}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}$ | m | $\bar{x}_i$ measured from common X-axis |
| 3 | Composite CG by Weight | $\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum W_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Used for LCG/VCG of ship |
| 4 | Composite CG by Mass | $\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum m_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | m | Equivalent to weight form |
| 5 | First Moment of Area | $Q \;=\; \sum A_i \bar{x}_i$ | m³ | Used in shear-stress calc |
| 6 | Total Area | $A_{total} \;=\; \sum A_i$ | m² | Algebraic sum (signed areas for cutouts) |
| 7 | Signed Area (for holes) | $A_i$ entered as **negative** | m² | Subtract cutouts |
| 8 | Centroid of Rectangle | $(\tfrac{b}{2},\,\tfrac{h}{2})$ from corner | m | Most-used primitive |
| 9 | Centroid of Right Triangle | $(\tfrac{b}{3},\,\tfrac{h}{3})$ from right-angle corner | m | Common in ship framing |
| 10 | Centroid of Semicircle | $(0,\;\tfrac{4r}{3\pi})$ from flat base | m | Used for tank cross-sections |
| 11 | Centroid of Circular Sector | $(\tfrac{2r\sin\alpha}{3\alpha},\;0)$ from apex | m | $\alpha$ in radians |
| 12 | Centroid of Quarter Circle | $(\tfrac{4r}{3\pi},\;\tfrac{4r}{3\pi})$ from corner | m | Curved ship frames |

### 2.4 Centroid Formulas of Standard Geometric Primitives (Critical for Solving Part B)

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** You will not be given a centroid table in the exam — these standard positions **must be memorized**.

- **Rectangle** of width $b$, height $h$: centroid at $(\tfrac{b}{2}, \tfrac{h}{2})$ from any corner.
- **Triangle** (any shape): centroid lies at the intersection of medians, i.e., at $\tfrac{1}{3}$ the distance from each side.
- **Semicircle** of radius $r$: centroid at $\tfrac{4r}{3\pi}$ from the diameter, along the axis of symmetry.
- **Quarter circle** of radius $r$: centroid at $\tfrac{4r}{3\pi}$ from each straight edge.
- **Trapezium** with parallel sides $a$ and $b$, height $h$: $\bar{y} = \dfrac{h(2a+b)}{3(a+b)}$ from the base of length $a$.
- **Circular segment** (height $h$, radius $r$): $\bar{x} = \dfrac{4r\sin^3(\theta/2)}{3(2\theta - \sin 2\theta)}$ where $2\theta$ is the angle subtended.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Composite Centroid Formula

We begin from the **rigorous definition of the centroid of an area**. The first moment of an elemental area $dA$ about the Y-axis is defined as $dM_y = x \cdot dA$.

**Step 1: Definition of the First Moment of the Entire Area.**

For a continuous area $A$, the total first moment about the Y-axis is:

$$
M_y \;=\; \int_A x \, dA
$$

**Step 2: Express the Total Moment as an Algebraic Sum Over Discretised Sub-Areas.**

We partition the total area $A$ into $n$ non-overlapping sub-areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$, each with its own centroid $(\bar{x}_i, \bar{y}_i)$:

$$
\int_A x \, dA \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{A_i} x \, dA \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{x}_i \cdot A_i
$$

In the second equality we used the fact that within each sub-region $A_i$ that is sufficiently simple, the centroid coordinate $\bar{x}_i$ is **constant** and can be factored out of the integral. This converts the continuous integral into a discrete sum.

**Step 3: Define the Centroid of the Composite Body.**

By definition, the centroid $\bar{X}$ of the entire composite body is the point where the *entire* first moment can be expressed as a single moment of the total area:

$$
\bar{X} \cdot A_{total} \;=\; \int_A x \, dA \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{x}_i A_i
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\bar{X}$ (the Master Composition Formula).**

Dividing both sides by $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$, we obtain the **canonical KTU composition formula**:

$$
\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, \bar{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion of the derivation:**
> The identical logic applies in the Y-direction, yielding $\bar{Y}$. When the figure has holes or cut-outs, the areas of those holes are entered as **negative** values. When the composite body consists of *weights* (such as engine mass, cargo mass, ballast mass) rather than areas, we simply substitute $A_i \rightarrow W_i$ or $A_i \rightarrow m_i$. This unification of geometry and weight is what makes the compositions method so powerful in ship design.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How to Tackle a Typical KTU Composition Problem

| Stage | Action | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|-------|--------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Draw a clean, labelled sketch with origin marked. | Forgetting to indicate the reference axis. |
| 2 | Identify and number all elementary shapes. | Treating overlapping shapes as separate pieces. |
| 3 | Compute each $A_i$ from given dimensions. | Confusing radii with diameters. |
| 4 | Locate each $(\bar{x}_i, \bar{y}_i)$ from the **common** origin. | Mixing reference origins between parts. |
| 5 | Tabulate $(i, A_i, \bar{x}_i, \bar{y}_i, A_i \bar{x}_i, A_i \bar{y}_i)$. | Sign errors for holes. |
| 6 | Sum the columns to get $\sum A_i$, $\sum A_i \bar{x}_i$, $\sum A_i \bar{y}_i$. | Forgetting to sum. |
| 7 | Compute $\bar{X} = \sum A_i \bar{x}_i / \sum A_i$ and $\bar{Y}$ similarly. | Dividing by wrong sum. |
| 8 | Verify the centroid lies *within* the composite body. | If outside, recheck signs. |

### 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Demonstration

> **Example Setup (Verifying the Formula with a Composite Section):**
> A ship-deck section consists of a **rectangle of 6 m × 2 m** with a **semicircular cut-out of radius 1 m** removed from the top edge (centered). Using the compositions method, locate the centroid $\bar{Y}$ measured from the base of the rectangle.

**Solution:**

We treat the figure as a *positive* rectangle and a *negative* semicircle.

$$
A_{rect} = 6 \times 2 = 12 \;\; \text{m}^2 \qquad \bar{y}_{rect} = \tfrac{2}{2} = 1.0 \;\; \text{m}
$$

$$
A_{semi} = \tfrac{\pi (1)^2}{2} = 1.5708 \;\; \text{m}^2 \qquad \bar{y}_{semi} = 2 + \tfrac{4(1)}{3\pi} = 2 + 0.4244 = 2.4244 \;\; \text{m}
$$

Applying the compositions formula (with $A_{semi}$ entered as negative because it is a cut-out):

$$
\bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{A_{rect}\,\bar{y}_{rect} \;-\; A_{semi}\,\bar{y}_{semi}}{A_{rect} \;-\; A_{semi}}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{(12)(1.0) \;-\; (1.5708)(2.4244)}{12 \;-\; 1.5708}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{12.000 \;-\; 3.8077}{10.4292} \;=\; \dfrac{8.1923}{10.4292} \;\approx\; 0.7856 \;\; \text{m}
$$

The centroid of the composite section lies at **0.786 m above the base**, which is consistent with intuition (the cut-out at the top pulls the centroid downward from 1.0 m toward the base).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Compositions Method

The diagram below maps the **logical flow** of solving any compositions problem — from decomposition to final centroid verification — using clean, alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs (no reserved keywords) and uppercase-only labels inside double quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT COMPOSITE FIGURE"] --> nodeB["STEP 1 CHOOSE REFERENCE ORIGIN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 2 DECOMPOSE INTO PRIMITIVES"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 3 CLASSIFY ADDED VS SUBTRACTED REGIONS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 4 COMPUTE INDIVIDUAL AREAS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 5 LOCATE CENTROIDS OF EACH PRIMITIVE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 6 CONSTRUCT MOMENT TABLE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 7 SUM COLUMNS SUM A AND SUM A X BAR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 8 APPLY COMPOSITION FORMULA X BAR DIVIDE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["STEP 9 REPEAT FOR Y DIRECTION"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["STEP 10 VERIFY CENTROID LIES WITHIN BOUNDARY"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["OUTPUT FINAL CENTROID COORDINATES"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Applications in Ship Stability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["SHIP HULL COMPOSITIONS"] --> nodeB1["LCG COMPUTATION"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeC1["VCG COMPUTATION"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeD1["TCG COMPUTATION"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeE1["TRIM CALCULATION"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeF1["GM AND STATICAL STABILITY"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeG1["LIST AND DAMAGE STABILITY"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeH1["LOAD LINE AND FREEBOARD"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeH1
    nodeG1 --> nodeH1
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of a Typical Composite Ship Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph baseLayer["BASELINE OF SHIP"]
        nodeBL["BL Z EQUALS ZERO"]
    end
    nodeBL --> nodeR1["RECTANGULAR HULL SECTION B H"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeT1["TRIANGICAL BILGE KEEL BASE"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeS1["SEMICIRCULAR TANK CROSS SECTION"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeCut1["CIRCULAR CUTOUT FOR PIPE TUNNEL R R"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the schematic:** In a real midship section, the **hull plating** is treated as a rectangle, the **bilge radius** as a quarter circle, the **sheer strake** as a triangle, and any **openings** (manholes, tunnels) as negative-area circles. Compositions of all these primitives yield the VCG of the hull section, which is then propagated to the **whole-ship loadline computation**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "centroid of an area." How is it distinguished from the "center of gravity"?**

**Model Answer:**
The **centroid of an area** is defined as the point where the *entire area* of a plane figure is considered to be concentrated such that the first moment of the area about any axis through that point is zero. Mathematically, it is the geometric center $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$ where $\bar{X} = \int x\, dA / \int dA$ and $\bar{Y} = \int y\, dA / \int dA$. It is a purely *geometric* property and depends only on the shape, not on the material.

The **center of gravity (CG)** of a body is the point where its *entire weight* is considered to act vertically downward due to the gravitational field. Mathematically, $\bar{X} = \int x\, dW / \int dW$, where $dW = g\,dm$. For a body of *uniform density and thickness*, the **centroid and CG coincide** in position; however, they are conceptually distinct entities.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. State the principle of moments (Varignon's Theorem) as applied to composite bodies, and write the composition formula for locating the center of gravity along the longitudinal axis.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Principle of Moments for composite bodies** (an extension of Varignon's Theorem) states that *the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual component masses (or weights or areas) of a composite body about any reference axis equals the moment of the whole composite body taken about the same reference axis.*

Mathematically, if a body of total weight $W$ is decomposed into $n$ components of weights $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_n$ with their individual CGs at distances $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ from a reference axis, then:

$$
W \cdot \bar{X} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot \bar{x}_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \bar{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}
$$

This equation is the **fundamental working formula** of the compositions method in naval architecture for determining the LCG of a vessel.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Adaptation]*

> A ship hull cross-section is constructed from the following primitives (all measurements taken from the **baseline at the keel**):
> - **(i)** A **rectangle** of width 8 m and height 4 m, with its base on the keel.
> - **(ii)** A **semicircle** of radius 2 m attached on top of the rectangle, with its flat side coincident with the top of the rectangle.
> - **(iii)** A **circular hole** of radius 0.5 m cut out from the rectangle, with its center located at 2 m above the keel and 4 m from the centerline.
>
> Using the compositions method, determine the coordinates $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$ of the centroid of this composite section. Take the origin at the **bottom-left corner of the rectangle**.

**(a) [7 Marks] Set up the decomposition, compute individual areas, and tabulate the centroidal data.**

**Model Solution:**

We choose the origin at the **bottom-left corner of the rectangle**. By symmetry of the rectangle and the semicircle about the vertical centerline of the section, the horizontal centroid $\bar{X}$ will be at the centerline ($4$ m). The circular hole, however, is offset, so we must compute carefully.

**Decomposition:**
1. **Part 1 — Rectangle:** width $b = 8$ m, height $h = 4$ m.
2. **Part 2 — Semicircle:** radius $r = 2$ m, sitting on top of the rectangle.
3. **Part 3 — Circular hole:** radius $r = 0.5$ m, centered at $(4,\, 2)$ m, treated as a *negative area*.

**Step 1: Areas:**

$$
A_1 \;=\; 8 \times 4 \;=\; 32 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \pi (2)^2 \;=\; 2\pi \;\approx\; 6.2832 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_3 \;=\; -\pi (0.5)^2 \;=\; -0.25\pi \;\approx\; -0.7854 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Individual centroids from the bottom-left corner of the rectangle:**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \tfrac{8}{2} = 4 \;\; \text{m}, \qquad \bar{y}_1 = \tfrac{4}{2} = 2 \;\; \text{m}
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = 4 \;\; \text{m} \;\; \text{(centered on rectangle)}, \qquad \bar{y}_2 = 4 + \tfrac{4(2)}{3\pi} = 4 + 0.8488 = 4.8488 \;\; \text{m}
$$

$$
\bar{x}_3 = 4 \;\; \text{m}, \qquad \bar{y}_3 = 2 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Tabulation:**

| Part | $A_i$ (m²) | $\bar{x}_i$ (m) | $\bar{y}_i$ (m) | $A_i \bar{x}_i$ | $A_i \bar{y}_i$ |
|------|------------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 — Rectangle | $+32.0000$ | $4$ | $2$ | $128.0000$ | $64.0000$ |
| 2 — Semicircle | $+6.2832$ | $4$ | $4.8488$ | $25.1328$ | $30.4655$ |
| 3 — Hole | $-0.7854$ | $4$ | $2$ | $-3.1416$ | $-1.5708$ |
| **Sum** | $\mathbf{37.4978}$ | — | — | $\mathbf{149.9912}$ | $\mathbf{92.8947}$ |

**(b) [7 Marks] Apply the composition formula and obtain the centroid coordinates with proper units.**

**Model Solution:**

**Total Area:**

$$
A_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{3} A_i \;=\; 32.0000 + 6.2832 - 0.7854 \;=\; 37.4978 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Total First Moments:**

$$
\sum A_i \bar{x}_i \;=\; 128.0000 + 25.1328 - 3.1416 \;=\; 149.9912 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
\sum A_i \bar{y}_i \;=\; 64.0000 + 30.4655 - 1.5708 \;=\; 92.8947 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Composite Centroid Coordinates:**

$$
\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum A_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum A_i} \;=\; \dfrac{149.9912}{37.4978} \;\approx\; 4.0000 \;\; \text{m}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum A_i \bar{y}_i}{\sum A_i} \;=\; \dfrac{92.8947}{37.4978} \;\approx\; 2.4775 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;(\bar{X},\,\bar{Y}) \;=\; (4.000\;\text{m},\;\; 2.478\;\text{m})\;}
$$

The centroid lies on the vertical centerline of symmetry at a height of **2.478 m above the keel baseline**, which is consistent with intuition (slightly above the rectangle's mid-height of 2 m due to the semicircular bulge on top).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]*

> A small craft has the following components contributing to its displacement. Use the compositions method to find the **Longitudinal Center of Gravity (LCG)** measured from the **stern (aft perpendicular, AP)**:
>
> | Component | Weight (tonnes) | LCG from AP (m) |
> |-----------|----------------|-----------------|
> | Hull (steel) | 80 | 6.0 |
> | Main engine | 25 | 2.5 |
> | Aux. machinery | 8 | 5.0 |
> | Fuel oil | 12 | 1.0 (forward of AP — hence negative) |
> | Fresh water | 5 | 8.0 |
> | Crew and effects | 2 | 7.0 |
>
> Also calculate the **VCG** of the same vessel if the vertical CGs (above keel) of each component are: Hull 1.5 m, Engine 1.2 m, Aux. 1.8 m, Fuel 0.5 m, Fresh water 1.0 m, Crew 3.5 m.

**(a) [7 Marks] Construct the moment table and compute the LCG.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Tabulation of Longitudinal Data (positive x = forward of AP):**

| Component | $W_i$ (t) | $\bar{x}_i$ (m) | $W_i \bar{x}_i$ (t·m) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Hull | $+80$ | $+6.0$ | $+480.0$ |
| Main engine | $+25$ | $+2.5$ | $+62.5$ |
| Aux. machinery | $+8$ | $+5.0$ | $+40.0$ |
| Fuel oil | $+12$ | $-1.0$ | $-12.0$ |
| Fresh water | $+5$ | $+8.0$ | $+40.0$ |
| Crew & effects | $+2$ | $+7.0$ | $+14.0$ |
| **Total** | $\mathbf{132}$ | — | $\mathbf{624.5}$ |

**Step 2: Apply the LCG Composition Formula:**

$$
LCG \;=\; \bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum W_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum W_i} \;=\; \dfrac{624.5}{132} \;\approx\; 4.731 \;\; \text{m forward of AP}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks] Compute the VCG using the same composition principle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Tabulation of Vertical Data (positive y = upward from keel):**

| Component | $W_i$ (t) | $\bar{y}_i$ (m) | $W_i \bar{y}_i$ (t·m) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Hull | $+80$ | $+1.5$ | $+120.0$ |
| Main engine | $+25$ | $+1.2$ | $+30.0$ |
| Aux. machinery | $+8$ | $+1.8$ | $+14.4$ |
| Fuel oil | $+12$ | $+0.5$ | $+6.0$ |
| Fresh water | $+5$ | $+1.0$ | $+5.0$ |
| Crew & effects | $+2$ | $+3.5$ | $+7.0$ |
| **Total** | $\mathbf{132}$ | — | $\mathbf{182.4}$ |

**Step 2: Apply the VCG Composition Formula:**

$$
VCG \;=\; \bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum W_i \bar{y}_i}{\sum W_i} \;=\; \dfrac{182.4}{132} \;\approx\; 1.382 \;\; \text{m above the keel}
$$

**Final Summary:**

$$
\boxed{\;LCG \;=\; 4.73\;\text{m forward of AP}, \qquad VCG \;=\; 1.38\;\text{m above keel}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Pitfall:** When a component lies **abaft (behind) the AP**, the KTU rubric requires entering its longitudinal coordinate as **negative**, not as a positive number with the answer "subtracted". Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for sloppy sign handling.
> 2. **Forgotten Units:** Always write the final answer with **explicit units (m forward of AP, m above keel)**. A bare number with no unit loses **0.5 mark** per occurrence.
> 3. **Tabulation Discipline:** KTU board examiners award a **1-mark bonus** (out of 14) for a clean, properly-labelled moment table with column headers. Tabulating on scratch-paper-style scribbles forfeits this.
> 4. **Mis-application of Weight vs. Mass:** If a problem gives mass in **kg**, you must **either** convert to tonnes **or** keep everything in kg consistently. Mixing kg and tonnes in the same calculation leads to a **factor-of-1000 error** — a fatal flaw.
> 5. **Total Weight vs. Displacement Trap:** In ship-load cases, the *displacement* $\Delta$ must equal $\sum W_i$. If your $\sum W_i$ does not match the given displacement, recheck for **forgotten items** (e.g., cargo, stores, ballast).
> 6. **Symmetry Shortcut Abuse:** You may use symmetry only when the figure is **provably symmetric**. The circular hole in Question A above broke the symmetry of the moment — a student assuming symmetry would lose 3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Compositions" — Module 3**

- ✅ **Definition:** Compositions = finding the centroid/CG of a composite body by **resolving it into simple primitives** and applying the **Principle of Moments (Varignon's Theorem)**.

- ✅ **Master Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum A_i}$  *(area form)*
  - $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum W_i}$  *(weight form — used for LCG/VCG/TCG of ships)*
  - Substitute $W_i \to m_i$ for mass-based versions.

- ✅ **Standard Centroid Locations to Memorize:**
  - Rectangle: $(\tfrac{b}{2}, \tfrac{h}{2})$ from corner.
  - Triangle: at $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of base / $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of height.
  - Semicircle: $\tfrac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.424\,r$ from the diameter.
  - Quarter circle: $\tfrac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.424\,r$ from each straight edge.
  - Trapezium: $\bar{y} = \dfrac{h(2a+b)}{3(a+b)}$ from the base of length $a$.

- ✅ **Sign Convention:**
  - **Cutouts / holes** → enter $A_i$ as **negative**.
  - **Components behind AP** → enter $\bar{x}_i$ as **negative**.
  - **Components below keel** → enter $\bar{y}_i$ as **negative** (rare in naval architecture but possible for external appendages).

- ✅ **Worked Strategy for KTU Exams:**
  1. Draw and label a clean diagram.
  2. Mark the reference origin explicitly.
  3. Tabulate $i, A_i$ (or $W_i$), $\bar{x}_i$, $\bar{y}_i$, $A_i \bar{x}_i$, $A_i \bar{y}_i$.
  4. Sum the columns.
  5. Divide moment-sum by area-sum.
  6. Sanity-check: the centroid must lie **inside** the composite boundary.

- ✅ **Real-World Linkage:** Every IMO intact-stability criterion (SOLAS Ch II-1, IS Code 2008, ML Code) requires the **VCG/LCG** of a vessel, and these are **always** computed using the compositions method.

- ✅ **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Mixing up centroids of semicircle vs. quarter-circle.
  - Forgetting to subtract hole areas.
  - Using different reference origins for different parts of the body.
  - Failing to check dimensional consistency.

- ✅ **Numericals to Practice:** Composite areas with rectangles + triangles + semicircles + circular cutouts; ship displacement problems with 5–7 weight components including both positive and negative longitudes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Ship Structural Members
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Ship Structural Members

# Introduction to Ship Structural Members

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Structural Members** are the load-bearing skeletal components of a ship's hull that together form a complex, three-dimensional, anisotropic structure capable of resisting hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, gravitational, and environmental loads while maintaining the vessel's geometric integrity throughout its service life.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the structural framework of a ship consists of a **plating system** (the skin) stiffened by a **framing system** (the skeleton). These two systems work synergistically — the plating provides the watertight envelope and local strength, while the framing system provides overall rigidity and load transfer paths.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship as a **giant steel eggshell** floating on water. Just like a human skeleton gives the body its shape and supports all organs, the ship's structural members are its **skeleton**, while the shell plating acts as its **skin**. If you imagine building a giant rectangular tank out of thin steel sheets and then trying to make it survive being dropped into the ocean, you would quickly realize that thin steel bends easily. This is why engineers add a network of internal "ribs," "spines," and "struts" — exactly like the bones inside our bodies. The keel is the **spine** that runs along the bottom, frames are the **ribs** spaced along the length, and decks act as the **diaphragm** dividing the inside into manageable compartments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Ship Metrics (KTU Reference)**
> - **Hull girder**: The entire ship considered as a hollow beam spanning between the bow and stern.
> - **Scantlings**: The dimensions (thickness, depth, breadth) of structural members specified by classification societies.
> - **Rule length (L)**: The distance in meters used for structural calculations, defined by IACS unified rules.

### Classification of Ship Structural Members

Ship structural members can be broadly classified into the following functional groups:

1. **Longitudinal Members** — run fore-aft along the ship's length (keel, longitudinals, stringers).
2. **Transverse Members** — run athwartships, i.e., side-to-side (frames, beams, transverse bulkheads).
3. **Vertical / Web Members** — run vertically (webs, floors, girders, bulkheads).
4. **Plating Members** — the outer skin and deck sheets (bottom, side, deck plating).
5. **Secondary / Tertiary Members** — stiffeners, brackets, collars, lugs, and fittings.

> [!NOTE]
> The hull can be either **longitudinally framed** (heavier longitudinals, lighter frames — used in large vessels) or **transversely framed** (closely spaced frames — used in smaller vessels). Modern cargo ships typically use a **mixed framing system** combining both.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hull Girder Concept

The most fundamental theoretical concept in ship structural engineering is the **Hull Girder**. The entire ship is treated as a long, thin-walled, hollow box girder spanning between the supports provided by the waves (sagging and hogging conditions).

### Sagging and Hogging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hogging** occurs when the ship is supported amidships by a wave crest (bow and stern in troughs) → the keel is in **tension** and the deck is in **compression**.
>
> **Sagging** occurs when the ship is supported at the bow and stern by wave crests (amidships in a trough) → the keel is in **compression** and the deck is in **tension**.

The bending moment at any section of the hull girder is given by:

$$
M = \frac{w L^{2}}{g} \cdot k
$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment (kN·m)
- $w$ = Weight of vessel per unit length (kN/m)
- $L$ = Length between perpendiculars (m)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $k$ = Wave factor (typically between 0.0625 and 0.125)

## 2.2 Primary Structural Members — Detailed Functions

### A. Longitudinal Members

| Member | Location | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Keel** | Bottom centerline | Acts as the **backbone**; resists bending and grounding loads |
| **Stem** | Forward extremity | Connects bottom plating to bow; resists wave impact and ramming |
| **Sternpost** | Aft extremity | Supports the rudder and propeller |
| **Inner Keel / Centre Keelson** | Above keel | Increases longitudinal strength |
| **Side Stringers** | On side shell | Provide longitudinal strength; resist shear forces |
| **Longitudinals** | Inside shell, running fore-aft | Stiffen the plating between frames |

### B. Transverse Members

| Member | Location | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Frames (Ribs)** | Transverse sections | Maintain hull shape; support shell plating |
| **Web Frames** | Heavy transverse members | Provide lateral strength at critical sections |
| **Deck Beams** | Under each deck | Support deck plating; transmit loads to frames |
| **Transverse Bulkheads** | Vertical, athwartship | Divide hull into watertight compartments |

### C. Web and Girder Members

| Member | Location | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Floors** | Transverse, on bottom | Vertical plate connecting port and starboard bottom plating |
| **Centre Girders** | Longitudinal, vertical | Connects tank top to bottom plating at centerline |
| **Side Girders** | Longitudinal, vertical | Parallel to centre girder, offset from centerline |
| **Pillars / Stanchions** | Vertical posts | Support decks and girders against compression |

### D. Plating Members

| Member | Location | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom Plating** | Lowest shell | Resists hydrostatic pressure and grounding |
| **Side Plating** | Vertical sides | Resists lateral water pressure |
| **Deck Plating** | Top horizontal | Forms the strength deck; resists compression/tension |
| **Sheer strake** | Top strake of side plating | Increased thickness; part of longitudinal strength |
| **Stringer plate** | At deck edge | Connects deck and side plating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **strength deck** is the uppermost continuous deck that forms part of the hull girder. It is the most highly stressed longitudinal member in tension during hogging.

## 2.3 Load Transfer Pathway

The loading hierarchy in a ship works as follows:

1. **Local pressure loads** (water, cargo) act on the **shell plating** and **deck plating**.
2. The **plating** transfers these loads to the nearest **stiffeners/frames**.
3. The **frames and beams** carry loads to **webs, floors, and girders**.
4. The **primary supporting members** (transverse webs, longitudinal girders) transfer loads to **bulkheads**.
5. **Bulkheads** and the **hull girder** ultimately transfer the cumulative loads to the supports (water, drydock, or wave).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{g} \cdot k$ | Still-water bending moment (wave factor form) | kN·m |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from NA | N/mm² |
| 3 | $I = \sum b t^{3} / 12 + A d^{2}$ | Moment of inertia of hull section | m⁴ |
| 4 | $Z = I / y_{\max}$ | Section modulus of hull girder | m³ |
| 5 | $P_{sw} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | kN/m² |
| 6 | $t = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot k}{1000 \sigma_{\text{all}}}}$ | Plating thickness (IACS-style) | mm |
| 7 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Metacentric radius (stability) | m |
| 8 | $h_{s} = h_{w} \cdot C_{b}$ | Wave height (significant) | m |
| 9 | $L_{eff} = 0.5 L$ | Effective span for frame calculations | m |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear stress | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, $\rho = \mathbf{1025 \, kg/m^{3}}$ (seawater density) and $g = \mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^{2}}$ are standard constants used throughout KTU naval architecture problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding ship structural members is essential for:
- **Classification society compliance** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS).
- **Damage control and repair** scheduling in shipyards.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** of new hull designs.
- **Weight estimation** in the early design phase.
- **Fatigue life prediction** for offshore structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hull Girder Section Modulus

We will derive the **section modulus (Z)** of a simplified box-girder representation of a ship's hull, as used in KTU problems.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Consider the hull as a rectangular box girder with:
- Total length $L$
- Depth $D$ (keel to strength deck)
- Breadth $B$ (port to starboard)
- Bottom plating thickness $t_{b}$
- Deck plating thickness $t_{d}$
- Side plating thickness $t_{s}$

The neutral axis lies at the mid-depth (approximately), so:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{D}{2}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Moment of Inertia

For a thin-walled box girder, the moment of inertia about the horizontal neutral axis is the sum of contributions from each plate:

$$
I = I_{\text{bottom}} + I_{\text{deck}} + I_{\text{side,port}} + I_{\text{side,stbd}}
$$

Each contribution is computed as $I = A d^{2}$ for a thin plate (where $A$ is area and $d$ is distance from NA), since the $bt^{3}/12$ term is negligible.

For the bottom plating (area = $B \cdot t_{b}$, distance = $D/2$):

$$
I_{\text{bottom}} = B \cdot t_{b} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

For the deck plating (area = $B \cdot t_{d}$, distance = $D/2$):

$$
I_{\text{deck}} = B \cdot t_{d} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

For each side plating (area = $D \cdot t_{s}$, distance = 0 at NA):

$$
I_{\text{side,port}} = I_{\text{side,stbd}} \approx \frac{D^{3} \cdot t_{s}}{12}
$$

> This is because the side plating is centered on the neutral axis, so its self-contribution $D \cdot t_{s}^{3}/12$ is negligible but its $A d^{2}$ contribution gives $0$ at NA. We must integrate over its width to get $\int t_s y^2 dy$ from $-D/2$ to $D/2$.

### Step 3: Side Plating Contribution — Detailed Integration

$$
I_{\text{side}} = \int_{-D/2}^{D/2} t_{s} \cdot y^{2} \, dy
$$

$$
I_{\text{side}} = t_{s} \left[ \frac{y^{3}}{3} \right]_{-D/2}^{D/2}
$$

$$
I_{\text{side}} = t_{s} \left( \frac{(D/2)^{3}}{3} - \frac{(-D/2)^{3}}{3} \right)
$$

$$
I_{\text{side}} = t_{s} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{D^{3}}{8}
$$

$$
I_{\text{side}} = \frac{t_{s} \cdot D^{3}}{12}
$$

### Step 4: Total Moment of Inertia

Summing all four plates (two sides):

$$
I = B \cdot t_{b} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{4} + B \cdot t_{d} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{t_{s} \cdot D^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
I = \frac{B D^{2}}{4}(t_{b} + t_{d}) + \frac{t_{s} D^{3}}{6}
$$

### Step 5: Section Modulus

The section modulus is the moment of inertia divided by the maximum distance from the neutral axis:

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{I}{D/2}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{2I}{D}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Z = \frac{2}{D} \left[ \frac{B D^{2}}{4}(t_{b} + t_{d}) + \frac{t_{s} D^{3}}{6} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{B D}{2}(t_{b} + t_{d}) + \frac{t_{s} D^{2}}{3}}
$$

### Step 6: Verification — Units Check

Each term has units of $\text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{m} = \text{m}^{3}$, which matches the units of section modulus. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure on Bottom Plating

For a ship floating at draft $T$:

$$
P_{\text{sw}} = \rho g h
$$

where $h$ is the depth below the waterline.

At the keel ($h = T$):

$$
P_{\text{keel}} = \rho g T = 1025 \times 9.81 \times T
$$

$$
P_{\text{keel}} = 10057.25 \cdot T \;\; \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

For a typical cargo ship with $T = 10$ m:

$$
P_{\text{keel}} = 100572.5 \;\; \text{N/m}^{2} \approx 100.57 \;\; \text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Load Path Mapping

| Load Type | Acting On | First Transfer To | Final Transfer To |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea pressure | Shell plating | Frames/longitudinals | Floors, bulkheads, hull girder |
| Cargo weight | Inner bottom / tank top | Floors, girders | Side girders, bulkheads |
| Wave impact | Bow shell plating | Stem, side stringers | Hull girder, forward bulkheads |
| Wind/weather | Superstructure | Deckhouses, bulwarks | Strength deck, shear strake |
| Dynamic loads | All plating | Stiffeners | Primary supporting members |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ship Structural Members

The following diagram maps the hierarchical relationship between all primary and secondary structural members of a ship's hull. This block-level view is preferred over physical drawings because the structural interdependencies are best understood through logical connections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[HULL GIRDER SYSTEM] --> B1[Longitudinal Members]
    A1 --> B2[Transverse Members]
    A1 --> B3[Vertical Web Members]
    A1 --> B4[Plating System]

    B1 --> C1[Keel Assembly]
    B1 --> C2[Stem and Sternpost]
    B1 --> C3[Longitudinal Stringers]
    B1 --> C4[Sheer Strake]

    B2 --> D1[Frames Ribs]
    B2 --> D2[Deck Beams]
    B2 --> D3[Web Frames]
    B2 --> D4[Transverse Bulkheads]

    B3 --> E1[Floors]
    B3 --> E2[Centre Girder]
    B3 --> E3[Side Girders]
    B3 --> E4[Pillars and Stanchions]

    B4 --> F1[Bottom Plating]
    B4 --> F2[Side Plating]
    B4 --> F3[Deck Plating]
    B4 --> F4[Tank Top]

    C1 --> G1[Backbone and grounding resistance]
    C2 --> G2[Bow and stern connections]
    C3 --> G3[Longitudinal strength]
    C4 --> G4[Deck to side transition]

    D1 --> H1[Maintain hull shape]
    D2 --> H2[Support deck plating]
    D3 --> H3[Lateral strength]
    D4 --> H4[Watertight subdivision]

    E1 --> I1[Connect port and starboard bottom]
    E2 --> I2[Vertical centerline support]
    E3 --> I3[Offset vertical support]
    E4 --> I4[Compressional supports]

    F1 --> J1[Hydrostatic load resistance]
    F2 --> J2[Lateral water pressure]
    F3 --> J3[Strength deck in bending]
    F4 --> J4[Cargo and ballast boundary]

    G1 --> K1[HULL INTEGRITY]
    G2 --> K1
    G3 --> K1
    G4 --> K1
    H1 --> K1
    H2 --> K1
    H3 --> K1
    H4 --> K1
    I1 --> K1
    I2 --> K1
    I3 --> K1
    I4 --> K1
    J1 --> K1
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    J4 --> K1

    K1[SAFE FLOATING STRUCTURE]
```

flowchart LR

    L2 --> M1[Shell and Deck Plating]
    M1 --> M2[Stiffeners and Frames]

    M3 --> M4[Bulkheads]
    M4 --> M5[Hull Girder]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following with a neat sketch:**
(a) Keel
(b) Stem
(c) Sternpost

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Keel**: The keel is the principal longitudinal strength member of a ship, located at the bottom centerline of the hull. It runs from the stem to the sternpost and forms the backbone of the vessel. The keel resists hogging and sagging bending moments and provides grounding protection.
>
> **(b) Stem**: The stem is a heavy fabricated plate section at the forward extremity of the ship, welded to the keel and side shell plating. It is shaped to give a hydrodynamic entry and resists wave impact, ramming, and mooring forces.
>
> **(c) Sternpost**: The sternpost is a vertical structural member at the aft end of the ship, attached to the keel. It supports the rudder, propeller shaft, and provides a watertight closure at the stern. In single-screw vessels, it is usually combined with the propeller post.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between longitudinal framing and transverse framing systems of a ship.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Longitudinal Framing | Transverse Framing |
|---|---|---|
| Primary stiffeners | Many longitudinals running fore-aft | Closely spaced transverse frames |
| Frame spacing | Wider frame spacing (2.5–4 m) | Closer frame spacing (0.5–0.8 m) |
| Plating area supported | Larger unsupported panels | Smaller unsupported panels |
| Material efficiency | More efficient use of steel | Less efficient |
| Typical use | Large tankers, bulk carriers, container ships | Tugs, small craft, naval vessels |
| Construction complexity | Higher | Lower |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **primary structural members of a ship's hull**. Classify them into longitudinal, transverse, and vertical members. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars $L = 150$ m
- Breadth $B = 22$ m
- Depth $D = 14$ m
- Draft $T = 9$ m
- Block coefficient $C_{b} = 0.78$
- Bottom plating thickness $t_{b} = 18$ mm
- Deck plating thickness $t_{d} = 22$ mm
- Side plating thickness $t_{s} = 16$ mm

Calculate the **section modulus (Z)** of the hull girder and the **maximum bending stress** for a still-water bending moment $M_{sw} = 250{,}000$ kN·m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Primary Structural Members:**

The hull of a ship consists of the following primary structural members:

1. **Longitudinal Members**: Run fore and aft along the ship's length.
   - Keel (centre, side keelsons)
   - Stem and Sternpost
   - Longitudinals on shell and decks
   - Side stringers
   - Sheer strake

2. **Transverse Members**: Run athwartships (port to starboard).
   - Frames (main and tween deck)
   - Web frames
   - Deck beams
   - Transverse bulkheads

3. **Vertical/Web Members**: Run vertically.
   - Floors (bottom transverse)
   - Centre girder
   - Side girders
   - Pillars / Stanchions

4. **Plating Members**: Form the watertight envelope.
   - Bottom plating
   - Side plating
   - Deck plating (strength deck)
   - Tank top plating

Each member plays a defined role in resisting hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and gravity loads.

**Part (b) — Section Modulus Calculation:**

**Step 1**: Use the formula derived in Section 3:

$$
Z = \frac{B D}{2}(t_{b} + t_{d}) + \frac{t_{s} D^{2}}{3}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the values (convert all thicknesses to meters):

$$
B = 22 \text{ m}, \quad D = 14 \text{ m}
$$

$$
t_{b} = 0.018 \text{ m}, \quad t_{d} = 0.022 \text{ m}, \quad t_{s} = 0.016 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3**: First term:

$$
\frac{B D}{2}(t_{b} + t_{d}) = \frac{22 \times 14}{2} \times (0.018 + 0.022)
$$

$$
= \frac{308}{2} \times 0.040 = 154 \times 0.040 = 6.16 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4**: Second term:

$$
\frac{t_{s} D^{2}}{3} = \frac{0.016 \times 14^{2}}{3} = \frac{0.016 \times 196}{3}
$$

$$
= \frac{3.136}{3} = 1.0453 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5**: Total section modulus:

$$
\boxed{Z = 6.16 + 1.0453 = 7.2053 \text{ m}^{3}}
$$

**Step 6**: Maximum bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}
$$

Convert $M = 250{,}000$ kN·m = $250 \times 10^{6}$ N·m:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{250 \times 10^{6}}{7.2053} = 34.696 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 34.70 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors**: Students often forget to convert mm to m when using the section modulus formula. KTU strictly deducts **1 mark** for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Neutral axis assumption**: This derivation assumes NA at mid-depth. For ships with different $t_b$ and $t_d$, the NA shifts slightly, requiring a parallel-axis correction.
> 3. **Sign convention**: Bending stress can be positive (tension) or negative (compression). Always mention the location where it occurs.
> 4. **Missing sub-parts**: Part (a) requires both a sketch AND classification — missing the sketch costs up to **2 marks**.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the **hogging and sagging conditions** of a ship's hull girder. Indicate the locations of maximum tensile and compressive stresses in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of length $L = 120$ m and weight $W = 12{,}000$ tonnes floats at draft $T = 8$ m. The vessel passes through a wave of height equal to $L/20$. Calculate:
1. The still-water bending moment (use $k = 0.0625$).
2. The maximum hydrostatic pressure at the keel.
3. The wave-induced additional bending moment if the wave coefficient factor $C_{w} = 0.045$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Hogging and Sagging:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hogging Condition**: The ship is supported amidships by a wave crest while the bow and stern are in wave troughs. The hull bends with the ends drooping down. As a result, the **keel is in tension** and the **deck is in compression**.
>
> **Sagging Condition**: The ship is supported at the bow and stern by wave crests while amidships is in a wave trough. The hull sags in the middle. As a result, the **keel is in compression** and the **deck is in tension**.

Diagrams:

```
HOGGING:              SAGGING:
   ___                   ___
  /   \                 /   \
 |     |  Trough at ends |     |  Trough midship
  \___/                  \___/
  T E N S I O N at KEEL  COMPRESSION at KEEL
  COMP at DECK           TENSION at DECK
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 120$ m
- $W = 12{,}000$ tonnes = $12{,}000 \times 1000$ kg = $12 \times 10^{6}$ kg
- $T = 8$ m
- $k = 0.0625$
- $C_{w} = 0.045$
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Weight per unit length**

$$
w = \frac{W \cdot g}{L} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6} \times 9.81}{120}
$$

$$
w = \frac{117.72 \times 10^{6}}{120} = 981{,}000 \text{ N/m} = 981 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2: Still-water bending moment (SWBM)**

$$
M_{sw} = w L^{2} \cdot k
$$

Wait — re-examining the formula carefully. The SWBM based on a uniformly distributed weight approximation is:

$$
M_{sw} = \frac{w L^{2}}{g} \cdot k
$$

Note: $w$ here is mass per unit length. Let's redo this carefully:

Mass per unit length:

$$
m = \frac{W}{L} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{120} = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_{sw} = \frac{m \cdot L^{2}}{g} \cdot k = \frac{100{,}000 \times 120^{2}}{9.81} \times 0.0625
$$

$$
= \frac{100{,}000 \times 14400}{9.81} \times 0.0625
$$

$$
= \frac{1.44 \times 10^{9}}{9.81} \times 0.0625
$$

$$
= 146{,}789{,}909 \times 0.0625
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{sw} \approx 9.174 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·m} = 9174.4 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum hydrostatic pressure at keel**

$$
P_{\max} = \rho g T = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8
$$

$$
P_{\max} = 1025 \times 78.48 = 80{,}442 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\max} \approx 80.44 \text{ kN/m}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Wave-induced bending moment**

The wave-induced bending moment is often computed as:

$$
M_{w} = C_{w} \cdot W \cdot L
$$

$$
M_{w} = 0.045 \times (12{,}000 \times 10^{3} \times 9.81) \times 120
$$

$$
= 0.045 \times 117.72 \times 10^{6} \times 120
$$

$$
= 0.045 \times 14.1264 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{w} \approx 635.69 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·m} = 635{,}688 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula confusion**: The formula $M = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{g} \cdot k$ uses $w$ as **mass per unit length**, not weight. Many students use weight directly, causing a dimensional error.
> 2. **Wave height not needed here**: For this specific question, wave height is given but only used in conceptual discussion, not numerical solution — don't waste time on it.
> 3. **Hogging vs Sagging**: Always clearly state the bending type. Confusing them leads to **wrong sign of stress** and **loss of 2 marks**.
> 4. **Pressure units**: Express hydrostatic pressure in kN/m² (or kPa), NOT N/m². KTU deducts marks for inconsistent pressure units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ship Structural Members**

- ✅ **Keel** = backbone of the ship, located at the bottom centerline.
- ✅ **Stem** = forward extremity; **Sternpost** = aft extremity (supports rudder/propeller).
- ✅ **Frames (Ribs)** = transverse members maintaining hull shape.
- ✅ **Floors** = transverse vertical plates at bottom connecting port/starboard.
- ✅ **Centre girder** = longitudinal vertical plate on centerline.
- ✅ **Side girders** = longitudinal vertical plates offset from centerline.
- ✅ **Pillars/Stanchions** = vertical posts supporting decks against compression.
- ✅ **Strength deck** = the uppermost continuous deck in the hull girder.
- ✅ **Sheer strake** = top strake of side plating with increased thickness.
- ✅ **Bulkheads** = vertical plates dividing hull into compartments (transverse or longitudinal).
- ✅ **Hogging** → Tension at keel, Compression at deck.
- ✅ **Sagging** → Compression at keel, Tension at deck.
- ✅ **Section Modulus** $Z = \dfrac{BD}{2}(t_b + t_d) + \dfrac{t_s D^{2}}{3}$.
- ✅ **Standard constants**: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ **Longitudinal framing** → heavy longitudinals + widely spaced frames (large ships).
- ✅ **Transverse framing** → closely spaced frames (small ships).
- ✅ **Mixed framing** → combination; most common in modern vessels.
- ✅ **Load transfer path**: Plating → Stiffeners → Primary Members → Bulkheads → Hull Girder → Supports.
- ✅ **Hull girder** = entire ship treated as a hollow box beam.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Introduction to Ship Structural Members**
