topics.title: Structural Components such as Bottom Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structural Components such as Bottom Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — STRUCTURAL COMPONENTS: BOTTOM STRUCTURE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> The **bottom structure** of a floating body (ship/vessel) refers to the assembly of primary and secondary structural members located at the lowest region of the hull, which together transmit hydrodynamic loads (water pressure, wave slamming, grounding), cargo weight, and still-water shear forces from the shell plating to the transverse bulkheads, decks, and side shell. It functions as the **load-bearing foundation** of the entire hull girder.

The bottom structure is composed of the **bottom shell plating**, **keel**, **floors**, **keelsons (centerline girders)**, **side girders**, **bottom longitudinals**, and auxiliary fittings like **bilge keels** and **garboard strakes**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a multi-storey building standing on loose soil. The building's vertical columns carry the load down to a massive **raft foundation** at the bottom. The raft distributes the concentrated column loads over a wide soil area and resists upward water pressure (uplift).
>
> The **bottom structure of a ship is exactly this raft foundation**, but inverted and floating:
> - **Water pressure pushes UP** → analogous to soil reaction pushing UP on a foundation.
> - **Cargo and ship weight push DOWN** → analogous to column loads pushing DOWN.
> - The keel, floors, and girders form a **3-D grillage** that spreads these competing loads across the entire hull cross-section.

This is why the bottom structure is sometimes called the **"wet deck"** or **"tank top of the sea"** — it is the structural interface between the ship and the ocean.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Values Used in Bottom Structure Design**
> - **Sea water density:** **ρ = 1.025 t/m³** (1025 kg/m³)
> - **Acceleration of gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Steel density (Hull material):** **ρ_steel ≈ 7.85 t/m³**
> - **Minimum design slamming pressure (IACS S21):** **p_slam = 235 kN/m²** (bow region, depending on ship length)
> - **Rule scantling reference yield stress:** **σ_y = 235 MPa** (mild shipbuilding steel)

### 1.4 Functional Objectives of the Bottom Structure

The bottom structure must satisfy **five simultaneous functional demands**:

1. **Resist hydrostatic pressure** from the sea pushing inward (outward bending of bottom).
2. **Resist wave-induced slamming pressure** (impulsive, localized).
3. **Transmit hull girder bending moments** between port and starboard side shells (keelsons/girders act as flanges).
4. **Distribute concentrated loads** (engines, cargo on inner bottom, propeller weight) to the hull.
5. **Provide a stiff mounting base** for machinery, double-bottom tanks, and bilge piping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy — Primary, Secondary, Tertiary

The bottom structure follows a strict **hierarchical load path**, from fine components up to the hull girder:

### Tier-1: Shell Plating (Primary)
- **Bottom plating** (flat plates between floors)
- **Garboard strake** — the first strake adjacent to the keel
- Carries direct water pressure; buckles under compression.

### Tier-2: Local Stiffening (Secondary)
- **Bottom longitudinals** (in longitudinal framing) OR **bottom frames** (in transverse framing)
- Acts like the ribs of a leaf spring.

### Tier-3: Major Transverse Members (Tertiary)
- **Floors** — transverse vertical plates, typically every **2 – 4 m** spacing
- **Bracket floors** — partial floors between full floors.

### Tier-4: Major Longitudinal Members (Tertiary)
- **Center keelson (centerline girder)** — vertical plate running fore-aft on the centerline.
- **Side girders** — vertical plates offset from the centerline.
- **Side keelsons** — additional longitudinal girders.

### Tier-5: Backbone
- **Keel** — the outer flat-bar or rolled section on the hull exterior.
- **Bar keel / Duct keel** — additional appendage.

## 2.2 Types of Framing Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Approved Framing Arrangements (per IACS CSR / KTU Naval Arch Syllabus)**

| System | Dominant Direction | When Used | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Framing** | Frames spaced 0.6 – 0.8 m, Floors every 2 – 4 m | Short ships, tankers, naval vessels | Simple fabrication, easy maintenance |
| **Longitudinal Framing** | Longitudinals spaced 0.3 – 0.5 m, Transverse floors every 2.5 – 4 m | Large tankers, bulk carriers, container ships | Higher hull girder section modulus (≈ 20 – 30 % gain) |
| **Mixed Framing** | Combined above | Machinery spaces, forward / aft regions | Optimizes for local loads |

## 2.3 Detailed Function of Each Component

### (a) Keel
- **Outermost longitudinal backbone** of the ship.
- **Types:**
  - **Flat keel** — flat bar welded to bottom plating along centerline (typical modern construction).
  - **Bar keel** — heavy rolled T-section or rectangular bar (older/small vessels).
  - **Duct keel** — flat keel with internal pipe passage for ballast/trimming.
- **Function:** Provides longitudinal continuity, resists grounding impact, serves as reference line for hull form measurement.

### (b) Garboard Strake
- The **first strake of bottom plating** next to the keel.
- **Thicker** than adjacent bottom plates (typically 1 – 2 mm extra) due to high stress concentration at the keel-bilge junction.

### (c) Floors
- **Transverse plate webs** extending from keel to bilge (and to side shell in single-bottom ships).
- **Solid plate floors** in single-bottom ships.
- **Bracket floors** (with centerline bracket only) in double-bottom ships.
- Spaced at frame intervals in transverse framing; at every 2 – 4 webs in longitudinal framing.

### (d) Centerline Keelson / Centerline Girder
- **Vertical plate** running fore-aft on the centerline inside the double bottom.
- Acts as the **inner keel**, providing the **flange area** for hull girder bending strength.
- Height: typically **0.6 – 1.2 × web frame spacing**.

### (e) Side Girders
- **Vertical plates** parallel to center keelson, offset **2 – 3 m** each side.
- Support the **inner bottom plating** against sea pressure.
- Reduce **slenderness** (b/t ratio) of bottom plating.

### (f) Bottom Longitudinals
- Continuous **T-section or flat-bar stiffeners** running fore-aft.
- Spaced at **longitudinal frame spacing** (300 – 500 mm).
- Provide the **primary local stiffness** in longitudinal framing.

### (g) Bilge Keels
- External **fin-like projections** at the bilge (turn of the bilge region).
- Length: typically **30 % – 60 % Lpp**.
- Function: **Roll damping** — they create hydrodynamic lift opposing roll motion.
- They are the **only non-structural** bottom component in this module — purely a hydrodynamic device.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable as per KTU 2024 OENST611 Module 3 syllabus.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress (Pa) | Maximum stress in hull girder |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | Section modulus (m³) | Required hull girder strength |
| 3 | $t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\,p\,} + t_c$ | $s$ = stiffener spacing (m); $p$ = design pressure (kN/m²); $t_c$ = corrosion allowance (mm) | Minimum bottom plate thickness |
| 4 | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{p}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{s}{\ell}\right)^2$ | Plate bending stress (Pa) for plate with stiffener spacing $s$ and span $\ell$ | Local panel bending check |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot t}$ | Shear stress in web | Floor / keelson shear check |
| 6 | $p_{\text{hydro}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure (Pa) at depth $h$ | Design pressure on bottom plating |
| 7 | $p_{\text{slam}} = k_s \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | IACS S21 slamming pressure formula (kN/m²); $k_s$, $C$ = coefficients | Bow region dynamic design pressure |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{s}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Plate slenderness (dimensionless) | Plate buckling classification |
| 9 | $A_{\text{eff}} = b \cdot t$ | Effective shear area (m²) | Floor plate shear capacity |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\text{vm}} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | von Mises equivalent stress | Combined loading check |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters Beyond the Exam Hall**

The bottom structure is **the most heavily loaded** and **most failure-prone** region of a ship. The most catastrophic maritime casualties in history — *MV Derbyshire* (1980), *MOL Comfort* (2013) — involved bottom-structure fatigue and buckling failures. Naval architects design the bottom structure to satisfy:
- **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — no collapse under 1.67 × design load.
- **Fatigue Limit State (FLS)** — 25-year service life under wave spectrum.
- **Accidental Limit State (ALS)** — survival after grounding / collision damage.

Modern classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) publish Common Structural Rules (CSR) that explicitly govern every formula in §2.4.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All derivations below use classical thin-plate theory and elementary strength-of-materials, both standard in the KTU Naval Architecture syllabus.

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Bottom Plate Thickness (Plate on Stiffener Spacing)

A bottom plate between two adjacent longitudinals behaves as a **rectangular plate simply supported on four edges** (loaded by uniform hydrostatic pressure).

**Step 1 — Apply plate bending theory (Timoshenko, 1959).**

For a simply supported rectangular plate of length $\ell$ (between transverse frames) and breadth $s$ (between longitudinals), under uniform pressure $p$, the maximum bending moment per unit width is:

$$
M_x = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\mu + 1}\right)
$$

where $\mu$ is Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.3 for steel).

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum bending stress.**

For a plate of unit width (1 m) and thickness $t$, the section modulus is:

$$
Z_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

Therefore the bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_x = \dfrac{M_x}{Z_{\text{plate}}} = \dfrac{6 \, M_x}{t^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Set stress equal to allowable value and solve for $t$.**

Equating $\sigma_x = \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ and rearranging:

$$
t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \, M_x}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $M_x$ and simplify.**

Substituting Step 1 into Step 3 and using $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 0.9 \sigma_y$ (DNV rule):

$$
t = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \, p}{8 \, (\mu + 1) \cdot 0.9 \, \sigma_y}}
$$

For $\mu = 0.3$ and $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$, the constant reduces to **0.0155** when $p$ is in **kN/m²** and $s$ in **metres**, giving the **rule formula**:

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \, s \sqrt{p} + t_c}
$$

This matches formula (3) in the cheat sheet.

## 3.2 Derivation of Section Modulus of Bottom Flange

The hull girder is treated as a **box beam**. The bottom structure contributes the **lower flange area**, $A_b$.

**Step 1 — Define the neutral axis.**

By symmetry about the centerline (in hogging or sagging), the neutral axis lies at midship depth $d/2$ from keel.

**Step 2 — Define the flange area contributed by the bottom.**

The bottom flange area includes:
- Bottom plating: $A_1 = B \cdot t_b$
- Keel plate: $A_2 = 0.5 \, t_k \cdot h_k$ (accounting for half-symmetry)

Total effective bottom flange area:

$$
A_b = A_1 + A_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance from neutral axis to flange centroid.**

For the bottom flange centroid at distance $y_b$ from the neutral axis:

$$
y_b = \dfrac{d}{2} - \dfrac{t_b}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus formula.**

By definition:

$$
Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{A_b \cdot y_b}{1} = A_b \cdot \left( \dfrac{d}{2} - \dfrac{t_b}{2} \right)
$$

Expanding:

$$
\boxed{Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{A_b \cdot (d - t_b)}{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Total hull girder section modulus.**

Summing bottom and top flanges symmetrically:

$$
Z_{\text{hull}} = 2 \cdot Z_{\text{bottom}} = A_b \cdot (d - t_b)
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

**Given:**
- Ship length $L_{pp}$ = 120 m
- Breadth $B$ = 18 m
- Depth $d$ = 11 m
- Design pressure $p$ = 90 kN/m²
- Stiffener spacing $s$ = 0.45 m
- Yield stress $\sigma_y$ = 235 MPa
- Corrosion allowance $t_c$ = 2 mm

**Find:** (a) Minimum bottom plating thickness; (b) Section modulus of bottom flange; (c) Maximum bending stress under still-water bending moment $M_{sw}$ = 200 MN·m.

**Solution (a) — Plate thickness:**

$$
t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \times 0.45 \times \sqrt{90} + 2
$$

$$
\sqrt{90} = 9.4868
$$

$$
t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \times 0.45 \times 9.4868 + 2
$$

$$
t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0662 + 2.0
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{bottom}} = 2.07 \text{ mm (rule value)}}
$$

In practice, shipyards adopt **5 mm – 8 mm** as minimum bottom plate thickness (welding & fabrication practicals).

**Solution (b) — Section modulus:**

Using $A_b = B \cdot t_b = 18 \times 0.008 = 0.144 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{A_b \cdot (d - t_b)}{2} = \dfrac{0.144 \times (11 - 0.008)}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{\text{bottom}} = 0.791 \text{ m}^3}
$$

**Solution (c) — Bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{sw}}{Z_{\text{hull}}} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^6}{2 \times 0.791}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 126.4 \text{ MPa} < 0.9 \sigma_y = 211.5 \text{ MPa} \;\;\text{SAFE}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Load Path Summary

| Load Source | Carried By | Transferred To |
|---|---|---|
| Sea pressure (local) | Bottom plating | Longitudinals / Frames |
| Cargo weight (local) | Inner bottom | Floors + girders |
| Hull girder bending (global) | Bottom flange (plating + keel + girders) | Side shell + deck |
| Slamming (impulsive) | Forward bottom + garboard | Floors + side shell |
| Propeller weight | Aft bottom | Stern frame + floors |
| Roll damping | Bilge keels | Side shell brackets |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRRAM — BOTTOM STRUCTURE SCHEMATIC

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Layout & Load Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Sea Water Pressure] --> nodeB[Bottom Shell Plating]
    nodeC[Wave Slamming Load] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Bottom Longitudinals]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Bottom Frames - Transverse]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Floor Plates - Transverse Web]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Centerline Keelson - Vertical Plate]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Side Girder - Port]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Side Girder - Starboard]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Keel - Flat Bar]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Hull Girder Bending Resistance]
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeL[Garboard Strake] --> nodeB
    nodeM[Inner Bottom Plating] --> nodeG
    nodeM --> nodeH
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeN[Bilge Keel - Hydrodynamic Fin] --> nodeO[Side Shell - Bilge Region]
    nodeP[Stern Frame Connection] --> nodeF
    nodeQ[Bulkhead - Transverse] --> nodeF
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[INPUT: Hydrostatic and Wave Loads] --> stepB[Tier 1: Bottom Plating]
    stepB --> stepC[Tier 2: Stiffeners - Longitudinals and Frames]
    stepC --> stepD[Tier 3: Major Transverse Floors and Longitudinal Girders]
    stepD --> stepE[Tier 4: Centerline Keelson and Keel]
    stepE --> stepF[OUTPUT: Distributed Load to Side Shell and Bulkheads]
    stepF --> stepG[Hull Girder Bending and Shear Resistance]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Component Identification (Double-Bottom Configuration)

```
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Deck
       │                                              │
       │          SIDE   SHELL   PLATING              │
       │                                              │
       │   ┌──────┐                    ┌──────┐       │
       │   │ Side │                    │ Side │       │
       │   │Girder│                    │Girder│       │
       │   └──┬───┘                    └──┬───┘       │
       │      │     INNER  BOTTOM         │           │
       ├──────┴────┬──────────────┬───────┴───────────┤  ← Inner Bottom
       │           │              │                   │
       │    ┌──────┴──────┐ ┌─────┴──────┐            │
       │    │  FLOOR      │ │   FLOOR    │            │  ← Transverse floors
       │    │  PLATE      │ │   PLATE    │            │     spaced @ 2.5 m
       │    └──────┬──────┘ └─────┬──────┘            │
       │           │              │                   │
       │      ┌────┴──────────────┴────┐              │
       │      │  CENTERLINE KEELSON    │              │  ← Vertical plate
       │      └────┬──────────────┬────┘              │
       ├───────────┴──────────────┴───────────────────┤  ← Bottom plating
       │                  KEEL                        │  ← Outer flat keel
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 4.4 Reading the Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Use These Diagrams in an Exam**
> In a **Part B drawing question**, students are expected to:
> 1. Label all **five tiers** of bottom structure.
> 2. Mark the **load path arrows** (from sea pressure upward, from keel upward).
> 3. Indicate **typical dimensions** (floor spacing, girder spacing, plate thickness).
> 4. Show the **inner-bottom tank** if the ship is a double-bottom tanker.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** Define the term *bottom structure* of a ship. List any four primary components of the bottom structure.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1a]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):**
> The bottom structure of a ship is the assembly of structural members located at the lowest region of the hull, designed to transmit hydrostatic pressure, wave-induced slamming, cargo loads, and still-water shear forces to the rest of the hull girder. It includes shell plating, keel, floors, keelsons, side girders, and bottom longitudinals.

> **Four Components (½ mark each, total 1 mark):**
> 1. Bottom shell plating
> 2. Keel (flat keel / bar keel)
> 3. Floors (solid / bracket)
> 4. Centerline keelson (centerline girder)

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between a **transverse framing system** and a **longitudinal framing system** used in bottom structures.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q1b]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|---|---|---|
| **Dominant member** | Bottom frames (transverse) | Bottom longitudinals (fore-aft) |
| **Spacing** | Frames @ 0.6 – 0.8 m, Floors every 2 – 4 m | Longitudinals @ 0.3 – 0.5 m, Floors every 2.5 – 4 m |
| **Hull girder strength** | Lower section modulus | **Higher** section modulus (+20 – 30 %) |
| **Application** | Short ships, naval vessels, machinery spaces | Large tankers, bulk carriers, container ships |
| **Fabrication** | Simpler, lower cost | More welding, higher cost |

(2 marks for the table + 1 mark for any valid engineering justification, e.g. — *Longitudinal framing aligns with hull girder bending stresses, hence more efficient.*)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q4a]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional sketch, describe the **bottom structure of a double-bottom ship**, clearly identifying the keelson, side girders, floors, garboard strake, and inner-bottom plating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **functions of the bilge keel** and the **garboard strake**. Why is the garboard strake made thicker than the adjacent bottom plating? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **Sketch (3 marks):** A clear cross-section must be drawn showing:
> - Bottom plating (lower)
> - Inner-bottom plating (upper)
> - Centerline keelson (vertical plate at center)
> - Two side girders (one on each side)
> - At least two floors (transverse plates)
> - Garboard strake at the keel junction
> - Label spacing and approximate dimensions

> **Description (4 marks — distributed):**
> - **Inner bottom plating** forms the top of the double bottom and is used as the upper flange for cargo holds or as tank-top for ballast tanks.
> - **Centerline keelson** is a vertical plate welded to the inner bottom and bottom plating along the centerline, providing the inner flange for hull girder bending.
> - **Side girders** are vertical plates parallel to the center keelson, spaced 2 – 3 m off-center, supporting the inner bottom against hydrostatic pressure and reducing panel slenderness.
> - **Floors** are transverse vertical plates connecting the inner bottom to the bottom plating, spaced at 2.5 – 4 m. They resist hydrostatic pressure and transfer local loads.
> - **Garboard strake** is the first strake of bottom plating adjacent to the keel, with extra thickness.
> - **Bracket floors** are partial floors with centerline brackets only, fitted between full floors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> **Bilge Keel (3 marks):**
> - **Definition:** Bilge keels are external fin-like projections fitted on the bilge (turn-of-bilge) region of the hull, typically 30 – 60 % of the ship's length.
> - **Function:** They create hydrodynamic lift opposing the roll motion of the ship, thereby **damping roll oscillations** and improving crew comfort, cargo safety, and stability.
> - **Structural note:** They are NOT part of the load-bearing hull girder; they are hydrodynamic devices.

> **Garboard Strake (4 marks):**
> - **Definition:** The garboard strake is the **first strake of bottom plating** adjacent to the keel on each side of the centerline.
> - **Reasons for extra thickness (any 3 valid reasons):**
>   1. **High stress concentration** at the keel-bilge junction due to hull girder bending and shear forces.
>   2. **Grounding risk:** The keel region contacts the seabed first in grounding incidents.
>   3. **Welding convergence:** Multiple welds meet at the garboard, requiring thicker parent metal for fatigue strength.
>   4. **Corrosion exposure:** Bilge region accumulates water and debris, requiring extra corrosion allowance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Q4b]`

**(a)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars: Length $L_{pp}$ = 150 m, Breadth $B$ = 22 m, Depth $d$ = 14 m, longitudinal frame spacing $s$ = 0.5 m, design pressure $p$ = 95 kN/m², yield stress $\sigma_y$ = 235 MPa, corrosion allowance $t_c$ = 2 mm. Calculate the **minimum required thickness of bottom plating**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the same hull, calculate the **section modulus of the bottom flange** assuming the bottom plating is now 12 mm thick. Also compute the **maximum bending stress** under still-water bending moment $M_{sw}$ = 350 MN·m and verify whether the section is safe. Use allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$ = 175 MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **Given data (1 mark):**
> $s$ = 0.5 m, $p$ = 95 kN/m², $\sigma_y$ = 235 MPa, $t_c$ = 2 mm.

> **Formula (2 marks):**
>
> $$ t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \, s \, \sqrt{p} + t_c $$

> **Calculation (3 marks):**
>
> $$ \sqrt{p} = \sqrt{95} = 9.7468 $$
>
> $$ t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0155 \times 0.5 \times 9.7468 + 2 $$
>
> $$ t_{\text{bottom}} = 0.0755 + 2.0 $$
>
> $$ \boxed{t_{\text{bottom}} = 2.08 \text{ mm (rule minimum)}} $$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):**
> In practice, shipyards adopt **8 mm – 10 mm** as the actual bottom plate thickness (welding, corrosion, fabrication considerations dominate over the pure stress minimum).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1 — Bottom flange area (1 mark):**
>
> $$ A_b = B \cdot t_b = 22 \times 0.012 = 0.264 \text{ m}^2 $$

> **Step 2 — Section modulus of bottom flange (2 marks):**
>
> $$ Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{A_b \cdot (d - t_b)}{2} = \dfrac{0.264 \times (14 - 0.012)}{2} $$
>
> $$ Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{0.264 \times 13.988}{2} $$
>
> $$ \boxed{Z_{\text{bottom}} = 1.846 \text{ m}^3} $$

> **Step 3 — Total hull girder section modulus (1 mark):**
>
> $$ Z_{\text{hull}} = 2 \times Z_{\text{bottom}} = 3.693 \text{ m}^3 $$

> **Step 4 — Maximum bending stress (2 marks):**
>
> $$ \sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{sw}}{Z_{\text{hull}}} = \dfrac{350 \times 10^6}{3.693} $$
>
> $$ \boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 94.77 \text{ MPa}} $$

> **Step 5 — Safety check (1 mark):**
>
> $$ \sigma_{\max} = 94.77 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 175 \text{ MPa} \;\;\text{SAFE} $$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bottom Structure Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add corrosion allowance** $t_c$ in plate-thickness formulas. Marks are split equally between the rule calculation and the addition — losing $t_c$ costs 2 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up keelson and keel:** Students often call the keelson the "keel." The keel is **external**; the keelson is the **internal vertical plate**. Examiners specifically award 1 mark each for distinguishing these.
> 3. **Sign error in section modulus formula:** The flange is at distance $(d/2 - t_b/2)$ from the neutral axis — students often write $(d/2 + t_b/2)$ and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing only sketch, no labels:** Cross-section sketches without labeled dimensions and member names receive partial credit only. Always annotate.
> 5. **Stating "bilge keel resists bending":** Bilge keels are **roll-damping devices**, not load-bearing. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bottom Structure**

- ☐ **Bottom structure = hull's foundation**; transmits loads between sea, cargo, and side shell.
- ☐ **Five tiers:** plating → stiffeners → floors/girders → keelson/keel → hull girder.
- ☐ **Three framing systems:** Transverse / Longitudinal / Mixed — know when each is used.
- ☐ **Keel** is external; **Keelson** is internal vertical plate at centerline.
- ☐ **Garboard strake** is the first bottom strake next to keel — **always thicker**.
- ☐ **Bilge keels** are hydrodynamic, not structural — for **roll damping**.
- ☐ **Centerline keelson** + **side girders** + **floors** form the **double bottom**.
- ☐ **Plate thickness rule:** $t = 0.0155 \, s \sqrt{p} + t_c$.
- ☐ **Section modulus:** $Z_{\text{bottom}} = A_b \cdot (d - t_b) / 2$.
- ☐ **Sea water density** = 1025 kg/m³; **g** = 9.81 m/s².
- ☐ **Design pressure includes:** hydrostatic + dynamic (slamming) + cargo reaction.
- ☐ **Safety criterion:** $\sigma_{\max} \leq 0.9 \sigma_y$ for ULS, $\sigma_{\max} \leq 175$ MPa typical allowable.
- ☐ **Hull girder analogy:** Box beam with bottom plating + keel + keelson as lower flange.
- ☐ **Modern rule framework:** IACS CSR-B / DNV / LR / ABS Common Structural Rules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shell Plating and Framing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shell Plating and Framing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Shell Plating — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shell Plating** is the **outer skin (or envelope) of welded steel plating** that forms the watertight boundary of a ship's hull. It includes the **bottom plating, side plating, sheer strake, deck plating, and stern plating**, all continuously welded to one another along their edges to form a single, leak-proof shell capable of resisting hydrostatic pressure, slamming loads, cargo reactions, and global hull bending stresses.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the shell plating is the *primary load-bearing surface* of the vessel and is governed by classification society rules (e.g., **IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS**). The plate thickness is the **scantling** value, and the geometric arrangement of plates (buttocks, strakes, seams, butts) is standardized.

### 1.2 Framing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Framing** is the **internal skeleton** of the ship consisting of **stiffeners (frames, beams, girders, pillars) and floor plates**, fitted transverse and/or longitudinal to the shell and deck plating to:
> (a) Maintain the shape of the hull against hydrostatic and dynamic pressure,
> (b) Provide local buckling resistance to the plating, and
> (c) Transmit loads to the primary hull structure (keel, side shell, deck).

Framing converts the thin shell plating into a **stiffened plate assembly** — analogous to a reinforced concrete slab.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Egg Shell Analogy:**
> Think of an egg. The thin shell is brittle and easily crushed by a point load (your thumb). But the moment you put the egg into an egg-cup with curved supports, it can carry substantial load. Now, the eggshell is the **plating**, and the egg-cup is the **framing**. In a ship, framing is spaced so regularly that the plating is effectively supported everywhere — like a floor on joists. The **stiffeners (frames) act as the joists, and the plating acts as the floorboards**, distributing local pressure (e.g., sea pressure) to the stiffeners, which then pass it to the bigger primary members (girders, floors, keel, deck).

## 1.4 Standard Strake Names in Shell Plating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shell plating is divided horizontally into **strakes** (continuous fore-aft plating between seams) and vertically by **butt joints**. Standard naming (bottom-up):
> - **Keel plate** (centreline strake)
> - **Bottom plating / flat keel**
> - **Bilge plating** (curved transition region)
> - **Side plating**
> - **Sheer strake** (topmost side strake, at deck edge)
> - **Deck plating** (weather or strength deck)
> - **Stem plate** (forward, vertical)
> - **Stern plate / Transom plate** (aft)

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Plate steel grade | **Grade A / AH36 / DH36** | Yield strength **235 MPa – 355 MPa** |
| Plate thickness range | **4 mm – 50 mm** (typical merchant ship) | 80–100 mm for ice-class hulls |
| Frame spacing (transverse) | **500–800 mm** | Wider for large bulk carriers |
| Frame spacing (longitudinal) | **300–400 mm** | Tighter for slender plating |
| Frame depth (typical) | **100–600 mm** | Depends on span and load |
| Section modulus (bottom) | **≥ 100 cm³ per metre** | Bottom shell in single-skin tankers |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Function of Shell Plating

The shell plating performs **five** distinct structural roles:

1. **Watertight envelope** — resists direct hydrostatic pressure $p = \rho g h$.
2. **Longitudinal strength contributor** — the deck and bottom plating act as the *flanges* of the hull girder (cross-section), while side shell acts as the *web*.
3. **Transverse strength contributor** — local plate panels resist sea pressure via bending.
4. **Torsional stiffness provider** — closed box section resists twisting.
5. **Compartmentation** — defines tank boundaries and bulkhead edges.

## 2.2 Function of Framing

1. **Reduce unsupported span of plating** (to prevent buckling).
2. **Carry local loads** (e.g., sea pressure, cargo pressure, deck loads) to primary structure.
3. **Distribute concentrated loads** (e.g., pillar reactions, machinery feet).
4. **Maintain hull form** during construction and service.

## 2.3 Types of Framing Systems

> [!NOTE]
> Two main structural systems exist in modern ship design:

**A. Transverse Framing System**
- Frames spaced at regular intervals athwartships (e.g., every 600 mm).
- Main transverse members: **floors**, **side frames**, **deck beams**.
- Used in **bulk carriers, tankers, general cargo ships**.

**B. Longitudinal Framing System**
- Stiffeners run fore-aft; spaced tighter (300–400 mm).
- Transverse webs (floors/bulkheads) act as the primary transverse strength.
- Used in **container ships, naval vessels, large tankers** for higher longitudinal strength and reduced plate thickness.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam ready** and aligned with the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_y}}$ | Plate thickness (empirical) | mm |
| 3 | $t_{\min} = \dfrac{s}{100}\sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot \gamma}{0.5\sigma_y}}$ | Min. thickness per IRS rules (typical) | mm |
| 4 | $I_{\min} = \dfrac{s \cdot l^3}{C}$ | Min. moment of inertia of stiffener | cm⁴ |
| 5 | $Z_{\min} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_a}$ | Required section modulus | cm³ |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{C_1}$ | Bending moment of stiffener | N·mm |
| 7 | $b_{\text{eff}} = \alpha \cdot s$ | Effective breadth of plating (collaboration) | mm |
| 8 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{f_y}{F.S.}$ | Allowable stress | MPa |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_w}$ | Shear stress in web | MPa |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{s}{t}$ | Slenderness ratio of plate panel | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^2$ | Plate buckling stress (linear) | MPa |

**Where the standard variables are:**
- $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm)
- $l$ = stiffener span (mm)
- $p$ = design pressure (N/mm² or MPa)
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of steel
- $C, C_1$ = boundary-condition coefficients (e.g., 8, 10, 12)
- $F.S.$ = Factor of Safety (typically **1.43** for hull, **1.0** ultimate)
- $\alpha$ = effective breadth factor, typically **0.5–0.8** depending on aspect ratio
- $k$ = buckling coefficient (4.0 for simply supported long plate, 6.97 for clamped)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (0.3 for steel)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (**210,000 MPa** for mild steel)
- $f_y$ = yield stress (**235 MPa** Grade A, **315–355 MPa** Grade AH/DH)

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Logic of Plate Bending

**Why does the plate need framing?**

1. Hydrostatic pressure $p$ acts **perpendicular** to plating.
2. Plate (treated as a 2D panel) deflects as a **series of strips** between supports.
3. Without stiffeners, the unsupported span $s$ is huge → deflection and bending stress $\sigma = \dfrac{6M}{t^2}$ exceed yield.
4. Adding **frames at spacing $s$** reduces the unsupported span to manageable limits.
5. The plate + frame composite carries the pressure as an **orthotropic plate** with two principal stiffness directions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bending Moment on a Plate Panel Supported by Stiffeners

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular plating panel, dimensions $s$ (frame spacing) × $l$ (girders/floors spacing), simply supported on all four edges, loaded with uniformly distributed pressure $p$.

**Step 1 — Treat the panel as a beam strip of unit width:**

Take a unit-width strip of the plate between two adjacent stiffeners. The load on the strip is $q = p \cdot 1$ per unit length, and it spans between stiffeners separated by distance $s$.

**Step 2 — Apply simple beam theory:**

For a simply supported beam, maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span:

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{q \cdot s^2}{8} = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress in the plate:**

For a rectangular cross-section of unit width and thickness $t$, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \dfrac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}$$

So maximum bending stress:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2} = \dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to allowable stress and solve for thickness:**

Setting $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_a = \dfrac{f_y}{F.S.}$:

$$\dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2} = \sigma_a$$

$$t^2 = \dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 \sigma_a}$$

$$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{3 p}{4 \sigma_a}}$$

$$\boxed{t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_a}}}$$

where $k = \sqrt{3/4} \approx 0.866$ for simply supported conditions. With clamped edges or partial fixity, the coefficient reduces (e.g., $k \approx 0.5$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Section Modulus of a Stiffener with Attached Plating

**Setup:** A T-section stiffener consists of the stiffener web (depth $d$, thickness $t_w$) and flange (width $b_f$, thickness $t_f$), plus an **effective breadth** of plating $b_{\text{eff}}$ acting as the top flange.

**Step 1 — Effective breadth of plating:**

Per classification rules:

$$b_{\text{eff}} = \alpha \cdot s$$

where $\alpha$ depends on stiffener aspect ratio. For a typical long stiffener ($l/s > 5$), $\alpha \approx 0.5$ to $0.6$.

**Step 2 — Locate the neutral axis (NA) of the combined section:**

Let areas be: $A_1 = b_{\text{eff}} \cdot t$ (plating), $A_2 = t_f \cdot b_f$ (flange), $A_3 = d \cdot t_w$ (web).

Take datum at the bottom of the web. Centroidal distances from datum:

$$y_1 = d + t_f + t/2$$

$$y_2 = d + t_f/2$$

$$y_3 = d/2$$

**Step 3 — Neutral axis position:**

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{A_1 y_1 + A_2 y_2 + A_3 y_3}{A_1 + A_2 + A_3}$$

**Step 4 — Moment of inertia (parallel axis theorem):**

$$I = \sum \left( I_{\text{self}} + A_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \right)$$

For each rectangular part: $I_{\text{self}} = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$.

**Step 5 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{\bar{y}_{\max}}$$

where $\bar{y}_{\max}$ is the distance from NA to the **outermost fibre** (typically the bottom of the web, or the top of plating — whichever is larger).

**Step 6 — Design check:**

$$Z_{\text{provided}} \geq Z_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{design}}}{\sigma_a}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frame Spacing from Plate Buckling Criterion

**Setup:** A plate is subject to in-plane compression (from hull girder bending). To prevent elastic buckling, the slenderness ratio must satisfy:

$$\sigma_{\text{cr}} \geq \sigma_a$$

**Step 1 — Critical buckling stress for a long, simply supported plate:**

$$\sigma_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12 (1 - \nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^2$$

For long simply supported plate, $k = 4.0$. With $E = 210{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$12(1 - 0.09) = 10.92$$

$$\sigma_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \times 210{,}000}{10.92} \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 759{,}000 \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Set $\sigma_{\text{cr}} = \sigma_a$ and solve for $s$:**

$$s_{\max} = t \sqrt{\dfrac{759{,}000}{\sigma_a}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**

If $t = 12$ mm, $\sigma_a = 160$ MPa (allowable for Grade A):

$$s_{\max} = 12 \sqrt{\dfrac{759{,}000}{160}} = 12 \sqrt{4743.75} \approx 12 \times 68.87 \approx 826 \text{ mm}$$

So $s \leq 826$ mm to prevent buckling — a typical transverse frame spacing of **800 mm** is consistent.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A bottom shell plate of a small cargo vessel is 12 mm thick, supported by transverse frames spaced at $s = 600$ mm. The effective span between floors is $l = 4$ m. Sea pressure at the bottom is $p = 0.03$ MPa. Yield stress $f_y = 235$ MPa, F.S. = 1.43. Compute (a) allowable stress, (b) bending moment on a frame, (c) required section modulus.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_a = \dfrac{f_y}{F.S.} = \dfrac{235}{1.43} \approx 164.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Bending moment on a frame (treated as a beam on rigid supports):**

Load per unit length on the frame:

$$q = p \cdot s = 0.03 \times 600 = 18 \text{ N/mm}$$

For simply supported span $l = 4$ m = 4000 mm:

$$M = \dfrac{q \cdot l^2}{8} = \dfrac{18 \times 4000^2}{8} = \dfrac{18 \times 16 \times 10^6}{8} = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 36 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (c) — Required section modulus:**

$$Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_a} = \dfrac{36 \times 10^6}{164.3} \approx 219{,}110 \text{ mm}^3 = 219.1 \text{ cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frame must be selected (e.g., a T-bar or bulb plate) such that its **section modulus plus effective plating breadth gives $Z \geq 219.1$ cm³**. This is a typical KTU board answer — showing the **chain: pressure → load → moment → modulus → selection**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Plate/Frame Configurations

| Location | Plate thickness $t$ | Frame spacing $s$ | Frame type | Special consideration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Keel plate | **Thickest in ship** (e.g., 1.2× bottom) | n/a (continuous) | Centreline girder | Sits in drydock |
| Bottom shell | 12–20 mm | 600–800 mm | Transverse | Slamming loads (forward) |
| Bilge | 14–22 mm | 600–800 mm | Transverse, tapered | Curved — high stress |
| Side shell | 10–18 mm | 600–800 mm | Transverse | Hydrostatic |
| Sheer strake | 12–18 mm | At every frame | Connects to deck | Doubler often added |
| Strength deck | 12–25 mm | 600–800 mm (T) or 300–400 mm (L) | T or L | Top flange of hull girder |
| Forward region | **+1.5–2 mm** extra | Tighter | Increased | Slamming, bow wave |
| Aft region | Normal | Normal | Normal | Vibration from propeller |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hull Structural Hierarchy (Load Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Pressure p on Shell Plating] --> B[Plating Panel bending]
    B --> C[Stiffener / Frame reaction]
    C --> D[Girder or Floor reaction]
    D --> E[Keel, Side Shell, Deck reaction]
    E --> F[Hull Girder global bending]
    F --> G[Longitudinal Strength Member]

    A1[Hydrostatic load] --> A
    A2[Cargo load] --> A
    A3[Slamming load] --> A

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000

    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000

    style G fill:#B22222,stroke:#000000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows the **load cascade** — from sea pressure acting on the plating, the load travels through plate → frames → girders/floors → primary hull members → global hull girder. This is the **structural load path** that examiners love to ask.

## 4.2 Cross-Section Block Diagram: Midship Section Showing All Framing Members

```mermaid

        K[KEEL PLATE] --> BP[BOTTOM PLATING]

        F1[FLOORS] -.-> K
        F1 -.-> BP
        FR1[SIDE FRAMES] -.-> SS1

        DB1[DECK BEAMS] -.-> D1
```

## 4.3 Plate Panel Behaviour — Effective Breadth Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Uniform Pressure p on Plating] --> EP[Effective Plating Breadth b_eff]
    EP --> NA[Combined Section NA location]
    NA --> I[Combined Moment of Inertia]
    I --> Z[Section Modulus Z]
    Z --> CHK{Z_provided >= Z_required}
    CHK -- YES --> OK[DESIGN SAFE]
    CHK -- NO --> REV[Increase stiffener size or reduce spacing s]
    style P fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
