# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define shell plating. List any four strakes of a ship's shell plating with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Shell plating** is the outer skin of welded steel plates forming the watertight envelope of the hull, designed to resist hydrostatic pressure and contribute to global longitudinal strength.

| Strake | Function |
|---|---|
| (i) **Keel plate** | Centreline strake; transmits global bending to dry-dock blocks; thickest plate. |
| (ii) **Bottom plating** | Carries hydrostatic pressure; lower flange of hull girder. |
| (iii) **Bilge plating** | Curved transition plate between bottom and side; handles slamming and concentrated stresses. |
| (iv) **Sheer strake** | Top strake of side shell; connects side to deck plating; upper chord contributor to hull girder. |

*(3 marks — full credit for definition + 4 strakes with one-line functions)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal framing systems? State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|---|---|---|
| **Stiffener direction** | Frames run athwartships (side to side) | Stiffeners run fore-aft |
| **Frame spacing** | Wider (500–800 mm) | Tighter (300–400 mm) |
| **Plate thickness** | Greater | Can be reduced |
| **Use** | Bulk carriers, tankers, general cargo | Container ships, naval vessels, large tankers |
| **Advantage** | Simpler construction, easier access for inspection | Higher longitudinal strength, lighter scantlings, smoother hull |

*(3 marks — clear tabular comparison + one advantage each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7**. The student answers **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **shell plating arrangement** of a typical single-skin merchant ship, identifying the strakes from keel to sheer. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A **bottom shell plate** of thickness **15 mm** is supported by **transverse frames at 700 mm spacing**. The span between floors is **4.5 m**. The maximum sea pressure on the plate is **$p = 0.025$ N/mm²**. Take yield stress $f_y = 235$ MPa and factor of safety = **1.43**. Compute:
   (i) Allowable bending stress
   (ii) Bending moment per frame
   (iii) Required section modulus of the frame
   (iv) Comment on the suitability of a standard **T-section 200×10 + 100×12 mm** with $Z = 220$ cm³. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Shell plating arrangement (single-skin ship, from keel to sheer):**

> **Sketch (textual block representation):**
>
> ```
> DECK STRINGER  ============================  ←  Deck plating
>                                          
>   |   DECK BEAMS  (athwartships, every 600 mm)
>   |
>   v
>   SHEER STRake   ============================   ←  Top strake
>   SIDE STRake 2  ============================
>   SIDE STRake 1  ============================   ←  Side shell
>   BILGE STRake   ============================   ←  Curved region
>   BOTTOM STRake  ============================
>   BOTTOM STRake  ============================   ←  Bottom shell
>   KEEL PLATE     ============================   ←  Centreline
>                                           
>   |    FLOORS (athwartships, every frame space)
>   v
>   CENTRE GIRDER or VERTICAL KEEL
> ```

**Naming convention:**
1. **Keel plate** – centreline strake, 1.2× bottom thickness.
2. **Bottom strakes** (port & starboard) – flat or slightly cambered.
3. **Bilge strake** – curved transition; carries slamming loads in the forward region.
4. **Side strakes** – vertical side shell; 2–4 strakes typical.
5. **Sheer strake** – topmost strake; connects to deck plating.
6. **Deck plating** – weather deck; top flange of hull girder.

**Construction features:**
- Plates arranged in **strakes** (longitudinal runs) and **butt joints** (transverse joints between adjacent plate ends).
- **Efficient dispensing** (also called *British Standard dispensing* or *isometrical dispensing*) ensures the **butt joints are staggered** to avoid continuous transverse weak lines.
- **Sheer strake** and **keel plate** are typically **one plate wider** (called *strake-in* or *doubler*).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $t = 15$ mm
- $s = 700$ mm
- $l = 4.5$ m = 4500 mm
- $p = 0.025$ N/mm² = 0.025 MPa
- $f_y = 235$ MPa
- F.S. = 1.43

**(i) Allowable bending stress:**

$$\sigma_a = \dfrac{f_y}{F.S.} = \dfrac{235}{1.43} \approx 164.34 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Bending moment per frame:**

Load per unit length on frame:

$$q = p \cdot s = 0.025 \times 700 = 17.5 \text{ N/mm}$$

For simply supported span (worst case):

$$M = \dfrac{q \cdot l^2}{8} = \dfrac{17.5 \times 4500^2}{8}$$

$$M = \dfrac{17.5 \times 20.25 \times 10^6}{8} = \dfrac{354.375 \times 10^6}{8}$$

$$M = 44.297 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 44.30 \text{ kN·m}$$

**(iii) Required section modulus:**

$$Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_a} = \dfrac{44.297 \times 10^6}{164.34} \approx 269{,}560 \text{ mm}^3 = 269.6 \text{ cm}^3$$

**(iv) Comment on suitability of T 200×10 + 100×12 with $Z = 220$ cm³:**

$$Z_{\text{provided}} = 220 \text{ cm}^3 < Z_{\text{req}} = 269.6 \text{ cm}^3$$

**→ The given T-section is NOT suitable.** A heavier section (e.g., **T 250×12 + 120×15**, or **HP 260 bulb plate**) with $Z \geq 270$ cm³ must be selected. Alternatively, frame spacing can be reduced from 700 mm to ~600 mm.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **types of stiffeners used as ship frames** with neat sketches, listing the relative merits of each. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum plate thickness formula** for a plate panel subject to uniform lateral pressure, simply supported on all four edges, and state the assumptions made. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Common Stiffener Profiles Used in Shipbuilding:**

| Profile | Sketch (text) | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat bar** | `───` | Cheapest, easy to fit | Low strength/weight ratio |
| **Angle bar (L)** | `⌐` | Easy to fit, weldable one side | Asymmetric — causes eccentricity |
| **T-section** | `⊥` | Symmetric, high moment of inertia, efficient | More expensive than L |
| **Bulb plate** | `◀` | High $I$ and $Z$ per kg, smooth, no cavitation | Premium cost |
| **HP (Half-round)** | `◐` | Used in ice-class; high buckling resistance | Specialized application |

**Selection criteria (in order of priority):**
1. Section modulus $Z$ requirement (most critical).
2. Minimum radius of gyration $r$ (stiffness criterion).
3. Web depth-to-thickness ratio (web buckling).
4. Cost and weldability.
5. Connection type (welded bracket vs. end-fillet).

**Standard profile sources:** classification society rule books (e.g., IRS Pt 3 Ch 4) tabulate standard profiles like **T 100×10+75×12** with associated $A$, $I$, $Z$ values.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Derivation of Minimum Plate Thickness:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Plate is **thin, isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic**.
2. Plate panel is **simply supported on all four edges**.
3. Load $p$ is **uniformly distributed and lateral** (perpendicular to plate plane).
4. Plate deflections are **small** (linear theory).
5. **Kirchhoff-Love plate theory** applies (neglecting transverse shear deformation).

**Setup:** Rectangular plate, dimensions $s$ (short span) × $l$ (long span), with $l > s$, simply supported.

**Step 1 — Effective width strip method:**

Because $l > s$, the plate bends primarily across the short span. The plate can be analysed as a **series of beam strips** of unit width, each spanning between two adjacent stiffeners (span $s$).

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment per unit width:**

For a simply supported beam of unit width under UDL $p$:

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8} \text{ (per unit width)}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress in plate:**

For a unit-width plate of thickness $t$, the section modulus is $Z = t^2/6$:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{p s^2 / 8}{t^2/6} = \dfrac{6 p s^2}{8 t^2} = \dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to allowable stress and solve:**

$$\sigma \leq \sigma_a$$

$$\dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2} \leq \sigma_a$$

$$t^2 \geq \dfrac{3 p s^2}{4 \sigma_a}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\min} = \dfrac{s}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{3 p}{\sigma_a}} = s \cdot 0.866 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_a}}}$$

**Numerical validation:** If $p = 0.025$ MPa, $s = 700$ mm, $\sigma_a = 164.34$ MPa:

$$t_{\min} = 700 \times 0.866 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.025}{164.34}} = 606.2 \times 0.01234 \approx 7.48 \text{ mm}$$

So minimum thickness ≈ **7.5 mm**. Practically, plates of **10–12 mm** are used in this region for corrosion margin and impact resistance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Shell Plating & Framing Questions (loss of 2–4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units**: Pressure in MPa vs N/mm² vs kN/m² — they are equal, but students often forget the conversion of $M$ to **N·mm** when $Z$ is in mm³. Always state: $Z = M/\sigma$ in consistent units.
> 2. **Forgetting factor of safety**: Allowable stress is **NOT** $f_y$ directly. Always divide by F.S. (typically 1.43 for merchant ships, 1.0 for ultimate strength).
> 3. **Neglecting effective breadth of plating**: A frame does NOT work alone — it works **with attached plating** as a composite section. Forgetting $b_{\text{eff}}$ leads to undersized sections.
> 4. **Confusing span**: The frame span $l$ is **NOT** the ship length; it is the **distance between primary supports** (floors, bulkheads, side girders). The frame spacing $s$ is the perpendicular distance between adjacent frames.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention in section modulus**: $Z$ must use the **maximum** distance from NA to the outermost fibre — not just the bottom.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch**: In 7-mark questions, the KTU valuation key **mandates a labelled sketch**. A student who only writes text loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Plate vs. frame confusion**: Don't answer "frame design" when asked for "plate thickness" — these are TWO different calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shell Plating & Framing**

- ☐ **Shell plating** = outer hull skin; **strakes** (fore-aft runs) + **butt joints** (transverse).
- ☐ **Strakes from bottom up**: Keel → Bottom → Bilge → Side → Sheer → Deck.
- ☐ **Keel plate is the thickest** in the hull; sheer strake is also reinforced.
- ☐ **Framing** = internal skeleton; primary function is to **reduce unsupported span** of plating.
- ☐ **Transverse framing** = frames athwartships; simpler, used in bulk carriers/tankers.
- ☐ **Longitudinal framing** = stiffeners fore-aft; higher strength, used in container ships/naval.
- ☐ **Plate bending** = strip method; $M = p s^2 / 8$ per unit width; $t_{\min} = 0.866 s \sqrt{p/\sigma_a}$.
- ☐ **Frame bending** = beam on supports; $M = q l^2 / C$ ($C = 8$ SS, $10$ propped, $12$ fixed).
- ☐ **Section modulus** required: $Z_{\text{req}} = M / \sigma_a$.
- ☐ **Effective breadth** of plating: $b_{\text{eff}} = \alpha s$ (typically 0.5–0.8).
- ☐ **Allowable stress** = yield stress / factor of safety (FS = 1.43 for hull girder, 1.0 for ultimate).
- ☐ **Standard profiles**: flat bar → angle → T → bulb plate (HP) (increasing strength-to-weight).
- ☐ **Plate buckling** check: $\sigma_{\text{cr}} = 759{,}000 (t/s)^2$ for SS long plate in steel.
- ☐ **Stiffener spacing** typical: 500–800 mm (T), 300–400 mm (L).
- ☐ **Forward region** gets **+1.5–2 mm extra thickness** for slamming.
- ☐ **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = s/t$ — controls plate buckling vulnerability.
- ☐ **Slab longitudinal** = full length between bulkheads.
- ☐ **Always draw a labelled sketch** in 7-mark questions (KTU valuation rule).
- ☐ **Remember to convert units**: 1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m².

---

**End of Module 3 — Shell Plating and Framing Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Decks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In naval architecture, a **Deck** is defined as a horizontal (or nearly horizontal) plating system forming the permanent covering of a compartment or level in a ship, acting simultaneously as a transverse strength member, a platform for accommodation, cargo stowage, machinery mounting, and a weather-tight shell for the hull. Structurally, the deck behaves as the **top flange of the ship's hull girder**, while the keel and bottom shell form the bottom flange, and the side shell plating acts as the web. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, "Decks" refers specifically to the **structural design of deck plating, deck longitudinals, deck girders, effective breadth of deck as a strength member, and the section modulus contribution of the deck to the hull girder**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Storey Building Plate Imagine a long, hollow rectangular tube floating on water — that is essentially a ship. Now imagine placing multiple horizontal "floors" inside this tube, just like the floors of a tall apartment building. Each floor: - Prevents the walls of the tube from collapsing **inward** (transverse strength), - Carries its own weight plus cargo/equipment placed on it (local strength), - Together with the top of the tube, forms an "I"-beam shape that resists the ship from **hogging** (sagging at the ends) and **sagging** (bending in the middle). These floors are exactly what **decks** are in a ship. The **main deck** (top-most continuous weather-tight deck) is the most important because it is the upper chord of the ship's giant **hogging/sagging resisting girder**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Reference Metrics - **Rule-based plating thickness** is governed by classification society formulae using **stiffener spacing** (mm) and **design pressure** (kN/m²). - **Corrosion Allowance (c.a.)** is typically **0.5 mm to 3.0 mm** depending on plate location and vessel service. - **Section Modulus** of hull at deck is measured in **cm³**, and must exceed the value demanded by the relevant Load Line / Classification Rules. - The **Modulus of Elasticity (E) for mild shipbuilding steel** is universally taken as **E = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 205 GPa) in KTU problems unless stated otherwise. ### 1.4 Important Nomenclature of Decks (KTU Frequently Tested) > [!NOTE] > The following terms appear regularly in KTU Part A short-answer questions (3 marks each). | Term | Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Strength Deck** | The deck that contributes most effectively to the longitudinal strength of the hull girder. Usually the **uppermost continuous weather-tight deck**. | | **Weather Deck** | The deck exposed to sea and weather; the uppermost complete deck. | | **Poop Deck** | A short raised deck at the **stern (aft)**, above the main deck. | | **Forecastle Deck** | A short raised deck at the **bow (forward)**, above the main deck. | | **Superstructure Deck** | A deck on top of the superstructure (e.g., boat deck, navigation deck). | | **Tween Deck** | An intermediate horizontal deck between the main deck and inner bottom, used to subdivide cargo holds. | | **Platform Deck** | A partial deck (not full breadth) used locally to support machinery or boilers. | | **Strength Deck Plating** | The deck plating worked together with longitudinals to form the **upper flange** of the hull girder. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Role of a Deck (The "Why" and "How") A deck in a ship is **not** just a floor. It performs **four simultaneous engineering duties**, and KTU questions often test which duty dominates under a given condition. 1. **Transverse Strength Member** — Acts as a horizontal diaphragm resisting hydrostatic pressure on side shell plating. Supported by **deck transverses** and girders. 2. **Local Load-Bearing Platform** — Carries local loads (cattle, cargo, crew, equipment). Loads are transmitted to **deck longitudinals → deck girders → pillars → bottom**. 3. **Longitudinal Strength Contributor (Hull Girder Flange)** — The deck plating, with attached longitudinals, acts as the **upper flange** of the entire hull girder resisting **still-water bending moment (SBM)** and **wave bending moment (WBM)**. 4. **Compartmentalization / Safety** — Together with watertight bulkheads, decks provide **reserve buoyancy** if the hull is breached (a key concept in subdivision and damage stability). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the formulae below — they appear in nearly every Part B question on Decks. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Deck plating thickness (IRS / Lloyd's rule) | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{225} + c.a.$ (mm) | $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm); $p$ = design pressure (kN/m²); $c.a.$ = corrosion allowance (mm) | | 2 | Design head of water (deck pressure) | $p = 9.81 \cdot h \cdot \rho_{\text{sea}}$ ≈ $10.05 \cdot h$ (kN/m²) | $h$ = head of water (m); $1.025$ t/m³ for sea | | 3 | Section modulus of a rectangular plate strip | $SM = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$ (mm³) | $b$ = width of strip (mm); $t$ = thickness (mm) | | 4 | Moment of inertia of plate strip | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ (mm⁴) | $b$ = width (mm); $t$ = thickness (mm) | | 5 | Effective breadth of deck flange | $b_{\text{eff}} = \text{min}(B,\ \text{actual width contributing})$ (mm) | $B$ = ship breadth (mm) | | 6 | Section modulus of hull girder at deck | $SM_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{NA about NA}}}{y_{\text{NA to deck}}}$ | cm³ units typically | | 7 | Pillar (stanchion) slenderness check | $\lambda = \dfrac{L}{k}$, with $k = \dfrac{d}{4}$ for circular | $L$ = unsupported length; $d$ = diameter | | 8 | Allowable load on a pillar (Euler) | $P = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot E \cdot I}{L^{2}}$ (N) | $E$ = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm² | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shipbuilders** use the rule-based plating thickness formula directly to cut steel plates in the workshop — it determines procurement cost. - **Naval Architects** use the **effective breadth** concept to compute the section modulus of the hull girder, which is then compared against the **required section modulus** from Classification Rules. If $SM_{\text{actual}} \ge SM_{\text{required}}$, the design is accepted; otherwise, plate thickness or longitudinals must be increased. - **Surveyors** check deck plating for wastage (corrosion); the rule thickness minus corrosion allowance sets the **rejection limit** during classification surveys. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Deck Plating Thickness Formula (Plate on Closely Spaced Stiffeners) **Assumption (per KTU/IRS convention):** The deck plating acts as a continuous plate supported on closely spaced parallel stiffeners. Under uniform pressure $p$, the maximum bending moment per unit width of plating is: $$M = \dfrac{p \cdot s^{2}}{12}$$ where $s$ is the spacing of the supporting stiffeners (mm) and $p$ is in N/mm² if $s$ is in mm. Converting pressure from kN/m² to N/mm²: $$p_{\text{(N/mm²)}} = \dfrac{p_{\text{(kN/m²)}}}{10^{6}}$$ The maximum bending stress in the plate is: $$\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M}{(t^{2}/6)} \quad \text{where } Z = \dfrac{1 \cdot t^{2}}{6} \text{ per unit width}$$ Equating the bending stress to the **allowable bending stress** $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ (≈ **175 N/mm²** for mild steel, i.e., **175 MPa**): $$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{6M}{t^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^{2}}{12 \cdot t^{2}} = \dfrac{p \cdot s^{2}}{2 t^{2}}$$ Solving for $t$: $$t^{2} = \dfrac{p \cdot s^{2}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \sigma_{\text{allow}}}}$$ Substituting $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 175$ N/mm²: $$t = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p_{\text{(N/mm²)}}}{350}} = \dfrac{s \sqrt{p_{\text{(kN/m²)}}}}{350 \cdot 10^{3/2}} \cdot 1000$$ The empirical rule coefficient **225** in the denominator (used in IRS/Lloyd's formula) is obtained from $\sqrt{2 \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot 10^{6}} = \sqrt{350 \times 10^{6}} \approx 18721$. The empirical constant **225** in the IRS form arises after substituting standard sea-pressure values and a practical safety factor — yielding the final rule-based form: $$\boxed{\, t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{225} + c.a.\, (\text{mm})\,}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Deck Plating Thickness (KTU Standard 7-Mark Sub-Question) **Problem:** A deck of a cargo ship is supported by longitudinals spaced **600 mm** apart. The design pressure on the deck is **17.5 kN/m²**. If the corrosion allowance is **2.0 mm**, compute the required thickness of the deck plating using the rule formula. Take allowable bending stress = **175 N/mm²**. **Solution:** Given: - $s = 600$ mm - $p = 17.5$ kN/m² - $c.a. = 2.0$ mm - $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 175$ N/mm² **Step 1:** Compute the as-built (rule) thickness using the empirical formula: $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{225} + c.a.$$ $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{600 \cdot \sqrt{17.5}}{225} + 2.0$$ $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{600 \cdot 4.1833}{225} + 2.0$$ $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{2509.98}{225} + 2.0 = 11.155 + 2.0 = 13.155 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Verify using the rigorous derivation: $$p_{\text{N/mm²}} = \dfrac{17.5}{10^{6}} = 1.75 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N/mm²}$$ $$t_{\text{theory}} = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \sigma_{\text{allow}}}} = 600 \sqrt{\dfrac{1.75 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 175}}$$ $$t_{\text{theory}} = 600 \sqrt{\dfrac{1.75 \times 10^{-5}}{350}} = 600 \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$t_{\text{theory}} = 600 \times 2.236 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1342 \text{ mm}$$ This bare theoretical value is too thin for shipbuilding — empirical rule factors add safety + corrosion allowance, giving the practical **~13 mm** result. **Step 3:** Adopt next higher standard plate (per IRS plate schedule, in mm): **14 mm** or **15 mm**. **Final Answer:** Adopt deck plate thickness = **15 mm** (standard next size above 13.16 mm). --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Effective Breadth of Deck Flange (KTU 7-Mark Sub-Question) **Problem:** The midship section of a ship has breadth $B = 20$ m and depth $D = 12$ m. The deck plating is 14 mm thick, fitted with longitudinals of total cross-sectional area $A_L = 120$ cm² on each side of the centreline. Compute the effective sectional area of the deck acting as the upper flange of the hull girder. Take efficiency factor $\eta = 0.85$. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Total effective breadth at deck level is taken equal to the **ship breadth**: $$b_{\text{eff}} = B = 20 \text{ m} = 20\,000 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Area of deck plating alone: $$A_{\text{plate}} = b_{\text{eff}} \times t_{\text{plate}} = 20\,000 \times 14 = 280\,000 \text{ mm}^{2} = 2800 \text{ cm}^{2}$$ **Step 3:** Add the longitudinals' area (both sides of centreline): $$A_{\text{total, gross}} = 2800 + 120 = 2920 \text{ cm}^{2}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the efficiency (effective breadth) factor $\eta$: $$A_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times A_{\text{total, gross}} = 0.85 \times 2920 = 2482 \text{ cm}^{2}$$ **Step 5:** The centroid of this effective area lies at mid-thickness of the deck plate, i.e., $y = D/2 + t/2$ from the keel, or in the NA-frame: $$\bar{y}_{\text{deck}} = 6.0 + 0.007 \approx 6.007 \text{ m above keel}$$ **Final Answer:** Effective flange area at deck = **2482 cm²**, with its centroid lying at approximately **6.0 m** above the keel. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Treat the Deck as Which Member) | Loading Condition | Deck Behaviour to Assume | Relevant Formula | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Side shell under external sea pressure | Deck acts as a **transverse ring frame flange** | $t = s\sqrt{p}/225 + c.a.$ | | Local cargo or heavy point load on deck | Deck plating acts as **plate on closely spaced stiffeners** | Local bending + shear check | | Ship in still water or waves (hog/sag) | Deck = **upper flange of hull girder** | $SM = I/y$ computed for whole section | | Open hatch (no plating) | Deck loses flange continuity; net section must be **reduced** | Apply hatch-correction factor | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Path Through a Deck ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Local Load on Deck
Cargo / Crew / Snow"] B["Deck Plating
Thickness t computed by rule"] C["Deck Longitudinals
Spaced at s mm"] D["Deck Transverse / Beam
Spaced at larger pitch"] E["Deck Girder
Longitudinal box girder under deck"] F["Pillars / Stanchions
Vertical supports to bottom"] G["Bottom Shell + Keel
Lower flange of hull girder"] H["Side Shell
Acts as web of hull girder"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G B -. Upper Flange .-> H G -. Lower Flange .-> H ``` **Interpretation:** - The **left branch** (solid arrows) shows the **local load path**: load → plating → longitudinal → transverse → girder → pillar → bottom. - The **right branch** (dotted arrows) shows the **hull-girder action**: deck (upper flange) ↔ side shell (web) ↔ bottom (lower flange), all resisting global bending. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid — Cross-Sectional Sketch of Hull Girder (Text Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Hull_Girder_Cross_Section D["Deck Plating
Upper Flange
Section Modulus = SM_d"] SS["Side Shell
Acts as Web
Vertical Depth = D"] BS["Bottom Shell
Lower Flange
Includes Keel"] end WBM["Wave Bending Moment
Sagging / Hogging"] SBM["Still Water Bending Moment"] WBM --> Hull_Girder_Cross_Section SBM --> Hull_Girder_Cross_Section ``` **Note on Physical Sketch (rendered textually for KTU board clarity):** A typical midship section viewed from the front shows: ``` ___________________________ | | <-- DECK PLATING (upper flange) | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~| | | <-- Tween deck (optional) | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~| | | | | <-- SIDE SHELL (web) | | |___________________________| <-- BOTTOM (lower flange) \\_______________________// Keel plate at base ``` The neutral axis (NA) lies somewhere near amidships between deck and bottom (often slightly below mid-depth for tankers, near mid-depth for bulk carriers). --- ### 4.3 Mermaid — Deck Plating Stiffener Arrangement (Top-Down View) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Main Ship Centreline CL"] B["Deck Longitudinal L1"] C["Deck Longitudinal L2"] D["Deck Longitudinal L3"] E["Deck Longitudinal Ln"] F["Spacing s between
adjacent longitudinals
(used in t = s.sqrt p / 225 + ca)"] A --- B B --- F F --- C C --- D D --- E ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each longitudinal acts as a **support line** for the plating. The shorter the spacing $s$, the thinner the plating may be for the same pressure — but more longitudinals = more welding and weight. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Strength Deck" and explain why it is structurally the most important deck in a ship.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A *strength deck* is the uppermost continuous weather-tight deck that acts as the **top flange of the ship's hull girder**, working in conjunction with the keel and bottom shell plating to resist longitudinal bending. - **Structural importance (2 marks):** It carries the maximum compressive or tensile longitudinal stress when the ship hogs or sags in a seaway. Its plating thickness, longitudinal spacing, and effective breadth together determine the **section modulus** of the hull girder at midship — the single most important strength parameter of the vessel. Increasing strength-deck thickness is the most efficient way to raise hull-girder section modulus. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List any six different types of decks found in a modern cargo ship with one-line functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each for six points):** 1. **Main / Strength Deck** – upper flange of hull girder. 2. **Poop Deck** – raised deck aft, provides reserve buoyancy and shelter. 3. **Forecastle Deck** – raised deck forward, breaks waves, provides buoyancy. 4. **Tween Deck** – intermediate deck subdividing cargo holds. 5. **Superstructure Deck** – deck above the superstructure, used for boats/navigation. 6. **Platform Deck** – partial deck supporting boilers or engines. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Students often **omit the corrosion allowance (c.a.)** at the end — losing **0.5 to 1 mark**. > - Many forget to **convert kN/m² → N/mm²** correctly when cross-checking with the theoretical derivation — losing another mark. > - Final answer **must be rounded UP** to the next standard plate thickness per IRS/Lloyd's schedule (e.g., 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 mm) — partial credit is lost if you stop at the calculated value. > - For section modulus problems, students frequently confuse the **section modulus of a flange alone** with the **section modulus of the entire hull girder** — read the question carefully. --- #### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose ONE) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3 Internal Choice]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* A deck of a passenger-cum-cargo vessel is subjected to a design head of water equivalent to **2.5 m**. The longitudinal spacing is **750 mm**. Calculate the required thickness of the deck plating using the empirical rule. Take **c.a. = 1.5 mm**, **σ_allow = 175 N/mm²**, **ρ_sea = 1.025 t/m³**. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain with a neat sketch how the **strength deck acts as the upper flange of the hull girder** while the ship is sagging in still water. Show the direction of stresses in the deck and bottom plating and derive the relationship between section modulus and bending moment. --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks) **Given:** - Head $h = 2.5$ m - Stiffener spacing $s = 750$ mm - $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 175$ N/mm² - $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ $= 1025$ kg/m³ - $c.a. = 1.5$ mm **Step 1 — Compute design pressure $p$ (kN/m²):** $$p = \rho_{\text{sea}} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.5$$ $$p = 25\,137.4 \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 25.14 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$ *(Accept $g = 10$ for simplification → $p = 25.625$ kN/m². Both are acceptable in KTU.)* **Step 2 — Apply the rule formula:** $$t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{225} + c.a.$$ $$t = \dfrac{750 \cdot \sqrt{25.14}}{225} + 1.5$$ $$t = \dfrac{750 \cdot 5.014}{225} + 1.5$$ $$t = \dfrac{3760.5}{225} + 1.5 = 16.71 + 1.5 = 18.21 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Adopt next standard plate thickness:** Per IRS plate schedule → choose **20 mm** deck plating. ✅ **Final Answer:** Deck plate thickness to be adopted = **20 mm** (against the calculated requirement of 18.21 mm). --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks) **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** When a ship **sags** (i.e., supported amidships by a wave crest, with bow and stern in wave troughs), the **hogging** curvature is reversed; the hull bends **concave-up**, putting the **deck in tension** and the **bottom plating in compression**. This is one of the two critical still-water bending conditions. In this state, the hull behaves as a simply supported beam of length $L$ (ship length) loaded by buoyancy (upward at centre) and weight (downward at ends). The internal bending moment $M$ at midship is: $$M = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{8} \quad \text{(approximately)}$$ where $W$ is the total weight displacement distributed along the length. **Mathematical Relationship (4 marks):** By Euler–Bernoulli beam theory, the maximum bending stress in the **upper flange** (deck) at distance $y_d$ from the Neutral Axis (NA) is: $$\sigma_d = \dfrac{M \cdot y_d}{I}$$ where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the whole hull cross-section about the neutral axis. The **Section Modulus** at the deck is defined as: $$SM_d = \dfrac{I}{y_d} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_d = \dfrac{M}{SM_d}$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\, SM_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}\,}$$ This is the most fundamental equation in hull-girder design. The actual $SM_d$ from the cross-section (plating + longitudinals + effective breadth) must equal or exceed $SM_{\text{required}}$. **Sketch (drawn on board):** ``` DECK (Tension + + + + +) ← upper flange in tension | | ↕ y_d | --- NA --- ← Neutral Axis | | ↕ y_b | BOTTOM (Compression - - - -) ← lower flange in compression ``` ✅ **Marks Distribution:** 3 for concept + sketch; 4 for formula derivation with proper labels. --- #### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* The deck of a bulk carrier is supported by longitudinals spaced at **650 mm** with a design pressure of **15 kN/m²**. Corrosion allowance is **1.0 mm**. Determine: 1. The required deck plating thickness by rule formula. 2. The as-built (gross) moment of inertia and section modulus of a **1 m wide strip** of this plate (neglect longitudinals). **(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **classification of decks** in a ship based on their function. Also, state **three design considerations** that govern the choice of deck plating thickness in ship design. --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks) **Given:** $s = 650$ mm; $p = 15$ kN/m²; $c.a. = 1.0$ mm. **Part 1 — Plate thickness (3 marks):** $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{650 \cdot \sqrt{15}}{225} + 1.0$$ $$t_{\text{rule}} = \dfrac{650 \cdot 3.873}{225} + 1.0 = \dfrac{2517.45}{225} + 1.0$$ $$t_{\text{rule}} = 11.19 + 1.0 = 12.19 \text{ mm}$$ Adopt standard **14 mm** plate. **Part 2 — Section properties of 1 m strip (4 marks):** For $b = 1000$ mm and $t = 14$ mm (as-built): $$I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 14^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 2744}{12} = 228\,667 \text{ mm}^{4}$$ $$SM = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 14^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 196}{6} = 32\,667 \text{ mm}^{3}$$ ✅ **Final Answers:** $t = 14$ mm; $I = 2.29 \times 10^{5}$ mm⁴; $SM = 3.27 \times 10^{4}$ mm³. --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks) **Classification of Decks (4 marks — with diagram):** ```mermaid flowchart TB D["Decks in a Ship"] D --> E1["By Location
Main, Poop, Forecastle,
Tween, Superstructure"] D --> E2["By Function
Strength, Weather,
Cargo, Platform"] D --> E3["By Continuity
Continuous, Partial,
Detached Superstructure"] ``` **Three Design Considerations Governing Plate Thickness (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Design Head of Water / Cargo Load:** Higher expected head → larger $p$ → thicker plate (since $t \propto \sqrt{p}$). 2. **Longitudinal Spacing $s$:** Larger spacing between stiffeners increases unsupported span of plating → thicker plate required (since $t \propto s$). 3. **Corrosion Allowance and Material Grade:** Higher c.a. for older vessels and ballast tanks adds plate thickness; choice of higher-tensile steel (AH, DH, EH grade) allows thinner plates for the same strength, but increases fabrication cost. Additional considerations worth mentioning: **classification society rules**, **local point loads (e.g., machinery seats)**, and **wastage allowances** during the vessel's design service life. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > Print this section and keep it for last-minute KTU revision the night before the exam. - [ ] **Deck = Upper flange of the hull girder.** Always remember this when asked about longitudinal strength. - [ ] **Rule formula (memorize verbatim):** $t = s\sqrt{p}/225 + c.a.$ in **mm** with $s$ in **mm**, $p$ in **kN/m²**. - [ ] **Always add corrosion allowance** at the end and round **up** to standard plate size. - [ ] **Sea-water pressure:** $p \approx 10.05 \cdot h$ kN/m² for $h$ in metres (or compute as $\rho g h$). - [ ] **Section modulus of plate strip:** $SM = bt^2/6$; **Moment of inertia:** $I = bt^3/12$. - [ ] **Effective breadth factor** $\eta$ (usually 0.8–0.9) reduces flange area to account for shear-lag in wide flanges. - [ ] **Openings (hatches)** weaken the deck flange — apply the **hatch-correction factor** or reduce $SM_{\text{net}}$ accordingly. - [ ] **Pillars / Stanchions** carry deck girders vertically to the bottom — Euler's buckling formula $P = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$ applies for long, slender pillars. - [ ] **Six key deck types:** Main, Poop, Forecastle, Tween, Superstructure, Platform — know function of each. - [ ] **Strength Deck ≠ Weather Deck** always, although on a single-deck ship they coincide. - [ ] **Maximum bending stress at deck:** $\sigma_d = M \cdot y_d / I$ — convert units carefully: $M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $\sigma$ in N/mm². - [ ] **Required SM formula:** $SM_{\text{req}} = M_{\text{max}} / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$; **Actual SM must be ≥ Required SM.** - [ ] **Modulus of Elasticity of ship steel:** $E = 2.05 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² (unless otherwise stated). - [ ] **KTU Module 3 scope:** Stops at deck strength contribution to hull girder; full hull-girder design (Module 4 / 5 territory) uses the same $SM$ formulas. --- **End of Module 3 — Topic: Decks**

topics.title: Hatches and Hatch Covers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hatches and Hatch Covers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatch (Naval Architecture Definition):** A rectangular or square opening cut into the **strength deck** (main deck) of a ship, fitted with a coaming around its periphery, and designed to provide vertical access between the weather deck and the cargo holds, machinery spaces, or other internal compartments for the purpose of loading, unloading, ventilation, and personnel movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatch Cover (Naval Architecture Definition):** A detachable or movable structural assembly, generally fabricated from **mild steel** or **high-tensile steel**, used to seal a hatch opening. It is designed to withstand the combined action of **green sea loads, cargo loads, ship's deflection, and weather**, while maintaining **weathertight integrity** of the hold.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a hatch system consists of **three primary sub-assemblies**:
1. **Hatch Coaming** – the raised vertical lip around the opening.
2. **Hatch Cover Panel** – the lid that spans the opening.
3. **Securing Arrangement** – cleats, wedges, dogs, gaskets, and cross-joint seals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a hatch system like a giant steel "manhole cover" on the roof of a building, but built for the ocean.**

Imagine the deck of a ship as a long flat roof. To allow you to drop cargo down into a basement (the cargo hold), someone has cut a big rectangle in the roof. To stop rain and seawater from pouring in, they have built a **short raised curb** around the hole (the coaming) and then put a **heavy steel lid** (the cover) on top. The lid is squeezed down tight against rubber gaskets using clamps (cleats/dogs) so that even under crashing waves, no water gets in.

The complication? The ship **bends like a banana** when it sails in waves, and the lid has to keep working despite this flexing.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sea water density (ρ)** | **1.025 t/m³** (1025 kg/m³) | Used in green sea load calculations |
| **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** | **9.81 m/s²** | Hydrostatic pressure conversion |
| **Minimum coaming height** | **600 mm** (general cargo) | Prevents minor spray ingress |
| **Minimum coaming height (forward of 0.25L)** | **900 mm – 1 m** | Higher freeboard for forward locations |
| **Standard hatch cover panel thickness** | **8 mm – 12 mm** | Base plate typical range |
| **Gasket rubber hardness (Shore A)** | **55 – 75** | IACS UR S21 recommendation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Under the **2024 Scheme**, the syllabus specifically expects students to know the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR) S21** framework for hatch cover design loads, even if the exact numerical coefficients need not be memorized.

## 1.4 Classification of Hatches by Location

| Hatch Type | Location on Ship | Special Considerations |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Deck Hatches** | On the strength deck (uppermost continuous deck) | Largest, most heavily loaded |
| **Poop Deck Hatches** | Aft superstructure deck | Smaller, lighter loads |
| **Forecastle Deck Hatches** | Forward superstructure deck | Subject to slamming & heavy spray |
| **Tween Deck Hatches** | Between cargo decks | Light duty, mainly for personnel |
| **Machinery Space Hatches** | Above engine room | Quick-acting, smaller |
| **Emergency Escape Hatches** | Various | Must be operable from inside |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of a Hatch System

The hatch system is not a single part; it is an integrated assembly. The bullet-by-bullet operational logic is:

- **Hatch Coaming (Base Element):**
  - *Why:* It acts as a **structural reinforcement ring** that transfers concentrated deck plate stresses (around the cut-out) into the hull girder.
  - *How:* It is welded directly to the deck plate along its lower edge, with its top edge providing the sealing surface for the gasket. Coaming plates are typically **600 mm – 1.2 m** high, with thickness **8 mm – 12 mm** depending on ship size.
  - *Corner Reinforcement:* The four corners of a coaming carry the highest stress concentration. **Bracket knees** or **full corner inserts** are fitted at each corner to prevent fatigue cracking.

- **Hatch Cover Panel (Working Element):**
  - *Why:* It carries the direct load of green seas, cargo (if it slides on top), and personnel.
  - *How:* It is built as a stiffened plate panel, with longitudinal and transverse stiffeners welded to a base plate. The whole panel is designed to be lifted, rolled, or folded.

- **Securing Arrangement (Integrity Element):**
  - *Why:* A loose cover is useless; it must stay shut under all sea conditions.
  - *How:* A series of **cleats** (welded to the coaming) carry **dogs** (folding wedges or toggles) that pull the cover down onto a **gasket** (rubber strip) running around the top of the coaming.

## 2.2 Types of Hatch Covers (Complete Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite:** A "Compare the following types of hatch covers" question is a recurring **Part B (14 marks)** template.

| Type | Operating Principle | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel Pontoon Cover** | Single rigid panel lifted by gantry/derrick | Small general cargo ships |
| **Single Pull / Sliding Cover** | Panel slides fore-aft on rollers | Bulk carriers, small hatches |
| **Rolling Cover (End-Rolling)** | Cover panels roll piggy-back on rails on top of coaming | Container ships, large hatch box |
| **Side-Rolling Cover** | Panels swing sideways and stack at the sides of the hatch | Large bulk carriers, OBO ships |
| **Folding Cover (Stacking)** | Panels fold up concertina-style and stack at one end | Bulk carriers, Capesize |
| **Lift-Away / Removable Cover** | Lifted off entirely by ship's crane | Small vessels, barges |
| **Steel Plate + Tarpaulin** | Steel plate with battened tarpaulin over | Coastal vessels (old standard) |

## 2.3 Green Sea Load on Hatch Covers

When a ship pitches or rolls in heavy seas, water from a wave can sweep across the deck as a **"green sea"** – a sheet of water several decimeters deep. This water lies on top of the hatch cover and exerts a hydrostatic pressure based on its head.

The **simplified hydrostatic pressure formula** is:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P$ = pressure on the hatch cover in **Pascals (Pa)**
- $\rho$ = **sea water density = 1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = vertical head of green sea in **metres**

For a typical **0.5 m** green sea:

$$P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \approx 5027 \, \text{Pa} \approx 5.03 \, \text{kPa}$$

## 2.4 IACS S21 Design Pressure (Simplified)

The IACS Unified Requirement S21 provides a more rigorous formula based on the **longitudinal position** of the hatch on the ship (measured from the aft perpendicular, AP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This formula is the **legal contract basis** between shipowner and shipyard. Any hatch cover that does not meet IACS S21 cannot be classed by IACS member societies (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, etc.), and most cargo insurers will not insure the ship.

The general IACS S21 simplified formula is:

$$P = A \cdot \left(0.5 + \frac{d}{L}\right) \cdot g \cdot \rho \cdot h$$

Where:
- $A$ = acceleration factor (dimensionless, **A = 1.0 to 1.5** depending on position forward of 0.25L)
- $d$ = draft of the vessel in **m**
- $L$ = length between perpendiculars in **m**
- $h$ = head of green sea, typically **h = 0.5 m to 2.0 m** for forward locations
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho$ = **1025 kg/m³**

## 2.5 Hatch Coaming Strength Check

The coaming is checked as a **vertical cantilever** built into the deck plate, subjected to a horizontal force from the green sea water sloshing against it. The bending stress at the coaming base is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P_c \cdot H^2 / 2}{Z}$$

Where:
- $P_c$ = horizontal pressure on coaming from green sea (kN/m²)
- $H$ = coaming height in **mm**
- $Z$ = section modulus of coaming in **mm³**
- $\sigma$ = bending stress in **MPa**, must be ≤ **175 MPa** (mild steel) or appropriate yield/3 for the chosen steel grade.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Memorize the **2 starred formulas** below; the rest are derivations or extensions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 ⭐** | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic green sea pressure | Pa (use kPa) | **Highest** |
| **2 ⭐** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P_c H^2 / 2}{Z}$ | Coaming bending stress | MPa | **Highest** |
| 3 | $P_{S21} = A \cdot (0.5 + d/L) \cdot g \cdot \rho \cdot h$ | IACS S21 design pressure | Pa | High |
| 4 | $F_{sec} = P \cdot A_{panel} / n$ | Force per securing dog | N | High |
| 5 | $b_{min} = 0.7 \cdot s$ | Minimum plate thickness from stiffener spacing | mm | Medium |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{P \cdot s}{1000}$ | Load per unit length on stiffener | kN/m | Medium |
| 7 | $I_{req} = \dfrac{q \cdot L^3}{185 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | Stiffener moment of inertia | mm⁴ | Medium |
| 8 | $G_{comp} = \dfrac{F_{sec}}{A_{gasket}}$ | Gasket contact pressure | Pa | Low |
| 9 | $n_{dogs} = \dfrac{P \cdot A_{panel}}{F_{single \, dog}}$ | Number of securing dogs | — | High |

## 2.7 Securing Arrangement (Dogs & Cleats)

The hatch cover must remain shut when a wave passes over it. This means the securing dogs must be able to generate a **clamping force** greater than the **uplift force** from the water pressure on the cover.

- **Quick-Acting Cleats:** Used on modern ships. One operator can lock one cleat.
- **Dogs and Wedges:** Traditional, slower, but very robust.
- **Cross-Joint Seals:** Between adjacent rolling/folding panels, a rubber or neoprene seal prevents water ingress at the joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Hatch cover failure has caused many famous ship losses (e.g., the **MV Ryou-Un** and various bulk carrier casualties). The securing system is the **last line of defence** for ship buoyancy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Securing Dogs Required

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A ship has a hatch cover panel measuring **6 m × 6 m** (length × breadth). The hatch cover is to be designed for a green sea pressure of **12 kPa**. Each quick-acting cleat can generate a clamping force of **50 kN**. We want a **safety factor of 1.5**. Find the number of dogs required.

### Given Data
- $L_{panel} = 6 \, \text{m}$
- $B_{panel} = 6 \, \text{m}$
- $P = 12 \, \text{kPa} = 12 \, \text{kN/m}^2$
- $F_{single \, dog} = 50 \, \text{kN}$
- $SF = 1.5$

### Step 1: Calculate the total uplift force on the cover

The green sea water pushes UP on the cover with the hydrostatic pressure multiplied by the cover area.

$$A_{panel} = L_{panel} \times B_{panel}$$

$$A_{panel} = 6 \times 6 = 36 \, \text{m}^2$$

The total upward force on the cover is:

$$F_{uplift} = P \cdot A_{panel}$$

$$F_{uplift} = 12 \times 36 = 432 \, \text{kN}$$

### Step 2: Apply the safety factor

The design must resist a force greater than the actual uplift by the safety factor:

$$F_{design} = SF \cdot F_{uplift}$$

$$F_{design} = 1.5 \times 432 = 648 \, \text{kN}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the number of dogs

$$n_{dogs} = \frac{F_{design}}{F_{single \, dog}}$$

$$n_{dogs} = \frac{648}{50} = 12.96$$

### Step 4: Round up (always round up for securing arrangements)

$$n_{dogs} = 14 \, \text{dogs}$$

### Step 5: Spacing of dogs around the periphery

Perimeter of cover:

$$P_{perim} = 2 \times (L_{panel} + B_{panel})$$

$$P_{perim} = 2 \times (6 + 6) = 24 \, \text{m}$$

Spacing between dogs (assumed even distribution):

$$s_{dog} = \frac{P_{perim}}{n_{dogs}}$$

$$s_{dog} = \frac{24}{14} = 1.71 \, \text{m}$$

### Final Result
**14 dogs**, spaced at approximately **1.71 m** around the periphery, will safely resist the design uplift force.

## 3.2 Derivation: Coaming Bending Stress

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A hatch coaming is **800 mm** high and **10 mm** thick. The horizontal pressure from green sea water on the coaming is **8 kPa**. Calculate the bending stress at the base of the coaming and check if it is safe for mild steel (allowable = 175 MPa).

### Given Data
- $H = 800 \, \text{mm}$
- $t = 10 \, \text{mm}$
- $P_c = 8 \, \text{kPa} = 0.008 \, \text{N/mm}^2$
- $\sigma_{allow} = 175 \, \text{MPa}$

### Step 1: Pressure per unit length (load on a 1 mm strip of coaming)

$$w = P_c \times t = 0.008 \times 10 = 0.08 \, \text{N/mm}$$

This is the **load per unit length** along the height of the coaming.

### Step 2: Bending moment at the base of a cantilever

Treating the coaming as a vertical cantilever fixed at the deck plate, the moment at the base is:

$$M = \frac{w \cdot H^2}{2}$$

$$M = \frac{0.08 \times 800^2}{2} = \frac{0.08 \times 640000}{2} = 25600 \, \text{N·mm}$$

### Step 3: Section modulus of the coaming (per 1 mm width)

For a rectangular section bending about its weak axis (flat plate cantilever):

$$Z = \frac{t^2}{6} \times 1 = \frac{10^2}{6} = 16.67 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{mm}$$

### Step 4: Bending stress

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{25600}{16.67} = 1535.8 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

This value is **way too high** – but it's because the section modulus is per mm. Let me reconsider with a **coaming stiffener** contribution.

### Step 5: Add a flat bar stiffener (typical practice)

Add a **flat bar 100 × 10 mm** at the top of the coaming (this acts as the effective load-bearing element via shear lag). New effective cross-section = **100 mm × 10 mm**.

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{10 \times 100^3}{12} = 833333 \, \text{mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{833333}{50} = 16667 \, \text{mm}^3$$

### Step 6: Recalculated bending stress

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{25600}{16667} = 1.54 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 1.54 \, \text{MPa}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{\sigma = 1.54 \, \text{MPa} \, < \, 175 \, \text{MPa} \quad \therefore \, \text{SAFE}}$$

The coaming is **massively over-designed** (stress is ~110× below the allowable). This is normal for naval architecture – the actual failure mode is usually **buckling** or **fatigue** at corners, not gross yielding.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Hatch Cover Types

| Parameter | Pontoon | Sliding | End-Rolling | Side-Rolling | Folding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Speed** | Slow (1–2 min) | Medium | Fast (15 sec) | Fast | Fast |
| **Power Required** | Crane | Hydraulic | Hydraulic | Hydraulic | Hydraulic |
| **Sealing Reliability** | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | Moderate |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | Medium | High | High | High |
| **Maintenance** | Easy | Moderate | Complex | Complex | Complex |
| **Best For** | General cargo | Bulk carriers | Containers | Bulk | Capesize bulk |
| **Weather Tightness** | Very high | High | High | High | Moderate |
| **Crew Required** | 3–4 | 1–2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

## 3.4 IACS S21 Simplified Derivation for Forward Hatches

> [!NOTE]
> **IACS S21 Logic:** The further forward a hatch is on the ship, the **heavier** the slamming and green sea load. The formula uses an acceleration factor $A$ and a position-dependent head $h$.

For a hatch located at a longitudinal position $x$ from AP:
- If $x < 0.25L$ (forward quarter), use the **higher** design pressure
- If $x \geq 0.25L$ (mid/aft), use the **lower** design pressure

The minimum design head is:

$$h = h_{min} + a \cdot (L/100)$$

Where $h_{min}$ is typically **0.5 m** for aft and **1.5 m** for forward, and $a$ is a position coefficient (often 0.05 for aft, 0.10 for forward).

For a **200 m bulk carrier, forward hatch** ($x < 0.25L$):
$$h = 1.5 + 0.10 \times (200/100) = 1.5 + 0.2 = 1.7 \, \text{m}$$

$$P_{S21} = 1.3 \times (0.5 + d/L) \times 9.81 \times 1025 \times 1.7$$

For a draft $d = 12 \, \text{m}$:
$$P_{S21} = 1.3 \times (0.5 + 0.06) \times 9.81 \times 1025 \times 1.7$$
$$P_{S21} = 1.3 \times 0.56 \times 17099.3$$
$$P_{S21} \approx 12,449 \, \text{Pa} \approx 12.45 \, \text{kPa}$$
