<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between an impulse and a reaction steam turbine with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| Pressure drop location | Only in nozzle | In both stator and rotor |
| Rotor blade shape | Symmetrical / unsymmetrical | Asymmetrical (airfoil) |
| Degree of reaction | 0% | 50% (typical Parsons) |
| Blade pressure sides | Both sides same pressure | Pressure difference across blades |
| Example | De Laval, Curtis | Parsons, Ljungström |
| Efficiency per stage | Lower | Higher |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**State and explain the function of a condenser in a marine steam power plant.**

**Model Answer:**

A **condenser** is a heat exchanger that condenses the exhaust steam from the LP turbine into water for reuse in the boiler. **Functions:**

1. **Creates low back pressure** — Improves specific work output and thermal efficiency.
2. **Recovers distilled water** — Marine boilers require high-purity feedwater to prevent scaling.
3. **Reduces exhaust steam energy loss** — Releases latent heat to the cooling seawater.

A typical surface condenser maintains a vacuum of **0.05–0.1 bar absolute**.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — Steam Engine Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Revised Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **double-acting reciprocating steam engine**. List its advantages over single-acting type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steam engine has a cylinder diameter of **300 mm** and a stroke of **450 mm**. The mean effective pressure is **0.8 MPa**. Calculate the **indicated power** when the engine runs at **180 rpm**. Assume the engine is double-acting. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working of Double-Acting Steam Engine:**

In a double-acting engine, steam acts on **both sides of the piston alternately**.

**Working Cycle:**

1. **Top Side Admission:** Steam enters via the slide valve into the upper cylinder space, pushing the piston downward (power stroke).
2. **Bottom Side Admission (after cross-over):** As the piston descends, the lower side connects to steam, while the upper side opens to exhaust — making the upward stroke also a power stroke.
3. Thus, **every revolution yields TWO power strokes** instead of one.

**Components:**
- Steam chest (top), slide valve (D-slide or piston valve)
- Cylinder with liner, piston with rings
- Crosshead, connecting rod, crankshaft, eccentric
- Flywheel, governor, condenser

**Advantages over single-acting:**
- Higher power output for same cylinder size (effectively doubled)
- Better crank turning moment (more uniform)
- Compact design — saves space (critical in ships)
- Higher mechanical efficiency

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> Students often forget to mention that the **slide valve is operated by an eccentric on the crankshaft** — this is mandatory for full marks. Also, do NOT confuse with internal combustion — steam is supplied from outside (external combustion).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given Data:**
- Cylinder bore: $D = 300\,\text{mm} = 0.3\,\text{m}$
- Stroke length: $L = 450\,\text{mm} = 0.45\,\text{m}$
- Mean effective pressure: $P_{mep} = 0.8\,\text{MPa} = 0.8 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$
- Speed: $N = 180\,\text{rpm}$
- Engine: **Double-acting** (factor = 2 power strokes per revolution)

**Step 1: Piston (Cross-sectional) Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.3)^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.09 = 0.0225\pi$$

$$A \approx 0.0707\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Work Done per Stroke (Single Side)**

$$W_{stroke} = P_{mep} \times A \times L$$

$$W_{stroke} = (0.8 \times 10^6) \times 0.0707 \times 0.45$$

$$W_{stroke} = 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.0318$$

$$W_{stroke} \approx 25{,}447\,\text{J}$$

**Step 3: Work Done per Revolution (Double-Acting = 2 strokes)**

$$W_{rev} = 2 \times W_{stroke} = 2 \times 25{,}447 = 50{,}893\,\text{J/rev}$$

**Step 4: Number of Revolutions per Second**

$$n = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{180}{60} = 3\,\text{rev/s}$$

**Step 5: Indicated Power**

$$IP = W_{rev} \times n$$

$$IP = 50{,}893 \times 3$$

$$IP = 152{,}680\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{IP \approx 152.68\,\text{kW}}$$

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Steam Turbine Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Revised Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** Explain with a velocity triangle the working of a **single-stage impulse turbine**. Define the **blade velocity ratio** and **blade friction coefficient**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an impulse turbine, the steam leaves the nozzle at **350 m/s**. The blade speed is **140 m/s**. The blade friction coefficient is **0.85**. The blade angle at exit is **20°**. Determine: **(i)** Relative velocity at inlet and outlet, **(ii)** Work done per kg of steam, **(iii)** Axial thrust on the blades. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Velocity Triangle for Single-Stage Impulse Turbine:**

```
          V1 (Absolute Inlet)
         ↘
          \  α (Nozzle Angle)
           \
      ──────────────  U (Blade velocity)
           /  |  β1
          /   |
         /    V_r1 (Relative Inlet)
        ↗
       V2 (Absolute Outlet, axial)
```

**Key Definitions:**
- **Blade Velocity Ratio** ($\rho$): The ratio of blade velocity to jet velocity, $\rho = u/v_1$. Optimal value for maximum work is $\rho = (1+k)/4$.
- **Blade Friction Coefficient** ($k$): The ratio of relative velocity at exit to relative velocity at inlet, $k = v_{r2}/v_{r1}$. Typical values 0.7–0.9.

**Working:** Steam at velocity $V_1$ from nozzle enters moving blade at relative velocity $V_{r1}$. Due to friction and change of direction, steam leaves at reduced relative velocity $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$ with absolute velocity $V_2$.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 350\,\text{m/s}$, $u = 140\,\text{m/s}$, $k = 0.85$
- Exit blade angle $\beta_2 = 20°$

Assume inlet blade angle equals exit blade angle (symmetric blade): $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 20°$

**Step 1: Relative Velocity at Inlet (Vector Triangle)**

From inlet velocity triangle:

$$v_{r1}^2 = v_1^2 + u^2 - 2\,v_1\,u\,\cos\alpha$$

For simplicity, assume nozzle angle $\alpha = 20°$ (typical KTU assumption):

$$v_{r1}^2 = (350)^2 + (140)^2 - 2(350)(140)\cos 20°$$

$$v_{r1}^2 = 122{,}500 + 19{,}600 - 2 \times 350 \times 140 \times 0.9397$$

$$v_{r1}^2 = 142{,}100 - 92{,}057$$

$$v_{r1}^2 = 50{,}043$$

$$v_{r1} \approx 223.7\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Relative Velocity at Outlet**

$$v_{r2} = k \cdot v_{r1} = 0.85 \times 223.7$$

$$\boxed{v_{r2} \approx 190.1\,\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Work Done per kg of Steam (Euler Equation)**

$$W = u\,(v_{w1} + v_{w2})$$

Whirl components:

$$v_{w1} = v_1\cos\alpha - u = 350 \times 0.9397 - 140 = 328.9 - 140 = 188.9\,\text{m/s}$$

$$v_{w2} = u - v_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 140 - 190.1 \times \cos 20°$$

$$v_{w2} = 140 - 190.1 \times 0.9397 = 140 - 178.6 = -38.6\,\text{m/s}$$

(Negative sign means whirl reversal — desirable for maximum work)

$$W = u\,(v_{w1} + v_{w2}) = 140 \times (188.9 + (-38.6))$$

$$W = 140 \times 150.3$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 21{,}042\,\text{J/kg} = 21.04\,\text{kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 4: Axial Thrust**

Absolute velocities in axial direction (assuming axial exit):

$$v_{f1} = v_1\sin\alpha = 350 \times \sin 20° = 350 \times 0.342 = 119.7\,\text{m/s}$$

$$v_{f2} = v_{r2}\sin\beta_2 = 190.1 \times \sin 20° = 190.1 \times 0.342 = 65.0\,\text{m/s}$$

Axial thrust per kg/sec:

$$F_a = (v_{f1} - v_{f2}) = (119.7 - 65.0) = 54.7\,\text{N per kg/s}$$

If mass flow $\dot{m}$ is required (assume $\dot{m} = 1\,\text{kg/s}$ for per kg result):

$$\boxed{F_a \approx 54.7\,\text{N per kg/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Sign convention for whirl component** — forgetting that outlet whirl can be negative (KTU accepts "negative whirl = velocity reversal").
> 2. **Units** — must express work in **kJ/kg**, not just J/kg, in final answer for full marks.
> 3. **Axial thrust sign** — depends on frame of reference; mention clearly "thrust on blades in direction of steam flow".

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Steam **engine** = reciprocating piston; Steam **turbine** = rotary bladed rotor.
- ✅ Turbine cycle = **Rankine cycle**; key processes: evaporation, expansion, condensation, compression.
- ✅ **Impulse turbine** — pressure drop only in nozzle, $R = 0$.
- ✅ **Reaction turbine** — pressure drop in both stator and rotor, $R \approx 0.5$ for Parsons.
- ✅ Blade velocity ratio $\rho = u/v_1$; optimum for max work: $\rho = (1+k)/4$.
- ✅ Maximum single-stage impulse efficiency: $\eta_{max} = (1+k)^2 / 4$.
- ✅ Marine propulsion uses **geared steam turbines** for high power + low propeller rpm.
- ✅ **Cut-off ratio** in steam engines controls the expansion ratio and hence efficiency.
- ✅ **Condenser** creates vacuum → increases specific work output.
- ✅ Remember the formula sheet (10 formulas) — at least 2 questions per paper are formula-based.
- ✅ Units check: pressure in Pa or bar, work in J or kJ, power in kW.
- ✅ **Memorize**: $1\,\text{MPa} = 10^6\,\text{Pa}$; $1\,\text{kW} = 1\,\text{kJ/s}$.
- ✅ Ship's **astern (reverse) motion** in turbine plants requires a separate reverse turbine — not just gear reversal.
- ✅ Marine boilers operate at **40–60 bar** with superheat temperatures of **450–500 °C**.
- ✅ Critical water point: **22.064 MPa, 374.15 °C** — appears in supercritical boiler questions.
- ✅ Indicated Power formula: $IP = (P_{mep} \times L \times A \times N \times k) / 60$ where $k = 1$ (single-acting) or $k = 2$ (double-acting).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Gas Turbine** is a rotary internal combustion engine in which a working fluid (typically air) is compressed, heated at constant pressure by combustion of fuel, and then expanded through a turbine to produce useful shaft work. In marine and naval architecture applications, the gas turbine operates on an **open cycle Brayton cycle** (also called the Joule cycle), where atmospheric air is drawn in, compressed, mixed with fuel, burned, expanded across turbine blades, and finally exhausted to the atmosphere.

The four essential components that constitute any gas turbine power plant are:
1. **Compressor** — Atmospheric air is pressurized (pressure ratio typically 6:1 to 30:1).
2. **Combustion Chamber (Combustor)** — Fuel is injected and burned at *approximately constant pressure*.
3. **Turbine** — Hot combustion gases expand across blades, delivering rotational work.
4. **Exhaust & Heat Recovery Systems** (optional) — For waste-heat recovery (cogeneration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the course *Fundamentals of Floating Bodies*, gas turbines are studied because they form the **primary high-speed propulsion plant** for modern naval combatants (frigates, destroyers, aircraft carriers like the INS Vikrant) and fast ferries. Their high power-to-weight ratio and rapid start-up capability make them indispensable for vessels requiring burst speeds above **30 knots**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing up a balloon and then releasing it so the air rushes out — that rushing air is doing work (pushing against the atmosphere). A **gas turbine is essentially a "controlled balloon release" running in a continuous loop**:

1. A huge **blower (compressor)** squishes a great volume of air into a small chamber — this is like forcefully pushing air into the balloon.
2. **Fuel is sprayed** into this compressed air and ignited — the air gets enormously hot, just like the rubber balloon would heat up if you could compress it more.
3. This hot, high-pressure gas is then allowed to escape through a **set of fan blades (turbine)** — but instead of wasting the energy like the balloon flying away, the spinning blades are captured on a shaft.
4. Most of that shaft power spins the **compressor itself**, and the leftover surplus is sent to a **propeller or generator**.

The entire process takes a little over **a tenth of a second** from intake to exhaust — which is why gas turbines can spin at **tens of thousands of RPM** and deliver enormous power from a unit the size of a refrigerator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metric:** Marine gas turbines (e.g., GE LM2500, Rolls-Royce Marine Spey) deliver between **20 MW to 40 MW** per unit, weigh under **5 tonnes**, and reach thermal efficiencies of **38%–42%** in simple cycle operation.

---

## Classification of Gas Turbines

| Type | Description | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Cycle** | Air is taken from atmosphere and exhausted back | Standard marine propulsion |
| **Closed Cycle** | Working fluid (air or helium) is recycled in a sealed loop | Submarine closed-cycle variants |
| **Simple Cycle** | No heat recovery | Peak-load naval propulsion |
| **Regenerative / Recuperated** | Turbine exhaust preheats compressed air | Improves efficiency to ~45% |
| **Intercooled & Reheated** | Multiple compressions and expansions | High-power surface combatants |
| **Free Turbine** | Gas generator drives separate power turbine via coaxial shaft | Most marine arrangements (CODOG, COGAG) |

---

## Why Gas Turbines Dominate Modern Warships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Justification:** A gas turbine produces **3 to 4 times more power per ton of machinery weight** than a diesel engine and **2 times more** than a steam turbine. For a frigate needing **50 MW** of installed power, the gas turbine plant occupies roughly **one-fifth the volume** of an equivalent diesel plant.

The four properties that make gas turbines the **gold standard for high-performance marine propulsion** are:
- **Power Density:** Typically **5 kW/kg**, versus ~1 kW/kg for diesels.
- **Fast Response:** Cold start to full power in under **120 seconds** (versus 30+ minutes for steam plants).
- **Modular Footprint:** Self-contained modules can be installed in pre-fabricated compartments.
- **Vibration & Signature:** Lower vibration than reciprocating engines; with appropriate silencing, acoustic signature comparable to diesels.
---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Brayton (Joule) Cycle — The Heart of Every Gas Turbine

The **ideal air-standard Brayton cycle** consists of four internally reversible processes:

1. **Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression** — Air is compressed in the axial/centrifugal compressor.
2. **Process 2 → 3 : Constant Pressure Heat Addition** — Fuel is burned in the combustor, raising temperature from $T_2$ to $T_3$.
3. **Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion** — Hot gases expand through the turbine, delivering work.
4. **Process 4 → 1 : Constant Pressure Heat Rejection** — Exhaust gases are discharged to atmosphere.

For an **ideal cycle** with air as the working fluid (constant specific heats $c_p$, $c_v$, ratio $\gamma = c_p/c_v = 1.4$), the key parameters are:

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r_p$ | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | dimensionless |
| $T_2$ | Compressor Exit Temp | $T_2 = T_1 \, r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K |
| $T_3$ | Turbine Inlet Temp (TIT) | Given as design input | K |
| $T_4$ | Turbine Exit Temp | $T_4 = T_3 \left(\dfrac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | K |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Thermal Efficiency | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless |
| $W_C$ | Compressor Work | $c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg |
| $W_T$ | Turbine Work | $c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg |
| $W_{net}$ | Net Work Output | $W_T - W_C$ | kJ/kg |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass Flow Rate | $\dfrac{\text{Power Output}}{W_{net}}$ | kg/s |
| $SFC$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | $\dfrac{q_{in}}{\eta_{th} \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | kg/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** The thermal efficiency of an *ideal* Brayton cycle depends **ONLY on the pressure ratio**, not on the turbine inlet temperature! However, the **net work output** depends on both. This is a recurring KTU trap question.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Flow Analysis

### Step 1: Identify State Points

At the inlet (state 1), we typically assume **ambient conditions**: $T_1 = 298 \, \text{K} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ and $P_1 = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ (International Standard Atmosphere — ISA).

### Step 2: Apply Isentropic Relations for Compressor

For an isentropic compression, the temperature ratio is governed by:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

Substituting $\gamma = 1.4$, we get $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 \approx 0.2857$.

This exponent is the **"isentropic exponent for hot air"** and appears in **every single Brayton cycle calculation**.

### Step 3: Apply Constant Pressure Heat Addition

Since the combustor operates at (approximately) constant pressure, $P_3 = P_2$, and the heat added per kg of air is:

$$q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

### Step 4: Apply Isentropic Relations for Turbine

For an isentropic expansion:

$$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{(T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

---

## 2.3 The Real-World Twist: Component Efficiencies

In an *actual* gas turbine, the compression and expansion are **not isentropic**. We account for irreversibilities using **isentropic (adiabatic) efficiencies**:

For the **compressor**:
$$\eta_C = \frac{\text{Isentropic Work}}{\text{Actual Work}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$$

For the **turbine**:
$$\eta_T = \frac{\text{Actual Work}}{\text{Isentropic Work}} = \frac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s}}$$

### The Compressor Work Ratio

A subtle but **highly tested concept** in KTU is the *work ratio*. The compressor in a simple cycle gas turbine consumes roughly **50% to 65%** of the turbine's gross output. This is captured as:

$$r_W = \frac{W_C}{W_T} \approx 0.50 \text{ to } 0.65$$

The remaining 35%–50% is the **net available work** for the propeller or generator.

---

## 2.4 Effect of Pressure Ratio on Performance

A beautiful and exam-favourite result: **As pressure ratio $r_p$ increases**, the Brayton cycle efficiency rises monotonically:

| $r_p$ | $\eta_{th}$ (ideal) | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 24.2% | Low-pressure ratio — limited efficiency |
| 8 | 36.5% | Typical industrial simple cycle |
| 12 | 43.0% | Marine LM2500 nominal |
| 20 | 50.0% | Aero-derivative high-pressure ratio |
| 30 | 54.6% | Modern fighter engine cruise |

However, **net work output decreases** as $r_p$ rises above an optimum (because compressor work grows faster than turbine work beyond a certain point). This trade-off creates the famous **"optimum pressure ratio"** for maximum specific work output — which KTU frequently asks to derive.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine and naval architecture, gas turbines are deployed in **combined propulsion modes**:

- **CODOG** — Combined Diesel or Gas (one or the other operates)
- **COGAG** — Combined Gas and Gas (two gas turbines, both for cruise, both for boost)
- **COGAS** — Combined Gas and Steam (exhaust heat from gas turbine drives a steam turbine)
- **CODLAG** — Combined Diesel, Electric, and Gas (most modern amphibious assault ships)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Naval Example:** The INS *Kamorta*-class corvettes use **CODOG** with two Pielstick diesels for cruising and two GE LM2500 gas turbines for sprints, enabling bursts above **32 knots** while maintaining 2,500 nautical mile endurance at cruise.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work

This is the **single most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams on Gas Turbines. Let me derive it step-by-step, with every algebraic transition explicit.

### Given
- Air-standard Brayton cycle with constant specific heats ($c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$)
- Turbine Inlet Temperature $T_3$ is fixed
- Compressor Inlet Temperature $T_1$ is fixed (= ambient)
- Pressure ratio $r_p$ is variable

### Step 1: Express Compressor Exit Temperature in Terms of $r_p$

From isentropic relation:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

Define $x = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma$. For air, $x = 0.2857$.

So:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^x$$

### Step 2: Express Turbine Exit Temperature in Terms of $r_p$

Since $T_3$ is fixed and the pressure ratio across the turbine equals $r_p$ (since $P_3 = P_2$ and $P_4 = P_1$):

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^x}$$

### Step 3: Write the Net Specific Work Output

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{net} &= w_T - w_C \\
w_{net} &= c_p(T_3 - T_4) - c_p(T_2 - T_1) \\
w_{net} &= c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Rearrange

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{net} &= c_p \left[ T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} - T_1 \cdot r_p^x + T_1 \right] \\
w_{net} &= c_p \left[ (T_3 + T_1) - T_1 \cdot r_p^x - \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Differentiate with Respect to $r_p$ and Set to Zero

For maximum $w_{net}$:

$$\frac{d w_{net}}{d r_p} = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d w_{net}}{d r_p} = c_p \left[ -T_1 \cdot x \cdot r_p^{x-1} + T_3 \cdot x \cdot r_p^{-(x+1)} \right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Simplify by Grouping Like Terms

Dividing throughout by $c_p \cdot x \cdot r_p^{x-1}$ (which is non-zero):

$$-T_1 + \frac{T_3}{r_p^{2x}} = 0$$

### Step 7: Solve for the Optimum Pressure Ratio $r_{p,opt}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_3}{r_p^{2x}} &= T_1 \\
r_p^{2x} &= \frac{T_3}{T_1} \\
r_{p,opt} &= \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{1/(2x)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The exponent $\dfrac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}$ equals $\dfrac{1.4}{2(0.4)} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.8} = 1.75$ for air. So $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{1.75}$. This is a standard formula examiners expect students to quote.

### Worked Numerical Example

Given: $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 1500 \text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{p,opt} &= \left(\frac{1500}{300}\right)^{1.75} \\
&= (5)^{1.75}
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute $(5)^{1.75} = 5^1 \cdot 5^{0.75}$. Since $5^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 5} = e^{0.75 \times 1.6094} = e^{1.2071} \approx 3.343$.

$$\therefore r_{p,opt} = 5 \times 3.343 \approx 16.72$$

**Interpretation:** For these conditions, the gas turbine delivers maximum specific work at a pressure ratio of about **17:1**. Above this, net work decreases (compressor work grows too fast). This is exactly why modern marine gas turbines operate in the range **15:1 to 25:1**.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem: Performance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A gas turbine operates on the Brayton cycle with a pressure ratio of 8. The compressor inlet temperature is 300 K and turbine inlet temperature is 1300 K. Assuming air-standard ideal cycle with $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate: (a) thermal efficiency, (b) net work per kg of air, (c) mass flow rate for 10 MW power output.

### Solution

#### Part (a): Thermal Efficiency

Compute the isentropic exponent:
$$x = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

Compute the temperature-pressure term:
$$r_p^x = 8^{0.2857}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(8^{0.2857}) = 0.2857 \times \ln 8 = 0.2857 \times 2.0794 = 0.5941$

So $8^{0.2857} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%$$

#### Part (b): Net Work per kg

Compute $T_2$:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^x = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}$$

Compute $T_4$:
$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.7 \text{ K}$$

Compressor work:
$$w_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.4 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.4 = 244.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Turbine work:
$$w_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 717.7) = 1.005 \times 582.3 = 585.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work:
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_C = 585.2 - 244.6 = 340.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

#### Part (c): Mass Flow Rate for 10 MW

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{w_{net}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ kW}}{340.6 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 29.36 \text{ kg/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by alternative method:** $\eta_{th} = w_{net}/q_{in}$. We have $q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 543.4) = 760.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Then $\eta_{th} = 340.6 / 760.7 = 0.4478 \approx 44.78\%$ ✓ (matches Part (a) within rounding).

---

## 3.3 Effect of Variable Specific Heats — A Refinement

For high-turbine-inlet-temperature applications (above 1000 K), the assumption of constant $c_p$ introduces noticeable errors. KTU may ask a 7-mark sub-part on this. The variable specific heat approach uses:

$$c_p = a + bT$$

For air, $a = 1.0035 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $b = 0.0001 \text{ kJ/kg·K}^2$ (approximate linear fit).

The isentropic relation becomes:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{R^*/c_{p,avg}}$$

where $R^* = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ is the specific gas constant for air, and $c_{p,avg}$ is the average value over the temperature range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As temperature rises, $c_p$ increases, which **reduces the temperature ratio** $T_2/T_1$ for a given pressure ratio. This is why high-temperature cycles with variable $c_p$ show **slightly lower compressor exit temperatures** and **slightly higher efficiencies** than the constant-$c_p$ prediction.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Marine Gas Turbine System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Inlet Duct and Filter]
    B --> C[Axial Compressor Multi-Stage]
    C --> D[Diffuser Section Pressure Rise]
    D --> E[Combustion Chamber Primary Zone]
    E --> F[Fuel Injector and Swirler]
    F --> G[Combustion Chamber Dilution Zone]
    G --> H[Turbine Nozzle Guide Vanes Stage 1]
    H --> I[Turbine Rotor Blades Stage 1]
    I --> J[Turbine Nozzle Stage 2]
    J --> K[Turbine Rotor Stage 2]
    K --> L[Turbine Rotor Stage 3 Power Section]
    L --> M[Exhaust Duct and Gas Up-take]
    M --> N[Gas Up-take to Funnel]
    L --> O[Reduction Gearbox]
    O --> P[Propeller Shaft or Generator]
    P --> Q[Propulsion or Electrical Load]
    C -.drives.-> R[Compressor Drive Shaft]
    R -.coaxial.-> L

    classDef process fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#f8fafc
    classDef output fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff7ed
    classDef input fill:#064e3b,stroke:#022c22,stroke-width:2px,color:#ecfdf5

    class A,B,F process
    class C,D,E,G,H,I,J,K,L process
    class M,N,O,P,Q output
    class R input
```

## 4.2 Brayton Cycle P-V and T-S Diagram (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph THERMO["Brayton Cycle on T-S and P-V Diagrams"]
        direction TB
        S1["State 1: Compressor Inlet. Pressure P1, Temperature T1"]
        S2["State 2: Compressor Exit. Pressure P2 equals P3, Temperature T2 greater than T1"]
        S3["State 3: Turbine Inlet. Pressure P3 equals P2, Temperature T3 maximum. Heat added q_in at constant pressure"]
        S4["State 4: Turbine Exit. Pressure P4 equals P1, Temperature T4. Heat rejected q_out at constant pressure"]
    end

    S1 -- Isentropic Compression: 1 to 2 --> S2
    S2 -- Constant Pressure Heat Addition: 2 to 3 --> S3
    S3 -- Isentropic Expansion: 3 to 4 --> S4
    S4 -- Constant Pressure Heat Rejection: 4 to 1 --> S1

    classDef stateBox fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#082f49,stroke-width:2px,color:#f0f9ff
    classDef processBox fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#faf5ff

    class S1,S2,S3,S4 stateBox
    class THERMO processBox
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        INLET[Atmospheric Air Intake. Ambient T1 and P1]
        FILTER[Air Filter and Silencer]
        INLET --> FILTER
    end

    subgraph GASGEN["GAS GENERATOR SECTION"]
        COMP[Multi-Stage Axial Compressor. Pressure Ratio rp]
        DIFF[Diffuser and Plenum]
        COMB[Reverse Flow Combustion Chamber]
        HPT[High Pressure Turbine. Drives Compressor]
        COMP --> DIFF
        DIFF --> COMB
        COMB --> HPT
        HPT -.shaft.-> COMP
    end

    subgraph POWERSEC["POWER TURBINE SECTION"]
        PT[Free Power Turbine. Extracts Net Work]
        SHAFT[Output Shaft. Typically 3000 to 3600 RPM]
        REDUCER[Reduction Gearbox. Matches Propeller Speed]
        PT --> SHAFT
        SHAFT --> REDUCER
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        PROP[Controllable Pitch Propeller or Waterjet]
        GEN[Ship Service Generator Optional]
        REDUCER --> PROP
        REDUCER --> GEN
    end

    subgraph EXHAUST["EXHAUST SUBSYSTEM"]
        UPTAKE[Exhaust Gas Up-take]
        STACK[Funnel and Silencer]
        HPT --> PT
        PT --> UPTAKE
        UPTAKE --> STACK
    end

    FILTER --> COMP

    classDef subsystem fill:#312e81,stroke:#1e1b4b,stroke-width:2px,color:#eef2ff
    classDef component fill:#9a3412,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff7ed
    class INPUT,GASGEN,POWERSEC,OUTPUT,EXHAUST subsystem
    class INLET,FILTER,COMP,DIFF,COMB,HPT,PT,SHAFT,REDUCER,PROP,GEN,UPTAKE,STACK component
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Air enters at ambient conditions, is pressurized by the gas generator (compressor + combustor + high-pressure turbine), then drives a *free power turbine* that is mechanically independent of the gas generator. This is the classic **two-spool** arrangement used in marine LM2500 and Spey engines.

## 4.4 Plant Layout — Typical Warship Engine Room (CODOG)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ER["ENGINE ROOM COMPARTMENT"]
        direction TB
        GT1[Gas Turbine Module 1. Starboard]
        GT2[Gas Turbine Module 2. Port]
        D1[Diesel Engine 1. Starboard Cruise]
        D2[Diesel Engine 2. Port Cruise]
        GB1[Gearbox and Clutch Assembly]
        SS[Ship Service Generator Set]
    end

    GT1 --> GB1
    GT2 --> GB1
    D1 --> GB1
    D2 --> GB1
    GB1 --> PROP_SHAFT[Propeller Shaft Line]
    SS --> SHIP_GRID[Ship Electrical Distribution Board]

    classDef gasTurb fill:#9f1239,stroke:#881337,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff1f2
    classDef diesel fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#eff6ff
    classDef aux fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#f0fdf4
    classDef output fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#faf5ff

    class GT1,GT2 gasTurb
    class D1,D2 diesel
    class SS aux
    class GB1,PROP_SHAFT,SHIP_GRID output
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The two gas turbines sit *outboard* for accessibility and survivability. Cross-connected gearboxes allow any prime mover to drive either shaft — a critical damage-control feature in naval architecture.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the Brayton cycle. Why is the pressure ratio a critical parameter in gas turbine design?

#### Model Answer
The **Brayton cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isentropic processes (compression and expansion) and two constant-pressure processes (heat addition and heat rejection). For an ideal cycle with constant specific heats, the thermal efficiency is given by:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

The **pressure ratio** $r_p$ is critical because: (i) it is the **sole determinant of ideal cycle efficiency** (for fixed $\gamma$), (ii) it controls the net specific work output through an optimum value $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}$, and (iii) it dictates compressor stage count and mechanical stresses on blades. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List four advantages and two disadvantages of gas turbines for marine propulsion.

#### Model Answer
**Advantages:** (i) High power-to-weight ratio (5 kW/kg typical). (ii) Rapid start-up (under 2 minutes to full power). (iii) Modular construction simplifies installation. (iv) Multi-fuel capability (diesel, kerosene, marine distillate, JP-5).

**Disadvantages:** (i) High specific fuel consumption at part-load (poor part-load efficiency). (ii) High exhaust gas temperatures cause infra-red signature (a tactical drawback for warships). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Comprehension and Application Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

A marine gas turbine operates on an air-standard Brayton cycle with:
- Pressure ratio $r_p = 10$
- Compressor inlet temperature $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$
- Turbine inlet temperature $T_3 = 1400 \text{ K}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Calculate the **thermal efficiency**, **net work output per kg/s**, and the **turbine exit temperature** assuming ideal cycle conditions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
If the **compressor isentropic efficiency is 88%** and **turbine isentropic efficiency is 90%**, calculate the modified thermal efficiency, net work output, and compare with the ideal case.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Ideal Cycle

**Step 1:** Compute the isentropic exponent:
$$x = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

**Step 2:** Compute $r_p^x$:
$$r_p^x = 10^{0.2857}$$
$$\ln(10^{0.2857}) = 0.2857 \times 2.3026 = 0.6579$$
$$r_p^x = e^{0.6579} = 1.9307$$

**Step 3:** Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.9307} = 1 - 0.5180 = 0.4820 = 48.20\%$$

**Step 4:** Compressor exit temperature:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^x = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Turbine exit temperature:
$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} = \frac{1400}{1.9307} = 725.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6:** Net specific work:
$$w_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$$
$$w_{net} = 1.005 \times [(1400 - 725.2) - (579.2 - 300)]$$
$$w_{net} = 1.005 \times [674.8 - 279.2] = 1.005 \times 395.6 = 397.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Summary (Part a):** $\eta_{th} = 48.20\%$, $T_4 = 725.2 \text{ K}$, $w_{net} = 397.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — With Component Efficiencies

**Step 1:** Actual compressor exit temperature using $\eta_C = 0.88$:
$$T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{579.2 - 300}{0.88} = 300 + \frac{279.2}{0.88} = 300 + 317.3 = 617.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Actual compressor work:
$$w_{C,actual} = c_p(T_{2a} - T_1) = 1.005 \times (617.3 - 300) = 1.005 \times 317.3 = 318.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3:** Actual turbine exit temperature using $\eta_T = 0.90$:
$$T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_T(T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1400 - 0.90 \times (1400 - 725.2)$$
$$T_{4a} = 1400 - 0.90 \times 674.8 = 1400 - 607.3 = 792.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4:** Actual turbine work:
$$w_{T,actual} = c_p(T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 792.7) = 1.005 \times 607.3 = 610.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Actual net work:
$$w_{net,actual} = 610.3 - 318.9 = 291.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** Actual heat input:
$$q_{in,actual} = c_p(T_3 - T_{2a}) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 617.3) = 1.005 \times 782.7 = 786.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Actual thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{291.4}{786.6} = 0.3704 = 37.04\%$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | Ideal | Actual | Degradation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta_{th}$ | 48.20% | 37.04% | −11.16 percentage points |
| $w_{net}$ (kJ/kg) | 397.6 | 291.4 | −26.7% reduction |
| $T_4$ (K) | 725.2 | 792.7 | +67.5 K higher |

**Conclusion:** Component irreversibilities reduce both efficiency and specific work substantially. The turbine exit temperature rises by ~67 K, which has implications for exhaust system design. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Design-Oriented Focus

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

A naval architect is designing the propulsion plant for a new corvette. You are asked to:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(i)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **simple open-cycle marine gas turbine**.
**(ii)** Derive the expression for the **optimum pressure ratio** that maximizes net specific work output, assuming constant specific heats.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A gas turbine has a measured **air mass flow rate of 25 kg/s**. The compressor pressure ratio is 12, with $T_1 = 290 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 1450 \text{ K}$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$. The actual compressor exit temperature is 660 K. Compute:
(i) The compressor isentropic efficiency.
(ii) The turbine exit temperature (assuming turbine isentropic efficiency of 90%).
(iii) The net power output in **kW**.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a)

**(i) Working Principle (Brief description):**

Atmospheric air enters the **inlet duct** and is pressurized by the **axial-flow compressor** (rotor + stator blade rows). High-pressure air enters the **combustion chamber**, where atomized fuel is injected, mixed, and continuously burned at approximately constant pressure. The resulting hot combustion gases (at temperature $T_3$) expand through the **turbine**, where they drive blades mounted on a shaft. About 60% of the turbine's mechanical power is consumed in driving the compressor; the remainder is the **net shaft power**, which is transmitted (via reduction gear) to the propeller or generator. Exhaust gases leave through the up-take and funnel.

**(ii) Derivation of Optimum Pressure Ratio:**

Let $x = (\gamma-1)/\gamma$. From the isentropic relations:

$$T_2 = T_1 \, r_p^x, \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^x}$$

Net specific work:
$$w_{net} = c_p\left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right] = c_p\left[(T_3 + T_1) - \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} - T_1 r_p^x\right]$$

Differentiate with respect to $r_p$ and set $\dfrac{dw_{net}}{dr_p} = 0$:

$$c_p\left[\frac{T_3 \cdot x}{r_p^{x+1}} - T_1 \cdot x \cdot r_p^{x-1}\right] = 0$$

Dividing by $c_p \cdot x \cdot r_p^{x-1}$ (non-zero):

$$\frac{T_3}{r_p^{2x}} = T_1$$

$$\therefore \boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{2x}} = \left(\frac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b)

**Given:** $\dot{m} = 25 \text{ kg/s}$, $r_p = 12$, $T_1 = 290 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 1450 \text{ K}$, $T_{2a} = 660 \text{ K}$, $\eta_T = 0.90$.

**(i) Compressor isentropic efficiency:**

First compute the **ideal** compressor exit temperature:
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^x = 290 \times 12^{0.2857}$$

Compute $12^{0.2857}$:
$$\ln(12^{0.2857}) = 0.2857 \times \ln 12 = 0.2857 \times 2.4849 = 0.7099$$
$$12^{0.2857} = e^{0.7099} = 2.0339$$

So $T_{2s} = 290 \times 2.0339 = 589.8 \text{ K}$.

Compressor isentropic efficiency:
$$\eta_C = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1} = \frac{589.8 - 290}{660 - 290} = \frac{299.8}{370} = 0.8103 = 81.03\%$$

**(ii) Turbine exit temperature:**

Ideal turbine exit temperature:
$$T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^x} = \frac{1450}{2.0339} = 712.9 \text{ K}$$

Actual turbine exit temperature using $\eta_T = 0.90$:
$$T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1450 - 0.90 \times (1450 - 712.9)$$
$$T_{4a} = 1450 - 0.90 \times 737.1 = 1450 - 663.4 = 786.6 \text{ K}$$

**(iii) Net power output:**

Actual compressor work (per kg):
$$w_{C,actual} = c_p(T_{2a} - T_1) = 1.005 \times (660 - 290) = 1.005 \times 370 = 371.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Actual turbine work (per kg):
$$w_{T,actual} = c_p(T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \times (1450 - 786.6) = 1.005 \times 663.4 = 666.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net specific work:
$$w_{net,actual} = 666.7 - 371.85 = 294.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net power output:
$$\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m} \times w_{net,actual} = 25 \times 294.85 = 7371.25 \text{ kW} \approx 7.37 \text{ MW}$$

**Final Answers (Part b):**
- (i) $\eta_C = 81.03\%$
- (ii) $T_{4a} = 786.6 \text{ K}$
- (iii) $\dot{W}_{net} = 7371 \text{ kW}$ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusion between $T_2$ and $T_{2a}$:** Students frequently use the ideal $T_2$ when compressor efficiency is given. Always check — *which* temperature is given in the problem?
> 2. **Sign error in isentropic exponent:** Forgetting the negative sign and writing $\eta_{th} = 1 + 1/r_p^x$. This will give $\eta_{th} > 100\%$, an immediate loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by $\dot{m}$:** Power output requires the mass flow rate. A common 14-mark error is calculating only the **specific** work and stopping there.
> 4. **Mixing up pressure ratio definition:** Some texts define $r_p = P_1/P_2$. KTU consistently uses $r_p = P_2/P_1$ (compressor pressure rise). Stick with $P_2/P_1$.
> 5. **Using wrong units:** $c_p$ is in **kJ/kg·K**, not J/kg·K. A factor of 1000 error loses 1 mark per instance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Finals:**

- [x] **Cycle Identity:** Gas turbines operate on the **Brayton cycle** (two isentropic + two constant-pressure processes).
- [x] **Key Formulae (must memorize):**
  - $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$
  - $r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma / [2(\gamma-1)]}$
  - $w_{net} = w_T - w_C = c_p[(T_3-T_4) - (T_2-T_1)]$
  - $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{net}$
- [x] **Standard Values:** $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$, $c_{p,air} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $R^* = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $T_{1,ISA} = 298 \text{ K}$, $P_{1,ISA} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- [x] **Typical Ranges:** Marine $r_p = 8$–$25$, $T_3 = 1200$–$1600 \text{ K}$, $\eta_{th,simple} = 35$–$42\%$.
- [x] **Component Efficiencies:** $\eta_C \approx 0.85$–$0.90$ (multi-stage axial), $\eta_T \approx 0.88$–$0.92$ (multi-stage).
- [x] **Compressor Work Ratio:** $w_C / w_T \approx 0.50$–$0.65$.
- [x] **Marine Architecture Vocabulary:** Free turbine, gas generator, two-spool, CODOG, COGAG, COGAS, CODLAG.
- [x] **Trick Question Trap:** Ideal Brayton efficiency depends **only** on $r_p$, *not* on $T_3$ — but real cycle efficiency *does* depend on $T_3$ through irreversibilities and blade cooling limits.
- [x] **Why Higher $T_3$ Matters:** Higher $T_3$ allows higher net specific work, but is limited by metallurgical constraints (single-crystal super-alloy blades, thermal barrier coatings, blade cooling).
- [x] **Why Higher $r_p$ is Limited:** Above $r_{p,opt}$, the compressor consumes disproportionately more work than the turbine produces, reducing net output.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Heat added is positive (into system); heat rejected is negative (out of system). Work done BY the system (turbine) is positive; work done ON the system (compressor) is negative in the net accounting.
- [x] **Typical Exam Wording:** "An ideal gas turbine operates on..." — assume constant $c_p$. "An actual gas turbine has compressor efficiency..." — use the modified formulae.
- [x] **Numericals Strategy:** Always identify state points, compute $r_p^x$ first, then cascade temperatures.
- [x] **Indian Naval Examples to Quote:** GE LM2500 (used on *Kamorta*-class), Rolls-Royce Marine Spey (used on *Godavari*-class frigates), Zorya-Mashproekt M36 (Ukrainian, used on some export frigates).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diesel-Electric Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Diesel-Electric Drive (DED)** is a marine propulsion arrangement in which one or more **diesel engines (prime movers)** are mechanically coupled to **electrical generators** to produce electrical power, which is then routed through a switchboard and supplied to **electric motors** that directly (or through a reduction gear) drive the **propeller shaft(s)**. The conventional mechanical shaft line connecting the engine to the propeller is replaced by an **electrical power transmission link**, decoupling the rotational speed of the prime mover from the propeller.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between *Mechanical Drive* (direct shaft coupling), *Diesel-Electric Drive* (engine → generator → motor), and *Hybrid Drive* (combined mechanical + electrical). The board examiner expects an explicit block diagram in long-answer questions.

The system is formally classified into:
- **DC Diesel-Electric Drive** — Uses DC generators and DC series/compound motors (legacy systems).
- **AC Diesel-Electric Drive** — Uses synchronous generators with synchronous or asynchronous (induction) motors (modern ships).
- **COGAG / COGAS / CODLAG** — Combined diesel-electric + gas turbine variants (advanced naval architecture).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it as a "Power Adapter for Ships":**
> In your home, a laptop charger takes **AC power from the wall socket** and converts it to **DC power** for the laptop. Similarly, in a diesel-electric ship, the **diesel engine spins a generator** (just like a windmill spins a small dynamo) producing electricity. This electricity is then routed through thick **power cables** (like extension cords) to a big **electric motor** placed near the propeller. The motor spins the propeller. 
>
> Why bother with electricity? Because it lets you place the engines *anywhere* in the ship (bow, mid, or stern) and run only **thin electrical cables** to the propeller, instead of a long, heavy, alignment-sensitive mechanical shaft. It's like using Wi-Fi to connect devices instead of a long tangled wire.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Ship Value |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| $P_{BHP}$ | Brake Horse Power of Diesel Engine | 5 MW – 25 MW per unit |
| $P_{SHP}$ | Shaft Horse Power delivered to propeller | 80–85% of $P_{BHP}$ |
| $\eta_{overall}$ | Overall propulsive efficiency | **22 % – 28 %** |
| $V_L$ | Line Voltage (AC system) | **3.3 kV / 6.6 kV / 11 kV** |
| $f$ | Generator Frequency | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** |
| $N_s$ | Synchronous Motor Speed | 1500 / 1800 rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchor for Exams:** Remember the acronym **G-M-S-P**:
> **G**enerator → **M**otor → **S**haft → **P**ropeller.
> Power flow: Mechanical (engine) → Electrical (generator → motor) → Mechanical (shaft → propeller).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-wise Operational Breakdown

### (a) Prime Mover — Diesel Engine
- Usually **4-stroke marine diesel engines** (medium-speed: 400–1000 rpm; high-speed: >1000 rpm; low-speed: <300 rpm).
- Operates at **constant speed** to maintain constant generator frequency (essential for synchronous motor operation).
- Multiple engines (typically 2–4) are installed for **redundancy** and **partial-load efficiency**.

### (b) Generator
- **AC System:** 3-phase synchronous generator (alternator) — produces balanced 3-phase AC at constant voltage and frequency.
- **DC System:** Separately excited or self-excited DC shunt/compound generator.
- The generator absorbs **mechanical rotational energy** and converts it to **electrical energy** via electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law).

### (c) Switchboard & Power Distribution
- The main switchboard acts as the **central electrical hub**.
- Routes generator power to motors, auxiliaries, and emergency systems.
- Includes **circuit breakers, bus bars, transformers, and protective relays**.

### (d) Electric Motor (Propulsion Motor)
- **Synchronous Motor** — runs at $N_s = 120f/P$, used in heavy-duty modern ships.
- **Induction (Asynchronous) Motor** — simple, rugged, no commutator; used in most commercial vessels.
- **DC Series Motor** — used in older DC drives; offers high starting torque.

### (e) Reduction Gear & Shafting
- Reduces the high motor speed (e.g., 1500 rpm) to the propeller speed (e.g., 100–200 rpm).
- Efficiency typically **97 % – 98 %** per stage.

### (f) Propeller
- Converts rotational mechanical power into **thrust force** ($T$) for ship motion.
- Operates in the **wake field** of the hull.

## 2.2 Energy Conversion Chain (Why & How)

The complete power flow involves **four distinct energy transformations**:

1. **Chemical → Mechanical** (Diesel engine: combustion of fuel → rotation of crankshaft)
2. **Mechanical → Electrical** (Generator: Faraday's Law — rotating coil in magnetic field induces EMF)
3. **Electrical → Mechanical** (Motor: Lorentz force on conductors in magnetic field)
4. **Mechanical → Kinetic/Thrust** (Propeller: hydrodynamic lift on blades)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each conversion incurs **irreversible losses** (heat, friction, electrical resistance, hysteresis). The product of all stage efficiencies determines the **overall propulsive efficiency** — this is why understanding individual efficiencies is critical for design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{prop}$ | Overall propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 2 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of AC motor | rpm |
| 3 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N = N_s (1 - s)$ | Rotor speed of induction motor | rpm |
| 5 | $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | 3-phase AC power | W |
| 6 | $P_{DC} = V \cdot I$ | DC power | W |
| 7 | $E = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ | Back EMF (DC machine) | V |
| 8 | $T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque (DC machine) | N·m |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N}$ | Shaft torque from power | N·m |
| 10 | $T_{thrust} = K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ | Propeller thrust (KT formulation) | N |
| 11 | $P_{D} = K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^3 \cdot D^5$ | Propeller delivered power (KQ formulation) | W |
| 12 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Icebreakers & Submarines:** DED allows instant reversal of propeller rotation by simply reversing motor phase sequence — critical for icebreaking and crash-stop maneuvers.
- **DP (Dynamic Positioning) Class Ships:** Drill ships, cable layers, and offshore platforms require **azimuthal (360°) thrust** — DED enables this with simple joystick control.
- **Cruise Ships (CODELOD):** Diesel + Electric (Low-speed) — improves fuel efficiency and passenger comfort (vibration isolation).
- **Naval Warships:** The **Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier** uses COGAG (Combined Gas-turbine And Gas-turbine) with full electric drive — a flagship example of DED in modern navies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall Propulsive Efficiency

The **overall propulsive efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the **useful thrust power (delivered to the ship)** to the **input power from the fuel**.

### Step 1: Identify Power at Each Stage

Let $P_{in}$ be the input fuel power at the diesel engine.

**Stage 1 — Diesel Engine Output:**
$$
P_{BHP} = \eta_{eng} \cdot P_{in}
$$
where $\eta_{eng}$ is the **brake thermal efficiency** of the engine (typically 0.38 – 0.45).

**Stage 2 — Generator Output (Electrical):**
$$
P_{elec} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{BHP} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{in}
$$

**Stage 3 — Motor Output (Shaft Mechanical):**
$$
P_{shaft} = \eta_{mot} \cdot P_{elec} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot P_{in}
$$

**Stage 4 — After Reduction Gear:**
$$
P_{prop,input} = \eta_{gear} \cdot P_{shaft}
$$

**Stage 5 — Useful Thrust Power:**
$$
P_{thrust} = \eta_{prop} \cdot P_{prop,input}
$$

### Step 2: Combine All Stages

$$
P_{thrust} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{prop} \cdot P_{in}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{thrust}}{P_{in}} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{prop}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The product of independent stage efficiencies gives the overall efficiency because each stage multiplies the power flowing through it. A single weak stage drags down the entire chain — this is why each component is engineered with high reliability.

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A diesel-electric drive has the following efficiencies: $\eta_{eng}=0.40$, $\eta_{gen}=0.95$, $\eta_{mot}=0.92$, $\eta_{gear}=0.97$, $\eta_{prop}=0.65$. The diesel engine receives 20,000 kW of fuel energy. Find the overall efficiency and the useful thrust power.

**Step 3.1 — Overall Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.40 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \times 0.65
$$

Computing progressively:
$$
0.40 \times 0.95 = 0.380
$$
$$
0.380 \times 0.92 = 0.3496
$$
$$
0.3496 \times 0.97 = 0.3391
$$
$$
0.3391 \times 0.65 = 0.2204
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 0.2204 \; \text{or} \; 22.04\%}
$$

**Step 3.2 — Useful Thrust Power:**
$$
P_{thrust} = \eta_{overall} \times P_{in} = 0.2204 \times 20{,}000
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{thrust} \approx 4408 \; \text{kW}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Synchronous Motor Speed & Slip Relationship

For a 3-phase induction motor driving a propeller:

### Step 1: Synchronous Speed from Frequency and Poles

By electromagnetic theory, the rotating magnetic field produced by a 3-phase stator has speed:
$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}
$$
where $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = number of magnetic poles (even integer).

### Step 2: Define Slip

The rotor cannot rotate at synchronous speed due to relative motion required for torque induction. Define slip $s$:
$$
s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Actual Rotor Speed

Rearranging:
$$
N = N_s (1 - s)
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, $s = 0.04$ (4% slip)

**Step 3.3 — Synchronous Speed:**
$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \; \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 3.4 — Actual Rotor Speed:**
$$
N = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96
$$
$$
\boxed{N = 1440 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

### Step 4: Current Drawn for Given Power

For a 3-phase AC motor delivering mechanical power $P_{mech}$ at efficiency $\eta_{mot}$:
$$
P_{elec,in} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{mot}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi
$$

Solving for line current:
$$
I_L = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{mot}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $P_{mech} = 1500$ kW, $V_L = 440$ V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging, $\eta_{mot} = 0.93$:

$$
I_L = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.85 \times 0.93}
$$

Computing denominator:
$$
\sqrt{3} \times 440 = 1.7321 \times 440 = 762.11
$$
$$
762.11 \times 0.85 = 647.79
$$
$$
647.79 \times 0.93 = 602.45
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{602.45} \approx 2490.0 \; \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L \approx 2490 \; \text{A}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy Note:** The very high current (≈2490 A) is why large ships use **medium-voltage** systems (3.3 kV or 6.6 kV) — to keep current manageable and cable sizes practical.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diesel-Electric Drive — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete power flow, control loops, and auxiliary feedback paths in a modern AC diesel-electric propulsion system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DE1 - Diesel Engine 1] -->|Mechanical Torque| B[GEN1 - Synchronous Generator 1]
    A2[DE2 - Diesel Engine 2] -->|Mechanical Torque| B2[GEN2 - Synchronous Generator 2]
    A3[DE3 - Diesel Engine 3] -->|Mechanical Torque| B3[GEN3 - Synchronous Generator 3]
    B -->|3-Phase AC| C[MSB - Main Switchboard]
    B2 -->|3-Phase AC| C
    B3 -->|3-Phase AC| C
    C -->|Controlled AC| D[VFD - Variable Frequency Drive]
    D -->|Variable f and V| E[PM - Propulsion Motor Synchronous or Induction]
    E -->|Rotational Torque| F[RG - Reduction Gear]
    F -->|Reduced Speed| G[SH - Propeller Shaft]
    G --> H[PR - Propeller]
    C -->|Aux Power| I[AUX - Auxiliary Loads Hotel Services]
    C -->|Emergency| J[EMG - Emergency Switchboard]
    J --> K[EMD - Emergency Motor]
    K --> G
    CTL[CON - Bridge Control Console] -.->|Speed Command| D
    CTL -.->|Power Demand| A
    CTL -.->|Power Demand| A2
    CTL -.->|Power Demand| A3
    FBG[FEED - Shaft Speed Feedback Sensor] -.-> CTL
    FBG2[FEED2 - Power Meter] -.-> CTL
```

### Diagram Interpretation (for KTU Board Exams)

| Block | Function | KTU Key Concept |
|-------|----------|-----------------|
| DE1 / DE2 / DE3 | Multiple diesel prime movers for redundancy | **Multi-engine redundancy** |
| GEN1 / GEN2 / GEN3 | Convert mechanical to electrical energy | **Faraday's law of induction** |
| MSB | Central power routing and protection | **Electrical distribution** |
| VFD | Controls motor speed via frequency variation | **V/f control strategy** |
| PM | Converts electrical back to mechanical | **Lorentz force principle** |
| RG | Reduces high motor RPM to propeller RPM | **Mechanical gearing** |
| PR | Generates hydrodynamic thrust | **Propeller hydrodynamics** |
| CON | Bridge control with feedback loops | **Closed-loop control** |
| EMG / EMD | Emergency power path for fire/blackout scenarios | **SOLAS compliance** |

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — AC vs DC Diesel-Electric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Diesel-Electric Drive Type] --> Q1{Vessel Application?}
    Q1 -->|Modern Cruise, Naval, Submarine| AC[AC Diesel-Electric Drive]
    Q1 -->|Legacy, Submarine Pre-1990| DC[DC Diesel-Electric Drive]
    AC --> AC1[Use 3-Phase Synchronous Generator]
    AC1 --> AC2[Use Synchronous or Induction Motor]
    AC2 --> AC3[Advantages: Higher Power, No Commutator, Easy Maintenance]
    DC --> DC1[Use DC Compound Generator]
    DC1 --> DC2[Use DC Series or Compound Motor]
    DC2 --> DC3[Advantages: Simple Speed Control, High Starting Torque]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AC is Preferred Today:** Brushless construction, no commutator wear, higher power density, lower maintenance, and suitability for **variable frequency drives (VFDs)** for fine speed control. The last major DC-driven ship class built for the US Navy was the *Spruance*-class destroyer (1975). All modern warships use AC drives.
