# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Diesel-Electric Drive. State any three advantages of using it in modern ships.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A diesel-electric drive is a propulsion system in which the **diesel engine drives a generator** to produce electrical power, which is then supplied to an **electric motor coupled to the propeller shaft**, replacing the conventional mechanical shaft line with an electrical transmission link.

> **Advantages (1 mark each, any 3):**
> 1. **High maneuverability** — instant reversing and speed control by varying motor frequency/voltage.
> 2. **Flexible layout** — engines can be located anywhere; long shaft lines are eliminated.
> 3. **Multi-engine operation** — multiple smaller engines can drive one propeller at part loads with high efficiency.
> 4. **Reduced vibration and noise** in passenger areas (useful in cruise ships).
> 5. **No mechanical alignment issues** between engine and propeller.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Compare AC and DC diesel-electric drive systems on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Tabular form recommended):**

| Parameter | AC Diesel-Electric Drive | DC Diesel-Electric Drive |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Generator | 3-phase synchronous alternator (brushless) | DC shunt/compound generator (with brushes) |
| Motor | Synchronous or induction motor | DC series/compound motor |
| Maintenance | Low (no commutator) | High (brush replacement, commutator wear) |
| Speed Control | Via VFD (frequency variation) | Via field/armature current control |
| Power Capacity | High (up to 25+ MW per motor) | Limited by commutator (~3–5 MW max) |
| Modern Use | Almost all new ships | Legacy submarines, special applications |

> (Any three parameters with clear contrast = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either Q (A) or Q (B)** — internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *Explain the working principle of a Diesel-Electric Drive system with a neat block diagram. List its main components and explain the function of each.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

The diesel-electric drive operates on the principle of **multiple energy conversions** in sequence. One or more diesel engines run at **constant optimal speed** and are coupled to synchronous generators (alternators). The generators produce **3-phase AC electrical power**, which is fed to a **main switchboard** for distribution. From the switchboard, power is supplied to a **propulsion motor** (synchronous or induction) through a **variable frequency drive (VFD)**. The VFD controls the motor's speed and torque by varying the frequency and voltage of the supply. The motor drives a **reduction gear**, which steps down the rotational speed to a value suitable for the **propeller**, producing the thrust that propels the ship.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Diesel Engine → Synchronous Generator → Main Switchboard 
    → VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) → Propulsion Motor 
    → Reduction Gear → Propeller Shaft → Propeller
```

(Students should draw a proper box-and-arrow diagram with all 8 blocks labeled.)

**Main Components and Functions (2 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Diesel Engine | Prime mover — converts fuel chemical energy to mechanical rotation |
| Synchronous Generator | Converts mechanical rotation to 3-phase AC electrical energy |
| Main Switchboard | Distributes and protects the electrical network |
| VFD | Controls motor speed by varying frequency and voltage |
| Propulsion Motor | Converts electrical energy back to mechanical rotation |
| Reduction Gear | Reduces motor RPM to propeller RPM |
| Propeller Shaft | Transmits torque from gear to propeller |
| Propeller | Generates hydrodynamic thrust |

---

> **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]**
> *A ship uses a diesel-electric drive with the following data:*
> - *Diesel engine efficiency: 40%*
> - *Generator efficiency: 95%*
> - *Motor efficiency: 92%*
> - *Reduction gear efficiency: 97%*
> - *Propeller efficiency: 65%*
> - *Fuel input power: 22,000 kW*
>
> *Calculate: (i) Overall propulsive efficiency, (ii) Useful thrust power delivered to the ship.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$\eta_{eng}=0.40$, $\eta_{gen}=0.95$, $\eta_{mot}=0.92$, $\eta_{gear}=0.97$, $\eta_{prop}=0.65$, $P_{in}=22{,}000$ kW

**Part (i) — Overall Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{eng} \times \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{mot} \times \eta_{gear} \times \eta_{prop}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.40 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \times 0.65
$$

Step-wise:
$$
0.40 \times 0.95 = 0.380
$$
$$
0.380 \times 0.92 = 0.3496
$$
$$
0.3496 \times 0.97 = 0.3391
$$
$$
0.3391 \times 0.65 = 0.2204
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 0.2204 \;\text{or}\; 22.04\%}
$$

**Part (ii) — Useful Thrust Power (4 marks):**

$$
P_{thrust} = \eta_{overall} \times P_{in}
$$

$$
P_{thrust} = 0.2204 \times 22{,}000 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{thrust} = 4848.8 \; \text{kW} \approx 4849 \; \text{kW}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *With the help of a schematic, explain the components of a typical AC diesel-electric propulsion system. Also discuss why AC drives are preferred over DC drives in modern vessels.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Schematic (2 marks):**

```
[Prime Mover]  →  [Alternator]  →  [Main Switchboard]
            ↓
    [VFD / Soft Starter]  →  [Synchronous / Induction Motor]
            ↓
    [Reduction Gear]  →  [Shaft]  →  [Propeller]
```

**Component Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Prime Mover:** High-speed or medium-speed diesel engine running at constant speed for stable frequency.
2. **Alternator (Synchronous Generator):** Produces 3-phase AC power; brushless construction.
3. **Main Switchboard:** Houses bus bars, breakers, and protection gear.
4. **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive):** Uses IGBTs/SCRs to vary frequency and voltage, controlling motor speed precisely.
5. **Propulsion Motor:** Synchronous (for high power) or squirrel-cage induction (for reliability).
6. **Reduction Gear & Shaft:** Match motor speed to propeller optimum.
7. **Propeller:** Produces thrust.

**Why AC is Preferred (2 marks — any four):**

1. **No commutator** → lower maintenance, higher reliability.
2. **Higher power density** per unit weight — critical for naval ships.
3. **Easy speed control** via VFD (no need for bulky resistors).
4. **Brushless construction** → suitable for explosive/humid marine environments.
5. **Higher efficiency** at partial loads.
6. **Easier integration** with modern electric auxiliaries and DP systems.

---

> **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]**
> *A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor drives the propeller of a ship. The motor runs at 4% slip. Calculate:*
> *(i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Actual rotor speed, (iii) The line current drawn if the propeller absorbs 900 kW at 0.86 power factor lagging. Assume motor efficiency = 94% and line voltage = 660 V.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f=50$ Hz, $P=6$ poles, $s=0.04$, $P_{mech}=900$ kW, $\cos\phi=0.86$, $\eta_{mot}=0.94$, $V_L=660$ V

**Part (i) — Synchronous Speed (2 marks):**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \times f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_s = 1000 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Actual Rotor Speed (2 marks):**

$$
N = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1000 \times 0.96
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 960 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Line Current (3 marks):**

Electrical input power:
$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{mot}} = \dfrac{900}{0.94} \approx 957.45 \; \text{kW}
$$

For 3-phase AC:
$$
P_{elec} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi
$$

Solving for $I_L$:
$$
I_L = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{957.45 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 660 \times 0.86}
$$

Denominator:
$$
\sqrt{3} \times 660 \times 0.86 = 1.7321 \times 660 \times 0.86 = 982.95
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{957{,}450}{982.95} \approx 974.0 \; \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L \approx 974 \; \text{A}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include all efficiency stages** — Many students multiply only 2–3 efficiencies instead of all 5. The KTU model answer key deducts **1 mark** for each missing stage.
>
> 2. **Confusing DC power formula with 3-phase AC formula** — A 3-phase motor MUST use $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$. Using $P = V \cdot I$ costs **2 full marks**.
>
> 3. **Not converting kW to W consistently** — Mixing kW and V·A in the same equation is a common arithmetic error leading to **wrong order of magnitude answers**. Always convert to base SI units first.
>
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** — In a 7-mark question on working principle, **at least 2 marks** are reserved for the diagram. Drawing a "verbal diagram" without proper labeled boxes loses both marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up slip and speed** — Slip is **dimensionless** (a ratio), while speed has units of **rpm**. Writing $N_s = 120f/P$ without the **120** factor (i.e., writing $N_s = f/P$) is a frequent typo that examiners instantly penalize.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning constant-speed engine operation** — The DED system requires prime movers at **constant speed** for stable frequency. Failing to state this loses 1 mark in the working principle question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Checklist for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **Definition:** DED = Engine → Generator → Motor → Propeller (electrical link replaces shaft).
- [x] **Two Types:** AC (modern) and DC (legacy). AC dominates today's shipbuilding.
- [x] **Key Formula — Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{eng} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{prop}$
- [x] **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$ (must remember the 120, not 60).
- [x] **Slip Relation:** $N = N_s(1-s)$ where $s$ is in decimal (e.g., 0.04 for 4%).
- [x] **3-Phase Power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ — NOT $P = V \cdot I$.
- [x] **DC Power:** $P = V \cdot I$ (no $\sqrt{3}$, no power factor).
- [x] **Typical Overall Efficiency:** 22% – 28% (around 0.22 for typical exam values).
- [x] **Five Advantages:** Maneuverability, layout flexibility, multi-engine operation, low vibration, no alignment.
- [x] **Two Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more energy conversion losses than direct drive.
- [x] **Applications:** Icebreakers, submarines, tugs, DP vessels, cruise ships, modern warships.
- [x] **AC Preferred over DC because:** No commutator, higher power, brushless, VFD control.
- [x] **Memory Anchor:** G-M-S-P = **G**enerator → **M**otor → **S**haft → **P**ropeller.
- [x] **Remember:** Constant-speed engine operation is mandatory for stable AC frequency.
- [x] **Board Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** in working principle questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚛️ Nuclear Power Plants — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Power Plant (NPP):** A thermal power station in which the heat energy required for the generation of steam is liberated by the controlled **nuclear fission** of radioactive isotopes (typically **Uranium-235**, **Plutonium-239**, or **Thorium-232** breeder cycles). The liberated thermal energy is subsequently converted into mechanical work via a **Rankine steam cycle**, which finally drives an electrical generator (or, in marine applications, a propeller shaft).

The heat produced per unit mass of fuel in a nuclear reaction is of the order of **8 × 10¹³ J/kg**, which is approximately **2.5 million times** greater than the heat liberated by burning an equivalent mass of coal (**≈ 3 × 10⁷ J/kg**). This extraordinary energy density is the primary engineering motivation behind nuclear prime movers.

### Relevance to Floating Bodies
Within the context of naval architecture and marine engineering (KTU Module 4 advanced propulsion), nuclear power plants are pivotal for:
- **Nuclear-powered submarines** (e.g., SSN/SSBN classes) where air-independent propulsion (AIP) is mandatory.
- **Nuclear icebreakers** and aircraft carriers requiring virtually unlimited range.
- **Merchant nuclear ships** (NS *Savannah*, NS *Otto Hahn*) explored mid-20th century.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Campfire vs. Wild Fire":**
> Think of a conventional coal/wood fire in a fireplace — useful heat, but uncontrolled and inefficient. A nuclear reactor is analogous to a **highly controlled campfire**: instead of burning carbon with oxygen, we are *splitting heavy atoms* in a precisely moderated environment. The control rods act like a **thermostat** that absorbs neutrons, slowing or speeding the reaction. The moderator is like the "fire's speed bump," ensuring neutrons hit fuel atoms at the right pace to sustain chain reactions without explosion. The containment dome is the **reinforced chimney**, confining any radiation even under worst-case scenarios.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** |
| Energy from 1 U-235 fission | $E_f$ | **≈ 200 MeV** |
| 1 MeV equivalent | – | **1.602 × 10⁻¹³ J** |
| 1 atomic mass unit | $u$ | **1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg** |
| Critical mass of U-235 | – | **≈ 52 kg (sphere)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Physics Behind Nuclear Fission

A **nuclear fission** event occurs when a slow-moving (**thermal**) neutron strikes a fissile nucleus (U-235, U-233, or Pu-239), causing it to split into two lighter fragments, liberate **2–3 fast neutrons**, and release enormous binding energy in the form of kinetic energy of fragments plus gamma radiation.

### Step-by-Step Fission Logic:
1. **Neutron Capture:** A thermal neutron ($E_n \approx 0.025$ eV) is absorbed by U-235 forming unstable U-236.
2. **Nuclear Distortion:** The U-236 nucleus oscillates violently due to excess energy.
3. **Scission:** The nucleus splits into two fission fragments (e.g., Ba-141 + Kr-92) plus **2–3 free neutrons** and γ-rays.
4. **Energy Release:** Total ≈ **200 MeV per fission**, distributed as:
   - Kinetic energy of fragments ≈ **165 MeV**
   - Neutron kinetic energy ≈ **5 MeV**
   - Instantaneous γ-rays ≈ **5–10 MeV**
   - β and γ decay of fragments ≈ **15–25 MeV**

### Chain Reaction — The "Four-Factor Formula" (Four-Factor η)
The **Multiplication Factor (k)** defines the average number of neutrons in one generation that cause fission in the next generation:

$$k = \eta \cdot \epsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = neutron reproduction factor (neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel)
- $\epsilon$ = fast fission factor
- $p$ = resonance escape probability
- $f$ = thermal utilization factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criticality Conditions (KTU High-Yield):**
> - $k < 1$ → **Subcritical** (reaction dies out)
> - $k = 1$ → **Critical** (steady-state, controlled operation)
> - $k > 1$ → **Supercritical** (reaction grows — power excursion)

## 2.2 Mass-Energy Equivalence — Derivation Basis

From Einstein's special relativity, the equivalence between mass defect ($\Delta m$) and energy release is:

$$E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$$

For U-235 fission, total mass of products is approximately **0.1% less** than the reactants. The energy per fission is therefore:

$$E_f = \Delta m \cdot c^2 \approx (3.5 \times 10^{-28}\,\text{kg}) \cdot (3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})^2 \approx 200\,\text{MeV}$$

## 2.3 Thermal Efficiency of NPP

A nuclear power plant is essentially a **Rankine cycle** with a nuclear reactor replacing the boiler. Thermal efficiency is given by:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

For typical PWR plants, net efficiency ≈ **33–37%** (lower than fossil plants due to lower steam temperatures ≈ 300 °C vs. 600 °C in supercritical coal plants).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \Delta m \, c^2$ | Mass-energy equivalence | Joules |
| 2 | $E_f = 200\,\text{MeV/fission}$ | Energy per U-235 fission | MeV |
| 3 | $N_f = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{E_f}$ | Number of fissions required for power $P$ over time $t$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $k = \eta \, \epsilon \, p \, f$ | Four-factor multiplication formula | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Rankine cycle thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{E_f \cdot N_A / M}$ | Fuel consumption rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $CR = \dfrac{V_f}{V_c}$ | Moderator-to-fuel volume ratio (CANDU) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat carried by coolant | Watts |
| 9 | $\tau_{doppler} = -\dfrac{T \, dk}{k \, dT}$ | Doppler reactivity coefficient | pcm/K |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{P_e}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | Specific energy output | MJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The specific energy output ($S$) of nuclear fuel is ≈ **8 × 10⁷ MJ/kg**, whereas coal is only **24 MJ/kg**. This 3-million-fold advantage is why 1 kg of enriched uranium can theoretically replace ~20,000 tonnes of coal in a typical reactor lifetime.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Nuclear propulsion is the only feasible prime mover for:
- **Ballistic Missile Submarines (SSBN)** — indefinite submerged operation.
- **Aircraft Carriers (CVN)** — 25+ year refuelling cycles.
- **Polar Icebreakers** — Russia operates the world's only nuclear icebreaker fleet (*Arktika*-class).
- **Spacecraft/Rovers** — RTGs (Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generators) on *Curiosity*, *Voyager*.
- **Future Maritime:** IAEA is currently evaluating floating NPPs (e.g., Russian *Akademik Lomonosov*) for remote Arctic coastal supply.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Released Per Fission Event

**Given:**
- Mass of U-235 nucleus: $m_{U} = 235.0439\,u$
- Mass of neutron: $m_n = 1.0087\,u$
- Mass of fission fragments (Ba-141 + Kr-92): $m_f = 140.9177\,u + 91.8854\,u = 232.8031\,u$
- Energy-equivalent of 1 u: $931.5\,\text{MeV/c}^2$

**Step 1:** Compute total mass of reactants (LHS):

$$m_{LHS} = m_{U} + m_n = 235.0439\,u + 1.0087\,u = 236.0526\,u$$

**Step 2:** Compute total mass of products (RHS), including 3 emitted neutrons (average):

$$m_{RHS} = m_f + 3\,m_n = 232.8031\,u + 3 \times 1.0087\,u = 235.8292\,u$$

**Step 3:** Compute mass defect ($\Delta m$):

$$\Delta m = m_{LHS} - m_{RHS} = 236.0526\,u - 235.8292\,u = 0.2234\,u$$

**Step 4:** Convert mass defect to energy via $E = \Delta m \, c^2$:

$$E = 0.2234\,u \times 931.5\,\text{MeV/u}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 208.1\,\text{MeV per fission}}$$

> This value matches the experimentally observed **~200 MeV** per fission (allowing for prompt gamma losses and minor fragment variability).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Fissions Required for Given Power Output

**Problem:** A naval nuclear reactor produces **P = 100 MW** of thermal power. How many fissions occur per second?

**Step 1:** Convert power to energy per second:

$$P = 100\,\text{MW} = 100 \times 10^6\,\text{J/s}$$

**Step 2:** Convert energy per fission to Joules:

$$E_f = 200\,\text{MeV} = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Compute fission rate:

$$\dot{N}_f = \frac{P}{E_f} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{3.204 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{N}_f \approx 3.12 \times 10^{18}\,\text{fissions/second}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Even at 100 MW thermal, **3 quadrillion fissions per second** occur. This staggering rate explains why only ~250 kg of U-235 can power a submarine reactor for over 20 years.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Fuel Consumption Rate

**Given:** Reactor thermal output $P_{th} = 3000\,\text{MW}_t$ (typical large commercial PWR). Energy per fission $E_f = 200\,\text{MeV}$. Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$. Molar mass of U-235 $M = 235\,\text{g/mol}$.

**Step 1:** Energy per mole of U-235:

$$E_{mol} = N_A \cdot E_f = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J}$$

$$E_{mol} = 1.929 \times 10^{13}\,\text{J/mol}$$

**Step 2:** Mass consumption rate:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{th} \cdot M}{E_{mol} \cdot 1000}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3 \times 10^9 \cdot 235}{1.929 \times 10^{13} \cdot 1000}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 3.65 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg/s} \approx 3.15\,\text{kg/day}}$$

**Conclusion:** A 3000 MW$_t$ reactor consumes only about **3 kg of U-235 per day** — equivalent energy in coal would be **~9 million kg/day**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reactor Type Comparison

| Parameter | PWR | BWR | CANDU | GCR (Magnox) | LMFBR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Light Water (H₂O) | Light Water (H₂O) | Heavy Water (D₂O) | Graphite | None (Fast) |
| **Coolant** | Pressurized H₂O | Boiling H₂O | D₂O | CO₂ | Liquid Sodium |
| **Fuel** | Enriched UO₂ (3–5%) | Enriched UO₂ | Natural UO₂ | Natural U Metal | PuO₂/UO₂ (MOX) |
| **Pressure (bar)** | 155 | 70 | 90 | 20–40 | 1 (atm) |
| **Coolant T (°C)** | 320 | 285 | 310 | 640 (outlet) | 550 |
| **Efficiency (%)** | 33 | 34 | 30 | 40 | 40 |
| **Marine Application** | Submarines (US) | – | – | – | Future concepts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** PWR is overwhelmingly the most common marine and terrestrial reactor type. **Always remember** the primary circuit (reactor loop) is isolated from the secondary circuit (steam loop) — heat exchange occurs via **steam generators** (U-tube bundle), preventing radioactive contamination of the turbine hall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) Nuclear Power Plant

The following Mermaid block illustrates the functional architecture and heat-flow pathways of a typical PWR-based nuclear power plant, which is the dominant architecture for both terrestrial and marine (submarine) applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[REACTOR CORE\nUranium-235 Fuel Rods\nNeutron-Induced Fission]:::core
    nodeB[REACTOR PRESSURE VESSEL\nRPV - 155 bar\nContainment Structure]:::vessel
    nodeC[PRIMARY COOLANT LOOP\nPressurized H2O at 320 C\nCirculation Pumps]:::loop1
    nodeD[STEAM GENERATOR\nU-Tube Heat Exchanger\nPrimary to Secondary Transfer]:::sg
    nodeE[SECONDARY LOOP\nSaturated Steam at 60 bar\nDrives Turbine]:::loop2
    nodeF[STEAM TURBINE\nHP and LP Stages\nMechanical Power Output]:::turb
    nodeG[ELECTRICAL GENERATOR\nThree-Phase AC\nTypical 1000 MWe]:::gen
    nodeH[CONDENSER\nCold Sea Water Cooling\nSteam to Liquid Water]:::cond
    nodeI[FEEDWATER PUMP\nReturns Water to SG\nClosed Rankine Loop]:::pump
    nodeJ[CONTROL ROD ASSEMBLY\nB4C or Ag-In-Cd\nNeutron Absorption]:::cr
    nodeK[CONTAINMENT DOME\nReinforced Concrete\nBiological Shield]:::dome
    nodeL[SPENT FUEL POOL\nCooling and Storage\nSpent U-235 Bundles]:::sfp
    nodeM[CONDENSATE CLEANUP\nIon Exchange Resins\nWater Treatment]:::treat

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeJ -.->|Regulates k| nodeA
    nodeB -.->|Decay Heat| nodeL
    nodeK === nodeA

    classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef vessel fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    classDef sg fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### Block Diagram — Marine Nuclear Propulsion System (Submarine/Carrier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[SHIP'S REACTOR\nPWR Compact Design\nHe-3 Free Loop]:::mr
    nodeB[PRIMARY LOOP\nH2O at 300 bar\nRC Pumps]:::mlp
    nodeC[STEAM GENERATOR\nCompact Marine Unit\nHeat Exchanger]:::msg
    nodeD[TURBINE SET\nMain Propulsion Turbine\nReduction Gearbox]:::mtg
    nodeE[PROPELLER SHAFT\n20 to 80 MW output\nFixed Pitch]:::mpr
    nodeF[AUX TURBINE\nShip Service Generators\nElectrical Distribution]:::mat
    nodeG[CONDENSER\nSea Water Cooling\nDeep Submergence Rated]:::mcd
    nodeH[CONTROL CONSOLES\nReactor Operator\nEOOW Supervision]:::mcc

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeH -.->|Command| nodeA

    classDef mr fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef mlp fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef msg fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mtg fill:#9370DB,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mpr fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef mat fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mcd fill:#00CED1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mcc fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Component Functional Roles

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Rods** | House U-235 pellets, sustain fission | UO₂ sintered pellets in Zircaloy-4 cladding |
| **Moderator** | Slows fast neutrons to thermal energies | H₂O, D₂O, or graphite |
| **Control Rods** | Absorb neutrons, control k value | B₄C, Ag-In-Cd alloy, Hf |
| **Coolant** | Transfers core heat to steam generator | H₂O, D₂O, Na, CO₂ |
| **Pressure Vessel** | Houses core, contains coolant pressure | Carbon steel with SS liner |
| **Containment** | Final barrier against radiation release | Reinforced concrete + steel liner |
| **Steam Generator** | Transfers primary loop heat to secondary | Inconel U-tubes |
| **Turbine** | Converts thermal to mechanical energy | Chromium-steel blades |
| **Condenser** | Rejects waste heat to environment | Titanium tubes (sea water side) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

*State Einstein's mass-energy equivalence relation. A nuclear fission of U-235 releases approximately 200 MeV of energy. Express this in Joules.*

**Model Answer:**
Einstein's mass-energy equivalence relation states that **mass and energy are interconvertible** as:

$$E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$$

where $\Delta m$ is the mass defect (kg), $c$ is the speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s), and $E$ is the energy released (Joules).

For 200 MeV:

$$E = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J}$$

This is the energy released **per single fission event**. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

*Define the terms "Critical", "Subcritical", and "Supercritical" with reference to a nuclear reactor.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Multiplication Factor (k)** defines reactor state based on the ratio of neutrons in successive generations:

- **Critical ($k = 1$):** The chain reaction is self-sustaining at a constant rate. This is the desired steady-state operating condition of a nuclear reactor.

- **Subcritical ($k < 1$):** The chain reaction decays exponentially with each generation. Reactor is being shut down — control rods are inserted.

- **Supercritical ($k > 1$):** The chain reaction grows exponentially. Used momentarily during startup to ramp up power. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) nuclear power plant. List the major components. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:** A PWR uses pressurized light water as both moderator and primary coolant. The primary loop is maintained at ~155 bar to prevent boiling inside the reactor core. Heat generated by U-235 fission in the fuel rods is transferred via the primary coolant to a **steam generator (SG)**, where it boils the secondary-loop water without mixing. The resulting saturated steam at ~60 bar drives a **turbine**, which is coupled to an **electrical generator**. After expanding, the steam is condensed using sea water cooling and returned to the SG via feedwater pumps, completing the Rankine cycle.

**Major Components:**
1. Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) with fuel assemblies
2. Control rod drive mechanisms
3. Primary coolant pumps (RC pumps)
4. Steam generators (U-tube type)
5. Primary and secondary piping loops
6. Turbine-generator set
7. Condenser and feedwater system
8. Containment building

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A PWR reactor produces 3000 MW of thermal power. Calculate:
(i) The number of fissions occurring per second.
(ii) The daily mass consumption of U-235.
Take energy per fission = 200 MeV. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Fission Rate:**

Convert $E_f = 200\,\text{MeV} = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J}$

$$\dot{N}_f = \frac{P_{th}}{E_f} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{3.204 \times 10^{-11}} = 9.36 \times 10^{19}\,\text{fissions/second}$$

**(ii) Daily Fuel Consumption:**

Energy per mole of U-235:

$$E_{mol} = N_A \cdot E_f = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J} = 1.929 \times 10^{13}\,\text{J/mol}$$

Energy produced per day:

$$E_{day} = P_{th} \times 86400\,\text{s} = 3 \times 10^9 \times 86400 = 2.592 \times 10^{14}\,\text{J/day}$$

Moles consumed per day:

$$n = \frac{2.592 \times 10^{14}}{1.929 \times 10^{13}} = 13.44\,\text{mol/day}$$

Mass per day:

$$m = n \times 235\,\text{g/mol} = 13.44 \times 235 = 3158\,\text{g} \approx 3.16\,\text{kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{N}_f \approx 9.36 \times 10^{19}\,\text{fissions/s}, \quad m_{daily} \approx 3.16\,\text{kg U-235/day}}$$ **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare the four main types of nuclear reactors: PWR, BWR, CANDU, and Gas-Cooled Reactor. Tabulate your answer with respect to moderator, coolant, fuel enrichment, and efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:** *(See Section 3.4 Analytical Matrix table above.)*

| Parameter | PWR | BWR | CANDU | GCR (Magnox) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moderator | Light H₂O | Light H₂O | Heavy D₂O | Graphite |
| Coolant | Pressurized H₂O | Boiling H₂O | D₂O | CO₂ gas |
| Fuel | 3–5% enriched UO₂ | 3–5% enriched UO₂ | Natural UO₂ | Natural Uranium |
| Coolant Pressure (bar) | 155 | 70 | 90 | 20–40 |
| Efficiency (%) | 33 | 34 | 30 | 40 |

**Key Observations:**
- **PWR** dominates marine applications due to compact design and high power density.
- **CANDU** can use natural uranium due to excellent D₂O moderation.
- **GCR** achieves higher outlet temperatures → better efficiency, but large physical size.
- **BWR** has simpler design (no separate SG) but lower pressure. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the energy released per fission of U-235 from mass defect data:

- $m(\text{U-235}) = 235.0439\,u$
- $m(\text{Ba-141}) = 140.9177\,u$
- $m(\text{Kr-92}) = 91.8854\,u$
- $m(\text{neutron}) = 1.0087\,u$
- 3 neutrons emitted per fission.

Calculate the energy in MeV. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — LHS Mass (Reactants):**

$$m_{LHS} = m_{U-235} + m_n = 235.0439 + 1.0087 = 236.0526\,u$$

**Step 2 — RHS Mass (Products):**

$$m_{RHS} = m_{Ba-141} + m_{Kr-92} + 3\,m_n$$

$$m_{RHS} = 140.9177 + 91.8854 + 3 \times 1.0087$$

$$m_{RHS} = 232.8031 + 3.0261 = 235.8292\,u$$

**Step 3 — Mass Defect:**

$$\Delta m = 236.0526 - 235.8292 = 0.2234\,u$$

**Step 4 — Energy Released:**

Using conversion factor $1\,u \equiv 931.5\,\text{MeV}$:

$$E = \Delta m \times 931.5 = 0.2234 \times 931.5$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 208.1\,\text{MeV/fission}}$$

This closely matches the experimentally observed **~200 MeV**, with the difference attributable to prompt gamma emissions and minor fragment variability. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MeV → Joules** in numerical problems. Board examiners deduct **1 mark** if units are not explicitly converted.
> 2. **Confusing $k=1$ with supercritical.** Subcritical ($k<1$) means reaction **dies out**, not "is critical." Write it as "neutron population decreases each generation."
> 3. **Mixing up primary and secondary loops.** The primary loop is radioactive; the secondary loop drives the turbine and is **non-radioactive** under normal operation.
> 4. **Omitting control rod material.** Always name a specific absorber: B₄C, Ag-In-Cd, or Hafnium. Generic answers lose **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets:** If you draw a block diagram in the exam, **label all loops** (primary, secondary, condenser, feedwater). Diagrams without arrows lose marks.
> 6. **Naval/marine context:** When answering for OENST611, emphasize **submerged endurance**, **zero-emission AIP**, and **compactness** — not just terrestrial efficiency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nuclear Power Plants**
> - ⚛️ **Einstein Equation:** $E = \Delta m \, c^2$ — foundation of all fission energy calculations.
> - ⚛️ **Energy per fission:** ~200 MeV (U-235), distributed as KE of fragments (~165 MeV), neutron KE (~5 MeV), γ-rays (~15 MeV), β-decay (~15 MeV).
> - ⚛️ **Multiplication Factor $k = \eta \epsilon p f$** — Criticality requires $k=1$.
> - ⚛️ **PWR is the marine standard** — 155 bar primary pressure, light water moderator/coolant.
> - ⚛️ **Three Loops in PWR:** Primary (radioactive), Secondary (steam), Tertiary (condenser cooling — sea water).
> - ⚛️ **Components to memorize:** Fuel rods (UO₂ + Zircaloy), Control rods (B₄C/Ag-In-Cd), Moderator (H₂O/D₂O), Coolant, RPV, SG, Turbine, Condenser, Containment.
> - ⚛️ **Energy Density:** Nuclear fuel is ~3 million × denser than coal.
> - ⚛️ **Marine Applications:** Submarines (SSN/SSBN), Aircraft Carriers (CVN), Icebreakers — unlimited range, AIP capability.
> - ⚛️ **Critical Mass:** ~52 kg U-235 in spherical geometry without reflector.
> - ⚛️ **Efficiency:** 30–37% (PWR/BWR/CANDU), up to 40% (GCR/LMFBR).
> - ⚛️ **Reactivity Coefficients:** Doppler (negative), moderator temperature (negative in PWR) — self-stabilizing.
> - ⚛️ **Famous Reactor Equation:** $1\,\text{kg U-235} \approx 20{,}000\,\text{tonnes coal equivalent}$.
> - ⚛️ **Safety Systems:** Three independent shutdown systems (SCRAM), Containment dome, ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System).
> - ⚛️ **Spent Fuel:** Initially stored in spent fuel pools for 5–10 years, then dry cask storage or reprocessing.

> 🎯 **Final Examiner Tip:** When writing answers, **always pair definitions with numerical context** (e.g., "200 MeV = $3.2 \times 10^{-11}$ J"). This single habit elevates your answer from "average" to "above average" in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auxiliary Machinery - Power Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auxiliary Machinery - Power Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Auxiliary Machinery & Power Supply — The Heartbeat of a Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary Machinery** refers to all the secondary mechanical and electrical systems on a ship that are essential for the operation of the main propulsion plant, the safety of the vessel, the comfort of the crew, and the execution of the ship's commercial mission — but which are **not** part of the main propulsion engine itself.

### Formal KTU Definition

In naval architecture and marine engineering, the **auxiliary machinery** of a floating body (ship, offshore platform, submarine) encompasses the complete ecosystem of pumps, compressors, generators, purifiers, heat exchangers, steering gear, and electrical distribution systems that provide the supporting services required by the main engine and the ship's hotel load. The **power supply system** is the electrical and mechanical backbone that feeds energy to all these auxiliaries, including the main switchboard, emergency switchboard, shore power connection, and battery banks.

### Key Auxiliary Systems Classified

| Category | Typical Auxiliaries | Purpose |
|----------|--------------------|---------|
| **Electrical Power** | Diesel Generators (DG), Shaft Generators, Emergency Generators, Batteries | Provide AC/DC power throughout the ship |
| **Pumping** | Bilge, Ballast, Fire, Cooling Water, Fuel Oil, Lubricating Oil pumps | Fluid transfer and safety |
| **Compressed Air** | Main Air Compressor, Starting Air Compressor, Control Air Compressor | Engine starting, controls, services |
| **Heat Transfer** | Fresh Water Cooler, Lube Oil Cooler, Air Cooler, Evaporator | Temperature regulation |
| **Steering & Deck** | Steering Gear, Windlass, Mooring Winches, Cranes | Manoeuvring and cargo handling |
| **Fuel/Lube Treatment** | Fuel Purifier, Lube Oil Purifier, Self-Cleaning Filter | Fuel/lube oil conditioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A ship typically requires auxiliary power equivalent to **10–15%** of its main propulsion shaft power. A 30,000 kW bulk carrier, for instance, may have 3 × 1,500 kW diesel generators running in parallel to meet the total electrical demand.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Ship as a Floating City

Think of a modern ship as a **small floating city**. The **main engine** is the heart that pushes the city forward, but a city cannot function without electricity, water, sewage, fire-fighting, and air conditioning. The **auxiliary machinery** is the entire municipal infrastructure of this floating city:

- The **diesel generators** are like the city's power plants.
- The **switchboard** is the city's electrical grid substation.
- The **bilge and ballast pumps** are the city's water and sewage works.
- The **compressed air system** is the city's pneumatic utility grid.
- The **steering gear** is the city's traffic control system.

Without this infrastructure, even the most powerful main engine is useless — the ship cannot sail, cannot dock, cannot keep its crew alive, and cannot load/unload cargo.

### Standard Metrics & Classification Society Rules

- **Standard Voltage on Ships:** **440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase** (international standard) or **380 V, 50 Hz** (European/Asian ships).
- **Emergency Voltage:** **220 V / 24 V DC** for emergency lighting and control circuits.
- **Classification:** Governed by **IACS** (International Association of Classification Societies) members: **ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS, KR**.
- **Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS):** Required for navigational and communication equipment with autonomy of **30 minutes minimum**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Marine Electrical Power Supply System — Architecture

### 2.1 Sources of Electrical Power on a Ship

A modern ship employs a **layered, redundant** power supply architecture. The student must understand each layer and its purpose:

#### Layer 1: Main Generators (Diesel Generator Sets)
- **Prime Mover:** Marine diesel engine (typically 4-stroke, trunk-piston type).
- **Generator:** Synchronous AC generator with automatic voltage regulator (AVR).
- **Power Range:** 500 kW to 5,000 kW per unit.
- **Configuration:** Usually **3 or 4 sets**, with one as standby, operating in parallel on the main switchboard.
- **Frequency:** Maintained at **± 2.5%** of nominal (60 Hz or 50 Hz) by the governor and load sharing controller.

#### Layer 2: Shaft Generator (Take-Home Power)
- Driven by the main propulsion shaft via a gearbox or PTO (Power Take-Off).
- **Advantage:** Saves bunker fuel — the main engine burns cheap heavy fuel oil (HFO), while auxiliary engines burn expensive marine diesel oil (MDO).
- **Use:** Often used in **constant-load, long-voyage** vessels (tankers, bulk carriers).
- **Disadvantage:** Speed varies with ship speed — must use a **frequency converter (PTO/PTI)** to maintain constant frequency.

#### Layer 3: Emergency Generator
- **Location:** Above the bulkhead deck, outside the machinery casing, in a separate emergency generator room.
- **Power Source:** Independent diesel engine with its own fuel tank, starting battery, and cooling.
- **Capacity:** Sufficient to power all **emergency services** (steering gear, fire pumps, emergency lighting, navigational equipment, communication).
- **Autonomy:** Must run continuously for the duration of the SOLAS-required period (**36 hours** for passenger ships, **18 hours** for cargo ships above 500 GT).
- **Starting:** Must start automatically within **45 seconds** of main power failure.

#### Layer 4: Battery Banks (DC Emergency Source)
- **Type:** Lead-acid (vented or VRLA) or increasingly **Lithium-ion**.
- **Voltage:** 24 V DC or 110 V DC.
- **Capacity:** Sufficient to supply emergency lighting for **30 minutes minimum**.
- **Use:** Bridge electronics, GMDSS radio, fire detection, general alarm.

#### Layer 5: Shore Power Connection (Cold Ironing / AMP)
- **Alternative Maritime Power (AMP)**: Connection to the port's electrical grid to shut down shipboard generators while berthed.
- **Voltage/Frequency:** Standardised by **IEC 80005-1** (High Voltage Shore Connection) and **IEC 80005-3** (Low Voltage).
- **Environmental Benefit:** Eliminates port-area emissions from idling generators.

### 2.2 The Main Switchboard (MSB) — The Heart of the Electrical System

The main switchboard is a **dead-front, free-standing, metal-clad** assembly that:

1. Receives power from all running generators via circuit breakers.
2. Distributes power to all large consumers (thrusters, pumps, winches) via **Air Circuit Breakers (ACB)**.
3. Distributes power to small consumers via **Motor Control Centres (MCC)**.
4. Houses the **synchronising panel** for paralleling generators.
5. Contains protection relays (overcurrent, reverse power, earth fault, under-voltage, over-voltage).

### 2.3 Why Multiple Generators in Parallel?

> [!NOTE]
> A single large generator is technically possible but **operationally unacceptable** because:
> - **Reliability:** A single point of failure would blackout the entire ship.
> - **Efficiency:** Diesel engines are most efficient at 75–85% load; running a small generator at 20% load wastes fuel.
> - **Maintenance:** Generators must be overhauled periodically; redundancy allows one unit to be serviced while others carry the load.
> - **Load Following:** At sea the load may be 40% of total capacity; in port with cargo pumps it may be 90% — multiple smaller generators allow optimal loading of each running unit.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed of generator | $$n_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}$$ | rpm | $f$ = frequency (Hz), $p$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Generator apparent power | $$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$$ | kVA | $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current |
| 3 | Real power (3-phase) | $$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$ | kW | $\cos\phi$ = power factor |
| 4 | Reactive power | $$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$ | kVAR | |
| 5 | Generator loading percentage | $$\text{Load \%} = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{rated}} \times 100$$ | % | Should be 60–85% for efficiency |
| 6 | Reverse power threshold | $$P_{rev} = (0.02 \text{ to } 0.06) \times P_{rated}$$ | kW | Trips generator breaker on motoring |
| 7 | Shore power demand (cold ironing) | $$P_{shore} = P_{hotel} + P_{cargo\,aux}$$ | kW | Hotel load + cargo pumps etc. |
| 8 | Emergency battery autonomy | $$C = \frac{I_{load} \cdot t_{autonomy}}{k}$$ | Ah | $k$ = derating factor (0.8 for lead-acid) |
| 9 | Voltage drop in cable | $$\Delta V = I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$$ | V | Must be < 2.5% motor, < 1.5% lighting |
| 10 | Generator efficiency | $$\eta_{gen} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{mech,in}} \times 100$$ | % | Typically 93–97% for large marine units |
| 11 | Frequency droop (load sharing) | $$\Delta f = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_{droop}}{100}$$ | Hz | $R_{droop}$ set at 4–5% |
| 12 | Compressor work (isothermal) | $$W = p_1 V_1 \ln\frac{p_2}{p_1}$$ | J | For starting air compressors |
| 13 | Cooling water pump head | $$H = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{minor}$$ | m | Total head against which pump works |
| 14 | Heat to be removed by cooler | $$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ | kW | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $c_p$ = specific heat |
| 15 | Lube oil flow requirement | $$Q_{lo} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \times \dot{m}_{fuel}$$ | kg/h | Rule of thumb for diesel engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** For generator synchronisation, the following **four conditions** must be satisfied **simultaneously** before closing the breaker: (1) Same **voltage magnitude**, (2) Same **frequency**, (3) Same **phase sequence**, (4) Same **phase angle** (in phase).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The marine power supply system is a **microcosm of the entire electrical grid** — it has generation, transmission, distribution, protection, synchronisation, load sharing, and emergency backup, all packed into a steel room the size of a small apartment. Naval architects and marine engineers who master this topic gain a strong foundation in **integrated power systems** that applies to:
- Offshore platforms (oil rigs, FPSOs)
- Submarines (nuclear and diesel-electric)
- Hybrid-electric tugboats and ferries
- Shore-side microgrids in remote communities

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Derivations and Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed of a Marine Generator

The synchronous speed of an AC generator is the speed at which the rotor's magnetic field rotates. It is governed by the relationship between frequency and the number of magnetic poles.

**Starting point — Definition of frequency:**
Frequency $f$ is the number of cycles per second of the induced EMF. In one full mechanical revolution of a 2-pole machine, one complete AC cycle is produced. In a machine with $p$ poles, one mechanical revolution produces $p/2$ cycles.

**Step 1: Establish the relationship between mechanical speed and electrical cycles.**

Let $n_s$ be the synchronous speed in revolutions per minute (rpm). The number of revolutions per second is:

$$\frac{n_s}{60}$$

**Step 2: Each revolution produces $p/2$ electrical cycles.**

Therefore, the number of cycles per second (which is the frequency $f$) equals:

$$f = \frac{n_s}{60} \cdot \frac{p}{2}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to solve for $n_s$.**

$$f = \frac{n_s \cdot p}{120}$$

$$n_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}$$

**Step 4: Substitute typical marine values.**

For a 4-pole generator running at 60 Hz:

$$n_s = \frac{120 \times 60}{4} = 1800 \text{ rpm}$$

For a 4-pole generator running at 50 Hz:

$$n_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Marine diesel engines driving generators must run at these fixed speeds (1800 or 1500 rpm) regardless of the ship's speed. The ship's main propulsion engine, however, can vary its speed widely — this is why **shaft generators require frequency converters**.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Sizing an Emergency Battery Bank

**Problem (KTU 2024 typical):** A ship's emergency DC system must supply a load of 120 A for 30 minutes during a blackout. Using a lead-acid battery with a derating factor of 0.8, calculate the required battery capacity.

**Given:**
- Load current: $I_{load} = 120$ A
- Autonomy time: $t = 30$ min $= 0.5$ h
- Derating factor: $k = 0.8$

**Step 1: Convert autonomy to hours (or keep in minutes — both are valid).**

We will work in ampere-hours (Ah). For 30 minutes, the discharge is 120 A × 0.5 h = 60 Ah at the 30-minute rate.

**Step 2: Apply the battery capacity formula.**

$$C = \frac{I_{load} \cdot t}{k}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$C = \frac{120 \times 0.5}{0.8}$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$C = \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 5: Select standard size.**

Choose the next standard size: **100 Ah** (commercial availability and safety margin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Battery capacity is rated at a specific discharge rate (typically the 10-hour rate, denoted C10). The actual deliverable capacity is less at high discharge rates (Peukert's effect). The derating factor $k = 0.8$ accounts for aging, temperature derating, and end-of-life margin.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Generator Load Sharing with Frequency Droop

**Problem:** Two generators (DG1: 1000 kW, DG2: 1500 kW) are paralleled on the main switchboard. Both have a 4% frequency droop setting. Initially, the total load is 1800 kW, with each generator loaded proportionally. The ship's load suddenly increases by 400 kW. Calculate the new frequency and the load on each generator.

**Given:**
- DG1 rated: $P_{r1} = 1000$ kW
- DG2 rated: $P_{r2} = 1500$ kW
- Common droop: $R = 4\%$
- Initial load: $P_{total,1} = 1800$ kW
- Load increase: $\Delta P = 400$ kW
- New total load: $P_{total,2} = 2200$ kW
- Nominal frequency: $f_0 = 60$ Hz

**Step 1: Calculate the proportional load sharing.**

Generators with identical droop settings share load in proportion to their ratings:

$$\text{Initial load on DG1} = 1800 \times \frac{1000}{2500} = 720 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Initial load on DG2} = 1800 \times \frac{1500}{2500} = 1080 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate initial loading percentages.**

$$L_1 = \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72\%$$

$$L_2 = \frac{1080}{1500} \times 100 = 72\%$$

**Step 3: Both generators were equally loaded at 72% of rated capacity.**

**Step 4: Apply the droop equation to find new frequency.**

The droop $R$ is defined as the percentage frequency drop from no-load to full-load:

$$R = \frac{f_{nl} - f_{fl}}{f_{nl}} \times 100$$

With identical droop, the new frequency is determined by the combined governor characteristic. Each generator picks up load in proportion to its rating.

$$\text{New load on DG1} = 2200 \times \frac{1000}{2500} = 880 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{New load on DG2} = 2200 \times \frac{1500}{2500} = 1320 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Calculate new loading percentages.**

$$L_1^{new} = \frac{880}{1000} \times 100 = 88\%$$

$$L_2^{new} = \frac{1320}{1500} \times 100 = 88\%$$

**Step 6: Find the frequency drop.**

Each generator's loading increased by 16% (from 72% to 88%). The frequency drop is:

$$\Delta f = 88\% \times 4\% \times 60 \text{ Hz} = 0.88 \times 0.04 \times 60 = 2.112 \text{ Hz}$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully. The droop equation states:

$$\Delta f = f_{nl} - f = \left(\frac{P}{P_{rated}}\right) \times R \times f_{nl}$$

**Step 7: Recompute with this formula.**

$$\Delta f = 0.88 \times 0.04 \times 60 = 2.112 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{new} = 60 - 2.112 = 57.888 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 8: Check against IACS tolerance.**

IACS allows $\pm 5\%$ frequency tolerance transiently and $\pm 2.5\%$ steady-state. The new frequency of 57.89 Hz is 3.5% below nominal — this is **outside steady-state tolerance**, and the load-sharing controller or generator capacity is inadequate. **The operator would start a third generator.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Applying the droop equation using the *increase* in load percentage (16%) instead of the *new total* loading percentage (88%). Always use the absolute loading percentage relative to the rated capacity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Auxiliary System Inter-Relations

| System | Provides to / Receives from | Failure Consequence |
|--------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Main Generator (DG) | Supplies MSB; Receives HFO from settling/service tanks | Total blackout |
| Emergency Generator | Receives auto-start signal from MSB | Loss of emergency services |
| Main Air Compressor | Supplies starting air to main engine, control air | Cannot start main engine |
| Lube Oil Pump | Supplies LO to main engine bearings | Bearing seizure, engine damage |
| Cooling Sea Water Pump | Supplies heat exchangers (FW, LO, AC) | Engine overheat, blackout |
| Fresh Water Generator (Evaporator) | Produces FW from sea water | No potable/technical water |
| Steering Gear | Receives power from MSB + emergency | Loss of ship control |
| Bilge Pump | Receives power from MSB + emergency | Flooding risk |
| Fire Pump | Receives power from MSB + emergency | Inability to fight fire |
| Fuel Purifier | Receives power from MSB, supplies clean HFO | Fuel injector damage, engine shutdown |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Synchronisation Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DG2 READY TO PARALLEL"]:::start --> B["CHECK VOLTAGE MATCH\nV1 = V2 +- 5 percent"]:::check
    B --> C{"VOLTAGE OK?"}
    C -- "NO" --> B1["ADJUST DG2 AVR\nVOLTAGE RAISE OR LOWER"]:::adj
    B1 --> B
    C -- "YES" --> D["CHECK FREQUENCY MATCH\nF2 SLIGHTLY HIGHER THAN F1"]:::check
    D --> E{"FREQUENCY OK?"}
    E -- "NO" --> E1["ADJUST DG2 GOVERNOR\nSPEED RAISE OR LOWER"]:::adj
    E1 --> D
    E -- "YES" --> F["CHECK PHASE SEQUENCE\nABC SAME ORDER"]:::check
    F --> G["WAIT FOR SYNCHROSCOPE\nROTATION IN FAST DIRECTION"]:::check
    G --> H{"SYNC LAMP DARK\nOR SYNCHROSCOPE AT 12?"}
    H -- "NO" --> G
    H -- "YES" --> I["CLOSE ACB\nWITHIN 1 CYCLE"]:::action
    I --> J["PARALLEL OPERATION\nLOAD SHARING ACTIVE"]:::done
    J --> K["MONITOR kW kVAR\nLOAD REACTIVE POWER"]:::mon
```
