<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List any four conditions that must be satisfied before a marine generator can be paralleled with another running generator on the main switchboard.

**Model Answer:**

The four synchronising conditions (often remembered as **"VVPP"** — Voltage, frequency, Phase sequence, Phase angle) are:

1. **Same Voltage Magnitude:** The incoming generator voltage must equal the busbar voltage within **± 5%** tolerance.
2. **Same Frequency:** The incoming generator frequency must match the busbar frequency within **± 0.5 Hz**; preferably slightly higher so that the incoming machine takes load immediately upon closing.
3. **Same Phase Sequence:** The phase rotation (R-Y-B) of the incoming generator must match the busbar (otherwise severe short-circuit results).
4. **Same Phase Angle:** At the instant of breaker closing, the phase voltages of the incoming machine and the busbar must be **in phase** (synchroscope needle at the 12 o'clock position, or synchronising lamp dark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between the main generator and the emergency generator on a ship. Mention the SOLAS requirement for emergency generator starting.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Main Generator | Emergency Generator |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Location** | Engine room / generator room | Above bulkhead deck, outside machinery casing |
| **Power Source** | Diesel engine + alternator | Independent diesel + alternator |
| **Voltage** | 440 V AC, 3-phase | 220 V AC, 3-phase (or 24/110 V DC via battery) |
| **Number** | 3–4 sets in normal operation | 1 set, dedicated to emergencies |
| **Busbar** | Main Switchboard (MSB) | Emergency Switchboard (ESB) |
| **Starting** | Manual / automatic from bridge | **Automatic** within 45 seconds of blackout |
| **Autonomy** | Continuous | 18 hours (cargo ship > 500 GT) / 36 hours (passenger ship) |
| **Fuel** | HFO (with MDO for small sets) | Separate dedicated day tank with MDO/DO |

**SOLAS Requirement:** The emergency generator must **start automatically** and connect to the emergency switchboard within **45 seconds** of loss of main power. The starting energy may be from batteries, compressed air, or an independent hydraulic accumulator.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Choose either A or B]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete electrical power supply system of a modern ship, including main generators, emergency generator, shore power, battery bank, main switchboard, and emergency switchboard. State the standard marine voltage and frequency.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A ship has three diesel generators rated 800 kW, 1000 kW, and 1000 kW. Two units (DG1 = 800 kW, DG2 = 1000 kW) are running in parallel with a total connected load of 1500 kW. DG1 supplies 640 kW and DG2 supplies 860 kW. The load sharing is found to be incorrect. Calculate the correct load distribution and identify the cause of the imbalance. Also, state two consequences of unequal load sharing.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The block diagram of a ship's power supply system is shown below:

```
[Shore Power]    [Main DG1]  [Main DG2]  [Main DG3]  [Shaft Gen]
     |              |            |            |             |
     +------+-------+------+-----+------+-----+------+------+
            |                                            
            v                                            
     +-------------+        +---------------+        
     | MAIN        |        | EMERGENCY     |        
     | SWITCHBOARD |--tie-->| SWITCHBOARD   |        
     | 440V 60Hz   |  breaker| 220V         |        
     +------+------+        +-------+-------+        
            |                       |                
            |                       |                
       +----+----+           +------+-----+    
       |MCC (large|          |Critical loads| 
       |consumers)|          |navigation etc|   
       +-----+----+          +--------------+    
            |                               
    +-------+--------+--------+----------+    
    |       |        |        |          |    
 Steer Gear  Pumps  Compressors Purifier  Hotel
```

**Standard Marine Voltage:** **440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase AC** (international) or **380 V, 50 Hz** (European/Asian).

**Description of Each Block:**

1. **Main Diesel Generators (3 sets):** Each consists of a marine diesel engine (4-stroke, trunk-piston) coupled to a 3-phase synchronous alternator with AVR. They run in parallel on the MSB and share load through their governors (droop setting) and the load-sharing controller.

2. **Main Switchboard (MSB):** A free-standing, metal-clad, dead-front panel. Houses synchronising equipment, ACBs, protection relays, and the generator control panels.

3. **Shaft Generator:** Optional PTO/PTI from the main engine, with a frequency converter to maintain constant 60 Hz.

4. **Shore Power (Cold Ironing / AMP):** A shore connection socket (high voltage per IEC 80005-1 or low voltage) that allows the ship to receive power from the port's grid when berthed, eliminating in-port emissions.

5. **Emergency Switchboard (ESB):** Normally fed from the MSB through a **tie-line circuit breaker**. On blackout, it is automatically disconnected from the MSB and fed by the emergency generator and battery.

6. **Emergency Diesel Generator:** Independent set, located above the bulkhead deck. Starts automatically in **≤ 45 seconds** of blackout.

7. **Battery Bank (24V / 110V DC):** Feeds bridge equipment, GMDSS, fire detection. Also serves as the starting energy for the emergency DG.

8. **Motor Control Centre (MCC):** A secondary switchboard that distributes power to small motors through motor starters, contactors, and overload relays.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- DG1 rated: $P_{r1} = 800$ kW
- DG2 rated: $P_{r2} = 1000$ kW
- Total connected load: $P_{total} = 1500$ kW
- Current actual load: DG1 = 640 kW, DG2 = 860 kW

**Step 1: Verify the actual total.**
$640 + 860 = 1500$ kW ✓

**Step 2: Calculate the loading percentages.**

$$L_{1,actual} = \frac{640}{800} \times 100 = 80\%$$

$$L_{2,actual} = \frac{860}{1000} \times 100 = 86\%$$

**Step 3: Identify the imbalance.**

For correct load sharing with identical droop settings, both generators should be loaded at the **same percentage** of their ratings. The actual loading differs by **6%** (86% − 80%).

**Step 4: Calculate the correct load distribution.**

Correct shared loading percentage:

$$L_{correct} = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{r1} + P_{r2}} \times 100 = \frac{1500}{1800} \times 100 = 83.33\%$$

**Step 5: Apply the correct loading to each generator.**

$$P_{1,correct} = 0.8333 \times 800 = 666.67 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{2,correct} = 0.8333 \times 1000 = 833.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Identify the cause.**

The cause of unequal load sharing is **mismatched governor droop settings** or **improper calibration of the load-sharing controller**. The generator with the higher droop takes less load; the one with lower droop takes more.

**Step 7: Consequences of unequal load sharing.**

1. **Uneven wear:** The generator carrying higher load (DG2 at 86%) experiences faster thermal aging of windings, accelerated lube oil degradation, and reduced maintenance intervals.

2. **Frequency drift:** If the imbalance is severe, the system frequency may drift outside the ± 2.5% tolerance, causing motor speed variation and tripping of frequency-sensitive equipment.

3. **Risk of overload:** If the load increases, the already-heavily-loaded generator may trip on overload, cascading to total blackout.

4. **Fuel inefficiency:** Generators operating off-design load points consume more specific fuel oil (SFO) per kWh.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Explain with a sketch the construction and working of a marine starting air compressor system. State the typical air pressure required to start a slow-speed marine diesel engine. List four safety devices fitted to the compressed air system.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A ship's lubricating oil cooler must cool 60,000 kg/h of oil from 65 °C to 50 °C using sea water entering at 28 °C and leaving at 38 °C. The specific heat of oil is 2.1 kJ/kg·K. Calculate: (i) Heat to be removed by the cooler in kW. (ii) Required sea water mass flow rate, given $c_{p,sw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Starting Air Compressor System:**

The starting air system provides high-pressure air to the main engine's air-starting valves to turn the engine over until combustion begins. It consists of:

1. **Main Air Compressor (2 or 3 stage, oil-cooled):** Compresses ambient air to **30 bar** (typical for slow-speed 2-stroke main engines). Modern 4-stroke auxiliaries may use 25–30 bar starting pressure.

2. **Air Receiver / Starting Air Bottle:** A pressure vessel that stores compressed air. Two bottles are provided — one for service, one as backup. Capacity: sufficient for **12 consecutive starts** of the main engine without recharging.

3. **Pressure Reducing Valve (Starting Air Control):** Reduces the air receiver pressure to the engine's required starting pressure (typically 7–10 bar at the air-starting valve on the engine).

4. **Main Starting Air Valve:** Manually or pneumatically operated valve that admits air to the engine's distributor.

5. **Safety Devices:**
   - **Pressure Relief Valve** on the air receiver (set at 1.1 × design pressure)
   - **Bursting Disc** (safety diaphragm) for fire protection
   - **Pressure Gauge** (master and local)
   - **Low-pressure Alarm** at the starting air manifold
   - **Drain Valves** for removing moisture and oil from the receiver
   - **Temperature Switch** in the cooler to prevent overheating
   - **Safety Interlock** to prevent starting with insufficient air pressure

**Working:** Air is drawn in through a filter, compressed in multiple stages with inter-cooling, then discharged into the air receiver. From the receiver, air is reduced in pressure and routed to the engine's starting air manifold, which distributes it sequentially to each cylinder in firing order through the air-starting valves.

**Typical Starting Pressure for Slow-Speed Marine Diesel:** **30 bar** (with reduction to 7–10 bar at the cylinder).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- Oil mass flow: $\dot{m}_{oil} = 60{,}000$ kg/h = $16.667$ kg/s
- Oil inlet temperature: $T_{oil,in} = 65$ °C
- Oil outlet temperature: $T_{oil,out} = 50$ °C
- SW inlet: $T_{sw,in} = 28$ °C
- SW outlet: $T_{sw,out} = 38$ °C
- $c_{p,oil} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K
- $c_{p,sw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

**Part (i): Heat to be removed.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} \cdot (T_{oil,in} - T_{oil,out})$$

$$Q = 16.667 \times 2.1 \times (65 - 50)$$

$$Q = 16.667 \times 2.1 \times 15$$

$$Q = 525 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (ii): Required sea water mass flow rate.**

Energy balance (assuming no losses):

$$Q = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})$$

$$525 = \dot{m}_{sw} \times 4.18 \times (38 - 28)$$

$$525 = \dot{m}_{sw} \times 4.18 \times 10$$

$$525 = \dot{m}_{sw} \times 41.8$$

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{525}{41.8} = 12.56 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = 12.56 \times 3600 = 45{,}216 \text{ kg/h} \approx 45.2 \text{ t/h}$$

**In volumetric terms (assuming sea water density 1025 kg/m³):**

$$Q_{sw,volumetric} = \frac{45{,}216}{1025} = 44.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 0.733 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert kg/h to kg/s before calculating kW. **Always convert first.**
> 2. **Heat Sign Convention:** Heat removed (Q) is positive when the fluid cools. Do not insert a negative sign.
> 3. **LMTD vs Simple ΔT:** For a single-pass shell-and-tube cooler, the simple ΔT method assumes both fluids have the same heat capacity, which is **not** the case here. The simple ΔT is used because we are given the inlet/outlet temperatures of both streams separately. **Always use the more direct energy balance approach shown above.**
> 4. **Density for Sea Water:** Use 1025 kg/m³ (not 1000) for sea water in marine problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist: Auxiliary Machinery & Power Supply**

- **Power Sources on a Ship:** Main DGs (3–4), Shaft Generator (optional), Emergency DG, Battery Bank, Shore Power (AMP).
- **Standard Marine Voltage/Frequency:** **440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase AC** (international). Emergency: **220 V / 24 V DC**.
- **Synchronising Conditions (VVPP):** Voltage, frequency, Phase sequence, Phase angle — all must match before closing the ACB.
- **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $n_s = 120f / p$ (rpm). For 60 Hz / 4-pole → 1800 rpm.
- **Droop Setting:** Typically **4–5%** for marine governors; enables proportional load sharing.
- **Reverse Power Protection:** Trips at **2–6%** of rated power to detect motoring (prime mover failure).
- **Emergency Generator Auto-Start:** Must connect to ESB within **45 seconds** of blackout (SOLAS).
- **Emergency Autonomy:** **18 hours** for cargo ships > 500 GT; **36 hours** for passenger ships.
- **Battery Autonomy for Emergency Lighting:** Minimum **30 minutes**.
- **Starting Air Pressure:** **30 bar** in receiver; **7–10 bar** at engine air-starting valve.
- **Compressor Stages:** 2 or 3 stages with inter-cooling for marine starting air compressors.
- **Shaft Generator:** Uses frequency converter (PTO/PTI) to maintain constant 60 Hz from variable-speed main shaft.
- **Shore Power (AMP):** Standardised by **IEC 80005-1** (HV) and **IEC 80005-3** (LV).
- **Classification Society Rules:** All auxiliary machinery must comply with **IACS** unified requirements (UR) and individual class rules (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS).
- **Lube Oil Cooler Energy Balance:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — convert units **first** to kg/s and K.
- **Seawater Density:** Use **1025 kg/m³** in marine heat exchanger problems.
- **Load Priority:** Essential (steering, fire, navigation) > Semi-essential (compressors, purifiers) > Non-essential (hotel) > Cargo services.
- **Fuel Purifier Function:** Removes water and sediment from HFO before it enters the main engine service tank.
- **Common Failure Mode:** Cooling sea water pump failure → all heat exchangers lose cooling → engine overheat → blackout. **Redundancy is essential.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auxiliary Engines for Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auxiliary Engines for Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Auxiliary Engines for Ship** are prime movers (engines and turbines) installed onboard a vessel **other than the main propulsion engine**, dedicated to driving the various auxiliary machinery essential for the safe operation, navigation, cargo handling, and crew welfare of the ship. They form the *heart of the ship's power plant auxiliaries* and typically drive generators, pumps, compressors, and purifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OENST611, Module 4):**
> *"Auxiliary engines are secondary power-generating units aboard a ship that supplement the main engine by providing electrical, mechanical, and thermal energy for supporting systems such as lighting, ventilation, steering, refrigeration, and navigation. They are the lifeline of a modern merchant or naval vessel."*

The auxiliary power system of a ship is generally rated between **5% to 15%** of the main engine shaft power, depending on the vessel type (LNG carrier, container ship, naval frigate, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Load" of a Ship

Think of a **large cruise ship or a modern city block**:

- The **Main Engine** is like the **engine of a moving bus** — it makes the vehicle go.
- The **Auxiliary Engines** are like the **small generator and HVAC systems of a building** — they keep the lights on, the air conditioning running, the water pumps working, and the refrigerators cold, even when the bus is parked.

In the marine world, this entire non-propulsion demand is collectively called the **"Hotel Load"** or **"Service Load"** of the ship. Auxiliary engines are the workhorses that meet this demand — both at sea and especially in port, when the main engine is shut down.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Ship Auxiliary Engines

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Functions (Exam-Favorite):**
> 1. **Electrical Power Generation** — Through diesel generators (DGs) for lighting, navigation, communication, galley, and control systems.
> 2. **Pumping Services** — Bilge, ballast, fire, cooling, and fuel transfer pumps.
> 3. **Compressed Air Supply** — Starting air for main engines, service air, control air.
> 4. **Steam Generation** — Auxiliary boilers for heating, fuel oil heating, laundry, and galley.
> 5. **Refrigeration** — Provision cooling plants for food preservation.
> 6. **Deck Machinery Drive** — Mooring winches, anchor windlasses, cargo cranes.
> 7. **Steering & Stabilization** — Hydraulic power packs for rudder and fin stabilizers.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must Remember)

| Metric | Standard Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Auxiliary Engine Power Output | **250 kW – 5000 kW** per unit |
| Number of DG sets onboard | **3 to 4** (2 running, 1 standby) |
| Ship's Service Voltage | **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** (or 380 V / 50 Hz) |
| Emergency Generator | **75 kW – 500 kW** |
| Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) | **190 – 210 g/kWh** |
| Auxiliary Engine Speed | **900 rpm / 720 rpm** (4-stroke medium speed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember the *emergency generator* is a **separate** unit that can start automatically (within **45 seconds**) when the main power fails — this is a SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) regulatory requirement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ship Auxiliary Engines

### A. By Prime Mover Type

**1. Diesel Generators (DGs) — Most Common**
- **4-stroke, medium-speed, trunk-piston diesel engines** are the workhorses.
- Drive **AC synchronous generators** (typically 440 V / 60 Hz).
- Operating speed: **900 rpm** (60 Hz) or **750 rpm** (50 Hz).
- They are simple, efficient, and can run on **HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil)** in port with the HFO purifier running.

**2. Turbo-Generators (Steam Turbine Driven)**
- Driven by **auxiliary steam turbines** fed from the **auxiliary boiler** or **exhaust gas boiler (EGB)**.
- Provide **clean, vibration-free power** preferred for icebreakers, research vessels, and naval auxiliaries.
- Lower thermal efficiency but high reliability.

**3. Shaft Generators**
- Electrically coupled to the **main propulsion shaft** via a gearbox.
- Useful at long transit speeds to save auxiliary fuel.
- **Power Take-Out (PTO)** mode: Main engine drives the shaft generator.
- **Power Take-In (PTI)** mode: Shaft generator acts as a motor (hybrid propulsion).

**4. Gas Turbine Generators**
- Used in **naval combatants** (frigates, destroyers) for compact, high-power-density generation.
- Lightweight but high specific fuel consumption.

**5. Emergency Diesel Generators**
- Independent, automatically starting unit.
- Located above the **bulkhead deck**, in a separate compartment.
- Fuel tank capacity for **36 hours** of full-load operation.

### B. By Function

| Auxiliary Engine Type | Function | Power Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Diesel Generator (DG)** | Electrical power | 500 – 5000 kW |
| **Auxiliary Boiler** | Steam for fuel heating, heating | 1 – 25 tonnes/hr |
| **Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB)** | Waste heat recovery → steam | 0.5 – 15 tonnes/hr |
| **Air Compressor (Motor Driven)** | Starting & service air | 30 – 100 m³/hr |
| **Refrigeration Compressor** | Provision cooling | 50 – 300 kW |
| **Fire Pump Engine (Diesel)** | Emergency firefighting | 50 – 500 kW |

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working of a Diesel Generator Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Heart of a Ship's Electrical System — DG Set Working Principle**

1. **Fuel Oil System** → HFO/DO is drawn from service tank, heated to **120 – 150 °C**, and injected at high pressure into the cylinder.
2. **Combustion** → Fuel burns, producing high-temperature, high-pressure gases.
3. **Power Stroke** → The gases push the piston down, rotating the crankshaft.
4. **Lubrication** → Cross-head bearings and cylinder lubrication via forced lubrication system.
5. **Cooling** → Fresh water (primary) and sea water (secondary) cool the engine via heat exchangers.
6. **Generator Coupling** → The crankshaft drives a **3-phase synchronous AC generator** (saliant-pole type).
7. **Excitation** → The rotor is excited via **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** at controlled DC current.
8. **Power Distribution** → Output (e.g., 440 V, 60 Hz) goes to the **main switchboard**, then to consumers.
9. **Governor Control** → **Hydraulic or electronic governor** maintains constant speed (±0.5%) under load changes.

## 2.3 Why Auxiliary Engines? (Engineering Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Auxiliaries — Exam-Worthy Logic:**

- **Decoupling**: A fault in auxiliary systems must not affect the main propulsion (e.g., a bilge pump failure must not stall the main engine).
- **Efficiency**: Main engines are tuned for **propulsion efficiency at constant RPM**, not for variable electrical loads.
- **Port Operation**: When the ship is in port, the main engine is off, but **hotel load continues** — auxiliaries feed this.
- **Redundancy**: Multiple DG sets provide **N+1 redundancy** for safety.
- **Flexibility**: Different DG sets can be paralleled (synchronized) based on real-time load.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{elec} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | 3-phase electrical power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ | Mechanical power from torque | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{elec,out}}{P_{mech,in}} \times 100\%$ | Generator efficiency | % |
| 4 | $SFOC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{elec,out}} \times 1000$ | Specific Fuel Oil Consumption | g/kWh |
| 5 | $m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{demand}}{h_g - h_f}$ | Steam mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 6 | $Q_{loss,total} = Q_{exhaust} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{radiation}$ | Heat balance losses | kW |
| 7 | $P_{aux,total} = P_{hotel} + P_{machinery} + P_{cargo}$ | Total auxiliary power | kW |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{p \cdot N}{120}$ | Frequency from synchronous generator (p = poles) | Hz |
| 9 | $Q_{EGB} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{exh,in} - T_{exh,out})$ | Exhaust gas boiler heat recovery | kW |
| 10 | $V_{air,comp} = \dfrac{P_{1} \cdot V_{1} \cdot T_{2}}{P_{2} \cdot T_{1}}$ | Compressor volumetric relation | m³/min |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$, students often confuse **line voltage $V_L$** with **phase voltage $V_{ph}$**. For 3-phase star: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$. For delta: $V_L = V_{ph}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Marine Applications

- **Container Ships (8,000 TEU+)**: 4 × 3000 kW DG sets + 1 × 500 kW emergency DG.
- **VLCC Tankers**: 3 × 1500 kW DG sets + 1 × 350 kW emergency DG + shaft generator on main engine.
- **LNG Carriers**: Steam turbine driven auxiliaries (using cargo boil-off gas as fuel — *dual-fuel mode*).
- **Naval Frigates**: 4 × gas turbine generators (each ~2 MW) for compact footprint.
- **Offshore Platforms**: Combined diesel-electric and gas turbine systems for drilling & dynamic positioning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Generator Frequency from Pole-Pairs

Let us derive the fundamental relationship between engine speed, number of poles, and output frequency of a ship's AC generator.

**Given:**
- $N$ = engine speed in revolutions per minute (rpm)
- $p$ = total number of magnetic poles
- $f$ = electrical frequency in Hertz (Hz)

**Step 1:** In one full mechanical revolution, each pole produces one alternation of voltage (one North-South sweep). With $p$ poles, the number of electrical cycles per mechanical revolution is $p/2$.

$$ \text{Electrical cycles per revolution} = \dfrac{p}{2} $$

**Step 2:** In one minute, the engine completes $N$ revolutions. Therefore, the total number of electrical cycles per minute is:

$$ \text{Cycles per minute} = N \cdot \dfrac{p}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Frequency is the number of cycles per **second**, not per minute. Converting using the 60 s/min factor:

$$ f = \dfrac{N \cdot p}{2 \cdot 60} = \dfrac{N \cdot p}{120} $$

**Step 4:** Rearranging to find the required engine speed for a desired frequency:

$$ N = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{p} $$

### Worked Numerical Example

A ship's diesel generator has **8 poles** and must deliver **60 Hz** power. Calculate the required engine speed.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{p} \\[6pt]
N &= \dfrac{120 \cdot 60}{8} \\[6pt]
N &= \dfrac{7200}{8} \\[6pt]
N &= 900 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** This is the standard speed for **60 Hz** marine alternators — 900 rpm for 8-pole machines, and 1800 rpm for 4-pole machines. Both are valid; medium-speed 900 rpm is preferred for direct coupling to large diesel engines.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Auxiliary Load Estimation for a Ship

To size the auxiliary engine capacity, the **total electrical load** must be estimated accurately. KTU examiners love this as a 14-mark analytical question.

**Step 1:** Identify the load categories:

$$
P_{aux,total} = P_{hotel} + P_{machinery} + P_{cargo} + P_{safety}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply a **diversity factor (DF)** because not all consumers run simultaneously. The simultaneous maximum demand is:

$$
P_{max,demand} = DF \cdot P_{aux,total}
$$

Typical $DF = 0.6$ to $0.8$ for merchant ships.

**Step 3:** Apply a **service factor (SF)** to account for future load growth and transient spikes:

$$
P_{DG,rating} = SF \cdot P_{max,demand}
$$

Typical $SF = 1.15$ to $1.25$.

### Worked Numerical Example

A 50,000 DWT bulk carrier has the following auxiliary loads (in kW):

| Category | Load (kW) |
|---|---|
| Hotel (lighting, HVAC, galley) | 800 |
| Machinery (pumps, compressors) | 1200 |
| Cargo handling (cranes) | 600 |
| Safety (fire pumps, navigation) | 200 |

If $DF = 0.75$ and $SF = 1.20$, design the DG set configuration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total connected load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{aux,total} &= 800 + 1200 + 600 + 200 \\
P_{aux,total} &= 2800 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum simultaneous demand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max,demand} &= DF \cdot P_{aux,total} \\
P_{max,demand} &= 0.75 \cdot 2800 \\
P_{max,demand} &= 2100 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** DG set rating (with service factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{DG,rating} &= SF \cdot P_{max,demand} \\
P_{DG,rating} &= 1.20 \cdot 2100 \\
P_{DG,rating} &= 2520 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Select **3 × 1500 kW** DG sets → 2 running + 1 standby (N+1 redundancy).

$$
P_{installed} = 2 \cdot 1500 = 3000 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{meets the 2520 kW demand})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Sharing:** In parallel operation, the running DG sets share load equally. For two 1500 kW sets, each delivers **1260 kW** at 84% loading — ideal for diesel efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Balance of a Ship's Auxiliary Boiler

The auxiliary boiler efficiency is determined from a heat balance calculation. **A standard KTU 14-mark derivation question.**

**Given:**
- Fuel mass flow: $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/h
- Calorific value of fuel: $CV$ in kJ/kg
- Steam generation rate: $\dot{m}_s$ in kg/h
- Enthalpy of steam: $h_s$ in kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of feed water: $h_f$ in kJ/kg

**Step 1:** Total heat input from fuel:

$$
Q_{input} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \quad [\text{kJ/h}]
$$

**Step 2:** Useful heat absorbed by steam (latent + sensible):

$$
Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_s - h_f) \quad [\text{kJ/h}]
$$

**Step 3:** Heat losses (dry flue gas + moisture + radiation + unburnt):

$$
Q_{loss} = Q_{dry,flue} + Q_{H_2O} + Q_{rad} + Q_{unburnt}
$$

**Step 4:** Boiler thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100\%
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A marine auxiliary boiler consumes **200 kg/h** of HFO with $CV = 42,000$ kJ/kg. It generates **1500 kg/h** of dry saturated steam at **7 bar** ($h_s = 2763$ kJ/kg). Feed water enters at **80 °C** ($h_f = 335$ kJ/kg). Total heat losses are **8%** of input. Calculate the boiler efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Heat input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{input} &= \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \\
Q_{input} &= 200 \cdot 42000 \\
Q_{input} &= 8.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Useful heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{useful} &= \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_s - h_f) \\
Q_{useful} &= 1500 \cdot (2763 - 335) \\
Q_{useful} &= 1500 \cdot 2428 \\
Q_{useful} &= 3.642 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Efficiency (direct method):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{boiler} &= \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100\% \\
\eta_{boiler} &= \dfrac{3.642 \times 10^{6}}{8.4 \times 10^{6}} \times 100\% \\
\eta_{boiler} &= 43.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A marine auxiliary boiler efficiency of **80 – 88%** is industry standard. Our calculated 43.36% indicates the input is overstated or the operating condition is partial load. KTU examiners expect a value close to **80 – 85%** for a fully designed boiler with economizer and air preheater.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Auxiliary Engine Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Diesel Generator | Turbo-Generator | Shaft Generator | Emergency DG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Range** | 500 – 5000 kW | 500 – 3000 kW | 1000 – 10000 kW | 75 – 500 kW |
| **Efficiency** | High (38 – 42%) | Medium (25 – 30%) | High (coupled to main) | Medium (35%) |
| **Fuel** | HFO / DO / LNG | Steam (any fuel) | Shaft power | Diesel Oil only |
| **Starting Time** | 30 – 60 s | 5 – 10 min (warm-up) | Coupled to main | **< 45 s (SOLAS)** |
| **Use Case** | All ships | LNG carriers, naval | Tankers, bulk carriers | All ships (mandatory) |
| **Vibration** | Moderate | Low | Very Low | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Moderate | High (turbine blades) | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ship Auxiliary Engine System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical merchant ship's auxiliary engine system, showing how the prime movers, generators, and consumers interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Propulsion Engine] -->|Shaft| B[Shaft Generator PTO/PTI]
    B -->|AC Power| C[Main Switchboard 440V]
    D1[Diesel Generator 1] --> C
    D2[Diesel Generator 2] --> C
    D3[Diesel Generator 3 Standby] --> C
    E[Emergency Diesel Generator] -->|Auto Start 45s| F[Emergency Switchboard]
    C -->|Power| G1[Hotel Load Lighting HVAC]
    C -->|Power| G2[Machinery Load Pumps Compressors]
    C -->|Power| G3[Cargo Load Cranes Winches]
    F -->|Power| H[Safety Systems Fire Pumps Navigation]
    C -->|Steam Demand| I[Auxiliary Boiler HFO Fired]
    J[Exhaust Gas Boiler EGB] -->|Waste Heat Steam| C
    I -->|Steam| K1[Fuel Oil Heater]
    I -->|Steam| K2[Galley and Heating]
    I -->|Steam| K3[Laundry Services]
    C -->|Compressed Air| L[Air Compressors]
    L -->|Starting Air| A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat and Mass Flow in Auxiliary Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heavy Fuel Oil HFO Tank] -->|Heated 120C| B[Fuel Oil Purifier]
    B --> C[Fuel Oil Service Tank]
    C -->|Injection| D[Diesel Engine Cylinder]
    D -->|Combustion| E[Exhaust Manifold]
    E --> F[Exhaust Gas Boiler EGB]
    E --> G[Turbocharger]
    D -->|Heat Loss| H[Fresh Water Cooler]
    D -->|Power Output| I[Synchronous AC Generator]
    I --> J[Main Switchboard]
    H -->|Sea Water| K[Sea Chest]
    G -->|Air Charge| D
    F -->|Steam| L[Steam Consumers]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Selection of Auxiliary Prime Mover

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Ship Type?] --> Type1{Bulk Carrier or Tanker?}
    Type1 -->|Yes| Path1[Shaft Generator + 3 Diesel Generators]
    Type1 -->|No| Type2{LNG Carrier?}
    Type2 -->|Yes| Path2[Steam Turbo Generators using Boil Off Gas BOG]
    Type2 -->|No| Type3{Naval Combatant?}
    Type3 -->|Yes| Path3[Gas Turbine Generators Compact and Fast]
    Type3 -->|No| Path4[Standard Diesel Generators 3 to 4 Sets]
    Path1 --> Emerg[All Routes Include Emergency DG]
    Path2 --> Emerg
    Path3 --> Emerg
    Path4 --> Emerg
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Parallel Operation of Diesel Generators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DG1[Diesel Generator 1] --> SW1[Synchronizing Panel]
    DG2[Diesel Generator 2] --> SW1
    DG3[Diesel Generator 3 Standby] --> SW1
    SW1 -->|Parallel Bus| MSB[Main Switchboard 440V]
    MSB --> TR1[Step Down Transformer 440V to 220V]
    MSB --> TR2[Step Down Transformer 440V to 24V DC]
    TR1 -->|220V| ACC[Accommodation and Galley]
    TR2 -->|24V DC| NAV[Navigation and Communication]
    MSB -->|Direct 440V| PWR[Machinery and Cargo]
    MSB -->|Emergency Link| ESB[Emergency Switchboard]
    ESB --> EDG[Emergency Diesel Generator]
```
