# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q1. What is an auxiliary engine in a ship? List any four functions it performs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **auxiliary engine** is a secondary prime mover installed onboard a ship, other than the main propulsion engine, used to drive the various supporting machinery essential for ship operation.

**Four functions:**
1. Electrical power generation via diesel generators (DGs).
2. Pumping services (bilge, ballast, cooling, fire pumps).
3. Compressed air supply for main engine starting.
4. Steam generation via auxiliary boilers for fuel heating and hotel services.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four functions = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q2. Why is an emergency diesel generator (EDG) provided separately on a ship? Mention the SOLAS requirement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Emergency Diesel Generator (EDG)** is a separate, independently fueled diesel generator set installed in a protected compartment above the **bulkhead deck**. It is provided to ensure continuous supply of electrical power to **safety-critical systems** (fire pumps, emergency lighting, navigation, communication) when the main power source fails.

**SOLAS Requirement:** The EDG must be capable of **automatic starting within 45 seconds** of a main power failure, and its fuel tank must have a capacity sufficient for **36 hours of continuous full-load operation** without refueling.

*(1.5 marks for explanation + 1 mark for SOLAS time + 0.5 mark for fuel duration = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q-Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a)** Classify the various **auxiliary engines** used in merchant ships with their typical power ranges and applications. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **ship's diesel generator set** from fuel injection to power distribution. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Classification of Auxiliary Engines (7 marks):**

| S.No | Type of Auxiliary Engine | Function | Typical Power Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Diesel Generator (DG)** | AC electrical power generation | 500 kW – 5000 kW |
| 2 | **Shaft Generator** | Power take-off from main engine shaft | 1000 kW – 10000 kW |
| 3 | **Turbo-Generator (Steam)** | Electrical power using steam | 500 kW – 3000 kW |
| 4 | **Auxiliary Boiler** | Steam generation for heating & services | 1 – 25 tonnes/hr |
| 5 | **Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB)** | Waste heat recovery from main engine exhaust | 0.5 – 15 tonnes/hr |
| 6 | **Air Compressor (Motor Driven)** | Starting & service air supply | 30 – 100 m³/hr |
| 7 | **Emergency Diesel Generator (EDG)** | Backup power for safety systems | 75 kW – 500 kW |
| 8 | **Refrigeration Compressor** | Provision cold storage | 50 – 300 kW |

**Application Notes:**
- **DGs** are the most common — used for normal electrical supply at sea and in port.
- **Shaft generators** are used during long transits to save auxiliary fuel.
- **Turbo-generators** are used where clean, vibration-free power is needed (LNG carriers, research vessels).
- **EDG** is **mandatory** under SOLAS for all commercial vessels.
- **Auxiliary boilers + EGBs** work in tandem — the boiler provides steam in port, while the EGB recovers waste heat at sea.

*(2 marks for classification table + 2 marks for function + 2 marks for application + 1 mark for diagrammatic clarity if any — 7 marks total)*

---

### **Part (b) — Working of a Ship's Diesel Generator Set (7 marks):**

The working of a marine diesel generator set is explained in the following **sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Fuel Oil System:**
Heavy fuel oil (HFO) is drawn from the **service tank**, heated to **120 – 150 °C** in the **fuel oil heater** to reduce viscosity, and passed through **fuel oil purifiers** to remove water and sediment. The cleaned fuel is then injected into the engine cylinders via high-pressure fuel injection pumps.

**Step 2 — Combustion & Power Generation:**
The fuel-air mixture is compressed (compression ratio **16:1 to 20:1**) and ignited by the high temperature of compression. The combustion pushes the piston down, generating a **rotary mechanical power** on the crankshaft.

**Step 3 — Cooling System:**
The engine is cooled by a **two-stage cooling system**:
- **Primary (Fresh Water)** — circulates through the engine block, cylinder head, and air cooler.
- **Secondary (Sea Water)** — cools the fresh water via a **heat exchanger**.

**Step 4 — Lubrication System:**
Forced lubrication pumps deliver lube oil to the **main bearings, cross-head bearings, and cylinder walls** through oil coolers and filters.

**Step 5 — Generator Coupling:**
The diesel engine crankshaft is directly coupled (or geared) to a **3-phase synchronous AC generator** (typically **salient-pole type**). The generator's rotor is excited via **DC excitation** from an **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)**.

**Step 6 — Synchronization & Distribution:**
The generator output (**440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase**) is synchronized with the **main switchboard** (matching **voltage, frequency, and phase sequence**) and fed to consumers — hotel load, machinery load, cargo load, and safety systems.

**Step 7 — Governor Control:**
A **hydraulic or electronic governor** continuously adjusts the fuel rack to maintain **constant engine speed (N = 120f/p)** despite load variations.

*(1 mark per step × 7 steps = 7 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q-Set B — Alternative Choice]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**a)** Explain the role of **auxiliary boilers and exhaust gas boilers (EGB)** in a ship's steam system. Compare their operating principles with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A bulk carrier has the following connected auxiliary loads: Hotel — 600 kW, Pumps — 900 kW, Cargo — 400 kW, Navigation — 150 kW. If the **diversity factor is 0.7** and the **service factor is 1.15**, determine the **number and rating of diesel generator sets** required. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Auxiliary Boiler vs. Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB) (7 marks):**

| Feature | Auxiliary Boiler (Oil-Fired) | Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | HFO / Diesel oil combustion | Waste heat from main engine exhaust |
| **Operating Mode** | In port (when main engine is off) | At sea (when main engine is running) |
| **Capacity** | 1 – 25 tonnes/hr steam | 0.5 – 15 tonnes/hr steam |
| **Efficiency** | 80 – 88% | 60 – 70% (lower ΔT) |
| **Capital Cost** | Higher | Lower (no burner system) |
| **Auxiliaries** | Burner, FD fan, FD/ID dampers | Soot blowers, exhaust gas bypass damper |

**Operating Principle:**

- The **Auxiliary Boiler** is a **fire-tube boiler** where HFO is atomized through a **pressure jet or rotary cup burner** and ignited in the combustion chamber. The hot gases pass through tubes surrounded by water, generating steam at **7 – 10 bar** for fuel heating, laundry, galley, and accommodation heating.

- The **EGB** is a **water-tube or smoke-tube boiler** installed in the **exhaust gas trunk** of the main engine. As the hot exhaust gases (**300 – 400 °C**) pass over the tubes, water inside is converted to steam **without any fuel consumption**, recovering waste heat.

- The two boilers work in **complementary mode**: the EGB at sea, the auxiliary boiler in port, ensuring **continuous steam availability** for ship services.

*(2 marks for comparison + 2 marks for principle + 2 marks for diagram explanation + 1 mark for complementary operation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Part (b) — DG Sizing Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $P_{hotel} = 600$ kW
- $P_{pumps} = 900$ kW
- $P_{cargo} = 400$ kW
- $P_{nav} = 150$ kW
- $DF = 0.7$
- $SF = 1.15$

**Step 1:** Total connected load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{aux,total} &= 600 + 900 + 400 + 150 \\
P_{aux,total} &= 2050 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum simultaneous demand (with diversity factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max,demand} &= DF \cdot P_{aux,total} \\
P_{max,demand} &= 0.7 \cdot 2050 \\
P_{max,demand} &= 1435 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Required DG rating (with service factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{DG,rating} &= SF \cdot P_{max,demand} \\
P_{DG,rating} &= 1.15 \cdot 1435 \\
P_{DG,rating} &= 1650.25 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Select **3 × 1000 kW** DG sets (2 running + 1 standby = N+1 redundancy):

$$
P_{installed} = 2 \cdot 1000 = 2000 \text{ kW} > 1650.25 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5:** Each running DG load:

$$
\text{Load per DG} = \dfrac{1435}{2} = 717.5 \text{ kW} = 71.75\% \text{ of rated}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is within the **70 – 85% optimal loading range** for diesel generators, ensuring best fuel efficiency and longest engine life.

**Conclusion:** The ship should be fitted with **three diesel generator sets, each rated at 1000 kW**, with two running in parallel and one on standby. Additionally, a separate **emergency diesel generator (EDG) of ~200 kW** must be provided as per SOLAS regulations.

*(1.5 marks for Step 1, 1.5 marks for Step 2, 1.5 marks for Step 3, 1.5 marks for Step 4, 1 mark for loading check — total 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing Diversity Factor with Service Factor**: Students often multiply by both incorrectly. Diversity factor accounts for *simultaneity*; service factor accounts for *future load growth*. Apply them **sequentially**, not as a single product.
> 2. **Forgetting N+1 Redundancy**: Always specify **2 running + 1 standby** in your final answer; otherwise 1 mark is deducted.
> 3. **Wrong Formula for 3-Phase Power**: Mixing up $P = VI\cos\phi$ (single phase) with $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (3-phase) is a 1–2 mark loss.
> 4. **Ignoring SOLAS EDG Requirement**: Any answer discussing ship power systems without mentioning the **45-second automatic start** and **36-hour fuel capacity** of the EDG will lose 0.5 – 1 mark.
> 5. **Steam Enthalpy Values**: In heat balance questions, students use **sensible heat only**, forgetting to include **latent heat of vaporization**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Auxiliary Engines for Ship**

- **Auxiliary Engine**: A secondary prime mover other than the main propulsion engine, serving the *hotel load* of a ship.

- **Key Types**: Diesel Generators (DGs), Shaft Generators, Turbo-Generators, Emergency DGs, Auxiliary Boilers, Exhaust Gas Boilers (EGB), Air Compressors.

- **DGs**: Most common, 4-stroke medium-speed diesel coupled to 3-phase AC synchronous generator at **900 rpm** for **60 Hz** output.

- **Frequency Equation**: $f = Np / 120$ — must be memorized for the 14-mark analytical question.

- **DG Configuration**: **3 to 4 sets**, **N+1 redundancy** (2 running + 1 standby).

- **Load Categories**: Hotel load, Machinery load, Cargo load, Safety load.

- **Auxiliary Power Sizing**: 
  1. Sum total connected load.
  2. Multiply by **Diversity Factor (DF = 0.6 – 0.8)**.
  3. Multiply by **Service Factor (SF = 1.15 – 1.25)**.

- **Emergency DG (EDG)**: Mandatory under **SOLAS**, auto-start within **45 seconds**, fuel capacity **36 hours**.

- **Auxiliary Boiler**: Oil-fired, used **in port**; provides steam for fuel heating, laundry, galley.

- **Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB)**: Waste-heat recovery from main engine exhaust, used **at sea**; complementary to auxiliary boiler.

- **Steam Pressure**: Marine auxiliary boilers typically generate **7 – 10 bar** dry saturated steam.

- **Heat Balance**: $\eta_{boiler} = Q_{useful} / Q_{input} \times 100\%$; efficiency range **80 – 88%** with economizer.

- **3-Phase Power**: $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ — remember the **√3 factor**.

- **Generator Efficiency**: $\eta_{gen} = P_{elec,out} / P_{mech,in} \times 100\%$ — typically **95 – 98%** for large marine alternators.

- **SFOC of Aux Diesel**: **190 – 210 g/kWh** — used to calculate fuel consumption for a voyage.

- **Standard Marine Voltage**: **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** (or 380 V / 50 Hz for European ships).

- **Poles Calculation**: A 4-pole generator needs **1800 rpm** for 60 Hz; an 8-pole needs **900 rpm** (preferred for direct coupling to medium-speed diesels).

- **Compressed Air**: Required at **30 bar** for main engine starting; capacity typically **30 – 100 m³/hr** with 2 or 3 compressors.

- **SOLAS Key Takeaway**: Emergency generator must be **above the bulkhead deck**, in a **separate compartment**, and start **automatically** without human intervention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auxiliary Engines for Engine Plant Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auxiliary Engines for Engine Plant Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Auxiliary Engines for Engine Plant Operation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of marine engineering plant operation, **Auxiliary Engines** refer to the secondary, support-oriented prime movers installed on a floating vessel (ship, FPSO, drilling rig, or barge) that drive the auxiliary machinery essential for the operation of the main propulsion system, hotel services, cargo handling, and safety-critical functions. Unlike the main engine which directly drives the propeller, auxiliary engines typically convert fuel energy into mechanical shaft power or electrical power for auxiliary consumers.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *OENST611 – Fundamentals of Floating Bodies*, auxiliary engines in marine engine plants include:

1. **Auxiliary Diesel Generators (ADGs)** – produce electrical power.
2. **Auxiliary Boilers** – produce steam for heating, fuel treatment, and turbine drives.
3. **Emergency Generators** – supply power during blackout conditions.
4. **Air Compressors (Main & Starting)** – provide compressed air for starting main engines.
5. **Purifier Drives** – separate water and sludge from fuel/lub oil.
6. **Pump Drives** – bilge, ballast, cooling, fuel transfer, lubrication pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Auxiliary engines are differentiated from the main engine by their function (support vs. propulsion), their operational duty (continuous vs. intermittent), and their coupling arrangement (electrical generator coupling vs. direct propeller shaft coupling).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large commercial kitchen restaurant**:
- The **main engine** is like the *primary chef* cooking the signature dishes (pushing the ship through water).
- The **auxiliary engines** are the *supporting staff* — the dishwasher, the refrigerator compressor, the exhaust fan, the water pump — without whom the chef simply cannot work efficiently.
- The **emergency generator** is the *backup generator* that keeps the freezers running when the main power fails (during a storm/blackout).

In a ship, the auxiliary engines ensure that the main engine is:
- Properly **cooled** (cooling seawater pumps),
- Properly **lubricated** (LO pumps),
- Properly **fueled** (FO supply pumps),
- Properly **started** (starting air compressors),
- The **accommodation** is livable (HVAC, lighting, galley).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering constants and metrics are universally applied in marine auxiliary engine plant design:

- **Service Factor (SF):** typically **1.15** for marine auxiliary engines (per ISO 3046/1).
- **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC):** **190–210 g/kWh** for modern auxiliary diesel engines.
- **Standard Reference Conditions:** Intake air at **298 K (25°C)**, barometric pressure **100 kPa (1 bar)**, relative humidity **30%**.
- **Auxiliary Boiler Steam Pressure:** typically **0.7 MPa to 1.0 MPa** (exhaust gas / oil-fired).
- **Emergency Generator Start Time:** within **45 seconds** of blackout (per SOLAS Ch. II-1).
- **Electrical Frequency:** **60 Hz** (American/Japanese ships) or **50 Hz** (European/Asian ships).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Auxiliary Engine:** A prime mover, generally an internal combustion engine (diesel) or steam turbine, installed on board a vessel to drive auxiliary machinery such as generators, pumps, purifiers, and compressors, distinct from the main propulsion engine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Auxiliary Engines

The auxiliary engines in a ship's engine plant can be classified along several engineering axes:

### 2.1.1 By Prime Mover Type
- **Diesel Engines (Auxiliary Diesel Generators – ADGs):** Most common; high thermal efficiency (38–45%).
- **Steam Turbines:** Used in steam ships (turbine-electric propulsion); driven by auxiliary boilers.
- **Gas Turbines:** Used in specialized naval vessels for high power-to-weight ratio.

### 2.1.2 By Service Function
- **Generating Set:** Diesel + Alternator → Electrical Power.
- **Pumping Set:** Diesel/Electric Motor → Pump.
- **Compressor Set:** Diesel/Electric Motor → Air Compressor.

### 2.1.3 By Operating Mode
- **Continuous Duty:** ADGs running under base load (typically 70–80% of rated load).
- **Standby/Peak Duty:** Emergency generators, harbor generators.
- **Intermittent Duty:** Deck machinery, bow thrusters.

## 2.2 Auxiliary Diesel Generator (ADG) Operational Logic

The ADG operates on the following structured logic chain:

1. **Fuel Injection:** Heavy fuel oil (HFO) or marine diesel oil (MDO) is injected at high pressure into the combustion chamber.
2. **Combustion:** Adiabatic flame temperature reaches **~2200 K**; peak cylinder pressure **~150–180 bar**.
3. **Power Stroke:** Combustion gas drives the piston, converting chemical energy → mechanical work.
4. **Mechanical Transmission:** Piston → connecting rod → crankshaft → coupling.
5. **Electrical Conversion:** Crankshaft drives the alternator rotor at **1800 rpm (60 Hz)** or **1500 rpm (50 Hz)**.
6. **Power Distribution:** Generated electricity is routed via switchboards to consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ADGs are sized for 80% load:** Marine electrical practice avoids running generators below 30% load (wet stacking, poor combustion) or above 90% load (insufficient reserve). Hence **3 × 50% capacity** or **4 × 33% capacity** generator arrangements are standard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Generator Electrical Output** | $P_e = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | kW | $V_L$ = Line Voltage, $I_L$ = Line Current, $\cos\phi$ = Power Factor |
| **Mechanical → Electrical Power** | $P_m = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_g}$ | kW | $\eta_g$ = Generator efficiency (0.95–0.98) |
| **Specific Fuel Consumption** | $SFOC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_e}$ | g/kWh | $m_f$ = fuel mass flow rate |
| **Heat Balance (Diesel Engine)** | $Q_{in} = Q_{mech} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{rad}$ | kJ | Energy balance: 100% = 35–40% mech + 25–30% coolant + 30–35% exhaust + 5% radiation |
| **Mean Effective Pressure** | $P_{me} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_s}$ | bar | $W_{net}$ = net work per cycle, $V_s$ = swept volume |
| **Engine Indicated Power** | $P_i = \dfrac{P_{me} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ | kW | $k$ = number of cylinders |
| **Engine Brake Power** | $P_b = P_i \cdot \eta_m$ | kW | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (0.85–0.92) |
| **Service Correction Factor** | $P_{corr} = P_b \cdot SF$ | kW | $SF = 1.10$ to $1.15$ |
| **Air Consumption for Combustion** | $m_{air} = \lambda \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot AFR_{stoich}$ | kg/s | $\lambda$ = excess air ratio (≈1.8–2.2 for diesel) |
| **Steam Mass Flow (Boiler)** | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{boiler}}{h_g - h_f}$ | kg/s | $h_g, h_f$ = steam/water enthalpy |
| **Starting Air Consumption** | $V_{air} = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}}{n_{cycles}}$ | m³ | $n$ = polytropic index ≈ 1.4 |
| **Purifier Throughput** | $Q_{pur} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\rho_{oil}}$ | m³/h | Based on oil density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on $\vert x \vert$ notation:** In all KTU 2024 board exam answer sheets, absolute value expressions inside table rows must use the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent markdown table parsing conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

The auxiliary engine plant design directly affects:
- **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** compliance under IMO MARPOL Annex VI.
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** ratings.
- **Vessel's Autonomy:** Hours of operation without shore power.
- **Class Society Certification:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV approval requires redundancy calculations on auxiliary machinery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "auxiliary engine" with "auxiliary machinery." Auxiliary engines are *prime movers* (drivers), while auxiliary machinery includes pumps, compressors, and separators that may be driven by these engines OR by electric motors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Auxiliary Generator Capacity for a Vessel

### Problem Setup
A bulk carrier has the following electrical consumers while at sea (maneuvering condition):

| Consumer | Power (kW) | Diversity Factor | Continuous Load? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steering Gear | 45 | 0.5 | No |
| Navigation Equipment | 25 | 1.0 | Yes |
| Fuel Oil Purifier | 35 | 0.8 | No |
| Lub Oil Pump | 20 | 1.0 | Yes |
| Sea Water Cooling Pump | 55 | 1.0 | Yes |
| Fresh Water Generator | 30 | 0.7 | No |
| Galley & HVAC | 80 | 0.6 | Yes |
| Lighting | 60 | 1.0 | Yes |
| Deck Machinery (bow thruster) | 400 | 0.4 | No |

Determine the **minimum installed auxiliary generator capacity** required, assuming 80% maximum permissible loading on each running generator.

---

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify continuous and non-continuous loads.**

$$
P_{cont} = P_{nav} + P_{LO} + P_{SW} + P_{galley} + P_{light}
$$

$$
P_{cont} = 25 + 20 + 55 + 80 + 60 = 240 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply diversity factors to non-continuous loads.**

$$
P_{nc} = \sum_i (P_i \cdot DF_i)
$$

$$
P_{nc} = (45 \cdot 0.5) + (35 \cdot 0.8) + (30 \cdot 0.7) + (400 \cdot 0.4)
$$

$$
P_{nc} = 22.5 + 28 + 21 + 160 = 231.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total simultaneous demand (worst-case running).**

$$
P_{demand} = P_{cont} + P_{nc} = 240 + 231.5 = 471.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply service factor of 1.15 (margin for transient loads).**

$$
P_{req} = P_{demand} \cdot SF = 471.5 \cdot 1.15 = 542.225 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to generator capacity with 80% maximum loading policy.**

$$
P_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{req}}{0.80} = \dfrac{542.225}{0.80} = 677.78 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Select standard generator set (round up to next commercial size).**

$$
P_{gen,installed} = 750 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify by checking number of generators.** With 3 × 750 kW generators, at sea any two can run sharing the load:

$$
P_{2\,running} = 2 \cdot 750 \cdot 0.80 = 1200 \text{ kW} \gg 542.225 \text{ kW} \checkmark
$$

The arrangement has **74% redundancy**, exceeding class minimum (typically 10% on each running machine).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) Calculation

### Given
A 4-stroke auxiliary diesel generator produces **500 kW** of electrical power at the alternator terminals. The engine consumes **102 kg of fuel per hour**. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$, generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.96$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute brake power at engine crankshaft.**

$$
P_b = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_g} = \dfrac{500}{0.96} = 520.83 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute indicated power.**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{520.83}{0.90} = 578.70 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fuel mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{102 \text{ kg}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 0.02833 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SFOC based on electrical output.**

$$
SFOC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_e} = \dfrac{0.02833 \cdot 3600}{500} = 0.204 \text{ kg/kWh} = 204 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC).**

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{0.02833 \cdot 3600}{520.83} = 0.1958 \text{ kg/kWh} = 195.8 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute overall thermal efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{3600}{SFOC \cdot LCV_{fuel}}
$$

Assuming LCV of marine diesel oil: $LCV = 42{,}700 \text{ kJ/kg}$

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{3600}{0.204 \cdot 42700} = \dfrac{3600}{8710.8} = 0.4134 \approx 41.34\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A 41% thermal efficiency is realistic for a modern 4-stroke marine auxiliary diesel generator. Older engines may show 35–38%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Auxiliary Boiler Steam Output for Fuel Heating

### Problem
An auxiliary oil-fired boiler must heat fuel oil from **90°C to 130°C** at a flow rate of **3.5 kg/s**. Specific heat of fuel oil $c_p = 2.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Steam enters at **0.8 MPa** saturated (enthalpy $h_g = 2769 \text{ kJ/kg}$) and leaves as saturated liquid (enthalpy $h_f = 721 \text{ kJ/kg}$). Compute the steam mass flow rate required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute heat duty required.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 3.5 \cdot 2.0 \cdot (130 - 90) = 280 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute heat released per kg of steam (latent heat).**

$$
\Delta h_{steam} = h_g - h_f = 2769 - 721 = 2048 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute steam mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{fuel}}{\Delta h_{steam}} = \dfrac{280}{2048} = 0.1367 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard units.**

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.1367 \cdot 3600 = 492.2 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation is the basis for **Fuel Oil Heating Sizing** in Class-approved heat balance diagrams submitted during ship design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Starting Air Receiver Capacity for Main Engine

### Problem
A 2-stroke main engine with **6 cylinders**, bore $D = 0.50 \text{ m}$, stroke $S = 1.5 \text{ m}$, requires compressed air at **30 bar** for starting. Atmospheric pressure $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$. Calculate the receiver volume $V_1$ such that **12 successive starts** are possible without compressor recharging. Take polytropic index $n = 1.35$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute swept volume of one cylinder.**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot S = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.50)^2 \cdot 1.5 = 0.2945 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Air volume consumed per start (assume one cylinder at a time over 12 cycles).**

$$
V_{consumed} = 6 \cdot V_s = 6 \cdot 0.2945 = 1.767 \text{ m}^3 \text{ at 30 bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to atmospheric equivalent for receiver sizing.**

$$
V_{atm} = V_{consumed} \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 1.767 \cdot 30 = 53.01 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Apply receiver capacity formula.**

$$
V_{receiver} = V_{atm} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} - 1}
$$

$$
V_{receiver} = 53.01 \cdot \dfrac{1}{(30/1)^{1/1.35} - 1}
$$

$$
(30)^{1/1.35} = 30^{0.7407} = e^{0.7407 \cdot \ln 30} = e^{0.7407 \cdot 3.401} = e^{2.519} = 12.41
$$

$$
V_{receiver} = 53.01 \cdot \dfrac{1}{12.41 - 1} = 53.01 \cdot \dfrac{1}{11.41} = 4.65 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor of 1.25.**

$$
V_{receiver,installed} = 4.65 \cdot 1.25 = 5.81 \text{ m}^3 \approx 6 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Class societies (DNV, Lloyd's) typically require the receiver to support **at least 12 consecutive starts** for unattended machinery spaces (AUT notation).

---

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Derivation | Core Equation | Result | Marine Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 ADG Capacity | $P_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{demand} \cdot SF}{LF}$ | 677.78 kW → 750 kW | Sets generator selection for ship design. |
| 3.2 SFOC | $SFOC = \dot{m}_f / P_e$ | 204 g/kWh | Determines bunker fuel cost & EEDI. |
| 3.3 Boiler Steam | $\dot{m}_s = \dot{Q} / \Delta h$ | 0.137 kg/s | Sizes fuel heating system. |
| 3.4 Air Receiver | $V_R = V_{atm} / [(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} - 1]$ | 5.81 m³ | Sizes starting air system. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Marine Auxiliary Engine Plant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[HFO Storage Tank] --> A2[Fuel Oil Purifier]
    A2 --> A3[FO Service Tank]
    A3 --> A4[FO Supply Pump]
    A4 --> A5[Auxiliary Diesel Generator 1]
    A4 --> A6[Auxiliary Diesel Generator 2]
    A4 --> A7[Emergency Generator]
    A5 --> A8[Main Switchboard]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A9[Emergency Switchboard]
    A8 --> A10[Main Propulsion Motor]
    A8 --> A11[Steering Gear Motor]
    A8 --> A12[Ballast Pump]
    A8 --> A13[Cooling Sea Water Pump]
    A8 --> A14[HVAC and Galley Load]
    A8 --> A15[Navigation Bridge Equipment]
    B1[Auxiliary Oil Fired Boiler] --> B2[Steam Manifold]
    B2 --> B3[FO Heater]
    B2 --> B4[FO Viscosity Controller]
    B2 --> B5[Tank Cleaning Steam]
    C1[Main Air Compressor] --> C2[Starting Air Receiver]
    C2 --> A10
    C2 --> A5
    C2 --> A6
    D1[LO Purifier] --> D2[LO Storage Tank]
    D2 --> A5
    D2 --> A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the **functional interdependence** of auxiliary engines. The ADGs, the boiler, and the air compressor form three independent but coupled subsystems feeding the main propulsion chain.

## 4.2 Mermaid Hierarchy: Classification of Auxiliary Engines

```mermaid
graph TB
    N1[Auxiliary Engines on Board]
    N2[By Prime Mover]
    N3[Diesel Engines]
    N4[Steam Turbines]
    N5[Gas Turbines]
    N6[By Function]
    N7[Power Generation ADG]
    N8[Air Compression]
    N9[Fluid Pumping]
    N10[Oil Heating Boilers]
    N11[By Duty]
    N12[Continuous Duty]
    N13[Standby Duty]
    N14[Emergency Duty]
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N2 --> N4
    N2 --> N5
    N1 --> N6
    N6 --> N7
    N6 --> N8
    N6 --> N9
    N6 --> N10
    N1 --> N11
    N11 --> N12
    N11 --> N13
    N11 --> N14
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Power Flow from ADG to Consumers

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant FU as Fuel Oil
    participant ADG as Auxiliary Diesel Generator
    participant SW as Main Switchboard
    participant SSR as Shore Supply Connection
    participant EMG as Emergency Generator
    participant CONS as Consumers
    FU->>ADG: Inject HFO at 600 bar
    ADG->>ADG: Combustion in 4 stroke cycle
    ADG->>SW: Deliver 440 V, 60 Hz, 3 phase
    SW->>CONS: Distribute to motors, pumps, lighting
    Note over SW,SSR: Shore power takes over at port
    SSR->>SW: Incoming supply at 440 V
    Note over SW,EMG: Blackout triggers emergency start
    EMG->>SW: Energize emergency bus within 45 s
    SW->>CONS: Critical loads only
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Engine Plant Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> X2[Heat Energy in Combustion]
    X2 --> X3[Mechanical Energy at Crankshaft]
    X3 --> X4[Electrical Energy at Alternator]
    X4 --> X5[Useful Work at Consumers]
    X2 --> X6[Coolant Heat Loss 30 percent]
    X2 --> X7[Exhaust Heat Loss 33 percent]
    X2 --> X8[Radiation and Friction 2 percent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Energy Diagram:** In a marine auxiliary diesel engine, only **~38–42%** of fuel chemical energy is converted to useful electrical output. The rest is wasted as heat — which is why modern ships integrate **Waste Heat Recovery Systems (WHRS)** into auxiliary engine exhaust lines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define auxiliary engine with respect to marine engine plant operation. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
An auxiliary engine is a secondary prime mover installed on board a vessel to drive support machinery such as electrical generators, pumps, purifiers, air compressors, and boilers. Unlike the main propulsion engine, the auxiliary engine does not directly drive the propeller but provides essential services like electrical power generation, fuel heating, compressed air supply, and fluid transfer. The most common auxiliary engines in modern ships are auxiliary diesel generators (ADGs), auxiliary boilers, and air compressors.

### Question 2: State any three standard operating conditions for auxiliary diesel generators as per ISO 3046/1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reference ambient temperature:** 298 K (25°C) at the air intake.
2. **Reference barometric pressure:** 100 kPa (1 bar).
3. **Reference relative humidity:** 30%.
4. **Service factor:** 1.10 to 1.15 (depending on continuous vs. intermittent duty).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**Q.A (a)** Explain the different types of auxiliary engines used in a typical marine engine plant. Discuss their functions and operational duty cycles. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A typical marine engine plant contains the following categories of auxiliary engines:

**1. Auxiliary Diesel Generators (ADGs):**
- **Function:** Convert chemical energy in HFO/MDO into electrical energy.
- **Configuration:** 3 or 4 sets (e.g., 3 × 750 kW).
- **Duty:** Continuous — typically 2 running at sea, 1 standby; 1 running at port.

**2. Auxiliary Boilers:**
- **Function:** Produce steam for fuel oil heating, tank cleaning, and heating coils.
- **Types:** Oil-fired (most common) and exhaust gas (waste heat recovery).
- **Duty:** Continuous at sea; intermittent at port.

**3. Air Compressors:**
- **Function:** Charge starting air receivers (typically at 30 bar) for main engine.
- **Configuration:** 2 main + 1 emergency compressor.
- **Duty:** Standby (auto-start when receiver pressure drops).

**4. Purifier Drives:**
- **Function:** Separate water/sludge from HFO and LO via centrifugal force.
- **Duty:** Intermittent — run for a few hours per day based on oil throughput.

**5. Emergency Generator:**
- **Function:** Supply power to critical consumers during blackout.
- **Duty:** Standby — must auto-start within 45 seconds (SOLAS).

---

**Q.A (b)** A bulk carrier requires continuous electrical load of **320 kW** at sea and peak load of **180 kW** (non-continuous, diversity factor 0.6). Determine the size and number of auxiliary diesel generators to be installed, assuming maximum permissible loading is 80%. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute diversified peak load:
$$
P_{peak,diver} = 180 \cdot 0.6 = 108 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Total simultaneous demand:
$$
P_{demand} = 320 + 108 = 428 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply service factor (1.15):
$$
P_{req} = 428 \cdot 1.15 = 492.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply 80% loading policy:
$$
P_{gen,each} = \dfrac{492.2}{0.80} = 615.25 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Select standard generator size:
$$
P_{gen,selected} = 700 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6:** Number of generators:
- At sea: 2 running (1400 kW available) → load sharing = 246 kW each = 35% — too low.
- **Better arrangement:** 3 × 500 kW at 82% combined.
- Optimal: **3 × 350 kW**, with 2 running at sea (2 × 350 × 0.80 = 560 kW > 492.2 kW), 1 standby.

$$
\boxed{P_{gen} = 3 \times 350 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**Q.B (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the layout of an auxiliary engine plant on a modern merchant ship. Identify at least **six** auxiliary engines and their functions. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The auxiliary engine plant on a modern merchant ship is laid out across the **engine room** with subsystems extending to the deck and accommodation. The block layout is as follows:

1. **Auxiliary Diesel Generators (ADGs)** — Located on the upper platform; coupled to alternators feeding the **main switchboard**.
2. **Emergency Generator** — Located in the **emergency generator room** above the bulkhead deck; feeds the emergency switchboard.
3. **Main Air Compressors** — On the lower platform; charge starting air receivers at 30 bar.
4. **Auxiliary Boilers** — In the boiler room on the upper deck; supply steam manifold.
5. **Fuel Oil Purifiers** — On the FO treatment platform; purify HFO before transfer to service tanks.
6. **LO Purifiers** — Adjacent to LO storage tanks.
7. **Fresh Water Generator** — On the cooler upper platform; uses waste heat.
8. **Bilge and Ballast Pumps** — In the pump room; centrifugal type driven by electric motors (auxiliary engines fed through switchboard).

The block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram** above) shows the functional coupling of these auxiliary engines through fuel, steam, air, and electrical networks.

---

**Q.B (b)** An auxiliary oil-fired boiler on a tanker produces **2.5 tonnes/h** of saturated steam at **0.7 MPa**. The feed water enters at **80°C**. Calculate: **(i)** the heat duty in kW, and **(ii)** the boiler efficiency if the fuel consumption is **210 kg/h** with LCV = 42,000 kJ/kg. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_s = 2.5 \text{ t/h} = 2500/3600 = 0.6944 \text{ kg/s}$
- Saturated steam at 0.7 MPa: $h_g = 2763 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Feed water at 80°C: $h_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 210/3600 = 0.05833 \text{ kg/s}$
- $LCV = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Heat output (Q_out):**
$$
\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_g - h_f) = 0.6944 \cdot (2763 - 335)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{out} = 0.6944 \cdot 2428 = 1686.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat input (Q_in):**
$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LCV = 0.05833 \cdot 42{,}000 = 2450 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Boiler efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{out}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{1686.1}{2450} = 0.6883 \approx 68.83\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{out} = 1686.1 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta_{boiler} \approx 68.8\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently use **HHV (Higher Heating Value)** instead of **LCV (Lower Calorific Value)** for boiler efficiency calculations. For boiler heat balance, **LCV is the correct basis** since water leaves as vapor in flue gas.
> - In steam enthalpy lookup, ensure you use the **saturated steam enthalpy at the boiler operating pressure**, not at atmospheric pressure.
> - For **3-mark Part A** answers, do not exceed **one page** — keep it concise. Board examiners penalize verbose answers in Part A.
> - For **Part B derivations**, missing units or conversion errors (e.g., kg/h vs kg/s) typically cost **1–2 marks** out of 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Auxiliary Engines:**

- **Auxiliary Engine Definition:** Secondary prime mover driving support machinery (generators, pumps, purifiers, compressors, boilers). NOT the main propulsion engine.
- **Most Common Auxiliary Engines:** Auxiliary Diesel Generators (ADGs), Auxiliary Boilers, Air Compressors, Purifier Drives.
- **Generator Loading Policy:** 80% maximum load on each running machine → design with **N+1 redundancy** (e.g., 3 × 50% generators; any 2 can handle full load).
- **Reference Conditions (ISO 3046/1):** 298 K, 100 kPa, 30% RH; Service Factor 1.10–1.15.
- **SFOC for Auxiliary Diesels:** 190–210 g/kWh; Thermal Efficiency 38–42%.
- **SOLAS Emergency Generator:** Auto-start within **45 seconds** of blackout.
- **Electrical Frequency:** 50 Hz (1500 rpm) or 60 Hz (1800 rpm) for 4-pole alternators.
- **Starting Air Pressure:** Typically **30 bar** for main engines; receiver sized for **12 consecutive starts**.
- **Boiler Steam Pressure:** 0.7–1.0 MPa for auxiliary oil-fired boilers.
- **Boiler Efficiency Range:** 65–75% for oil-fired; up to 80% for exhaust gas boilers.
- **Key Formula Triad:** $P_e = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$; $SFOC = \dot{m}_f / P_e$; $V_R = V_{atm}/[(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} - 1]$.
- **Energy Flow Losses:** ~30% coolant, ~33% exhaust, ~2% radiation → only ~38–42% useful.
- **Classification Axes:** By prime mover (diesel/turbine), by function (generation/pumping/heating), by duty (continuous/standby/emergency).
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing "auxiliary engine" with "auxiliary machinery" — engines are drivers; machinery are driven.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steering Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FLOATING BODIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steering Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steering Gear** is the complete electro-mechanical and/or hydraulic system fitted on a floating vessel (ship, boat, or any floating body) that is responsible for **controlling the angular position of the rudder** in response to commands from the helm (wheel) on the navigation bridge, thereby governing the directional motion of the vessel. As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 29**, every ship of 100 GT and above must be provided with a main steering gear and an auxiliary steering gear of adequate strength and capability to steer the ship at maximum service speed.

In Naval Architecture terminology, the steering gear assembly consists of:
- **Rudder stock** (vertical shaft transmitting torque)
- **Tiller arm / quadrant** (lever arm attached to rudder stock)
- **Power unit** (hydraulic ram, electric motor, or mechanical linkage)
- **Control mechanism** (telemotor/follow-up system)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of the steering gear as the **"neck muscles" of a ship**. Your eyes (the helm) tell the neck muscles which direction to turn the head, and the muscles apply torque to rotate it. Similarly, the helmsman commands an angle, the steering gear applies torque on the rudder stock, and the rudder (the "head") turns to push water, causing the ship to change direction.

Imagine steering a bicycle - you turn the handlebar (small input), which is connected via cables/linkages to rotate the front wheel. The same principle applies, but on a ship, the water resistance on the rudder is enormous, requiring **massive hydraulic rams** to rotate it.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (SOLAS Compliant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Main Steering Gear** must be capable of putting the rudder over from **35° on one side to 35° on the other side** at the **maximum service speed** of the ship.
> - **Auxiliary Steering Gear** must do the same but at a speed of **7 knots** or **half the maximum service speed** (whichever is greater).
> - Time allowed: **28 seconds** for main gear; **60 seconds** for auxiliary gear.
> - Rudder angular velocity at service speed: typically **2.5° to 3° per second**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Values

- Density of seawater: **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**
- Density of freshwater: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Empirical constant for rudder force: **K = 0.13** (for balanced rudder, Taylor's formula)
- Standard rudder area ratio: **A_R = (1.5% to 6%) of L × T** (Length × Draft product)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Steering Gear

The working of a steering gear system follows this sequence:

1. **Command Initiation**: Helmsman turns the wheel or autopilot sends electrical signal.
2. **Signal Transmission**: Through telemotor (mechanical) or servo valve (hydraulic) to control unit.
3. **Power Application**: Hydraulic pump delivers high-pressure oil to the ram.
4. **Mechanical Conversion**: Hydraulic pressure pushes the ram piston, which rotates the tiller.
5. **Rudder Rotation**: Tiller movement rotates the rudder stock and the attached rudder blade.
6. **Hydrodynamic Action**: Water flowing past the angled rudder generates a transverse force.
7. **Yaw Moment**: This force creates a turning moment about the ship's center of gravity.
8. **Feedback**: Rudder angle indicator confirms actual position to bridge.

## 2.2 Types of Steering Gear Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Major Categories (KTU High-Yield):**

| Type | Mechanism | Application |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Mechanical** | Gear trains, chains, wire ropes | Small vessels, tugs, fishing boats |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic rams with oil pressure | Medium ships (bulk carriers, tankers) |
| **Electro-Hydraulic** | Electric motor + hydraulic pump + ram | Modern large vessels, naval ships |

## 2.3 Types of Rudders (Critical for KTU)

The **balance ratio** of a rudder determines the torque required to turn it:

- **Unbalanced Rudder**: Entire rudder area is aft of the stock → Maximum torque required.
- **Balanced Rudder**: A portion (typically 20-33%) of the area is forward of the stock → Reduces torque.
- **Semi-Balanced Rudder**: Partial balance with a small forward area.

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Definition of Balance Ratio

The **balance ratio** is the ratio of the rudder area forward of the stock to the total rudder area.

$$\text{Balance Ratio} = \frac{A_f}{A_f + A_a}$$

Where:
- $A_f$ = Rudder area **forward** of the stock
- $A_a$ = Rudder area **aft** of the stock

A balance ratio of **0.20 to 0.33** is typical for merchant ships.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALL formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 Scheme.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rudder Force (Taylor) | $F_R = K \cdot A_R \cdot V^2$ | kN | $K = 0.13$, $A_R$ in m², $V$ in m/s |
| 2 | Rudder Force (Maltby) | $F_R = 0.00535 \cdot A_R \cdot V_s^2$ | tonnes-force | $A_R$ in ft², $V_s$ in knots |
| 3 | Turning Moment (Unbalanced) | $M_T = F_R \cdot \dfrac{L}{3}$ | kN·m | Approximation |
| 4 | Turning Moment (Balanced) | $M_T = F_R \cdot e$ | kN·m | $e$ = eccentricity of stock |
| 5 | Eccentricity | $e = \dfrac{L}{3} (1 - b)$ | m | $b$ = balance ratio |
| 6 | Torque on Rudder Head | $T_{RH} = F_R \cdot e$ | kN·m | Same as turning moment |
| 7 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in kN·m |
| 8 | Rudder Area (Empirical) | $A_R = (0.015 \text{ to } 0.06) L \cdot T$ | m² | $L$ = length, $T$ = draft |
| 9 | Aspect Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{h^2}{A_R}$ | dimensionless | $h$ = rudder height |
| 10 | Lift Coefficient | $C_L = \dfrac{2 F_R}{\rho A_R V^2}$ | dimensionless | Aerodynamic form |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Steering Gear Theory:**
> - **Ship Design Studios**: Use these formulas to size the rudder and select the steering gear.
> - **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS): Verify compliance with SOLAS regulations.
> - **Maritime Accident Investigators**: Reconstruct collision scenarios by computing rudder-induced yaw moments.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rudder Force (Empirical Approach)

The rudder force is derived from the dynamic pressure exerted by water on the inclined rudder surface.

### Step 1: Consider the flow past an inclined flat plate (rudder)

When a ship moves with velocity $V$ and the rudder is at an angle $\alpha$ (helm angle), water strikes the rudder with a relative velocity $V$. The dynamic pressure is:

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

### Step 2: This pressure, integrated over the rudder area, produces a normal force

The total normal force on the rudder is proportional to the dynamic pressure and the wetted area. The proportionality constant absorbs the lift coefficient dependency on angle:

$$F_R \propto \rho \cdot A_R \cdot V^2$$

### Step 3: Introduction of empirical constant K

Experimental data on full-scale and model tests shows that for a typical merchant ship rudder:

$$F_R = K \cdot A_R \cdot V^2$$

Where **K ≈ 0.13** when:
- $F_R$ is in **kN** (kilonewtons)
- $A_R$ is in **m²**
- $V$ is in **m/s**

### Step 4: Unit verification of K

Let us check the units of K by dimensional analysis:

$$[F_R] = [K] \cdot [A_R] \cdot [V^2]$$

$$N = [K] \cdot m^2 \cdot \left(\frac{m}{s}\right)^2$$

$$[K] = \frac{N \cdot s^2}{m^4} = \frac{kg}{m \cdot s^2} \cdot \frac{s^2}{m^4} = \frac{kg}{m^5}$$

The empirical constant K has units of **kg/m⁵**, and its numerical value depends on the unit system chosen.

## 3.2 Derivation of Turning Moment for Unbalanced Rudder

For an unbalanced rudder, the entire force $F_R$ acts at the **center of pressure**, which is approximately located at **one-third the chord length from the leading edge** (for a rectangular plate).

### Step 1: Position of center of pressure

The center of pressure is at a distance of L/3 from the leading edge, where L is the mean chord length.

$$x_{cp} = \frac{L}{3}$$

### Step 2: Torque arm from rudder stock

For an unbalanced rudder, the stock is at the **leading edge**. Therefore, the moment arm is exactly L/3.

$$\text{Moment Arm} = \frac{L}{3}$$

### Step 3: Computing the turning moment

The turning moment is force multiplied by moment arm:

$$M_T = F_R \cdot \frac{L}{3}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Turning Moment for Balanced Rudder

A balanced rudder has its stock positioned at a distance $e$ from the leading edge (where $e > 0$).

### Step 1: Define the eccentricity

The eccentricity $e$ is the distance from the leading edge to the rudder stock:

$$e = \frac{L}{3}(1 - b)$$

where $b$ is the balance ratio defined earlier.

### Step 2: Compute the net turning moment

For a balanced rudder, two opposing moments act:
- Moment from forward area: $M_f = F_{forward} \cdot e$ (acts clockwise)
- Moment from aft area: $M_a = F_{aft} \cdot (L/3 - e)$ (acts counter-clockwise)

The net moment is the difference:

$$M_T = F_a \cdot \left(\frac{L}{3} - e\right) - F_f \cdot e$$

Since $F_f = F_a \cdot \dfrac{b}{1-b}$ (using the balance ratio), substituting and simplifying:

$$M_T = F_R \cdot e$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The turning moment of a balanced rudder is simply **$M_T = F_R \cdot e$**, which is significantly less than that of an unbalanced rudder.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Given:**
- Ship Length: $L_s = 150$ m
- Ship Draft: $T = 9$ m
- Ship Speed: $V = 15$ knots
- Balance ratio: $b = 0.25$
- Rudder area: $A_R = 25$ m²

**Find:** (a) Rudder Force, (b) Turning Moment on rudder head

### Solution:

**Step 1: Convert speed to m/s**

$$V = 15 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \frac{m/s}{knot} = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate rudder force using Taylor's formula**

$$F_R = K \cdot A_R \cdot V^2$$

$$F_R = 0.13 \times 25 \times (7.716)^2$$

$$F_R = 0.13 \times 25 \times 59.54$$

$$F_R = 193.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Calculate mean chord length**

Using the empirical rudder area relationship assuming a rectangular rudder with aspect ratio ≈ 1.5:
- Rudder height $h = 5$ m
- Mean chord $L_c = A_R / h = 25/5 = 5$ m

**Step 4: Calculate eccentricity**

$$e = \frac{L_c}{3}(1 - b) = \frac{5}{3}(1 - 0.25)$$

$$e = \frac{5}{3} \times 0.75 = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate turning moment**

$$M_T = F_R \cdot e = 193.5 \times 1.25$$

$$M_T = 241.875 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** If the rudder were unbalanced (b = 0), the moment would have been:
> $M_T = 193.5 \times (5/3) = 322.5$ kN·m
> The balanced rudder reduced the moment by **~25%**, exactly matching the balance ratio.

## 3.5 Power Required Calculation

Continuing the above example, if the ram is to be operated by a hydraulic motor rotating at 1500 rpm:

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 241.875}{60}$$

$$P = 38,008 \text{ kW} \approx 38 \text{ MW}$$

This high power requirement is why modern ships use **electro-hydraulic** systems with multiple pump-motor units and accumulators.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Steering Gear System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Helm[Helm Wheel] -->|Mechanical Input| Telemotor[Telemotor Unit]
    Telemotor -->|Hydraulic Signal| ServoValve[Servo Valve]
    ServoValve -->|High Pressure Oil| PumpBank[Hydraulic Pump Bank]
    PumpBank -->|Oil Flow| RamUnit[Hydraulic Ram Cylinder]
    RamUnit -->|Linear Motion| Tiller[Tiller Arm]
    Tiller -->|Rotational Motion| RudderStock[Rudder Stock]
    RudderStock -->|Rotation| RudderBlade[Rudder Blade]
    RudderBlade -->|Hydrodynamic Force| WaterFlow[Water Flow Past Hull]
    WaterFlow -->|Yaw Moment| ShipHull[Ship Hull Direction Change]
    RudderStock -->|Feedback| AngleIndicator[Rudder Angle Indicator]
    AngleIndicator -->|Visual Display| Helm
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Force and Torque Transmission Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input["INPUT PHASE"]
        A1[Ship Speed V] --> A2[Relative Water Velocity]
        A3[Helm Angle alpha] --> A2
    end

    subgraph Force["FORCE GENERATION PHASE"]
        A2 --> B1[Dynamic Pressure 0.5 rho V squared]
        B1 --> B2[Pressure on Rudder Surface]
        B2 --> B3[Rudder Force F_R]
    end

    subgraph Moment["MOMENT GENERATION PHASE"]
        B3 --> C1[Center of Pressure at L by 3]
        C1 --> C2[Force times Arm]
        B3 --> C3[Balance Ratio Factor]
        C3 --> C2
        C2 --> C4[Turning Moment M_T]
    end

    subgraph Output["OUTPUT PHASE"]
        C4 --> D1[Torque on Rudder Head]
        D1 --> D2[Required Steering Gear Capacity]
        D2 --> D3[Steering Gear Selection]
    end
```

## 4.5 Physical Description (Fallback for Shipyard Drawing)

Since steering gear systems are typically drawn in shipyard General Arrangement (GA) plans, here is a textual representation of the **physical layout**:

1. **Upper Rudder Bearing**: Located above the hull line, supports the upper part of the rudder stock.
2. **Rudder Stock**: A forged steel cylindrical shaft running from the rudder blade up to the steering gear compartment.
3. **Tiller Arm**: A horizontal lever arm bolted to the rudder stock at the top of the steering gear compartment.
4. **Crosshead**: Sliding element that converts the tiller rotation into linear motion (or vice versa).
5. **Hydraulic Rams**: Two opposed cylinders (port and starboard) with pistons connected to the crosshead.
6. **Quadrant or Piston Type**: Indicates the connection style between the tiller and the rams.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define steering gear. State the SOLAS requirements for main and auxiliary steering gear.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Steering gear is the complete system of mechanisms, power units, and controls that rotate the rudder of a ship to change its heading. It transmits the helm command from the bridge to the rudder stock and applies the necessary torque to overcome hydrodynamic forces.

**SOLAS Requirements (1.5 Marks):**
- **Main Steering Gear**: Must turn the rudder from 35° on one side to 35° on the other side at the **maximum service speed** within **28 seconds**.
- **Auxiliary Steering Gear**: Must perform the same rudder movement at a speed of **7 knots** (or half the maximum service speed, whichever is greater) within **60 seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Award Marks:** 1.5 for definition, 1.0 for main gear specs, 0.5 for auxiliary gear specs.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Differentiate between balanced and unbalanced rudders with reference to turning moment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Unbalanced Rudder | Balanced Rudder |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Stock Position | At leading edge | Behind leading edge |
| Turning Moment | $M_T = F_R \cdot (L/3)$ (High) | $M_T = F_R \cdot e$ (Lower) |
| Balance Ratio | $b = 0$ | $b = 0.20$ to $0.33$ |
| Power Required | Higher | Lower (25-33% less) |
| Construction | Simple, robust | Complex, requires forward flap |

**Turning Moment Comparison (1.5 Marks):** For the same rudder force, a balanced rudder with balance ratio 0.25 produces 25% less turning moment than an unbalanced one, because the forward portion of the area generates a counter-moment that reduces the net torque.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Question (a) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of an electro-hydraulic steering gear system. List its advantages over mechanical steering gear.

**Question (b) [7 Marks]:** A ship of 120 m length and 8 m draft has a balanced rudder of area 18 m² and balance ratio 0.30. The ship moves at 14 knots. Calculate (i) the rudder force using Taylor's formula, (ii) the turning moment on the rudder head, and (iii) the power required to operate the steering gear at 1450 rpm.

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Working of Electro-Hydraulic Steering Gear (4 Marks):**

The electro-hydraulic steering gear operates on the **follow-up control principle**:

1. **Helm Input**: The helmsman turns the wheel, sending an electrical signal to the steering control unit.
2. **Servo Valve Actuation**: The control unit operates a servo valve that directs high-pressure oil from the pump to one side of the hydraulic ram.
3. **Ram Operation**: Oil enters the port-side chamber of the ram, pushing the piston (and attached tiller) to starboard, or vice versa.
4. **Rudder Rotation**: Tiller rotation turns the rudder stock, rotating the rudder blade to the commanded angle.
5. **Follow-up Feedback**: A feedback transmitter (potentiometer/LVDT) on the rudder stock sends the actual rudder angle back to the control unit.
6. **Servo Centering**: When the actual angle equals the commanded angle, the servo valve centers, holding the rudder in position.

**Sketch (1.5 Marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 diagram]

**Advantages over Mechanical Steering Gear (1.5 Marks):**
- Higher torque capacity (suitable for large ships).
- Faster response and smoother control.
- Reduced manual effort by the helmsman.
- Self-locking and self-centering capability.
- Compatible with autopilot systems.
- Less maintenance than chains and gears in mechanical systems.

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Given Data:**
- $L_s = 120$ m
- $T = 8$ m
- $A_R = 18$ m²
- $b = 0.30$
- $V = 14$ knots
- $K = 0.13$
- $N = 1450$ rpm

**Step 1: Convert speed to m/s (0.5 Marks)**

$$V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate rudder force (2 Marks)**

$$F_R = K \cdot A_R \cdot V^2 = 0.13 \times 18 \times (7.2016)^2$$

$$F_R = 0.13 \times 18 \times 51.86$$

$$F_R = 121.35 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Calculate eccentricity (1 Mark)**

Assuming a rectangular rudder with aspect ratio 1.5:
- Rudder height $h = \sqrt{1.5 \times 18} = 5.196$ m
- Mean chord $L_c = 18 / 5.196 = 3.464$ m

$$e = \frac{L_c}{3}(1 - b) = \frac{3.464}{3}(1 - 0.30) = 1.155 \times 0.70 = 0.808 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Calculate turning moment (1.5 Marks)**

$$M_T = F_R \cdot e = 121.35 \times 0.808 = 98.05 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate power required (2 Marks)**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times 98.05}{60}$$

$$P = \frac{893,228}{60} = 14,887 \text{ kW} \approx 14.89 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert speed from **knots to m/s** when using Taylor's formula with $K = 0.13$. The constant $K$ is calibrated for SI units, so direct substitution of $V$ in knots will give a **completely wrong answer**. Always convert first!

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Another common error is using $L/3$ for an unbalanced rudder formula on a balanced rudder. Always check the balance ratio $b$ first and use $M_T = F_R \cdot e$ when $b \neq 0$.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Question (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain with diagrams the different types of rudders used in ships. Discuss the concept of balance ratio and its effect on rudder torque.

**Question (b) [7 Marks]:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars: 180 m
- Draft: 12 m
- Block coefficient: 0.72
- Service speed: 16 knots

Design a balanced rudder (assume balance ratio = 0.27) and calculate the maximum turning moment at service speed. Use $K = 0.13$ for Taylor's formula.

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Types of Rudders (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Unbalanced Rudder**: Stock at leading edge. Maximum torque, used in small vessels.
2. **Balanced Rudder**: Stock shifted aft of leading edge by 20-33% of chord. Reduces torque significantly.
3. **Semi-Balanced Rudder**: Intermediate type with small forward flap.
4. **Spade Rudder**: Single-plate rudder supported only by the stock (no hull support).
5. **Skeg Rudder**: Mounted on a fin-like skeg for additional support.
6. **Twin Rudders**: Two rudders used in tugboats for high maneuverability.

**Balance Ratio Concept (2 Marks):**

Balance ratio $b = \dfrac{A_f}{A_f + A_a}$

Effect on torque:
- Higher $b$ → Lower torque → Less steering gear capacity needed
- Lower $b$ → Higher torque → Greater steering gear capacity
- **Optimal** $b = 0.20$ to $0.33$ balances torque reduction with hydrodynamic efficiency.

**Diagram (1.5 Marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 schematic for a labeled diagram]

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Step 1: Estimate rudder area (1.5 Marks)**

Using the empirical formula:

$$A_R = 0.025 \times L \times T = 0.025 \times 180 \times 12 = 54 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert speed to m/s (0.5 Marks)**

$$V = 16 \times 0.5144 = 8.23 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate rudder force (2 Marks)**

$$F_R = K \cdot A_R \cdot V^2 = 0.13 \times 54 \times (8.23)^2$$

$$F_R = 0.13 \times 54 \times 67.73 = 475.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Determine rudder dimensions (1 Mark)**

For a rectangular rudder with aspect ratio $\lambda = 1.5$:
- $h^2 / A_R = 1.5 \Rightarrow h = \sqrt{1.5 \times 54} = 9.0$ m
- Mean chord: $L_c = 54 / 9.0 = 6.0$ m

**Step 5: Calculate eccentricity (1 Mark)**

$$e = \frac{L_c}{3}(1 - b) = \frac{6.0}{3}(1 - 0.27) = 2.0 \times 0.73 = 1.46 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate maximum turning moment (1 Mark)**

$$M_T = F_R \cdot e = 475.4 \times 1.46 = 694.1 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students will use the wrong empirical constant for rudder area. **0.015 to 0.06** is the acceptable range, but using $0.015$ will underestimate by a factor of **2**, leading to a significantly undersized rudder. The choice depends on ship type: tankers and bulk carriers use larger rudder area ratios (~0.025-0.04) for better maneuverability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for Steering Gear:**

### Core Concepts:
- ✅ Steering gear = system that rotates the rudder using mechanical, hydraulic, or electro-hydraulic power.
- ✅ Main steering gear operates at **maximum service speed** within **28 seconds** (35° to 35°).
- ✅ Auxiliary steering gear operates at **7 knots** within **60 seconds**.
- ✅ Electro-hydraulic is the most common type for modern merchant and naval ships.

### Rudder Types:
- ✅ **Unbalanced** rudder → entire force creates moment, stock at leading edge.
- ✅ **Balanced** rudder → forward area reduces net moment, stock shifted aft.
- ✅ Balance ratio $b = A_f / A_{total}$, typically **0.20 to 0.33**.

### Key Formulas (Memorize):
- ✅ $F_R = 0.13 \cdot A_R \cdot V^2$ (Taylor's formula, SI units)
- ✅ $F_R = 0.00535 \cdot A_R \cdot V_s^2$ (Maltby's formula, $A_R$ in ft², $V$ in knots, $F_R$ in tonnes)
- ✅ $M_T = F_R \cdot e$ where $e = (L/3)(1 - b)$
- ✅ $P = 2\pi N T / 60$
- ✅ $A_R = (0.015 \text{ to } 0.06) L \cdot T$

### Unit Conversions (Critical):
- ✅ 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**
- ✅ 1 tonne-force = **9.81 kN**
- ✅ 1 ft² = **0.0929 m²**

### Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid:
- ❌ Never mix units in Taylor's formula.
- ❌ Never use unbalanced formula for balanced rudder.
- ❌ Always convert rudder area ratio from L·T product, not from displacement.
- ❌ For SOLAS, remember **28 seconds (main)** and **60 seconds (auxiliary)**.

### Industrial / SOLAS Compliance:
- ✅ SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 29 governs steering gear standards.
- ✅ Classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, IRS) verify compliance.
- ✅ Modern ships have **two independent steering gear systems** for redundancy.

### Application Areas Beyond Ships:
- ✅ Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)
- ✅ Submarines (with diving planes)
- ✅ Floating offshore platforms (dynamic positioning systems)
